beginner.explanation: The woman touched the edge of Jesus' cloak and was immediately healed. Jesus felt power go out from him and asked who touched him. He told the woman, 'Your faith has healed you. Go in peace.'

beginner.question: In the city of Nain, Jesus came across a funeral procession for a widow's only son. What did Jesus do?
options: He raised the boy from the dead
beginner.explanation: Jesus had compassion on the grieving widow and told her not to cry. He touched the coffin and said, 'Young man, I say to you, get up!' The dead man sat up and began to talk, and Jesus gave him back to his mother.

beginner.question: Jesus told a parable about a rich man who stored up grain in bigger and bigger barns but ignored God. What happened to the rich man that night?
beginner.explanation: God said to the rich fool, 'You fool! This very night your life will be demanded from you. Then who will get what you have prepared for yourself?' Jesus used this to warn against greed and ignoring God.

beginner.question: Jesus told a story about ten young women waiting for a bridegroom. Five were wise and five were foolish. Why were the foolish ones left out?
options: They had no oil for their lamps
beginner.explanation: The five foolish virgins did not bring enough oil for their lamps and went to buy more. While they were away, the bridegroom arrived. The door was shut and they were left out. Jesus taught readiness for his return.

beginner.question: In a parable, Jesus described a father who had two sons. The younger son left home, wasted his money, and ended up feeding pigs. What did he decide to do?
beginner.explanation: The prodigal son came to his senses and decided to return home, willing to be treated as a servant. But his father saw him from a distance, ran to him, embraced him, and threw a feast to celebrate his return.

beginner.question: Jesus often taught using short stories with moral lessons. What are these stories called?
beginner.explanation: Jesus frequently used parables — simple stories from everyday life that taught deeper spiritual truths. Well-known parables include the Good Samaritan, the Prodigal Son, the Sower, and the Mustard Seed.

beginner.question: According to the Gospels, Jesus declared that he was the 'bread of _____' and that whoever comes to him will never go hungry.
beginner.explanation: Jesus said, 'I am the bread of life. Whoever comes to me will never go hungry, and whoever believes in me will never be thirsty.' He meant that he satisfies the deepest spiritual needs of humanity.

beginner.question: Jesus said, 'I am the vine; you are the _____.' What word completes this saying?
beginner.explanation: Jesus said, 'I am the vine; you are the branches.' He taught that just as branches must stay connected to the vine to bear fruit, his followers must remain connected to him to live fruitful spiritual lives.

beginner.question: When a paralysed man was brought to Jesus, his friends could not get through the crowd. How did they get the man to Jesus?
options: They lowered him through a hole in the roof
They carried him over the crowd
beginner.explanation: The man's friends were so determined that they climbed onto the roof, made an opening, and lowered their paralysed friend on his mat right down in front of Jesus. Jesus was moved by their faith and healed the man.

beginner.question: Jesus encountered a man possessed by many demons near a lake. After Jesus cast out the demons, where did the demons go?
beginner.explanation: Jesus cast the demons out of the man, and they went into a large herd of pigs. The pigs rushed down a steep bank into the lake and drowned. The healed man wanted to follow Jesus, but Jesus told him to go home and tell what God had done.

beginner.question: A synagogue leader named Jairus begged Jesus for help because his daughter was dying. What did Jesus do?
options: He said it was too late
He raised the girl from the dead
beginner.explanation: By the time Jesus arrived, the girl had died. Jesus told the mourners, 'She is not dead but sleeping.' He took the girl's hand and said, 'Little girl, get up!' She immediately stood up and walked around.

beginner.question: One of Jesus' 'I am' statements is 'I am the door' (or gate). What did he mean people enter through him?
beginner.explanation: Jesus said, 'I am the gate; whoever enters through me will be saved.' Just as a gate provides the way into safety for sheep, Jesus is the way to salvation and eternal life for all who believe in him.

beginner.question: Jesus said, 'I am the resurrection and the _____. The one who believes in me will live, even though they die.'
beginner.explanation: Jesus spoke these words to Martha before raising her brother Lazarus from the dead. He declared himself to be the source of both resurrection and eternal life, promising life beyond death for all who believe.

beginner.question: Nicodemus, a Pharisee and member of the Jewish council, visited Jesus at night. What famous teaching did Jesus give him?
beginner.explanation: Jesus told Nicodemus, 'Very truly I tell you, no one can see the kingdom of God unless they are born again.' This conversation also includes John 3:16, one of the most famous verses in the Bible.

beginner.question: One of the most famous Bible verses, John 3:16, begins with 'For God so loved the _____.'
beginner.explanation: 'For God so loved the world that he gave his one and only Son, that whoever believes in him shall not perish but have eternal life.' This verse summarises the core message of Christianity — God's love for all people.

beginner.question: At a well in Samaria, Jesus had a conversation with a woman who was surprised he spoke to her. Why was she surprised?
options: Jews normally did not speak with Samaritans
beginner.explanation: The Samaritan woman was surprised because Jews and Samaritans had a long history of hostility and usually avoided each other. Jesus broke social barriers by speaking with her and offering her 'living water.'

beginner.question: At the well in Samaria, Jesus offered the woman something he called 'living _____.' What word completes this?
beginner.explanation: Jesus told the Samaritan woman that whoever drinks the living water he gives will never be thirsty again. He was speaking about the spiritual life and salvation he offers, not physical water.

beginner.question: When Jesus called the fishermen James and John to follow him, they were in a boat with their father mending nets. Who was their father?
beginner.explanation: James and John were the sons of Zebedee. They were fishing partners with Simon Peter. When Jesus called them, they immediately left their father Zebedee in the boat with the hired men and followed Jesus.

beginner.question: Jesus gave James and John a nickname meaning 'Sons of Thunder.' Why did he call them this?
beginner.explanation: Jesus nicknamed James and John 'Boanerges,' meaning Sons of Thunder, likely because of their bold and passionate temperaments. They once asked Jesus if they should call down fire from heaven on a Samaritan village.

beginner.question: After his resurrection, Jesus appeared to his disciples by the Sea of Galilee and cooked them breakfast. What food did he prepare?
beginner.explanation: Jesus had a charcoal fire ready with fish on it, and bread. He told the disciples to bring some of their catch too. This was a touching scene of Jesus caring for his friends after rising from the dead.

beginner.question: By the Sea of Galilee after his resurrection, Jesus asked Peter three times 'Do you love me?' How many times had Peter previously denied Jesus?
beginner.explanation: Peter had denied knowing Jesus three times on the night of his arrest. Jesus' three questions mirrored those three denials, giving Peter the chance to affirm his love three times and be fully restored.

beginner.question: Jesus performed a miracle at a wedding by turning water into wine. Who first told Jesus that the wine had run out?
beginner.explanation: Mary, Jesus' mother, noticed the wine had run out and told Jesus. Although Jesus said his time had not yet come, Mary told the servants, 'Do whatever he tells you.' Jesus then performed the miracle.

beginner.question: Jesus told his followers that two sparrows are sold for a small coin, yet God cares about each one. He then said his followers are worth more than many _____.
beginner.explanation: Jesus said, 'Are not two sparrows sold for a penny? Yet not one of them will fall to the ground outside your Father's care... So don't be afraid; you are worth more than many sparrows.' This teaches God's personal care.

beginner.question: In one of his last statements on the cross, Jesus cried out 'It is _____.' What word completes this?
beginner.explanation: Jesus said, 'It is finished' just before he died, indicating that his mission of salvation was complete. The Greek word 'tetelestai' means 'paid in full,' signifying his sacrifice covered the debt of sin.

beginner.question: A man named Simon was forced by Roman soldiers to help Jesus carry his cross. Where was Simon from?
beginner.explanation: Simon of Cyrene was passing by on his way in from the country when the soldiers forced him to carry Jesus' cross. Cyrene was a city in North Africa (modern-day Libya). He helped Jesus when he was too weakened to carry it alone.

beginner.question: After the resurrection, Jesus told his disciples to wait in Jerusalem for a special gift. What were they waiting for?
beginner.explanation: Jesus told his disciples to stay in Jerusalem and wait for the gift the Father had promised — the Holy Spirit. This was fulfilled at Pentecost, when the Holy Spirit came upon the believers with power.

beginner.question: Jesus once cursed a fig tree because it had no fruit, and the tree withered. What lesson did he teach from this?
beginner.explanation: When the disciples marvelled at the withered fig tree, Jesus taught them about the power of faith: 'If you have faith and do not doubt... you can say to this mountain, "Go, throw yourself into the sea," and it will be done.'

beginner.question: On the Sea of Galilee, Peter tried walking on water toward Jesus but began to sink. What did Jesus say to him?
options: You of little faith, why did you doubt?
beginner.explanation: Jesus immediately reached out his hand, caught Peter, and said, 'You of little faith, why did you doubt?' When they climbed into the boat, the wind died down and the disciples worshipped Jesus.

beginner.question: A rich young man asked Jesus what he must do to have eternal life. Jesus told him to sell everything and follow him. What did the young man do?
options: He went away sad because he had great wealth
beginner.explanation: The rich young man went away sad because he had great wealth and was unwilling to give it up. Jesus then said, 'It is easier for a camel to go through the eye of a needle than for someone who is rich to enter the kingdom of God.'

beginner.question: Jesus was visiting the home of two sisters, Mary and Martha. Martha was busy preparing food. What was Mary doing?
options: Sitting at Jesus' feet listening to him
beginner.explanation: Mary sat at Jesus' feet, listening to his teaching. When Martha complained that Mary wasn't helping, Jesus said Mary had chosen 'what is better' — suggesting that spending time with Jesus is more important than being busy.

beginner.question: Jesus healed a blind beggar near Jericho who cried out 'Jesus, Son of David, have mercy on me!' What was this man's name?
beginner.explanation: Bartimaeus was a blind beggar sitting by the roadside near Jericho. Despite the crowd telling him to be quiet, he kept calling out to Jesus. Jesus stopped, called him over, and healed his blindness because of his faith.

beginner.question: At the Last Supper, when Jesus announced that one of them would betray him, the disciples were shocked. What did they each ask?
options: Surely you don't mean me, Lord?
beginner.explanation: The disciples were deeply sad and began saying one after another, 'Surely you don't mean me, Lord?' Even Judas, the one who would betray him, asked the same question. Jesus indicated it would be the one who dipped bread with him.

beginner.question: During the temptation in the wilderness, the devil took Jesus to a very high place and showed him all the kingdoms of the world. What did the devil offer in exchange for Jesus worshipping him?
options: All the kingdoms of the world
beginner.explanation: Satan offered Jesus all the kingdoms of the world and their splendour if Jesus would bow down and worship him. Jesus refused, saying, 'Away from me, Satan! For it is written: Worship the Lord your God, and serve him only.'

beginner.question: Jesus told the story of a Pharisee and a tax collector who both went to the Temple to pray. Which man went home justified (right with God)?
beginner.explanation: The tax collector humbly beat his chest and prayed, 'God, have mercy on me, a sinner.' Jesus said this man, not the proud Pharisee, went home justified before God. The story teaches that God values humility over self-righteousness.

beginner.question: Jesus was once in a boat during a terrible storm. What were the disciples doing while Jesus was sleeping?
beginner.explanation: The disciples were terrified as waves crashed over the boat. They woke Jesus, crying, 'Lord, save us! We're going to drown!' Jesus got up, rebuked the wind and waves, and there was complete calm.

beginner.question: What did Jesus say to the repentant criminal who was crucified next to him?
options: Today you will be with me in paradise
beginner.explanation: One of the criminals being crucified alongside Jesus defended him and asked, 'Jesus, remember me when you come into your kingdom.' Jesus replied, 'Truly I tell you, today you will be with me in paradise.'

beginner.question: How did Jesus enter the world differently from all other humans, according to the Gospel accounts?
options: He came down from a cloud
He was born of a virgin
He was found in a river
beginner.explanation: According to the Gospels, Jesus was born to the Virgin Mary, conceived by the Holy Spirit without a human father. This miraculous birth — the virgin birth — is a foundational belief in Christianity.

beginner.question: What did the Wise Men do after visiting baby Jesus? An angel warned them not to return to King Herod, so they went home by a different _____.
beginner.explanation: Having been warned in a dream not to go back to Herod, the Wise Men returned to their country by another route. Herod had asked them to report back so he could find and kill the child.

beginner.question: Jesus said 'Blessed are those who mourn.' According to him, what will they receive?
beginner.explanation: In the Beatitudes, Jesus said, 'Blessed are those who mourn, for they will be comforted.' This promised that God sees our grief and will bring comfort, both in this life and in eternity.

beginner.question: When Jesus fed a crowd of 4,000 people in a separate miracle, how many baskets of leftovers were collected?
beginner.explanation: In addition to feeding the 5,000, Jesus also fed 4,000 people with seven loaves and a few fish. After everyone had eaten, seven baskets of broken pieces were left over, demonstrating God's abundant provision.

beginner.question: Jesus met a Samaritan woman at Jacob's Well. At what time of day did this encounter take place?
beginner.explanation: Jesus sat by the well at about the sixth hour, which was noon. It was unusual for a woman to draw water at the hottest part of the day, suggesting she may have been avoiding other townspeople.

beginner.question: After raising Lazarus from the dead, Jesus called out to the tomb. What were his exact words?
beginner.explanation: Jesus cried out in a loud voice, 'Lazarus, come out!' Lazarus walked out of the tomb, still wrapped in his burial cloths. Jesus then told the people, 'Take off the grave clothes and let him go.'

beginner.question: What did Jesus say was the second greatest commandment, after loving God with all your heart?
beginner.explanation: Jesus said the second greatest commandment is 'Love your neighbour as yourself.' He said all the Law and the Prophets hang on these two commandments — loving God and loving your neighbour.

beginner.question: On the night he was arrested, Jesus prayed so intensely in the Garden of Gethsemane that his sweat was like drops of what?
beginner.explanation: Luke records that Jesus prayed so earnestly that his sweat was like drops of blood falling to the ground. This detail shows the immense anguish Jesus felt knowing the suffering that awaited him.

beginner.question: After his resurrection, Jesus appeared to his disciples in a locked room. What was the first thing he said to them?
beginner.explanation: Jesus appeared to his frightened disciples behind locked doors and said, 'Peace be with you!' He then showed them his hands and side. The disciples were overjoyed when they saw the Lord alive again.

beginner.question: Jesus was born in the town of Bethlehem.
beginner.explanation: Jesus was born in Bethlehem, a small town near Jerusalem. Mary and Joseph had travelled there from Nazareth because of a Roman census ordered by Caesar Augustus.

beginner.question: When Jesus was born, Mary laid him in a manger because there was no room at the inn.
beginner.explanation: Because the inn was full, Mary wrapped baby Jesus in cloths and placed him in a manger — a feeding trough for animals. This humble beginning is a central part of the Christmas story.

beginner.question: Jesus was born in the city of Jerusalem, the capital of Israel.
beginner.explanation: Jesus was not born in Jerusalem. He was born in Bethlehem, a small town about six miles south of Jerusalem. Mary and Joseph travelled there for a Roman census.

beginner.question: An angel named Gabriel told Mary that she would give birth to the Son of God.
beginner.explanation: The angel Gabriel was sent by God to Nazareth and appeared to Mary, telling her she had found favour with God and would conceive a son to be named Jesus. Mary accepted God's plan.

beginner.question: Joseph, the earthly father of Jesus, worked as a fisherman.
beginner.explanation: Joseph was a carpenter (or craftsman), not a fisherman. People in Nazareth knew Jesus as 'the carpenter's son.' Some of Jesus' disciples, such as Peter and Andrew, were fishermen.

beginner.question: Farmers working in their fields were the first to hear about Jesus' birth when angels appeared to them at night.
beginner.explanation: It was shepherds, not farmers, who first heard about Jesus' birth. Angels appeared to shepherds watching their flocks near Bethlehem at night and announced the birth of the Saviour.

beginner.question: The Wise Men (Magi) followed a bright star to find the newborn Jesus.
beginner.explanation: The Wise Men from the East saw a special star and followed it to find the child Jesus. The star guided them all the way to where Jesus was, and they worshipped him and presented gifts.

beginner.question: The Wise Men brought Jesus gifts of gold, silver, and diamonds.
beginner.explanation: The Wise Men brought gold, frankincense, and myrrh — not silver and diamonds. Gold honoured him as King, frankincense was used in worship, and myrrh was a burial spice foreshadowing his death.

beginner.question: King Herod tried to kill baby Jesus because he felt threatened by a new 'King of the Jews.'
beginner.explanation: When the Wise Men told Herod about the newborn King, he was deeply threatened. He ordered all boys under two years old in Bethlehem to be killed. Joseph was warned in a dream and fled to Egypt with Mary and Jesus.

beginner.question: Mary and Joseph fled to Rome to escape King Herod.
beginner.explanation: Mary and Joseph did not flee to Rome. An angel warned Joseph in a dream to take the family to Egypt, where they stayed until King Herod died. They then returned to Israel and settled in Nazareth.

beginner.question: Jesus grew up in the town of Bethlehem, where he had been born.
beginner.explanation: Jesus did not grow up in Bethlehem. Although he was born there, his family settled in Nazareth in the region of Galilee. He became known as 'Jesus of Nazareth.'

beginner.question: When Jesus was 12 years old, he amazed the teachers in the Temple in Jerusalem with his understanding.
beginner.explanation: During a Passover visit to Jerusalem, 12-year-old Jesus stayed behind in the Temple. His parents found him sitting among the teachers, listening to them and asking questions. Everyone was amazed at his understanding.

beginner.question: John the Baptist baptised Jesus in the Red Sea.
beginner.explanation: Jesus was baptised in the Jordan River, not the Red Sea. John the Baptist was preaching and baptising in the Jordan when Jesus came from Galilee to be baptised by him.

beginner.question: When Jesus was baptised, the Holy Spirit came down on him in the form of a flame of fire.
beginner.explanation: The Holy Spirit descended on Jesus in the form of a dove, not a flame of fire. Flames of fire appeared later at Pentecost when the Holy Spirit came upon the disciples. At Jesus' baptism, a voice from heaven also said, 'This is my Son, whom I love.'

beginner.question: Jesus spent 40 days fasting and being tempted by the devil in the wilderness.
beginner.explanation: After his baptism, Jesus was led by the Spirit into the wilderness for 40 days and nights. He fasted the entire time and was tempted by the devil, but he resisted every temptation by quoting Scripture.

beginner.question: Jesus spent only 7 days fasting in the wilderness before beginning his ministry.
beginner.explanation: Jesus fasted for 40 days and nights in the wilderness, not 7 days. The number 40 echoes Moses' 40 days on Mount Sinai and Israel's 40 years in the desert.

beginner.question: Jesus chose exactly 12 disciples to be his closest followers.
beginner.explanation: Jesus called 12 men to be his apostles. They included Peter, Andrew, James, John, Philip, Bartholomew, Matthew, Thomas, James son of Alphaeus, Thaddaeus, Simon the Zealot, and Judas Iscariot.

beginner.question: Peter and Andrew, the first disciples Jesus called, were farmers when Jesus found them.
beginner.explanation: Peter and Andrew were fishermen, not farmers. They were casting nets into the Sea of Galilee when Jesus called them, saying, 'Follow me, and I will make you fishers of men.'

beginner.question: Matthew, one of Jesus' 12 disciples, was a tax collector before Jesus called him.
beginner.explanation: Matthew (also called Levi) was sitting at his tax collection booth when Jesus said, 'Follow me.' Tax collectors were despised in Jewish society because they worked for the Romans, but Jesus chose Matthew anyway.

beginner.question: Jesus' first miracle was healing a blind man in Jerusalem.
beginner.explanation: Jesus' first miracle was turning water into wine at a wedding in Cana of Galilee, not healing a blind man. His mother Mary told him the wine had run out, and Jesus transformed water in six stone jars into wine.

beginner.question: At a wedding in Cana, Jesus turned water into wine as his first recorded miracle.
beginner.explanation: When the wine ran out at a wedding in Cana, Jesus told servants to fill stone jars with water. He then turned the water into excellent wine. This was the first of his miraculous signs.

beginner.question: Jesus fed a crowd of 5,000 people with just seven loaves of bread and three fish.
beginner.explanation: Jesus used five loaves and two fish (not seven and three) to feed the 5,000. A young boy offered his small lunch, and Jesus miraculously multiplied it to feed the entire crowd with 12 baskets left over.

beginner.question: After feeding the 5,000, nothing was left over — the crowd ate every last crumb.
beginner.explanation: There were actually 12 baskets of leftovers collected after everyone had eaten their fill. The abundance of leftovers demonstrated the miraculous nature of the feeding and God's generous provision.

beginner.question: Jesus walked on the water of the Sea of Galilee.
beginner.explanation: Jesus walked on the Sea of Galilee during a storm to reach his disciples' boat. When they first saw him, they were terrified and thought he was a ghost. Jesus told them, 'Take courage! It is I. Don't be afraid.'

beginner.question: When Jesus walked on water, the disciple John stepped out of the boat to walk to him.
beginner.explanation: It was Peter, not John, who stepped out of the boat. Peter walked on the water toward Jesus but became afraid of the wind and waves and began to sink. Jesus reached out and caught him.

beginner.question: Jesus raised his friend Lazarus from the dead after Lazarus had been in the tomb for four days.
beginner.explanation: Lazarus, the brother of Mary and Martha, had been dead and buried for four days when Jesus arrived. Jesus called out, 'Lazarus, come out!' and Lazarus walked out of the tomb, still wrapped in burial cloths.

beginner.question: 'Jesus wept' is the shortest verse in the Bible, showing his grief over Lazarus' death.
beginner.explanation: John 11:35, 'Jesus wept,' is widely recognised as the shortest verse in the Bible. It reveals Jesus' deep human emotion and compassion as he grieved alongside Mary and Martha before raising Lazarus.

beginner.question: Jesus calmed a storm on the Sea of Galilee by praying quietly for several hours.
beginner.explanation: Jesus did not pray quietly for hours. He simply stood up and rebuked the wind and waves, commanding 'Peace, be still!' The storm immediately ceased. The disciples were amazed that even the wind and waves obeyed him.

beginner.question: Jesus healed ten lepers, and all ten came back to thank him.
beginner.explanation: Only one of the ten healed lepers returned to thank Jesus, and he was a Samaritan. Jesus asked, 'Were not all ten cleansed? Where are the other nine?' This story highlights the importance of gratitude.

beginner.question: The Sermon on the Mount is one of the most famous sermons Jesus ever gave, found in the Gospel of Matthew.
beginner.explanation: The Sermon on the Mount is recorded in Matthew chapters 5-7. It includes the Beatitudes, the Lord's Prayer, the Golden Rule, and many of Jesus' most important teachings about kingdom living.

beginner.question: The Lord's Prayer was taught by Jesus when his disciples asked him how to pray.
beginner.explanation: The disciples asked Jesus, 'Lord, teach us to pray.' He gave them what is now called the Lord's Prayer, beginning with 'Our Father, who art in heaven.' It has become the most widely prayed prayer in Christianity.

beginner.question: The Golden Rule — 'Do to others what you would have them do to you' — was taught by Jesus in the Sermon on the Mount.
beginner.explanation: Jesus taught the Golden Rule as part of the Sermon on the Mount. It summarises the ethical teaching of the Law and the Prophets: treat others the way you would want to be treated.

beginner.question: The Beatitudes are a list of punishments that Jesus warned people about.
beginner.explanation: The Beatitudes are blessings, not punishments. Each begins with 'Blessed are...' and describes the qualities God values, such as humility, mercy, purity, and peacemaking, with promises of God's reward.

beginner.question: In the Parable of the Good Samaritan, a Samaritan man helped a wounded stranger after a priest and a Levite had walked past without helping.
beginner.explanation: A man was beaten and left for dead on the road. Both a priest and a Levite passed by without helping. A Samaritan stopped, bandaged the man's wounds, and took him to an inn, paying for his care.

beginner.question: In the Parable of the Prodigal Son, the father refused to welcome back his wasteful son.
beginner.explanation: The father did not refuse his son. When the prodigal son returned home, the father ran to embrace him, kissed him, and threw a great celebration. This parable illustrates God's unconditional love and forgiveness.

beginner.question: Jesus said the greatest commandment is to keep the Sabbath day holy above all else.
beginner.explanation: Jesus said the greatest commandment is to 'Love the Lord your God with all your heart and with all your soul and with all your mind,' not to keep the Sabbath. Love for God is the foundation of all other commandments.

beginner.question: Jesus said 'I am the good shepherd' who lays down his life for the sheep.
beginner.explanation: Jesus described himself as the Good Shepherd who willingly lays down his life for his sheep. Unlike a hired worker who flees when danger comes, the good shepherd protects his flock at any cost.

beginner.question: In the Parable of the Lost Sheep, a shepherd left 50 sheep to find one that was lost.
beginner.explanation: The shepherd left 99 sheep, not 50, to search for the one that was lost. When he found it, he rejoiced and carried it home on his shoulders. Jesus used this to show how God values every individual.

beginner.question: Jesus compared the Kingdom of God to a tiny mustard seed that grows into a large tree.
beginner.explanation: In the Parable of the Mustard Seed, Jesus said the kingdom of heaven is like the smallest of all seeds, but when it grows, it becomes the largest of garden plants — a tree where birds can nest in its branches.

beginner.question: Jesus told his followers they are 'the sugar of the earth.'
beginner.explanation: Jesus said 'You are the salt of the earth,' not sugar. Salt was valued for preservation and adding flavour. Jesus meant his followers should have a positive, preserving influence on the world around them.

beginner.question: Jesus said 'I am the way, the truth, and the life.'
beginner.explanation: Jesus declared, 'I am the way, the truth, and the life. No one comes to the Father except through me.' This is one of the most well-known statements in the Bible, spoken to his disciple Thomas.

beginner.question: Jesus said 'I am the light of the world.'
beginner.explanation: Jesus declared, 'I am the light of the world. Whoever follows me will never walk in darkness, but will have the light of life.' He contrasted spiritual light with the darkness of life without God.

beginner.question: In the Parable of the Wise and Foolish Builders, the wise man built his house on sand.
beginner.explanation: The wise man built his house on rock, not sand. The foolish man built on sand. When storms came, the house on rock stood firm while the house on sand collapsed. Obeying Jesus' words is like building on rock.

beginner.question: Jesus told Peter to forgive someone who wrongs him seventy times seven times, meaning there should be no limit to forgiveness.
beginner.explanation: When Peter asked if forgiving seven times was enough, Jesus said seventy times seven. This wasn't a literal number — it meant forgiveness should be unlimited. Christians are called to forgive as God forgives them.

beginner.question: Jesus told his disciples to send the children away because he was too busy for them.
beginner.explanation: Jesus did the opposite. When the disciples tried to keep children away, Jesus rebuked them and said, 'Let the little children come to me, and do not hinder them, for the kingdom of heaven belongs to such as these.'

beginner.question: A short tax collector named Zacchaeus climbed a sycamore tree to see Jesus passing through Jericho.
beginner.explanation: Zacchaeus was too short to see over the crowd, so he ran ahead and climbed a sycamore-fig tree. Jesus saw him and said, 'Zacchaeus, come down immediately. I must stay at your house today.'

beginner.question: On Palm Sunday, Jesus rode into Jerusalem on a white horse like a conquering king.
beginner.explanation: Jesus rode into Jerusalem on a donkey, not a horse. A horse symbolised military power, while a donkey symbolised peace. This humble entry fulfilled the prophecy of Zechariah and showed Jesus came in peace.

beginner.question: On Palm Sunday, the crowd waved olive branches and shouted 'Hallelujah!' as Jesus entered Jerusalem.
beginner.explanation: The crowd waved palm branches (not olive branches) and shouted 'Hosanna!' (not Hallelujah). 'Hosanna' means 'Save us!' and was a shout of praise. This is why the event is called Palm Sunday.

beginner.question: Jesus overturned the tables of money changers in the Temple and drove out the merchants.
beginner.explanation: Jesus entered the Temple and drove out those buying and selling. He overturned the tables of the money changers and said, 'My house will be called a house of prayer, but you are making it a den of robbers.'

beginner.question: At the Transfiguration, Jesus' face shone like the sun and his clothes became as white as light.
beginner.explanation: On a high mountain, Jesus was transfigured before Peter, James, and John. His face shone like the sun and his clothes became dazzling white. Moses and Elijah appeared and spoke with him.

beginner.question: At the Transfiguration, Abraham and David appeared and spoke with Jesus.
beginner.explanation: It was Moses and Elijah, not Abraham and David, who appeared at the Transfiguration. Moses represented the Law and Elijah represented the Prophets, showing that Jesus fulfilled both.

beginner.question: The Last Supper was a breakfast meal Jesus shared with his disciples on Easter morning.
beginner.explanation: The Last Supper was an evening Passover meal, not a breakfast. It took place the night before Jesus' crucifixion, not on Easter morning. During this meal, Jesus broke bread, shared wine, and gave his disciples final teachings.

beginner.question: At the Last Supper, Jesus said the bread represented his body, which would be broken for them.
beginner.explanation: Jesus took bread, gave thanks, broke it, and said, 'Take and eat; this is my body.' This act is remembered by Christians in Communion (also called the Lord's Supper or Eucharist).

beginner.question: At the Last Supper, the wine represented Jesus' tears of sorrow.
beginner.explanation: The wine represented Jesus' blood, not his tears. Jesus said, 'This is my blood of the covenant, which is poured out for many for the forgiveness of sins.' It symbolised his sacrifice on the cross.

beginner.question: Jesus washed his disciples' hands at the Last Supper as a gesture of blessing.
beginner.explanation: Jesus washed his disciples' feet, not their hands. Foot-washing was a job normally done by the lowest servant. Jesus performed this humble act to teach his disciples that true leadership means serving others.

beginner.question: Judas Iscariot betrayed Jesus for 30 pieces of silver.
beginner.explanation: Judas went to the chief priests and agreed to betray Jesus in exchange for 30 pieces of silver, the price of a slave. He then looked for an opportunity to hand Jesus over when no crowd was present.

beginner.question: Judas identified Jesus to the soldiers by shaking his hand.
beginner.explanation: Judas identified Jesus with a kiss, not a handshake. He told the soldiers, 'The one I kiss is the man; arrest him.' Judas greeted Jesus with a kiss, and the soldiers seized him immediately.

beginner.question: Jesus was arrested at the Last Supper table while still eating with his disciples.
beginner.explanation: Jesus was not arrested at the supper table. After the Last Supper, he went to the Garden of Gethsemane on the Mount of Olives to pray. It was there that Judas arrived with soldiers to arrest him.

beginner.question: Peter denied knowing Jesus three times before a rooster crowed, just as Jesus predicted.
beginner.explanation: After Jesus' arrest, Peter was recognised by people near the high priest's courtyard. Three times he denied knowing Jesus. When the rooster crowed, Peter remembered Jesus' prediction and wept bitterly.

beginner.question: Thomas was the disciple who denied knowing Jesus three times before the rooster crowed.
beginner.explanation: It was Peter, not Thomas, who denied knowing Jesus three times. Thomas is known as 'Doubting Thomas' because he initially refused to believe in Jesus' resurrection until he could see and touch Jesus' wounds.

beginner.question: Pontius Pilate was the Roman governor who sentenced Jesus to be crucified.
beginner.explanation: Pontius Pilate was the Roman governor of Judea. Although he found no fault in Jesus, he gave in to the pressure of the crowd and handed Jesus over to be crucified.

beginner.question: Pontius Pilate washed his hands to symbolically show he was not taking responsibility for Jesus' death.
beginner.explanation: Pilate washed his hands in front of the crowd and said, 'I am innocent of this man's blood. It is your responsibility.' This gesture became a well-known symbol for refusing to accept blame.

beginner.question: The crowd asked Pilate to release Jesus instead of the criminal Barabbas.
beginner.explanation: The crowd did the opposite — they asked Pilate to release Barabbas, a notorious prisoner, and to crucify Jesus. Pilate offered them the choice, and the chief priests stirred the crowd to choose Barabbas.

beginner.question: Jesus carried his own cross (at least part of the way) to the place of crucifixion.
beginner.explanation: Jesus carried his cross through the streets of Jerusalem toward Golgotha. He was weakened from being beaten, and Simon of Cyrene was forced by the soldiers to help carry the cross for part of the journey.

beginner.question: Roman soldiers placed a crown of thorns on Jesus' head to mock him.
beginner.explanation: The soldiers twisted together a crown of thorns and pressed it onto Jesus' head. They also put a purple robe on him and mockingly called him 'King of the Jews,' striking him and spitting on him.

beginner.question: Jesus was crucified at a place called Golgotha, which means 'Place of the Skull.'
beginner.explanation: Golgotha is an Aramaic word meaning 'Place of the Skull.' The site is also known by its Latin name, Calvary. It was located outside the city walls of Jerusalem.

beginner.question: Jesus was crucified alone, with no one else being executed that day.
beginner.explanation: Jesus was not crucified alone. Two criminals were crucified alongside him — one on his right and one on his left. One criminal mocked Jesus, while the other repented and was promised paradise.

beginner.question: A sign above Jesus on the cross read 'King of the Jews.'
beginner.explanation: Pilate had a sign written and fastened to the cross. It read 'Jesus of Nazareth, the King of the Jews' in Hebrew, Latin, and Greek. The chief priests protested, but Pilate refused to change it.

beginner.question: While on the cross, Jesus cursed those who crucified him and called for God's punishment.
beginner.explanation: Jesus did the opposite — he prayed for forgiveness for those who crucified him, saying, 'Father, forgive them, for they do not know what they are doing.' His mercy even in suffering is one of the most powerful moments in the Gospels.

beginner.question: Darkness covered the land for three hours while Jesus hung on the cross.
beginner.explanation: From noon until three in the afternoon, darkness covered the whole land. This supernatural darkness lasted three hours and is recorded in all three Synoptic Gospels (Matthew, Mark, and Luke).

beginner.question: When Jesus died, the curtain of the Temple was torn in two from top to bottom.
beginner.explanation: At the moment Jesus died, the thick curtain separating the Holy of Holies in the Temple was torn in two from top to bottom. This symbolised that the barrier between God and people had been removed through Jesus' sacrifice.

beginner.question: A Roman centurion at the crucifixion said, 'Truly this was the Son of God.'
beginner.explanation: The Roman centurion who witnessed Jesus' death and the events surrounding it exclaimed, 'Truly this was the Son of God!' Even a pagan soldier recognised something divine about Jesus.

beginner.question: Jesus' body was placed in a tomb belonging to Joseph of Arimathea.
beginner.explanation: Joseph of Arimathea, a wealthy and respected member of the Jewish council who was secretly a follower of Jesus, asked Pilate for the body. He wrapped it in linen cloth and placed it in his own new rock-cut tomb.

beginner.question: A large stone was rolled in front of Jesus' tomb to seal it.
beginner.explanation: Joseph of Arimathea rolled a large stone against the entrance of the tomb. The chief priests and Pharisees also asked Pilate to post guards and officially seal the stone to prevent anyone from taking the body.

beginner.question: Jesus rose from the dead on the fifth day after his crucifixion.
beginner.explanation: Jesus rose on the third day, not the fifth. He was crucified on Friday and rose on Sunday morning, just as he had repeatedly predicted. The resurrection on the third day is central to the Christian faith.

beginner.question: Mary Magdalene was the first person to find the empty tomb on Easter morning.
beginner.explanation: Mary Magdalene went to the tomb early on Sunday morning while it was still dark. She found the stone rolled away and the tomb empty. She was also the first person to see and speak with the risen Jesus.

beginner.question: An angel at the empty tomb told the women 'He is not here; he has risen.'
beginner.explanation: An angel in dazzling white clothes was sitting at the tomb. He told the women, 'Do not be afraid, for I know that you are looking for Jesus, who was crucified. He is not here; he has risen, just as he said.'

beginner.question: Thomas believed immediately when the other disciples told him Jesus had risen from the dead.
beginner.explanation: Thomas did not believe immediately. He said, 'Unless I see the nail marks in his hands and put my finger where the nails were, I will not believe.' A week later, Jesus appeared and Thomas believed, exclaiming 'My Lord and my God!'

beginner.question: After his resurrection, Jesus appeared to his disciples for only 3 days before ascending to heaven.
beginner.explanation: Jesus appeared to his disciples over a period of 40 days, not 3 days. During this time he gave many convincing proofs that he was alive and taught them about the kingdom of God before ascending to heaven.

beginner.question: The Ascension is the event when Jesus physically rose into heaven in front of his disciples.
beginner.explanation: Forty days after his resurrection, Jesus was taken up into heaven while his disciples watched. A cloud hid him from their sight. Two angels appeared and promised that Jesus would one day return the same way.

beginner.question: Jesus said 'I am the bread of life,' meaning he satisfies spiritual hunger.
beginner.explanation: Jesus declared, 'I am the bread of life. Whoever comes to me will never go hungry, and whoever believes in me will never be thirsty.' He was teaching that he fulfils the deepest spiritual needs of humanity.

beginner.question: Jesus said 'I am the vine; you are the roots,' meaning his followers should stay grounded.
beginner.explanation: Jesus said 'I am the vine; you are the branches,' not the roots. Just as branches must remain attached to a vine to bear fruit, his followers must remain connected to him to live spiritually fruitful lives.

beginner.question: Jesus healed a man born blind by placing mud on his eyes and telling him to wash in the Pool of Siloam.
beginner.explanation: Jesus made mud with his saliva, put it on the blind man's eyes, and told him to go wash in the Pool of Siloam. The man washed and came back seeing. It was the first time he had ever seen in his life.

beginner.question: Jesus raised a widow's son from the dead in the town of Nain.
beginner.explanation: When Jesus entered the town of Nain, he met a funeral procession for the only son of a widow. Moved with compassion, Jesus touched the coffin and said, 'Young man, I say to you, get up!' The dead man sat up and began to talk.

beginner.question: Four friends lowered a paralysed man through a hole in the roof so Jesus could heal him.
beginner.explanation: Because the house was too crowded, the man's friends went up on the roof, made an opening, and lowered their paralysed friend down on his mat directly in front of Jesus. Seeing their faith, Jesus healed the man.

beginner.question: Jesus told Nicodemus 'You must be born again' when Nicodemus visited him at night.
beginner.explanation: Nicodemus, a Pharisee and member of the Jewish council, came to Jesus at night. Jesus told him, 'No one can see the kingdom of God unless they are born again.' He was speaking of spiritual rebirth, not physical.

beginner.question: John 3:16 says 'For God so loved the righteous that he gave his one and only Son.'
beginner.explanation: John 3:16 says 'For God so loved the world' — not just the righteous. This is a key point: God's love extends to all people, not only those who are already good. It is one of the most quoted verses in the Bible.

beginner.question: Jesus spoke to a Samaritan woman at a well and offered her 'living water.'
beginner.explanation: Jesus broke social barriers by speaking with a Samaritan woman at Jacob's Well. He offered her 'living water' — a metaphor for eternal life and the Holy Spirit. She became one of the first to spread the news about Jesus.

beginner.question: Peter cut off the ear of the high priest's servant when soldiers came to arrest Jesus.
beginner.explanation: When the soldiers arrived to arrest Jesus in the garden, Peter drew his sword and struck the high priest's servant, cutting off his right ear. Jesus told Peter to put his sword away and healed the servant's ear.

beginner.question: The woman who touched Jesus' garment and was healed had been sick for 20 years.
beginner.explanation: The woman had been suffering from bleeding for 12 years, not 20. She had spent all her money on doctors but only got worse. When she touched the edge of Jesus' cloak, she was immediately healed.

beginner.question: Jesus gave Simon the name Peter, which means 'pillar.'
beginner.explanation: Peter means 'rock,' not 'pillar.' Jesus said, 'You are Peter (Petros), and on this rock I will build my church.' The name comes from the Greek word for rock, signifying Peter's foundational role.

beginner.question: Jesus said it is easier for a camel to go through the eye of a needle than for a rich person to enter the kingdom of God.
beginner.explanation: After a rich young man chose his wealth over following Jesus, Jesus used this vivid image. He wasn't saying it's impossible for the wealthy to be saved, but that trusting in riches instead of God makes it extremely difficult.

beginner.question: At Mary and Martha's home, Jesus said Mary chose the better thing by sitting and listening to his teaching.
beginner.explanation: While Martha was busy preparing food, her sister Mary sat at Jesus' feet listening. When Martha complained, Jesus said, 'Mary has chosen what is better, and it will not be taken away from her.'

beginner.question: Jesus' last words on the cross were 'It is finished.'
beginner.explanation: According to John's Gospel, Jesus' final words were 'It is finished' before he bowed his head and gave up his spirit. The Greek word 'tetelestai' means 'completed' or 'paid in full,' signifying his mission was accomplished.

beginner.question: Simon of Cyrene helped Jesus carry his cross on the way to the crucifixion.
beginner.explanation: The Roman soldiers forced Simon of Cyrene, a man coming in from the countryside, to carry Jesus' cross. Jesus was weakened from the beatings and scourging he had endured before the crucifixion.

beginner.question: Jesus refused to speak to the criminals who were crucified beside him.
beginner.explanation: Jesus did speak to one of the criminals. When the repentant criminal asked to be remembered, Jesus said, 'Truly I tell you, today you will be with me in paradise.' Jesus showed mercy even in his final moments.

beginner.question: After his resurrection, Jesus appeared to two disciples on the road to Emmaus.
beginner.explanation: Two disciples were walking to the village of Emmaus when the risen Jesus joined them, though they didn't recognise him. He explained the Scriptures to them, and they finally recognised him when he broke bread.

beginner.question: Jesus commanded his followers to 'Go and make disciples of all nations.' This is known as the Great Commission.
beginner.explanation: Before ascending to heaven, Jesus told his disciples, 'Go and make disciples of all nations, baptising them in the name of the Father and of the Son and of the Holy Spirit.' This is the Great Commission.

beginner.question: Jesus said 'I am the resurrection and the life' to Mary before raising her brother Lazarus.
beginner.explanation: Jesus said 'I am the resurrection and the life' to Martha, not Mary. Martha had said to Jesus, 'If you had been here, my brother would not have died.' Jesus reassured her with this powerful declaration before raising Lazarus.

beginner.question: Jesus was about 30 years old when he began his public ministry.
beginner.explanation: Luke's Gospel records that Jesus was about 30 years old when he began his ministry. This was after his baptism by John the Baptist and his 40-day temptation in the wilderness.

beginner.question: Jesus only taught using long, complex theological lectures that were hard for ordinary people to understand.
beginner.explanation: Jesus primarily used parables — simple, relatable stories from everyday life — to teach spiritual truths. Farmers, shepherds, fishermen, and ordinary people could all understand his stories about seeds, sheep, coins, and feasts.

beginner.question: Jesus was born during the time of the Roman Empire, when Caesar Augustus was emperor.
beginner.explanation: Luke's Gospel places Jesus' birth during the reign of Caesar Augustus, who ordered the census that brought Mary and Joseph to Bethlehem. The Roman Empire controlled the region of Palestine at the time.

beginner.question: Jesus had four brothers named James, Joseph, Simon, and Judas.
beginner.explanation: The Gospels mention Jesus' brothers by name: James, Joseph (Joses), Simon, and Judas. Mark 6:3 records people in Nazareth saying, 'Isn't this the carpenter? Isn't this Mary's son and the brother of James, Joseph, Judas, and Simon?'

beginner.question: John the Baptist was related to Jesus — their mothers, Mary and Elizabeth, were relatives.
beginner.explanation: Mary and Elizabeth were relatives (often translated as 'cousins'). When the angel told Mary she would have a baby, he also told her that Elizabeth was expecting a son (John). Mary visited Elizabeth, and John leaped in the womb.

beginner.question: Jesus was tempted by the devil to jump off the Temple, but he refused.
beginner.explanation: During the temptation in the wilderness, the devil took Jesus to the highest point of the Temple and told him to throw himself down, quoting Scripture about angels protecting him. Jesus refused, saying, 'Do not put the Lord your God to the test.'

beginner.question: James and John were the sons of a fisherman named Zebedee.
beginner.explanation: James and John, two of Jesus' closest disciples, were the sons of Zebedee. They were in a boat with their father mending fishing nets when Jesus called them to follow him.

beginner.question: After his resurrection, Jesus never ate or drank with his disciples again.
beginner.explanation: Jesus did eat with his disciples after the resurrection. He cooked fish and bread for breakfast by the Sea of Galilee and also ate broiled fish in Jerusalem to prove he was physically real and not a ghost.

beginner.question: After his resurrection, Jesus asked Peter 'Do you love me?' three times.
beginner.explanation: By the Sea of Galilee, Jesus asked Peter three times, 'Do you love me?' This mirrored Peter's three denials. Each time Peter affirmed his love, Jesus told him to 'Feed my sheep,' restoring him to ministry.

beginner.question: Mary, the mother of Jesus, first told Jesus about the wine shortage at the wedding in Cana.
beginner.explanation: Mary noticed the wine had run out and told Jesus, 'They have no more wine.' She then told the servants, 'Do whatever he tells you.' Jesus proceeded to turn water into wine as his first miracle.

beginner.question: The Parable of the Sower teaches about different ways people respond to God's word.
beginner.explanation: In the Parable of the Sower, seeds fall on four types of soil: the path (hard hearts), rocky ground (shallow faith), thorns (worldly worries), and good soil (receptive hearts). Each represents a different response to God's message.

beginner.question: The Pharisees were a group who generally supported Jesus and helped him teach.
beginner.explanation: The Pharisees were religious leaders who frequently opposed Jesus. They criticised him for healing on the Sabbath, eating with sinners, and claiming to be the Son of God. Jesus challenged their hypocrisy.

beginner.question: Jesus performed miracles on the Sabbath (the Jewish day of rest), which angered the religious leaders.
beginner.explanation: Jesus healed people on the Sabbath multiple times, which the Pharisees considered a violation of the law. Jesus argued that doing good and saving life on the Sabbath was always right.

beginner.question: Jesus said 'Blessed are the troublemakers, for they will be called children of God.'
beginner.explanation: Jesus said 'Blessed are the peacemakers,' not troublemakers. In the Beatitudes, Jesus taught that those who work for peace reflect God's character and will be honoured as his children.

beginner.question: Jesus never claimed to be the Son of God during his lifetime.
beginner.explanation: Jesus made numerous claims to be the Son of God. At his trial, the high priest directly asked him, and Jesus affirmed it. He also said, 'I and the Father are one,' and accepted worship as God's Son.

beginner.question: The Parable of the Ten Virgins teaches about being prepared for Jesus' return.
beginner.explanation: Five wise virgins brought extra oil for their lamps, while five foolish ones did not. When the bridegroom arrived at midnight, only the prepared ones could enter the wedding feast. Jesus taught readiness for his return.

beginner.question: Jesus cast demons out of a man and sent them into a herd of pigs, which then ran into a lake.
beginner.explanation: Jesus encountered a man possessed by many demons near the Sea of Galilee. He cast the demons out, and they entered a herd of about 2,000 pigs, which rushed down a steep bank into the lake and drowned.

beginner.question: Jairus was a synagogue leader whose daughter Jesus raised from the dead.
beginner.explanation: Jairus begged Jesus to come heal his dying daughter. By the time Jesus arrived, the girl had died. Jesus told the mourners she was only sleeping, took her by the hand, and said, 'Little girl, get up!' She immediately rose.

beginner.question: Jesus taught the Parable of the Pharisee and the Tax Collector to show that God values humility over self-righteousness.
beginner.explanation: The Pharisee prayed proudly about his own goodness, while the tax collector humbly begged for mercy. Jesus said the tax collector went home justified before God, teaching that God lifts up the humble.

beginner.question: A rich young ruler chose to follow Jesus and give away all his wealth.
beginner.explanation: The rich young ruler went away sad when Jesus told him to sell everything and follow him. He had great wealth and was unwilling to part with it. Jesus used this to teach about the danger of trusting in riches.

beginner.question: Jesus healed a blind beggar named Bartimaeus near Jericho.
beginner.explanation: Bartimaeus was a blind beggar sitting by the road near Jericho. Despite the crowd telling him to be quiet, he kept shouting to Jesus for mercy. Jesus stopped, called him over, and healed him because of his faith.

beginner.question: Jesus said 'Do not judge, or you too will be judged.'
beginner.explanation: In the Sermon on the Mount, Jesus warned against judging others, because the same standard of judgment would be applied back to us. He illustrated this with the speck and plank metaphor about noticing others' faults while ignoring our own.

beginner.question: After Jesus ascended to heaven, his disciples immediately went back to their old jobs as fishermen.
beginner.explanation: After Jesus ascended, his disciples returned to Jerusalem as Jesus had instructed. They waited in prayer for the gift of the Holy Spirit, which came at Pentecost. They then began preaching the gospel.

beginner.question: Jesus predicted his own death and resurrection multiple times before they happened.
beginner.explanation: On several occasions, Jesus told his disciples that he would be betrayed, suffer, be killed, and rise again on the third day. The disciples often did not understand what he meant until after the resurrection.

beginner.question: Jesus said 'Where two or three gather in my name, there am I with them.'
beginner.explanation: Jesus promised his presence wherever believers gather in his name, even in small groups. This assurance encourages Christians that Jesus is spiritually present in their worship and fellowship.

beginner.question: Jesus said 'You are the darkness of the world' to warn his followers about sin.
beginner.explanation: Jesus said 'You are the light of the world,' not the darkness. He told his followers to let their light shine before others through good deeds, so that people would praise God.

beginner.question: Jesus said 'The truth will set you free.'
beginner.explanation: Jesus said, 'If you hold to my teaching, you are really my disciples. Then you will know the truth, and the truth will set you free.' He was speaking about spiritual freedom from sin through knowing him.

beginner.question: In his parable about a lost coin, the woman who finds it throws a party to celebrate.
beginner.explanation: Jesus told of a woman who had ten silver coins and lost one. She lit a lamp, swept the house, and searched carefully until she found it. Then she called her friends and neighbours to rejoice with her.

beginner.question: Jesus was known to eat with tax collectors and sinners, which upset the Pharisees.
beginner.explanation: Jesus frequently shared meals with tax collectors and sinners, which scandalised the religious leaders. Jesus said, 'It is not the healthy who need a doctor, but the sick. I have not come to call the righteous, but sinners.'

beginner.question: Jesus turned five loaves and two fish into enough food to feed 500 people.
beginner.explanation: Jesus fed 5,000 men (plus women and children), not 500. He used a boy's lunch of five barley loaves and two small fish to miraculously feed the entire crowd, with 12 baskets of leftovers.

beginner.question: Jesus taught 'Demand and it will be given to you; command and you will receive.'
beginner.explanation: Jesus actually said 'Ask and it will be given to you; seek and you will find; knock and the door will be opened to you.' The words are about humble, persistent prayer — not demanding or commanding.

beginner.question: Mary Magdalene initially mistook the risen Jesus for a gardener.
beginner.explanation: On Easter morning, Mary Magdalene was weeping at the empty tomb when Jesus appeared to her. She did not recognise him at first and thought he was the gardener. When Jesus said her name, 'Mary,' she recognised him.

beginner.question: The risen Jesus appeared to more than 500 people at one time.
beginner.explanation: Paul writes that after his resurrection, Jesus appeared to more than 500 brothers and sisters at the same time. Most of these witnesses were still alive when Paul wrote, making their testimony verifiable.

beginner.question: When the risen Jesus appeared to his disciples in a locked room, his first words were 'Peace be with you.'
beginner.explanation: The disciples were hiding behind locked doors for fear of the Jewish leaders when Jesus suddenly appeared among them. His first words were 'Peace be with you,' bringing comfort and reassurance.

beginner.question: Jesus said 'I am the door' (or 'the gate'), meaning he is the way to salvation.
beginner.explanation: Jesus said, 'I am the gate; whoever enters through me will be saved.' He used the image of a gate to a sheep pen, teaching that he is the only true entrance to safety and eternal life.

beginner.question: In the Garden of Gethsemane, Jesus prayed 'Not my will, but yours be done.'
beginner.explanation: In deep anguish before his arrest, Jesus prayed to God, 'Father, if you are willing, take this cup from me; yet not my will, but yours be done.' He submitted himself completely to God's plan despite the suffering ahead.

beginner.question: In the Garden of Gethsemane, the disciples stayed awake all night praying with Jesus.
beginner.explanation: The disciples fell asleep three times while Jesus prayed in anguish. Jesus returned each time and found them sleeping. He said, 'The spirit is willing, but the flesh is weak.'

beginner.question: Jesus cursed a fig tree that had no fruit, and it withered and died.
beginner.explanation: On his way to Jerusalem, Jesus was hungry and approached a fig tree. Finding no fruit on it, he said, 'May you never bear fruit again!' The fig tree withered immediately, and Jesus used it to teach about faith.

beginner.question: Jesus was born in a palace because his family was part of the royal family.
beginner.explanation: Jesus was not born in a palace. He was born in humble circumstances — laid in a manger (animal feeding trough) because there was no room for his family at the inn in Bethlehem.

beginner.question: Jesus ate fish after his resurrection to prove to his disciples that he was real and not a ghost.
beginner.explanation: When Jesus appeared to his disciples after the resurrection, they were startled and thought they were seeing a ghost. To prove he was physically real, Jesus asked for something to eat and ate a piece of broiled fish.

beginner.question: Jesus healed Peter's mother-in-law of a fever.
beginner.explanation: When Jesus visited Peter's home, he found Peter's mother-in-law lying in bed with a fever. Jesus touched her hand, the fever left her, and she got up and began to serve them. It was one of his earliest healings.

beginner.question: Jesus told a parable about talents (money) to teach about using the gifts God gives us.
beginner.explanation: A master gave talents (large sums of money) to three servants. Two invested and doubled theirs; the third buried his. The master rewarded the faithful servants and punished the lazy one, teaching that God expects us to use our gifts.

beginner.question: Jesus' ministry lasted approximately 30 years.
beginner.explanation: Jesus' public ministry lasted approximately 3 years, not 30. He began around age 30 after his baptism and continued until his crucifixion at around age 33. During these years, he taught, healed, and trained his disciples.

beginner.question: Jesus told his disciples to be 'as wise as serpents and as innocent as doves.'
beginner.explanation: When sending his disciples out to spread his message, Jesus warned them they would face opposition. He told them to be shrewd (wise as serpents) in navigating danger while remaining morally pure (innocent as doves).

beginner.question: At the Last Supper, Jesus predicted that Peter would deny knowing him three times.
beginner.explanation: During the Last Supper, Jesus told Peter, 'Before the rooster crows, you will deny me three times.' Peter insisted he would never deny Jesus, but later that night, he did exactly as Jesus predicted.

beginner.question: Jesus conducted most of his public ministry in the city of Jerusalem.
beginner.explanation: Jesus conducted most of his ministry in the region of Galilee, not Jerusalem. He taught in towns like Capernaum and Nazareth and around the Sea of Galilee. He visited Jerusalem for festivals and spent his final week there.

beginner.question: Jesus taught that praying should be done publicly so everyone can see how holy you are.
beginner.explanation: Jesus taught the opposite. He said, 'When you pray, go into your room, close the door and pray to your Father, who is unseen.' He criticised people who prayed loudly in public just to be seen by others.

beginner.question: The feeding of the 5,000 is the only miracle (besides the resurrection) recorded in all four Gospels.
beginner.explanation: The miracle of feeding the 5,000 is recorded in Matthew, Mark, Luke, and John — making it the only miracle other than the resurrection that appears in all four Gospel accounts, showing its importance.

beginner.question: Jesus described himself as 'the resurrection and the life.'
beginner.explanation: Jesus declared, 'I am the resurrection and the life. The one who believes in me will live, even though they die.' He spoke these words to Martha just before raising her brother Lazarus from the dead.

beginner.question: Judas Iscariot was the disciple chosen to replace Jesus as leader after the Ascension.
beginner.explanation: Judas was not chosen to replace Jesus. Judas was the disciple who betrayed Jesus and later took his own life. After the Ascension, the remaining disciples chose Matthias to replace Judas and restore the number of apostles to 12.

beginner.question: Jesus was crucified, died, and was buried, and rose again on the third day — these are the central events of the Christian faith.
beginner.explanation: The death, burial, and resurrection of Jesus are the core of Christianity. Paul wrote that this is 'of first importance' — that Christ died for our sins, was buried, and was raised on the third day according to the Scriptures.

beginner.question: In the very first chapter of the Bible, God creates the entire world. According to the Book of Genesis, how many days did it take God to create everything?
beginner.explanation: According to Genesis chapter 1, God created the heavens, earth, animals, and humans in six days. On the seventh day, God rested from all His work.

beginner.question: In the creation story, God made the first man from the dust of the ground and breathed life into him. What was the name of this first man?
beginner.explanation: God formed Adam from the dust of the ground and breathed into his nostrils the breath of life. Adam became the first human being and lived in the Garden of Eden.

beginner.question: After creating the first man Adam, God made the first woman from one of Adam's ribs. What was her name?
beginner.explanation: God caused Adam to fall into a deep sleep, took one of his ribs, and formed Eve. She was the first woman and Adam's companion in the Garden of Eden.

beginner.question: On the very first day of creation, God spoke and something appeared out of nothing. What was the first thing God created?
beginner.explanation: God said 'Let there be light,' and there was light. This was the very first act of creation recorded in the Bible, happening on the first day.

beginner.question: After finishing creation in six days, what did God do on the seventh day?
beginner.explanation: After six days of creation, God rested on the seventh day and blessed it. This is why the seventh day became known as a holy day of rest.

beginner.question: In the Book of Genesis, God decided to flood the entire earth because of human wickedness. He chose one righteous man to build a large boat and save his family and the animals. Who was this man?
beginner.explanation: Noah was the one righteous man God found in his generation. God instructed Noah to build an ark to survive the great flood that would cover the earth.

beginner.question: God told Noah to build a massive boat to survive the flood. What is the famous name of this boat?
beginner.explanation: The boat is called Noah's Ark. God gave Noah specific instructions on how to build it, including its dimensions and materials. It was large enough to hold Noah's family and pairs of every kind of animal.

beginner.question: Noah brought animals onto the ark before the great flood. How many of each kind of animal did God tell Noah to bring?
beginner.explanation: God told Noah to bring two of every kind of animal — one male and one female — so that they could survive the flood and repopulate the earth afterwards.

beginner.question: After the flood waters receded, Noah sent out a bird that came back with an olive branch, showing that dry land had appeared. What kind of bird was it?
beginner.explanation: Noah sent out a dove, and it returned with a freshly plucked olive leaf in its beak. This showed Noah that the floodwaters were going down and plants were growing again.

beginner.question: After the great flood, God placed a sign in the sky as a promise that He would never flood the entire earth again. What was this sign?
beginner.explanation: God set a rainbow in the sky as a sign of His covenant with Noah and all living creatures. It was a promise that He would never again destroy the earth with a flood.

beginner.question: In the Book of Exodus, a man was looking after sheep in the desert when he saw a bush that was on fire but was not burning up. God spoke to him from the bush. Who was this man?
beginner.explanation: Moses was tending sheep near Mount Horeb when he saw a bush that was burning but not consumed by the flames. God spoke to him from the bush and called him to lead the Israelites out of Egypt.

beginner.question: When Moses saw a bush on fire that was not burning up, God spoke to him and gave him a mission. What did God tell Moses to do?
options: Lead the Israelites out of Egypt
beginner.explanation: God told Moses to go to Pharaoh and demand the release of the Israelites from slavery in Egypt. God promised to be with Moses and help him free His people.

beginner.question: God spoke to Moses from a miraculous burning bush on a mountain. What was this mountain called?
beginner.explanation: The burning bush appeared to Moses at Mount Horeb, also known as the mountain of God. Later, Moses would return to this same mountain area to receive the Ten Commandments.

beginner.question: When God wanted to free the Israelites from slavery in Egypt, the Pharaoh refused to let them go. God sent a series of terrible disasters upon Egypt. How many plagues did God send in total?
beginner.explanation: God sent ten plagues upon Egypt to convince Pharaoh to release the Israelites. The plagues included turning water to blood, frogs, gnats, flies, livestock disease, boils, hail, locusts, darkness, and the death of the firstborn.

beginner.question: The very first plague God sent upon Egypt turned the water of a major river into blood. Which river was it?
beginner.explanation: God turned the water of the Nile River into blood as the first plague. The fish died, the river smelled terrible, and the Egyptians could not drink the water.

beginner.question: One of the ten plagues of Egypt covered the entire land with small jumping creatures. Which creatures invaded Egypt in this plague?
beginner.explanation: The second plague brought frogs everywhere — in houses, bedrooms, ovens, and kneading troughs. The frogs covered the entire land of Egypt until Pharaoh begged Moses to pray for their removal.

beginner.question: One of the plagues of Egypt brought a swarm of insects that ate all the crops and vegetation. What were these insects?
beginner.explanation: The eighth plague brought a massive swarm of locusts that covered the ground and devoured every plant and every fruit on the trees. Nothing green remained in all of Egypt.

beginner.question: One of the plagues brought complete darkness over the land of Egypt. How long did this miraculous darkness last?
beginner.explanation: The ninth plague brought thick darkness over all of Egypt for three days. No one could see anyone else or leave their home, but the Israelites had light where they lived.

beginner.question: The tenth and final plague of Egypt was the most devastating. What happened in this plague?
options: A great fire consumed the land
beginner.explanation: In the tenth plague, every firstborn son in Egypt died, from Pharaoh's son to the firstborn of prisoners and livestock. This final plague finally convinced Pharaoh to let the Israelites go.

beginner.question: To protect their firstborn sons from the tenth plague, the Israelites were told to put something on their doorposts. What did they put on their doors?
beginner.explanation: God told the Israelites to sacrifice a lamb and put its blood on their doorposts. When the angel of death saw the blood, it 'passed over' that house. This event is why the holiday is called Passover.

beginner.question: After leaving Egypt, the Israelites were trapped between the Egyptian army and a large body of water. God parted this sea so they could cross on dry ground. Which sea was it?
beginner.explanation: God parted the Red Sea so Moses and the Israelites could walk through on dry ground. When the Egyptian army tried to follow, the waters closed back and the entire army was destroyed.

beginner.question: When God parted the Red Sea, Moses held something over the water as God commanded. What did Moses stretch out over the sea?
beginner.explanation: God told Moses to stretch out his hand over the sea. When he did, the Lord drove the sea back with a strong east wind and turned it into dry land, with walls of water on both sides.

beginner.question: After the Israelites crossed the Red Sea and were wandering in the desert, they ran out of food. God miraculously provided a special food that appeared on the ground each morning. What was it called?
beginner.explanation: God sent manna from heaven to feed the Israelites in the desert. It appeared on the ground each morning like frost and tasted like wafers made with honey. The Israelites ate it for forty years.

beginner.question: In the desert, God provided manna for the Israelites to eat. How often did they need to collect it?
beginner.explanation: The Israelites collected manna every morning. God instructed them to gather only enough for each day. On the sixth day, they gathered double because none would appear on the Sabbath.

beginner.question: While wandering in the desert, the Israelites were desperate for water. God told Moses to strike a rock, and something miraculous happened. What came out of the rock?
beginner.explanation: When the thirsty Israelites complained, God told Moses to strike a rock with his staff. Water gushed out of the rock, providing enough for all the people and their livestock to drink.

beginner.question: When Moses struck the rock to get water in the desert, what did he use to strike it?
beginner.explanation: God told Moses to take his staff — the same one he had used before Pharaoh — and strike the rock. When Moses struck it with his staff, water poured out for all the people.

beginner.question: When the Israelites arrived at the city of Jericho, its walls were too strong to break through. God told them to march around the city for a number of days. How many days did they march?
beginner.explanation: God told the Israelites to march around Jericho once a day for six days, and seven times on the seventh day. After they blew their trumpets and shouted on the seventh day, the walls came crashing down.

beginner.question: After the Israelites marched around Jericho and blew their trumpets, something miraculous happened to the city's walls. What happened?
beginner.explanation: When the people shouted and the trumpets sounded on the seventh day, the walls of Jericho collapsed completely. The Israelites charged straight in and captured the city.

beginner.question: Who was the leader of the Israelites when the walls of Jericho fell down?
beginner.explanation: Joshua led the Israelites after Moses died. He followed God's unusual battle plan to march around Jericho, and God brought the walls down miraculously.

beginner.question: On the final day of marching around Jericho, how many times did the Israelites march around the city?
beginner.explanation: On the seventh day, the Israelites marched around Jericho seven times instead of once. After the seventh lap, the priests blew their trumpets and the people gave a great shout, and the walls fell flat.

beginner.question: In the Book of Judges, there was an incredibly strong man whose power came from God. He once killed a lion with his bare hands. Who was this strong man?
beginner.explanation: Samson was given supernatural strength by God. The Spirit of the Lord came upon him powerfully, and he tore apart a young lion with his bare hands as if it were a small animal.

beginner.question: Samson was the strongest man in the Bible. His miraculous strength was connected to something he was never supposed to cut. What was it?
beginner.explanation: Samson's strength was tied to a vow to God called the Nazirite vow, which included never cutting his hair. When his hair was cut by Delilah's scheme, his strength left him.

beginner.question: Samson lost his supernatural strength after a woman tricked him into revealing his secret. What was the name of this woman?
beginner.explanation: Delilah was bribed by the Philistines to discover the secret of Samson's strength. After persistent nagging, Samson told her his hair had never been cut. She had it shaved while he slept, and his strength left him.

beginner.question: In one famous story, Samson used an unusual weapon to defeat a thousand Philistine soldiers. What was this strange weapon?
beginner.explanation: The Spirit of the Lord came upon Samson, and he picked up a fresh jawbone of a donkey and struck down a thousand Philistines with it. This showed that God's power worked through him.

beginner.question: The prophet Elijah challenged 450 prophets of a false god named Baal to a contest on Mount Carmel. The challenge was to see whose god would send fire from heaven. Whose offering was consumed by fire?
beginner.explanation: Elijah prayed to God, and fire fell from heaven and consumed his offering, the wood, the stones, the soil, and even the water around it. The prophets of Baal had prayed all day with no response.

beginner.question: Before calling fire from heaven, Elijah did something surprising to make the miracle even more impressive. What did he pour over his offering?
beginner.explanation: Elijah had water poured over his offering three times, filling the trench around the altar. This made the miracle even more dramatic when God's fire consumed everything, including the water.

beginner.question: The prophet Elijah did not die a normal death. Instead, God took him to heaven in a dramatic way. How did Elijah leave the earth?
options: A chariot of fire carried him up in a whirlwind
He was turned into an angel
He disappeared in a flash of light
beginner.explanation: As Elijah and his student Elisha were walking and talking, a chariot of fire with horses of fire appeared, and Elijah was taken up to heaven in a whirlwind. He never experienced death.

beginner.question: When Elijah was taken up to heaven, who was the prophet watching him go — the man who would take over as prophet after him?
beginner.explanation: Elisha was Elijah's faithful student and successor. He watched Elijah go up to heaven in a whirlwind and picked up Elijah's cloak, which became a symbol of the prophetic power passed to him.

beginner.question: The prophet Elisha performed a miracle for a poor widow who was about to lose her sons to debt. God miraculously multiplied something in her house. What was it?
beginner.explanation: The widow had only a small jar of olive oil. Elisha told her to borrow many empty jars and pour her oil into them. The oil kept flowing until every jar was full, and she sold it to pay her debts.

beginner.question: The prophet Elisha told a foreign army commander named Naaman to wash in a river to cure his skin disease. Which river did Naaman wash in?
