beginner.explanation: The master of the banquet did not know where the wine had come from — only the servants who had drawn the water knew. He simply complimented the bridegroom for saving the best wine for last.

beginner.question: Jesus healed a Roman centurion's servant without even going to the centurion's house.
beginner.explanation: The centurion said he was not worthy to have Jesus come to his home and believed Jesus could heal with just a word. Jesus marvelled at his faith and healed the servant from a distance.

beginner.question: Jesus healed a woman who had been bent over and crippled for eight years.
beginner.explanation: The woman had been crippled for eighteen years — not eight. She was bent over and unable to straighten up at all. Jesus laid His hands on her and she immediately stood up straight and praised God.

beginner.question: Jesus healed a man with a withered hand on the Sabbath in a synagogue, which angered the religious leaders.
beginner.explanation: Jesus told the man to stretch out his withered hand, and it was completely restored. The Pharisees were furious because Jesus healed on the Sabbath, which they considered unlawful work.

beginner.question: Blind Bartimaeus was healed by Jesus near the city of Jericho.
beginner.explanation: Bartimaeus sat by the roadside near Jericho, begging. When he heard Jesus was passing, he shouted for mercy. Jesus stopped, called him over, and said, 'Your faith has healed you.' Bartimaeus immediately received his sight.

beginner.question: When Jesus told the paralysed man at the Pool of Bethesda to get up, the man first had to enter the pool before he was healed.
beginner.explanation: Jesus did not tell the man to enter the pool. He simply said, 'Get up! Pick up your mat and walk.' The man was healed instantly by Jesus' word alone, without needing to enter the water.

beginner.question: Jesus cursed a fig tree because it had no fruit, and the tree withered and died.
beginner.explanation: Jesus was hungry and approached a fig tree, but it had only leaves and no fruit. He cursed the tree, and by the next day it had completely withered from the roots. He used it to teach about faith.

beginner.question: At the first miraculous catch of fish in Luke chapter 5, Peter's nets were so full they began to break.
beginner.explanation: Peter had fished all night and caught nothing. When Jesus told him to cast his nets in deep water, so many fish were caught that the nets began to break and Peter had to call another boat to help.

beginner.question: The disciple Philip was miraculously transported from one location to another after baptising an Ethiopian official.
beginner.explanation: After Philip baptised the Ethiopian eunuch, the Spirit of the Lord suddenly took Philip away. He appeared at the town of Azotus and continued preaching the gospel throughout the region.

beginner.question: A young man named Eutychus died after falling out of a window during one of Paul's sermons, and Paul raised him back to life.
beginner.explanation: Eutychus fell asleep during Paul's lengthy sermon and fell from a third-floor window. He was picked up dead, but Paul went down, embraced him, and the young man was restored to life.

beginner.question: God created the sun, moon, and stars on the first day of creation.
beginner.explanation: God created the sun, moon, and stars on the fourth day — not the first. On the first day, God created light itself. The celestial bodies were made on the fourth day to govern day and night.

beginner.question: After Jesus was tempted in the desert for forty days, angels came and ministered to Him.
beginner.explanation: After Jesus resisted all of the devil's temptations in the wilderness, the devil departed from Him. Angels then came and attended to Jesus, providing comfort after His long ordeal of fasting and temptation.

beginner.question: When Peter was freed from prison by an angel, the iron gate of the prison opened by itself.
beginner.explanation: The angel led Peter past the guards and to the iron gate leading to the city. The gate opened on its own, and they walked through it. Peter initially thought he was seeing a vision.

beginner.question: God confused the languages of the people at the Tower of Babel because they were trying to build a tower to reach heaven.
beginner.explanation: The people of Babel wanted to build a tower reaching heaven and make a name for themselves. God confused their language so they could not understand each other, and they scattered across the earth.

beginner.question: The Transfiguration happened on a mountain, and Peter, James, and John were the three disciples present.
beginner.explanation: Jesus took Peter, James, and John — His three closest disciples — up a high mountain. There, His appearance was transformed: His face shone like the sun and His clothes became dazzling white.

beginner.question: During the Transfiguration, a voice from a cloud said, 'This is my servant, listen to him.'
beginner.explanation: The voice from the cloud said, 'This is my Son, whom I love; with him I am well pleased. Listen to him!' — calling Jesus God's Son, not merely a servant.

beginner.question: Gideon asked God for a miraculous sign using a wool fleece, and God provided it.
beginner.explanation: Gideon asked God to make a wool fleece wet with dew while the ground stayed dry. God did it. Then Gideon asked for the reverse — dry fleece on wet ground — and God did that too, confirming His promise.

beginner.question: The Israelites collected manna every day of the week, including the Sabbath.
beginner.explanation: No manna appeared on the Sabbath (the seventh day). God told the Israelites to gather a double portion on the sixth day so they could rest on the Sabbath. Any manna left over on other days would spoil.

beginner.question: When Jesus raised Lazarus, He shouted 'Lazarus, come out!' and Lazarus walked out of the tomb still wrapped in burial cloths.
beginner.explanation: Jesus called with a loud voice, 'Lazarus, come out!' The man who had been dead for four days came out with his hands and feet wrapped in linen strips and a cloth around his face. Jesus said, 'Take off the grave clothes and let him go.'

beginner.question: Before raising Lazarus, Jesus said, 'I am the way and the life.'
beginner.explanation: Before raising Lazarus, Jesus said, 'I am the resurrection and the life' — not 'the way and the life.' 'I am the way, the truth, and the life' is a separate statement found in John 14:6.

beginner.question: Noah brought exactly one of every kind of animal onto the ark before the flood.
beginner.explanation: Noah brought two of every kind — one male and one female — not just one. God specifically instructed him to bring pairs so the animals could reproduce and repopulate the earth after the flood.

beginner.question: The prophet Elisha fed a hundred men with twenty loaves of barley bread, and there was food left over.
beginner.explanation: A man brought Elisha twenty loaves of barley bread. Elisha said to give it to the hundred men to eat. His servant was doubtful, but they all ate and had some left over, just as God's word promised.

beginner.question: Jesus walked on the Dead Sea to reach His disciples during a storm.
beginner.explanation: Jesus walked on the Sea of Galilee — not the Dead Sea. His disciples were crossing the Sea of Galilee in a boat when Jesus came to them, walking on the water during a storm.

beginner.question: When Jesus calmed the storm, the disciples asked, 'Who is this? Even the wind and the waves obey him!'
beginner.explanation: After Jesus commanded the wind and waves to be still and they immediately obeyed, the terrified disciples asked each other this question. The miracle revealed Jesus' divine authority over nature.

beginner.question: The coin that Peter found in the fish's mouth was enough to pay the temple tax for both Jesus and Peter.
beginner.explanation: Jesus told Peter to catch a fish and open its mouth, where he would find a four-drachma coin — exactly enough to pay the temple tax for both of them. This showed Jesus' knowledge and provision.

beginner.question: After being healed of leprosy, Naaman initially refused to wash in the Jordan River because he thought it was beneath him.
beginner.explanation: Naaman was angry at the simple instruction to wash in the Jordan. He said the rivers of Damascus were better than any water in Israel. His servants convinced him to obey, and he was healed.

beginner.question: When the Israelites marched around Jericho, they blew trumpets made of bronze.
beginner.explanation: The trumpets were made from ram's horns (shofars) — not bronze. Seven priests carried these ram's horn trumpets as they marched around the city, and their sound preceded the miraculous collapse of the walls.

beginner.question: The disciple Ananias restored Saul's (Paul's) sight by praying for him in Damascus.
beginner.explanation: God sent a disciple named Ananias to the house where blind Saul was staying. Ananias placed his hands on him and said, 'Brother Saul, the Lord Jesus has sent me so that you may see again.' Immediately scales fell from Saul's eyes.

beginner.question: God rested on the sixth day after finishing creation.
beginner.explanation: God rested on the seventh day — not the sixth. He completed all His work of creation in six days and rested on the seventh, blessing it and making it holy.

beginner.question: Elisha performed more recorded miracles than any other Old Testament prophet.
beginner.explanation: Elisha performed roughly twice as many miracles as his predecessor Elijah. His miracles included healing Naaman, multiplying oil, raising the Shunammite's son, purifying poison stew, making an axe head float, and many more.

beginner.question: When Jesus healed the man born blind, the Pharisees were happy about the miracle.
beginner.explanation: The Pharisees were not happy — they were upset because Jesus performed the healing on the Sabbath. They questioned the man repeatedly and eventually threw him out of the synagogue for defending Jesus.

beginner.question: The Egyptian army followed the Israelites into the parted Red Sea and were drowned when the waters returned.
beginner.explanation: Pharaoh's army pursued the Israelites into the parted sea. When Moses stretched his hand over the sea again, the waters rushed back and covered the chariots, horsemen, and entire Egyptian army. Not one survived.

beginner.question: God told the Israelites to walk around Jericho three times on the final day before the walls fell.
beginner.explanation: On the seventh and final day, the Israelites marched around Jericho seven times — not three. After the seventh lap, the priests blew their trumpets, the people shouted, and the walls collapsed.

beginner.question: When Jesus healed the ten lepers, He told them to go show themselves to the priests.
beginner.explanation: Jesus told the ten lepers to go show themselves to the priests, as the Law of Moses required for anyone healed of leprosy. As they went on their way, they were miraculously cleansed.

beginner.question: Mary Magdalene initially recognised Jesus immediately when she saw Him at the empty tomb after His resurrection.
beginner.explanation: Mary Magdalene did not recognise Jesus at first — she mistook Him for the gardener. It was only when Jesus said her name, 'Mary,' that she recognised Him and was filled with joy.

beginner.question: Jonah prayed to God from inside the great fish, and God commanded the fish to spit him out on dry land.
beginner.explanation: While inside the great fish for three days and nights, Jonah prayed a prayer of thanksgiving and repentance to God. Then God commanded the fish, and it vomited Jonah onto dry land.

beginner.question: When Peter was freed from prison by an angel, the other believers immediately believed it was really Peter at the door.
beginner.explanation: When Peter knocked on the door after his escape, a servant girl named Rhoda recognised his voice but was so overjoyed she forgot to open the door. The other believers told her she was out of her mind — they didn't believe her at first.

beginner.question: The disciple Thomas said 'My Lord and my God!' when he finally saw the risen Jesus and touched His wounds.
beginner.explanation: When Jesus appeared and invited Thomas to touch His nail-scarred hands and pierced side, Thomas exclaimed, 'My Lord and my God!' Jesus then said, 'Blessed are those who have not seen and yet have believed.'

beginner.question: In the parable of the Good Samaritan, a man was robbed and left on the road. Who was the unexpected person that stopped to help him?
beginner.explanation: A priest and a Levite both walked past the wounded man without helping. It was a Samaritan — someone the Jews looked down on — who stopped, bandaged his wounds, and took him to an inn. Jesus told this story to teach that everyone is our neighbour.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Good Samaritan, the injured man was travelling between two cities when he was attacked by robbers. Which road was he on?
options: The road from Egypt to Canaan
The road from Galilee to Samaria
The road from Nazareth to Bethlehem
The road from Jerusalem to Jericho
beginner.explanation: The man was travelling from Jerusalem down to Jericho, a road notorious for being dangerous and rocky. Robbers attacked him, stripped him, beat him, and left him half dead on the roadside.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Good Samaritan, the kind traveller treated the wounded man's injuries. What did the Samaritan pour on the man's wounds?
beginner.explanation: The Samaritan poured oil and wine on the man's wounds — oil to soothe and wine to clean them, which were common remedies at the time. He then bandaged the wounds and placed the man on his own donkey.

beginner.question: After helping the wounded man in the parable of the Good Samaritan, where did the Samaritan take him to recover?
beginner.explanation: The Samaritan took the injured man to an inn and cared for him there. The next day, he gave money to the innkeeper and said he would pay any extra costs on his return. This showed extraordinary generosity to a stranger.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Good Samaritan, two religious men saw the wounded traveller but walked past without helping. Who was the first person to walk past?
beginner.explanation: A priest was the first to come along the road, but when he saw the beaten man, he crossed to the other side and walked past. A Levite did the same. Only the Samaritan stopped to help.

beginner.question: Jesus told the parable of the Good Samaritan in response to a question someone asked him. What question prompted Jesus to tell this story?
options: What must I do to be saved?
How many times should I forgive?
beginner.explanation: An expert in the law asked Jesus 'Who is my neighbour?' to test him. Jesus responded with this parable to show that a neighbour is anyone in need, regardless of their background or nationality.

beginner.question: The parable of the Good Samaritan teaches an important lesson about how we should treat others. What is the main message of this story?
options: Show love and mercy to everyone, even strangers
Religious leaders always do the right thing
beginner.explanation: Jesus taught that loving your neighbour means showing mercy to anyone in need, regardless of who they are. The Samaritan, despite being from a group despised by the Jews, was the true neighbour because he showed compassion.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Good Samaritan, a legal expert tested Jesus with a question. After telling the parable, what did Jesus tell the man to do?
beginner.explanation: After telling the parable, Jesus asked the expert which person was a true neighbour. When the man answered 'the one who showed mercy,' Jesus said 'Go and do likewise.' The instruction was simple: show mercy to everyone.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Prodigal Son, a young man left home after making a bold request to his father. What did the younger son ask for?
options: A new house of his own
His share of the inheritance early
beginner.explanation: The younger son asked his father for his share of the family inheritance before the father had even died, which was deeply disrespectful in that culture. The father gave it to him, and the son left for a distant country.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Prodigal Son, after the young man spent all his money and a famine struck, he was desperate for work. What job did he end up doing?
beginner.explanation: The son was so desperate he took a job feeding pigs, which was one of the lowest and most shameful jobs a Jewish person could have, since pigs were considered unclean animals. He was so hungry he wanted to eat the pig food.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Prodigal Son, the young man eventually decided to go home. When his father saw him coming from a distance, what did the father do?
options: Ran to him and embraced him
Sent a servant to meet him
Waited at the door with arms crossed
Turned his back and walked away
beginner.explanation: The father had been watching and waiting. When he saw his son far off, he was filled with compassion, ran to him, threw his arms around him, and kissed him. This shows how God eagerly welcomes back those who return to him.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Prodigal Son, the overjoyed father gave his returning son several gifts. Which of these did the father give him?
options: The best robe, a ring, and sandals
beginner.explanation: The father ordered servants to bring the best robe, put a ring on his son's finger, and sandals on his feet. These were signs of honour, authority, and belonging — showing the son was fully restored as a member of the family.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Prodigal Son, the father threw a big celebration when his son came home. Who was upset about the party?
beginner.explanation: The older brother was angry and refused to join the celebration. He had stayed home and worked faithfully, so he felt it was unfair that his wasteful brother got a party. The father reminded him that everything he had was already his.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Prodigal Son, the father threw a feast to celebrate his younger son's return. What animal was killed for the celebration?
beginner.explanation: The father ordered the fattened calf to be killed for a grand feast. A fattened calf was reserved for very special occasions, showing just how joyful the father was that his lost son had come home alive and well.

beginner.question: The parable of the Prodigal Son teaches an important lesson about God. What is the main message of this story?
options: God punishes those who leave him
God rejoices when someone who was lost comes back to him
God favours obedient children over disobedient ones
beginner.explanation: The father in the story represents God, who lovingly waits and celebrates when a lost person returns. The parable teaches that no matter how far someone has strayed, God's love and forgiveness are always available.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Prodigal Son, the younger son rehearsed a speech on his way home. What did he plan to say to his father?
options: I'm sorry I left but I deserve to come home
I have sinned — make me like one of your hired servants
beginner.explanation: The son planned to say 'Father, I have sinned against heaven and against you. I am no longer worthy to be called your son; make me like one of your hired servants.' But the father interrupted him with love before he could finish.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Lost Sheep, Jesus told a story about a shepherd who lost one of his animals. How many sheep did the shepherd originally have?
beginner.explanation: The shepherd had 100 sheep, and when one went missing, he left the other 99 to go search for it. Jesus used this story to show how much God cares about each and every individual person.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Lost Sheep, when the shepherd realised one sheep was missing, what did he do with the other 99?
options: Left them in the open country to search for the lost one
Asked another shepherd to sell them
Locked them in a pen and went home
beginner.explanation: The shepherd left the 99 sheep in the open country and went after the one that was lost. He searched until he found it, showing that every single sheep mattered to him — just as every person matters to God.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Lost Sheep, the shepherd searched and finally found his missing sheep. What did he do when he found it?
options: Left it to walk back on its own
Joyfully put it on his shoulders and carried it home
Scolded it and tied it up
beginner.explanation: When the shepherd found the lost sheep, he joyfully put it on his shoulders and carried it all the way home. He then called his friends and neighbours to celebrate, saying 'Rejoice with me; I have found my lost sheep.'

beginner.question: In the parable of the Lost Sheep, Jesus says there is rejoicing in heaven over what event?
options: A rich man giving to the poor
A priest saying a perfect prayer
beginner.explanation: Jesus said there is more rejoicing in heaven over one sinner who repents than over ninety-nine righteous people who do not need to repent. This parable shows the incredible value God places on each person who turns back to him.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Lost Sheep, the shepherd represents someone important. Who does the shepherd represent?
beginner.explanation: The shepherd represents Jesus or God, who actively seeks out those who are lost. Just as a good shepherd won't rest until every sheep is safe, God pursues every person with love and doesn't want anyone to be lost.

beginner.question: Jesus told the parable of the Lost Sheep to a particular group of people who were criticising him. Who was grumbling about Jesus spending time with sinners?
options: The Pharisees and teachers of the law
beginner.explanation: The Pharisees and teachers of the law were muttering 'This man welcomes sinners and eats with them.' Jesus told this parable (along with the Lost Coin and Prodigal Son) to explain why he cared for outcasts and sinners.

beginner.question: The parable of the Lost Sheep teaches an important truth about God's character. What is the main lesson?
options: God actively searches for every lost person and celebrates when they are found
Only large flocks matter to God
beginner.explanation: The main lesson is that God cares deeply about every individual. He doesn't write off anyone as too far gone. Like the shepherd who wouldn't rest until his one lost sheep was found, God pursues every lost person with love.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Lost Coin, a woman lost a valuable coin and searched her whole house. How many coins did she originally have?
beginner.explanation: The woman had ten silver coins (drachmas) and lost one. These coins may have been part of her dowry or savings, making each one very precious. She searched carefully until she found the missing coin.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Lost Coin, the woman lost one of her precious coins inside her house. What did she do to find it?
options: She asked her neighbours to search
She lit a lamp, swept the house, and searched carefully
She gave up and forgot about it
beginner.explanation: The woman lit a lamp and swept every corner of her house, searching carefully until she found the coin. Houses at that time often had dirt floors and no windows, making a lost coin very hard to spot without a light.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Lost Coin, when the woman finally found her lost coin, what did she do to celebrate?
options: She called her friends and neighbours to rejoice with her
She gave it to the temple
She hid it in a safe place
She spent it at the market
beginner.explanation: The woman was so happy she called together her friends and neighbours, saying 'Rejoice with me; I have found my lost coin!' Jesus compared this joy to the joy in heaven when one sinner repents.

beginner.question: The parable of the Lost Coin has the same main message as the parables of the Lost Sheep and the Prodigal Son. What does it teach?
options: Women are good at finding things
There is great joy in heaven when even one lost person turns back to God
Money is the most important thing
beginner.explanation: Like the Lost Sheep and the Prodigal Son, this parable teaches that God values every single person. When even one sinner repents, all of heaven celebrates. God never gives up searching for those who are lost.

beginner.question: The parables of the Lost Sheep, the Lost Coin, and the Prodigal Son are all found in the same chapter of the Bible. Which book and chapter contains all three?
beginner.explanation: All three 'lost' parables appear together in Luke chapter 15. Jesus told them in response to the Pharisees' criticism that he welcomed sinners. Each parable emphasises God's joy when something lost is found.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Sower, a farmer went out to scatter seeds. In this story, what does the seed represent?
beginner.explanation: Jesus explained that the seed represents the word of God — his message and teachings. The different types of soil represent how different people receive and respond to God's word when they hear it.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Sower, some seeds fell on the footpath. What happened to the seeds that landed on the hard path?
options: Birds came and ate them up
They were washed away by rain
beginner.explanation: The seeds that fell on the path were quickly eaten by birds. Jesus said this represents people who hear God's word, but the devil comes and takes it away from their hearts before they can believe it.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Sower, some seeds fell on rocky ground where the soil was thin. What happened to these seeds?
options: They grew into the strongest plants
Birds ate them before they could grow
They sprouted quickly but withered because they had no deep roots
beginner.explanation: Seeds on rocky ground sprang up quickly but had no deep roots. When the sun came up, they were scorched and withered away. This represents people who receive God's word with joy but fall away when trouble comes.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Sower, some seeds fell among thorns. What happened to the seeds that grew among thorny plants?
options: The thorns protected them from the sun
They blew away in the wind
They grew taller than the thorns
The thorns grew up and choked the plants so they couldn't produce fruit
beginner.explanation: The thorns grew up alongside the plants and choked them, so they produced no crop. Jesus said the thorns represent the worries of life, the love of money, and desire for other things that crowd out God's word.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Sower, some seeds fell on good, fertile soil. What happened to those seeds?
options: They grew and produced an abundant crop — thirty, sixty, or a hundred times what was sown
beginner.explanation: The seeds on good soil grew strong and produced a massive harvest — some thirty, sixty, or even a hundred times what was planted. This represents people who hear God's word, understand it, and live it out faithfully.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Sower, seeds fell on four different types of ground. What does the rocky ground represent?
options: People who accept God's word joyfully but give up when times get hard
People who never hear God's word
People who are strong in their faith
beginner.explanation: The rocky ground represents people who hear God's word and receive it with immediate joy, but because they have no root, they quickly fall away when persecution or difficulty comes. Their faith is shallow and short-lived.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Sower, what does the good soil represent?
options: People who hear God's word, understand it, and produce fruit in their lives
beginner.explanation: Good soil represents people with honest and good hearts who hear God's word, retain it, and produce a harvest of good things through patience and faithfulness. They let the word take deep root and transform their lives.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Sower, Jesus compared the thorny ground to a problem many people face. What do the thorns represent?
options: Worries of life, love of money, and worldly desires
beginner.explanation: Jesus said the thorns represent the worries of this life, the deceitfulness of wealth, and desires for other things. These distractions crowd out God's word and prevent it from producing fruit in a person's life.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Mustard Seed, Jesus compared the Kingdom of Heaven to a tiny seed. What is special about the mustard seed?
options: It is one of the smallest seeds but grows into a very large plant
It is the most colourful seed
It is the most expensive seed
It is the hardest seed to find
beginner.explanation: Jesus said the mustard seed is the smallest of all garden seeds, yet when it is planted, it grows into one of the largest garden plants — big enough for birds to perch in its branches. This shows how God's kingdom starts small but grows enormously.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Mustard Seed, Jesus said the tiny seed grows into a large plant. What comes and nests in its branches?
beginner.explanation: Jesus said the mustard seed grows into a tree so large that 'the birds of the air come and perch in its branches.' This imagery suggests that the Kingdom of God, though starting small, provides shelter and blessing for many.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Mustard Seed, Jesus used a tiny seed to teach a lesson. What was Jesus comparing the mustard seed to?
beginner.explanation: Jesus said 'The kingdom of heaven is like a mustard seed.' He was teaching that God's kingdom may start with small, humble beginnings — like a handful of followers — but it will grow into something magnificent.

beginner.question: The parable of the Mustard Seed teaches an encouraging lesson about God's kingdom. What is the main message?
options: Great things can grow from the smallest beginnings
Mustard is the best plant to grow
Only big things matter to God
beginner.explanation: The parable teaches that God's kingdom starts from something tiny and seemingly insignificant but grows into something vast and magnificent. It encourages believers not to despise small beginnings, because God can make great things from them.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Mustard Seed, a man planted the seed in a specific place. Where did he plant it?
options: In a pot on his windowsill
beginner.explanation: The man took the mustard seed and planted it in his field (Matthew says 'field', Luke says 'garden'). Despite being the smallest seed, it grew into the largest of garden plants, illustrating how God's kingdom expands.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Wise and Foolish Builders, Jesus compared two men who each built a house. What did the wise man build his house on?
beginner.explanation: The wise man dug deep and built his house on solid rock. When the storms came — rain, floods, and wind — his house stood firm because it had a strong foundation. Jesus said this represents someone who hears his words and puts them into practice.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Wise and Foolish Builders, the foolish man chose a poor foundation for his house. What did the foolish man build his house on?
beginner.explanation: The foolish man built his house on sand without a proper foundation. It may have been quicker and easier, but when the storms came, his house collapsed completely. This represents someone who hears Jesus' words but does not obey them.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Wise and Foolish Builders, both houses were hit by a storm. What happened to the house built on rock?
options: It was carried away by the flood
It stood firm and did not fall
beginner.explanation: The rain poured down, the streams rose, and the winds blew and beat against the house on rock, but it did not fall because its foundation was solid. This shows the security of building your life on Jesus' teachings.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Wise and Foolish Builders, both houses faced the same storm. What happened to the house built on sand?
options: It fell with a great crash
It stood firm like the other house
beginner.explanation: When the rain, floods, and wind hit the house on sand, it fell with a great crash — a total collapse. Jesus used this dramatic image to warn that a life not built on obedience to God's word will not survive life's storms.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Wise and Foolish Builders, what three things struck both houses during the storm?
beginner.explanation: Jesus described rain falling, streams rising (floods), and winds blowing against both houses. The same storms hit both builders, but only the one with a strong foundation survived. Life's difficulties test what we have built on.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Wise and Foolish Builders, Jesus said the wise builder represents a certain type of person. Who does the wise builder represent?
options: Someone who hears Jesus' words and puts them into practice
beginner.explanation: Jesus said 'Everyone who hears these words of mine and puts them into practice is like a wise man who built his house on the rock.' The key is not just hearing God's word, but actually living it out in your daily life.

beginner.question: The parable of the Wise and Foolish Builders comes at the end of one of Jesus' most famous sermons. Which sermon does it conclude?
beginner.explanation: This parable is the conclusion of the Sermon on the Mount, found in Matthew chapters 5-7. Jesus ended his most famous sermon with this powerful illustration about the importance of not just hearing his words, but obeying them.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Talents, a master gave money to his servants before going away. What was the master about to do?
beginner.explanation: The master was going on a long journey and entrusted his property to his servants while he was away. He gave each servant a different number of talents (a large unit of money) based on their ability.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Talents, the master gave different amounts to three servants. How many talents did the first servant receive?
beginner.explanation: The first servant received five talents, the second received two, and the third received one — each according to his ability. A talent was a very large sum of money, worth about 20 years' wages for a common labourer.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Talents, the servant who received five talents put them to work. What happened to his investment?
options: He doubled them to ten talents
beginner.explanation: The servant with five talents invested wisely and earned five more, doubling his master's money. Similarly, the servant with two talents also doubled his to four. Both were praised for their faithfulness and initiative.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Talents, the servant who received only one talent handled it very differently from the others. What did he do with his single talent?
options: He gave it to the poor
He dug a hole and buried it in the ground
beginner.explanation: The servant with one talent was afraid of losing it, so he dug a hole in the ground and hid his master's money. When the master returned, this servant could only return the original amount with nothing gained.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Talents, when the master returned, he praised the servants who had multiplied their money. What famous words did he say to them?
options: You are the greatest servants ever
Well done, good and faithful servant
beginner.explanation: The master said 'Well done, good and faithful servant! You have been faithful with a few things; I will put you in charge of many things. Come and share your master's happiness.' This phrase has become one of the most beloved in the Bible.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Talents, the servant who buried his one talent was punished when the master returned. What happened to his talent?
options: He was allowed to keep it
The master doubled it for him
It was donated to the temple
It was taken away and given to the servant who had ten
beginner.explanation: The master took the one talent from the unfaithful servant and gave it to the one who had ten. Jesus taught that those who use what God gives them will receive more, but those who waste their gifts will lose even what they have.

beginner.question: The parable of the Talents teaches an important lesson about what God expects from us. What is the main message?
options: Only talented people matter to God
Save everything and take no risks
Money is the most important thing in life
Use the gifts and abilities God gives you faithfully
beginner.explanation: The parable teaches that God entrusts each person with different gifts, abilities, and opportunities, and he expects us to use them faithfully — not hide them away out of fear. Faithful stewardship, not the amount, is what God rewards.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Wedding Feast, a king prepared a grand banquet to celebrate an important event. What was the occasion?
beginner.explanation: The king prepared a magnificent wedding banquet for his son and sent out invitations. This parable represents God (the king) inviting people into his kingdom through Jesus (the son), but many reject the invitation.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Wedding Feast, the king sent servants to invite guests. How did the originally invited guests respond?
options: They came eagerly and brought gifts
They refused to come and made excuses
They asked for a different date
beginner.explanation: The invited guests refused to come. Some ignored the invitation and went about their business — one to his field, another to his shop. Some even mistreated and killed the king's servants. They took the generous invitation for granted.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Wedding Feast, after the original guests refused, who did the king invite instead?
options: Only the priests and religious leaders
Anyone found on the streets — both good and bad
beginner.explanation: The king told his servants to go out to the street corners and invite anyone they could find. The servants gathered all the people they found, both good and bad, and the wedding hall was filled. This represents God's grace being extended to everyone.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Wedding Feast, when the king came in to see the guests, he noticed something wrong with one man. What was the problem?
options: He was being rude to other guests
He had not brought a gift
He was not wearing wedding clothes
beginner.explanation: The king noticed one guest who was not wearing wedding clothes. In that culture, appropriate garments would have been provided. Refusing to wear them was an insult to the host. This represents someone who accepts God's invitation but refuses to change.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Wedding Feast, what happened to the guest who was not wearing the proper wedding clothes?
options: He was seated at the back of the room
He was given new clothes and seated at the head table
He was simply asked to leave politely
He was thrown outside into the darkness
beginner.explanation: The king ordered his attendants to tie the man hand and foot and throw him outside into the darkness. Jesus ended with the saying 'Many are invited, but few are chosen.' This warns that God's invitation requires a genuine response.

beginner.question: The parable of the Wedding Feast ends with a famous saying by Jesus. What did he say?
options: Many are invited, but few are chosen
beginner.explanation: Jesus concluded with 'For many are invited, but few are chosen.' This means that while God's invitation to his kingdom goes out to everyone, not everyone responds properly. Both accepting the invitation and coming prepared are essential.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Rich Fool, a wealthy farmer had an exceptionally good year. What happened on his land?
options: A fire burned down his barns
A terrible drought destroyed his crops
His land produced an enormous, abundant harvest
beginner.explanation: The rich man's land produced such an abundant harvest that he didn't have enough room to store all his grain. Instead of thanking God or helping others, he began to plan how to keep everything for himself.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Rich Fool, the wealthy farmer had so much grain he ran out of storage space. What did he decide to build?
options: Bigger barns to store all his grain
A marketplace to sell his crops
beginner.explanation: The rich man decided to tear down his existing barns and build bigger ones to store all his grain and goods. His focus was entirely on accumulating wealth for himself rather than sharing his abundance with those in need.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Rich Fool, after planning his bigger barns, the wealthy man spoke to himself about his future plans. What did he say?
options: I must give to the poor and share my blessings
Take life easy — eat, drink, and be merry
I need to pray and thank God
I should work even harder next year
beginner.explanation: The rich man said to himself 'You have plenty of grain laid up for many years. Take life easy; eat, drink, and be merry.' He was focused entirely on his own comfort and pleasure, with no thought for God or others.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Rich Fool, the wealthy man made his selfish plans that very night. But what did God say to him?
options: Build even more barns for the future
Well done, you have worked hard
Go and share your wealth with the poor
You fool! This very night your life will be demanded from you
beginner.explanation: God said 'You fool! This very night your life will be demanded from you. Then who will get what you have prepared for yourself?' The man died that night, and all his stored-up wealth meant nothing. He had no treasure in heaven.

beginner.question: The parable of the Rich Fool carries a strong warning from Jesus. What is the main lesson of this story?
options: Farming is too risky as a career
Don't be greedy — be rich toward God instead of storing up treasure only for yourself
Working hard is a waste of time
beginner.explanation: Jesus warned against greed, saying 'Watch out! Be on your guard against all kinds of greed; life does not consist in an abundance of possessions.' The lesson is to be generous and invest in your relationship with God, not just material things.

beginner.question: Jesus told the parable of the Rich Fool after someone in the crowd made a request. What did the person ask Jesus to do?
options: Tell his brother to divide the family inheritance with him
Explain the meaning of a scripture
beginner.explanation: Someone in the crowd said 'Teacher, tell my brother to divide the inheritance with me.' Jesus refused to be a judge in family disputes and instead told this parable to warn everyone about the danger of greed.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Pharisee and the Tax Collector, two men went to the temple to pray. Who were these two men?
options: A Pharisee and a tax collector
beginner.explanation: A Pharisee (a religious leader who was seen as very righteous) and a tax collector (seen as a traitor and sinner) both went to the temple to pray. Jesus used these two contrasting figures to teach about true humility before God.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Pharisee and the Tax Collector, the Pharisee stood up and prayed confidently. What did his prayer focus on?
options: Listing how good he was and how superior he was to others
Praying for the sick and needy
beginner.explanation: The Pharisee prayed about himself, thanking God that he was 'not like other people — robbers, evildoers, adulterers — or even like this tax collector.' He listed his own good deeds: fasting twice a week and tithing. His prayer was full of pride.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Pharisee and the Tax Collector, the tax collector prayed very differently from the Pharisee. How did the tax collector pray?
options: He beat his chest and said 'God, have mercy on me, a sinner'
He stood at the front and prayed loudly
He listed all his good deeds
He praised himself for paying his taxes
beginner.explanation: The tax collector stood at a distance, wouldn't even look up to heaven, and beat his chest saying 'God, have mercy on me, a sinner.' His prayer was short, honest, and humble — a genuine cry for God's forgiveness.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Pharisee and the Tax Collector, Jesus revealed which man went home in right standing with God. Who was it?
options: The tax collector, because he humbly asked for mercy
The Pharisee, because he kept all the rules
beginner.explanation: Jesus said the tax collector, not the Pharisee, went home justified before God. The tax collector's humble, honest prayer was accepted because 'everyone who exalts himself will be humbled, and he who humbles himself will be exalted.'

beginner.question: The parable of the Pharisee and the Tax Collector teaches an important lesson about prayer and how we approach God. What is the main message?
options: God honours humility, not pride or self-righteousness
Tax collectors are better than Pharisees
Long prayers are the most effective
Only religious leaders can pray properly
beginner.explanation: The parable teaches that God looks at the heart, not outward appearances. A humble, sincere prayer from a repentant sinner is more pleasing to God than a proud, self-congratulatory prayer from someone who thinks they are already perfect.

beginner.question: Jesus told the parable of the Pharisee and the Tax Collector to a specific audience. Who was he speaking to?
options: People who were confident in their own righteousness and looked down on others
beginner.explanation: Luke tells us Jesus told this parable 'to some who were confident of their own righteousness and looked down on everyone else.' The story was a direct challenge to those who believed they were better than others in God's eyes.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Unforgiving Servant, a servant owed the king an enormous debt. How much did he owe?
beginner.explanation: The servant owed 10,000 talents, which was an astronomical, unpayable amount — equivalent to millions in today's money. Jesus used this extreme number to illustrate the magnitude of the debt that God forgives when he pardons our sins.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Unforgiving Servant, the servant begged the king for patience on his huge debt. What did the king do?
options: Made him work it off over many years
Had compassion and forgave the entire debt
beginner.explanation: The king took pity on the servant, cancelled the entire massive debt, and let him go free. This incredible act of mercy represents God's willingness to completely forgive all our sins when we ask for his grace.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Unforgiving Servant, the forgiven servant immediately went out and found someone. What did he do?
options: He shared his good fortune with everyone
He found a fellow servant who owed him a small amount and demanded payment
He went and thanked God at the temple
beginner.explanation: Right after being forgiven a massive debt, the servant found a fellow servant who owed him just 100 denarii (a tiny fraction of what he had been forgiven). He grabbed him by the throat and demanded 'Pay back what you owe me!'

beginner.question: In the parable of the Unforgiving Servant, the fellow servant owed a much smaller debt. Compared to the 10,000-talent debt the king forgave, how much did this second servant owe?
options: 100 denarii — a tiny fraction of the first debt
beginner.explanation: The fellow servant owed only 100 denarii, which was a few months' wages — a tiny sum compared to the 10,000 talents (millions) that had been forgiven. This contrast highlights the hypocrisy of refusing to forgive small debts after receiving enormous forgiveness.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Unforgiving Servant, when the king heard what the forgiven servant had done, how did he respond?
options: He angrily handed the servant over to be punished until he could pay back everything
beginner.explanation: The king was furious and said 'You wicked servant! I cancelled all that debt because you begged me. Shouldn't you have had mercy on your fellow servant just as I had on you?' He then handed him over to be punished.

beginner.question: The parable of the Unforgiving Servant teaches a clear lesson about forgiveness. What is the main message?
options: Only forgive people who deserve it
Since God forgives us so much, we must also forgive others
Always keep track of what people owe you
beginner.explanation: Jesus concluded by saying 'This is how my heavenly Father will treat each of you unless you forgive your brother or sister from your heart.' Since God has forgiven us an enormous debt of sin, we must willingly forgive others their smaller debts against us.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Good Shepherd, Jesus made a bold claim about himself. He said 'I am the...' what?
beginner.explanation: Jesus declared 'I am the good shepherd,' one of his famous 'I am' statements in the Gospel of John. He described himself as a shepherd who knows each of his sheep personally and cares for them deeply.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Good Shepherd, Jesus said the good shepherd does something remarkable for his sheep. What does the good shepherd do?
options: He abandons them when danger comes
He sells the sheep for profit
He lets them fend for themselves
He lays down his life for the sheep
beginner.explanation: Jesus said 'The good shepherd lays down his life for the sheep.' This was a prophecy about his own death on the cross — he would willingly give up his life to save and protect those who follow him.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Good Shepherd, Jesus contrasted himself with a hired hand. When a wolf attacks the flock, what does the hired hand do?
options: He calls for help from the shepherd
He abandons the sheep and runs away
beginner.explanation: Jesus said the hired hand runs away when he sees the wolf coming because he doesn't truly care about the sheep — he's only working for pay. The wolf then attacks and scatters the flock. Unlike the hired hand, the good shepherd stays and protects.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Good Shepherd, Jesus described a special bond between himself and his followers. He said his sheep know what about him?
beginner.explanation: Jesus said 'My sheep listen to my voice; I know them, and they follow me.' In the Middle East, sheep truly do recognise their own shepherd's voice and will only follow him. Jesus used this to describe the personal relationship between himself and believers.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Good Shepherd, Jesus described himself as the gate or door for the sheep. What did he mean by calling himself 'the gate'?
options: He blocks people from entering heaven
He is the only way to be saved — anyone who enters through him will be safe
beginner.explanation: Jesus said 'I am the gate; whoever enters through me will be saved.' In ancient times, the shepherd literally lay across the entrance to the sheep pen at night, becoming the door. Jesus meant he is the way to salvation and safety.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Good Shepherd, Jesus mentioned that he has sheep beyond the current flock. What did he say about these 'other sheep'?
options: They belong to a different shepherd
He must bring them also, and they will listen to his voice
beginner.explanation: Jesus said 'I have other sheep that are not of this sheep pen. I must bring them also. They too will listen to my voice, and there shall be one flock and one shepherd.' This referred to non-Jewish people who would also come to follow him.

beginner.question: The parable of the Good Shepherd teaches about the relationship between Jesus and his followers. What is the main lesson?
options: Jesus knows, loves, and protects each of his followers personally
Wolves are the biggest problem in life
beginner.explanation: The central teaching is that Jesus is not a distant, uncaring leader but a shepherd who knows each person by name, cares deeply about them, and is willing to sacrifice everything — even his own life — to keep them safe.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Workers in the Vineyard, a landowner went out to hire workers for his vineyard. What time did he first go out to hire?
beginner.explanation: The landowner went out early in the morning to hire workers and agreed to pay them a denarius (a standard day's wage) for their day of work. He then went out again at different times throughout the day to hire more workers.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Workers in the Vineyard, the landowner hired more workers throughout the day. When were the very last workers hired?
options: About the eleventh hour — near the end of the working day
beginner.explanation: The last workers were hired at about the eleventh hour — only one hour before the working day ended. Despite working for such a short time, they still received the same pay as those who had worked all day.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Workers in the Vineyard, when payday came, the workers who were hired last — at the eleventh hour — received their wages. How much did they get?
options: Double a denarius as a bonus
A full denarius — the same as those who worked all day
beginner.explanation: The last workers received a full denarius — the exact same amount as those who had worked since dawn. This shocked the early workers and is the key surprise of the parable, illustrating that God's generosity is not based on human measures of fairness.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Workers in the Vineyard, the workers who had worked since early morning saw the late arrivals get a full day's pay. How did they react?
options: They asked for more work tomorrow
They complained and grumbled that it was unfair
beginner.explanation: The early workers expected to receive more since they had worked all day, so they grumbled against the landowner when they got the same wage. They thought equal pay for unequal work was unjust.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Workers in the Vineyard, the landowner responded to the grumbling workers. What did he say to defend his generosity?
options: You're right, I made a mistake
Don't I have the right to do what I want with my own money? Are you envious because I am generous?
beginner.explanation: The landowner reminded the workers that he had paid them exactly what was agreed. He said 'Don't I have the right to do what I want with my own money? Or are you envious because I am generous?' This illustrates God's sovereign grace.

beginner.question: The parable of the Workers in the Vineyard teaches an important lesson about God's kingdom. What is the main message?
options: Only early believers receive God's love
God's grace and generosity are not based on human ideas of fairness — he gives freely to all
beginner.explanation: The parable teaches that God's grace cannot be earned or measured by human standards. Whether someone comes to God early in life or at the very last moment, God's love and generosity are given freely. His kingdom doesn't run on merit-based rewards.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Ten Virgins (bridesmaids), ten young women were waiting for someone to arrive. Who were they waiting for?
beginner.explanation: The ten virgins were bridesmaids waiting for the bridegroom to arrive for the wedding celebration. In Jewish wedding customs, the bridegroom would come at night, and the bridal party needed lamps to go out and meet him.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Ten Virgins, Jesus said five of the young women were wise and five were foolish. How many were wise?
beginner.explanation: Jesus described five as wise and five as foolish. The difference between them was not their lamps (they all had lamps) but their preparation. The wise ones planned ahead while the foolish ones did not.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Ten Virgins, the wise bridesmaids did something the foolish ones forgot to do. What did the wise ones bring with them?
beginner.explanation: The wise virgins brought extra flasks of oil for their lamps along with them. The foolish ones took their lamps but didn't bring any extra oil. When the bridegroom was delayed, only the prepared ones had enough oil to keep their lamps burning.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Ten Virgins, the bridegroom took longer than expected to arrive. What did all ten young women do while waiting?
options: They lit a fire to keep warm
beginner.explanation: Because the bridegroom was so long in coming, all ten of them — both the wise and the foolish — became drowsy and fell asleep. This detail shows that even good preparation doesn't mean the wait is easy.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Ten Virgins, at midnight a cry rang out that the bridegroom was coming. What happened to the foolish virgins' lamps?
options: Their lamps were going out because they had no extra oil
beginner.explanation: When the shout came at midnight, everyone woke up and trimmed their lamps. But the foolish virgins discovered their lamps were going out — they had run out of oil. They frantically asked the wise ones to share, but there wasn't enough for everyone.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Ten Virgins, the foolish bridesmaids rushed out to buy oil. When they returned, what did they find?
options: The door to the wedding banquet was shut and they were locked out
The bridegroom was still on his way
The door was open and they walked right in
beginner.explanation: While the foolish virgins were away buying oil, the bridegroom arrived and the wedding banquet began. When they returned and knocked on the door, the bridegroom said 'Truly I tell you, I don't know you.' They were shut out.

beginner.question: The parable of the Ten Virgins teaches a lesson about being ready. What is the main message Jesus was teaching?
options: Be prepared and spiritually ready for Jesus' return, because you don't know when it will happen
Always carry a lamp with you
beginner.explanation: Jesus concluded with 'Therefore keep watch, because you do not know the day or the hour.' The parable warns believers to stay spiritually prepared and alert, because Jesus' return could happen at any time — and there won't be a chance to get ready at the last second.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Pearl of Great Price, a merchant spent his life searching for something. What was he looking for?
beginner.explanation: The merchant was a pearl dealer who spent his career searching for the finest pearls. One day he found one of extraordinary value — so beautiful and precious that it was worth more than everything else he owned combined.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Pearl of Great Price, when the merchant found an exceptionally valuable pearl, what did he do to get it?
options: He decided it was too expensive and walked away
He sold everything he had and bought it
He stole it during the night
He offered to trade some of his other pearls
beginner.explanation: The merchant was so captivated by this pearl's value that he went away, sold everything he owned, and came back to buy it. He gave up all his other possessions because this one pearl was worth more than everything else combined.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Pearl of Great Price, what does the incredibly valuable pearl represent?
beginner.explanation: Jesus said 'The kingdom of heaven is like a merchant looking for fine pearls.' The pearl of great price represents the Kingdom of God, which is so valuable that it is worth giving up everything else to possess it.

beginner.question: The parable of the Pearl of Great Price teaches an important lesson about the Kingdom of God. What is the main message?
options: The Kingdom of God is so valuable that it's worth sacrificing everything to have it
Material wealth should be your top priority
beginner.explanation: The parable teaches that discovering God's kingdom is like finding the most precious thing imaginable — something so wonderful that giving up everything else for it is not a sacrifice but a joyful trade. Nothing else compares to knowing God.

beginner.question: The parable of the Pearl of Great Price is very similar to another short parable Jesus told right before it. What is the other parable about?
options: A fishing net in the sea
A lost sheep in a field
A tree growing from a seed
A treasure hidden in a field
beginner.explanation: Just before the Pearl parable, Jesus told the parable of the Hidden Treasure — about a man who found treasure in a field and sold everything to buy that field. Both parables teach the same lesson: the Kingdom of Heaven is worth everything.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Hidden Treasure, a man made an amazing discovery while in a field. What did he find?
options: A bag of gold coins on the road
beginner.explanation: A man found treasure that was hidden in a field. In ancient times, people often buried valuables in the ground to keep them safe from thieves and invading armies, so finding hidden treasure was not unheard of.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Hidden Treasure, after the man found the treasure in the field, what was the first thing he did?
options: He left it there and forgot about it
He told all his friends about it
He hid it again, then joyfully sold everything he had to buy the field
He took the treasure and ran away
beginner.explanation: The man hid the treasure back in the field, then in his great joy went and sold everything he owned so he could buy the field — and the treasure with it. His joy made the sacrifice feel like nothing.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Hidden Treasure, how did the man feel when he discovered the treasure?
options: Angry that someone else had buried it
beginner.explanation: The man was filled with joy at his discovery. His happiness was so great that selling everything he owned didn't feel like a loss at all — he knew the treasure was worth far more than anything he was giving up.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Hidden Treasure, what does the hidden treasure represent?
beginner.explanation: Jesus began the parable by saying 'The kingdom of heaven is like treasure hidden in a field.' The treasure represents the incredible value of belonging to God's kingdom — something so precious that it's worth giving up everything else.

beginner.question: The parable of the Hidden Treasure teaches about the value of God's kingdom. What is the main lesson?
options: Buy as much land as you can afford
Discovering God's kingdom is so wonderful that everything else pales in comparison
Keep your treasure a secret from others
Always dig in fields because you might find gold
beginner.explanation: The parable teaches that the Kingdom of God is the most valuable thing a person can ever find. When you truly discover it, you'll joyfully give up anything and everything to be part of it — and it will be the best decision of your life.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Wheat and the Weeds (Tares), a farmer planted good wheat in his field. But an enemy came secretly and planted something else. What did the enemy sow?
options: More wheat to confuse the farmer
beginner.explanation: The enemy came at night and sowed weeds (tares) among the wheat. These weeds looked almost identical to wheat while growing, making them very hard to tell apart until harvest time. This represents evil mixed in with good in the world.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Wheat and the Weeds, when did the enemy come to plant the weeds among the farmer's wheat?
options: At night while everyone was sleeping
During the middle of the day
Right after the wheat was harvested
beginner.explanation: The enemy came at night while everyone was sleeping and sowed weeds among the wheat. This sneaky, under-cover-of-darkness action represents how evil often works secretly and deceptively in the world.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Wheat and the Weeds, the servants noticed weeds growing among the good grain and asked the master what to do. What did the master say?
options: Pull up all the weeds immediately
Plant more wheat to crowd out the weeds
Let both grow together until harvest time, then separate them
beginner.explanation: The master wisely said 'Let both grow together until the harvest.' He was concerned that pulling up the weeds too early would uproot the wheat as well. At harvest time, he would have the reapers separate them properly.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Wheat and the Weeds, what happens to the weeds at harvest time?
options: They are gathered first, tied in bundles, and burned
They are kept with the wheat
beginner.explanation: At harvest time, the reapers first collect the weeds, tie them in bundles, and burn them. Then they gather the wheat into the barn. This represents the final judgment when God will separate the righteous from the wicked.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Wheat and the Weeds, Jesus explained who the enemy who sowed the weeds represents. Who is the enemy?
beginner.explanation: Jesus explained privately to his disciples that the enemy who sowed the weeds is the devil. The field is the world, the good seeds are the people of God's kingdom, and the weeds are the people of the evil one.

beginner.question: The parable of the Wheat and the Weeds teaches a lesson about good and evil in the world. What is the main message?
options: Weeds are harmless and can be ignored
Farmers should guard their fields better
Good and evil exist side by side in the world, but God will separate them at the final judgment
Evil people should be immediately destroyed
beginner.explanation: The parable teaches that good and evil coexist in the world right now, but this is temporary. God patiently allows both to grow together, but at the final judgment, he will perfectly separate the righteous from the wicked.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Net, fishermen cast something into the sea. What did they use?
options: A fishing line with a hook
beginner.explanation: The fishermen cast a large dragnet into the lake, which caught all kinds of fish as it was pulled through the water. This type of net was designed to sweep up everything in its path without discrimination.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Net, the large net dragged through the sea caught many things. What did it catch?
options: Only treasure from the sea floor
Fish of every kind — both good and bad
beginner.explanation: The net caught fish of every kind — good and bad, big and small. This represents how God's kingdom message goes out to all people, and all kinds of people respond to it, though not all responses are genuine.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Net, when the fishermen pulled their full net to shore, what did they do with the catch?
options: They sat down and sorted the good fish into containers and threw the bad away
beginner.explanation: The fishermen sat down on the shore and sorted through the catch. They collected the good fish into baskets and threw the bad fish away. This careful sorting represents God's future judgment separating the righteous from the wicked.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Net, Jesus said the sorting of fish is like something that will happen in the future. What event was he describing?
options: A market day at the harbour
The end of the age, when angels will separate the wicked from the righteous
beginner.explanation: Jesus said 'This is how it will be at the end of the age. The angels will come and separate the wicked from the righteous.' Just as fishermen sort their catch, God will sort all people at the final judgment.

beginner.question: The parable of the Net is very similar in its message to another parable Jesus told. Which parable shares the same theme of God separating good from evil at the end?
beginner.explanation: Both the parable of the Net and the parable of the Wheat and the Weeds teach that good and evil exist together now, but at the end of the age, God will perform a final separation. Both emphasise God's perfect justice at the last day.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Rich Man and Lazarus, a very poor man sat outside a rich man's gate every day. What was the poor man's name?
beginner.explanation: The poor man was named Lazarus — notably, he is the only character in any of Jesus' parables who is given a name. He sat at the rich man's gate, covered in sores, longing for scraps of food from the table.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Rich Man and Lazarus, the poor beggar named Lazarus was in terrible condition. What covered his body?
beginner.explanation: Lazarus was covered in sores, and even the dogs came and licked his wounds. He was in such a desperate state that he longed just to eat the scraps that fell from the rich man's table. His suffering was extreme.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Rich Man and Lazarus, the poor beggar Lazarus was so hungry he longed for something. What did Lazarus wish he could eat?
options: The crumbs and scraps that fell from the rich man's table
A full meal from the kitchen
Fruit from the rich man's garden
beginner.explanation: Lazarus longed to eat even the scraps that fell from the rich man's table, but apparently no one helped him. The rich man feasted lavishly every day while Lazarus starved at his gate, highlighting extreme inequality and indifference.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Rich Man and Lazarus, what animals came to the suffering beggar?
options: Dogs came and licked his sores
beginner.explanation: Dogs came and licked Lazarus's sores. In that culture, dogs were not beloved pets but unclean scavengers, so this detail adds to the picture of Lazarus's total misery and humiliation — even street dogs paid more attention to him than the rich man did.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Rich Man and Lazarus, both men eventually died. Where did the poor beggar Lazarus go after death?
options: Back to the rich man's gate
beginner.explanation: When Lazarus died, angels carried him to Abraham's side (also called 'Abraham's bosom'), a place of comfort and peace. Despite his terrible suffering on earth, Lazarus was now comforted in the afterlife.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Rich Man and Lazarus, the rich man also died. Where did the rich man find himself after death?
options: Nowhere — the story just mentions his burial
In Hades, a place of torment
beginner.explanation: The rich man died and was buried, but found himself in Hades in torment. He could see Lazarus far away at Abraham's side and cried out for help. Their earthly positions were completely reversed in the afterlife.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Rich Man and Lazarus, the rich man in torment made a request to Abraham. What did he beg Abraham to do?
options: Allow him to go back and collect his wealth
Give him a second chance at life
Send Lazarus to warn his five brothers so they wouldn't end up in the same place
Let him switch places with Lazarus
beginner.explanation: The rich man begged Abraham to send Lazarus to his father's house to warn his five brothers, so they would change their ways and avoid his fate. Abraham replied that they already had Moses and the Prophets to guide them.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Barren Fig Tree, a man had a fig tree planted in a specific location. Where was it planted?
beginner.explanation: The man had planted a fig tree in his vineyard — a common practice in ancient Israel, where fig trees and grapevines were often grown together. He came looking for fruit on it year after year but found none.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Barren Fig Tree, the owner came looking for fruit on his tree for several years. How many years had he been waiting for it to produce fruit?
beginner.explanation: The owner had come looking for fruit for three years and found none. Three years was a reasonable time to expect a fig tree to produce, so his frustration was understandable. He saw the tree as wasting good soil.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Barren Fig Tree, the frustrated owner wanted to take action after finding no fruit for years. What did he tell the gardener to do?
options: Leave it alone for another decade
Transplant it to a better location
Cut it down because it was wasting soil
beginner.explanation: The owner told the gardener 'Cut it down! Why should it use up the soil?' He saw no point in keeping a fruitless tree that was taking nutrients and space from the vineyard without producing anything useful.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Barren Fig Tree, the gardener pleaded with the owner to spare the tree. What did the gardener ask for?
options: A new tree to plant instead
One more year to dig around it and fertilise it
More workers to help tend the vineyard
beginner.explanation: The gardener said 'Sir, leave it alone for one more year, and I'll dig around it and fertilise it. If it bears fruit next year, fine! If not, then cut it down.' He interceded for the tree, asking for grace and one more chance.

beginner.question: The parable of the Barren Fig Tree teaches about God's patience and expectations. What is the main lesson?
options: Trees that don't produce fruit are worthless
God is patient and gives people time to change, but he also expects them to bear good fruit in their lives
Fig trees are difficult to grow
beginner.explanation: The parable teaches that God is patient and merciful, giving people time and opportunity to repent and live fruitful lives. But his patience has a purpose — he genuinely expects us to grow and produce good fruit. Grace is not an excuse to stay the same.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Persistent Widow, a woman kept going back to the same person to ask for justice. Who did she keep approaching?
beginner.explanation: The widow kept going to an unjust judge who 'neither feared God nor cared what people thought.' Despite his indifference, she refused to give up and kept coming back to plead her case for justice against her adversary.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Persistent Widow, the judge is described in a particular way. How was the judge characterised?
options: He neither feared God nor cared about people
Old, tired, and about to retire
beginner.explanation: Jesus deliberately described the judge as someone who 'neither feared God nor cared about people' — the worst possible judge to have. Yet even this terrible judge eventually responded to the widow's persistence, which is the whole point of the story.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Persistent Widow, the uncaring judge eventually agreed to help the widow. Why did he finally give in?
options: He had a change of heart and became a good man
Because she kept coming and wearing him out with her persistence
beginner.explanation: The judge said to himself 'Even though I don't fear God or care about people, this widow keeps bothering me. I will see that she gets justice, so she won't eventually come and attack me!' Her relentless persistence wore him down.

beginner.question: The parable of the Persistent Widow teaches a lesson about prayer. What is the main message Jesus was communicating?
options: Always pray and never give up — God, unlike the unjust judge, truly cares and will answer
Only pray once and wait quietly
beginner.explanation: Jesus' point was this: if even an unjust, uncaring judge will eventually respond to persistent requests, how much more will God — who is loving and just — respond to his people who cry out to him day and night? Keep praying and never lose heart.

beginner.question: Luke's Gospel tells us why Jesus told the parable of the Persistent Widow. What was his stated reason for telling this story?
options: To show his disciples that they should always pray and not give up
To encourage people to become lawyers
beginner.explanation: Luke 18:1 says 'Then Jesus told his disciples a parable to show them that they should always pray and not give up.' The entire purpose of the story was to encourage persistent, faithful prayer even when it seems like nothing is happening.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Two Sons, a father asked both of his sons to go work in a certain place. Where did the father ask them to work?
beginner.explanation: The father asked both sons to go and work in his vineyard that day. The vineyard was a common setting in Jesus' parables, often representing God's kingdom work. The two sons responded to their father's request very differently.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Two Sons, the first son initially refused his father's request to work in the vineyard. What happened next?
options: He later changed his mind and went to work
He stayed home and did nothing
beginner.explanation: The first son said 'I will not' — a flat refusal. But afterward he regretted his answer, changed his mind, and went to work in the vineyard as his father had asked. His actions ultimately matched what was right, despite his initial refusal.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Two Sons, the second son eagerly agreed to go work in his father's vineyard. What did he actually end up doing?
options: He sent a friend in his place
He went and worked hard all day
He said 'I will, sir' but then never actually went
He went but came back early
beginner.explanation: The second son politely said 'I will, sir,' but he never went. His words sounded respectful and obedient, but his actions told a different story. He was all talk and no follow-through.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Two Sons, Jesus asked the religious leaders which son actually did what the father wanted. Which son did the will of the father?
options: The first son, who initially said no but then went
The second son, who said yes but never went
beginner.explanation: The religious leaders correctly answered that the first son did the father's will. Despite his initial refusal, he actually went and worked. Jesus used this to confront the leaders: tax collectors and sinners who repented were entering God's kingdom ahead of them.

beginner.question: The parable of the Two Sons teaches an important lesson about obedience to God. What is the main message?
options: What matters is not what you say but what you actually do — actions speak louder than words
The second son was more polite
Always say yes to authority figures
beginner.explanation: Jesus taught that saying the right words means nothing without follow-through. The religious leaders claimed to serve God but didn't truly obey, while sinners who repented and changed their behaviour were doing God's will. Obedience is shown by actions, not just words.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Leaven (Yeast), a woman mixed yeast into a batch of flour. How many measures of flour did she use?
beginner.explanation: The woman mixed yeast into three measures (about 22 litres or 50 pounds) of flour — enough to make bread for a huge crowd. This large amount emphasises that the Kingdom of God's influence is far-reaching and transforms everything it touches.
