beginner.question: The prophet Elijah lived during the reign of wicked King Ahab and Queen Jezebel in the northern kingdom of Israel. Elijah boldly challenged the prophets of which false god to a contest on Mount Carmel?
beginner.explanation: In 1 Kings 18, Elijah challenged 450 prophets of Baal to a dramatic contest on Mount Carmel. Each side would prepare a sacrifice and call on their god to send fire. The false prophets of Baal cried out all day with no response, but God answered Elijah with fire from heaven.

beginner.question: On Mount Carmel, Elijah and the prophets of Baal each prepared a sacrifice and called on their god to send fire. The prophets of Baal shouted and danced all day with no answer. What happened when Elijah prayed?
options: God sent fire from heaven that consumed the sacrifice
beginner.explanation: In 1 Kings 18:38, after Elijah prayed, 'the fire of the Lord fell and burned up the sacrifice, the wood, the stones and the soil, and also licked up the water in the trench.' This dramatic miracle proved to all the people that the Lord is the true God.

beginner.question: Before the contest on Mount Carmel, Elijah made the miracle even more dramatic by having water poured over his sacrifice and the wood. How many times did he have water poured over it?
beginner.explanation: In 1 Kings 18:34, Elijah ordered four large jars of water to be poured on the sacrifice and wood, and he said, 'Do it again' — then 'Do it a third time.' By the end, the water ran down and filled the trench around the altar, making God's fire even more impressive.

beginner.question: During a severe famine in Israel, God told Elijah to go to a brook called Cherith. God provided food for Elijah there in an unusual way. What brought Elijah food?
beginner.explanation: In 1 Kings 17:6, ravens brought Elijah bread and meat in the morning and bread and meat in the evening, and he drank from the brook. God used these unlikely birds to sustain His prophet during the famine, showing that God can provide through any means.

beginner.question: When the brook Cherith dried up during the famine, God sent Elijah to a widow in the town of Zarephath. The widow was about to make her last meal with her remaining flour and oil. What miracle did God perform?
options: The widow found gold coins in her jar
Her jar of flour and jug of oil never ran out
Rain immediately fell on her garden
beginner.explanation: In 1 Kings 17:14-16, God promised through Elijah that 'the jar of flour will not be used up and the jug of oil will not run dry' until the famine ended. The widow obeyed, and miraculously, there was food every day for her, her son, and Elijah.

beginner.question: The widow of Zarephath who fed Elijah during the famine later experienced a tragedy — her son became very ill and died. What did Elijah do?
options: He left the town in sorrow
He prayed over the boy and God raised him back to life
He offered a sacrifice at the temple
beginner.explanation: In 1 Kings 17:21-22, Elijah stretched himself out over the boy three times and cried out to God, 'Lord my God, let this boy's life return to him!' God heard Elijah's prayer, and the boy's life returned. This was a powerful demonstration of God's power over death.

beginner.question: After Elijah's great victory over the prophets of Baal on Mount Carmel, Queen Jezebel sent a message threatening to kill him. How did Elijah react to this threat?
options: He fled into the wilderness, afraid for his life
He went to the temple to hide
beginner.explanation: In 1 Kings 19:3, Elijah was afraid and ran for his life into the wilderness. Despite just witnessing God's incredible power on Mount Carmel, Elijah became exhausted and fearful. This shows that even great prophets experienced human weakness and fear.

beginner.question: While hiding in the wilderness, Elijah was exhausted and prayed that he might die. God sent an angel to care for him and told him to travel to a specific mountain. Which mountain was Elijah told to visit?
beginner.explanation: In 1 Kings 19:8, after being fed by an angel, Elijah travelled forty days and nights to Mount Horeb (also known as Mount Sinai), the same mountain where God had given Moses the Ten Commandments. There, God spoke to Elijah and renewed his mission.

beginner.question: When Elijah stood on Mount Horeb, God passed by and there was a powerful wind, an earthquake, and a fire. But God was not in any of these. How did God finally speak to Elijah?
options: In a gentle whisper (a still, small voice)
beginner.explanation: In 1 Kings 19:12, after the wind, earthquake, and fire, there came 'a gentle whisper' (or 'a still small voice'). God spoke to Elijah in this quiet, gentle way, showing that God's presence is not always in dramatic events but often in quiet, intimate moments.

beginner.question: The prophet Elijah did not die a normal death. Instead, he was taken directly to heaven in a dramatic fashion. How did Elijah leave this earth?
options: He walked into the sea and disappeared
He was taken up in a whirlwind with a chariot and horses of fire
He vanished while praying in the temple
beginner.explanation: In 2 Kings 2:11, as Elijah and Elisha were walking together, 'a chariot of fire and horses of fire appeared and separated the two of them, and Elijah went up to heaven in a whirlwind.' Elijah is one of only two people in the Bible taken to heaven without dying.

beginner.question: Before Elijah was taken to heaven, his faithful servant and successor Elisha made a bold request. What did Elisha ask Elijah for?
options: A double portion of Elijah's spirit
beginner.explanation: In 2 Kings 2:9, Elisha asked for 'a double portion of your spirit.' A double portion was the inheritance right of the firstborn son, so Elisha was asking to be Elijah's spiritual heir and successor. Elijah said it was a difficult request but would be granted if Elisha saw him taken up.

beginner.question: Elijah confronted King Ahab after Ahab and Queen Jezebel arranged the murder of a man in order to steal his vineyard. Who was the innocent man whose vineyard they took?
beginner.explanation: In 1 Kings 21, Naboth owned a vineyard that King Ahab wanted. When Naboth refused to sell his family inheritance, Jezebel arranged false accusations and had Naboth stoned to death. Elijah then confronted Ahab, declaring that God would bring disaster on his family for this wicked act.

beginner.question: Elijah appeared in the New Testament alongside Moses during a miraculous event on a mountain, witnessed by three of Jesus' disciples. What is this event called?
beginner.explanation: In Matthew 17:1-3, Jesus took Peter, James, and John up a high mountain where He was transfigured — His face shone like the sun and His clothes became white as light. Moses and Elijah appeared and talked with Jesus, representing the Law and the Prophets.

beginner.question: When Elijah first declared a drought upon Israel as judgment for worshipping Baal, how long did the drought and famine last?
beginner.explanation: According to 1 Kings 18:1 and confirmed in James 5:17, the drought lasted about three and a half years. During this time there was no rain in Israel, causing a severe famine. The drought ended after Elijah's victory on Mount Carmel when God sent rain.

beginner.question: The last Old Testament prophet, Malachi, prophesied that God would send the prophet Elijah before the great day of the Lord. Christians believe this prophecy was fulfilled by whom?
beginner.explanation: Malachi 4:5 prophesied that God would send 'the prophet Elijah' before the day of the Lord. Jesus Himself identified John the Baptist as the fulfilment of this prophecy (Matthew 11:14), saying that John came 'in the spirit and power of Elijah' to prepare the way.

beginner.question: Elisha was the prophet who succeeded Elijah. Before becoming a prophet, Elisha was working on his family's farm when Elijah called him. What was Elisha doing when Elijah found him?
beginner.explanation: In 1 Kings 19:19, Elijah found Elisha 'ploughing with twelve yoke of oxen.' Elijah threw his cloak over Elisha, symbolically calling him to prophetic ministry. Elisha immediately left his oxen, said goodbye to his parents, and followed Elijah.

beginner.question: After Elijah was taken to heaven, Elisha picked up Elijah's fallen cloak and performed his first miracle. He struck the water with the cloak and it parted. Which body of water did Elisha part?
beginner.explanation: In 2 Kings 2:14, Elisha struck the waters of the Jordan River with Elijah's cloak, and the water divided to the right and to the left. This confirmed that the spirit of Elijah now rested on Elisha, and the other prophets who witnessed it acknowledged his authority.

beginner.question: A powerful army commander named Naaman had a terrible skin disease called leprosy. He travelled to see the prophet Elisha, hoping for a cure. What did Elisha tell Naaman to do?
options: Offer a sacrifice at the temple
Wash seven times in the Jordan River
beginner.explanation: In 2 Kings 5:10, Elisha sent a messenger telling Naaman, 'Go, wash yourself seven times in the Jordan, and your flesh will be restored and you will be cleansed.' Naaman was initially angry at such a simple instruction but eventually obeyed and was completely healed.

beginner.question: When Naaman the army commander followed Elisha's instructions and washed seven times in the Jordan River, what happened to him?
options: His skin was completely restored and healed
beginner.explanation: 2 Kings 5:14 says that Naaman 'dipped himself in the Jordan seven times, as the man of God had told him, and his flesh was restored and became clean like that of a young boy.' This miracle showed that God's power works through simple obedience and faith.

beginner.question: Naaman, the army commander who came to Elisha for healing of his leprosy, served as the commander of which foreign army?
beginner.explanation: 2 Kings 5:1 says Naaman was 'commander of the army of the king of Aram' (also known as Syria). Despite being an enemy of Israel, God showed mercy by healing Naaman through Elisha, demonstrating that God's compassion extends beyond national boundaries.

beginner.question: A poor widow came to Elisha in desperation, saying creditors were coming to take her two sons as slaves to pay her debts. She only had a small jar of olive oil. What miracle did Elisha perform?
options: He paid her debts with gold coins
He made her small amount of oil multiply to fill many jars
He turned water into wine for her to sell
He commanded the creditors to forgive her debt
beginner.explanation: In 2 Kings 4:1-7, Elisha told the widow to borrow as many empty jars as she could from her neighbours. Then she poured from her small jar, and the oil kept flowing until every jar was full. She sold the oil, paid her debts, and she and her sons lived on the rest.

beginner.question: A group of prophets were cutting down trees by the Jordan River to build a new meeting place when one of them accidentally dropped a borrowed iron axe head into the water. What miracle did Elisha perform?
options: He drained the river to find it
He made the iron axe head float to the surface
He created a new axe out of thin air
He dove in and retrieved it himself
beginner.explanation: In 2 Kings 6:5-6, the man was distressed because the axe was borrowed. Elisha cut a stick, threw it into the water at the spot where the axe had fallen, and the heavy iron axe head floated to the surface. This showed God's care even for everyday practical problems.

beginner.question: A wealthy woman in the town of Shunem regularly hosted the prophet Elisha. Elisha promised she would have a son, and she did. But years later, the boy suddenly died. What did Elisha do?
options: He mourned and held a funeral
He sent a servant with medicine
He went to the boy and raised him from the dead
He prayed and a new son was born to her
beginner.explanation: In 2 Kings 4:32-35, Elisha went to the Shunammite woman's house, lay on the dead child, mouth to mouth, eyes to eyes, hands to hands, and the child's body grew warm. The boy sneezed seven times and opened his eyes, restored to life by God's power.

beginner.question: When the king of Aram sent a large army to capture Elisha, the prophet's servant was terrified. Elisha prayed that God would open his servant's eyes. What did the servant then see?
options: A thick fog hiding them from the enemy
Hills full of horses and chariots of fire protecting them
beginner.explanation: In 2 Kings 6:17, when Elisha prayed, God opened the servant's eyes and 'he looked and saw the hills full of horses and chariots of fire all around Elisha.' This heavenly army showed that God's protection was far greater than the enemy threat.

beginner.question: Elisha once purified a pot of stew that prophets had accidentally made poisonous by adding wild gourds. What did Elisha add to the pot to make it safe to eat?
beginner.explanation: In 2 Kings 4:41, Elisha said, 'Get some flour,' and he threw it into the pot, saying, 'Serve it to the people to eat.' The stew was miraculously purified. The flour itself had no power to cure poison — it was God's power working through the prophet's actions.

beginner.question: A man brought Elisha twenty loaves of barley bread as an offering. Elisha told his servant to give it to the people to eat. The servant protested because there were a hundred men. What happened?
options: The bread multiplied and fed them all with leftovers
Elisha prayed and manna fell from heaven
They divided the bread into tiny pieces
beginner.explanation: In 2 Kings 4:43-44, despite the servant's doubt, Elisha said, 'Give it to the people to eat. For this is what the Lord says: They will eat and have some left over.' The twenty loaves fed a hundred men with food left over — foreshadowing Jesus' feeding miracles.

beginner.question: After Naaman was healed of leprosy, Elisha refused to accept any payment. But Elisha's servant Gehazi secretly chased after Naaman and asked for gifts. What punishment did Gehazi receive?
options: He was struck with Naaman's leprosy
beginner.explanation: In 2 Kings 5:27, Elisha told Gehazi, 'Naaman's leprosy will cling to you and to your descendants for ever.' Gehazi left Elisha's presence with skin as white as snow. His greed and dishonesty brought upon him the very disease that Naaman had been cured of.

beginner.question: When the dying prophet Elisha told King Joash of Israel to strike the ground with arrows, the king only struck three times and stopped. Why was Elisha angry with the king?
options: The king used the wrong arrows
Three strikes meant only three victories instead of complete defeat of the enemy
beginner.explanation: In 2 Kings 13:18-19, Elisha was angry because the king should have struck five or six times, which would have meant complete victory over Aram. By striking only three times, the king showed limited faith, and Elisha said he would only defeat Aram three times.

beginner.question: God told the prophet Jonah to go to the great city of Nineveh and warn the people about their wickedness. Instead of obeying God, what did Jonah do?
options: He ran away and boarded a ship heading to Tarshish
He sent someone else in his place
beginner.explanation: Jonah 1:3 says Jonah 'ran away from the Lord and headed for Tarshish,' which was in the opposite direction from Nineveh. He went down to the port of Joppa, found a ship bound for Tarshish, paid the fare, and sailed away, trying to flee from God's command.

beginner.question: When Jonah tried to sail to Tarshish to escape God's command, God sent a violent storm that threatened to destroy the ship. The terrified sailors asked Jonah what they should do. What did Jonah tell them?
options: Throw him overboard into the sea
beginner.explanation: In Jonah 1:12, Jonah told the sailors, 'Pick me up and throw me into the sea, and it will become calm. I know that it is my fault that this great storm has come upon you.' The sailors reluctantly did so, and the sea immediately became calm.

beginner.question: After Jonah was thrown into the raging sea, God provided a great fish (often called a whale) that swallowed Jonah whole. How long was Jonah inside the fish?
beginner.explanation: Jonah 1:17 says the Lord provided a great fish to swallow Jonah, and 'Jonah was in the belly of the fish three days and three nights.' Jesus later compared this to His own death and resurrection, saying He would be three days in the heart of the earth.

beginner.question: While trapped inside the great fish for three days, Jonah did something important that showed he was turning back to God. What did Jonah do inside the fish?
options: He prayed to God and gave thanks
beginner.explanation: Jonah chapter 2 records Jonah's prayer from inside the fish. He cried out to God in his distress, remembered God's faithfulness, and said, 'Salvation comes from the Lord.' After Jonah prayed, the Lord commanded the fish to vomit Jonah onto dry land.

beginner.question: After the great fish spit Jonah out onto dry land, God spoke to Jonah a second time, again telling him to go to Nineveh. This time, how did Jonah respond?
options: He obeyed and went to Nineveh
He asked God to send someone else
beginner.explanation: Jonah 3:3 says, 'Jonah obeyed the word of the Lord and went to Nineveh.' Having learned from his experience in the fish, Jonah this time followed God's instructions and travelled to the great city to deliver God's warning message to its people.

beginner.question: When Jonah finally arrived in Nineveh and began preaching, he warned the people that the city would be overthrown in a specific number of days. How many days did Jonah give them?
beginner.explanation: Jonah 3:4 records Jonah's message: 'Forty more days and Nineveh will be overthrown.' This short, direct warning was enough to cause the entire city — from the king to the poorest citizen — to repent of their wickedness and turn to God.

beginner.question: After hearing Jonah's warning that Nineveh would be destroyed in forty days, the people of Nineveh had a remarkable response. What did they do?
options: They laughed at him and ignored the warning
They repented, fasted, and put on sackcloth
They built a wall to protect the city
beginner.explanation: Jonah 3:5 says, 'The Ninevites believed God. A fast was proclaimed, and all of them, from the greatest to the least, put on sackcloth.' Wearing sackcloth and fasting were signs of genuine mourning and repentance in the ancient world.

beginner.question: Even the king of Nineveh responded to Jonah's warning by removing his royal robes and sitting in ashes. He issued a decree commanding all the people to do what?
options: Give up their most prized possessions to Jonah
Pray, fast, and turn from their evil ways
Build a temple to Jonah's God
beginner.explanation: In Jonah 3:7-8, the king decreed that no person or animal should eat or drink, that everyone should be covered with sackcloth, and that they should call urgently on God and give up their evil ways. This was an extraordinary display of citywide repentance.

beginner.question: When God saw that the entire city of Nineveh had repented and turned from their evil ways, what did God decide to do?
options: He showed mercy and did not destroy the city
He sent another prophet to punish them
He told Jonah to stay and become their king
beginner.explanation: Jonah 3:10 says, 'When God saw what they did and how they turned from their evil ways, he relented and did not bring on them the destruction he had threatened.' God's compassion and willingness to forgive is a central message of the book of Jonah.

beginner.question: After God spared Nineveh from destruction, the prophet Jonah was upset rather than relieved. Why was Jonah angry that God showed mercy to Nineveh?
options: He thought God was too compassionate and merciful toward Israel's enemies
He wanted to stay in Nineveh longer
He was tired from the long journey
He was angry the fish had swallowed him
beginner.explanation: In Jonah 4:2, Jonah complained to God, saying he knew God was 'a gracious and compassionate God, slow to anger and abounding in love, a God who relents from sending calamity.' Jonah had wanted Nineveh — an enemy of Israel — to be punished, not forgiven.

beginner.question: While Jonah sat outside Nineveh sulking, God provided a plant that grew up quickly to give him shade. But the next day, God sent something that destroyed the plant. What destroyed it?
beginner.explanation: In Jonah 4:7, God provided a worm at dawn the next day, which chewed the plant so that it withered. Then God sent a scorching east wind and the sun blazed on Jonah's head. God used this experience to teach Jonah about divine compassion for all people.

beginner.question: God used the lesson of the withered plant to teach Jonah an important truth. Jonah cared about a plant he did not grow. God pointed out that He cared about Nineveh's many people. How many people did God say were in Nineveh?
options: More than a hundred and twenty thousand
beginner.explanation: In Jonah 4:11, God said Nineveh had 'more than a hundred and twenty thousand people who cannot tell their right hand from their left.' God's point was clear: if Jonah could feel sorry for a plant, how much more should God care about a great city full of people?

beginner.question: The city of Nineveh, where God sent Jonah to preach repentance, was the capital of which ancient empire that was a fierce enemy of Israel?
beginner.explanation: Nineveh was the great capital of the Assyrian Empire, one of the most powerful and feared empires in the ancient world. The Assyrians were notorious for their cruelty in warfare, which helps explain why Jonah was reluctant to offer them a chance at God's mercy.

beginner.question: Jesus referenced the story of Jonah being inside the great fish for three days as a prophetic sign. According to Jesus, Jonah's experience pointed to what future event?
options: Jesus' own death, burial, and resurrection after three days
The coming of the Holy Spirit at Pentecost
beginner.explanation: In Matthew 12:40, Jesus said, 'For as Jonah was three days and three nights in the belly of a huge fish, so the Son of Man will be three days and three nights in the heart of the earth.' Jesus used Jonah's story as a sign pointing to His death and resurrection.

beginner.question: When the violent storm hit the ship Jonah was on, the desperate sailors cast lots (a form of drawing straws) to find out who was to blame for the disaster. Who did the lot fall on?
options: No one — the lots were inconclusive
beginner.explanation: In Jonah 1:7, the lot fell on Jonah, revealing that he was the cause of the storm. The sailors then questioned him, and Jonah admitted he was running away from the Lord. This confirmed what God was showing them — that the storm was a consequence of Jonah's disobedience.

beginner.question: Moses, one of the greatest prophets in the Bible, was chosen by God to lead the Israelites out of slavery. In which country were the Israelites enslaved before Moses led them to freedom?
beginner.explanation: The Israelites were enslaved in Egypt for about 400 years before God called Moses to deliver them. God used Moses to confront Pharaoh, bring ten plagues upon Egypt, and lead the people through the Red Sea to freedom, as recorded in the book of Exodus.

beginner.question: God first spoke to Moses through an amazing sight in the wilderness — a bush that was burning with fire but was not consumed. What was Moses doing at the time?
options: Tending the flock of his father-in-law Jethro
beginner.explanation: In Exodus 3:1-2, Moses was tending the flock of his father-in-law Jethro near Mount Horeb when he saw a bush that was on fire but did not burn up. Curious, Moses went closer, and God spoke to him from the bush, calling him to deliver Israel from Egypt.

beginner.question: When God called Moses at the burning bush to go to Pharaoh and demand the release of the Israelites, Moses was hesitant and said he was not a good speaker. Who did God appoint to help Moses speak?
beginner.explanation: In Exodus 4:14-16, God appointed Aaron, Moses' older brother, to serve as his spokesman. God said, 'He will speak to the people for you, and it will be as if he were your mouth and as if you were God to him.' Aaron accompanied Moses throughout the confrontation with Pharaoh.

beginner.question: Moses received the Ten Commandments directly from God on a mountain, where God wrote them on two stone tablets. On which mountain did Moses receive the Ten Commandments?
beginner.explanation: In Exodus 19-20, God called Moses up to Mount Sinai (also called Mount Horeb), which was covered in smoke and fire, and gave him the Ten Commandments. Moses spent forty days and forty nights on the mountain receiving God's law for the people of Israel.

beginner.question: God used Moses to bring ten plagues upon Egypt to convince Pharaoh to release the Israelites from slavery. What was the tenth and final plague, the most devastating of all?
beginner.explanation: The tenth plague was the death of every firstborn son in Egypt, from Pharaoh's household to the lowest servant (Exodus 12:29). The Israelites were protected by putting lamb's blood on their doorposts — the origin of the Passover. After this plague, Pharaoh finally let them go.

beginner.question: Moses led the Israelites in a miraculous crossing of a body of water, with walls of water on either side, while fleeing from Pharaoh's army. Which body of water did they cross?
beginner.explanation: In Exodus 14:21-22, God parted the Red Sea through Moses, and the Israelites walked through on dry ground with walls of water on their right and left. When the Egyptian army tried to follow, the waters collapsed and the entire army was destroyed.

beginner.question: In the wilderness, the Israelites complained about having no water. God told Moses to speak to a rock and water would flow from it. Instead, Moses struck the rock with his staff. What was the consequence of Moses' disobedience?
options: Moses was not allowed to enter the Promised Land
The Israelites had to wander for an extra year
beginner.explanation: In Numbers 20:12, God told Moses, 'Because you did not trust in me enough to honour me as holy in the sight of the Israelites, you will not bring this community into the land I give them.' Even though water still flowed, Moses' disobedience had a serious consequence.

beginner.question: Moses is described in the book of Deuteronomy as a prophet unlike any other in Israel. What made Moses' relationship with God unique compared to other prophets?
options: God spoke to him face to face
He could perform miracles without praying
He wrote more books than anyone
beginner.explanation: Deuteronomy 34:10 says, 'Since then, no prophet has risen in Israel like Moses, whom the Lord knew face to face.' While God spoke to other prophets through visions and dreams, God spoke with Moses directly, as a man speaks with his friend (Exodus 33:11).

beginner.question: Moses sent twelve spies into the Promised Land of Canaan to scout it before the Israelites entered. How many of the twelve spies came back with a faith-filled, positive report?
beginner.explanation: In Numbers 14:6-9, only Joshua and Caleb gave a positive report, urging the people to trust God and enter the land. The other ten spies spread fear, saying the people of Canaan were too strong. Because the people believed the fearful report, God made them wander forty years.

beginner.question: Before Moses died, God allowed him to see the Promised Land from the top of a mountain, even though he could not enter it. From which mountain did Moses view the Promised Land?
beginner.explanation: Deuteronomy 34:1 says, 'Moses climbed Mount Nebo from the plains of Moab to the top of Pisgah, across from Jericho.' There God showed him the entire Promised Land spread out before him. Moses died there on the mountain and was buried by God Himself.

beginner.question: The prophet Samuel was dedicated to God's service as a young boy by his mother, Hannah, who had desperately prayed for a child. Where did young Samuel grow up serving God?
beginner.explanation: In 1 Samuel 1:24-28, Hannah brought young Samuel to the tabernacle at Shiloh and presented him to the priest Eli, saying, 'I prayed for this child, and the Lord has granted me what I asked. So now I give him to the Lord.' Samuel served God in the tabernacle from that time.

beginner.question: When Samuel was a boy serving in the tabernacle, God called out his name in the night. Samuel kept running to the priest Eli, thinking Eli had called him. How many times did God call before Samuel understood it was God speaking?
beginner.explanation: In 1 Samuel 3:1-8, God called Samuel three times, and each time Samuel ran to Eli. After the third time, Eli realised it was God calling the boy and told Samuel to say, 'Speak, Lord, for your servant is listening.' The next time God called, Samuel responded as instructed.

beginner.question: The prophet Samuel was responsible for anointing the very first king of Israel, at the people's request. Who was the first king that Samuel anointed?
beginner.explanation: In 1 Samuel 10:1, Samuel anointed Saul as the first king of Israel. Saul was from the tribe of Benjamin, and he stood a head taller than everyone else. The people were eager for a king so they could be like the surrounding nations.

beginner.question: After King Saul repeatedly disobeyed God, Samuel was sent to the home of a man named Jesse in Bethlehem to anoint a new king. Which of Jesse's sons did God choose?
beginner.explanation: In 1 Samuel 16:11-13, God rejected Jesse's older sons and chose the youngest, David, who was out tending sheep. Samuel anointed David, and 'from that day on the Spirit of the Lord came powerfully upon David.' God saw David's faithful heart rather than outward appearance.

beginner.question: When Samuel went to Jesse's house to anoint a new king, he initially thought the eldest son, Eliab, was God's choice because of his tall, impressive appearance. What did God tell Samuel?
options: "The Lord looks at the heart, not the outward appearance"
beginner.explanation: In 1 Samuel 16:7, God told Samuel, 'Do not consider his appearance or his height, for I have rejected him. The Lord does not look at the things people look at. People look at the outward appearance, but the Lord looks at the heart.' This is one of the most quoted verses in the Bible.

beginner.question: The prophet Samuel served Israel in multiple leadership roles throughout his life. In addition to being a prophet, Samuel also served as Israel's last what?
beginner.explanation: Samuel served as the last of the judges of Israel before the monarchy began. 1 Samuel 7:15 says, 'Samuel continued as Israel's leader all the days of his life.' He was a prophet, priest, and judge — a unique combination of leadership roles.

beginner.question: Samuel's mother, Hannah, was so grateful when God answered her prayer for a child that she sang a beautiful prayer of praise. Hannah's prayer is recorded in which chapter of 1 Samuel?
beginner.explanation: Hannah's prayer of praise is found in 1 Samuel 2:1-10. It is a joyful song thanking God for hearing her cry and giving her a son. Her prayer influenced later praise songs in the Bible, including Mary's 'Magnificat' in Luke 1 when Mary learned she would bear Jesus.

beginner.question: Young Samuel grew up in the tabernacle under the guidance of the elderly priest Eli. Eli had two sons who were also priests, but they were wicked and dishonoured God. What were their names?
beginner.explanation: 1 Samuel 2:12 says Eli's sons, Hophni and Phinehas, were 'scoundrels who had no regard for the Lord.' They stole from the offerings and acted corruptly. God eventually judged them — both died on the same day in battle, as the prophet Samuel had foretold.

beginner.question: When the Israelites came to Samuel and demanded a king so they could be 'like all the other nations,' both Samuel and God were displeased. According to God, by asking for a king, the people were rejecting whom?
beginner.explanation: In 1 Samuel 8:7, God told Samuel, 'It is not you they have rejected, but they have rejected me as their king.' God had been Israel's true king, leading them through prophets and judges. Their demand for a human king showed they wanted to rely on human power instead of God.

beginner.question: When King Saul grew impatient waiting for Samuel to arrive and offered a burnt sacrifice himself — a duty reserved for priests — Samuel arrived and confronted him. What did Samuel tell Saul?
options: That God was pleased with his initiative
That his kingdom would not endure because he had disobeyed God
That he should offer another sacrifice immediately
That the war was already won
beginner.explanation: In 1 Samuel 13:13-14, Samuel told Saul, 'You have done a foolish thing. You have not kept the command the Lord your God gave you. Your kingdom will not endure.' Samuel then said that God had already sought out 'a man after his own heart' — referring to David.

beginner.question: Even after his death, the prophet Samuel appeared one final time. The desperate King Saul consulted a medium (a woman who claimed to speak with the dead) to bring Samuel back. What city was this medium from?
beginner.explanation: In 1 Samuel 28, Saul went to a medium at Endor. When Samuel's spirit appeared, he told Saul that God had departed from him and that the next day Saul and his sons would die in battle. This tragic event showed the consequences of Saul's long disobedience.

beginner.question: The prophet Amos was not a professional prophet or from a prophetic family. Before God called him to prophesy, what was Amos's occupation?
options: A shepherd and fig tree farmer
beginner.explanation: In Amos 7:14, the prophet said, 'I was neither a prophet nor the son of a prophet, but I was a shepherd, and I also took care of sycamore-fig trees.' God called this humble farmer to deliver powerful messages about justice and righteousness to the nation of Israel.

beginner.question: Although Amos was from the southern kingdom of Judah, God sent him to deliver His message to a different part of the divided nation. Where did God send Amos to prophesy?
beginner.explanation: Amos 7:15 records that God took Amos from tending the flock and said, 'Go, prophesy to my people Israel.' Even though Amos was from Tekoa in Judah, God sent him north to confront the sin and injustice in the prosperous northern kingdom of Israel.

beginner.question: Amos delivered one of the Bible's most powerful statements about justice and righteousness. He famously said, 'Let justice roll on like a river, and righteousness like a...' what?
beginner.explanation: Amos 5:24 declares, 'But let justice roll on like a river, righteousness like a never-failing stream!' This verse is one of the most quoted in all of Scripture and has been used throughout history as a call for social justice and fair treatment of all people.

beginner.question: Amos strongly condemned the wealthy people of Israel for mistreating a specific group. Who did Amos say the wealthy were oppressing and cheating?
beginner.explanation: Throughout the book of Amos, the prophet condemned the wealthy for trampling the poor, taking bribes, and denying justice to the oppressed (Amos 2:6-7; 5:11-12). God's message through Amos was clear: prosperity without justice and compassion is unacceptable to God.

beginner.question: The priest of Bethel, named Amaziah, tried to stop Amos from prophesying and told him to leave. What did Amaziah tell Amos to do?
options: Stop speaking and become a farmer again
Go back to Judah and prophesy there instead
Bow down to the golden calf at Bethel
Write his prophecies in a scroll and leave them behind
beginner.explanation: In Amos 7:12-13, Amaziah told Amos, 'Get out, you seer! Go back to the land of Judah. Earn your bread there and do your prophesying there. Don't prophesy any more at Bethel.' But Amos stood firm, insisting that God Himself had sent him to Israel.

beginner.question: Amos used the image of a basket of ripe summer fruit in one of his visions from God. What did the ripe fruit symbolise about the nation of Israel?
options: That Israel would enjoy a bountiful harvest
That Israel was ripe for God's judgment — the end was near
That Israel would share its wealth with the poor
That a time of celebration was coming
beginner.explanation: In Amos 8:1-2, God showed Amos a basket of ripe fruit and said, 'The time is ripe for my people Israel; I will spare them no longer.' Just as ripe fruit is at the end of its season, Israel had come to the end of God's patience with their sin and injustice.

beginner.question: Amos prophesied during the reign of King Jeroboam II, when the northern kingdom of Israel appeared prosperous and powerful. What was Amos's message about this prosperity?
options: It was a blessing from God for their faithfulness
It was built on injustice and would not last because the people had turned from God
It proved that Israel was the greatest nation on earth
It meant they should build more temples
beginner.explanation: Amos warned that Israel's outward prosperity masked deep moral and spiritual decay. The wealthy exploited the poor, justice was corrupted, and worship was empty ritual. Amos declared that God would judge the nation despite its wealth, because true prosperity requires justice and faithfulness to God.

beginner.question: God asked the prophet Hosea to do something very unusual as a living illustration of His relationship with unfaithful Israel. What did God tell Hosea to do?
options: Shave his head and wear sackcloth
Marry an unfaithful woman named Gomer
Live alone in the desert for a year
Build an altar on a mountain
beginner.explanation: In Hosea 1:2, God told Hosea, 'Go, marry a promiscuous woman.' Hosea married Gomer, who was unfaithful to him. This painful marriage was a living picture of how Israel had been unfaithful to God by worshipping idols, despite God's faithful love for them.

beginner.question: Hosea's wife Gomer was unfaithful and eventually left him. God then told Hosea to do something that illustrated God's persistent love for Israel. What did God tell Hosea to do?
options: Find her and bring her back
Forget about her and move on
Send her a letter of rebuke
beginner.explanation: In Hosea 3:1, God told Hosea, 'Go, show your love to your wife again, though she is loved by another man and is an adulteress. Love her as the Lord loves the Israelites.' This act of redemption showed that God's love pursues His people even when they are unfaithful.

beginner.question: Hosea and Gomer had children whose names were symbolic messages from God. One child was named 'Lo-Ammi.' What does this name mean?
beginner.explanation: In Hosea 1:9, God said, 'Call him Lo-Ammi, for you are not my people, and I am not your God.' This shocking name was a warning that Israel's persistent unfaithfulness could lead to God withdrawing His covenant relationship with them.

beginner.question: Hosea wrote one of the most beautiful verses about what God truly desires from His people: 'For I desire mercy, not sacrifice, and acknowledgment of God rather than burnt offerings.' This shows God values what most?
options: A genuine, loving relationship over empty rituals
beginner.explanation: Hosea 6:6 reveals that God cares more about the heart than about religious ceremonies. God wants His people to know Him, love Him, and show mercy to others — not just go through the motions of worship. Jesus quoted this verse twice in the Gospels.

beginner.question: In the book of Hosea, the prophet compared Israel's worship of false gods to a very personal kind of unfaithfulness. What metaphor did Hosea use throughout his book?
beginner.explanation: Throughout the book of Hosea, Israel's idolatry is compared to adultery. Just as Gomer was unfaithful to her husband Hosea, Israel was unfaithful to God by chasing after other gods. This powerful metaphor shows how deeply Israel's sin hurt God, who loved them as a devoted spouse.

beginner.question: Despite all of Hosea's warnings about judgment, his message also contained hope. God promised through Hosea that He would one day restore Israel and love the people freely. What did God say He would heal?
beginner.explanation: In Hosea 14:4, God promises, 'I will heal their waywardness and love them freely, for my anger has turned away from them.' Even after all of Israel's unfaithfulness, God's love remained constant, offering restoration and forgiveness to those who return to Him.

beginner.question: Another of Hosea's children was given the name 'Lo-Ruhamah.' Like the name 'Lo-Ammi,' this was a warning from God. What does 'Lo-Ruhamah' mean?
beginner.explanation: In Hosea 1:6, God told Hosea to name his daughter Lo-Ruhamah, meaning 'not loved' or 'no mercy.' This name warned Israel that God's patience had limits. However, Hosea 2:23 later promises God would reverse this, saying, 'I will show my love to the one I called Not my loved one.'

beginner.question: The prophet Micah made a famous prophecy about the exact birthplace of the future Messiah. According to Micah 5:2, in which small town would the ruler of Israel be born?
beginner.explanation: Micah 5:2 says, 'But you, Bethlehem Ephrathah, though you are small among the clans of Judah, out of you will come for me one who will be ruler over Israel.' This prophecy was fulfilled when Jesus was born in Bethlehem, as recorded in the Gospels of Matthew and Luke.

beginner.question: Micah's prophecy about the Messiah being born in Bethlehem was cited in the New Testament when the wise men came looking for the newborn king. The chief priests quoted Micah to King Herod. In which Gospel is this recorded?
beginner.explanation: In Matthew 2:4-6, when the wise men asked King Herod where the Messiah was to be born, the chief priests and teachers of the law quoted Micah 5:2, identifying Bethlehem as the birthplace. This shows how Old Testament prophecy directly guided people to find Jesus.

beginner.question: Micah summarised what God truly requires of His people in one of the most quoted verses in the Old Testament. He said God requires three things: to act justly, to love mercy, and to do what?
beginner.explanation: Micah 6:8 says, 'He has shown you, O mortal, what is good. And what does the Lord require of you? To act justly and to love mercy and to walk humbly with your God.' This verse beautifully summarises God's expectations: justice, compassion, and humble faithfulness.

beginner.question: Micah prophesied to both the northern kingdom of Israel and the southern kingdom of Judah, warning that both nations were guilty of serious sins. What did Micah primarily accuse the leaders and people of?
beginner.explanation: Micah condemned leaders who 'hate good and love evil' (Micah 3:2), prophets who led people astray for money, and judges who accepted bribes. He declared that both Israel and Judah were filled with injustice, corruption, and false worship, which would bring God's judgment.

beginner.question: Micah condemned the false prophets of his time who told people what they wanted to hear in exchange for payment. What did Micah accuse these false prophets of doing?
options: Prophesying for money and leading people astray
beginner.explanation: In Micah 3:5, 11, Micah condemned prophets who 'lead my people astray' and said 'her prophets tell fortunes for money.' These false prophets gave positive messages to whoever paid them, rather than telling the truth God had given them, regardless of how unpopular it was.

beginner.question: The prophet Micah served during the reigns of three kings of Judah. Which of these was one of the kings during Micah's ministry?
beginner.explanation: Micah 1:1 says the prophet received God's word 'during the reigns of Jotham, Ahaz and Hezekiah, kings of Judah.' King Hezekiah was a relatively good king who undertook religious reforms. Micah was a contemporary of the prophet Isaiah during this period.

beginner.question: John the Baptist was the prophet who prepared the way for Jesus' public ministry. John was related to Jesus through their mothers. What was the family connection between John's mother Elizabeth and Jesus' mother Mary?
beginner.explanation: Luke 1:36 records the angel Gabriel telling Mary that 'even Elizabeth your relative is going to have a child in her old age.' The exact nature of their relationship is not specified in Greek, but they were family. This made John and Jesus related as well.

beginner.question: John the Baptist lived a simple, rugged life in the wilderness before beginning his preaching ministry. According to the Gospels, what was John's unusual diet?
beginner.explanation: Matthew 3:4 says John's 'food was locusts and wild honey.' Combined with his clothing of camel's hair and a leather belt, John's simple lifestyle reflected the austerity of his prophetic message: a call to repentance and preparation for the coming Messiah.

beginner.question: John the Baptist baptised people in a river as a sign of repentance and turning away from sin. In which river did John perform his baptisms?
beginner.explanation: Matthew 3:6 records that people 'were baptised by him in the Jordan River.' The Jordan River was a significant location in Israel's history — it was the same river the Israelites crossed to enter the Promised Land. John's baptisms there symbolised a new spiritual beginning.

beginner.question: When Jesus came to the Jordan River to be baptised by John, John was hesitant at first. Why did John not want to baptise Jesus?
options: He was too busy with other people
He felt unworthy, saying Jesus should baptise him instead
He had run out of water
beginner.explanation: In Matthew 3:14, John tried to deter Jesus, saying, 'I need to be baptised by you, and do you come to me?' John recognised that Jesus was sinless and greater than he was. But Jesus insisted, saying it was proper to 'fulfil all righteousness,' and John agreed.

beginner.question: After John baptised Jesus in the Jordan River, the heavens opened and the Holy Spirit descended like a dove. A voice from heaven spoke. What did the voice say?
options: "This is my Son, whom I love; with him I am well pleased"
beginner.explanation: Matthew 3:17 records the voice from heaven saying, 'This is my Son, whom I love; with him I am well pleased.' This was God the Father publicly affirming Jesus' identity and mission. The event also revealed all three persons of the Trinity — Father, Son, and Holy Spirit.

beginner.question: John the Baptist used harsh language to confront the religious leaders (Pharisees and Sadducees) who came to his baptism without genuine repentance. What did John call them?
beginner.explanation: In Matthew 3:7, John said, 'You brood of vipers! Who warned you to flee from the coming wrath?' John was not impressed by their religious status and demanded they show genuine repentance through changed lives, not just outward religious observance.

beginner.question: John the Baptist gave Jesus a powerful title that highlighted Jesus' role as the ultimate sacrifice for sin. What did John call Jesus when he saw Him approaching?
options: The Lamb of God, who takes away the sin of the world
beginner.explanation: In John 1:29, when John the Baptist saw Jesus coming toward him, he declared, 'Look, the Lamb of God, who takes away the sin of the world!' This title connected Jesus to the sacrificial lambs of the Old Testament and pointed to His future sacrifice on the cross.

beginner.question: John the Baptist was eventually arrested and put in prison by the ruler Herod Antipas. Why did Herod have John arrested?
options: John criticised Herod for unlawfully marrying his brother's wife, Herodias
John tried to overthrow Herod's government
beginner.explanation: In Mark 6:17-18, Herod had John arrested because 'John had been saying to Herod, "It is not lawful for you to have your brother's wife."' John boldly spoke the truth about Herod's immoral marriage to Herodias, which led to his imprisonment and eventual execution.

beginner.question: John the Baptist's birth was miraculous. His father Zechariah was a priest who was visited by an angel while serving in the temple. Which angel appeared to Zechariah to announce the birth of John?
beginner.explanation: In Luke 1:19, the angel said, 'I am Gabriel. I stand in the presence of God, and I have been sent to speak to you and to tell you this good news.' Gabriel announced that Zechariah and his elderly wife Elizabeth would have a son who would prepare the people for the Lord.

beginner.question: When the angel Gabriel told Zechariah that he and Elizabeth would have a son, Zechariah doubted because they were both very old. What happened to Zechariah because of his doubt?
options: He was struck mute and could not speak until the baby was born
He fell into a deep sleep
beginner.explanation: In Luke 1:20, Gabriel said, 'You will be silent and not able to speak until the day this happens, because you did not believe my words.' Zechariah was unable to speak for the entire pregnancy. When the baby was born and named John, Zechariah's speech was immediately restored.

beginner.question: The book of Malachi is the very last book of the Old Testament. Malachi delivered God's message to the people of Israel after they had returned from exile. What was his main criticism?
options: The people were building too many temples
The people and priests had become careless and unfaithful in worship
The people were too generous with their offerings
The people refused to rebuild the walls of Jerusalem
beginner.explanation: Malachi rebuked the returned exiles and especially the priests for offering blemished sacrifices, withholding tithes, and being spiritually apathetic. God asked through Malachi, 'Where is the respect due to me?' The people had become complacent in their relationship with God.

beginner.question: In the book of Malachi, God challenged the people to test Him in a specific area. He told them to bring their full tithes into the storehouse and promised He would open the 'windows of' what and pour out blessing?
beginner.explanation: Malachi 3:10 says, 'Bring the whole tithe into the storehouse... Test me in this, says the Lord Almighty, and see if I will not throw open the floodgates of heaven and pour out so much blessing that there will not be room enough to store it.' This is a rare invitation from God to test Him.

beginner.question: Malachi rebuked the priests because they were offering unacceptable sacrifices to God. What was wrong with the animals they were bringing as offerings?
options: The animals were blind, lame, and sick instead of the best of the flock
The animals were the wrong colour
beginner.explanation: In Malachi 1:8, God says, 'When you offer blind animals for sacrifice, is that not wrong? When you sacrifice lame or diseased animals, is that not wrong?' God's law required the best, unblemished animals as offerings. Offering the worst showed contempt for God.

beginner.question: Malachi prophesied that God would send a messenger to prepare the way before the Lord. Christians understand this as a prophecy about which New Testament figure?
beginner.explanation: Malachi 3:1 says, 'I will send my messenger, who will prepare the way before me.' Jesus identified John the Baptist as this messenger in Matthew 11:10. John's ministry of calling people to repentance prepared the nation of Israel for the arrival of Jesus.

beginner.question: The book of Malachi is the last book of the Old Testament, and after it, there is a long period of silence before the events of the New Testament begin. Roughly how many years passed between Malachi and the birth of Jesus?
beginner.explanation: After Malachi, there were approximately 400 years before the events of the New Testament — a period sometimes called the 'intertestamental period' or the 'silent years.' During this time, no prophetic voice spoke to Israel, until John the Baptist broke the silence.

beginner.question: The very last prophecy of the Old Testament, found at the end of Malachi, promises that God will send which prophet before the great and dreadful day of the Lord?
beginner.explanation: Malachi 4:5 says, 'See, I will send the prophet Elijah to you before that great and dreadful day of the Lord comes.' Jesus said in Matthew 11:14 that John the Baptist came 'in the spirit and power of Elijah,' fulfilling this final Old Testament prophecy.

beginner.question: The prophet Isaiah had a vision of God sitting on a high throne in the temple, surrounded by angels called seraphim who sang 'Holy, holy, holy is the Lord Almighty.'
beginner.explanation: Isaiah 6:1-3 describes exactly this scene. Isaiah saw the Lord seated on a throne, high and exalted, and seraphim were flying above Him, calling to one another, 'Holy, holy, holy is the Lord Almighty; the whole earth is full of His glory.'

beginner.question: Isaiah prophesied that the Messiah would be born of a virgin and the child would be called Immanuel, which means 'God with us.'
beginner.explanation: Isaiah 7:14 says, 'The virgin will conceive and give birth to a son, and will call him Immanuel.' The name Immanuel literally means 'God with us.' Christians believe this prophecy was fulfilled in the birth of Jesus Christ.

beginner.question: Isaiah prophesied that the coming Messiah would be called 'Wonderful Counsellor, Mighty God, Everlasting Father, Prince of Peace.'
beginner.explanation: Isaiah 9:6 lists these four magnificent titles for the promised Messiah: Wonderful Counsellor, Mighty God, Everlasting Father, and Prince of Peace. These titles describe the divine nature and character of the Saviour that God would send.

beginner.question: Isaiah prophesied that the Messiah would be born in Jerusalem, the capital city of Israel.
beginner.explanation: Isaiah did not prophesy Jerusalem as the Messiah's birthplace. It was the prophet Micah who prophesied that the Messiah would be born in Bethlehem (Micah 5:2), a small and humble town. This was fulfilled when Jesus was born in Bethlehem.

beginner.question: When God first called Isaiah to be a prophet, a seraph touched Isaiah's lips with a burning coal from the altar to cleanse him and make him ready to serve.
beginner.explanation: In Isaiah 6:6-7, after Isaiah said he was 'a man of unclean lips,' a seraph flew to him with a live coal taken from the altar. The seraph touched Isaiah's mouth and said, 'Your guilt is taken away and your sin atoned for,' purifying him for prophetic service.

beginner.question: Isaiah was a prophet who served primarily in the northern kingdom of Israel, preaching to the ten northern tribes.
beginner.explanation: Isaiah prophesied primarily to the southern kingdom of Judah, not the northern kingdom. Isaiah 1:1 says he received visions 'concerning Judah and Jerusalem' during the reigns of kings Uzziah, Jotham, Ahaz, and Hezekiah — all kings of Judah.

beginner.question: Isaiah prophesied about a future time of peace when nations would 'beat their swords into ploughshares and their spears into pruning hooks.'
beginner.explanation: Isaiah 2:4 contains this famous peace prophecy: 'They will beat their swords into ploughshares and their spears into pruning hooks. Nation will not take up sword against nation, nor will they train for war any more.' This envisions a future era of perfect peace under God's rule.

beginner.question: Isaiah chapter 53, which describes a 'Suffering Servant' who is pierced for our transgressions, was written about the life of King David, not the Messiah.
beginner.explanation: While some Jewish interpreters have debated its meaning, Christians understand Isaiah 53 as a prophecy about the Messiah — Jesus Christ. The chapter describes someone who was 'pierced for our transgressions, crushed for our iniquities,' which matches Jesus' suffering and crucifixion.

beginner.question: Isaiah prophesied that a 'voice crying in the wilderness' would prepare the way for the Lord. This was fulfilled by John the Baptist in the New Testament.
beginner.explanation: Isaiah 40:3 says, 'A voice of one calling: In the wilderness prepare the way for the Lord.' All four Gospels quote this passage and identify John the Baptist as the fulfilment. John preached repentance in the wilderness before Jesus began His public ministry.

beginner.question: When God first called Isaiah to be a prophet, Isaiah eagerly said 'Here am I, send me!' without any hesitation or feelings of unworthiness.
beginner.explanation: Isaiah did eventually say 'Here am I. Send me!' (Isaiah 6:8), but only after first crying out, 'Woe to me! I am ruined! For I am a man of unclean lips' (Isaiah 6:5). He needed to be cleansed by the burning coal before he felt ready to volunteer for God's mission.

beginner.question: The book of Isaiah is one of the longest books in the Old Testament, containing 66 chapters.
beginner.explanation: Isaiah does indeed contain 66 chapters, making it one of the longest books in the entire Bible. Some scholars have noted an interesting parallel: the Bible has 66 books, and Isaiah has 66 chapters, with the first 39 chapters focusing on judgment and the last 27 on comfort and hope.

beginner.question: Isaiah prophesied that the Messiah would come as a powerful military conqueror who would immediately overthrow all earthly kingdoms by force.
beginner.explanation: While some prophecies describe the Messiah's future reign, Isaiah 53 describes the Messiah as a 'Suffering Servant' who would be 'despised and rejected,' 'led like a lamb to the slaughter.' Jesus' first coming was as a humble servant, not a military conqueror.

beginner.question: Jeremiah is often referred to as 'the weeping prophet' because he was deeply grieved over the sins of his people and the coming destruction of Jerusalem.
beginner.explanation: Jeremiah earned this title because of his deep sorrow over Judah's sin and coming judgment. Jeremiah 9:1 captures his anguish: 'Oh, that my head were a spring of water and my eyes a fountain of tears! I would weep day and night for the slain of my people.'

beginner.question: God told Jeremiah that He knew him and set him apart as a prophet even before Jeremiah was born.
beginner.explanation: Jeremiah 1:5 records God saying, 'Before I formed you in the womb I knew you, before you were born I set you apart; I appointed you as a prophet to the nations.' This powerful verse shows that God had a plan and purpose for Jeremiah from before his birth.

beginner.question: Jeremiah prophesied that the people of Judah would be taken into exile in Babylon for a period of 70 years.
beginner.explanation: Jeremiah 25:11 says, 'This whole country will become a desolate wasteland, and these nations will serve the king of Babylon for seventy years.' This prophecy was fulfilled — the exile lasted approximately 70 years before the Persian king Cyrus allowed the Jews to return.

beginner.question: When God called Jeremiah to be a prophet, Jeremiah eagerly accepted and said he was fully ready and able to speak on God's behalf.
beginner.explanation: Jeremiah did not eagerly accept. In Jeremiah 1:6, he protested, saying, 'Alas, Sovereign Lord! I do not know how to speak; I am too young.' God had to reassure Jeremiah, promising to be with him and put the right words in his mouth.

beginner.question: Jeremiah was thrown into a muddy cistern (a deep pit used to collect water) by officials who were angry about his prophecies of doom for Jerusalem.
beginner.explanation: In Jeremiah 38:6, officials lowered Jeremiah by ropes into the cistern of Malkijah, where he sank into the mud. They wanted to silence his message of surrender to Babylon. Fortunately, an Ethiopian official named Ebed-Melek rescued him with ropes and rags.

beginner.question: God told Jeremiah to buy a field during the siege of Jerusalem as a sign that the people would one day return to their land after exile.
beginner.explanation: In Jeremiah 32:6-15, God told Jeremiah to buy a field in Anathoth, even while the Babylonian army was besieging Jerusalem. This seemingly foolish purchase was a sign of hope — God promised, 'Houses, fields and vineyards will again be bought in this land.'

beginner.question: Jeremiah is traditionally believed to have written the book of Lamentations, which mourns the destruction of Jerusalem.
beginner.explanation: Lamentations is traditionally attributed to the prophet Jeremiah. The book consists of five poems expressing deep grief over the destruction of Jerusalem and the temple by the Babylonians in 586 BC. Its mournful tone matches Jeremiah's reputation as 'the weeping prophet.'

beginner.question: Jeremiah prophesied that Jerusalem would never be destroyed because God would always protect His holy city from invaders.
beginner.explanation: Jeremiah actually prophesied the opposite — that Jerusalem would be destroyed by the Babylonians as punishment for the people's sin (Jeremiah 25:9-11). This unpopular message is precisely why Jeremiah was persecuted, imprisoned, and thrown into a cistern by officials who rejected his warning.

beginner.question: Jeremiah prophesied about a 'new covenant' in which God would write His law on people's hearts, rather than on stone tablets.
beginner.explanation: Jeremiah 31:33 says, 'I will put my law in their minds and write it on their hearts. I will be their God, and they will be my people.' This 'new covenant' prophecy is one of the most important in the Old Testament. Christians believe it was fulfilled through Jesus Christ.

beginner.question: Jeremiah was a popular and well-loved prophet who was celebrated by the people of Judah for his encouraging messages.
beginner.explanation: Jeremiah was widely rejected and persecuted, not celebrated. He was beaten, put in stocks (Jeremiah 20:2), thrown into a cistern (Jeremiah 38:6), and imprisoned. His message of coming judgment was deeply unpopular, and the people and leaders of Judah consistently opposed him.

beginner.question: Jeremiah primarily prophesied to the northern kingdom of Israel, warning them about the Assyrian invasion.
beginner.explanation: Jeremiah prophesied primarily to the southern kingdom of Judah, not the northern kingdom of Israel. By Jeremiah's time, the northern kingdom had already fallen to Assyria over a century earlier (722 BC). Jeremiah warned Judah about the coming Babylonian conquest.

beginner.question: The prophet Ezekiel had a famous vision of a valley filled with dry bones that came back to life, symbolising the future restoration of the nation of Israel.
beginner.explanation: In Ezekiel 37:1-14, God showed Ezekiel a valley of dry bones and told him to prophesy to them. The bones came together, were covered with flesh, and came to life. God explained that the bones represented Israel, and He would restore the nation from exile.

beginner.question: Ezekiel was both a priest and a prophet, giving him a unique perspective on both temple worship and God's messages.
beginner.explanation: Ezekiel 1:3 identifies him as 'Ezekiel the priest, the son of Buzi.' His priestly background meant he was deeply familiar with the temple, sacrifices, and worship rituals, which influenced many of his visions, including his detailed vision of a future temple in chapters 40-48.

beginner.question: Ezekiel received his prophetic calling and delivered his messages while living in exile in Babylon, far from his homeland of Judah.
beginner.explanation: Ezekiel was among the Jews taken captive to Babylon around 597 BC. He received his first vision by the Kebar River in Babylon (Ezekiel 1:1-3) and ministered to the exiled Jewish community there throughout his prophetic career, never returning to Jerusalem.

beginner.question: In Ezekiel's vision of the valley of dry bones, the bones came to life on their own without Ezekiel needing to do anything.
beginner.explanation: God specifically commanded Ezekiel to prophesy to the bones (Ezekiel 37:4) and then to prophesy to the breath/wind (Ezekiel 37:9). It was through Ezekiel's obedient prophesying, empowered by God's word, that the bones came together, were covered with flesh, and received the breath of life.

beginner.question: God frequently addressed the prophet Ezekiel as 'Son of man' throughout the book — a title used more than 90 times.
beginner.explanation: God called Ezekiel 'son of man' (or 'mortal') over 90 times in the book. This title emphasised Ezekiel's humanity in contrast to God's divine power and glory. Jesus later used 'Son of Man' as His own favourite title, echoing this Old Testament usage.

beginner.question: Ezekiel's first vision, described in chapter 1 of his book, included four living creatures and wheels within wheels, seen by the Kebar River.
beginner.explanation: Ezekiel 1:1-28 describes this extraordinary vision. By the Kebar River in Babylon, Ezekiel saw four living creatures, each with four faces and four wings, accompanied by gleaming wheels that moved in any direction. Above them was the likeness of the glory of God.

beginner.question: Ezekiel prophesied from the city of Jerusalem and never left the holy city during his entire ministry.
beginner.explanation: Ezekiel never prophesied from Jerusalem. He was taken into exile in Babylon around 597 BC, before Jerusalem was destroyed, and he received all his visions and delivered all his prophecies while living among the exiles by the Kebar River in Babylon (Ezekiel 1:1-3).

beginner.question: Ezekiel described God's glory departing from the temple in Jerusalem, symbolising that God was withdrawing His presence because of the people's persistent sin.
beginner.explanation: In Ezekiel 10-11, Ezekiel witnessed a vision of God's glory — His visible, radiant presence — leaving the temple and moving to the Mount of Olives east of the city. This departure signalled that God's protective presence was being withdrawn due to the people's idolatry and sin.

beginner.question: Ezekiel's vision of the valley of dry bones was about a literal army of skeleton warriors that God planned to raise up to fight against Babylon.
beginner.explanation: The dry bones vision was symbolic, not literal. Ezekiel 37:11 explains that the bones represent 'the whole house of Israel,' who felt hopeless in exile, saying, 'Our bones are dried up and our hope is gone.' The vision promised that God would restore the nation spiritually and physically.

beginner.question: Ezekiel was a prophet in the northern kingdom of Israel who ministered before the Assyrian conquest in 722 BC.
beginner.explanation: Ezekiel was not from the northern kingdom and did not minister before the Assyrian conquest. He was from the southern kingdom of Judah and prophesied during the Babylonian exile, roughly 593-571 BC — about 130 years after the northern kingdom fell to Assyria.

beginner.question: Ezekiel performed many dramatic symbolic acts as God commanded, including lying on his side for extended periods to represent the years of Israel and Judah's sin.
beginner.explanation: In Ezekiel 4:4-6, God told Ezekiel to lie on his left side for 390 days to represent Israel's years of sin, then on his right side for 40 days to represent Judah's years of sin. These dramatic visual demonstrations were meant to capture the attention of the exiles.

beginner.question: Daniel was a young man from the kingdom of Judah who was taken captive to Babylon and served in the royal court of King Nebuchadnezzar.
beginner.explanation: Daniel 1:1-6 records that Daniel was among the young men from Judah's royal and noble families taken to Babylon by Nebuchadnezzar. He was selected for training in the king's court because of his intelligence and good appearance, and he served faithfully for decades.

beginner.question: When Daniel and his friends were offered the king's rich food in Babylon, they chose to eat only vegetables and water instead. After a test period, they looked healthier than those who ate the royal food.
beginner.explanation: In Daniel 1:12-15, Daniel proposed a ten-day test of eating only vegetables and water. At the end of the ten days, Daniel and his friends 'looked healthier and better nourished than any of the young men who ate the royal food.' God blessed their faithfulness.

beginner.question: Daniel refused to interpret King Nebuchadnezzar's dream about the giant statue because he was afraid of the king's reaction.
beginner.explanation: Daniel did not refuse. In Daniel 2:27-28, after praying to God for revelation, Daniel went before the king and said, 'There is a God in heaven who reveals mysteries.' He then faithfully interpreted the entire dream, giving credit to God rather than his own wisdom.

beginner.question: Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego — Daniel's three friends — were thrown into a fiery furnace because they refused to bow down and worship King Nebuchadnezzar's golden statue.
beginner.explanation: Daniel 3:12-21 records that Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego refused to worship the golden image. Nebuchadnezzar was furious and ordered the furnace heated seven times hotter. The three men boldly declared, 'The God we serve is able to deliver us... but even if he does not, we will not worship your gods.'

beginner.question: When Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego were in the fiery furnace, King Nebuchadnezzar saw a mysterious fourth figure walking with them in the fire.
beginner.explanation: In Daniel 3:25, Nebuchadnezzar exclaimed, 'Look! I see four men walking around in the fire, unbound and unharmed, and the fourth looks like a son of the gods.' This miraculous fourth figure — believed by many to be an angel or a divine appearance — protected the three men from all harm.

beginner.question: Daniel was thrown into the lions' den as punishment for stealing treasure from the king's palace.
beginner.explanation: Daniel was thrown into the lions' den for praying to God, not for stealing. Jealous officials tricked King Darius into signing a decree that no one could pray to anyone but the king for thirty days. When Daniel continued to pray to God three times daily, he was arrested (Daniel 6:10-16).

beginner.question: God sent an angel who shut the mouths of the lions, and Daniel was completely unharmed after spending the night in the lions' den.
beginner.explanation: Daniel 6:22 records Daniel telling the king, 'My God sent his angel, and he shut the mouths of the lions. They have not hurt me, because I was found innocent in his sight.' When Daniel was lifted out of the den, not a scratch was found on his body.

beginner.question: Daniel interpreted the mysterious writing on the wall — 'Mene, Mene, Tekel, Parsin' — at King Belshazzar's feast in Babylon.
beginner.explanation: In Daniel 5:25-28, Daniel read and interpreted the writing that had appeared on the wall during Belshazzar's feast. He explained that God had numbered, weighed, and divided Belshazzar's kingdom. That very night, Belshazzar was killed and Babylon fell to the Medes and Persians.

beginner.question: After Daniel survived the lions' den unharmed, he retired from royal service and lived quietly for the rest of his life.
beginner.explanation: Daniel did not retire. Daniel 6:28 says, 'So Daniel prospered during the reign of Darius and the reign of Cyrus the Persian.' Daniel continued to serve in positions of high authority, demonstrating his faithfulness throughout multiple changes of government in Babylon.

beginner.question: The writing on the wall at Belshazzar's feast — 'Mene, Mene, Tekel, Parsin' — meant that Belshazzar's kingdom had been numbered, weighed, and found wanting, and would be divided.
beginner.explanation: Daniel 5:26-28 gives the interpretation: 'Mene' — God has numbered the days of your reign and brought it to an end. 'Tekel' — you have been weighed on the scales and found wanting. 'Parsin' — your kingdom is divided and given to the Medes and Persians.

beginner.question: Daniel's three friends — Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego — were severely burned in the fiery furnace and did not survive the ordeal.
beginner.explanation: They survived completely unharmed. Daniel 3:27 says that the fire had not harmed their bodies, 'nor was a hair of their heads singed; their robes were not scorched, and there was no smell of fire on them.' God miraculously preserved them in the midst of the flames.

beginner.question: Daniel continued to pray to God three times every day, even after a law was passed making it illegal to pray to anyone except the king.
beginner.explanation: Daniel 6:10 says that even after Daniel learned the decree had been published, 'he went home to his upstairs room where the windows opened toward Jerusalem. Three times a day he got down on his knees and prayed, giving thanks to his God, just as he had done before.'

beginner.question: As Daniel had prophesied, King Nebuchadnezzar temporarily lost his sanity and lived outdoors like a wild animal, eating grass like an ox, until he acknowledged God's sovereignty.
beginner.explanation: Daniel 4:33 records that Nebuchadnezzar 'was driven away from people and ate grass like the ox. His body was drenched with the dew of heaven until his hair grew like the feathers of an eagle and his nails like the claws of a bird.' After seven periods, his sanity returned and he praised God.

beginner.question: Daniel served under only one king during his entire time in Babylon and never experienced a change of government.
beginner.explanation: Daniel served under multiple rulers. He served under Nebuchadnezzar, then Belshazzar, then Darius the Mede, and into the reign of Cyrus the Persian (Daniel 1:21; 5:30-31; 6:28). His long career spanned the entire Babylonian exile and beyond.

beginner.question: Daniel had a vision of four great beasts coming out of the sea, which represented four successive world kingdoms or empires.
beginner.explanation: In Daniel 7:3-7, Daniel saw four beasts emerge from the sea: a lion, a bear, a leopard, and a terrifying fourth beast. Daniel 7:17 explains, 'The four great beasts are four kings that will rise from the earth.' Scholars generally identify them as Babylon, Medo-Persia, Greece, and Rome.

beginner.question: The Babylonian name given to Daniel by King Nebuchadnezzar's officials was 'Shadrach.'
beginner.explanation: Daniel's Babylonian name was Belteshazzar, not Shadrach. Shadrach was the Babylonian name given to Daniel's friend Hananiah. The other friends — Mishael and Azariah — were renamed Meshach and Abednego respectively (Daniel 1:7).

beginner.question: The prophet Elijah challenged 450 prophets of the false god Baal to a dramatic contest on Mount Carmel to prove who was the true God.
beginner.explanation: In 1 Kings 18:19-24, Elijah boldly challenged 450 prophets of Baal. Each side would prepare a sacrifice and call on their god to send fire. The prophets of Baal shouted and danced all day with no response, but when Elijah prayed, God sent fire from heaven immediately.

beginner.question: During the contest on Mount Carmel, God answered Elijah's prayer by sending fire from heaven that consumed the sacrifice, the wood, the stones, and even the water in the trench.
beginner.explanation: 1 Kings 18:38 says, 'Then the fire of the Lord fell and burned up the sacrifice, the wood, the stones and the soil, and also licked up the water in the trench.' This spectacular miracle left no doubt that the Lord, not Baal, was the true God of Israel.

beginner.question: During the famine in Israel, God sent eagles to bring Elijah bread and meat at the brook Cherith.
beginner.explanation: God sent ravens, not eagles, to feed Elijah. 1 Kings 17:6 says, 'The ravens brought him bread and meat in the morning and bread and meat in the evening, and he drank from the brook.' Ravens are scavenger birds, making this an even more unlikely and miraculous provision from God.

beginner.question: God provided for the prophet Elijah during a severe famine by sending ravens to bring him bread and meat at the brook Cherith.
beginner.explanation: 1 Kings 17:4-6 records that God told Elijah to go to the brook Cherith and said, 'I have directed the ravens to supply you with food there.' The ravens faithfully brought Elijah bread and meat morning and evening until the brook dried up from the drought.

beginner.question: Elijah was taken up to heaven in a whirlwind, accompanied by a chariot of fire and horses of fire, without ever experiencing death.
beginner.explanation: 2 Kings 2:11 says, 'As they were walking along and talking together, suddenly a chariot of fire and horses of fire appeared and separated the two of them, and Elijah went up to heaven in a whirlwind.' Elijah is one of only two people in the Bible (along with Enoch) taken to heaven without dying.

beginner.question: After Queen Jezebel threatened to kill him following his victory on Mount Carmel, Elijah bravely stayed and confronted her face to face.
beginner.explanation: Elijah did not stay and confront Jezebel. 1 Kings 19:3 says 'Elijah was afraid and ran for his life.' Despite his great victory on Mount Carmel, the prophet fled into the wilderness in fear and exhaustion, even asking God to let him die. This shows even great prophets had moments of weakness.

beginner.question: When Elijah was hiding at Mount Horeb (Sinai), God spoke to him not in the powerful wind, earthquake, or fire, but in a gentle whisper.
beginner.explanation: 1 Kings 19:11-12 describes a powerful wind, an earthquake, and a fire, but God was not in any of these dramatic events. Instead, 'after the fire came a gentle whisper' — and it was in this quiet, still voice that God spoke to the exhausted and fearful prophet.

beginner.question: The widow of Zarephath's jar of flour and jug of oil miraculously never ran out during the entire famine, just as Elijah had promised through God's word.
beginner.explanation: 1 Kings 17:16 confirms, 'The jar of flour was not used up and the jug of oil did not run dry, in keeping with the word of the Lord spoken by Elijah.' This ongoing miracle sustained the widow, her son, and Elijah throughout the devastating famine.

beginner.question: Elijah died peacefully in his sleep and was buried by his servant Elisha in the land of Israel.
beginner.explanation: Elijah did not die at all in the traditional sense. 2 Kings 2:11 records that he was taken directly to heaven in a whirlwind, with a chariot and horses of fire. There was no death and no burial. Elisha watched him go and then picked up Elijah's fallen cloak.

beginner.question: Elijah raised the dead son of the widow of Zarephath back to life through prayer.
beginner.explanation: In 1 Kings 17:21-22, after the widow's son died, Elijah stretched himself over the boy three times and cried out, 'Lord my God, let this boy's life return to him!' The Lord heard Elijah's prayer, and the boy's life returned. Elijah carried him downstairs and gave him back to his mother.

beginner.question: Before Elijah was taken to heaven, his servant and successor Elisha asked for a double portion of Elijah's spirit.
beginner.explanation: In 2 Kings 2:9, when Elijah asked what he could do for Elisha before being taken away, Elisha replied, 'Let me inherit a double portion of your spirit.' A double portion was the inheritance right of the firstborn, so Elisha was asking to be Elijah's primary spiritual heir.

beginner.question: Elijah fully approved of King Ahab and Queen Jezebel seizing Naboth's vineyard, saying the king had the right to take whatever land he wanted.
beginner.explanation: Elijah did the exact opposite — he condemned Ahab harshly. In 1 Kings 21:19, Elijah told Ahab, 'Have you not murdered a man and seized his property? In the place where dogs licked up Naboth's blood, dogs will lick up your blood.' Elijah declared God's judgment on Ahab's family for this injustice.
