beginner.question: Elijah only poured water on his sacrifice once before calling on God to send fire during the contest on Mount Carmel.
beginner.explanation: Elijah had water poured on the sacrifice three times, not once. 1 Kings 18:34 records Elijah saying, 'Do it again' and 'Do it a third time.' Four large jars of water were poured each time, thoroughly soaking the sacrifice, the wood, and filling the trench — making God's fire even more miraculous.

beginner.question: After Elijah's great victory over the prophets of Baal on Mount Carmel, all of Israel permanently turned back to God and never worshipped idols again.
beginner.explanation: While the people initially fell on their faces and declared 'The Lord — he is God!' (1 Kings 18:39), this did not lead to permanent faithfulness. Queen Jezebel continued promoting Baal worship and even threatened Elijah's life. Israel continued to struggle with idolatry for many more generations.

beginner.question: Elijah appeared alongside Moses when Jesus was transfigured on a mountain, as witnessed by Peter, James, and John.
beginner.explanation: Matthew 17:1-3 records that Jesus took Peter, James, and John up a high mountain where 'his face shone like the sun, and his clothes became as white as the light.' Moses and Elijah appeared, talking with Jesus, representing the Law (Moses) and the Prophets (Elijah).

beginner.question: Elisha was ploughing a field with twelve yoke of oxen when the prophet Elijah threw his cloak on him, calling him to become a prophet.
beginner.explanation: 1 Kings 19:19 says Elijah found Elisha 'ploughing with twelve yoke of oxen, and he himself was driving the twelfth pair. Elijah went up to him and threw his cloak around him.' Elisha immediately understood this as a calling and left his farming life to follow Elijah.

beginner.question: Naaman, a powerful commander of the army of Aram (Syria), was healed of his leprosy after washing seven times in the Jordan River, as the prophet Elisha had instructed.
beginner.explanation: 2 Kings 5:14 records that Naaman 'went down and dipped himself in the Jordan seven times, as the man of God had told him, and his flesh was restored and became clean like that of a young boy.' Though Naaman was initially angry at the simple instruction, his obedience led to miraculous healing.

beginner.question: Elisha told Naaman the army commander to wash in the Nile River to be healed of his leprosy.
beginner.explanation: Elisha told Naaman to wash in the Jordan River, not the Nile. 2 Kings 5:10 records Elisha's instruction: 'Go, wash yourself seven times in the Jordan.' Naaman was actually offended, saying the rivers of Damascus were better than the waters of Israel, but eventually obeyed and was healed.

beginner.question: Elisha performed the miracle of making a heavy iron axe head float on the surface of the water after it fell into a river.
beginner.explanation: In 2 Kings 6:5-6, a prophet's borrowed axe head fell into the Jordan River. Elisha cut a stick, threw it where the axe had fallen, and the iron floated to the surface. This miracle showed God's care even in everyday practical situations — the man had been worried because the axe was borrowed.

beginner.question: Elisha raised the dead son of the Shunammite woman back to life after the boy suddenly fell ill and died.
beginner.explanation: In 2 Kings 4:32-35, Elisha went to the Shunammite woman's house, found the dead boy on his bed, and lay on the child. The child's body grew warm, and after Elisha stretched out on him again, the boy sneezed seven times and opened his eyes, miraculously restored to life.

beginner.question: Before becoming a prophet, Elisha was a wealthy merchant who traded goods between Israel and neighbouring nations.
beginner.explanation: Elisha was not a merchant. 1 Kings 19:19 tells us he was a farmer, 'ploughing with twelve yoke of oxen,' when Elijah called him. Having twelve yoke of oxen suggests his family was reasonably prosperous farmers, but he was working the land, not trading goods.

beginner.question: When an enemy army came to capture Elisha, he prayed for his terrified servant's eyes to be opened, and the servant saw the hills full of horses and chariots of fire protecting them.
beginner.explanation: In 2 Kings 6:17, Elisha prayed, 'Open his eyes, Lord, so that he may see.' God opened the servant's eyes, and 'he looked and saw the hills full of horses and chariots of fire all around Elisha.' This revealed that God's angelic army was far greater than the enemy force.

beginner.question: Elisha miraculously multiplied a widow's small jar of oil, filling many borrowed jars, so she could sell the oil and pay off her debts.
beginner.explanation: In 2 Kings 4:1-7, a widow's creditors were about to take her sons as slaves. Elisha told her to borrow empty jars from neighbours and pour from her small jar. The oil kept flowing until every jar was full. She sold the oil, paid her debts, and had enough left over to live on.

beginner.question: Elisha's servant Gehazi was rewarded by God for his honesty and integrity after Naaman the army commander was healed of leprosy.
beginner.explanation: Gehazi was punished, not rewarded. After Elisha refused payment from Naaman, Gehazi secretly chased Naaman and lied to get gifts for himself. When Elisha confronted him, Gehazi was struck with Naaman's leprosy (2 Kings 5:27). His greed and dishonesty brought severe consequences.

beginner.question: After Elijah was taken to heaven, Elisha picked up Elijah's fallen cloak, struck the Jordan River, and the waters parted for him to cross.
beginner.explanation: In 2 Kings 2:14, Elisha took the cloak that had fallen from Elijah and struck the water, saying, 'Where now is the Lord, the God of Elijah?' The water divided to the right and left, and Elisha crossed over. This confirmed that the spirit of Elijah now rested on Elisha.

beginner.question: Elisha performed only a small number of miracles during his ministry — far fewer than his predecessor Elijah.
beginner.explanation: Elisha actually performed roughly twice as many recorded miracles as Elijah. His miracles included healing Naaman, raising the Shunammite's son, multiplying oil, purifying stew, making an axe head float, multiplying bread, and many others. He received the 'double portion' of Elijah's spirit that he had asked for.

beginner.question: Elisha multiplied twenty loaves of barley bread to feed one hundred men, with food left over, in a miracle that foreshadowed Jesus' feeding of the multitudes.
beginner.explanation: In 2 Kings 4:42-44, a man brought twenty loaves to Elisha. The servant asked, 'How can I set this before a hundred men?' But Elisha said God promised they would eat and have some left over. They ate and there was food remaining, just as God had said.

beginner.question: Elisha purified a pot of poisonous stew by adding salt to it, making it safe for the prophets to eat.
beginner.explanation: Elisha added flour, not salt, to purify the poisonous stew. In 2 Kings 4:41, Elisha said, 'Get some flour,' and he put it into the pot and said, 'Serve it to the people to eat.' The stew was miraculously made safe. The flour itself was not the cure — it was God's power working through the prophet.

beginner.question: When God told the prophet Jonah to go preach to the city of Nineveh, Jonah disobeyed and instead boarded a ship sailing in the opposite direction toward Tarshish.
beginner.explanation: Jonah 1:3 says, 'But Jonah ran away from the Lord and headed for Tarshish. He went down to Joppa, where he found a ship bound for that port. After paying the fare, he went aboard and sailed for Tarshish to flee from the Lord.' He chose to run rather than obey God's command.

beginner.question: When a violent storm hit the ship Jonah was fleeing on, the terrified sailors cast lots and the lot fell on Jonah, revealing he was the cause of the storm.
beginner.explanation: Jonah 1:7 says the sailors said, 'Come, let us cast lots to find out who is responsible for this calamity.' They cast lots and the lot fell on Jonah. When questioned, Jonah admitted he was running from the Lord, and the sailors were filled with great fear.

beginner.question: Jonah was inside the belly of the great fish for seven days and seven nights before being spit out onto dry land.
beginner.explanation: Jonah was inside the fish for three days and three nights, not seven. Jonah 1:17 clearly states, 'Jonah was in the belly of the fish three days and three nights.' Jesus later referenced this specific timeframe as a sign of His own three days in the tomb (Matthew 12:40).

beginner.question: While inside the great fish, Jonah prayed to God, giving thanks and acknowledging that 'salvation comes from the Lord.'
beginner.explanation: Jonah chapter 2 records Jonah's heartfelt prayer from inside the fish. In Jonah 2:9, he declares, 'Salvation comes from the Lord.' Despite his dire situation, Jonah turned back to God in prayer, and God commanded the fish to spit Jonah out onto dry land.

beginner.question: After the great fish spit Jonah out on dry land, God gave him a second chance, and this time Jonah obeyed and went to Nineveh to deliver God's message.
beginner.explanation: Jonah 3:1-3 records, 'Then the word of the Lord came to Jonah a second time: Go to the great city of Nineveh and proclaim to it the message I give you. Jonah obeyed the word of the Lord and went to Nineveh.' Having learned from his ordeal, Jonah finally followed God's instructions.

beginner.question: When Jonah preached in Nineveh, the people of the city rejected his message completely and tried to have him killed.
beginner.explanation: The opposite happened — the people of Nineveh had one of the most dramatic responses to preaching in the entire Bible. Jonah 3:5 says, 'The Ninevites believed God. A fast was proclaimed, and all of them, from the greatest to the least, put on sackcloth' in repentance.

beginner.question: The entire city of Nineveh, from the king to the poorest citizen, repented and turned from their evil ways when they heard Jonah's warning about God's coming judgment.
beginner.explanation: Jonah 3:5-8 describes a citywide repentance. The people fasted and wore sackcloth. Even the king rose from his throne, removed his royal robes, covered himself with sackcloth, and sat in ashes. He issued a decree commanding everyone to pray urgently and turn from evil.

beginner.question: Because the people of Nineveh repented of their wickedness, God showed them mercy and did not destroy their city as He had warned.
beginner.explanation: Jonah 3:10 says, 'When God saw what they did and how they turned from their evil ways, he relented and did not bring on them the destruction he had threatened.' This is a powerful demonstration of God's compassion — He is always willing to forgive those who genuinely repent.

beginner.question: After God spared the city of Nineveh, Jonah was overjoyed that so many people had been saved from destruction.
beginner.explanation: Jonah was not happy at all — he was angry. Jonah 4:1 says, 'But to Jonah this seemed very wrong, and he became angry.' Jonah wanted Nineveh, an enemy of Israel, to be destroyed. He even told God that this was the very reason he had fled to Tarshish in the first place.

beginner.question: God provided a plant to shade Jonah from the sun, but then sent a worm to destroy it, using this as a lesson to teach Jonah about God's compassion for all people.
beginner.explanation: In Jonah 4:6-11, God grew a plant to shade Jonah, then sent a worm to destroy it. When Jonah was upset about the plant, God pointed out the inconsistency: Jonah cared about a plant he didn't create, yet was angry that God showed compassion to over 120,000 people in Nineveh.

beginner.question: The city of Nineveh, where God sent Jonah to preach, was the capital city of the Babylonian Empire.
beginner.explanation: Nineveh was the capital of the Assyrian Empire, not the Babylonian Empire. The capital of Babylon was the city of Babylon itself. The Assyrian Empire was one of the most powerful and feared empires in the ancient world, and Nineveh was a great city of enormous size.

beginner.question: The city of Nineveh, where Jonah was sent to preach, was the capital of the ancient Assyrian Empire, which was a fierce enemy of Israel.
beginner.explanation: Nineveh was indeed the great capital of Assyria. The Assyrians were notorious for their military brutality and were feared enemies of Israel. This helps explain why Jonah was so reluctant to go there — he did not want God to show mercy to his nation's cruel oppressor.

beginner.question: Jesus referenced the story of Jonah spending three days inside the great fish as a sign pointing to His own death and resurrection after three days in the tomb.
beginner.explanation: In Matthew 12:40, Jesus said, 'For as Jonah was three days and three nights in the belly of a huge fish, so the Son of Man will be three days and three nights in the heart of the earth.' Jesus used Jonah's real experience as a prophetic picture of His coming death, burial, and resurrection.

beginner.question: When God first told Jonah to go to Nineveh, Jonah gladly obeyed and went straight there without any hesitation.
beginner.explanation: Jonah did the exact opposite of obeying. Jonah 1:3 says he 'ran away from the Lord and headed for Tarshish,' which was in the opposite direction from Nineveh. He paid the fare for a ship and tried to flee from God's presence rather than deliver the message to Nineveh.

beginner.question: When the storm threatened to destroy the ship, Jonah bravely took charge and helped the sailors navigate to safety rather than suggesting they throw him overboard.
beginner.explanation: Jonah did not help navigate. In fact, he admitted he was the cause of the storm and told the sailors, 'Pick me up and throw me into the sea, and it will become calm. I know that it is my fault that this great storm has come upon you' (Jonah 1:12). The sailors reluctantly threw him overboard.

beginner.question: God spoke to Moses through a burning bush — a bush that was on fire but was not consumed by the flames — calling him to lead the Israelites out of slavery in Egypt.
beginner.explanation: Exodus 3:2-4 describes how Moses saw a bush that was blazing with fire yet did not burn up. When he went closer to investigate, God called to him from within the bush and commissioned him to go to Pharaoh and bring the Israelites out of Egypt.

beginner.question: Moses received the Ten Commandments from God on Mount Sinai, where he spent forty days and forty nights.
beginner.explanation: Exodus 24:18 says, 'Moses entered the cloud as he went on up the mountain. And he stayed on the mountain forty days and forty nights.' On Mount Sinai, God gave Moses the Ten Commandments written on two stone tablets, along with many other laws for the people of Israel.

beginner.question: Moses was confident and eager to speak for God when first called at the burning bush, immediately agreeing to go to Pharaoh without any protest.
beginner.explanation: Moses was far from confident. He made several excuses: 'Who am I to go to Pharaoh?' (Exodus 3:11), 'What if they do not believe me?' (Exodus 4:1), and 'I have never been eloquent... I am slow of speech and tongue' (Exodus 4:10). God had to patiently address each objection.

beginner.question: God sent ten plagues upon Egypt through Moses to convince Pharaoh to release the Israelites from slavery.
beginner.explanation: Exodus chapters 7-12 describe the ten plagues: water to blood, frogs, gnats, flies, livestock disease, boils, hail, locusts, darkness, and the death of the firstborn. Each plague was more severe than the last, and after the tenth plague, Pharaoh finally let the Israelites go.

beginner.question: Moses led the Israelites across the Red Sea on dry ground, with walls of water on either side, while Pharaoh's army was destroyed when the waters collapsed.
beginner.explanation: Exodus 14:21-28 describes how God parted the Red Sea through Moses. The Israelites walked through on dry ground with a wall of water on their right and left. When the Egyptian army followed, God released the waters and the entire army was destroyed. This was one of the greatest miracles in the Bible.

beginner.question: Moses was allowed to enter the Promised Land and lead the Israelites in conquering it after their forty years of wandering in the wilderness.
beginner.explanation: Moses was not allowed to enter the Promised Land. Because he struck the rock instead of speaking to it as God had commanded (Numbers 20:11-12), God told Moses he would only see the land from a distance. Moses viewed it from Mount Nebo but died before entering (Deuteronomy 34:1-5).

beginner.question: The Bible describes Moses as a prophet unlike any other in Israel, because God spoke to him 'face to face,' as a man speaks with his friend.
beginner.explanation: Deuteronomy 34:10 says, 'Since then, no prophet has risen in Israel like Moses, whom the Lord knew face to face.' Exodus 33:11 adds, 'The Lord would speak to Moses face to face, as one speaks to a friend.' This intimate relationship with God set Moses apart from all other prophets.

beginner.question: God appointed Moses' wife Zipporah to be his official spokesman before Pharaoh because Moses felt he was not a good speaker.
beginner.explanation: God appointed Moses' brother Aaron, not his wife Zipporah, as his spokesman. Exodus 4:14-16 records God saying, 'What about your brother, Aaron the Levite? I know he can speak well... He will speak to the people for you, and it will be as if he were your mouth.'

beginner.question: Of the twelve spies Moses sent to explore the Promised Land, only Joshua and Caleb gave a faithful report, trusting that God would help them conquer it.
beginner.explanation: Numbers 14:6-9 records that only Joshua and Caleb, out of the twelve spies, trusted God and urged the people to enter the land. The other ten spread fear, saying the inhabitants were too powerful. Because the people sided with the fearful majority, God made them wander forty years in the wilderness.

beginner.question: Moses was not allowed to enter the Promised Land because he had killed an Egyptian man decades earlier, before he fled to the wilderness.
beginner.explanation: While Moses did kill an Egyptian (Exodus 2:12), the specific reason God denied him entry to the Promised Land was that he struck the rock instead of speaking to it, as God had commanded, at Meribah (Numbers 20:12). God said Moses had failed to honour Him as holy before the people.

beginner.question: Samuel's mother, Hannah, dedicated her son to God's service before he was even born. She brought young Samuel to the tabernacle at Shiloh, where he grew up serving God.
beginner.explanation: In 1 Samuel 1:11, Hannah prayed, 'Lord Almighty, if you will... give your servant a son, then I will give him to the Lord for all the days of his life.' After Samuel was weaned, she brought him to the tabernacle at Shiloh (1 Samuel 1:24-28) where he served under the priest Eli.

beginner.question: When God called young Samuel in the night, Samuel at first thought it was the elderly priest Eli calling him, and he ran to Eli multiple times before understanding it was God.
beginner.explanation: In 1 Samuel 3:4-8, God called Samuel three times, and each time Samuel ran to Eli, saying, 'Here I am; you called me.' After the third time, Eli realised it was the Lord calling the boy and instructed Samuel to respond, 'Speak, Lord, for your servant is listening.'

beginner.question: The prophet Samuel anointed David as the very first king of Israel.
beginner.explanation: David was not the first king of Israel — Saul was. Samuel anointed Saul as Israel's first king in 1 Samuel 10:1. It was only after Saul disobeyed God that Samuel was sent to Bethlehem to anoint David, the son of Jesse, as the future second king (1 Samuel 16:13).

beginner.question: When Samuel went to anoint a new king from Jesse's family, God told him, 'The Lord does not look at the things people look at. People look at the outward appearance, but the Lord looks at the heart.'
beginner.explanation: 1 Samuel 16:7 records this famous statement from God. Samuel had been impressed by Jesse's eldest son Eliab because of his tall, handsome appearance, but God rejected him. God chose the youngest son, David, a humble shepherd boy, because of the quality of his heart.

beginner.question: Samuel anointed David, the youngest son of Jesse, as the future king of Israel, and the Spirit of the Lord came powerfully upon David from that day forward.
beginner.explanation: In 1 Samuel 16:13, Samuel took the horn of oil and anointed David 'in the presence of his brothers, and from that day on the Spirit of the Lord came powerfully upon David.' Although David would not become king immediately, God's Spirit was already at work in his life from this moment.

beginner.question: Samuel served only as a prophet and had no other leadership roles in the nation of Israel.
beginner.explanation: Samuel served in multiple roles. 1 Samuel 7:15 says, 'Samuel continued as Israel's leader (judge) all the days of his life.' He also served as a priest, offering sacrifices. Samuel was a prophet, priest, and the last judge of Israel — a remarkably broad set of leadership responsibilities.

beginner.question: The people of Israel came to the prophet Samuel and asked for a king so they could be 'like all the other nations.' This request displeased both Samuel and God.
beginner.explanation: In 1 Samuel 8:5-7, the elders of Israel demanded, 'Appoint a king to lead us, such as all the other nations have.' Samuel was displeased, and God told him, 'It is not you they have rejected, but they have rejected me as their king.' Still, God allowed Samuel to grant their request.

beginner.question: Samuel was delighted and fully supportive when the people of Israel demanded that he appoint a king over them.
beginner.explanation: 1 Samuel 8:6 says, 'When they said, "Give us a king to lead us," this displeased Samuel.' Samuel was troubled by the request because it represented the people's rejection of God's direct kingship. God told Samuel to warn the people about what a king would demand of them.

beginner.question: Samuel warned King Saul that his kingdom would not endure because Saul disobeyed God by impatiently offering a sacrifice that only a priest should have made.
beginner.explanation: In 1 Samuel 13:8-14, Saul grew impatient waiting for Samuel and offered the burnt offering himself, which was not his role. When Samuel arrived, he told Saul, 'You have done a foolish thing... your kingdom will not endure.' Samuel said God had already found 'a man after his own heart.'

beginner.question: Even after his death, the prophet Samuel's spirit appeared to King Saul through a medium at Endor and delivered a final, devastating message.
beginner.explanation: In 1 Samuel 28, the desperate King Saul consulted a medium at Endor who brought up Samuel's spirit. Samuel told Saul, 'The Lord has departed from you... Tomorrow you and your sons will be with me,' prophesying Saul's death in battle the very next day.

beginner.question: Samuel grew up in a wealthy household in the city of Jerusalem before becoming a prophet.
beginner.explanation: Samuel did not grow up in Jerusalem or a wealthy household. His mother Hannah dedicated him to God's service, and he grew up serving in the tabernacle at Shiloh (1 Samuel 1:24-28; 3:1) under the guidance of the elderly priest Eli. Shiloh, not Jerusalem, was the religious centre at that time.

beginner.question: The prophet Amos was a shepherd and fig tree farmer before God called him to prophesy, making him an ordinary working man rather than a trained religious leader.
beginner.explanation: In Amos 7:14, the prophet declared, 'I was neither a prophet nor the son of a prophet, but I was a shepherd, and I also took care of sycamore-fig trees. But the Lord took me from tending the flock and said to me, Go, prophesy to my people Israel.' God chose an unlikely messenger.

beginner.question: Although Amos was from the southern kingdom of Judah, God sent him to deliver His message of justice to the northern kingdom of Israel.
beginner.explanation: Amos was from Tekoa in Judah (Amos 1:1), but God specifically sent him north to prophesy to Israel during the reign of King Jeroboam II (Amos 7:15). This cross-border prophetic mission showed that God's concern for justice was not limited by national boundaries.

beginner.question: Amos praised the wealthy people of Israel for their generosity toward the poor and their faithful worship of God.
beginner.explanation: Amos did the exact opposite — he harshly condemned the wealthy for exploiting the poor. He rebuked those who 'trample the heads of the poor' (Amos 2:7), 'oppress the poor and crush the needy' (Amos 4:1), and live in luxury while ignoring justice and righteousness.

beginner.question: Amos delivered one of the Bible's most powerful calls for justice, declaring, 'Let justice roll on like a river, righteousness like a never-failing stream.'
beginner.explanation: Amos 5:24 contains these famous words. Rather than elaborate religious ceremonies, God wanted justice and righteousness to flow constantly like a river. This verse has been quoted throughout history by those calling for social justice and fair treatment of all people.

beginner.question: Amos was a trained priest who studied at the temple in Jerusalem for many years before beginning his prophetic ministry.
beginner.explanation: Amos was not a trained priest or prophet. He explicitly stated in Amos 7:14, 'I was neither a prophet nor the son of a prophet, but I was a shepherd, and I also took care of sycamore-fig trees.' God chose this untrained farmer to deliver His powerful message of justice.

beginner.question: The priest Amaziah at Bethel tried to silence Amos and told him to go back to the land of Judah and prophesy there instead.
beginner.explanation: In Amos 7:12-13, Amaziah the priest told Amos, 'Get out, you seer! Go back to the land of Judah. Earn your bread there and do your prophesying there. Don't prophesy any more at Bethel.' He wanted to silence the uncomfortable truth that Amos was speaking against Israel's sin.

beginner.question: Amos prophesied during a time of extreme poverty and suffering in Israel, when the nation was at its weakest and most vulnerable.
beginner.explanation: Amos prophesied during the prosperous reign of King Jeroboam II (Amos 1:1), when the northern kingdom was experiencing peace and wealth. The problem was that this prosperity was built on injustice and exploitation. Amos warned that outward success masked deep moral decay that would lead to judgment.

beginner.question: God told the prophet Hosea to marry an unfaithful woman named Gomer, as a living picture of how Israel had been unfaithful to God by worshipping idols.
beginner.explanation: Hosea 1:2-3 records God telling Hosea, 'Go, marry a promiscuous woman... because the land is guilty of the vilest adultery in departing from the Lord.' Hosea obeyed and married Gomer. Their troubled marriage became a living sermon about God's faithful love for His unfaithful people.

beginner.question: Hosea's children were given symbolic names that served as messages from God to the people of Israel.
beginner.explanation: Hosea's children were named Jezreel (meaning 'God scatters' — a warning of judgment, Hosea 1:4), Lo-Ruhamah (meaning 'not loved/no mercy,' Hosea 1:6), and Lo-Ammi (meaning 'not my people,' Hosea 1:9). Each name was a prophetic warning from God about the consequences of Israel's unfaithfulness.

beginner.question: Hosea's message was entirely about judgment and doom — he offered no hope or promise of restoration for the people of Israel.
beginner.explanation: While Hosea did warn of judgment, his book is also filled with hope and the promise of restoration. Hosea 14:4 records God promising, 'I will heal their waywardness and love them freely.' The book's central message is that God's love is relentless and He desires reconciliation, not destruction.

beginner.question: Throughout the book of Hosea, Israel's worship of false gods is compared to adultery — being unfaithful in a marriage — showing how deeply their sin hurt God.
beginner.explanation: Hosea uses the metaphor of marital unfaithfulness throughout the book. Just as Gomer was unfaithful to Hosea, Israel was unfaithful to God by chasing after Baal and other idols. This powerful comparison reveals that idolatry was not just rule-breaking but a deeply personal betrayal of God's love.

beginner.question: God told Hosea to permanently divorce his unfaithful wife Gomer and never take her back, as a symbol of God's permanent rejection of Israel.
beginner.explanation: God told Hosea to do the opposite — to go and bring Gomer back. Hosea 3:1 says, 'Go, show your love to your wife again, though she is loved by another man and is an adulteress. Love her as the Lord loves the Israelites.' This act of redemption symbolised God's persistent, forgiving love.

beginner.question: One of Hosea's children was named 'Lo-Ammi,' which means 'not my people,' serving as a warning from God that He might reject Israel for their unfaithfulness.
beginner.explanation: In Hosea 1:9, God said, 'Call him Lo-Ammi (not my people), for you are not my people, and I am not your God.' This was a devastating reversal of God's covenant promise. However, Hosea 2:23 promises future restoration: 'I will say to those called Not my people, You are my people.'

beginner.question: Hosea prophesied primarily to the southern kingdom of Judah, not to the northern kingdom of Israel.
beginner.explanation: Hosea prophesied primarily to the northern kingdom of Israel, not Judah. While Hosea 1:1 mentions kings of Judah, his messages were directed mainly at Israel (also called Ephraim in his book). He ministered during the final decades before the northern kingdom fell to Assyria in 722 BC.

beginner.question: The prophet Micah prophesied that the Messiah would be born in the small, humble town of Bethlehem — a prophecy fulfilled when Jesus was born there centuries later.
beginner.explanation: Micah 5:2 says, 'But you, Bethlehem Ephrathah, though you are small among the clans of Judah, out of you will come for me one who will be ruler over Israel.' This remarkable prophecy, made over 700 years before Jesus' birth, identified the exact town where the Messiah would be born.

beginner.question: Micah wrote one of the most quoted verses in the Old Testament, declaring that God requires people to 'act justly, love mercy, and walk humbly with your God.'
beginner.explanation: Micah 6:8 says, 'He has shown you, O mortal, what is good. And what does the Lord require of you? To act justly and to love mercy and to walk humbly with your God.' This verse is considered one of the finest summaries of what God expects from His people.

beginner.question: Micah prophesied that the Messiah would be born in Jerusalem, the great capital city of the nation.
beginner.explanation: Micah prophesied that the Messiah would be born in Bethlehem, not Jerusalem. Micah 5:2 specifically names 'Bethlehem Ephrathah,' calling it 'small among the clans of Judah.' God chose a humble, insignificant town rather than the grand capital, showing that His ways are different from human expectations.

beginner.question: Micah condemned the corrupt leaders, false prophets, and unjust practices he saw in both the northern kingdom of Israel and the southern kingdom of Judah.
beginner.explanation: Micah 1:1 says his prophecy concerned 'Samaria and Jerusalem' — the capitals of both Israel and Judah. He condemned leaders who 'hate good and love evil' (3:2), prophets who led people astray for money (3:5), and judges who accepted bribes (3:11) in both kingdoms.

beginner.question: When the wise men came to King Herod looking for the newborn king of the Jews, the chief priests quoted Micah's prophecy about Bethlehem to identify where the Messiah would be born.
beginner.explanation: In Matthew 2:4-6, when Herod asked the religious leaders where the Messiah was to be born, they quoted Micah 5:2 and identified Bethlehem. This shows that the Jewish leaders knew the prophecy well — Micah's words, written centuries earlier, guided the wise men to the right location.

beginner.question: Micah was a false prophet whose writings were rejected by the early church and are not considered reliable Scripture.
beginner.explanation: Micah was a true prophet of God whose book is part of the Old Testament canon, accepted by both Jews and Christians. His prophecy about Bethlehem (Micah 5:2) is quoted in the New Testament (Matthew 2:6), and his call to 'act justly, love mercy, and walk humbly' (Micah 6:8) remains one of the most treasured verses in Scripture.

beginner.question: John the Baptist lived in the wilderness, wore clothing made of camel's hair with a leather belt, and ate locusts and wild honey.
beginner.explanation: Matthew 3:4 says, 'John's clothes were made of camel's hair, and he had a leather belt around his waist. His food was locusts and wild honey.' This simple, rugged lifestyle was reminiscent of the Old Testament prophets, particularly Elijah, and matched his austere message of repentance.

beginner.question: John the Baptist baptised Jesus in the Jordan River, after which the heavens opened, the Holy Spirit descended like a dove, and God's voice spoke from heaven.
beginner.explanation: Matthew 3:16-17 records, 'As soon as Jesus was baptised, he went up out of the water. At that moment heaven was opened, and he saw the Spirit of God descending like a dove and alighting on him. And a voice from heaven said, "This is my Son, whom I love; with him I am well pleased."'

beginner.question: John the Baptist baptised people in the Sea of Galilee, where Jesus later called His first disciples.
beginner.explanation: John the Baptist baptised people in the Jordan River, not the Sea of Galilee. Matthew 3:6 says people 'were baptised by him in the Jordan River.' While Jesus did call disciples by the Sea of Galilee later, John's baptism ministry was centred on the Jordan River.

beginner.question: John the Baptist called Jesus 'the Lamb of God, who takes away the sin of the world' when he saw Jesus approaching him.
beginner.explanation: John 1:29 records this famous declaration: 'The next day John saw Jesus coming toward him and said, "Look, the Lamb of God, who takes away the sin of the world!"' This title connected Jesus to the sacrificial lambs of the Old Testament and pointed to His ultimate sacrifice on the cross.

beginner.question: John the Baptist's parents, Zechariah and Elizabeth, were both elderly when the angel Gabriel announced they would have a son.
beginner.explanation: Luke 1:7 says, 'They were both very old.' When the angel Gabriel appeared to Zechariah in the temple, he promised that Elizabeth would bear a son who would 'go on before the Lord, in the spirit and power of Elijah' to prepare the people for the coming of the Messiah (Luke 1:17).

beginner.question: John the Baptist was the biological son of Mary and Joseph, making him Jesus' brother.
beginner.explanation: John the Baptist was the son of Zechariah and Elizabeth, not Mary and Joseph. Luke 1:5-13 identifies his parents clearly. John and Jesus were relatives (their mothers Elizabeth and Mary were related, Luke 1:36), but they were not brothers — they had different parents.

beginner.question: John the Baptist was arrested and imprisoned by the ruler Herod Antipas because John publicly criticised Herod's unlawful marriage to Herodias, his brother's wife.
beginner.explanation: Mark 6:17-18 explains, 'Herod himself had given orders to have John arrested, and he had him bound and put in prison. He did this because of Herodias, his brother Philip's wife, whom he had married. For John had been saying to Herod, "It is not lawful for you to have your brother's wife."'

beginner.question: John the Baptist lived a luxurious lifestyle in the royal court of King Herod before beginning his wilderness ministry.
beginner.explanation: John the Baptist did not live in luxury. Matthew 3:4 describes his austere desert lifestyle: camel's hair clothing, a leather belt, and a diet of locusts and wild honey. Jesus Himself contrasted John with those in palaces: 'Those who wear fine clothes are in kings' palaces' (Matthew 11:8).

beginner.question: The angel Gabriel appeared to Zechariah while he was serving as a priest in the temple to announce the coming birth of John the Baptist.
beginner.explanation: Luke 1:11-19 records that while Zechariah was burning incense in the temple as part of his priestly duties, the angel Gabriel appeared to him and said, 'Do not be afraid, Zechariah; your prayer has been heard. Your wife Elizabeth will bear you a son, and you are to call him John.'

beginner.question: John the Baptist proclaimed himself to be the long-awaited Messiah that the people of Israel had been waiting for.
beginner.explanation: John the Baptist specifically denied being the Messiah. John 1:20 says he 'did not fail to confess, but confessed freely, "I am not the Messiah."' John knew his role was to prepare the way, saying about Jesus, 'He must become greater; I must become less' (John 3:28-30).

beginner.question: Malachi is the last book of the Old Testament, and the prophet's name means 'my messenger.'
beginner.explanation: Malachi is indeed the final book of the Old Testament. The name 'Malachi' comes from the Hebrew word meaning 'my messenger,' which is fitting since his book contains the promise that God would send a messenger to prepare the way before the Lord (Malachi 3:1).

beginner.question: Malachi rebuked the priests of Israel for offering blind, lame, and sick animals as sacrifices to God, instead of giving God the best of their flocks.
beginner.explanation: In Malachi 1:8, God asks through the prophet, 'When you offer blind animals for sacrifice, is that not wrong? When you sacrifice lame or diseased animals, is that not wrong? Try offering them to your governor! Would he be pleased with you?' God deserved the best, not the worst.

beginner.question: Malachi praised the people of Israel for their faithful tithing and generous offerings to God.
beginner.explanation: Malachi did the opposite — he rebuked the people for withholding their tithes. Malachi 3:8 says, 'Will a mere mortal rob God? Yet you rob me. But you ask, "How are we robbing you?" In tithes and offerings.' God challenged them to bring the full tithe and test His faithfulness.

beginner.question: Malachi prophesied during the Babylonian exile, before the Jewish people had returned to Jerusalem.
beginner.explanation: Malachi prophesied after the return from exile, not during it. The people had already rebuilt the temple and were living back in Jerusalem, but they had grown spiritually complacent and unfaithful. Malachi is a post-exilic prophet, likely writing around 430 BC.

beginner.question: Malachi taught that God does not care about tithes or offerings at all, only about personal prayer.
beginner.explanation: Malachi did not say God doesn't care about tithes. In fact, Malachi 3:10 records God specifically challenging the people to bring their full tithes: 'Bring the whole tithe into the storehouse... Test me in this, says the Lord Almighty, and see if I will not throw open the floodgates of heaven.'

beginner.question: The book of Malachi ends with a promise that God will send the prophet Moses before the great and dreadful day of the Lord.
beginner.explanation: Malachi 4:5 promises the return of Elijah, not Moses: 'See, I will send the prophet Elijah to you before that great and dreadful day of the Lord comes.' Jesus identified John the Baptist as the fulfilment of this prophecy, saying John came 'in the spirit and power of Elijah' (Luke 1:17).

beginner.question: In the Gospel of Luke, God sent the angel Gabriel to a young woman named Mary in the town of Nazareth to deliver an important message. Gabriel told Mary she would conceive a child by the Holy Spirit. What was the name of the child Gabriel said Mary would bear?
beginner.explanation: Gabriel told Mary to name her son Jesus. This event, known as the Annunciation, is one of the most famous angel appearances in the Bible. The name Jesus means 'the Lord saves.'

beginner.question: When the angel Gabriel appeared to Mary and told her she would have a son, Mary was troubled by his greeting. What did Gabriel say to reassure her before delivering his message?
options: The Lord commands you to listen
beginner.explanation: Gabriel's first words of comfort were 'Do not be afraid, Mary, you have found favor with God.' Angels often begin their messages this way because their appearance can be terrifying to humans.

beginner.question: The angel Gabriel visited Mary in Nazareth to announce that she would give birth to the Son of God. At the time of Gabriel's visit, Mary was engaged to be married. Who was Mary's fiancé?
beginner.explanation: Mary was pledged to be married to Joseph, a carpenter and descendant of King David. The angel later appeared to Joseph in a dream to reassure him about Mary's miraculous pregnancy.

beginner.question: When Gabriel told Mary she would conceive a child, Mary asked how this could happen since she was a virgin. Gabriel explained that a special power would come upon her. What did Gabriel say would overshadow her?
options: The power of the Most High
beginner.explanation: Gabriel explained that the Holy Spirit would come upon Mary and the power of the Most High would overshadow her. This is why the child would be called the Son of God — his conception was entirely miraculous.

beginner.question: During Gabriel's visit to Mary, he shared news about one of Mary's relatives who was miraculously pregnant in her old age. Who was this elderly relative that Gabriel mentioned as proof that nothing is impossible with God?
beginner.explanation: Gabriel told Mary that her relative Elizabeth was already six months pregnant despite being very old and previously barren. Gabriel used this as evidence that nothing is impossible with God.

beginner.question: The angel Gabriel told Mary that her son would be given a special throne and would reign forever. According to Gabriel's announcement, whose throne would God give to Mary's child?
beginner.explanation: Gabriel said God would give Jesus the throne of his father David, and he would reign over Jacob's descendants forever. This connected Jesus to the long-awaited Messiah from David's royal line.

beginner.question: After the angel Gabriel delivered his extraordinary message to Mary about giving birth to the Son of God, Mary responded with a famous statement of faith and obedience. What did Mary say?
options: I am the Lord's servant; may it be as you have said
Let your will be done in heaven and earth
I will follow wherever you lead me
Blessed be the name of the Lord forever
beginner.explanation: Mary humbly accepted God's plan by calling herself the Lord's servant and submitting to his will. Her faithful response is admired by Christians as a model of trust and obedience to God.

beginner.question: In the Gospel of Luke, the angel Gabriel appeared to an elderly priest while he was performing his duties in the temple in Jerusalem. This priest and his wife had been unable to have children for many years. What was the priest's name?
beginner.explanation: Zechariah was a priest who belonged to the priestly division of Abijah. He and his wife Elizabeth were both righteous before God but had no children because Elizabeth was barren, and they were both very old.

beginner.question: When Gabriel appeared to the priest Zechariah in the temple, he announced that Zechariah's elderly wife would bear a son. What was the name of Zechariah's wife who would miraculously become pregnant?
beginner.explanation: Elizabeth was Zechariah's wife and a descendant of Aaron. Despite being barren and advanced in years, she conceived a son just as the angel Gabriel had promised.

beginner.question: The angel Gabriel told the elderly priest Zechariah that his wife Elizabeth would have a son, and Gabriel told Zechariah exactly what to name the child. What name did Gabriel say to give the baby?
beginner.explanation: Gabriel instructed Zechariah to name the baby John. This child would grow up to be John the Baptist, the one who prepared the way for Jesus by calling people to repentance and baptism.

beginner.question: When Gabriel appeared to Zechariah in the temple and announced that his elderly wife would have a baby, Zechariah doubted the message because of their old age. How did Gabriel respond to Zechariah's disbelief?
options: Zechariah was struck mute and could not speak until the baby was born
Zechariah was struck blind for nine months
Zechariah was made to fast for forty days
Zechariah was banished from the temple
beginner.explanation: Because Zechariah did not believe Gabriel's message, he was unable to speak until the day his son was born and named. This served as both a sign and a consequence of his doubt.

beginner.question: When Zechariah questioned how he could be sure of Gabriel's message about having a son in his old age, Gabriel identified himself to establish his authority. Gabriel said he stands in the presence of whom?
beginner.explanation: Gabriel declared, 'I am Gabriel. I stand in the presence of God, and I have been sent to speak to you and to tell you this good news.' This established Gabriel's authority as a messenger direct from God's throne room.

beginner.question: In the Old Testament book of Daniel, the prophet Daniel had a vision he could not understand. God sent the angel Gabriel to explain the vision to Daniel. When Gabriel approached, Daniel was so terrified he fell to the ground. What did Gabriel call Daniel?
beginner.explanation: Gabriel addressed Daniel as 'son of man,' a term simply meaning 'human being.' Daniel was overwhelmed by the angelic encounter and fell face down on the ground in fear.

beginner.question: The angel Gabriel appeared to the prophet Daniel to explain a vision about a ram and a goat, which represented future kingdoms. Gabriel told Daniel that this vision concerned events at a specific time. When did Gabriel say the vision would be fulfilled?
options: At the time of the end
beginner.explanation: Gabriel told Daniel that the vision concerned 'the time of the end.' The ram represented the Medo-Persian Empire and the goat represented the Greek Empire under Alexander the Great.

beginner.question: In the book of Daniel, Gabriel visited Daniel a second time while Daniel was praying and confessing his people's sins. Gabriel came to give Daniel insight about a famous prophecy involving a specific number of weeks. How many weeks were decreed?
beginner.explanation: Gabriel revealed the prophecy of the 'seventy weeks' (or seventy sevens), which outlined God's timeline for the people of Israel. This prophecy is considered one of the most significant messianic prophecies in the Old Testament.

beginner.question: The angel Gabriel told the prophet Daniel that he came in swift flight to give Daniel understanding because Daniel held a special status before God. What did Gabriel say about Daniel?
options: That Daniel was greatly loved and highly esteemed
That Daniel was the wisest of all prophets
That Daniel was chosen before birth
That Daniel was the last of the seers
beginner.explanation: Gabriel told Daniel he was 'highly esteemed' or 'greatly beloved' by God. This is a remarkable statement — an angel from heaven affirming God's special love and regard for the prophet Daniel.

beginner.question: The Bible describes Michael as holding a special rank among all the angels. What unique title is given to Michael in the book of Jude, making him the only angel with this designation in Scripture?
beginner.explanation: Michael is called 'the archangel' in Jude 1:9, meaning 'chief angel' or 'ruling angel.' He is the only angel given this specific title in the Bible, indicating his high rank among the heavenly hosts.

beginner.question: In the book of Jude, Michael the archangel had a dispute with the devil about something specific. Rather than fighting on his own authority, Michael deferred to God. What were Michael and the devil arguing about?
beginner.explanation: Michael disputed with the devil over the body of Moses. The Bible does not explain why the devil wanted Moses' body, but Michael's response shows that even the highest angel relies on God's authority rather than his own.

beginner.question: During Michael's dispute with the devil over the body of Moses, the archangel did not dare to bring a slanderous accusation against the devil on his own authority. Instead, what did Michael say to the devil?
options: In the name of God, be gone!
beginner.explanation: Rather than directly confronting Satan with insults, Michael said, 'The Lord rebuke you!' This shows Michael's humility and reliance on God's power, even as the mightiest angel in heaven.

beginner.question: In the book of Revelation chapter 12, a great war broke out in heaven. Michael and his angels fought against the dragon and his angels. Who is the dragon identified as in this passage?
beginner.explanation: Revelation 12:9 explicitly identifies the dragon as 'that ancient serpent called the devil, or Satan, who leads the whole world astray.' Michael and his angels won the battle and cast Satan out of heaven.

beginner.question: In the book of Daniel, Michael is described as a great prince who has a special protective role over a specific group of people. According to Daniel 12:1, who does Michael protect?
beginner.explanation: Daniel 12:1 calls Michael 'the great prince who protects your people,' referring to the nation of Israel. Michael is portrayed as Israel's special angelic defender throughout the book of Daniel.

beginner.question: After the great war in heaven described in Revelation 12, Michael and his angels were victorious over Satan and his forces. What happened to Satan and his angels after they lost the battle?
options: They were hurled down to the earth
They were cast into the lake of fire
They were locked in chains in the abyss
They were scattered across the universe
beginner.explanation: Satan and his angels were hurled down to the earth after losing the war in heaven. Revelation 12:12 warns that the devil has gone down to earth full of fury, because he knows his time is short.

beginner.question: When Jesus was born in Bethlehem, an angel appeared to a group of people nearby to announce the joyful news of the Savior's birth. According to Luke's Gospel, to whom did the angel appear?
options: Shepherds watching their flocks at night
Wise men traveling from the east
Fishermen by the Sea of Galilee
beginner.explanation: The angel appeared to shepherds who were living out in the fields, keeping watch over their flocks at night. God chose to announce the birth of his Son to humble working people rather than to kings or religious leaders.

beginner.question: When the angel appeared to the shepherds on the night Jesus was born, the shepherds were terrified by the heavenly visitor. What was the first thing the angel said to calm them down?
beginner.explanation: The angel said, 'Do not be afraid. I bring you good news that will cause great joy for all the people.' This reassurance was needed because the glory of the Lord shone around them, causing great fear.

beginner.question: After the angel announced Jesus' birth to the shepherds on that first Christmas night, something spectacular happened in the sky above. A great company of what appeared alongside the angel?
options: The heavenly host — a multitude of angels
beginner.explanation: Suddenly a great company of the heavenly host appeared with the angel, praising God. This was a vast army of angels filling the night sky — a stunning display of heaven's joy at the birth of the Savior.

beginner.question: The multitude of angels that appeared to the shepherds on the night of Jesus' birth sang a famous song of praise that is still repeated in churches today. What did the angels say?
options: Glory to God in the highest, and on earth peace to those on whom his favor rests
Holy, holy, holy is the Lord Almighty
Praise the Lord, all you nations
Blessed is the King who comes in the name of the Lord
beginner.explanation: The angels' song 'Glory to God in the highest, and on earth peace' is known as the Gloria. It celebrates both God's glory in heaven and the peace that Jesus' birth would bring to humanity.

beginner.question: The angel told the shepherds they would find the newborn baby Jesus in a specific place. Where did the angel say the baby would be lying?
options: On a bed of straw in a house
beginner.explanation: The angel told the shepherds the baby would be wrapped in cloths and lying in a manger — a feeding trough for animals. This humble birthplace showed that God's Son came in humility, not in earthly splendor.

beginner.question: The angel described the baby born in Bethlehem using specific titles that identified the child's true identity. According to the angel's announcement to the shepherds, what did the angel call the baby?
options: A Savior, who is Christ the Lord
beginner.explanation: The angel announced, 'Today in the town of David a Savior has been born to you; he is the Messiah, the Lord.' These three titles — Savior, Christ (Messiah), and Lord — declare Jesus' full identity.

beginner.question: After the angels left the shepherds and returned to heaven on the night of Jesus' birth, the shepherds made a decision. What did the shepherds do after the angels departed?
options: They hurried to Bethlehem to find the baby
They fell asleep thinking it was a dream
They went to the temple to tell the priests
They stayed with their flocks until morning
beginner.explanation: The shepherds said to one another, 'Let's go to Bethlehem and see this thing that has happened.' They hurried off and found Mary, Joseph, and the baby lying in a manger, exactly as the angel described.

beginner.question: According to the Gospel of Matthew, after the wise men visited baby Jesus, an angel appeared to Joseph in a dream with an urgent warning about danger. What did the angel tell Joseph to do?
options: Flee to Egypt with Mary and the child
Hide in the temple in Jerusalem
Take the child to the Jordan River
beginner.explanation: The angel warned Joseph that King Herod was searching for the child to kill him. Joseph obeyed immediately, taking Mary and Jesus to Egypt that very night, where they stayed until Herod died.

beginner.question: On the Sunday morning after Jesus was crucified, women went to his tomb and found it open. According to the Gospel of Matthew, an angel had come and rolled away the large stone from the entrance. What did the angel look like?
options: His appearance was like lightning and his clothes were white as snow
He was dressed in golden armor
He appeared as a bright flame of fire
He looked like an ordinary young man
beginner.explanation: Matthew describes the angel's appearance as like lightning with garments white as snow. This dazzling, radiant appearance caused the Roman guards to shake with fear and become like dead men.

beginner.question: When the women arrived at Jesus' empty tomb on Easter morning, the angel sitting there told them not to be afraid and delivered the most important message in Christian history. What was the angel's key message?
options: He is not here; he has risen!
His body was taken by his disciples
beginner.explanation: The angel proclaimed, 'He is not here; he has risen, just as he said.' This is the central message of the Christian faith — that Jesus conquered death and rose from the grave on the third day.

beginner.question: The angel at Jesus' empty tomb gave the women who had come to visit specific instructions about what to do next. What did the angel tell the women to do?
options: Go quickly and tell the disciples that Jesus has risen
Stay and pray at the tomb until sunrise
Go to the temple and make an offering of thanks
Return home and wait for further instructions
beginner.explanation: The angel told the women to go quickly and tell the disciples that Jesus had risen from the dead and was going ahead of them to Galilee. The women were to be the first witnesses of the resurrection.

beginner.question: According to Matthew's account, when the angel rolled away the stone from Jesus' tomb on Easter morning, Roman soldiers were guarding the site. What happened to the guards when they saw the angel?
options: They shook with fear and became like dead men
They ran away screaming in terror
They fell to their knees and worshiped
They drew their swords and tried to fight
beginner.explanation: The guards were so afraid of the angel that they shook and became like dead men. Later, the chief priests bribed the soldiers to say the disciples had stolen the body while they were sleeping.

beginner.question: According to the Gospel of Luke, when the women went to Jesus' tomb and found it empty, beings in gleaming clothes appeared to them. How many of these angelic figures did the women see in Luke's account?
beginner.explanation: Luke describes two men in clothes that gleamed like lightning who suddenly appeared beside the women at the empty tomb. They reminded the women of Jesus' own prediction that he would rise on the third day.

beginner.question: The angels at the tomb in Luke's Gospel asked the women a thought-provoking and memorable question about their reason for being there. What did the angels ask?
options: Why do you look for the living among the dead?
Do you not remember what he told you?
Who are you seeking at this hour?
beginner.explanation: The angels asked, 'Why do you look for the living among the dead? He is not here; he has risen!' This powerful question highlighted that Jesus was alive and would no longer be found in a tomb.

beginner.question: In the Gospel of John, Mary Magdalene went to Jesus' tomb early on Sunday morning and found it empty. When she looked inside while weeping, she saw two angels in white. Where exactly were they sitting?
options: Where Jesus' body had been — one at the head and one at the feet
At the entrance of the tomb side by side
Standing on top of the rolled-away stone
In the garden just outside the tomb
beginner.explanation: The two angels were sitting where Jesus' body had been laid, one at the head and one at the foot. This detail emphasizes that Jesus' body was truly gone — he had risen from the dead.

beginner.question: In the book of Revelation, John describes a vast number of angels gathered around God's throne, worshiping the Lamb. John tried to express just how many angels he saw. How did John describe the number?
options: Thousands upon thousands and ten thousand times ten thousand
Exactly one hundred and forty-four thousand
beginner.explanation: John saw an enormous multitude of angels — 'numbering thousands upon thousands, and ten thousand times ten thousand.' This staggering number shows the vast scale of heaven's angelic host worshiping God.

beginner.question: In the book of Revelation, seven angels are given instruments to signal different judgments upon the earth. What were these seven angels given?
beginner.explanation: The seven angels were given seven trumpets, and as each angel sounded their trumpet, a different catastrophic event struck the earth. The trumpet judgments are one of three series of judgments in Revelation.

beginner.question: In Revelation, an angel showed John a vision of a beautiful holy city coming down out of heaven from God. What was the name of this glorious city?
beginner.explanation: The angel showed John the New Jerusalem descending from heaven, adorned like a bride. This city represents God's eternal dwelling with his people, where there will be no more death, mourning, or pain.

beginner.question: In the book of Revelation, four angels are described as standing at the four corners of the earth with a specific task. What were these four angels holding back?
options: The four winds of the earth
beginner.explanation: The four angels were holding back the four winds of the earth to prevent any wind from blowing on land, sea, or trees. They held the winds until God's servants were sealed with a mark on their foreheads.

beginner.question: In Revelation, an angel flying in midair proclaimed an eternal message to every nation, tribe, language, and people on earth. What did this angel cry out with a loud voice?
options: Fear God and give him glory, because the hour of his judgment has come
Repent, for the kingdom of heaven is at hand
Holy, holy, holy is the Lord God Almighty
The great day of the Lord is near
beginner.explanation: This angel proclaimed the 'eternal gospel' — calling all people to fear God and give him glory. The angel also called people to worship God as the Creator of heaven, earth, sea, and springs of water.

beginner.question: In Revelation chapter 20, John saw an angel come down from heaven holding a key and a great chain. What did this powerful angel do with the key and chain?
options: Bound Satan and locked him in the Abyss for a thousand years
Chained the beast from the sea
beginner.explanation: The angel seized Satan, bound him with the chain, and threw him into the Abyss, locking and sealing it for a thousand years. This prevented Satan from deceiving the nations during this period.

beginner.question: In the book of Genesis, Jacob was traveling alone and stopped for the night, using a stone as a pillow. While sleeping, he had one of the most famous dreams in the Bible. What did Jacob see in his dream?
options: A stairway reaching from earth to heaven with angels going up and down on it
A burning bush with an angel speaking from it
A chariot of fire carrying angels across the sky
A great tree reaching to heaven with angels in its branches
beginner.explanation: Jacob dreamed of a stairway (or ladder) reaching from earth to heaven, with angels ascending and descending on it. God stood at the top and renewed the covenant promises he had made to Abraham and Isaac.

beginner.question: After Jacob woke up from his dream of the heavenly stairway with angels going up and down, he was filled with awe and a holy fear. What did Jacob say about the place where he had the dream?
options: This is the gate of heaven
This is the mountain of God
This is the throne of the Almighty
beginner.explanation: Jacob exclaimed, 'How awesome is this place! This is none other than the house of God; this is the gate of heaven.' He was amazed that God had been present in what seemed like an ordinary place.

beginner.question: After waking from his dream of the ladder with angels ascending and descending, Jacob named the place where he had slept. He set up his stone pillow as a memorial pillar. What did Jacob call this place?
beginner.explanation: Jacob named the place Bethel, which means 'house of God' in Hebrew. Previously the town had been called Luz, but Jacob renamed it because of his encounter with God and the angels.

beginner.question: Jacob was on a journey when he stopped and had his famous dream of the stairway with angels. He was fleeing from his brother Esau, who wanted to kill him. Where was Jacob traveling to?
options: Harran, to stay with his uncle Laban
The Jordan River, to find a new home
beginner.explanation: Jacob was traveling to Harran in Mesopotamia, where his mother Rebekah's brother Laban lived. His mother had urged him to flee there after Jacob stole his brother Esau's blessing.

beginner.question: In Jacob's dream, God stood above the stairway with angels and made a promise to Jacob about his future descendants. What did God promise about Jacob's offspring?
options: They would be like the dust of the earth and spread in every direction
They would become a mighty army
They would build a great temple
They would rule over all other nations
beginner.explanation: God promised that Jacob's descendants would be as numerous as the dust of the earth and would spread out to the west, east, north, and south. All peoples on earth would be blessed through Jacob's family line.

beginner.question: In the book of Numbers, a prophet named Balaam was riding his donkey along a road when an angel of the Lord stood in the path to block his way. At first only the donkey could see the angel. What did the donkey do when she first saw the angel with a drawn sword?
options: She turned off the road into a field
She lay down and refused to move
beginner.explanation: The donkey saw the angel of the Lord standing in the road with a drawn sword and turned off the road into a field. Balaam, unable to see the angel, struck the donkey to get her back on the road.

beginner.question: When Balaam's donkey kept turning away from the invisible angel blocking their path, Balaam became angry and struck the donkey repeatedly. God then performed an extraordinary miracle. What happened?
options: God opened the donkey's mouth and she spoke in human words to Balaam
God made the angel visible to Balaam immediately
God struck Balaam blind as punishment
God turned the donkey into a pillar of salt
beginner.explanation: In one of the most unusual miracles in the Bible, God opened the donkey's mouth and she spoke to Balaam, asking why he had beaten her three times. This is the only time in Scripture an animal speaks.

beginner.question: When Balaam's donkey miraculously spoke to her master in human words, she asked Balaam a question about why he was hitting her. According to Numbers 22, how many times had Balaam struck his donkey?
beginner.explanation: Balaam had struck his donkey three times — once when she turned into a field, once when she pressed against a wall, and once when she lay down. Each time, the donkey was trying to avoid the angel.

beginner.question: After the donkey spoke to Balaam, God opened Balaam's eyes so he could finally see what had been blocking the path all along. What did Balaam see when his eyes were opened?
options: The angel of the Lord standing in the road with a drawn sword
A wall of fire across the road
A great chasm in the earth
A pillar of cloud and smoke
beginner.explanation: Balaam finally saw the angel of the Lord standing in the road with a drawn sword ready to kill him. Balaam immediately bowed low and fell facedown in fear and reverence.

beginner.question: The angel of the Lord told Balaam that if the donkey had not turned away from him those three times, the angel would have taken drastic action. What would the angel have done to Balaam?
options: Killed Balaam but spared the donkey alive
Sent Balaam back home in chains
beginner.explanation: The angel told Balaam, 'If the donkey had not turned away, I would certainly have killed you by now, but I would have spared her.' The donkey actually saved Balaam's life by avoiding the angel.

beginner.question: Balaam was traveling on his donkey because a king had hired him to curse the people of Israel, hoping to weaken them. Which king hired Balaam to place a curse on God's people?
beginner.explanation: King Balak of Moab was terrified of the Israelites and hired the prophet Balaam to curse them. However, God only allowed Balaam to bless Israel instead, which frustrated King Balak greatly.

beginner.question: In the book of Acts, the apostle Peter was arrested and thrown in prison by King Herod Agrippa. The night before his trial, an angel appeared in Peter's cell and woke him up. How did the angel wake Peter?
options: The angel struck Peter on the side
The angel called his name loudly
The angel shook the prison walls
beginner.explanation: The angel struck Peter on the side to wake him, saying 'Quick, get up!' At the same time, the chains fell off Peter's wrists. A bright light also filled the prison cell during the angel's visit.

beginner.question: When the angel appeared in Peter's prison cell to rescue him, something miraculous happened to the chains binding Peter's wrists. What happened to Peter's chains?
options: The chains fell off his wrists by themselves
The angel cut them with a sword
Peter pulled them apart with supernatural strength
beginner.explanation: As soon as the angel told Peter to get up, the chains simply fell off his wrists on their own. This supernatural deliverance showed God's power over any human imprisonment or restraint.

beginner.question: After Peter's chains fell off in prison, the angel gave Peter instructions before leading him to freedom. What did the angel tell Peter to put on before leaving the cell?
options: New clothes provided by the angel
beginner.explanation: The angel told Peter, 'Put on your clothes and sandals' and 'Wrap your cloak around you and follow me.' Even in this miraculous rescue, the angel attended to practical details for Peter's journey.

beginner.question: As the angel led Peter through the prison past multiple guard posts, they came to the iron gate leading out to the city. What happened when they reached this final gate?
options: The angel pushed it open with great force
Peter touched it and it crumbled
A second angel opened it from outside
beginner.explanation: The iron gate opened for them by itself — another miraculous detail in Peter's escape. After passing through the gate, they walked the length of one street, and then the angel suddenly left Peter.

beginner.question: While Peter was in prison awaiting trial, the early church was doing something urgently on his behalf. What were the believers doing for Peter during his imprisonment?
beginner.explanation: Acts 12:5 says 'the church was earnestly praying to God for him.' Their persistent prayers were answered when God sent an angel to miraculously free Peter from prison that very night.

beginner.question: After the angel freed Peter from prison, Peter went to a house where believers were praying for him. A servant girl came to answer the door and recognized Peter's voice. What was her name?
beginner.explanation: Rhoda was so overjoyed at recognizing Peter's voice that she ran back to tell the others without even opening the door, leaving Peter standing outside knocking.

beginner.question: In the book of Daniel, King Nebuchadnezzar of Babylon threw three young Hebrew men into a blazing furnace because they refused to worship a golden statue. What were the names of these three brave men?
beginner.explanation: Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego were Jewish exiles in Babylon who refused to worship the king's golden image, choosing to face the furnace rather than disobey God. These are their Babylonian names.

beginner.question: After King Nebuchadnezzar threw three men into the fiery furnace, he leaped to his feet in amazement. He asked his advisers how many men had been thrown in. What astonishing sight did the king see inside the furnace?
options: Four figures walking around unharmed in the fire
An army of angels filling the furnace
beginner.explanation: The king was shocked to see four men walking around freely in the fire, completely unharmed. Only three had been thrown in, so the mysterious fourth figure was a divine being — understood as an angel or the Son of God.

beginner.question: When King Nebuchadnezzar peered into the fiery furnace and saw four figures instead of three, he described the mysterious fourth person in a memorable way. How did the king describe the fourth figure's appearance?
options: Like a son of the gods
Like a pillar of shining light
Like a cloud of divine glory
beginner.explanation: Nebuchadnezzar said, 'The fourth looks like a son of the gods.' Many scholars believe this was an angel sent by God, while some see it as a pre-incarnate appearance of Christ protecting the faithful men.

beginner.question: King Nebuchadnezzar was furious when Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego refused to worship his golden statue, and he ordered the furnace heated to an extreme temperature. How hot did the king command the furnace to be compared to normal?
beginner.explanation: In his rage, Nebuchadnezzar ordered the furnace heated seven times hotter than usual. It was so hot that the soldiers who threw the three men into the furnace were killed by the intense heat.

beginner.question: After the three Hebrew men walked out of the fiery furnace unharmed, with a mysterious fourth figure having protected them, the king and all his officials examined them closely. What was remarkably absent from the three men?
options: There was no smell of fire, no singed hair, and their clothes were not burned
Their chains had melted off but they were otherwise unharmed
Their skin had turned golden from the heat
They were glowing with a supernatural light
beginner.explanation: The fire had absolutely no effect on them — not a hair was singed, their robes were not scorched, and they didn't even smell of smoke. This miraculous preservation demonstrated God's total power over the elements.

beginner.question: The reason Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego were thrown into the fiery furnace was that they refused to obey a direct command from King Nebuchadnezzar. What did they refuse to do?
options: Bow down and worship the golden statue the king had set up
Eat food that had been offered to Babylonian idols
Pray to the king as if he were a god
Serve as officers in the Babylonian army
beginner.explanation: King Nebuchadnezzar built a massive golden image and commanded everyone to bow down and worship it when music played. The three Hebrew men refused because they worshiped only the true God of Israel.

beginner.question: In Genesis, God tested Abraham by asking him to sacrifice his beloved son Isaac on Mount Moriah. Just as Abraham raised the knife, an angel intervened from heaven. What did the angel of the Lord call out?
options: Abraham! Abraham! Do not lay a hand on the boy
Stop! The Lord is pleased with your obedience
Put down your weapon, faithful servant of God
The boy shall live, for God has seen your heart
beginner.explanation: The angel urgently called Abraham's name twice — 'Abraham! Abraham!' — and told him not to harm the boy. God had seen that Abraham truly feared him and was willing to give up his only son.

beginner.question: After the angel of the Lord stopped Abraham from sacrificing his son Isaac on Mount Moriah, Abraham looked up and saw something caught in a thicket nearby. What did Abraham find to sacrifice instead of Isaac?
options: A ram caught by its horns in the thicket
A lamb caught by its wool in the bushes
A goat tangled in the branches
A dove resting on a nearby branch
beginner.explanation: Abraham looked up and saw a ram caught by its horns in a thicket. He sacrificed the ram as a burnt offering instead of his son. This is why Abraham named the place 'The Lord Will Provide.'

beginner.question: The angel of the Lord stopped Abraham from sacrificing Isaac because the test was complete. According to the angel's words, what had Abraham demonstrated by his willingness to sacrifice his only son?
options: That Abraham feared God and did not withhold his only son
That Abraham was the greatest prophet who ever lived
That Abraham deserved great earthly rewards and riches
That Abraham was worthy of becoming a mighty king
beginner.explanation: The angel said, 'Now I know that you fear God, because you have not withheld from me your son, your only son.' Abraham's willingness to obey even this extreme command proved the depth of his faith.

beginner.question: After Abraham sacrificed the ram instead of Isaac, the angel of the Lord spoke to Abraham a second time and made a covenant promise. What did God promise Abraham through the angel?
options: His descendants would be as numerous as the stars and the sand on the seashore
He would live to be two hundred years old
Isaac would become king of all nations
No enemy would ever defeat Abraham's family
beginner.explanation: God swore by himself to bless Abraham and make his descendants as numerous as the stars in the sky and the sand on the seashore. Through Abraham's offspring, all nations on earth would be blessed.

beginner.question: After the angel stopped the sacrifice of Isaac and Abraham offered the ram instead, Abraham gave a special name to that place on Mount Moriah. What name did Abraham give to the location?
beginner.explanation: Abraham named the place 'The Lord Will Provide' (Jehovah Jireh) because God had provided the ram as a substitute for Isaac. This name became a testimony that God provides for his people in their time of need.

beginner.question: In the book of Genesis, Abraham's wife Sarah had a servant named Hagar who became pregnant with Abraham's child. When Sarah treated Hagar harshly, Hagar fled into the desert. Who found Hagar at a spring in the wilderness?
options: Abraham, who came searching for her
beginner.explanation: The angel of the Lord found Hagar near a spring in the desert on the road to Shur. This is one of the first recorded appearances of the angel of the Lord in the Bible.

beginner.question: When the angel of the Lord found Hagar in the wilderness after she fled from Sarah, the angel told Hagar to name her unborn son a specific name. What name did the angel say to give her child?
beginner.explanation: The angel told Hagar to name her son Ishmael, which means 'God hears,' because the Lord had heard her misery. This name was a permanent reminder that God heard Hagar's cries of distress in the wilderness.

beginner.question: After speaking with the angel of the Lord in the wilderness, Hagar was amazed that God had seen her and spoken to her. She gave God a special name that no one else in the Bible used. What did Hagar call God?
options: The God who sees me (El Roi)
The God of mercy and comfort
beginner.explanation: Hagar called God 'El Roi,' meaning 'the God who sees me.' She was amazed that God would notice and care for a runaway servant in the desert, saying, 'I have now seen the One who sees me.'

beginner.question: Years later, after Isaac was born, Hagar and her son Ishmael were sent away by Abraham into the desert. When their water ran out and Hagar feared her son would die, she wept in despair. Who called to Hagar from heaven to rescue them?
options: A prophet traveling in the desert
beginner.explanation: The angel of God called to Hagar from heaven and told her not to be afraid, for God had heard the boy crying. God then opened her eyes to see a well of water, saving both their lives.

beginner.question: In the book of Genesis, the serpent (identified with Satan throughout the Bible) approached Eve in the Garden of Eden and tempted her to disobey God by eating fruit from a forbidden tree. Which tree did the serpent tempt Eve to eat from?
options: The tree of the knowledge of good and evil
The tree at the center of paradise
beginner.explanation: God had told Adam and Eve they could eat from any tree in the garden except the tree of the knowledge of good and evil. The serpent targeted this specific prohibition to undermine God's command.

beginner.question: When the serpent tempted Eve in the Garden of Eden, the serpent directly contradicted what God had said would happen if they ate the forbidden fruit. God warned they would die, but what lie did the serpent tell Eve?
options: You will gain power over all creatures
Nothing at all will happen to you
beginner.explanation: The serpent's first tactic was to contradict God's clear warning. By saying 'You will not certainly die,' Satan called God a liar. This bold deception led Eve to question God's truthfulness and motives.

beginner.question: The serpent in the Garden of Eden told Eve that eating the forbidden fruit would give her and Adam a special ability. According to the serpent, eating the fruit would make them like God in what specific way?
options: Having the power to create life
Being able to live forever without aging
