beginner.explanation: Jesus did NOT perform any miracle. He responded by quoting Scripture: 'Worship the Lord your God, and serve him only.' Throughout the entire temptation, Jesus relied solely on God's word, not on displays of power.

beginner.question: In the Gospel of Luke, Jesus told his seventy-two disciples, 'I saw Satan fall like lightning from heaven,' describing the swiftness and totality of Satan's fall from his former place of glory.
beginner.explanation: Luke 10:18 records Jesus' words to his disciples after they returned from a successful mission. Jesus' statement reveals his firsthand knowledge of Satan's expulsion from heaven — a cosmic event he personally witnessed.

beginner.question: The Bible teaches that Satan was once a lowly, insignificant angel with very little power, who gradually accumulated strength over time until he was strong enough to rebel against God.
beginner.explanation: The Bible suggests the opposite — passages like Ezekiel 28:12-17 describe Satan as a magnificent, powerful being ('the seal of perfection, full of wisdom and perfect in beauty') who fell due to PRIDE over his own greatness, not from a position of weakness.

beginner.question: The passage in Isaiah 14, often associated with Satan's fall, describes a being who declared 'I will ascend above the tops of the clouds; I will make myself like the Most High,' expressing extreme pride and ambition.
beginner.explanation: Isaiah 14:13-14 records five 'I will' statements of this proud being, culminating in the desire to be like God. This unchecked pride and self-exaltation is what led to his downfall and expulsion from heaven.

beginner.question: In the book of Revelation, when Satan was cast out of heaven after losing the war to Michael, he was identified as 'that ancient serpent called the devil, or Satan, who leads the whole world astray.'
beginner.explanation: Revelation 12:9 uses four names/titles for Satan: the great dragon, that ancient serpent, the devil, and Satan. Calling him 'that ancient serpent' connects him to the serpent who deceived Eve in Genesis 3.

beginner.question: According to the Bible, Satan willingly and peacefully chose to leave heaven on his own terms, departing without any conflict or forceful expulsion.
beginner.explanation: Satan was FORCIBLY thrown out of heaven. Revelation 12:9 says he was 'hurled to the earth' — the language of violent expulsion. He lost a war against Michael and his angels and was cast out against his will.

beginner.question: In the Gospels, Jesus encountered a demon-possessed man living among the tombs who was so strong that no one could bind him, even with chains, and the demons inside him identified themselves as 'Legion' because they were many.
beginner.explanation: Mark 5:1-9 describes this tormented man. He lived among tombs, cried out constantly, and cut himself with stones. When Jesus asked their name, the demons said 'Legion,' indicating a vast number of evil spirits.

beginner.question: When the demons called Legion were cast out of the possessed man by Jesus, they begged Jesus to send them into a nearby flock of sheep grazing on the hillside.
beginner.explanation: The demons begged to be sent into a herd of PIGS, not sheep. Mark 5:12 says the demons 'begged Jesus, Send us among the pigs; allow us to go into them.' Jesus permitted it, and the pigs rushed into the sea.

beginner.question: After Jesus cast the demons called Legion into a herd of about two thousand pigs, the entire herd rushed down a steep bank into the Sea of Galilee and drowned in the water.
beginner.explanation: Mark 5:13 says Jesus gave them permission, the demons came out and went into the pigs, and 'the herd, about two thousand in number, rushed down the steep bank into the lake and were drowned.'

beginner.question: When the local people came and saw the man who had been possessed by Legion now sitting clothed and in his right mind, they were afraid and asked Jesus to leave their region.
beginner.explanation: Mark 5:15-17 says the people saw the healed man and were afraid. Rather than celebrating the miracle, they 'began to plead with Jesus to leave their region' — possibly because they feared more economic loss.

beginner.question: After being healed of the demon possession called Legion, the man followed Jesus and became one of his twelve disciples, traveling with Jesus for the rest of his ministry.
beginner.explanation: The man wanted to follow Jesus, but Jesus told him to go HOME instead. Mark 5:19 says Jesus said, 'Go home to your own people and tell them how much the Lord has done for you.' The man became an evangelist in his own region.

beginner.question: Before Jesus cast out the demons called Legion, the possessed man would cry out among the tombs and in the hills and cut himself with stones, both day and night, in terrible suffering.
beginner.explanation: Mark 5:5 describes the man's constant torment: 'Night and day among the tombs and in the hills he would cry out and cut himself with stones.' The demons caused immense physical and emotional suffering.

beginner.question: The apostle Paul wrote in Ephesians that the Christian's battle is not against flesh and blood (other humans), but against spiritual rulers, authorities, and forces of evil in the heavenly realms.
beginner.explanation: Ephesians 6:12 says, 'Our struggle is not against flesh and blood, but against the rulers, against the authorities, against the powers of this dark world and against the spiritual forces of evil in the heavenly realms.'

beginner.question: According to Paul's letter to the Ephesians, the 'shield of faith' in the armor of God is designed to protect believers from physical attacks and bodily harm from their human enemies.
beginner.explanation: The shield of faith protects against SPIRITUAL attacks, not physical ones. Ephesians 6:16 says it can 'extinguish all the flaming arrows of the evil one' — Satan's attacks of doubt, temptation, lies, and discouragement.

beginner.question: Paul described the 'sword of the Spirit' in the armor of God as the Word of God, making it the only offensive weapon mentioned among the pieces of spiritual armor in Ephesians chapter 6.
beginner.explanation: All other pieces of armor (belt, breastplate, shoes, shield, helmet) are defensive. The sword of the Spirit — the Word of God — is the one offensive weapon, used to actively fight against spiritual enemies.

beginner.question: The apostle Peter warned believers to be watchful and alert because the devil prowls around like a roaring lion, looking for someone to devour, and they should resist him by standing firm in the faith.
beginner.explanation: 1 Peter 5:8-9 contains this warning: 'Be alert and of sober mind. Your enemy the devil prowls around like a roaring lion looking for someone to devour. Resist him, standing firm in the faith.'

beginner.question: The book of James teaches that if believers submit to God and resist the devil, the devil will flee from them — meaning Satan must retreat when actively resisted by faith.
beginner.explanation: James 4:7 gives this powerful promise: 'Submit yourselves, then, to God. Resist the devil, and he will flee from you.' Both parts are essential — submission to God enables effective resistance against the devil.

beginner.question: In the Gospel of Matthew, Jesus said that the angels of little children in heaven always see the face of the Father in heaven, suggesting that children have angels watching over them with direct access to God.
beginner.explanation: Matthew 18:10 records Jesus' words: 'See that you do not despise one of these little ones. For I tell you that their angels in heaven always see the face of my Father in heaven.' This is a key passage about guardian angels.

beginner.question: The Bible specifically and clearly states that every single person on earth has exactly one guardian angel assigned to them from the moment of birth.
beginner.explanation: While the Bible suggests angelic protection (Matthew 18:10, Psalm 91:11, Hebrews 1:14), it never specifically states that each person has exactly one guardian angel assigned at birth. The concept is implied but not explicitly detailed.

beginner.question: Psalm 91 promises that God will command his angels concerning those who trust in him, to guard them in all their ways and lift them up in their hands so they won't strike their foot against a stone.
beginner.explanation: Psalm 91:11-12 is one of the most well-known passages about angelic protection. It promises divine protection through angels for those who make the Lord their refuge and dwelling place.

beginner.question: The book of Hebrews describes angels as 'ministering spirits sent to serve those who will inherit salvation,' indicating that angels actively serve and help believers.
beginner.explanation: Hebrews 1:14 defines the role of angels: they are servants of God sent to minister to those who will receive salvation. Angels are not to be worshiped but are fellow servants working for God's purposes.

beginner.question: In the book of Acts, when the believers at Mary's house said 'It must be his angel' after hearing Peter was at the door, they meant that Peter had died and they were seeing a hallucination.
beginner.explanation: The believers were NOT saying Peter was dead. They reflected a common Jewish belief that each person had a guardian angel who could appear in their likeness. They thought Peter's angel had come, not that Peter was a ghost.

beginner.question: In the book of Genesis, after Adam and Eve were expelled from the Garden of Eden, God placed cherubim and a flaming sword that flashed back and forth to guard the way to the tree of life.
beginner.explanation: Genesis 3:24 says God 'placed on the east side of the Garden of Eden cherubim and a flaming sword flashing back and forth to guard the way to the tree of life.' This prevented fallen humanity from accessing eternal life.

beginner.question: In the design of the Ark of the Covenant as described in Exodus, God instructed Moses to place four golden cherubim on top of the mercy seat — one at each corner.
beginner.explanation: God instructed Moses to make TWO cherubim (not four) of hammered gold, one at each end of the mercy seat. Exodus 25:18 says, 'Make two cherubim out of hammered gold at the ends of the cover.'

beginner.question: The two golden cherubim on the Ark of the Covenant faced each other with their wings spread upward, overshadowing the mercy seat, and God said he would speak to Moses from between the two cherubim.
beginner.explanation: Exodus 25:20-22 describes the cherubim facing each other, wings overshadowing the cover, and God declared he would meet with Moses and speak to him from between the cherubim above the mercy seat.

beginner.question: In the prophet Ezekiel's vision, cherubim were described as having four faces each: the face of a cherub (or ox), a human face, a lion's face, and an eagle's face.
beginner.explanation: Ezekiel 10:14 lists the four faces of the cherubim. These four faces may symbolize different aspects of God's creation: the ox (strength/service), human (intelligence), lion (royalty/power), and eagle (swiftness/sovereignty).

beginner.question: Cherubim in the Bible are described as small, cute, chubby baby angels with tiny wings, similar to the 'cupid' figures seen in Renaissance art and Valentine's Day cards.
beginner.explanation: Biblical cherubim are NOTHING like the cute chubby babies of Renaissance art. They are powerful, awe-inspiring creatures with multiple faces, multiple wings, and a terrifying appearance. Ezekiel's visions describe them as extraordinary and fearsome beings.

beginner.question: In Isaiah's vision of God's throne room, the seraphim were angelic beings with six wings each: two covering their faces in reverence, two covering their feet in humility, and two used for flying.
beginner.explanation: Isaiah 6:2 describes the seraphim: 'Each had six wings: with two wings they covered their faces, with two they covered their feet, and with two they were flying.' Even these glorious beings showed humility before God.

beginner.question: The seraphim in Isaiah's throne room vision had four wings each and carried flaming swords as they circled around God's throne.
beginner.explanation: The seraphim had SIX wings, not four, and they did not carry swords. Isaiah 6:2 clearly states each seraph had six wings. No weapons are mentioned — the seraphim were focused on worship and praise, not combat.

beginner.question: The seraphim in Isaiah's vision called out to one another the famous words, 'Holy, holy, holy is the Lord Almighty; the whole earth is full of his glory!' and the sound shook the doorposts of the temple.
beginner.explanation: Isaiah 6:3-4 records this powerful scene: the seraphim called out this threefold declaration of God's holiness, and their voices were so powerful that the doorposts and thresholds shook and the temple filled with smoke.

beginner.question: After Isaiah confessed his unworthiness in God's presence, a seraph flew to him with a live coal from the altar and touched it to Isaiah's lips, saying his guilt was taken away and his sin atoned for.
beginner.explanation: Isaiah 6:6-7 describes this cleansing: the seraph took a burning coal with tongs from the altar, touched Isaiah's lips, and declared his guilt removed and sin forgiven. This purification prepared Isaiah for his prophetic calling.

beginner.question: The word 'seraphim' in Hebrew means 'guardian' or 'protector,' describing their primary role as bodyguards of God's heavenly throne.
beginner.explanation: The word 'seraphim' means 'burning ones' in Hebrew, likely referring to their fiery, radiant appearance in God's presence. Their primary role as described in Isaiah 6 is WORSHIP, not guarding — they ceaselessly praise God's holiness.

beginner.question: The prophet Elijah was so exhausted and afraid after Queen Jezebel threatened to kill him that he fled into the wilderness, sat under a broom tree, and prayed to God to take his life.
beginner.explanation: 1 Kings 19:3-4 shows even the great prophet Elijah reached a point of total despair. He said, 'I have had enough, Lord. Take my life.' God responded not with rebuke but with compassion, sending an angel to care for him.

beginner.question: An angel of the Lord came to the exhausted prophet Elijah as he slept under a tree in the wilderness, touched him, and provided him with bread baked over hot coals and a jar of water.
beginner.explanation: 1 Kings 19:5-6 describes the angel touching Elijah and saying, 'Get up and eat.' Elijah found bread baked over hot coals and a jar of water near his head. God met Elijah's physical needs before addressing his spiritual crisis.

beginner.question: After the angel fed Elijah in the wilderness, the supernatural strength from that food sustained Elijah for seven days and seven nights as he traveled to Mount Carmel.
beginner.explanation: The angel's food sustained Elijah for FORTY days and forty nights (not seven), and he traveled to Mount HOREB (Sinai), not Mount Carmel. Mount Carmel was where Elijah had defeated the prophets of Baal before fleeing.

beginner.question: The angel came to Elijah a second time because the journey ahead of him was too great for his own strength, and the supernatural food sustained Elijah for forty days and forty nights of travel to Mount Horeb.
beginner.explanation: 1 Kings 19:7-8 says the angel returned and said, 'Get up and eat, for the journey is too much for you.' Strengthened by the angel's food, Elijah traveled forty days and nights to Horeb, the mountain of God.

beginner.question: Elijah fled into the wilderness and was fed by an angel because he was running from King Nebuchadnezzar of Babylon, who had conquered Israel and wanted to capture the prophet.
beginner.explanation: Elijah was fleeing from QUEEN JEZEBEL, not Nebuchadnezzar. Jezebel sent a messenger to Elijah threatening to kill him because Elijah had defeated and killed her prophets of Baal on Mount Carmel. Nebuchadnezzar lived centuries later.

beginner.question: In the book of Genesis, three visitors appeared to Abraham while he sat at the entrance of his tent near the great trees of Mamre. Abraham showed them generous hospitality, and these visitors are understood to include divine messengers.
beginner.explanation: Genesis 18:1-8 describes Abraham bowing to the three visitors, washing their feet, and preparing a lavish meal. The Lord himself was among the visitors, and two of them went on to visit Sodom as angels (Genesis 19:1).

beginner.question: In the book of Judges, when an angel of the Lord appeared to Gideon and called him a 'mighty warrior,' Gideon was already leading a large army in a victorious battle against the Midianites.
beginner.explanation: Gideon was NOT leading an army — he was secretly threshing wheat in a winepress to HIDE it from the Midianites. Judges 6:11-12 shows Gideon was afraid and hiding, making the angel's greeting of 'mighty warrior' ironic and prophetic.

beginner.question: In the book of Daniel, after Daniel spent a night in the den of lions, he told King Darius that God had sent his angel to shut the lions' mouths so they could not harm him.
beginner.explanation: Daniel 6:22 records Daniel's words: 'My God sent his angel, and he shut the mouths of the lions. They have not hurt me.' God's angel protected Daniel throughout the entire night in the den of hungry lions.

beginner.question: In the book of Job, the devil approached God and asked permission to test the faith of the prophet Elijah by taking away his possessions, his family, and his health.
beginner.explanation: Satan asked to test JOB, not Elijah. Job 1:6-12 describes Satan appearing before God and challenging Job's faithfulness. God permitted Satan to test Job, resulting in the loss of his possessions, children, and eventually his health.

beginner.question: In the book of Job, Satan appeared before God along with other angelic beings (called 'sons of God') and received permission to test Job's faith by bringing terrible suffering upon him.
beginner.explanation: Job 1:6-12 says, 'One day the angels came to present themselves before the Lord, and Satan also came with them.' Satan claimed Job only worshiped God because of his blessings, and God allowed the test to prove Job's genuine faith.

beginner.question: Jesus taught that when he returns in glory at the end of time, he will come accompanied by all the angels of heaven, and they will help separate the righteous from the wicked at the final judgment.
beginner.explanation: Matthew 25:31-32 says, 'When the Son of Man comes in his glory, and all the angels with him, he will sit on his glorious throne. All the nations will be gathered before him, and he will separate the people.'

beginner.question: The Bible states that angels were created on the seventh day of creation, after God had finished making the heavens, the earth, and all living creatures.
beginner.explanation: The Bible does NOT say angels were created on the seventh day — in fact, the seventh day was when God RESTED from all his work. The exact timing of angelic creation is not specified, but Job 38:4-7 suggests angels existed before the earth was completed.

beginner.question: In the book of Acts, an angel of the Lord told Philip the evangelist to travel south to the desert road from Jerusalem to Gaza, where Philip met and baptized an Ethiopian official who was reading the book of Isaiah.
beginner.explanation: Acts 8:26-38 describes how an angel directed Philip to a specific road where he encountered an Ethiopian eunuch reading Isaiah 53. Philip explained the gospel, and the Ethiopian believed and was baptized on the spot.

beginner.question: In the book of Revelation, when the apostle John fell at an angel's feet to worship him, the angel accepted the worship and told John that angels deserve honor alongside God.
beginner.explanation: The angel immediately REFUSED worship. Revelation 22:8-9 says the angel told John, 'Don't do that! I am a fellow servant with you. Worship God!' Angels consistently refuse worship and redirect all praise to God alone.

beginner.question: In the parable of the rich man and Lazarus told by Jesus, when the poor beggar Lazarus died, angels carried him to Abraham's side — a place of comfort and rest.
beginner.explanation: Luke 16:22 says, 'The time came when the beggar died and the angels carried him to Abraham's side.' This parable portrays angels as escorts for the righteous into the presence of God's comfort after death.

beginner.question: The apostle Paul warned in 2 Corinthians that Satan masquerades as an 'angel of light,' meaning the devil can disguise himself as something attractive and good rather than appearing obviously evil.
beginner.explanation: 2 Corinthians 11:14 says, 'Satan himself masquerades as an angel of light.' This is a crucial warning — evil often presents itself as appealing and righteous, making spiritual discernment essential for believers.

beginner.question: In the Gospel of Matthew, it was Jesus who taught that Satan disguises himself as an angel of light to deceive the unwary.
beginner.explanation: It was the apostle PAUL who wrote this warning, not Jesus. Paul wrote in 2 Corinthians 11:14, 'Satan himself masquerades as an angel of light.' While Jesus taught much about Satan, this specific teaching came from Paul's letters.

beginner.question: The book of Hebrews encourages believers to show hospitality to strangers because by doing so, some people have 'entertained angels without knowing it.'
beginner.explanation: Hebrews 13:2 says, 'Do not forget to show hospitality to strangers, for by so doing some people have entertained angels without knowing it.' This echoes stories like Abraham hosting three visitors who turned out to be divine messengers.

beginner.question: The book of Revelation describes the devil as having red skin, horns, a pointed tail, and carrying a pitchfork — his most well-known physical description in the Bible.
beginner.explanation: The Bible NEVER describes the devil with red skin, horns, or a pitchfork — those images come from medieval art and popular culture. Revelation describes Satan as 'a great dragon' and 'that ancient serpent,' not the red-horned figure of folklore.

beginner.question: Jesus taught that in the resurrection, people will neither marry nor be given in marriage, but will be like the angels in heaven, which he said in response to a question from the Sadducees.
beginner.explanation: Matthew 22:30 records Jesus' answer to the Sadducees: 'At the resurrection people will neither marry nor be given in marriage; they will be like the angels in heaven.' The Sadducees had posed a trick question about marriage after death.

beginner.question: In the book of Revelation, Satan is described as 'the great dragon' and 'that ancient serpent,' connecting him to the serpent from the Garden of Eden who deceived Eve.
beginner.explanation: Revelation 12:9 uses both titles: 'The great dragon was hurled down — that ancient serpent called the devil, or Satan.' By calling Satan 'that ancient serpent,' Revelation explicitly links him to the serpent in Genesis 3.

beginner.question: In the book of Zechariah, the prophet had a vision of the high priest Joshua standing before the angel of the Lord, with Satan standing at Joshua's right hand to accuse him, but the Lord rebuked Satan.
beginner.explanation: Zechariah 3:1-2 describes Satan in his role as accuser, standing to oppose the high priest. But the Lord rebuked Satan and had Joshua's filthy garments replaced with clean ones, symbolizing forgiveness and restoration.

beginner.question: When Jesus sent out seventy-two disciples to preach and heal, the disciples reported that even the demons submitted to them when they used their own personal authority and spiritual power.
beginner.explanation: The demons submitted to the disciples IN JESUS' NAME, not by their own power. Luke 10:17 says the disciples reported, 'Lord, even the demons submit to us in your name.' The authority came from Jesus, not from the disciples themselves.

beginner.question: When Jesus sent out his seventy-two disciples on a preaching mission, they returned with joy, reporting that even the demons obeyed and submitted to them when they commanded the demons in Jesus' name.
beginner.explanation: Luke 10:17 records the disciples' excited report: 'Lord, even the demons submit to us in your name.' Jesus had given them spiritual authority, and it extended even over demonic forces when exercised in his name.

beginner.question: In the Gospels, when the Pharisees accused Jesus of casting out demons by the power of Beelzebul (the prince of demons), Jesus responded that a kingdom divided against itself cannot stand.
beginner.explanation: Mark 3:22-26 records this exchange. Jesus used simple logic: if Satan casts out Satan, he's fighting himself and his kingdom would collapse. Jesus was actually casting out demons by the power of God's Spirit, not by Satan's help.

beginner.question: According to the book of Revelation, at the very end of time after Satan's final rebellion, Satan will be given one last chance to repent and return to heaven.
beginner.explanation: Satan is given NO chance to repent. Revelation 20:10 says, 'The devil, who deceived them, was thrown into the lake of burning sulfur, where he will be tormented day and night for ever and ever.' His judgment is final and eternal.

beginner.question: In the book of Daniel, an angel told Daniel that the powerful spiritual being called 'the prince of Persia' had opposed the angel for twenty-one days, until Michael the archangel came to help him break through.
beginner.explanation: Daniel 10:13 reveals invisible spiritual warfare: the angel was delayed by the prince of Persia for three weeks until Michael intervened. This passage shows angelic and demonic powers engaged in conflict behind world events.

beginner.question: In the book of Genesis, when Jacob was traveling back home to meet his brother Esau, he encountered angels of God on his journey and exclaimed, 'This is the camp of God!' naming the place Mahanaim.
beginner.explanation: Genesis 32:1-2 says, 'Jacob also went on his way, and the angels of God met him. When Jacob saw them, he said, This is the camp of God! So he named that place Mahanaim' (meaning 'two camps').

beginner.question: In Revelation chapter 12, after Satan was thrown out of heaven by Michael's forces, Satan accepted his defeat peacefully and stopped trying to oppose God's people on earth.
beginner.explanation: Satan did NOT accept defeat peacefully. Revelation 12:12,17 says the devil went down to earth 'filled with fury' and went off to 'wage war against the rest of her offspring — those who keep God's commands.' Satan became even more aggressive.

beginner.question: In the book of Genesis, a crafty animal approached Eve in the Garden of Eden and tempted her to eat the forbidden fruit from the tree God had told them not to eat from. What kind of animal spoke to Eve?
beginner.explanation: Genesis 3:1 says the serpent was more cunning than any other beast of the field. The serpent deceived Eve by telling her she would not die if she ate the fruit, leading to the Fall of humanity.

beginner.question: In Genesis 3, the serpent told Eve that if she ate the forbidden fruit, she would not die but instead gain something special. According to the serpent's lie, what would happen if Eve ate the fruit?
options: She would live forever without God
She would gain wings to fly
Her eyes would be opened and she would be like God, knowing good and evil
beginner.explanation: The serpent told Eve that God knew eating the fruit would make them like God, knowing good and evil. This was a half-truth used to deceive Eve into disobeying God's command.

beginner.question: In Genesis 3, after the serpent deceived Eve and she and Adam ate the forbidden fruit, God pronounced a curse on the serpent. According to God's punishment, what would the serpent crawl on for the rest of its days?
beginner.explanation: God cursed the serpent above all livestock and wild animals, saying it would crawl on its belly and eat dust all the days of its life. This was part of the consequences of the serpent's role in tempting Eve.

beginner.question: In the Garden of Eden story told in Genesis 3, the Bible describes the serpent with a specific characteristic that set it apart from all other wild animals God had made. What trait did the serpent have?
beginner.explanation: Genesis 3:1 says the serpent was more crafty or cunning than any of the wild animals the Lord God had made. This craftiness enabled it to deceive Eve with twisting words and half-truths.

beginner.question: When God cursed the serpent in Genesis 3:15, He also made a promise about an ongoing conflict. God said there would be enmity (hostility) between the serpent and whom?
options: The other animals in the garden
beginner.explanation: God told the serpent that He would put enmity between the serpent and the woman, and between the serpent's offspring and her offspring. Many Christians see this as the first prophecy of Jesus who would ultimately defeat Satan.

beginner.question: In the Garden of Eden, the serpent specifically tempted Eve to eat fruit from a tree that God had told Adam and Eve not to eat from. What was this forbidden tree called in Genesis 2-3?
options: The Tree of Knowledge of Good and Evil
beginner.explanation: God told Adam he could eat from any tree in the garden except the Tree of the Knowledge of Good and Evil. The serpent targeted this specific prohibition to tempt Eve into disobedience.

beginner.question: After the serpent deceived Eve into eating the forbidden fruit in Genesis 3, what did Eve do next with the fruit?
options: She threw it away in guilt
She also gave some to Adam, who was with her, and he ate it
She offered it back to the serpent
beginner.explanation: After Eve ate the forbidden fruit, she gave some to her husband Adam, who was with her, and he ate it too. This act of disobedience by both Adam and Eve is known as the Fall.

beginner.question: In the book of Revelation 12:9, the Bible reveals the true identity behind the serpent that deceived Eve in the Garden of Eden. What name does Revelation give to the ancient serpent?
beginner.explanation: Revelation 12:9 says 'that ancient serpent called the devil, or Satan, who leads the whole world astray.' This identifies the serpent from Genesis as Satan himself, who used the form of a serpent to deceive humanity.

beginner.question: In the story of Noah's ark in Genesis 6-9, God told Noah to build a massive boat and bring animals onto it to save them from a great flood. How many of each kind of 'clean' animal did God instruct Noah to take aboard?
beginner.explanation: In Genesis 7:2, God told Noah to take seven pairs of every kind of clean animal and one pair of every kind of unclean animal. Clean animals were those acceptable for sacrifice and food under later Israelite law.

beginner.question: After the great flood described in Genesis 8, Noah sent out a bird from the ark to see if the floodwaters had receded. This bird came back carrying an olive leaf in its beak, a sign that dry land was reappearing. What kind of bird was it?
beginner.explanation: Noah sent out a dove, and it returned with a freshly plucked olive leaf in its beak. This showed Noah that the waters had receded enough for plants to grow again. The dove with an olive branch has become a universal symbol of peace.

beginner.question: In Genesis 8, before Noah sent out the dove from the ark, he first sent out a different bird to check whether the floodwaters had dried up. This bird flew back and forth over the waters but did not return to the ark. What kind of bird was it?
beginner.explanation: Noah first sent out a raven, which kept flying back and forth until the water had dried up from the earth. Unlike the dove, the raven did not return to the ark, likely because it could feed on floating debris.

beginner.question: In the story of Noah's ark found in Genesis 7, God told Noah to bring pairs of every kind of 'unclean' animal aboard the ark. How many of each unclean animal was Noah told to bring?
options: One pair (two of each, male and female)
beginner.explanation: God instructed Noah to bring two of every kind of unclean animal — one male and one female — to keep them alive through the flood. Clean animals were brought in larger numbers (seven pairs) since some would be used for sacrifice.

beginner.question: According to Genesis 6, why did God decide to send a great flood upon the earth and instruct Noah to build an ark to save his family and the animals?
options: Because the earth was filled with human wickedness and violence
Because the animals needed to be relocated
Because the rivers had overflowed naturally
Because Noah asked God for rain
beginner.explanation: Genesis 6:5-7 says God saw that human wickedness on earth was great and that every thought of the human heart was only evil all the time. God was grieved and decided to wipe out humanity, but Noah found favour in His eyes.

beginner.question: In Genesis 8, when Noah sent the dove out for the third and final time from the ark, something different happened compared to the first two times. What happened on the third attempt?
options: The dove returned with a branch
The dove did not return at all, because it had found dry land
beginner.explanation: When Noah sent the dove out the third time, it did not return to him. This told Noah that the earth was dry enough for the dove to find a place to live, and it was nearly time to leave the ark.

beginner.question: After Noah, his family, and all the animals left the ark following the great flood, God placed a sign in the sky as a promise that He would never again destroy the entire earth with a flood. What was this sign?
beginner.explanation: God set a rainbow in the clouds as the sign of His covenant with Noah and all living creatures. Whenever the rainbow appears in the clouds, it serves as a reminder of God's promise never to flood the entire earth again.

beginner.question: In Genesis 7, when God sent the great flood in the days of Noah, rain fell upon the earth for a specific number of days and nights. According to Genesis 7:12, how long did it rain?
beginner.explanation: The Bible says it rained for forty days and forty nights, causing the floodwaters to rise and cover even the highest mountains. The number forty appears frequently in the Bible to signify a period of testing or judgment.

beginner.question: According to Genesis 6:14-16, God gave Noah specific instructions for building the ark that would carry the animals through the flood. What material was Noah told to use to build the ark?
beginner.explanation: God told Noah to make the ark out of gopherwood (often translated as cypress wood) and to coat it with pitch inside and out to make it waterproof. God also gave Noah specific measurements for the ark's length, width, and height.

beginner.question: In the story of Noah's ark, the Bible tells us that Noah had three sons who entered the ark with him, along with their wives. Which of the following is NOT one of Noah's three sons?
beginner.explanation: Noah's three sons were Shem, Ham, and Japheth. Lot was actually Abraham's nephew, not one of Noah's sons. After the flood, the descendants of Shem, Ham, and Japheth repopulated the earth.

beginner.question: After Noah and the animals safely left the ark following the great flood, Noah built an altar and made a sacrifice to God. What kind of animals did Noah sacrifice, according to Genesis 8:20?
beginner.explanation: Noah took some of every kind of clean animal and clean bird and sacrificed burnt offerings on the altar. This is one reason God had instructed Noah to bring seven pairs of clean animals — so there would be enough for sacrifice and still preserve the species.

beginner.question: In Genesis 7, the Bible tells us Noah was a certain age when the floodwaters came upon the earth. How old was Noah when the flood began?
beginner.explanation: Genesis 7:6 states that Noah was six hundred years old when the floodwaters came on the earth. People in the early chapters of Genesis lived much longer than people do today. Noah lived to be 950 years old in total.

beginner.question: In the book of Jonah, God told the prophet Jonah to go and preach to the wicked city of Nineveh. Instead of obeying, Jonah ran away and boarded a ship sailing in the opposite direction. God sent a great storm, and Jonah was thrown overboard into the sea. What happened to Jonah next?
options: A dolphin carried him to land
He was rescued by another ship
beginner.explanation: God provided a great fish (often called a whale) to swallow Jonah. Inside the fish, Jonah prayed to God and repented for running away. This miraculous event taught Jonah that he could not run from God's commands.

beginner.question: After Jonah was swallowed by the great fish in the book of Jonah, he spent time inside the creature praying and repenting. According to Jonah 1:17, how many days and nights was Jonah inside the great fish?
beginner.explanation: Jonah was in the belly of the great fish for three days and three nights. During this time, he prayed earnestly to God. Jesus later compared His own time in the tomb to Jonah's three days in the fish.

beginner.question: In the book of Jonah, after spending three days and nights inside the great fish and praying to God, Jonah was released. How did Jonah get out of the fish?
options: The fish swam to shore and opened its mouth
The fish vomited Jonah onto dry land
beginner.explanation: Jonah 2:10 says the Lord commanded the fish, and it vomited Jonah onto dry land. After this dramatic experience, Jonah obeyed God and went to preach to the people of Nineveh as originally commanded.

beginner.question: In the story of Jonah, God originally told him to go preach to the people of a great city because of their wickedness. Which city did God send Jonah to?
beginner.explanation: God told Jonah to go to Nineveh, the capital of the Assyrian Empire (in modern-day Iraq). Nineveh was a huge, powerful city known for its wickedness, and Jonah did not want to go because the Assyrians were enemies of Israel.

beginner.question: In Jonah chapter 1, when the great storm hit the ship that Jonah had boarded while fleeing from God, the sailors were terrified and each cried out to their own gods. Meanwhile, what was Jonah doing during the storm?
beginner.explanation: While the storm raged and the sailors panicked, Jonah had gone below deck and had fallen into a deep sleep. The ship's captain found him and told him to get up and call on his God for help.

beginner.question: After the great fish vomited Jonah onto dry land, God gave Jonah the same command a second time: go to Nineveh and preach. This time Jonah obeyed. How did the people of Nineveh respond to Jonah's preaching?
options: They repented, fasted, and turned from their evil ways
They chased him out of the city
beginner.explanation: The people of Nineveh believed God's message through Jonah. From the king down to the common people, they declared a fast, put on sackcloth, and turned from their evil ways. God saw their repentance and did not bring the destruction He had threatened.

beginner.question: In Jonah chapter 1, when the terrible storm threatened to destroy the ship, the frightened sailors cast lots to find out who was responsible for the calamity. The lot fell on Jonah. What did Jonah tell the sailors to do to calm the storm?
options: Pick him up and throw him into the sea
beginner.explanation: Jonah told the sailors to throw him into the sea, and the storm would stop. He knew the storm was his fault for running from God. The sailors were reluctant at first and tried to row to shore, but eventually they threw Jonah overboard, and the sea became calm.

beginner.question: In the New Testament, Jesus compared His own coming death and resurrection to Jonah's experience inside the great fish. In Matthew 12:40, Jesus said just as Jonah was three days in the belly of the fish, so He would be three days and nights where?
options: In the heart of the earth
beginner.explanation: Jesus used Jonah's three days in the great fish as a sign pointing to His own death, burial, and resurrection. Just as Jonah emerged alive after three days, Jesus would rise from the grave after three days in the earth.

beginner.question: In Numbers 22, a prophet named Balaam was riding his donkey on a road when an angel of the Lord blocked the path. Only the donkey could see the angel, and it refused to move forward, so Balaam beat it. God then performed an amazing miracle. What happened?
options: The donkey spoke to Balaam in human words
The donkey grew wings and flew
The donkey turned into a horse
The donkey vanished into thin air
beginner.explanation: God opened the donkey's mouth and it spoke to Balaam, asking why he had beaten it three times. This is one of only two times in the Bible where an animal speaks with human words. God then opened Balaam's eyes to see the angel.

beginner.question: In the story of Balaam in Numbers 22, a foreign king wanted to hire the prophet Balaam to do something against the Israelites. What did King Balak of Moab want Balaam to do?
options: Build a wall to keep Israel out
beginner.explanation: King Balak of Moab was afraid of the large Israelite nation camped near his borders. He sent messengers to Balaam, a well-known prophet, offering him payment to come and pronounce a curse on Israel, believing Balaam's curses were powerful.

beginner.question: In Numbers 22, when Balaam's donkey saw the angel of the Lord standing in the road with a drawn sword, the donkey tried to avoid the angel three different times. What did the donkey do the very first time it saw the angel?
options: It lay down on the ground
It pressed against a wall, crushing Balaam's foot
It turned off the road into a field
beginner.explanation: The first time the donkey saw the angel, it turned off the road and went into a field. Balaam beat the donkey to turn it back onto the road, not realising an angel was blocking the way.

beginner.question: In Numbers 22:28, after Balaam struck his donkey three times for refusing to move forward, God opened the donkey's mouth and it spoke. What did the donkey say to Balaam?
options: 'I have been your faithful donkey for years'
'What have I done to you to make you beat me these three times?'
beginner.explanation: The donkey asked Balaam what it had done to deserve being beaten three times. Balaam actually answered the donkey, saying it had made a fool of him. God then opened Balaam's eyes to see the angel with the drawn sword.

beginner.question: In Numbers 22, after the donkey spoke and God opened Balaam's eyes, Balaam finally saw what his donkey had seen all along. What did Balaam see standing in the road?
options: The angel of the Lord with a drawn sword
beginner.explanation: Balaam saw the angel of the Lord standing in the road with a drawn sword in his hand. Balaam bowed low and fell face down. The angel told Balaam that if the donkey had not turned away, the angel would have killed Balaam.

beginner.question: In Numbers 22-24, even though King Balak of Moab pressured the prophet Balaam three times to curse the Israelites, Balaam found he could only speak what God told him to say. Instead of cursing Israel, what did Balaam end up doing?
options: He remained silent and said nothing
He fled back to his homeland
beginner.explanation: Each time Balaam tried to curse Israel as King Balak requested, God put words of blessing in Balaam's mouth instead. Balaam blessed the Israelites three times, much to King Balak's anger and frustration.

beginner.question: In Numbers 22, the angel of the Lord told Balaam something shocking about the donkey that had saved his life. The angel said that if the donkey had not turned away from him those three times, the angel would have done what?
options: Killed Balaam but let the donkey live
beginner.explanation: The angel told Balaam that the donkey had actually saved his life by turning aside. If the donkey had continued straight ahead, the angel would have killed Balaam but spared the donkey. Balaam's faithful donkey had been protecting him all along.

beginner.question: In Daniel 6, the prophet Daniel served faithfully under King Darius of Persia. Jealous officials tricked the king into making a law that anyone who prayed to any god or person other than the king for 30 days would be punished. Daniel kept praying to God, and as punishment he was thrown into a den full of what animals?
beginner.explanation: Daniel was thrown into a den of lions because he refused to stop praying to God. Despite the terrifying punishment, Daniel trusted God, and God sent an angel to shut the lions' mouths so they could not harm him.

beginner.question: In Daniel 6, after Daniel was thrown into the lions' den for praying to God, King Darius could not sleep all night because he was worried about Daniel. The next morning, the king rushed to the den and called out to Daniel. What had happened to Daniel?
options: God sent an angel who shut the lions' mouths, and Daniel was completely unharmed
Daniel had managed to escape through a secret exit
The lions had been taken away during the night
beginner.explanation: Daniel was alive and uninjured. He told the king that God had sent an angel to shut the lions' mouths because he was found innocent before God and had done no wrong to the king. Daniel's faith in God protected him.

beginner.question: In the story of Daniel and the lions' den in Daniel 6, Daniel was known for his devoted prayer life. Even after the law was passed forbidding prayer to anyone but the king, how many times per day did Daniel continue to pray to God?
beginner.explanation: Daniel prayed three times a day, just as he had always done. He went to his upstairs room, opened his windows toward Jerusalem, and got down on his knees to pray and give thanks to God, despite the new law.

beginner.question: In Daniel 6, after God miraculously rescued Daniel from the lions' den, King Darius issued a new decree to all the people in his vast kingdom. What did King Darius decree?
options: That Daniel should be banished from the kingdom
That no one should ever pray again
That the lions should all be released into the wild
That everyone must fear and reverence Daniel's God, the living God
beginner.explanation: King Darius was so amazed by Daniel's rescue that he issued a decree commanding all people to fear and reverence the God of Daniel. He declared that Daniel's God is the living God who rescues and saves and performs signs and wonders.

beginner.question: In Daniel 6, the jealous officials who had tricked King Darius into throwing Daniel into the lions' den faced serious consequences after Daniel was rescued. What happened to those officials?
options: They were thrown into the lions' den, and the lions overpowered them immediately
They were sent into exile in a distant land
They were forgiven by the king
They were promoted to higher positions
beginner.explanation: At the king's command, the men who had falsely accused Daniel were thrown into the lions' den, along with their wives and children. Before they even reached the floor of the den, the lions overpowered them and crushed their bones.

beginner.question: In Daniel 6, after Daniel was placed in the lions' den, a stone was rolled over the opening to seal him inside. What did King Darius do to the stone to make sure no one could tamper with it?
options: He posted armed guards around it
He sealed it with his own royal signet ring and the rings of his nobles
He built a wall around the den
beginner.explanation: The king sealed the stone with his own signet ring and the signet rings of his nobles. This ensured that Daniel's situation could not be changed by anyone — making God's miraculous rescue even more clearly a divine act.

beginner.question: In Daniel 6:10, when Daniel prayed three times a day in his upstairs room, the Bible says he opened his windows and faced a specific direction while praying. Which direction did Daniel face?
beginner.explanation: Daniel faced toward Jerusalem when he prayed. Jerusalem was the holy city where the temple of God had stood, and it was the direction faithful Jews prayed toward when they were far from home, following Solomon's prayer in 1 Kings 8:48.

beginner.question: In Daniel 6, King Darius was deeply distressed after he was forced to throw Daniel into the lions' den. The king spent that entire night in a very specific way. How did King Darius spend the night?
options: He held a banquet to distract himself
He consulted his advisors for a rescue plan
He fasted, refused entertainment, and could not sleep
beginner.explanation: King Darius spent the night without eating, refused any entertainment, and could not sleep. He clearly cared about Daniel and was anxious about his fate. At the first light of dawn, the king hurried to the lions' den.

beginner.question: In Judges 14, a mighty Israelite judge named Samson was walking toward the vineyards of Timnah when a young lion suddenly came roaring at him. The Spirit of the Lord came powerfully upon Samson. What did Samson do to the lion?
options: He tore it apart with his bare hands
He used a sword to kill it
He tamed it and kept it as a pet
beginner.explanation: When the Spirit of the Lord came upon Samson, he tore the lion apart with his bare hands as easily as one might tear apart a young goat. Samson's incredible strength was a gift from God related to his Nazirite vow.

beginner.question: In Judges 14, some time after Samson killed the young lion near Timnah, he returned to the same spot and found something surprising inside the lion's carcass. What did Samson discover?
options: A swarm of bees and honey
beginner.explanation: Samson found a swarm of bees and honey in the carcass of the dead lion. He scooped out the honey with his hands and ate some as he walked along, and also gave some to his parents, but he did not tell them where it came from.

beginner.question: In Judges 14, Samson used what he had found in the dead lion's body to make a clever riddle. He challenged thirty Philistine men at his wedding feast to solve it within seven days. What was Samson's famous riddle?
options: 'What is lighter than a feather but harder to hold?'
'Out of the eater, something to eat; out of the strong, something sweet'
'What walks on four legs in the morning and two at noon?'
beginner.explanation: Samson's riddle referred to the honey he found in the lion's carcass — the 'eater' (the lion) produced 'something to eat' (honey), and the 'strong' one produced 'something sweet.' The Philistines could not solve it without cheating.

beginner.question: In the book of Judges, Samson was an Israelite judge known for his extraordinary, God-given strength. According to Judges 13-16, what was the physical symbol of Samson's Nazirite vow to God — the thing that, if removed, would cause him to lose his strength?
beginner.explanation: Samson's strength came from his Nazirite vow to God, and his uncut hair was the outward sign of that vow. When Delilah eventually cut his hair, the Spirit of the Lord left him and he lost his superhuman strength.

beginner.question: In Judges 14, when Samson tore apart the young lion near the vineyards of Timnah with his bare hands, did he tell his parents what he had done?
options: No, he did not tell his father or mother
Yes, he told them right away
beginner.explanation: The Bible specifically says that Samson did not tell his father or mother what he had done. This was a private display of God-given strength. Later, when he found honey in the carcass, he gave some to his parents without telling them where it came from.

beginner.question: In Judges 14, Samson bet thirty Philistine men at his wedding feast that they could not solve his riddle about the lion and the honey within seven days. If they solved it, what did Samson promise to give them?
options: Thirty sets of clothes and thirty linen garments
beginner.explanation: Samson wagered thirty linen garments and thirty sets of clothes. The Philistines eventually solved the riddle by pressuring Samson's wife into getting the answer from him and telling them.

beginner.question: In 1 Samuel 17, a young shepherd boy named David volunteered to fight the giant warrior Goliath. When King Saul doubted David's ability, David explained that while tending his father's sheep he had fought and killed two dangerous wild animals. Which two animals had David killed?
beginner.explanation: David told King Saul that when a lion or a bear came and carried off a sheep from his flock, he would go after it, strike it, and rescue the sheep from its mouth. David's courage as a shepherd prepared him for his battle with Goliath.

beginner.question: In 1 Samuel 17, David told King Saul how he would protect his father's sheep as a shepherd. When a lion or bear took a sheep from the flock, what would young David do?
options: He would call for help from other shepherds
He would let the animal go and count it as a loss
He would set a trap and wait
He would chase the animal, strike it, and rescue the sheep from its mouth
beginner.explanation: David described how he would bravely pursue any predator that attacked his flock. He would chase it down, grab it by its fur, strike it, and rescue the lamb from its jaws. David credited God with delivering him from these beasts.

beginner.question: In 1 Samuel 17, the young shepherd David used his experience of protecting sheep from a lion and a bear to convince King Saul that God would help him defeat Goliath. What weapon did David famously use to defeat the Philistine giant?
beginner.explanation: David took five smooth stones from a stream and used his shepherd's sling to hurl a stone at Goliath, striking the giant in the forehead. Goliath fell face down on the ground, and David triumphed without conventional armour or weapons.

beginner.question: Before David became a great warrior and eventually king of Israel, he was a humble shepherd boy tending his father's sheep in the fields near Bethlehem. According to 1 Samuel 16, who was David's father?
beginner.explanation: David was the youngest son of Jesse of Bethlehem. When the prophet Samuel came to Jesse's house to anoint the next king of Israel, David was out in the fields tending the sheep and had to be called in.

beginner.question: King David, who had been a shepherd in his youth protecting sheep from lions and bears, wrote a famous psalm comparing God to a shepherd. What is the beloved opening line of Psalm 23?
options: 'Create in me a clean heart, O God'
'The Lord is my shepherd, I shall not want'
'The heavens declare the glory of God'
beginner.explanation: Psalm 23 begins with 'The Lord is my shepherd, I shall not want,' drawing on David's own experience as a shepherd. Just as David protected and cared for his sheep, he trusted that God would protect and provide for him.

beginner.question: In 1 Samuel 17:37, after David told King Saul about killing a lion and a bear that attacked his sheep, David expressed confidence that God would deliver him from a new threat as well. Who was David confident God would deliver him from?
beginner.explanation: David said the Lord who rescued him from the paw of the lion and the paw of the bear would also rescue him from the hand of the Philistine giant Goliath. Saul then gave David permission to go fight Goliath.

beginner.question: In 1 Samuel 16:11-13, when the prophet Samuel came to Jesse's house to anoint the next king of Israel, David was not present at first because he was away doing something. What was David doing when Samuel arrived?
options: Tending the sheep in the fields
beginner.explanation: David was out in the fields tending his father's sheep. He was the youngest of Jesse's sons and had been given the humble job of shepherding. When he was brought in, the Lord told Samuel to anoint him as the future king of Israel.

beginner.question: In 1 Kings 17, during a severe drought and famine in Israel, God told the prophet Elijah to hide by a brook called Cherith. God miraculously sent birds to deliver food to Elijah every morning and evening. What kind of birds brought Elijah his meals?
beginner.explanation: God commanded ravens to bring food to Elijah. The ravens brought him bread and meat every morning and every evening, and he drank water from the brook. This was a remarkable miracle of God's provision during the famine.

beginner.question: In 1 Kings 17, when the ravens miraculously brought food to the prophet Elijah at the brook Cherith each morning and evening, what specific types of food did the ravens deliver?
beginner.explanation: The ravens brought Elijah bread and meat in the morning, and bread and meat in the evening. God supernaturally provided for His prophet through these birds during one of the worst famines Israel had ever experienced.

beginner.question: In 1 Kings 17, while the prophet Elijah was hiding by the brook Cherith and being fed by ravens, what did Elijah drink?
beginner.explanation: God told Elijah to drink from the brook Cherith. The ravens provided food and the brook provided water. However, eventually the brook dried up because there was no rain in the land, and God sent Elijah elsewhere.

beginner.question: In 1 Kings 17, Elijah had proclaimed that there would be no rain in Israel as God's judgment on the nation's wickedness. Which wicked king and queen was Elijah standing against during this time?
options: King Solomon and the Queen of Sheba
beginner.explanation: Elijah opposed King Ahab and his wife Queen Jezebel, who had led Israel into the worship of the false god Baal. The drought was God's judgment on Israel's idolatry, and Elijah was God's messenger during this dark period.

beginner.question: In 1 Kings 17, after the brook Cherith dried up due to the ongoing drought, God told Elijah to leave and go to a specific town where a widow would provide for him. Where did God send Elijah next?
beginner.explanation: God sent Elijah to Zarephath, a town in the region of Sidon. There, a poor widow fed Elijah with a miraculous supply of flour and oil that never ran out during the entire drought, just as God had promised.

beginner.question: In 1 Kings 17-18, the drought that caused Elijah to depend on ravens for food at the brook Cherith lasted for a very long time. According to 1 Kings 18:1 and James 5:17, how long did the drought and famine last?
beginner.explanation: The drought lasted three and a half years. It only ended after Elijah's dramatic confrontation with the prophets of Baal on Mount Carmel, when God sent fire from heaven and then finally sent rain again upon the land.

beginner.question: In the book of Exodus (chapters 7-12), God sent ten terrible plagues upon Egypt to convince Pharaoh to free the Israelite slaves. In the second plague, swarms of a particular animal emerged from the rivers, streams, and ponds and covered the entire land. What animal was it?
beginner.explanation: In the second plague, frogs came up from the Nile and invaded all of Egypt — entering houses, bedrooms, beds, ovens, and kneading troughs. The frogs were everywhere, making life in Egypt unbearable.

beginner.question: In Exodus 8, during the plagues God sent upon Egypt, one plague involved massive swarms of a flying insect that filled the houses of the Egyptians. What insect swarmed throughout the land in the fourth plague?
beginner.explanation: Dense swarms of flies invaded Pharaoh's palace and the houses of his officials throughout Egypt. The land was ruined by the flies. Notably, God protected the Israelites in the region of Goshen from this plague.

beginner.question: In Exodus 10, one of the ten plagues God sent upon Egypt involved a massive swarm of insects that devoured every plant, tree, and crop in the entire land, leaving absolutely nothing green. What insect caused this devastation?
beginner.explanation: A wind from the east brought a plague of locusts that covered the ground until it was black. They ate everything growing in the fields and all the fruit on the trees. Nothing green remained on any tree or plant in all the land of Egypt.

beginner.question: In Exodus 9, God sent a severe plague that killed the Egyptians' livestock in the fields — their horses, donkeys, camels, cattle, sheep, and goats. However, not a single animal belonging to one group was harmed. Whose animals were spared?
beginner.explanation: Not one animal belonging to the Israelites died during this plague. God made a distinction between the livestock of Israel and the livestock of Egypt, demonstrating His protection over His people.

beginner.question: In Exodus 8:16-19, God told Moses to have Aaron strike the dust of the ground with his staff, and tiny biting insects swarmed over all the people and animals in Egypt. This was the third plague. What were these insects?
beginner.explanation: When Aaron struck the dust with his staff, all the dust throughout Egypt became gnats (or lice, depending on the translation). The Egyptian magicians tried to reproduce this plague but could not, and they told Pharaoh, 'This is the finger of God.'

beginner.question: In Exodus 7, the very first plague God sent upon Egypt affected all the water in the land. The Nile River and all the water in Egypt was turned into something that killed all the fish and made the water undrinkable. What was the water turned into?
beginner.explanation: God turned all the water in Egypt into blood. The fish in the Nile died, the river smelled terrible, and the Egyptians could not drink the water. This first plague struck at the heart of Egypt's dependence on the Nile River.

beginner.question: In Exodus 8, when the plague of frogs struck Egypt, the frogs invaded absolutely everywhere. According to Exodus 8:3, where did the frogs go?
options: Only into the rivers and ponds
Into the houses, bedrooms, beds, ovens, and kneading troughs
Only into the fields and gardens
beginner.explanation: The frogs were so numerous they got into every part of Egyptian life — their houses, bedrooms, beds, ovens, and even where they mixed their bread dough. It was impossible to escape them anywhere in Egypt.

beginner.question: In Exodus 10:13-15, God brought the devastating plague of locusts upon Egypt by sending a powerful wind that blew the insects into the land. From which direction did this wind blow the locusts into Egypt?
beginner.explanation: God made an east wind blow across the land all that day and all that night. By morning, the east wind had brought the locusts. They invaded all of Egypt and settled on every part of the country in enormous numbers.

beginner.question: During the plagues of Egypt in Exodus, the Israelites living in the region of Goshen were often miraculously protected from the plagues. When the plague killed all the Egyptian livestock, what happened to the Israelites' animals?
options: They were taken by the Egyptians
Not a single one of the Israelites' animals died
beginner.explanation: God made a clear distinction between the Egyptians' livestock and the Israelites' livestock. Not one animal belonging to the Israelites died, while all the Egyptian livestock in the fields perished. This showed God's protection over His people.

beginner.question: In Exodus 8, when the plague of frogs made life unbearable throughout Egypt, Pharaoh begged Moses to pray to God to remove them. Moses asked Pharaoh to set the time for the frogs to be removed. What surprising answer did Pharaoh give?
beginner.explanation: Surprisingly, Pharaoh said 'Tomorrow' instead of asking for immediate relief. This strange answer meant the Egyptians had to endure another night with frogs in their beds, houses, and food preparation areas.

beginner.question: In Exodus 10, after the devastating plague of locusts ate every plant in Egypt, Pharaoh urgently called Moses and Aaron back. He admitted he had sinned. What did Pharaoh ask Moses to do about the locusts?
options: Kill all the locusts with fire
Pray to God to take away the locusts
Send the locusts to another country
beginner.explanation: Pharaoh begged Moses to pray to God to take the deadly plague of locusts away from him. Moses prayed, and God sent a very strong west wind that carried every single locust away into the Red Sea. Not one locust was left in Egypt.

beginner.question: In Exodus 10:19, after Moses prayed for God to remove the plague of locusts from Egypt, God sent a very strong wind that blew all the locusts out of the country. Where did the wind carry the locusts?
options: Back to where they originally came from
beginner.explanation: God changed the wind to a very strong west wind, which caught up the locusts and carried them into the Red Sea. Not a single locust was left anywhere in Egypt. Despite this miracle, Pharaoh still hardened his heart.

beginner.question: In the Old Testament, God required the Israelites to offer animal sacrifices to atone for their sins. According to Leviticus 1-5, the most commonly offered animal for a sin offering by ordinary Israelites was from which group of animals?
beginner.explanation: Lambs, sheep, and goats were the most common animals used for sin offerings in the Old Testament sacrificial system. These animals had to be without blemish or defect, symbolising purity and foreshadowing Christ as the perfect sacrifice.

beginner.question: In Numbers 19, God gave Moses and Aaron instructions about a very special and rare sacrifice involving a cow of a specific colour. This cow had to have no blemish, no defect, and had never worn a yoke. What was this animal called?
beginner.explanation: The red heifer was a completely red cow used in a unique purification ritual. It was burned outside the camp, and its ashes were mixed with water to create a solution used for ceremonial cleansing from impurity, especially contact with the dead.

beginner.question: In the Old Testament sacrificial system described in Leviticus, the animal brought as a sin offering to the tabernacle had to meet a specific physical requirement. According to Leviticus 22:20-21, what condition was required?
options: It had to be the largest animal in the flock
It had to be a certain colour
It had to be without blemish or defect
It had to be at least five years old
beginner.explanation: God required that sacrificial animals be without any blemish, defect, or imperfection. This requirement symbolised offering God the very best, and it pointed forward to Jesus Christ, who would be the perfect, sinless sacrifice for humanity's sins.

beginner.question: In Numbers 19, the red heifer was a unique sacrifice in Israel. The animal was taken outside the camp and burned completely. Its ashes were then mixed with water. What was this mixture used for?
options: Painting the walls of the tabernacle
Purification from ceremonial uncleanness and sin
beginner.explanation: The ashes of the red heifer mixed with water created a 'water of purification' used to cleanse people who had become ceremonially unclean, particularly from contact with a dead body. This ritual pointed to the need for spiritual cleansing.

beginner.question: In Leviticus 16, on the Day of Atonement (Yom Kippur), the high priest took two goats for a special ceremony. One goat was sacrificed, and the other — called the 'scapegoat' — had the people's sins symbolically placed on its head. What happened to the scapegoat?
options: It was sent away into the wilderness, carrying the people's sins
It was kept in the tabernacle
It was given to the poor
It was released into the river
beginner.explanation: The high priest laid both hands on the scapegoat's head and confessed all the sins of Israel over it. The goat was then sent away into the wilderness, symbolically carrying the sins of the people far away from the community.

beginner.question: In Leviticus 4, when the Israelite high priest sinned and needed to make a sin offering, a specific animal was required. What animal did the high priest have to sacrifice for his own sin?
beginner.explanation: When the anointed priest sinned, he was required to bring a young bull without defect as a sin offering to the Lord. The bull was a more costly sacrifice, reflecting the greater responsibility of the priest's position before God.

beginner.question: In Exodus 12, on the night God was about to deliver the Israelites from slavery in Egypt, He told each family to sacrifice a lamb and put its blood on their doorframes. When the angel of death came through Egypt, what would the blood on the doorframe cause the angel to do?
options: Enter the house and bless it
Pass over the house, sparing those inside
beginner.explanation: The blood of the Passover lamb on the doorframe was a sign for the angel of death to pass over that house. Every household that obeyed was spared from the tenth plague — the death of the firstborn. This event became the Passover festival.

beginner.question: In Exodus 12, God gave the Israelites specific instructions about the Passover lamb. It had to be a year-old male without any defects. How were the Israelites told to prepare and cook the lamb?
options: They were to boil it in water
They were to eat it raw
They were to roast it over fire
They were to bake it in an oven
beginner.explanation: God instructed the Israelites to roast the Passover lamb over fire — not boiled in water or eaten raw. They were to roast the entire animal, including the head, legs, and internal organs, and eat it that same night.

beginner.question: In Exodus 12, on the night of the first Passover, the tenth and final plague struck Egypt — every firstborn son in the land died. The Israelites who had put the lamb's blood on their doorframes were spared. Whose firstborn son also died that night, finally convincing him to let Israel go?
beginner.explanation: Even Pharaoh's own firstborn son died that night. This devastating loss finally broke Pharaoh's stubborn resistance, and he urgently told Moses to take the Israelites and leave Egypt immediately.

beginner.question: In Exodus 12, God told the Israelites to eat the Passover lamb with two specific accompaniments on the night of their deliverance from Egypt. They ate it with bitter herbs and what else?
beginner.explanation: The Israelites ate the Passover lamb with bitter herbs (symbolising the bitterness of slavery) and unleavened bread (bread without yeast, because they had to leave Egypt in such a hurry there was no time for dough to rise).

beginner.question: In Exodus 12, God told the Israelites to eat the Passover meal dressed and ready to leave Egypt at a moment's notice. According to Exodus 12:11, how were they supposed to be dressed while eating?
options: With cloaks tucked into their belts, sandals on feet, and staff in hand — ready to travel
beginner.explanation: God told them to eat in haste, dressed for travel — cloaks tucked in, sandals on, and walking staves ready. This showed the urgency of God's deliverance: when the moment came, they needed to be ready to leave Egypt immediately.

beginner.question: In Exodus 12:46, God gave a very specific instruction about how the Israelites were to treat the bones of the Passover lamb. What did God command about its bones?
options: They must burn the bones with fire
They must not break any of its bones
They must grind the bones into powder
beginner.explanation: God commanded that not a single bone of the Passover lamb should be broken. Christians see this as a prophecy pointing to Jesus — when He was crucified, the soldiers did not break His legs (unlike the other men), fulfilling this Old Testament command.

beginner.question: In John 1:29, when John the Baptist saw Jesus walking toward him at the Jordan River, he made a famous declaration, giving Jesus a title that compared Him to a sacrificial animal. What did John the Baptist call Jesus?
beginner.explanation: John the Baptist declared, 'Look, the Lamb of God, who takes away the sin of the world!' This title connected Jesus to the Passover lamb and all the Old Testament sacrificial lambs, showing He would be the ultimate sacrifice for humanity's sins.

beginner.question: In John 1:29, John the Baptist called Jesus 'the Lamb of God who takes away the sin of the world.' According to this title, what was Jesus's role as the 'Lamb of God'?
options: To take away the sin of the world
To rule as a political king
To lead an army against Rome
To write new laws for Israel
beginner.explanation: John declared that Jesus, as the Lamb of God, would take away the sin of the world. Just as lambs were sacrificed in the Old Testament to atone for sin, Jesus would offer Himself as the final, perfect sacrifice for all of humanity's sins.

beginner.question: In 1 Corinthians 5:7, the apostle Paul wrote to the church in Corinth and compared Jesus to a specific Old Testament sacrifice. Paul said, 'Christ, our Passover lamb, has been sacrificed.' What did Paul mean by calling Jesus our 'Passover lamb'?
options: Jesus died as the ultimate sacrifice to save believers, just like the Passover lamb saved the Israelites in Egypt
Jesus ate lamb at every meal
Jesus was born in a barn with lambs
beginner.explanation: Paul connected Jesus's death on the cross to the original Passover lamb whose blood saved the Israelites from death in Egypt. Just as that lamb's blood caused death to pass over the Israelites, Jesus's sacrificial death saves believers from the penalty of sin.

beginner.question: In the book of Revelation, Jesus is frequently referred to as a Lamb. In Revelation 5, John saw a vision of heaven where a Lamb that appeared to have been slain stood in the centre of the throne. What was this Lamb worthy to do that no one else could?
options: Open the scroll with seven seals
beginner.explanation: The Lamb (Jesus) was the only one found worthy to open the scroll sealed with seven seals. All of heaven sang praise to the Lamb, declaring Him worthy because He was slain and by His blood purchased people for God from every tribe and nation.

beginner.question: In Isaiah 53:7, the prophet Isaiah wrote about a coming servant of God who would suffer for the sins of others. Isaiah compared this servant to a lamb being led to a specific place. Where was the lamb being led?
beginner.explanation: Isaiah wrote, 'He was led like a lamb to the slaughter, and as a sheep before its shearers is silent, so he did not open his mouth.' Christians see this as a prophecy about Jesus, who went willingly and silently to His crucifixion.

beginner.question: In 1 Peter 1:18-19, the apostle Peter told believers they were redeemed (set free from sin) not with gold or silver, but with something far more precious. What did Peter say redeemed them?
options: The precious blood of Christ, a lamb without blemish or defect
beginner.explanation: Peter told believers they were redeemed with the precious blood of Christ, whom he described as a lamb without blemish or defect. This echoed the Old Testament requirement that sacrificial lambs be perfect, pointing to Jesus's sinless life.

beginner.question: In John 10:11, Jesus used a well-known animal metaphor to describe His relationship with His followers. He said, 'I am the good _____.' What word completes this famous statement?
beginner.explanation: Jesus called Himself 'the Good Shepherd' who lays down His life for the sheep. This imagery would have been deeply meaningful to His listeners, as shepherds in Israel risked their lives daily to protect their flocks from predators and thieves.

beginner.question: In Jesus's parable of the lost sheep told in Luke 15:3-7, a shepherd had 100 sheep and one of them wandered away and became lost. What did the shepherd do when he realised one sheep was missing?
options: He left the 99 and went searching for the one lost sheep
He forgot about the lost sheep and stayed with the 99
He sent his dog to find it
He waited at home for the lost sheep to return on its own
beginner.explanation: The shepherd left the 99 sheep in the open country and went after the one lost sheep until he found it. When he found it, he joyfully put it on his shoulders and carried it home. Jesus told this parable to show God's love for every single person.

beginner.question: In Luke 15:3-7, when the shepherd in Jesus's parable finally found his one lost sheep, he was overjoyed. What did the shepherd do when he got home with the sheep?
options: He called his friends and neighbours to rejoice with him
He punished the sheep for wandering away
He sold the sheep at the market
He locked the sheep in a pen alone
beginner.explanation: The shepherd called together his friends and neighbours and said, 'Rejoice with me; I have found my lost sheep.' Jesus said that in the same way, there is more rejoicing in heaven over one sinner who repents than over ninety-nine who do not need to repent.

beginner.question: In Matthew 25:31-46, Jesus told a parable about the final judgment where He, as the King, would separate all people into two groups — just as a shepherd separates two types of animals. Which two animals did Jesus use in this parable?
beginner.explanation: Jesus said the Son of Man would separate the people as a shepherd separates the sheep from the goats. The sheep (representing the righteous) were placed on His right and received eternal life, while the goats were placed on His left.

beginner.question: In John 10:14-15, Jesus described Himself as the Good Shepherd and said something remarkable about what the Good Shepherd does for His sheep. What did Jesus say the Good Shepherd does?
options: Hires others to watch the sheep
Abandons the sheep when danger comes
Lays down His life for the sheep
beginner.explanation: Jesus said, 'I am the good shepherd. The good shepherd lays down his life for the sheep.' This was a clear prophecy of His upcoming death on the cross, where He would willingly die to save His followers.

beginner.question: In John 10:27, Jesus used the imagery of sheep and a shepherd to describe how His followers relate to Him. Jesus said, 'My sheep listen to my voice; I know them, and they _____ me.' What word fills the blank?
beginner.explanation: Jesus said His sheep hear His voice, He knows them, and they follow Him. Just as real sheep recognise and follow the voice of their own shepherd, Jesus's followers recognise His teaching and follow His lead in their lives.

beginner.question: In Psalm 23, David wrote, 'The Lord is my shepherd, I shall not want. He makes me lie down in green pastures, He leads me beside quiet waters.' In this psalm, David compared himself to what kind of animal?
beginner.explanation: By calling God his 'shepherd,' David was comparing himself to a sheep under God's care. Just as a shepherd provides food, water, protection, and guidance for sheep, God provides all these things for His people.

beginner.question: In Ezekiel 34:11-16, God promised to be like a shepherd who personally searches for His scattered sheep. God said He would rescue His sheep from all the places where they had been scattered on what kind of day?
beginner.explanation: God promised through the prophet Ezekiel that He would search for His sheep and rescue them from all the places they were scattered on a day of clouds and darkness. He would tend them, bind up the injured, and strengthen the weak.

beginner.question: In the New Testament, when Jesus was baptised by John the Baptist in the Jordan River, the Holy Spirit descended from heaven and rested on Jesus in the form of a specific animal. According to Matthew 3:16, what animal did the Holy Spirit appear as?
