beginner.explanation: When Jesus came up out of the water after His baptism, heaven was opened and the Spirit of God descended like a dove and alighted on Him. A voice from heaven also said, 'This is my Son, whom I love; with Him I am well pleased.'

beginner.question: At the baptism of Jesus in the Jordan River described in all four Gospels, the Holy Spirit descended in the bodily form of a dove. In the Bible, a dove is often used as a symbol of what?
beginner.explanation: The dove is a symbol of peace and purity throughout the Bible, starting with the dove that brought the olive leaf to Noah after the flood. The Holy Spirit appearing as a dove at Jesus's baptism symbolised peace, purity, and the gentle nature of God's Spirit.

beginner.question: In Matthew 10:16, Jesus sent out His twelve disciples on a mission and gave them advice about how to behave. He told them to be 'shrewd as serpents and innocent as' what animal?
beginner.explanation: Jesus told His disciples to be 'wise as serpents and innocent as doves.' He wanted them to be clever and discerning in a dangerous world, while maintaining the purity and innocence symbolised by doves.

beginner.question: In Luke 2:22-24, when Mary and Joseph brought the baby Jesus to the temple in Jerusalem to present Him to the Lord, they offered a sacrifice as required by the law of Moses. Because they were a poor family, what animals did they offer?
options: A pair of doves or two young pigeons
beginner.explanation: Mary and Joseph offered a pair of doves or two young pigeons, which was the sacrifice allowed for families who could not afford a lamb. This detail shows that Jesus was born into a humble, poor family.

beginner.question: In the Old Testament, the Song of Solomon 2:14 and other passages use the dove as a term of endearment. In Leviticus 1:14 and 5:7, doves and pigeons were allowed as sacrificial offerings specifically for which group of Israelites?
options: The poor who could not afford a lamb
beginner.explanation: Doves and pigeons served as an affordable sacrifice for poor Israelites who could not afford a lamb or goat. God made provision in His law so that even the poorest person could offer a proper sacrifice.

beginner.question: In Genesis 8:8-12, after the great flood, Noah sent out a dove three times to check if the floodwaters had receded. The dove eventually became a powerful symbol in the Bible. What does the dove with an olive branch symbolise throughout Western culture and the Bible?
beginner.explanation: The dove carrying an olive branch back to Noah's ark has become one of the most enduring symbols of peace and hope in the world. It represented the end of God's judgment and the beginning of a fresh start for humanity and all creation.

beginner.question: In the Gospels, Jesus performed a famous miracle where He fed a huge crowd of over 5,000 people using just a small amount of food from a boy's lunch. According to John 6:9, the boy's lunch included five loaves of bread and how many fish?
beginner.explanation: A boy had five small barley loaves and two small fish. Jesus took this tiny lunch, gave thanks, and miraculously multiplied it to feed more than 5,000 men, plus women and children, with twelve baskets of leftovers.

beginner.question: In the miracle of feeding the 5,000 described in all four Gospels, Jesus multiplied five loaves and two fish to feed a massive crowd. After everyone had eaten their fill, the disciples collected the leftovers. How many baskets of food were left over?
beginner.explanation: The disciples collected twelve full baskets of broken pieces of bread and fish that were left over. The abundance of leftovers demonstrated that God provides more than enough, and the twelve baskets may symbolise provision for the twelve tribes of Israel.

beginner.question: In Matthew 17:24-27, when tax collectors asked the apostle Peter whether Jesus paid the temple tax, Jesus told Peter to go fishing. Jesus said that when Peter caught the first fish and opened its mouth, he would find something inside to pay the tax. What was inside the fish's mouth?
beginner.explanation: Jesus told Peter to go to the lake, throw out his line, and open the mouth of the first fish he caught. Inside the fish's mouth would be a four-drachma coin — exactly the right amount to pay the temple tax for both Jesus and Peter.

beginner.question: In Luke 5:1-11, Jesus was teaching a crowd by the Sea of Galilee and got into Simon Peter's boat. After teaching, Jesus told Peter to go into deep water and let down his nets for a catch. Peter had been fishing all night with no success. What happened when he obeyed Jesus?
options: He caught one very large fish
He caught such a large number of fish that his nets began to break
beginner.explanation: When Peter obeyed Jesus and let down the nets, they caught such a large number of fish that their nets began to break. They had to signal their partners in another boat to come help, and both boats were so full of fish they began to sink.

beginner.question: In John 21:1-14, after Jesus rose from the dead, He appeared to His disciples at the Sea of Galilee. The disciples had been fishing all night and caught nothing. Jesus told them to cast their net on the right side of the boat. How many fish did they catch?
beginner.explanation: When the disciples obeyed the risen Jesus and cast their net on the right side, they caught 153 large fish. Despite the enormous number, the net was not torn. This miracle helped the disciples recognise that the man on the shore was Jesus.

beginner.question: In Matthew 4:18-20, Jesus was walking along the Sea of Galilee when He saw two brothers casting a fishing net into the lake. Jesus called out to them and invited them to follow Him. He said, 'Come, follow me, and I will make you fishers of' what?
beginner.explanation: Jesus told Simon Peter and his brother Andrew, 'Come, follow me, and I will send you out to fish for people.' They immediately left their nets and followed Jesus, becoming His first disciples. Jesus used their fishing background to describe their new mission.

beginner.question: In the miracle of feeding the 5,000, a young boy offered his small lunch to the disciples, and Jesus used it to feed the entire crowd. What kind of bread did the boy have, according to John 6:9?
beginner.explanation: The boy had five small barley loaves. Barley bread was the food of the poor, cheaper than wheat bread. The fact that Jesus used this humble offering to perform such a great miracle shows how God can do extraordinary things with whatever we offer Him.

beginner.question: In Luke 5:1-11, after Jesus caused Simon Peter to catch an overwhelming number of fish, Peter fell at Jesus's knees and said something to Jesus. What did Peter say?
options: 'Go away from me, Lord; I am a sinful man!'
beginner.explanation: Peter was so amazed and humbled by the miraculous catch that he recognised Jesus's divine power and his own sinfulness. He fell at Jesus's knees and said, 'Go away from me, Lord; I am a sinful man!' Jesus then called Peter to follow Him.

beginner.question: In Matthew 14:13-21, Jesus performed the miracle of feeding 5,000 men (plus women and children) with five loaves and two fish. According to the account, how many men were present, not counting women and children?
beginner.explanation: Matthew 14:21 says the number of those who ate was about five thousand men, besides women and children. The total crowd was likely much larger — possibly 15,000 to 20,000 people in all — making this miracle even more remarkable.

beginner.question: In Mark 8:1-10, Jesus performed a second miraculous feeding of a large crowd. This time He fed about 4,000 men with seven loaves of bread and a few small fish. How many baskets of leftovers did the disciples collect after this miracle?
beginner.explanation: After feeding the 4,000, the disciples picked up seven basketfuls of broken pieces that were left over. This was a different miracle from the feeding of the 5,000 (which had twelve baskets left over), showing Jesus's abundant provision on multiple occasions.

beginner.question: In the Gospels, when Jesus entered Jerusalem for the final time before His crucifixion on what is now called Palm Sunday, He rode into the city on a specific animal. According to Matthew 21:1-7, what animal did Jesus ride?
beginner.explanation: Jesus rode into Jerusalem on a donkey, fulfilling the Old Testament prophecy from Zechariah 9:9 which said the king would come 'gentle and riding on a donkey.' While kings usually rode horses, Jesus chose the humble donkey to show He came in peace.

beginner.question: In Matthew 21:1-7, Jesus sent two disciples ahead to a village to find the donkey He would ride into Jerusalem. Jesus told them they would find the donkey tied up with something else. What was tied alongside the donkey?
beginner.explanation: Jesus told the disciples they would find a donkey tied there, with her colt beside her. They brought both animals to Jesus, placed their cloaks on them, and Jesus sat on the young donkey as He rode into Jerusalem.

beginner.question: Jesus riding a donkey into Jerusalem on Palm Sunday fulfilled an Old Testament prophecy. Which Old Testament prophet predicted that the coming king would arrive 'righteous and victorious, lowly and riding on a donkey'?
beginner.explanation: The prophet Zechariah wrote in Zechariah 9:9, 'See, your king comes to you, righteous and victorious, lowly and riding on a donkey, on a colt, the foal of a donkey.' Jesus deliberately fulfilled this prophecy by choosing to ride a donkey into Jerusalem.

beginner.question: On Palm Sunday, when Jesus rode the donkey into Jerusalem, the large crowd that gathered was so excited they spread things on the road before Him as a sign of honour. What did the crowd spread on the road?
beginner.explanation: The crowds spread their cloaks on the road, and others cut palm branches from the trees and spread them on the path. They shouted 'Hosanna!' — a cry for salvation — as Jesus rode by. This is why the day is called Palm Sunday.

beginner.question: On Palm Sunday, as Jesus rode the donkey into Jerusalem, the crowds shouted joyful words of praise. According to Matthew 21:9, what Hebrew word did they shout, which means 'Save us'?
beginner.explanation: The crowds shouted 'Hosanna to the Son of David! Blessed is He who comes in the name of the Lord! Hosanna in the highest heaven!' Hosanna was a Hebrew exclamation meaning 'Save us!' and was used as a shout of praise and adoration.

beginner.question: Jesus chose to ride a humble donkey into Jerusalem rather than a war horse. In the ancient world, a king riding a horse typically symbolised coming for war, while riding a donkey symbolised coming in what manner?
beginner.explanation: In the ancient Near East, a king riding a donkey was a sign of coming in peace. By choosing a donkey over a horse, Jesus was declaring that He was a king who came not as a military conqueror but as a peaceful, humble servant-king.

beginner.question: In Matthew 19:24, Jesus made a famous and vivid statement about how hard it is for a rich person to enter the kingdom of God. He said it is easier for a camel to go through the eye of what object than for a rich person to enter God's kingdom?
beginner.explanation: Jesus said, 'It is easier for a camel to go through the eye of a needle than for someone who is rich to enter the kingdom of God.' This hyperbolic (exaggerated) statement emphasised that wealth can be a great obstacle to trusting fully in God.

beginner.question: In Matthew 19:23-26, after Jesus said it was easier for a camel to go through the eye of a needle than for a rich person to enter God's kingdom, His disciples were amazed and asked, 'Who then can be saved?' How did Jesus respond?
options: 'With man this is impossible, but with God all things are possible'
'You must give away everything you own'
beginner.explanation: Jesus told His disciples that while it is humanly impossible, with God all things are possible. Salvation is ultimately a work of God's grace, not of human effort or wealth. Even the impossible becomes possible through God's power.

beginner.question: Jesus's famous statement about a camel and the eye of a needle in Matthew 19 came after a conversation with a specific person. A wealthy young man had asked Jesus what he must do to get eternal life. What did Jesus tell the young man to do?
options: Go, sell everything you have, give to the poor, then come follow Me
beginner.explanation: Jesus told the rich young man to sell his possessions, give the money to the poor, and then follow Him. The young man went away sad because he had great wealth. This led Jesus to make His famous camel-and-needle statement.

beginner.question: In Genesis 24, when Abraham's servant traveled to find a wife for Isaac, he brought ten camels loaded with gifts. When the servant arrived at a well, he prayed for a sign. He asked God to show him the right woman by having her offer water not just to him but also to whom?
beginner.explanation: The servant prayed that the woman God chose would offer to water his ten camels as well. Rebekah appeared and did exactly that — she drew water for all ten camels, which was an enormous task. This confirmed she was God's choice for Isaac.

beginner.question: In Matthew 23:24, Jesus criticised the Pharisees (religious leaders) for focusing on tiny details of the law while ignoring bigger issues of justice and mercy. He used a humorous animal image, saying they 'strain out a gnat but swallow a' what?
beginner.explanation: Jesus accused the Pharisees of straining out a gnat (meticulously filtering tiny impurities from their drink) while swallowing a camel (ignoring huge sins like injustice and greed). The contrast between a tiny gnat and a huge camel made His point powerfully.

beginner.question: In Exodus 32, while Moses was on Mount Sinai receiving the Ten Commandments from God, the Israelites grew impatient and asked Aaron to make them a god they could see. Aaron collected gold jewellery from the people and made an idol in the shape of what animal?
beginner.explanation: Aaron collected gold earrings from the people, melted them down, and shaped the gold into a calf (young bull). The people then worshipped the golden calf, declaring it to be the god that brought them out of Egypt. This was a terrible sin against God.

beginner.question: In Exodus 32, who was the person who actually made the golden calf idol at the request of the Israelites while Moses was up on Mount Sinai?
beginner.explanation: Aaron, Moses's own brother and the high priest, made the golden calf. When the people pressured him, Aaron collected their gold earrings, melted them, and fashioned the idol. When Moses later confronted him, Aaron made excuses for his actions.

beginner.question: In Exodus 32, where did the gold come from that was used to make the golden calf idol while Moses was on Mount Sinai?
options: It was found in a cave
It was stolen from the Egyptians
The people gave their gold earrings
It was mined from the mountain
beginner.explanation: Aaron told the people to take off their gold earrings — from their wives, sons, and daughters — and bring them to him. He then used this gold jewellery to make the golden calf. The Israelites had received this gold from the Egyptians when they left Egypt.

beginner.question: In Exodus 32, when Moses came down from Mount Sinai and saw the golden calf idol and the people dancing around it, he was furious. What did Moses do with the two stone tablets of the Ten Commandments he was carrying?
options: He threw them down and broke them at the foot of the mountain
He placed them gently on the ground
He hid them in a cave
He gave them to Aaron to guard
beginner.explanation: Moses was so angry when he saw the golden calf and the people's wild celebration that he threw the stone tablets out of his hands and they broke at the foot of the mountain. Later, God had Moses cut two new tablets to replace them.

beginner.question: In Exodus 32, after Moses came down from Mount Sinai and confronted the people about their golden calf idol, what did Moses do with the golden calf?
options: He kept it as a warning
He buried it in the ground
He burned it, ground it to powder, scattered it on water, and made the Israelites drink it
He threw it into a deep valley
beginner.explanation: Moses took the golden calf the people had made, burned it in the fire, ground it to fine powder, scattered it on the water, and made the Israelites drink it. This dramatic act showed the complete worthlessness of the idol they had worshipped.

beginner.question: In Exodus 32, when Moses confronted his brother Aaron about making the golden calf, Aaron gave a rather weak excuse. What did Aaron claim happened with the gold?
options: He said a foreign spy made it
He said the people forced him at swordpoint
He said he threw the gold into the fire and the calf just came out by itself
beginner.explanation: Aaron tried to shift blame, telling Moses that the people were set on evil, and he merely threw their gold into the fire and 'out came this calf!' This excuse avoided taking personal responsibility for deliberately shaping the idol.

beginner.question: In 1 Kings 12:25-33, long after the golden calf incident in the wilderness, another Israelite leader made two golden calves and set them up for worship. He told the people, 'Here are your gods who brought you up out of Egypt.' Who was this king?
beginner.explanation: King Jeroboam, the first king of the northern kingdom of Israel, made two golden calves and placed one in Bethel and the other in Dan. He did this to keep people from traveling to Jerusalem (in the southern kingdom) to worship at the temple.

beginner.question: In Exodus 16 and Numbers 11, the Israelites were wandering in the wilderness after leaving Egypt and complained about the food God was providing. God sent a specific bird in enormous flocks that covered the camp so the people could eat meat. What bird did God send?
beginner.explanation: God sent quail in massive numbers that covered the ground around the camp. The people went out and gathered the quail for food. God provided the quail to satisfy the Israelites' craving for meat in the wilderness.

beginner.question: In Numbers 11, the Israelites were tired of eating manna every day in the wilderness and complained to Moses, crying out for meat. When God sent quail for them to eat, the quail fell around the camp in enormous quantities. How deep were the quail piled around the camp?
options: About three feet (two cubits) deep on the ground
beginner.explanation: Numbers 11:31 says the quail fell around the camp to a depth of about two cubits (approximately three feet or one metre) above the ground. The people spent two full days gathering quail — no one collected less than ten homers (about sixty bushels).

beginner.question: In Exodus 16, God provided two types of miraculous food for the Israelites in the wilderness. In the morning, a flaky bread-like substance called manna appeared on the ground. In the evening, God sent what animal for the people to eat as meat?
beginner.explanation: Each evening, quail came and covered the camp, providing meat for the Israelites. Each morning, manna appeared on the ground like dew. Together, these two provisions sustained the entire nation of Israel during their wilderness journey.

beginner.question: In Numbers 11, when the Israelites complained about being tired of manna and wanted meat, they recalled the foods they had eaten back in Egypt. Which of the following foods did they NOT mention missing from Egypt?
options: They missed all kinds of food from Egypt
beginner.explanation: The Israelites specifically remembered the fish they ate for free in Egypt, and the cucumbers, melons, leeks, onions, and garlic. They did not mention bread, cheese, or grapes. Their selective memory ignored the harsh slavery they endured in Egypt.

beginner.question: In Numbers 11, God told Moses He would send the Israelites meat to eat. God said the people would eat meat not just for one or two days, but for how long?
options: A whole month, until it comes out of their nostrils
beginner.explanation: God said the people would eat meat for a whole month until it came out of their nostrils and they loathed it, because they had rejected the Lord by complaining and wishing they had stayed in Egypt.

beginner.question: In Numbers 21, the Israelites were bitten by poisonous snakes in the wilderness as punishment for complaining against God and Moses. God told Moses to make a replica of a snake and put it on a pole. When anyone who was bitten looked at the snake on the pole, what happened to them?
options: They became immune to all snakes
beginner.explanation: God told Moses to make a bronze serpent and put it on a pole. Anyone who was bitten by a poisonous snake could look at the bronze serpent on the pole and they would live. This required an act of faith — simply looking in obedience to God's instruction.

beginner.question: In Numbers 21, when God instructed Moses to make a snake and place it on a pole so that anyone bitten could look at it and be healed, what material did Moses use to make the snake?
beginner.explanation: Moses made a snake out of bronze and put it up on a pole. Whenever anyone was bitten by a venomous snake and looked at the bronze snake, they lived. The bronze serpent became an important symbol later referenced by Jesus.

beginner.question: In John 3:14-15, Jesus compared Himself to the bronze serpent that Moses lifted up on a pole in the wilderness. Jesus said that just as Moses lifted up the snake, so He (the Son of Man) must be lifted up, so that everyone who believes in Him would have what?
beginner.explanation: Jesus said, 'Just as Moses lifted up the snake in the wilderness, so the Son of Man must be lifted up, that everyone who believes in Him may have eternal life.' Just as looking at the bronze snake brought physical healing, looking to Jesus in faith brings eternal salvation.

beginner.question: In Numbers 21, the reason the Israelites were attacked by venomous snakes in the wilderness was because they had complained against God and Moses. What specifically were the Israelites complaining about?
options: The food (manna) and the lack of water, saying they hated the miserable food
Having to walk too far each day
beginner.explanation: The Israelites spoke against God and Moses, complaining, 'Why have you brought us up out of Egypt to die in the wilderness? There is no bread! There is no water! And we detest this miserable food!' God sent venomous snakes as discipline.

beginner.question: In 2 Kings 18:4, many years after Moses made the bronze serpent in the wilderness, a good king of Judah destroyed the bronze serpent because the Israelites had begun worshipping it as an idol and burning incense to it. Which king destroyed the bronze serpent?
beginner.explanation: King Hezekiah, a righteous king of Judah, broke the bronze serpent into pieces because the Israelites had been burning incense to it as an idol. They had given it a name, Nehushtan. Hezekiah recognised that worshipping a bronze object was idolatry.

beginner.question: In Numbers 21, after the venomous snakes attacked the Israelites and many died, the people came to Moses and admitted their sin. They asked Moses to do something about the snakes. What did the people ask Moses to do?
options: Pray to the Lord to take the snakes away
Build a wall to keep the snakes out
Lead them to a different area
Kill all the snakes with fire
beginner.explanation: The people acknowledged they had sinned by speaking against the Lord and against Moses. They asked Moses to pray to the Lord to take the snakes away. Moses prayed for the people, and God provided the bronze serpent as the remedy.

beginner.question: In the Gospels, on the night before Jesus was crucified, He told the apostle Peter that Peter would deny knowing Him before a certain animal made a sound. What animal did Jesus say would signal Peter's denials?
beginner.explanation: Jesus told Peter that before the rooster crowed, Peter would deny knowing Jesus three times. Peter insisted he would never do such a thing, but later that night, Peter did exactly as Jesus had predicted.

beginner.question: According to the Gospels, on the night Jesus was arrested, the apostle Peter denied knowing Jesus three times out of fear. According to Luke 22:34, how many times did Jesus say Peter would deny Him before the rooster crowed?
beginner.explanation: Jesus predicted that Peter would deny Him three times before the rooster crowed that very night. Despite Peter's bold declaration that he was ready to go to prison and even die with Jesus, fear led him to deny Jesus exactly three times.

beginner.question: In Luke 22:54-62, after Peter denied Jesus for the third time, the rooster crowed just as Jesus had predicted. At that very moment, Jesus turned and looked at Peter. How did Peter respond when he realised what he had done?
options: He went outside and wept bitterly
He ran away and never came back
beginner.explanation: When Peter heard the rooster crow and remembered Jesus's prediction, he went outside and wept bitterly. Peter was devastated by his failure to stand by Jesus. However, after the resurrection, Jesus restored Peter and forgave him completely.

beginner.question: In Mark 14:66-72, while Jesus was being tried by the high priest, Peter was in the courtyard below. A servant girl recognised Peter as one of Jesus's followers. Peter denied it. According to Mark's account, after Peter denied Jesus the second time, what happened?
options: The rooster crowed for the first time
beginner.explanation: Mark's Gospel specifically mentions that the rooster crowed after Peter's second denial. Then, after Peter's third denial, the rooster crowed a second time, and Peter remembered that Jesus had said, 'Before the rooster crows twice, you will disown me three times.'

beginner.question: In John 21:15-17, after Jesus rose from the dead, He had a special conversation with Peter by the Sea of Galilee, restoring him after his three denials. Jesus asked Peter the same question three times. What question did Jesus ask Peter?
beginner.explanation: Jesus asked Peter three times, 'Do you love me?' — once for each of Peter's three denials. Each time Peter said yes, Jesus gave him a command: 'Feed my lambs,' 'Take care of my sheep,' and 'Feed my sheep,' restoring Peter to his role as a leader.

beginner.question: In the book of Genesis, a serpent tempted Eve in the Garden of Eden to eat the forbidden fruit from the Tree of Knowledge of Good and Evil. True or false?
beginner.explanation: Genesis 3:1-6 describes how the serpent, the most crafty of all wild animals, tempted Eve by telling her she would not die if she ate the forbidden fruit. Eve ate it and also gave some to Adam.

beginner.question: In Genesis 3, the serpent that tempted Eve in the Garden of Eden is described as the most 'beautiful' of all animals God had made. True or false?
beginner.explanation: The Bible describes the serpent as the most 'crafty' or 'cunning' of all wild animals, not the most beautiful. Genesis 3:1 says, 'Now the serpent was more crafty than any of the wild animals the Lord God had made.'

beginner.question: When God cursed the serpent in Genesis 3:14, He said the serpent would crawl on its belly and eat dust all the days of its life. True or false?
beginner.explanation: God told the serpent, 'Because you have done this, cursed are you above all livestock and all wild animals! You will crawl on your belly and you will eat dust all the days of your life.' This was part of the curse for deceiving Eve.

beginner.question: In Genesis 3, after the serpent deceived Eve, God said He would put enmity (hostility) between the serpent and the woman, and between the serpent's offspring and her offspring. True or false?
beginner.explanation: Genesis 3:15 records God's words to the serpent: 'I will put enmity between you and the woman, and between your offspring and hers.' Many Christians interpret this as the first prophecy of Christ defeating Satan.

beginner.question: In the story of the Garden of Eden in Genesis 3, the serpent told Eve the truth — that she would surely die if she ate the forbidden fruit. True or false?
beginner.explanation: The serpent lied to Eve. It said, 'You will not certainly die,' directly contradicting God's warning. The serpent deceived Eve into believing that eating the fruit would make her like God without any consequences.

beginner.question: In Revelation 12:9, the ancient serpent from the Garden of Eden is identified as the devil, or Satan. True or false?
beginner.explanation: Revelation 12:9 explicitly says, 'The great dragon was hurled down — that ancient serpent called the devil, or Satan, who leads the whole world astray.' This verse identifies the serpent from Genesis as Satan himself.

beginner.question: In the story of the Garden of Eden, only Eve ate the forbidden fruit — Adam refused to eat it. True or false?
beginner.explanation: Both Eve and Adam ate the forbidden fruit. Genesis 3:6 says Eve took some and ate it, and she also gave some to her husband Adam, who was with her, and he ate it too. Both of them disobeyed God's command.

beginner.question: In the story of Noah's ark in Genesis 7, God told Noah to bring two of every kind of unclean animal and seven pairs of every kind of clean animal onto the ark. True or false?
beginner.explanation: Genesis 7:2 says God told Noah to take seven pairs of every kind of clean animal and two of every kind of unclean animal. The extra clean animals were needed for sacrifice after the flood and for food.

beginner.question: In Genesis 8, after the great flood, Noah sent out an eagle from the ark to check if the floodwaters had receded, and it returned with an olive branch. True or false?
beginner.explanation: Noah sent out a dove, not an eagle. The dove returned with a freshly plucked olive leaf in its beak, showing that the floodwaters were receding and plants were beginning to grow again.

beginner.question: In Genesis 8, before sending out the dove, Noah first sent out a raven from the ark. The raven kept flying back and forth until the water had dried up from the earth. True or false?
beginner.explanation: Noah first sent out a raven, which flew back and forth until the floodwaters dried up. After the raven, Noah sent out the dove, which eventually returned with the olive leaf and later did not return at all.

beginner.question: After Noah and the animals left the ark, God placed a rainbow in the sky as a sign of His promise never to destroy the entire earth with a flood again. True or false?
beginner.explanation: Genesis 9:12-17 describes how God set the rainbow in the clouds as the sign of His covenant with Noah and all living creatures. The rainbow is a reminder that God will never again send a flood to destroy all life on earth.

beginner.question: In the story of Noah's ark, it rained for seven days and seven nights during the great flood. True or false?
beginner.explanation: According to Genesis 7:12, it rained for forty days and forty nights, not seven. The number forty appears frequently in the Bible to signify a significant period of testing or judgment.

beginner.question: According to Genesis 6:14, God told Noah to build the ark out of gopherwood (cypress wood) and to coat it with pitch inside and out to make it waterproof. True or false?
beginner.explanation: God gave Noah specific building instructions: use gopherwood, coat it with pitch, and build it to precise dimensions — about 450 feet long, 75 feet wide, and 45 feet high, with three decks.

beginner.question: When Noah sent the dove out from the ark for the third and final time, the dove returned empty-handed, with nothing in its beak. True or false?
beginner.explanation: When Noah sent the dove out the third time, it did not return at all. It was the second time that the dove returned with an olive leaf. The third time, the dove found dry land and stayed, signalling it was safe to leave the ark.

beginner.question: Noah's three sons who entered the ark were named Shem, Ham, and Japheth. True or false?
beginner.explanation: Genesis 6:10 lists Noah's three sons as Shem, Ham, and Japheth. All three entered the ark with Noah, along with their wives and Noah's wife, making eight people total who survived the flood.

beginner.question: In the book of Jonah, God told the prophet Jonah to go preach to the wicked city of Nineveh, and Jonah immediately obeyed without hesitation. True or false?
beginner.explanation: Jonah did not obey — he ran away in the opposite direction! He boarded a ship heading to Tarshish to flee from God's command. Only after being swallowed by a great fish and spending three days inside it did Jonah finally go to Nineveh.

beginner.question: In the book of Jonah, after Jonah was thrown overboard into the stormy sea, a great fish swallowed him. Jonah spent three days and three nights inside the fish. True or false?
beginner.explanation: Jonah 1:17 says, 'Now the Lord provided a huge fish to swallow Jonah, and Jonah was inside the fish three days and three nights.' During this time, Jonah prayed to God and repented of running away.

beginner.question: In the book of Jonah, the great fish that swallowed Jonah eventually spat him out into the deep ocean, where he had to swim to shore. True or false?
beginner.explanation: The great fish did not spit Jonah into the ocean. Jonah 2:10 says the Lord commanded the fish, and it vomited Jonah onto dry land. God ensured Jonah arrived safely on shore to continue his mission to Nineveh.

beginner.question: After Jonah finally went to Nineveh and preached God's message, the people of Nineveh repented, fasted, and turned from their evil ways. True or false?
beginner.explanation: The Ninevites believed God's warning through Jonah. From the king to the common people, everyone declared a fast, put on sackcloth, and turned from their wicked ways. God saw their repentance and relented from the destruction He had planned.

beginner.question: In the book of Jonah, during the great storm at sea, the sailors found Jonah hiding below deck, awake and praying desperately to God for help. True or false?
beginner.explanation: Jonah was not praying — he was fast asleep below deck! While the terrified sailors were crying out to their gods and throwing cargo overboard, Jonah was sound asleep. The ship's captain had to wake him and tell him to pray.

beginner.question: In Matthew 12:40, Jesus compared His own death and resurrection to Jonah's three days inside the great fish, saying He would spend three days in the heart of the earth. True or false?
beginner.explanation: Jesus said, 'For as Jonah was three days and three nights in the belly of a huge fish, so the Son of Man will be three days and three nights in the heart of the earth.' He used Jonah's experience as a sign pointing to His resurrection.

beginner.question: In Numbers 22, a prophet named Balaam was riding his donkey when the donkey saw an angel of the Lord blocking the road. God miraculously opened the donkey's mouth and it spoke to Balaam in human words. True or false?
beginner.explanation: God opened the donkey's mouth and it spoke to Balaam, asking why he had beaten it three times. This is one of only two instances in the Bible where an animal speaks with human language (the other being the serpent in Eden).

beginner.question: In the story of Balaam's donkey in Numbers 22, King Balak of Moab wanted to hire Balaam to bless the Israelites and make them stronger. True or false?
beginner.explanation: King Balak wanted Balaam to curse the Israelites, not bless them. Balak was afraid of the large Israelite nation and believed that if a powerful prophet like Balaam cursed them, the Moabites would be able to defeat them.

beginner.question: In Numbers 22, Balaam's donkey could see the angel of the Lord standing in the road with a drawn sword, but Balaam himself could not see the angel. True or false?
beginner.explanation: Only the donkey could see the angel of the Lord blocking the path with a drawn sword. Balaam could not see it, which is why he kept beating the donkey for refusing to go forward. God later opened Balaam's eyes to see the angel too.

beginner.question: In Numbers 22-24, when Balaam tried to curse the Israelites as King Balak requested, he successfully cursed them all three times. True or false?
beginner.explanation: Balaam could not curse Israel. Each time he opened his mouth to pronounce a curse, God put words of blessing in his mouth instead. Balaam blessed the Israelites three times, much to King Balak's fury and frustration.

beginner.question: In Numbers 22, the angel of the Lord told Balaam that if his donkey had not turned away three times, the angel would have killed Balaam but spared the donkey. True or false?
beginner.explanation: The angel said to Balaam, 'The donkey saw me and turned away from me these three times. If it had not turned away, I would certainly have killed you by now, but I would have spared it.' The donkey actually saved Balaam's life.

beginner.question: In Daniel 6, the prophet Daniel was thrown into a den of lions because he refused to stop praying to God, even when a law was made that forbade praying to anyone except King Darius for 30 days. True or false?
beginner.explanation: Jealous officials tricked King Darius into making a law that prohibited praying to any god or person other than the king for 30 days. Daniel continued his habit of praying to God three times daily, and was caught and thrown into the lions' den.

beginner.question: In the story of Daniel in the lions' den in Daniel 6, the lions attacked and injured Daniel, but he survived. True or false?
beginner.explanation: Daniel was completely unharmed — not a single scratch was found on him. God sent an angel who shut the lions' mouths so they could not hurt Daniel at all. Daniel told the king he was innocent before God and had done no wrong.

beginner.question: In Daniel 6, King Darius was happy when Daniel was thrown into the lions' den because he wanted to get rid of Daniel. True or false?
beginner.explanation: King Darius was greatly distressed when he realised Daniel would have to be thrown to the lions. He liked and respected Daniel, and spent the whole day trying to find a way to save him, but the law could not be changed.

beginner.question: In Daniel 6, after Daniel was miraculously saved from the lions' den, King Darius issued a decree that everyone in his kingdom must fear and reverence Daniel's God, the living God. True or false?
beginner.explanation: King Darius was so amazed by God's rescue of Daniel that he wrote to all peoples, nations, and languages, commanding them to fear and reverence the God of Daniel, whom he declared to be the living God whose kingdom will never be destroyed.

beginner.question: In Daniel 6:10, Daniel prayed three times a day with his windows open, facing toward Jerusalem. True or false?
beginner.explanation: Daniel went to his upstairs room where the windows opened toward Jerusalem, and three times a day he got down on his knees, prayed, and gave thanks to God, just as he had always done. This habitual prayer life was well known to others.

beginner.question: In Daniel 6, after Daniel was saved from the lions' den, the jealous officials who plotted against him were forgiven by King Darius and given new positions. True or false?
beginner.explanation: The officials were not forgiven. At the king's command, the men who had falsely accused Daniel were thrown into the lions' den along with their families, and the lions overpowered them immediately.

beginner.question: In Daniel 6, King Darius fasted, refused entertainment, and could not sleep the entire night that Daniel was in the lions' den. True or false?
beginner.explanation: The king spent the night without eating, and he refused any entertainment. He could not sleep at all. At the first light of dawn, he hurried to the lions' den to see if Daniel's God had saved him.

beginner.question: In Judges 14, the mighty judge Samson killed a young lion near the vineyards of Timnah by tearing it apart with his bare hands when the Spirit of the Lord came powerfully upon him. True or false?
beginner.explanation: Judges 14:5-6 says that when a young lion came roaring toward Samson, the Spirit of the Lord came powerfully upon him and he tore the lion apart with his bare hands as easily as one might tear a young goat.

beginner.question: In Judges 14, after Samson killed the lion near Timnah, he told his parents all about it and showed them the lion's carcass. True or false?
beginner.explanation: The Bible specifically says that Samson did not tell his father or mother what he had done. He kept the incident to himself. Later, when he found honey in the carcass and gave some to his parents, he still didn't tell them where it came from.

beginner.question: In Judges 14, when Samson later returned to the spot where he had killed the lion, he found a swarm of bees and honey in the lion's carcass. True or false?
beginner.explanation: Some time later, Samson went back and found that a swarm of bees had made honey in the dead lion's body. He scooped out the honey with his hands and ate it as he walked. He also gave some to his parents.

beginner.question: Samson's famous riddle 'Out of the eater, something to eat; out of the strong, something sweet' was about finding honey inside a dead bear he had killed. True or false?
beginner.explanation: The riddle was about a lion, not a bear. Samson had killed a young lion near Timnah, and later found a swarm of bees and honey inside its carcass. The 'eater' and 'strong' one was the lion, and the 'sweet' thing was the honey.

beginner.question: In 1 Samuel 17, the young shepherd David told King Saul that while tending his father's sheep, he had fought and killed both a lion and a bear that attacked his flock. True or false?
beginner.explanation: David told Saul that when a lion or bear came and carried off a sheep, he would chase it, strike it, and rescue the sheep. David killed both a lion and a bear, and he credited God with delivering him from these dangerous animals.

beginner.question: In 1 Samuel 17, David used a sword and shield to defeat Goliath the Philistine giant. True or false?
beginner.explanation: David did not use a sword and shield. He took five smooth stones from a stream and used his shepherd's sling to hurl a stone at Goliath, striking him in the forehead. David defeated the giant with the simple tools of a shepherd.

beginner.question: In Psalm 23, King David, who had been a shepherd in his youth, compared God to a shepherd and himself to a sheep, writing, 'The Lord is my shepherd, I shall not want.' True or false?
beginner.explanation: Psalm 23 is one of the most beloved passages in the Bible. David drew on his personal experience as a shepherd to describe how God cares for, protects, provides for, and guides His people, just as a shepherd does for his sheep.

beginner.question: In 1 Samuel 16, when the prophet Samuel came to anoint the next king of Israel, David was immediately available because he was at home studying. True or false?
beginner.explanation: David was not at home — he was out in the fields tending his father's sheep. He was the youngest son and had been given the humble task of shepherding. Samuel had to wait for someone to go fetch David from the fields.

beginner.question: In 1 Kings 17, during a severe drought, God sent ravens to bring bread and meat to the prophet Elijah every morning and evening by the brook Cherith. True or false?
beginner.explanation: God commanded ravens to feed Elijah during the drought. The ravens faithfully brought bread and meat to Elijah in the morning and in the evening, and he drank water from the brook. This was a remarkable example of God's supernatural provision.

beginner.question: In 1 Kings 17, the ravens that fed the prophet Elijah brought him fish and fruit every day. True or false?
beginner.explanation: The ravens did not bring fish and fruit. According to 1 Kings 17:6, the ravens brought Elijah bread and meat in the morning, and bread and meat in the evening. God specifically provided these substantial foods through the birds.

beginner.question: In 1 Kings 17, the prophet Elijah was hiding from the wicked King Ahab and Queen Jezebel, who had led Israel into the worship of the false god Baal. True or false?
beginner.explanation: Elijah opposed King Ahab and Queen Jezebel because they promoted Baal worship throughout Israel. Elijah had proclaimed a drought as God's judgment, and Ahab was furious with him, so God told Elijah to hide by the brook Cherith.

beginner.question: According to 1 Kings 18 and James 5:17, the drought during which ravens fed Elijah lasted for three and a half years. True or false?
beginner.explanation: James 5:17 says Elijah prayed earnestly that it would not rain, and it did not rain on the land for three and a half years. The drought only ended after Elijah's confrontation with the prophets of Baal on Mount Carmel.

beginner.question: In the plagues of Egypt described in Exodus, the second plague sent swarms of locusts throughout the land. True or false?
beginner.explanation: The second plague was frogs, not locusts. Frogs came up from the Nile and covered the entire land of Egypt, getting into houses, bedrooms, and ovens. The locust plague was actually the eighth plague, much later in the sequence.

beginner.question: In Exodus 8, the plague of frogs was so severe that frogs invaded the Egyptians' houses, bedrooms, beds, ovens, and kneading troughs. True or false?
beginner.explanation: Exodus 8:3-4 describes how the frogs went everywhere in Egypt — into houses, bedrooms, beds, ovens, and even kneading troughs where bread dough was mixed. There was absolutely no escape from the frogs anywhere in the land.

beginner.question: In Exodus 7, the first plague God sent upon Egypt turned all the water in the Nile River and throughout Egypt into blood, killing all the fish. True or false?
beginner.explanation: God turned all the water in Egypt to blood — the Nile River, the streams, canals, ponds, and even water in jars. All the fish died, the water stank, and the Egyptians could not drink it. This first plague struck at Egypt's most vital resource.

beginner.question: During the plague on livestock in Exodus 9, both the Egyptians' animals and the Israelites' animals died. True or false?
beginner.explanation: Only the Egyptian livestock died. God made a clear distinction between the animals of Egypt and those of Israel. Not a single animal belonging to the Israelites was harmed, demonstrating God's protection over His people.

beginner.question: In Exodus 10, the plague of locusts was brought into Egypt by a strong east wind and devoured every plant and tree in the land, leaving nothing green. True or false?
beginner.explanation: God sent an east wind that blew all day and night, bringing an enormous swarm of locusts that covered the entire land. They ate everything — every plant, every fruit on the trees. Nothing green remained in all of Egypt.

beginner.question: In Exodus 8, when the plague of frogs tormented Egypt, Pharaoh asked Moses to pray for God to remove the frogs immediately, and Moses did so right away. True or false?
beginner.explanation: When Moses asked Pharaoh to set the time for removing the frogs, Pharaoh surprisingly said 'Tomorrow' instead of immediately. This meant the Egyptians had to endure another full night with frogs in their beds and houses.

beginner.question: In Exodus 8:16-19, when the third plague of gnats (or lice) struck Egypt, the Egyptian magicians could not reproduce this plague and admitted, 'This is the finger of God.' True or false?
beginner.explanation: The Egyptian magicians had been able to replicate the first two plagues (blood and frogs) using their secret arts. But when Aaron struck the dust and it became gnats, the magicians could not do the same and told Pharaoh, 'This is the finger of God.'

beginner.question: In Exodus 10:19, after Moses prayed for God to remove the plague of locusts, a strong wind carried the locusts into the Mediterranean Sea. True or false?
beginner.explanation: The wind carried the locusts into the Red Sea, not the Mediterranean Sea. God changed the wind to a very strong west wind that swept every single locust out of Egypt and into the Red Sea. Not one locust remained.

beginner.question: In the Old Testament sacrificial system described in Leviticus, animals offered as sacrifices to God had to be without any blemish or defect. True or false?
beginner.explanation: Leviticus 22:20-21 required that sacrificial animals be without blemish, defect, or imperfection. Offering a flawed animal was considered an insult to God. This requirement also foreshadowed Jesus as the perfect, sinless sacrifice.

beginner.question: In Numbers 19, the red heifer sacrifice involved a completely red cow whose ashes were mixed with water and used for ceremonial purification. True or false?
beginner.explanation: The red heifer had to be completely red with no other colour, without blemish, and never have worn a yoke. It was burned outside the camp, and its ashes were mixed with water to create a purification solution used for cleansing from uncleanness.

beginner.question: In Leviticus 16, on the Day of Atonement, both goats used in the ceremony were sacrificed on the altar. True or false?
beginner.explanation: Only one goat was sacrificed as a sin offering. The other goat — called the scapegoat — had the people's sins symbolically placed on its head by the high priest and was then sent alive into the wilderness, carrying the sins away from the people.

beginner.question: In Exodus 12, the Israelites were told to put the blood of the Passover lamb on their doorframes so that the angel of death would pass over their homes and spare their firstborn. True or false?
beginner.explanation: God told the Israelites to slaughter a lamb and put its blood on the sides and tops of their doorframes. When the Lord went through Egypt to strike down the firstborn, He would see the blood and pass over that house, sparing those inside.

beginner.question: In Exodus 12, the Israelites were told to boil the Passover lamb in water before eating it. True or false?
beginner.explanation: God specifically told the Israelites NOT to boil the Passover lamb in water or eat it raw. They were to roast it over fire. The entire animal — head, legs, and internal organs — was to be roasted and eaten that same night.

beginner.question: In Exodus 12, the Passover lamb had to be a year-old male without any defects, and not a single bone of the lamb was to be broken. True or false?
beginner.explanation: The Passover lamb had specific requirements: it had to be a year-old male without defect, and none of its bones were to be broken. Christians see this as pointing to Jesus, who was crucified but whose bones were not broken, fulfilling this requirement.

beginner.question: In Exodus 12, on the night of the first Passover, Pharaoh's firstborn son was spared because Pharaoh finally agreed to let the Israelites go before the plague struck. True or false?
beginner.explanation: Pharaoh's firstborn son was not spared — he died along with every other firstborn in Egypt that night. It was only after this devastating loss that Pharaoh finally relented and urgently told Moses to take the Israelites and leave Egypt.

beginner.question: In John 1:29, John the Baptist called Jesus 'the Lamb of God, who takes away the sin of the world.' True or false?
beginner.explanation: When John the Baptist saw Jesus approaching, he declared, 'Look, the Lamb of God, who takes away the sin of the world!' This title connected Jesus to the Passover lamb and the entire sacrificial system of the Old Testament.

beginner.question: In 1 Corinthians 5:7, the apostle Paul called Jesus 'our Passover bull' who has been sacrificed. True or false?
beginner.explanation: Paul called Jesus 'our Passover lamb,' not 'Passover bull.' Paul wrote, 'Christ, our Passover lamb, has been sacrificed.' This directly linked Jesus's death on the cross to the original Passover lamb whose blood saved the Israelites in Egypt.

beginner.question: In the book of Revelation, Jesus is frequently referred to as a Lamb. In Revelation 5, the Lamb was the only one found worthy to open a scroll sealed with seven seals. True or false?
beginner.explanation: In Revelation 5, no one in heaven or on earth was found worthy to open the scroll until the Lamb (Jesus) stepped forward. He was declared worthy because He was slain and by His blood purchased people for God from every tribe, language, and nation.

beginner.question: In Isaiah 53:7, the prophet Isaiah compared the coming Messiah to a lamb being led to the slaughter — silent and not opening its mouth. True or false?
beginner.explanation: Isaiah wrote, 'He was led like a lamb to the slaughter, and as a sheep before its shearers is silent, so he did not open his mouth.' Christians believe this prophecy was fulfilled by Jesus, who went willingly and mostly silently to His crucifixion.

beginner.question: In 1 Peter 1:18-19, Peter said believers were redeemed with gold and silver, not with the blood of a lamb. True or false?
beginner.explanation: Peter said the exact opposite. He wrote that believers were redeemed NOT with perishable things like gold or silver, but with the precious blood of Christ, a lamb without blemish or defect. Christ's blood was infinitely more valuable than gold.

beginner.question: In John 10:11, Jesus called Himself 'the Good Shepherd' who lays down His life for the sheep. True or false?
beginner.explanation: Jesus said, 'I am the good shepherd. The good shepherd lays down his life for the sheep.' This was a clear declaration of His identity and mission — He would willingly die to save His followers, just as a devoted shepherd protects his flock.

beginner.question: In Jesus's parable of the lost sheep in Luke 15, a shepherd had 100 sheep, lost one, and decided it wasn't worth looking for since he still had 99. True or false?
beginner.explanation: The shepherd did the opposite — he left the 99 sheep and went searching for the one lost sheep until he found it. When he found it, he joyfully put it on his shoulders and carried it home. Jesus told this to show how much God values every single person.

beginner.question: In Matthew 25:31-33, Jesus said that at the final judgment, the Son of Man would separate all people like a shepherd separates sheep from goats. True or false?
beginner.explanation: Jesus described the final judgment as a shepherd separating sheep from goats. The sheep (the righteous) would be placed on His right and inherit the kingdom, while the goats would be placed on His left and face judgment.

beginner.question: In John 10:27, Jesus said, 'My sheep listen to my voice; I know them, and they follow me.' True or false?
beginner.explanation: Jesus used the familiar image of sheep following their shepherd's voice to describe the relationship between Himself and His followers. Just as sheep recognise their own shepherd's unique voice, true followers recognise and respond to Jesus's teaching.

beginner.question: In the parable of the lost sheep in Luke 15, when the shepherd found his lost sheep, he punished it for wandering away. True or false?
beginner.explanation: The shepherd did not punish the sheep at all. Instead, he joyfully placed it on his shoulders and carried it home. He then called his friends and neighbours to celebrate with him. Jesus used this to show God's joy over every sinner who repents.

beginner.question: At the baptism of Jesus described in Matthew 3:16, the Holy Spirit descended from heaven and rested on Jesus in the form of a dove. True or false?
beginner.explanation: When Jesus came up out of the water after being baptised by John the Baptist, heaven was opened and the Spirit of God descended like a dove and came to rest on Him. A voice from heaven also declared, 'This is my Son, whom I love.'

beginner.question: At Jesus's baptism, the Holy Spirit descended in the form of an eagle, symbolising power and strength. True or false?
beginner.explanation: The Holy Spirit descended in the form of a dove, not an eagle. The dove symbolises peace, purity, and gentleness. All four Gospels record that the Spirit came down like a dove when Jesus was baptised in the Jordan River.

beginner.question: In Matthew 10:16, Jesus told His disciples to be 'shrewd as serpents and innocent as doves.' True or false?
beginner.explanation: When sending out the twelve disciples on their mission, Jesus gave them this memorable advice. He wanted them to be wise and discerning (like serpents) while maintaining purity and innocence (like doves) in a hostile world.

beginner.question: In Luke 2:22-24, when Mary and Joseph brought baby Jesus to the temple, they offered a lamb and a goat as their sacrifice. True or false?
beginner.explanation: Mary and Joseph offered a pair of doves or two young pigeons, not a lamb and goat. This was the sacrifice permitted for poor families who could not afford a lamb, showing that Jesus was born into a humble, modest family.

beginner.question: In the Bible, the dove with an olive branch (from the story of Noah's ark) has become a well-known symbol of peace and hope. True or false?
beginner.explanation: The dove returning to Noah's ark with an olive leaf in its beak (Genesis 8:11) signalled the end of God's judgment and the beginning of a new era. This image has become one of the most recognised symbols of peace and hope in the world.

beginner.question: In the miracle of feeding the 5,000, Jesus used five barley loaves and two fish from a young boy's lunch to feed the entire crowd. True or false?
beginner.explanation: A boy had five small barley loaves and two small fish, which Andrew brought to Jesus. Jesus gave thanks, broke the bread, and miraculously multiplied it to feed over 5,000 men plus women and children, with twelve baskets left over.

beginner.question: After Jesus fed the 5,000 with five loaves and two fish, the disciples collected seven baskets of leftover food. True or false?
beginner.explanation: After feeding the 5,000, the disciples collected twelve baskets of leftovers, not seven. The seven baskets were from a different miracle — the feeding of the 4,000 described in Mark 8:1-10.

beginner.question: In Matthew 17:27, Jesus told Peter to go fishing, and the first fish he caught would have a four-drachma coin in its mouth — enough to pay the temple tax for both of them. True or false?
beginner.explanation: When tax collectors asked whether Jesus paid the temple tax, Jesus sent Peter to catch a fish. The first fish Peter caught had a four-drachma coin in its mouth, which was exactly the right amount to pay the temple tax for both Jesus and Peter.

beginner.question: In Luke 5:1-11, when Jesus told Simon Peter to let down his nets after fishing all night with no success, Peter caught so many fish that his nets began to break and two boats nearly sank. True or false?
beginner.explanation: The catch was so enormous that the nets began to break. Peter's partners in another boat had to come help, and both boats were so full of fish they began to sink. This miraculous catch led Peter to fall at Jesus's knees in awe.

beginner.question: In John 21:1-14, after Jesus rose from the dead, His disciples caught exactly 100 fish when they obeyed His instruction to cast the net on the right side of the boat. True or false?
beginner.explanation: The disciples caught 153 large fish, not 100. John 21:11 specifically records this number. Despite the huge catch, the net was not torn. This miraculous catch helped the disciples recognise the risen Jesus on the shore.

beginner.question: In Matthew 4:19, when Jesus called Simon Peter and Andrew to be His disciples, He said, 'Come, follow me, and I will make you fishers of men.' True or false?
beginner.explanation: Jesus saw Peter and Andrew casting their fishing nets into the Sea of Galilee and called them with this memorable phrase. They immediately left their nets and followed Jesus, becoming His first disciples.

beginner.question: In the feeding of the 5,000, the bread used was fine wheat bread, the most expensive kind available. True or false?
beginner.explanation: The bread was barley bread, not wheat bread. John 6:9 specifies that the boy had five small barley loaves. Barley bread was the food of the poor — cheaper and coarser than wheat bread — highlighting how God can use humble offerings for great things.

beginner.question: In Luke 5:8, after the miraculous catch of fish, Simon Peter fell at Jesus's knees and said, 'Go away from me, Lord; I am a sinful man!' True or false?
beginner.explanation: Peter was so overwhelmed by the miracle that he recognised Jesus's divine power and his own sinfulness. His response of humility and awe led Jesus to tell him, 'Don't be afraid; from now on you will fish for people.'

beginner.question: In the Gospels, Jesus entered Jerusalem on Palm Sunday riding a horse, as was customary for kings and conquerors. True or false?
beginner.explanation: Jesus did not ride a horse. He rode a donkey (and its colt), fulfilling the prophecy in Zechariah 9:9 about a humble king coming in peace. A horse symbolised war, while a donkey symbolised peace — Jesus came as a peaceful king.

beginner.question: Jesus riding a donkey into Jerusalem on Palm Sunday fulfilled the Old Testament prophecy written by the prophet Zechariah, who predicted the king would come 'gentle and riding on a donkey.' True or false?
beginner.explanation: Zechariah 9:9 prophesied, 'See, your king comes to you, righteous and victorious, lowly and riding on a donkey, on a colt, the foal of a donkey.' Jesus deliberately arranged to ride a donkey to fulfil this specific prophecy.

beginner.question: On Palm Sunday, the crowds who greeted Jesus as He rode the donkey into Jerusalem spread their cloaks and palm branches on the road and shouted 'Hosanna!' True or false?
beginner.explanation: The crowds showed Jesus great honour by spreading their cloaks and cutting palm branches to lay on the road before Him. They shouted, 'Hosanna to the Son of David!' and 'Blessed is He who comes in the name of the Lord!'

beginner.question: In Matthew 21, Jesus sent His disciples to find the donkey for His entry into Jerusalem, and they found it tied up alongside its colt (young donkey), just as Jesus had described. True or false?
beginner.explanation: Jesus told His disciples exactly where to find the donkey and her colt, and everything happened exactly as He said. When the owners questioned them, the disciples said, 'The Lord needs them,' and the owners let them take the animals.

beginner.question: In Matthew 19:24, Jesus said it is easier for a camel to go through the eye of a needle than for a rich person to enter the kingdom of God. True or false?
beginner.explanation: Jesus used this dramatic and intentionally impossible image to make the point that wealth can be a massive barrier to entering God's kingdom. The disciples were astonished, but Jesus added that with God, all things are possible.

beginner.question: When the disciples heard Jesus say it was easier for a camel to go through the eye of a needle than for a rich person to enter heaven, Jesus told them that no rich person can ever be saved. True or false?
beginner.explanation: Jesus did not say no rich person can be saved. When His amazed disciples asked, 'Who then can be saved?' Jesus replied, 'With man this is impossible, but with God all things are possible.' Salvation is God's work, available to everyone through grace.

beginner.question: In Matthew 23:24, Jesus criticised the Pharisees by saying they 'strain out a gnat but swallow a camel,' meaning they focused on tiny rules while ignoring major matters of justice and mercy. True or false?
beginner.explanation: Jesus used this vivid and humorous contrast to rebuke the Pharisees for their hypocrisy. They were meticulous about filtering tiny insects from their drinks (ceremonial purity) but ignored huge sins like injustice, greed, and lack of mercy.

beginner.question: In Genesis 24, when Abraham's servant traveled to find a wife for Isaac, he took ten camels loaded with gifts. Rebekah proved she was God's choice by offering water to both the servant and his camels. True or false?
beginner.explanation: The servant prayed for a specific sign: the right woman would offer water not just to him but also to his ten camels. Rebekah did exactly that — she drew water for all ten camels, which was hard physical work, confirming she was God's chosen bride for Isaac.

beginner.question: In Exodus 32, while Moses was on Mount Sinai receiving the Ten Commandments, the Israelites asked Aaron to make them a god. Aaron collected their gold earrings and made a golden calf. True or false?
beginner.explanation: The Israelites grew impatient waiting for Moses and demanded a visible god. Aaron took their gold earrings, melted them down, and shaped them into a golden calf. The people worshipped it and declared it the god who brought them out of Egypt.

beginner.question: In Exodus 32, it was Moses who made the golden calf for the Israelites to worship while Aaron was away on Mount Sinai. True or false?
beginner.explanation: It was Aaron, not Moses, who made the golden calf. Moses was on Mount Sinai receiving the Ten Commandments from God. Aaron, left in charge of the people, gave in to their pressure and fashioned the idol from their gold jewellery.

beginner.question: In Exodus 32, when Moses came down from Mount Sinai and saw the golden calf idol, he was so angry that he threw the two stone tablets of the Ten Commandments and broke them. True or false?
beginner.explanation: Moses was furious when he saw the golden calf and the people dancing around it. He threw the stone tablets out of his hands, shattering them at the foot of the mountain. God later had Moses carve new tablets to replace the broken ones.

beginner.question: In Exodus 32, Moses burned the golden calf, ground it to powder, scattered the powder on water, and made the Israelites drink it. True or false?
beginner.explanation: Moses took the golden calf, burned it in the fire, ground it to fine powder, sprinkled it on the water, and made the Israelites drink it. This dramatic act demonstrated the utter worthlessness of the idol they had been worshipping.

beginner.question: When Moses confronted Aaron about the golden calf in Exodus 32, Aaron took full responsibility and confessed that he had deliberately crafted the idol. True or false?
beginner.explanation: Aaron did not take responsibility. Instead, he blamed the people, saying they were 'prone to evil,' and made the absurd excuse that he simply threw the gold into the fire and the calf 'came out' by itself, as if it formed on its own.

beginner.question: In 1 Kings 12, King Jeroboam made two golden calves and set them up in Bethel and Dan so that people in the northern kingdom of Israel would not travel to Jerusalem to worship. True or false?
beginner.explanation: After the kingdom split, Jeroboam feared the northern tribes would be drawn back to Judah if they kept going to Jerusalem's temple. He made two golden calves and placed them in Bethel and Dan, repeating the same sin as the wilderness golden calf.

beginner.question: In Exodus 16 and Numbers 11, God sent quail to the Israelites in the wilderness to provide them with meat when they complained about having only manna to eat. True or false?
beginner.explanation: When the Israelites complained about the manna and craved meat, God sent enormous flocks of quail that covered the ground around the camp. The people gathered the quail in large quantities to satisfy their desire for meat.

beginner.question: In Numbers 11, God told Moses the Israelites would eat meat for only three days because of their complaining. True or false?
beginner.explanation: God said the people would eat meat not for three days but for a whole month, until it came out of their nostrils and they loathed it. This was because they had rejected the Lord by complaining and wishing they had stayed in Egypt.

beginner.question: In Numbers 11:31, when God sent quail to the Israelites' camp in the wilderness, the quail piled up about three feet (two cubits) deep on the ground around the camp. True or false?
beginner.explanation: Numbers 11:31 says the quail fell around the camp to a depth of about two cubits (roughly three feet or one metre) above the ground. The Israelites spent two full days gathering the enormous quantity of birds.

beginner.question: In the wilderness, God provided the Israelites with two types of miraculous food: manna in the morning and quail in the evening. True or false?
beginner.explanation: Exodus 16:12-13 says that in the evening, quail came and covered the camp, and in the morning there was a layer of dew that, when it evaporated, left the flaky manna on the ground. Together, these provided bread and meat for the Israelites.

beginner.question: In Numbers 21, God sent venomous snakes to bite the Israelites as punishment because they complained against God and Moses about the food and water in the wilderness. True or false?
beginner.explanation: The Israelites spoke against God and Moses, saying, 'Why have you brought us up out of Egypt to die in the wilderness? There is no bread! There is no water! And we detest this miserable food!' God sent venomous snakes, and many Israelites died.

beginner.question: In Numbers 21, when God sent venomous snakes among the Israelites, Moses made a bronze serpent and put it on a pole. Anyone who was bitten could look at the bronze serpent and be healed. True or false?
beginner.explanation: God told Moses to make a snake and put it on a pole. Moses made it out of bronze. Whenever someone was bitten by a snake and looked at the bronze serpent on the pole in faith, they lived. This required trusting God's instruction.

beginner.question: In Numbers 21, Moses made the serpent on the pole out of gold, the same material used for the golden calf. True or false?
beginner.explanation: Moses made the serpent out of bronze, not gold. Numbers 21:9 specifically says Moses made a bronze snake and put it up on a pole. Bronze was a common and practical metal, different from the gold used in the golden calf idol.

beginner.question: In John 3:14-15, Jesus compared Himself to the bronze serpent that Moses lifted up in the wilderness, saying that just as Moses lifted up the snake, so the Son of Man must be lifted up, so that everyone who believes may have eternal life. True or false?
beginner.explanation: Jesus drew a direct parallel between Moses's bronze serpent and His own coming crucifixion. Just as looking at the lifted-up serpent brought physical healing to the Israelites, looking to the lifted-up Jesus in faith brings eternal spiritual salvation.

beginner.question: The bronze serpent Moses made in the wilderness was eventually destroyed by King Solomon because people started worshipping it. True or false?
beginner.explanation: It was King Hezekiah, not Solomon, who destroyed the bronze serpent. By Hezekiah's time (2 Kings 18:4), the Israelites had been burning incense to the bronze serpent as an idol, calling it Nehushtan. Hezekiah broke it to pieces as part of his reforms.

beginner.question: On the night before Jesus was crucified, He warned Peter that Peter would deny knowing Him three times before the rooster crowed. True or false?
beginner.explanation: At the Last Supper, Jesus predicted Peter's denial, saying, 'Before the rooster crows, you will disown me three times.' Peter protested that he would never do this, but later that very night, he denied Jesus three times, exactly as predicted.

beginner.question: In the Gospels, Peter denied knowing Jesus only once before the rooster crowed. True or false?
beginner.explanation: Peter denied knowing Jesus three times, not once. Each Gospel records three separate denials: first to a servant girl, then to bystanders, and a third time with an oath. After the third denial, the rooster crowed, just as Jesus had predicted.

beginner.question: In Luke 22:61-62, after Peter denied Jesus for the third time and heard the rooster crow, he remembered Jesus's prediction and went outside and wept bitterly. True or false?
beginner.explanation: At the moment the rooster crowed, Jesus turned and looked straight at Peter. Peter remembered the Lord's words and was overcome with grief and shame. He went outside and wept bitterly, devastated by his failure to stand by Jesus.

beginner.question: After the resurrection, Jesus never forgave Peter for denying Him, and Peter was removed from his leadership role among the disciples. True or false?
beginner.explanation: Jesus lovingly restored Peter after the resurrection. In John 21:15-17, Jesus asked Peter three times 'Do you love me?' — once for each denial — and recommissioned him with the task of caring for His followers ('Feed my sheep'). Peter became a great leader of the early church.

beginner.question: In John 21:15-17, the risen Jesus asked Peter 'Do you love me?' three times, matching the three times Peter had denied Him. Each time Peter said yes, Jesus told him to care for His sheep. True or false?
beginner.explanation: This beautiful scene of restoration took place by the Sea of Galilee after the resurrection. Jesus asked Peter three times if he loved Him, and each time Peter affirmed his love, Jesus gave him a commission: 'Feed my lambs,' 'Take care of my sheep,' 'Feed my sheep.'

beginner.question: In Mark 14:72, Mark's Gospel says the rooster crowed twice before Peter's three denials were complete. True or false?
beginner.explanation: Mark's account is uniquely detailed: Jesus told Peter that before the rooster crowed twice, Peter would deny Him three times. The first crow came after the second denial, and the second crow came after the third denial, just as Jesus predicted.

beginner.question: In Luke 5:1-11, after Jesus caused the miraculous catch of fish, He told Peter not to be afraid and that from now on Peter would 'catch people' instead of fish. True or false?
beginner.explanation: After the astonishing catch of fish, Jesus told the awestruck Peter, 'Don't be afraid; from now on you will fish for people.' Peter and his partners James and John then left everything — their boats, nets, and fishing business — to follow Jesus.

beginner.question: The first brothers Jesus called as disciples — Simon Peter and Andrew — were fishermen working at the Sea of Galilee when Jesus called them. True or false?
beginner.explanation: Matthew 4:18-19 says Jesus was walking along the Sea of Galilee when He saw Simon (called Peter) and his brother Andrew casting a net into the lake, because they were fishermen. Jesus called them to follow Him and become fishers of people.

beginner.question: In the feeding of the 4,000 described in Mark 8, Jesus used five loaves and two fish, the same amounts as in the feeding of the 5,000. True or false?
beginner.explanation: The feeding of the 4,000 used seven loaves and a few small fish, not five loaves and two fish. The five loaves and two fish were used in the feeding of the 5,000. These were two separate miracles with different numbers.

beginner.question: In Judges 14:12-13, Samson bet thirty Philistine men at his wedding feast that they could not solve his riddle. If they won, Samson would give them thirty sets of clothes and thirty linen garments. True or false?
beginner.explanation: Samson wagered thirty linen garments and thirty sets of clothing. The Philistines eventually cheated by threatening Samson's wife until she got the answer from Samson and told them the solution to his riddle about the lion and honey.

beginner.question: In Judges 16, Samson's incredible strength came from a special diet of honey and locusts that he ate every day. True or false?
beginner.explanation: Samson's strength did not come from his diet. It came from the Spirit of the Lord, connected to his Nazirite vow. The outward symbol of this vow was his uncut hair. When Delilah had his hair cut, the Spirit departed and he lost his strength.

beginner.question: In 1 Samuel 17:40, David chose five smooth stones from a stream before going out to fight Goliath the Philistine giant. True or false?
beginner.explanation: David rejected King Saul's armour and instead took his shepherd's staff, chose five smooth stones from a stream, put them in his shepherd's bag, and carried his sling in his hand as he approached the giant Goliath.

beginner.question: In 1 Kings 17, after the brook Cherith dried up, God sent Elijah to a widow in Jerusalem who would provide for him. True or false?
beginner.explanation: God sent Elijah to a widow in Zarephath, not Jerusalem. Zarephath was a town in the region of Sidon, outside Israel. The widow there miraculously never ran out of flour and oil during the entire drought, just as God had promised.

beginner.question: In Ezekiel 34, God compared the leaders of Israel to bad shepherds who had not cared for their flock, and He promised to personally shepherd His people. True or false?
beginner.explanation: In Ezekiel 34, God strongly rebuked Israel's leaders (shepherds) for exploiting and neglecting the people (sheep). He promised to remove those bad shepherds and personally search for, rescue, tend, and care for His sheep.

beginner.question: In the plagues of Egypt, the very first plague turned the Nile River into milk. True or false?
beginner.explanation: The first plague turned the Nile River and all water in Egypt into blood, not milk. The fish in the Nile died, the river smelled foul, and the Egyptians could not drink the water. Blood, not milk, was the substance.

beginner.question: In the miracle described in John 21, the risen Jesus cooked breakfast for His disciples on the shore of the Sea of Galilee, including fish and bread on a charcoal fire. True or false?
beginner.explanation: When the disciples came ashore after their miraculous catch of 153 fish, they found Jesus had already prepared breakfast. He had a charcoal fire with fish on it and some bread. Jesus invited them, 'Come and have breakfast.'

beginner.question: In Psalm 23:4, David wrote, 'Even though I walk through the valley of the shadow of death, I will fear no evil, for You are with me; Your rod and Your staff, they comfort me.' The rod and staff were shepherd's tools. True or false?
beginner.explanation: The rod and staff were essential tools of a shepherd. The rod was used to protect the sheep from predators, and the staff with its curved hook was used to guide sheep and pull them from danger. David used these images to describe God's protection and guidance.

beginner.question: In Jonah chapter 4, after God spared Nineveh, Jonah was happy because he wanted the people of Nineveh to be saved. True or false?
beginner.explanation: Jonah was actually angry, not happy, that God spared Nineveh. He told God this was exactly why he had fled to Tarshish in the first place — because he knew God was gracious and merciful and might relent from destroying Israel's enemies.

beginner.question: In Genesis 9:3, after the flood, God gave Noah and his descendants permission to eat meat from animals, saying, 'Everything that lives and moves about will be food for you.' True or false?
