beginner.explanation: After the flood, God expanded the human diet beyond plants to include meat. He told Noah, 'Everything that lives and moves about will be food for you. Just as I gave you the green plants, I now give you everything,' with the restriction of not eating blood.

beginner.question: In 1 Samuel 17, David's father Jesse was the one who told David to volunteer to fight Goliath. True or false?
beginner.explanation: Jesse did not send David to fight Goliath. Jesse sent David to bring food to his older brothers who were in King Saul's army. When David arrived at the battlefield and heard Goliath's challenge, he volunteered on his own initiative to fight the giant.

beginner.question: In Matthew 7:15, Jesus warned His followers to watch out for false prophets, saying they come disguised in sheep's clothing but are actually ferocious wolves. True or false?
beginner.explanation: Jesus said, 'Watch out for false prophets. They come to you in sheep's clothing, but inwardly they are ferocious wolves.' This animal metaphor warns believers that dangerous false teachers may look harmless and trustworthy on the outside.

beginner.question: In Matthew 12:40, Jesus said He would be in the heart of the earth for forty days and forty nights, just as Jonah was in the belly of the great fish. True or false?
beginner.explanation: Jesus said three days and three nights, not forty. He said, 'For as Jonah was three days and three nights in the belly of a huge fish, so the Son of Man will be three days and three nights in the heart of the earth,' referring to His burial and resurrection.

beginner.question: In the Bible, the Jordan River is where John the Baptist baptised Jesus, and the Holy Spirit descended on Jesus like a dove. True or false?
beginner.explanation: The Gospels record that Jesus came from Galilee to the Jordan River to be baptised by John the Baptist. When Jesus came up from the water, the heavens opened, the Spirit descended like a dove, and God's voice said, 'This is my Son, whom I love.'

beginner.question: In Numbers 22, Balaam's donkey turned aside from the angel three separate times: first into a field, then it pressed against a wall crushing Balaam's foot, and finally it lay down under Balaam. True or false?
beginner.explanation: The donkey avoided the angel three times: first it turned into a field, then it pressed against a wall and crushed Balaam's foot, and finally it simply lay down and refused to move. Each time, Balaam beat the donkey, not seeing the angel.

beginner.question: In 1 Kings 18, the prophet Elijah challenged the 450 prophets of Baal to a contest on Mount Carmel. Each side prepared a bull as a sacrifice, and the god who answered by sending fire would be the true God. True or false?
beginner.explanation: Elijah proposed a test: both sides would prepare a bull on an altar without lighting the fire. They would then pray to their respective gods, and the one who answered by sending fire from heaven would be proved to be the true God.

beginner.question: In Matthew 10:29-31, Jesus told His disciples that not even a sparrow falls to the ground apart from God's will, and that they were worth more than many sparrows. True or false?
beginner.explanation: Jesus used the example of sparrows — one of the cheapest birds sold in the marketplace — to reassure His followers of God's care. If God notices every sparrow that falls, how much more does He care for people, who are worth far more than sparrows.

beginner.question: Noah was 300 years old when the great flood began, according to Genesis 7. True or false?
beginner.explanation: Noah was 600 years old, not 300, when the floodwaters came on the earth. Genesis 7:6 states this clearly. People in the early chapters of Genesis lived much longer lives than people do today. Noah lived to be 950 years old in total.

beginner.question: In Exodus 12, the Israelites were told to eat the Passover meal with bitter herbs and bread made without yeast (unleavened bread). True or false?
beginner.explanation: The Passover meal consisted of roasted lamb, bitter herbs (symbolising the bitterness of slavery in Egypt), and unleavened bread (bread made without yeast, because the Israelites had to leave Egypt in such a hurry there was no time for dough to rise).

beginner.question: In Daniel 6, the law that got Daniel thrown into the lions' den said no one could pray to any god or person other than King Darius for 60 days. True or false?
beginner.explanation: The law was for 30 days, not 60. The jealous officials tricked King Darius into signing a decree that for 30 days, anyone who prayed to any god or human except the king would be thrown into the lions' den.

beginner.question: In John 10:10, Jesus said, 'I have come that they may have life, and have it to the full,' contrasting Himself with the thief who comes only to steal, kill, and destroy the sheep. True or false?
beginner.explanation: Using the shepherd-and-sheep imagery, Jesus contrasted Himself with false leaders (thieves and robbers). While they come to exploit and destroy the sheep, Jesus came to give His followers abundant, overflowing life.

beginner.question: In the book of Jonah, the city of Nineveh was located in Egypt and was ruled by Pharaoh. True or false?
beginner.explanation: Nineveh was not in Egypt. It was the capital of the Assyrian Empire, located in what is now modern-day Iraq. The Assyrians were a powerful and feared nation, and their enemies of Israel, which is part of why Jonah did not want to go there.

beginner.question: In Numbers 11:4-6, when the Israelites complained about the manna, they remembered eating fish, cucumbers, melons, leeks, onions, and garlic back in Egypt. True or false?
beginner.explanation: The Israelites had a selective memory, fondly recalling the variety of food in Egypt while conveniently forgetting the harsh slavery. They specifically listed fish (which they claimed was free), cucumbers, melons, leeks, onions, and garlic.

beginner.question: In Revelation 5:5, Jesus is called both 'the Lion of the tribe of Judah' and 'the Lamb' — two very different animal images used for the same person. True or false?
beginner.explanation: In Revelation 5, an elder tells John that 'the Lion of the tribe of Judah' has triumphed and can open the scroll. But when John looks, he sees a Lamb that appears to have been slain. Jesus embodies both images — He is the conquering Lion and the sacrificial Lamb.

beginner.question: In Genesis 22, when God tested Abraham by asking him to sacrifice his son Isaac, Abraham told Isaac that God Himself would provide the lamb for the burnt offering — and God did provide a ram caught in a thicket. True or false?
beginner.explanation: When Isaac asked where the lamb for the sacrifice was, Abraham prophetically replied, 'God himself will provide the lamb.' At the last moment, God stopped Abraham and provided a ram caught by its horns in a thicket as a substitute sacrifice.

beginner.question: In John 10, Jesus described Himself as 'the gate for the sheep,' saying that whoever enters through Him will be saved. True or false?
beginner.explanation: Jesus said, 'I am the gate; whoever enters through me will be saved. They will come in and go out, and find pasture.' In ancient times, the shepherd literally lay across the opening of the sheep pen, becoming the gate that protected the sheep.

beginner.question: In Exodus 4:3, when God appeared to Moses at the burning bush, He told Moses to throw his shepherd's staff on the ground and it turned into a serpent. True or false?
beginner.explanation: God gave Moses this miraculous sign to convince the Israelites that God had appeared to him. When Moses threw his staff on the ground it became a snake, and when he grabbed it by the tail it turned back into a staff.

beginner.question: According to the book of Exodus, the plague of flies affected only Pharaoh's palace and did not spread to the rest of Egypt. True or false?
beginner.explanation: The plague of flies was not limited to Pharaoh's palace. Dense swarms of flies invaded Pharaoh's palace AND the houses of his officials AND throughout all of Egypt. However, the land of Goshen where the Israelites lived was protected from the flies.

beginner.question: In Matthew 26:34, Jesus predicted Peter would deny Him before the rooster crowed — and this prediction was made during the Last Supper on the night Jesus was arrested. True or false?
beginner.explanation: During the Last Supper, Jesus told His disciples that they would all fall away. When Peter insisted he would never deny Jesus, Jesus specifically told him, 'Truly I tell you, this very night, before the rooster crows, you will disown me three times.'

beginner.question: The golden calf that the Israelites worshipped in Exodus 32 was made from silver coins they had brought from Egypt. True or false?
beginner.explanation: The golden calf was made from gold earrings, not silver coins. Aaron asked the people to bring their gold earrings from their wives, sons, and daughters. He collected the gold, melted it down, and shaped it into the form of a calf.

beginner.question: In Luke 15:4-7, Jesus told the parable of the lost sheep to show that there is great joy in heaven over one sinner who repents. True or false?
beginner.explanation: After describing the shepherd who left 99 sheep to find one that was lost, Jesus said, 'I tell you that in the same way there will be more rejoicing in heaven over one sinner who repents than over ninety-nine righteous persons who do not need to repent.'

beginner.question: In Proverbs 30:25, the Bible describes ants as creatures that are small but extremely wise because they store up food in summer. True or false?
beginner.explanation: Proverbs 30:25 says, 'Ants are creatures of little strength, yet they store up their food in the summer.' The Bible uses various animals, including ants, to teach practical wisdom about planning ahead and being diligent.

beginner.question: In Matthew 21, when Jesus rode the donkey into Jerusalem on Palm Sunday, the crowd was angry and threw stones at Him. True or false?
beginner.explanation: The crowd was not angry — they were joyful and celebratory! They spread their cloaks and palm branches on the road, and shouted 'Hosanna to the Son of David!' They praised Jesus and welcomed Him as a king coming in the name of the Lord.

beginner.question: In Exodus 12:11, God told the Israelites to eat the Passover meal dressed and ready to travel — with cloaks tucked in, sandals on their feet, and staff in hand — because they would need to leave Egypt in a hurry. True or false?
beginner.explanation: God said, 'This is how you are to eat it: with your cloak tucked into your belt, your sandals on your feet and your staff in your hand. Eat it in haste; it is the Lord's Passover.' The urgency reflected the speed of God's deliverance that night.

beginner.question: In John 21:15-17, the risen Jesus asked Peter 'Do you love me?' two times, matching Peter's two denials on the night of Jesus's arrest. True or false?
beginner.explanation: Jesus asked Peter 'Do you love me?' three times, not two, because Peter had denied Jesus three times (not two). Each question corresponded to one of Peter's three denials, and each affirmative answer was met with a command to care for Jesus's followers.

beginner.question: One of the most famous verses in the entire Bible says, 'For God so loved the world, that he gave his only begotten Son, that whosoever believeth in him should not perish, but have everlasting life.' Which book and verse is this found in?
beginner.explanation: This iconic verse is John 3:16. Jesus spoke these words to Nicodemus, a Pharisee who came to see him at night. It summarises the core message of the Gospel — God's love for humanity and the promise of eternal life through faith.

beginner.question: John 3:16 says, 'For God so loved the world, that he gave his only begotten ___.' What word fills in the blank?
beginner.explanation: The verse says God gave his only begotten 'Son,' referring to Jesus Christ. This is the central claim of the Christian faith — that God sent his Son into the world as an act of love for all humanity.

beginner.question: In the famous verse John 3:16, it says 'that whosoever believeth in him should not perish, but have ___.' What does the verse promise believers will receive?
beginner.explanation: John 3:16 promises 'everlasting life' to those who believe. The verse contrasts perishing with eternal life, making it one of the clearest statements of the Gospel's promise of salvation.

beginner.question: John 3:16 is one of the most memorised Bible verses. According to this verse, what motivated God to give his only begotten Son to the world?
beginner.explanation: The verse begins with 'For God so loved the world,' making it clear that love was the motivation. God's love for all humanity is presented as the driving force behind sending Jesus.

beginner.question: In the Gospel of John, chapter 3, Jesus is having a private conversation at night when he says, 'For God so loved the world, that he gave his only begotten Son.' Who was Jesus speaking to?
beginner.explanation: Jesus was speaking to Nicodemus, a Pharisee and member of the Jewish ruling council. Nicodemus came to Jesus at night, likely to avoid being seen, and Jesus taught him about being 'born again' and God's love.

beginner.question: The verse John 3:16 says believers 'should not perish.' What does this verse say they will have instead of perishing?
beginner.explanation: The verse promises 'everlasting life' as the alternative to perishing. It presents a clear contrast: without faith, one perishes; with faith in God's Son, one receives eternal life.

beginner.question: John 3:16 begins with the words, 'For God so loved the ___.' What word completes this famous opening?
beginner.explanation: The verse says God loved 'the world,' meaning all of humanity, not just one nation or group. This universality is one of the reasons John 3:16 is so widely quoted and beloved.

beginner.question: Psalm 23 is one of the most beloved chapters in the Bible. It begins with the words, 'The Lord is my ___.' What word completes this famous opening line?
beginner.explanation: Psalm 23 opens with 'The Lord is my shepherd.' Written by King David, who was himself a shepherd in his youth, this psalm compares God's care for his people to a shepherd caring for his flock.

beginner.question: Psalm 23 says, 'The Lord is my shepherd; I shall not ___.' According to this famous psalm, what does the psalmist say he shall not do?
beginner.explanation: The verse says 'I shall not want,' meaning the psalmist will lack nothing because the Lord provides for him like a shepherd provides for his sheep. It expresses total trust in God's provision.

beginner.question: Psalm 23 says, 'He maketh me to lie down in green pastures: he leadeth me beside the still ___.' What word completes this peaceful image?
beginner.explanation: The verse says 'still waters.' This imagery of green pastures and calm, still waters paints a picture of peace, rest, and God's gentle provision, much like a shepherd leading sheep to safe resting places.

beginner.question: The beloved Psalm 23 includes the line, 'Yea, though I walk through the valley of the shadow of ___.' What word completes this well-known phrase?
beginner.explanation: The verse says 'the valley of the shadow of death.' Even in life's darkest and most dangerous moments, the psalmist declares he will fear no evil because God is with him.

beginner.question: In Psalm 23:4, after saying 'I walk through the valley of the shadow of death,' the psalmist says, 'I will fear no evil: for ___ art with me.' Who is the psalmist addressing?
beginner.explanation: The psalmist is speaking directly to God, saying 'thou art with me.' The entire psalm is about the Lord being the psalmist's shepherd, and here David expresses confidence that God's presence removes all fear.

beginner.question: Psalm 23 mentions two items that bring comfort in the valley of the shadow of death: 'Thy rod and thy ___ they comfort me.' What is the second item?
beginner.explanation: The verse says 'thy rod and thy staff they comfort me.' A shepherd's rod was used for protection and the staff for guiding sheep. Together they represent God's protection and guidance.

beginner.question: Psalm 23 is traditionally attributed to which Old Testament figure, who was himself a shepherd before becoming king of Israel?
beginner.explanation: Psalm 23 is attributed to King David. Before becoming king, David spent years as a shepherd boy tending his father's flocks, which gives the psalm's shepherd imagery a deeply personal meaning.

beginner.question: The very first verse of the entire Bible says, 'In the beginning God created the ___ and the ___.' What two things did God create according to Genesis 1:1?
beginner.explanation: Genesis 1:1 says, 'In the beginning God created the heaven and the earth.' This is the opening statement of the Bible and establishes God as the Creator of everything that exists.

beginner.question: Genesis 1:1 opens with three famous words that set the stage for the entire Bible. What are they?
beginner.explanation: 'In the beginning' are the opening words of the Bible in Genesis 1:1. These words establish that everything — time, space, and matter — had a starting point, and that God existed before it all.

beginner.question: The famous verse 'In the beginning God created the heaven and the earth' is found in which book of the Bible?
beginner.explanation: This verse is Genesis 1:1, the very first verse in the Bible. Genesis means 'origin' or 'beginning,' and the book tells the story of creation, the first humans, and the patriarchs of Israel.

beginner.question: In Genesis chapter 1, after God created the heavens and the earth, the Bible says 'the earth was without form, and void; and darkness was upon the face of the deep.' What did God create first to address the darkness?
beginner.explanation: God said 'Let there be light,' and there was light. This was on the first day of creation. Notably, the sun and stars were not created until the fourth day, so this initial light was distinct from them.

beginner.question: In Genesis 1:1, the Hebrew word used for God is 'Elohim.' The verse 'In the beginning God created the heaven and the earth' is which verse number in the Bible?
beginner.explanation: This is Genesis 1:1, the very first verse in the entire Bible. It is the foundational statement of the Scriptures, declaring that God is the Creator of all things.

beginner.question: The creation account in Genesis 1 describes God creating the world over how many days?
beginner.explanation: God created everything in six days and rested on the seventh day. Genesis 2:2 says, 'On the seventh day God ended his work which he had made; and he rested on the seventh day.'

beginner.question: The Old Testament prophet Jeremiah wrote, 'For I know the plans I have for you, declares the Lord, plans to ___ you and not to harm you.' What word fills in the blank?
beginner.explanation: Jeremiah 29:11 says 'plans to prosper you and not to harm you.' God spoke these words through the prophet Jeremiah to the Israelites who were in exile in Babylon, assuring them he had a good future planned for them.

beginner.question: Jeremiah 29:11 is a beloved verse that says, 'For I know the plans I have for you... plans to prosper you and not to harm you, plans to give you hope and a ___.' What word completes this promise?
beginner.explanation: The verse ends with 'hope and a future.' It is one of the most quoted Old Testament promises, assuring believers that God has good intentions and a hopeful plan for their lives.

beginner.question: The encouraging verse 'For I know the plans I have for you, declares the Lord' was written by which Old Testament prophet?
beginner.explanation: This verse was written by the prophet Jeremiah. He conveyed God's message to the Israelites during their exile in Babylon, promising that God had not abandoned them and had plans for their restoration.

beginner.question: Jeremiah 29:11 was originally written as a message from God to the Israelites during a specific period in their history. What situation were they in when they received this promise?
beginner.explanation: The Israelites were living in exile in Babylon when Jeremiah delivered this message. God was reassuring them that despite their difficult circumstances, he had plans to prosper them and give them a hopeful future.

beginner.question: In Jeremiah 29:11, God says he has plans for his people. According to the verse, these plans are described as plans that will NOT do what?
beginner.explanation: The verse says plans 'not to harm you.' God specifically contrasts prospering with harming, and hope with despair, to assure the exiled Israelites that his intentions are entirely good.

beginner.question: Which book of the Bible contains the famous verse, 'For I know the plans I have for you, declares the Lord, plans to prosper you and not to harm you, plans to give you hope and a future'?
beginner.explanation: This is Jeremiah 29:11. The book of Jeremiah records the prophecies and messages of the prophet Jeremiah, who ministered during the fall of Jerusalem and the Babylonian exile.

beginner.question: Jeremiah 29:11 promises two things at the end of the verse. God says he will give his people 'hope and a ___.' What is the second promise?
beginner.explanation: God promises 'hope and a future.' This was especially meaningful to the exiled Israelites who may have felt hopeless. God assured them that their story was not over and better days were ahead.

beginner.question: The apostle Paul wrote from prison, 'I can do all things through ___ which strengtheneth me.' Who does Paul credit for giving him strength in Philippians 4:13?
beginner.explanation: Paul says 'through Christ which strengtheneth me.' Writing from prison, Paul expressed that no matter his circumstances — whether in plenty or in need — Christ gave him the strength to endure anything.

beginner.question: Philippians 4:13 says, 'I can do ___ things through Christ which strengtheneth me.' What word fills in the blank?
beginner.explanation: The verse says 'all things.' Paul is expressing that with Christ's strength, he can face any situation — abundance or scarcity, comfort or hardship. It is a declaration of total reliance on Christ.

beginner.question: The famous verse 'I can do all things through Christ which strengtheneth me' is found in which New Testament letter?
beginner.explanation: This is Philippians 4:13. Paul wrote the letter to the Philippians while he was imprisoned, yet it is one of the most joyful letters in the New Testament, full of encouragement and gratitude.

beginner.question: Who wrote the words, 'I can do all things through Christ which strengtheneth me,' in a letter to the church at Philippi?
beginner.explanation: The apostle Paul wrote this verse. He penned it while imprisoned, likely in Rome. Despite his chains, Paul expressed confidence that Christ's power enabled him to handle every circumstance.

beginner.question: In the verses just before Philippians 4:13 ('I can do all things through Christ'), Paul explains that he has learned to be content in every situation. What is the key lesson Paul shares?
options: How to be content whether in plenty or in need
beginner.explanation: Paul says he has learned to be content whether well fed or hungry, whether living in plenty or in want. Philippians 4:13 is the conclusion of this thought — Christ is the source of his contentment in all circumstances.

beginner.question: Philippians 4:13 is one of the most popular verses among athletes and public figures. What was Paul's situation when he wrote it?
options: He was travelling on a missionary journey
He was preaching in the temple
He was on a boat during a storm
beginner.explanation: Paul wrote Philippians while he was imprisoned, likely in Rome. Despite being in chains, his letter is remarkably joyful and encouraging, and Philippians 4:13 reflects his confidence that Christ's strength transcends any hardship.

beginner.question: Romans 8:28 says, 'And we know that ___ things work together for good to them that love God.' What word fills in the blank?
beginner.explanation: The verse says 'all things work together for good.' Paul is writing to the Roman church, assuring believers that God uses every circumstance — even difficult ones — for the ultimate good of those who love him.

beginner.question: The famous verse 'All things work together for good to them that love God' is found in which book of the Bible?
beginner.explanation: This is Romans 8:28. Paul's letter to the Romans is a foundational theological text, and chapter 8 is considered one of the greatest chapters in all of Scripture, full of assurance and hope.

beginner.question: Romans 8:28 promises that all things work together for good to a specific group of people. Who does the verse say will experience this good?
beginner.explanation: The verse specifies 'them that love God, to them who are the called according to his purpose.' It is a promise directed at believers who love God and are living according to his calling.

beginner.question: Who wrote the encouraging words of Romans 8:28, 'And we know that all things work together for good to them that love God'?
beginner.explanation: The apostle Paul wrote the book of Romans. It is his longest and most theologically detailed letter, and Romans 8:28 is one of its most comforting and frequently quoted verses.

beginner.question: Romans 8:28 adds a further description of those who benefit from God working all things for good. It says they are 'called according to his ___.' What word completes the verse?
beginner.explanation: The full verse says they are 'called according to his purpose.' This means God has a specific purpose for each believer's life, and he orchestrates circumstances to fulfil that purpose.

beginner.question: Romans 8:28 begins with the confident phrase 'And we ___.' What word follows, showing the certainty of this promise?
beginner.explanation: Paul writes 'we know,' expressing certainty rather than mere hope or speculation. This confidence comes from faith in God's sovereign control over all circumstances for the benefit of his people.

beginner.question: The book of Proverbs says, 'Trust in the Lord with all thine ___; and lean not unto thine own understanding.' What word fills in the blank?
beginner.explanation: Proverbs 3:5 says 'with all thine heart.' This verse calls for wholehearted trust in God rather than relying on one's own limited understanding. It is one of the most frequently quoted wisdom verses in the Bible.

beginner.question: Proverbs 3:5 says to 'Trust in the Lord with all thine heart; and lean not unto thine own ___.' What does the verse warn against leaning on?
beginner.explanation: The verse warns against leaning on 'thine own understanding.' It teaches that human reasoning is limited, and true wisdom comes from fully trusting God rather than relying solely on our own judgement.

beginner.question: Proverbs 3:6 follows up the command to trust God wholeheartedly by saying, 'In all thy ways acknowledge him, and he shall direct thy ___.' What will God direct?
beginner.explanation: The verse promises 'he shall direct thy paths.' When we acknowledge God in every aspect of life, he promises to guide our direction. Together, Proverbs 3:5-6 is one of the Bible's clearest calls to trust and divine guidance.

beginner.question: The famous verses 'Trust in the Lord with all thine heart; and lean not unto thine own understanding. In all thy ways acknowledge him, and he shall direct thy paths' are found in which book?
beginner.explanation: These are Proverbs 3:5-6. The book of Proverbs is a collection of wise sayings, largely attributed to King Solomon, and these verses are among the most beloved instructions for living a God-directed life.

beginner.question: Proverbs 3:5-6 calls believers to trust God and acknowledge him in all their ways. The book of Proverbs is traditionally attributed to which wise king of Israel?
beginner.explanation: The book of Proverbs is largely attributed to King Solomon, known as the wisest man who ever lived. God granted Solomon extraordinary wisdom when he asked for it instead of riches or power (1 Kings 3).

beginner.question: Proverbs 3:6 says, 'In ___ thy ways acknowledge him.' How many of our ways should we acknowledge God in, according to this verse?
beginner.explanation: The verse says 'in all thy ways,' meaning every aspect of life — not just the big decisions, but everything. This comprehensive call to acknowledge God in all things is what makes the promise of directed paths so powerful.

beginner.question: Proverbs 3:5 instructs believers to trust God with ALL their heart. What does the verse say they should NOT lean on?
beginner.explanation: The verse says 'lean not unto thine own understanding.' It does not say understanding is bad, but that we should not rely on it above God's wisdom. The contrast is between self-reliance and God-reliance.

beginner.question: The prophet Isaiah wrote, 'But they that wait upon the Lord shall renew their ___.' According to Isaiah 40:31, what will be renewed?
beginner.explanation: Isaiah 40:31 promises that those who wait on the Lord 'shall renew their strength.' This verse offers encouragement to the weary, promising that patient trust in God leads to restored power and endurance.

beginner.question: Isaiah 40:31 says those who wait on the Lord 'shall mount up with wings as ___.' What bird is mentioned in this famous comparison?
beginner.explanation: The verse says 'wings as eagles.' Eagles are known for soaring to great heights with powerful, effortless flight. This imagery represents the spiritual strength and elevation that comes from trusting in God.

beginner.question: Isaiah 40:31 lists three promises for those who wait on the Lord: they shall mount up with wings as eagles, they shall run and not be weary, and they shall ___ and not faint. What is the third action?
beginner.explanation: The verse says they shall 'walk, and not faint.' The progression from soaring to running to walking shows that God gives strength for every season — the extraordinary, the active, and the everyday ordinary.

beginner.question: The famous verse about mounting up with wings as eagles and running without growing weary is found in which Old Testament book?
beginner.explanation: This is Isaiah 40:31. The prophet Isaiah wrote this to encourage the people of Israel, promising that God never grows tired and those who trust in him will find their strength renewed.

beginner.question: Isaiah 40:31 begins with a condition: 'But they that ___ upon the Lord shall renew their strength.' What must people do to receive this promise of renewed strength?
beginner.explanation: The verse says 'wait upon the Lord.' Waiting here means patiently trusting in God and looking to him expectantly, not passive idleness. It's an active trust that God will act in his perfect timing.

beginner.question: Isaiah 40:31 says those who wait on God shall 'run, and not be ___.' What word describes what they will NOT experience while running?
beginner.explanation: The verse says 'run, and not be weary.' God promises supernatural endurance to those who trust in him. Even when life demands great effort, those who wait on the Lord will not be exhausted.

beginner.question: Before ascending to heaven, Jesus gave his followers what is known as the Great Commission. He said, 'Go ye therefore, and teach all ___.' What did Jesus tell them to teach?
beginner.explanation: Jesus said 'teach all nations' (or 'make disciples of all nations'). The Great Commission in Matthew 28:19-20 expanded the mission beyond Israel to the entire world, commissioning the apostles to spread the Gospel globally.

beginner.question: The Great Commission in Matthew 28:19 instructs believers to go and make disciples of all nations, 'baptising them in the name of the Father, and of the Son, and of the ___.' Who is the third person mentioned?
beginner.explanation: Jesus says 'the Holy Ghost' (or Holy Spirit). This is one of the clearest references to the Trinity in Scripture — Father, Son, and Holy Spirit — and it establishes the baptismal formula used by churches to this day.

beginner.question: At the end of the Great Commission in Matthew 28:20, Jesus makes a comforting promise to his disciples. He says, 'And, lo, I am with you ___.' How long does Jesus promise to be with them?
options: Until the end of the year
Always, even unto the end of the world
beginner.explanation: Jesus promises 'I am with you always, even unto the end of the world.' This assurance means Jesus will never abandon his followers, no matter what they face in carrying out the Great Commission.

beginner.question: The Great Commission — Jesus's final instructions to go and make disciples of all nations — is found near the end of which Gospel?
beginner.explanation: The Great Commission is found in Matthew 28:19-20, at the very end of the Gospel of Matthew. It records Jesus's final words to his disciples before his ascension, charging them with a worldwide mission.

beginner.question: In the Great Commission (Matthew 28:19-20), Jesus tells his disciples to teach all nations to observe 'all things whatsoever I have ___ you.' What word fills the blank?
beginner.explanation: Jesus says 'commanded you.' The Great Commission is not just about evangelism but also about discipleship — teaching new believers to follow and obey everything Jesus taught during his earthly ministry.

beginner.question: The Great Commission begins with Jesus declaring, 'All ___ is given unto me in heaven and in earth.' What has been given to Jesus?
beginner.explanation: Jesus declares 'All authority is given unto me' (or 'all power' in KJV). This establishes the foundation for the Great Commission — because Jesus has supreme authority, his followers can go out with confidence.

beginner.question: When Jesus gave the Great Commission in Matthew 28:19-20, where were his disciples gathered to hear these final instructions?
options: In the upper room in Jerusalem
beginner.explanation: The disciples met Jesus on a mountain in Galilee, as he had instructed them. Matthew 28:16 says they went to the mountain where Jesus had told them to go, and it was there he gave the Great Commission.

beginner.question: In 1 Corinthians 13, often called 'the love chapter,' Paul describes what love is. He writes, 'Love is patient, love is ___.' What is the second quality of love mentioned?
beginner.explanation: Paul says 'love is kind.' First Corinthians 13 is one of the most famous passages about love in all of literature, and it begins by describing love as patient and kind before listing what love does not do.

beginner.question: In the love chapter (1 Corinthians 13), Paul lists three things that remain forever: faith, hope, and love. According to verse 13, which of these three is the greatest?
beginner.explanation: Paul declares that 'the greatest of these is love.' While faith and hope are essential, love is placed above them as the supreme virtue. This conclusion is the climax of the entire chapter.

beginner.question: First Corinthians 13, the famous 'love chapter,' is found in a letter written by Paul to the church in which city?
beginner.explanation: Paul wrote this to the church in Corinth. The Corinthian church was experiencing divisions and disputes, so Paul wrote this powerful chapter to remind them that love is more important than spiritual gifts or knowledge.

beginner.question: In 1 Corinthians 13:4, Paul says that love 'does not envy, it does not boast, it is not ___.' What negative quality does Paul say love does NOT have?
beginner.explanation: Paul says love 'is not proud.' The passage systematically describes what love does and does not do, and pride is listed as something incompatible with true love.

beginner.question: First Corinthians 13:8 makes a bold statement about the permanence of love. It says, 'Love never ___.' What word completes this declaration?
beginner.explanation: Paul declares 'love never fails.' While prophecies will cease and knowledge will pass away, love endures forever. This is one of the strongest claims in the chapter about the eternal nature of love.

beginner.question: In 1 Corinthians 13:13, Paul says, 'And now abideth faith, hope, love, these three.' How many lasting virtues does Paul list?
beginner.explanation: Paul lists three abiding virtues: faith, hope, and love. These three have become known as the 'theological virtues' in Christian tradition, with love being declared the greatest of the three.

beginner.question: In 1 Corinthians 13:1, Paul says that if he speaks with the tongues of men and of angels but does not have love, he is like a 'sounding brass, or a tinkling ___.' What instrument does Paul mention?
beginner.explanation: Paul compares loveless speech to a 'tinkling cymbal.' His point is that even the most impressive spiritual gifts are empty noise without love. This dramatic opening sets the tone for the entire love chapter.

beginner.question: Jesus taught his disciples how to pray in what is commonly known as the Lord's Prayer. It begins with the words, 'Our Father which art in ___.' Where does it say the Father is?
beginner.explanation: The Lord's Prayer begins 'Our Father which art in heaven.' Jesus taught this model prayer to show his disciples how to approach God — as a loving Father who dwells in heaven, whose name is holy.

beginner.question: In the Lord's Prayer, after addressing God as 'Our Father which art in heaven,' the next line says, '___ be thy name.' What word fills in the blank?
beginner.explanation: The prayer says 'Hallowed be thy name,' meaning 'may your name be treated as holy.' This establishes the priority of honouring God before making personal requests.

beginner.question: The Lord's Prayer includes the petition, 'Give us this day our daily ___.' What does the prayer ask God to provide?
beginner.explanation: The prayer asks for 'daily bread.' This request for basic sustenance shows that it is appropriate to bring even everyday physical needs to God in prayer, trusting him for provision one day at a time.

beginner.question: In the Lord's Prayer, Jesus teaches believers to pray, 'And forgive us our debts, as we forgive our ___.' Who must believers forgive?
beginner.explanation: The prayer says 'as we forgive our debtors,' meaning those who have wronged us. Jesus links God's forgiveness of us directly to our willingness to forgive others — a central theme of his teaching.

beginner.question: The Lord's Prayer asks God to 'lead us not into ___.' What does the prayer ask to be protected from?
beginner.explanation: The prayer says 'lead us not into temptation.' This petition acknowledges human weakness and asks God for protection from situations that could lead to sin and spiritual failure.

beginner.question: The Lord's Prayer is found in Jesus's most famous sermon. Which sermon contains this model prayer?
beginner.explanation: The Lord's Prayer is part of the Sermon on the Mount, found in Matthew chapters 5-7. In this sermon, Jesus taught about prayer, saying not to use vain repetitions but to pray sincerely, using this model prayer.

beginner.question: The Lord's Prayer concludes with the declaration, 'For thine is the kingdom, and the power, and the ___, for ever.' What word completes this closing praise?
beginner.explanation: The prayer ends with 'the glory, for ever. Amen.' This doxology (closing praise) returns the focus to God, acknowledging that all kingdom authority, power, and glory belong to him eternally.

beginner.question: In the Sermon on the Mount, Jesus teaches the Beatitudes — a series of blessings. The first one says, 'Blessed are the poor in spirit: for theirs is the kingdom of ___.' What word completes it?
beginner.explanation: Jesus says 'the kingdom of heaven.' The poor in spirit are those who recognise their spiritual need and dependence on God. Jesus promises them the greatest reward — the kingdom of heaven itself.

beginner.question: One of the Beatitudes says, 'Blessed are they that mourn: for they shall be ___.' What does Jesus promise to those who mourn?
beginner.explanation: Jesus promises they shall be 'comforted.' This Beatitude assures those who grieve — whether over sin, loss, or the brokenness of the world — that God himself will bring them comfort.

beginner.question: In the Beatitudes, Jesus says, 'Blessed are the meek: for they shall inherit the ___.' What will the meek inherit?
beginner.explanation: Jesus says the meek 'shall inherit the earth.' Meekness in the Bible is not weakness but gentle strength under control. Jesus promises that those with humble, gentle spirits will ultimately receive the earth as their inheritance.

beginner.question: Jesus said, 'Blessed are they which do hunger and thirst after ___: for they shall be filled.' What do the blessed hunger and thirst for?
beginner.explanation: Jesus says 'hunger and thirst after righteousness.' Those who deeply desire to live rightly before God — with the same intensity as physical hunger and thirst — are promised that God will satisfy that desire.

beginner.question: One of the Beatitudes declares, 'Blessed are the merciful: for they shall obtain ___.' What will the merciful receive?
beginner.explanation: Jesus promises the merciful will 'obtain mercy.' This Beatitude teaches a reciprocal principle: those who show compassion and forgiveness to others will receive mercy from God in return.

beginner.question: In the Beatitudes, Jesus says, 'Blessed are the peacemakers: for they shall be called the children of ___.' Whose children will they be called?
beginner.explanation: Jesus says peacemakers 'shall be called the children of God.' Those who actively work to bring peace and reconciliation reflect God's own character, and Jesus honours them with this title.

beginner.question: The Beatitudes are found at the beginning of Jesus's most famous sermon, delivered on a hillside. What is this sermon commonly called?
beginner.explanation: The Beatitudes open the Sermon on the Mount, found in Matthew chapters 5-7. Jesus went up on a mountainside and sat down to teach his disciples and the crowds these revolutionary blessings.

beginner.question: Jesus taught what is known as the Golden Rule: 'Therefore all things whatsoever ye would that men should do to you, do ye even so to ___.' Who should we treat this way?
beginner.explanation: Jesus says 'do ye even so to them,' meaning treat all other people the way you would want to be treated. The Golden Rule applies universally to everyone, not just friends or family.

beginner.question: The Golden Rule, 'Do unto others as you would have them do unto you,' is found in which Gospel?
beginner.explanation: The Golden Rule is found in Matthew 7:12 as part of the Sermon on the Mount. A similar version also appears in Luke 6:31. Jesus presents this principle as a summary of the Law and the Prophets.

beginner.question: After stating the Golden Rule in Matthew 7:12, Jesus says 'for this is the law and the ___.' What else does Jesus say the Golden Rule summarises besides the law?
beginner.explanation: Jesus says 'the law and the prophets.' By this, he means that the entire teaching of the Old Testament — both the Law of Moses and the writings of the Prophets — can be summed up in this one principle of treating others well.

beginner.question: The Golden Rule is part of which famous sermon that Jesus preached on a hillside?
beginner.explanation: The Golden Rule is part of the Sermon on the Mount (Matthew 5-7). This sermon contains many of Jesus's most well-known teachings, including the Beatitudes, the Lord's Prayer, and the Golden Rule.

beginner.question: The Golden Rule teaches us to treat others the way we want to be treated. In the Sermon on the Mount, Jesus presents this as a summary of what?
options: The entire Law and the Prophets
beginner.explanation: Jesus says the Golden Rule sums up 'the law and the prophets' — meaning the entire Old Testament moral teaching. This one simple principle captures the essence of how God wants people to treat one another.

beginner.question: The Golden Rule from Matthew 7:12 can be summarised as: 'Treat others the way you would want to be ___.' What word completes this summary?
beginner.explanation: The Golden Rule is about treating others the way you want to be treated. It shifts the focus from self-interest to empathy, asking us to consider how our actions affect others before we act.

beginner.question: The Ten Commandments were given by God to Moses on which famous mountain?
beginner.explanation: God gave the Ten Commandments to Moses on Mount Sinai. The Israelites had just been freed from slavery in Egypt, and God called Moses up the mountain to receive these foundational laws.

beginner.question: The first of the Ten Commandments says, 'Thou shalt have no other ___ before me.' What word fills in the blank?
beginner.explanation: The first commandment says 'no other gods before me.' God establishes exclusive loyalty as the foundation of the covenant relationship with Israel — he alone is to be worshipped.

beginner.question: The Ten Commandments include 'Thou shalt not ___,' which forbids taking the life of another person. What word fills the blank?
beginner.explanation: The commandment says 'Thou shalt not kill' (or 'murder'). This sixth commandment protects the sanctity of human life, reflecting the belief that every person is made in the image of God.

beginner.question: One of the Ten Commandments says, 'Remember the ___ day, to keep it holy.' Which day does this commandment refer to?
beginner.explanation: The commandment says 'Remember the sabbath day, to keep it holy.' God established the Sabbath as a day of rest, modelled after his own rest on the seventh day of creation.

beginner.question: The Ten Commandments include 'Honour thy ___ and thy ___.' Which two people does this commandment tell us to honour?
beginner.explanation: The commandment says 'Honour thy father and thy mother.' This is the fifth commandment and notably includes a promise — 'that thy days may be long upon the land which the Lord thy God giveth thee.'

beginner.question: The Ten Commandments are found in which book of the Bible?
beginner.explanation: The Ten Commandments are first recorded in Exodus chapter 20. They are also repeated in Deuteronomy 5. Exodus tells the story of Israel's liberation from Egypt and the giving of God's law at Mount Sinai.

beginner.question: According to the book of Exodus, how many commandments did God give to Moses on Mount Sinai on the stone tablets?
beginner.explanation: God gave Ten Commandments, which is why they are called 'the Ten Commandments' or the Decalogue. They were written by God's own finger on two stone tablets and given to Moses.

beginner.question: In the Gospel of John, Jesus makes a bold declaration: 'I am the way, the truth, and the ___: no man cometh unto the Father, but by me.' What is the third thing Jesus calls himself?
beginner.explanation: Jesus says 'I am the way, the truth, and the life.' In John 14:6, Jesus claims to be the exclusive path to God the Father, combining three powerful titles that describe his role in salvation.

beginner.question: John 14:6 contains Jesus's famous declaration, 'I am the way, the truth, and the life.' According to this verse, how can someone come to God the Father?
beginner.explanation: Jesus says 'no man cometh unto the Father, but by me.' This is one of the most exclusive claims in the Bible — Jesus declares himself the only way to reach God the Father.

beginner.question: When Jesus said 'I am the way, the truth, and the life' in John 14:6, he was speaking to his disciples during their last meal together. Which disciple had just asked Jesus a question that prompted this answer?
beginner.explanation: Thomas had said, 'Lord, we know not whither thou goest; and how can we know the way?' Jesus responded with this powerful declaration. This exchange took place during the Last Supper, shortly before Jesus's arrest.

beginner.question: The famous verse 'I am the way, the truth, and the life: no man cometh unto the Father, but by me' is found in which chapter of the Gospel of John?
beginner.explanation: This is John 14:6. Chapter 14 is part of Jesus's farewell discourse to his disciples at the Last Supper, where he comforts them about his coming departure and promises to prepare a place for them.

beginner.question: In John 14:6, Jesus says, 'I am the ___.' What is the first of the three things Jesus calls himself in this famous verse?
beginner.explanation: The first title is 'the way.' Jesus declares himself to be the path to God. The three titles together — way, truth, and life — cover how to reach God (the way), what to believe (the truth), and what to receive (the life).

beginner.question: Jesus's declaration 'I am the way, the truth, and the life' was spoken during which significant event in his final week?
beginner.explanation: Jesus spoke these words at the Last Supper, his final meal with his disciples before his arrest and crucifixion. He was preparing them for his departure and assuring them of their connection to the Father through him.

beginner.question: The apostle Paul wrote in Romans 3:23, 'For all have sinned, and come short of the ___ of God.' What have all people fallen short of?
beginner.explanation: Paul says all have come short of 'the glory of God.' This verse is a foundational statement about the universal nature of sin — every human being, without exception, has fallen short of God's perfect standard.

beginner.question: Romans 3:23 says, 'For ___ have sinned.' According to this verse, how many people have sinned?
beginner.explanation: Paul says 'all have sinned.' The word 'all' is emphatic — no one is excluded. This verse levels the playing field, declaring that every person, regardless of background, falls short of God's standard.

beginner.question: The verse 'For all have sinned, and come short of the glory of God' is found in which New Testament book?
beginner.explanation: This is Romans 3:23. Paul's letter to the Romans methodically explains the Gospel, and this verse is a key part of his argument that all of humanity needs God's grace because everyone has sinned.

beginner.question: Romans 3:23 teaches that all people have sinned. Who wrote this letter to the church in Rome?
beginner.explanation: The apostle Paul wrote the book of Romans. It is considered his masterpiece of theological writing, systematically explaining sin, salvation, and the Christian life. Romans 3:23 is central to his argument about universal sinfulness.

beginner.question: Romans 3:23 says people have 'come short' of God's glory. The phrase 'come short' means people have ___ to reach God's perfect standard.
beginner.explanation: To 'come short' means to fail to reach or measure up to a standard. Like an arrow falling short of its target, humanity has failed to meet God's perfect standard of righteousness, which is his glory.

beginner.question: Romans 3:23 is often paired with Romans 6:23 when explaining the Gospel message. Together these are sometimes called part of the 'Romans ___,' a method of explaining salvation using verses from Romans.
beginner.explanation: These verses are part of what is called the 'Romans Road' — a collection of verses from the book of Romans that together explain the Gospel: the problem of sin, the penalty for sin, and the gift of salvation.

beginner.question: Romans 6:23 says, 'For the wages of sin is death; but the gift of God is ___ life through Jesus Christ our Lord.' What kind of life is God's gift?
beginner.explanation: God's gift is 'eternal life.' This verse presents a stark contrast: sin earns death, but God freely gives eternal life. It is one of the clearest Gospel summaries in the Bible.

beginner.question: Romans 6:23 describes what sin earns using the metaphor of payment. It says, 'For the ___ of sin is death.' What word means 'payment earned'?
beginner.explanation: Paul uses the word 'wages,' meaning what is earned or deserved. Just as workers earn wages, sin earns death. But the second half of the verse offers hope — God's gift of eternal life is free, not earned.

beginner.question: Romans 6:23 contrasts two things: 'the wages of sin' and 'the gift of God.' The wages of sin is death, but the gift of God is eternal life through whom?
beginner.explanation: Eternal life comes 'through Jesus Christ our Lord.' Paul makes clear that salvation is not something we earn — it is a gift from God, made possible through the death and resurrection of Jesus Christ.

beginner.question: In Romans 6:23, Paul uses two contrasting words to describe how sin and salvation work. Sin earns 'wages,' but God offers a '___.' What word does Paul use for God's offer?
beginner.explanation: Paul calls it a 'gift.' The contrast is powerful: wages are earned, but a gift is free and undeserved. Salvation is not something humans can work for — it is God's free gift through Jesus Christ.

beginner.question: Romans 6:23 says, 'The wages of sin is ___.' According to this verse, what is the ultimate consequence of sin?
beginner.explanation: The wages of sin is 'death.' This refers to both physical death and spiritual separation from God. Paul uses this stark reality to highlight the incredible value of God's gift of eternal life.

beginner.question: Romans 6:23 is found in which New Testament book, written by Paul as a letter to believers in a major city of the Roman Empire?
beginner.explanation: This is from Paul's letter to the Romans. Written to the church in Rome, it is Paul's most systematic presentation of the Gospel, and Romans 6:23 is one of its most memorised and quoted verses.

beginner.question: Psalm 119:105 says, 'Thy word is a ___ unto my feet, and a light unto my path.' What object is God's word compared to?
beginner.explanation: God's word is compared to a 'lamp.' Just as a lamp illuminates the ground ahead so a traveller can see where to step, God's word guides believers and helps them make wise decisions in life.

beginner.question: Psalm 119:105 uses two images to describe God's word: a lamp unto the feet and a ___ unto the path. What is the second image?
beginner.explanation: The second image is a 'light unto my path.' Together, the lamp and light show that God's word provides both immediate guidance (where to step next) and broader direction (the overall path of life).

beginner.question: The verse 'Thy word is a lamp unto my feet, and a light unto my path' comes from Psalm 119. What is notable about Psalm 119 compared to other chapters of the Bible?
beginner.explanation: Psalm 119 is the longest chapter in the entire Bible, with 176 verses. Almost every verse in this psalm mentions God's word, law, statutes, or commandments, making it a grand celebration of Scripture.

beginner.question: Psalm 119:105 says God's word is a lamp to our feet. In this metaphor, what does 'thy word' refer to?
options: The words of the prophets only
beginner.explanation: In this context, 'thy word' refers to God's Scripture and teachings as a whole. The psalmist celebrates all of God's revealed truth as a guide for life, not just one particular portion.

beginner.question: The metaphor in Psalm 119:105 — 'a lamp unto my feet' — suggests that God's word helps us in what way?
options: It shows us the next step to take
beginner.explanation: A lamp at one's feet lights up the immediate ground ahead, showing the next safe step. The metaphor means God's word provides practical guidance for the decisions and steps we face every day.

beginner.question: In which book of the Bible would you find the verse, 'Thy word is a lamp unto my feet, and a light unto my path'?
beginner.explanation: This is Psalm 119:105, found in the book of Psalms. The Psalms are a collection of songs and prayers, and Psalm 119 is the longest, devoted entirely to praising and treasuring God's word.

beginner.question: Ephesians 2:8-9 says, 'For by ___ are ye saved through faith; and that not of yourselves: it is the gift of God.' What are we saved by according to this verse?
beginner.explanation: We are saved by 'grace.' Ephesians 2:8-9 is one of the clearest statements that salvation is a free gift of God's grace received through faith, not something we earn through our own efforts.

beginner.question: Ephesians 2:8 says we are saved by grace through ___. What is the means by which we receive God's grace?
beginner.explanation: Salvation comes 'through faith.' Grace is God's undeserved favour, and faith is the way we receive it. Paul emphasises that even this faith is a gift — 'not of yourselves: it is the gift of God.'

beginner.question: Ephesians 2:9 says salvation is 'not of works, lest any man should ___.' What does Paul say would happen if salvation were based on works?
beginner.explanation: Paul says 'lest any man should boast.' If people could earn salvation through their actions, they might take credit for it. By making salvation a free gift, God removes all basis for human pride or boasting.

beginner.question: The famous passage about being 'saved by grace through faith, not by works' is found in which New Testament letter?
beginner.explanation: This is Ephesians 2:8-9. Paul wrote this letter to the church in Ephesus, and these verses are among the most important statements about salvation in the entire New Testament.

beginner.question: Ephesians 2:8 says salvation is 'not of yourselves.' According to Paul, whose gift is salvation?
beginner.explanation: Paul says 'it is the gift of God.' Salvation is entirely God's initiative and generosity. Humans cannot earn it, deserve it, or produce it on their own — it comes solely from God's grace.

beginner.question: Who wrote the letter to the Ephesians that contains the famous passage, 'For by grace are ye saved through faith; and that not of yourselves: it is the gift of God: not of works, lest any man should boast'?
beginner.explanation: The apostle Paul wrote the letter to the Ephesians. He likely wrote it while imprisoned in Rome, and it contains some of the New Testament's most profound teachings about grace, salvation, and the unity of believers.

beginner.question: After Moses died, God spoke to his successor and said, 'Be strong and of a good courage.' Who was God speaking to in Joshua 1:9?
beginner.explanation: God was speaking to Joshua, who was about to lead the Israelites into the Promised Land after Moses's death. God encouraged Joshua not to be afraid because the Lord would be with him wherever he went.

beginner.question: Joshua 1:9 says, 'Have not I commanded thee? Be strong and of a good courage; be not afraid, neither be thou ___.' What word completes God's instruction?
beginner.explanation: God says 'neither be thou dismayed.' God gave Joshua a double command — not to be afraid and not to be dismayed (discouraged). Both courage and confidence were needed for the enormous task of conquering the Promised Land.

beginner.question: In Joshua 1:9, God gives a reason for Joshua to be brave: 'for the Lord thy God is with thee ___.' How far does God's presence extend according to this verse?
beginner.explanation: God promises to be with Joshua 'whithersoever thou goest' — wherever he goes. This unlimited promise of God's presence is what makes courage possible, no matter the circumstance or location.

beginner.question: The encouraging verse 'Be strong and of a good courage' in Joshua 1:9 was spoken at what critical moment in Israel's history?
beginner.explanation: This was spoken just before the Israelites entered the Promised Land under Joshua's leadership. Moses had died, and Joshua needed courage and reassurance from God to lead the people into this new chapter.

beginner.question: The verse 'Be strong and of a good courage; be not afraid, neither be thou dismayed: for the Lord thy God is with thee whithersoever thou goest' is found in which Old Testament book?
beginner.explanation: This is Joshua 1:9. The book of Joshua records how Joshua led the Israelites into the Promised Land after Moses's death. God's encouragement in the opening chapter sets the tone for the entire book.

beginner.question: Joshua 1:9 begins with God saying, 'Have not I ___ thee?' This opening reminds Joshua that the command to be courageous comes from the highest authority. What word fills the blank?
beginner.explanation: God says 'Have not I commanded thee?' This is not a suggestion — it is a command from God himself. The phrasing reminds Joshua that courage is not optional; it is a duty because God has spoken.

beginner.question: Galatians 5:22-23 lists the 'fruit of the Spirit.' The first fruit mentioned is ___. What is it?
beginner.explanation: The first fruit of the Spirit listed is 'love.' Paul lists nine qualities produced by the Holy Spirit in a believer's life, and love leads the list, reflecting its supreme importance in Christian life.

beginner.question: The 'fruit of the Spirit' passage in Galatians 5:22-23 lists nine qualities. The first three are love, ___, and peace. What is the second fruit?
beginner.explanation: The second fruit is 'joy.' The full list begins: love, joy, peace, longsuffering (patience), gentleness (kindness), goodness, faith (faithfulness), meekness (gentleness), temperance (self-control).

beginner.question: The famous list of the fruit of the Spirit — love, joy, peace, patience, kindness, goodness, faithfulness, gentleness, and self-control — is found in which New Testament letter?
beginner.explanation: The fruit of the Spirit is listed in Galatians 5:22-23. Paul wrote this letter to the churches in Galatia to contrast living by the Spirit versus living by the flesh.

beginner.question: How many qualities are listed as 'the fruit of the Spirit' in Galatians 5:22-23?
beginner.explanation: There are nine qualities: love, joy, peace, patience, kindness, goodness, faithfulness, gentleness, and self-control. Note that Paul uses the singular 'fruit,' not 'fruits,' suggesting these qualities form one unified character.

beginner.question: In Galatians 5:22-23, Paul uses the word 'fruit' (singular) rather than 'fruits' (plural). According to Paul, who produces this fruit in a believer's life?
beginner.explanation: It is called 'the fruit of the Spirit' because the Holy Spirit produces these qualities in the lives of believers. They are not achieved through human effort alone but are the natural result of the Spirit's work.

beginner.question: The last quality listed in the fruit of the Spirit (Galatians 5:22-23) is 'temperance,' which is also translated as what?
beginner.explanation: Temperance is most commonly translated as 'self-control' in modern Bible translations. It rounds out the list of nine spiritual qualities, showing that the Spirit empowers believers to govern their own desires and impulses.

beginner.question: Who wrote the letter to the Galatians that contains the famous list of the fruit of the Spirit?
beginner.explanation: The apostle Paul wrote Galatians. He wrote it to churches in the region of Galatia (modern-day Turkey) to address false teaching and to explain the freedom believers have in Christ and the Spirit-led life.

beginner.question: In the Sermon on the Mount, Jesus tells his followers, 'Ye are the ___ of the world.' What does Jesus call his followers in Matthew 5:14?
beginner.explanation: Jesus says 'Ye are the light of the world.' He uses this powerful metaphor to tell his followers that they have a responsibility to shine brightly and illuminate the world with goodness and truth.

beginner.question: In Matthew 5:14, after calling his followers 'the light of the world,' Jesus adds, 'A city that is set on a ___ cannot be hid.' What geographical feature does Jesus mention?
beginner.explanation: Jesus says 'a city that is set on a hill cannot be hid.' A hilltop city is visible from far away, especially at night. Jesus uses this image to show that his followers should be visibly different and impossible to overlook.

beginner.question: In Matthew 5:15, Jesus continues the light metaphor by asking, 'Neither do men light a candle, and put it under a ___.' What does Jesus say people should NOT hide a candle under?
beginner.explanation: Jesus says people don't put a candle 'under a bushel' (a measuring basket). Instead, they put it on a candlestick so it gives light to everyone. Likewise, believers should not hide their faith but let it shine for all to see.

beginner.question: Jesus says in Matthew 5:16, 'Let your light so shine before men, that they may see your good works, and glorify your ___ which is in heaven.' Who should ultimately receive glory?
beginner.explanation: Jesus says people should glorify 'your Father which is in heaven.' The purpose of letting your light shine is not to draw attention to yourself, but so that others see your good works and praise God.

beginner.question: Just before calling his followers 'the light of the world' in Matthew 5:14, Jesus also calls them 'the ___ of the earth' in verse 13. What other metaphor does Jesus use?
beginner.explanation: Jesus calls them 'the salt of the earth' in Matthew 5:13. Salt preserves and flavours food, just as believers are meant to preserve goodness and add flavour to the world. Together with light, these two metaphors define the Christian's role.

beginner.question: The verse 'Ye are the light of the world' (Matthew 5:14) is part of which famous sermon by Jesus?
beginner.explanation: This verse is from the Sermon on the Mount (Matthew 5-7). It comes near the beginning of the sermon, right after the Beatitudes, as Jesus describes the character and mission of his followers.

beginner.question: The Gospel of John opens with a famous prologue: 'In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was ___.' What does this verse say the Word was?
beginner.explanation: John 1:1 says 'the Word was God.' This verse declares that Jesus (the Word) existed from the very beginning, was in relationship with God, and was himself divine. It is one of the strongest statements of Jesus's deity.

beginner.question: John 1:1 says, 'In the beginning was the ___.' What title is given to Jesus in the opening of John's Gospel?
beginner.explanation: Jesus is called 'the Word' (Greek: Logos). John uses this title to express that Jesus is God's ultimate communication to humanity — the living expression of God himself, present from the very beginning.

beginner.question: The opening words of John's Gospel — 'In the beginning' — deliberately echo the opening words of which other book of the Bible?
beginner.explanation: John's opening echoes Genesis 1:1, which also begins 'In the beginning.' By using the same phrase, John connects Jesus to the creation account, showing that the Word existed before all created things.

beginner.question: John 1:1 says 'the Word was with God.' This phrase shows that the Word (Jesus) had what kind of relationship with God the Father before the world began?
beginner.explanation: The phrase 'with God' indicates a personal, intimate, face-to-face relationship. The Word was not separate from God or far away — he was in close communion with the Father from all eternity.

beginner.question: John 1:3 continues from John 1:1, saying, 'All things were made by him; and without him was not any thing made that was made.' What role does this say the Word (Jesus) played in creation?
options: He rested on the seventh day
beginner.explanation: John 1:3 says 'all things were made by him,' declaring that the Word was the agent of all creation. Nothing that exists was made without him, which further emphasises his divine nature and eternal existence.

beginner.question: The famous opening 'In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God' is found in which Gospel?
beginner.explanation: This is John 1:1, the opening verse of the Gospel of John. Unlike the other Gospels, which begin with genealogies or narratives, John starts with a theological prologue about the eternal nature of the Word.

beginner.question: John 1:14 says, 'And the Word was made flesh, and dwelt among us.' According to John's Gospel, what does it mean that 'the Word was made flesh'?
options: God wrote his word on scrolls
The law was given to Moses
beginner.explanation: The Word becoming flesh means Jesus — the eternal, divine Word — took on human nature and became a real person. This is the doctrine of the Incarnation, the central miracle of the Christian faith.

beginner.question: Who is traditionally believed to be the author of the Gospel of John, which opens with the famous words 'In the beginning was the Word'?
beginner.explanation: The Gospel of John is traditionally attributed to John the apostle, one of Jesus's twelve disciples and one of his closest followers (part of the inner circle with Peter and James). He is often called 'the beloved disciple.'

beginner.question: One of the most famous verses in the Bible says, 'For God so loved the world, that he gave his only begotten Son, that whosoever believeth in him should not perish, but have everlasting life.' This verse is John 3:16.
beginner.explanation: This is correct. John 3:16 is perhaps the most recognised verse in the entire Bible. Jesus spoke these words to Nicodemus during a private nighttime conversation about being born again.

beginner.question: John 3:16 says, 'For God so loved the world, that he gave his only begotten Son.' According to this verse, God gave his Son because he was angry with the world.
beginner.explanation: This is false. The verse says God gave his Son because he 'so loved the world,' not because he was angry. Love, not anger, is the motivation described in John 3:16.

beginner.question: In the Gospel of John, chapter 3, Jesus was speaking to Nicodemus — a Pharisee who came to him at night — when he said the famous words of John 3:16.
beginner.explanation: This is correct. Nicodemus was a Pharisee and member of the Jewish ruling council who came to Jesus at night, and their conversation included the famous words of John 3:16.

beginner.question: John 3:16 promises that whoever believes in God's Son will receive great wealth and earthly power.
beginner.explanation: This is false. John 3:16 promises 'everlasting life,' not wealth or earthly power. The verse says believers 'should not perish, but have everlasting life' — the promise is spiritual and eternal, not material.

beginner.question: John 3:16 says that 'whosoever believeth' in God's Son shall have everlasting life, meaning this promise is available to anyone who believes, not just a select few.
beginner.explanation: This is correct. The word 'whosoever' makes the promise universal — it is open to anyone and everyone who believes, regardless of background, nationality, or status.

beginner.question: John 3:16 is found in the Old Testament, in the book of the prophet John.
beginner.explanation: This is false. John 3:16 is in the New Testament, in the Gospel of John. There is no Old Testament book called 'John.' The Gospel of John is one of the four Gospels that tell the story of Jesus's life.

beginner.question: The verse John 3:16 contains both a warning ('should not perish') and a promise ('have everlasting life'), showing the two possible outcomes for humanity.
beginner.explanation: This is correct. John 3:16 presents two contrasting outcomes: perishing (for those who do not believe) and everlasting life (for those who do). This duality is central to the verse's message.

beginner.question: Psalm 23, one of the most beloved psalms, begins with the words 'The Lord is my shepherd; I shall not want.' This psalm is attributed to King David.
beginner.explanation: This is correct. Psalm 23 is attributed to King David, who was himself a shepherd before becoming king of Israel. His personal experience caring for sheep deeply influenced this psalm's imagery.

beginner.question: Psalm 23 says, 'The Lord is my shepherd; I shall not want,' meaning the psalmist has no desires or wishes at all.
beginner.explanation: This is false. 'I shall not want' means 'I shall lack nothing' or 'I will not be in need.' It does not mean having zero desires — it means God provides everything necessary, like a shepherd provides for his flock.

beginner.question: In Psalm 23, the psalmist says, 'He maketh me to lie down in green pastures: he leadeth me beside the still waters.' These images describe God's peaceful provision.
beginner.explanation: This is correct. Green pastures and still (calm) waters are images of abundance, safety, and peace. A good shepherd leads his flock to nourishing pastures and safe, calm water for drinking.

beginner.question: Psalm 23:4 says, 'Yea, though I walk through the valley of the shadow of death, I will fear no evil.' The psalmist says he will fear no evil because he is carrying a weapon.
beginner.explanation: This is false. The psalmist fears no evil not because of a weapon, but because God is with him: 'for thou art with me; thy rod and thy staff they comfort me.' It is God's presence, not a weapon, that removes fear.

beginner.question: Psalm 23 mentions that God prepares a table before the psalmist 'in the presence of mine enemies,' showing God's provision even in difficult circumstances.
beginner.explanation: This is correct. Psalm 23:5 says God prepares a table in the presence of enemies. This vivid image shows that God provides abundantly and cares for his people even when surrounded by threats.

beginner.question: Psalm 23 ends with the confident declaration that 'goodness and mercy shall follow me all the days of my life: and I will dwell in the house of the Lord for ever.'
beginner.explanation: This is correct. Psalm 23:6 is the closing verse, expressing confidence that God's goodness and mercy are constant companions throughout life, and that the psalmist will enjoy God's presence eternally.

beginner.question: Psalm 23 was written by King Solomon, who was famous for being a shepherd before he became king.
beginner.explanation: This is false. Psalm 23 is attributed to King David, not Solomon. It was David who was a shepherd in his youth, tending his father Jesse's flocks in Bethlehem before becoming king of Israel.

beginner.question: The very first verse of the Bible, Genesis 1:1, says, 'In the beginning God created the heaven and the earth.'
beginner.explanation: This is correct. Genesis 1:1 is the opening line of the entire Bible. It establishes God as the Creator of everything and marks the starting point of the biblical narrative.

beginner.question: According to Genesis 1:1, the first thing God created was light, before he created the heaven and the earth.
beginner.explanation: This is false. Genesis 1:1 says God first created 'the heaven and the earth.' Light was created afterward, when God said 'Let there be light' in Genesis 1:3, on the first day.

beginner.question: The word 'Genesis' means 'beginning' or 'origin,' which is fitting because the book starts with the creation of the world.
beginner.explanation: This is correct. 'Genesis' comes from the Greek word for 'origin' or 'beginning.' The book records the origins of the universe, humanity, sin, nations, and God's chosen people Israel.

beginner.question: According to the creation account in Genesis 1, God created everything in three days and rested on the fourth.
beginner.explanation: This is false. God created everything in six days and rested on the seventh day (Genesis 2:2). The six days of creation followed by a day of rest established the pattern for the weekly Sabbath.

beginner.question: Genesis 1:1 says 'In the beginning God created the heaven and the earth,' establishing that both the spiritual realm (heaven) and the physical realm (earth) were made by God.
beginner.explanation: This is correct. The verse declares God as the maker of both heaven and earth, encompassing all of creation — the seen and unseen, the spiritual and the physical.

beginner.question: Genesis 1:1 is found in the New Testament, as the opening verse of the Gospel of Matthew.
beginner.explanation: This is false. Genesis 1:1 is the opening verse of the Old Testament and the very first verse of the entire Bible. The Gospel of Matthew is the first book of the New Testament, not Genesis.

beginner.question: The prophet Jeremiah wrote, 'For I know the plans I have for you, declares the Lord, plans to prosper you and not to harm you, plans to give you hope and a future.' This is Jeremiah 29:11.
beginner.explanation: This is correct. Jeremiah 29:11 is one of the most beloved promises in the Old Testament. God spoke these words through Jeremiah to the Israelites during their exile in Babylon.

beginner.question: Jeremiah 29:11 says God's plans are to 'prosper you and not to harm you.' This verse was originally spoken to the Israelites while they were enslaved in Egypt.
beginner.explanation: This is false. Jeremiah 29:11 was spoken to the Israelites during their exile in Babylon, not during their slavery in Egypt. The Egyptian slavery occurred centuries earlier and is recorded in the book of Exodus.

beginner.question: Jeremiah 29:11 promises both 'hope' and 'a future,' assuring believers that God has positive intentions for their lives.
beginner.explanation: This is correct. The verse explicitly mentions both hope and a future. God was assuring the exiled Israelites that despite their current suffering, he had not abandoned them and better days were ahead.

beginner.question: Jeremiah 29:11 was written by the prophet Isaiah, one of the major prophets of the Old Testament.
beginner.explanation: This is false. Jeremiah 29:11 was written by the prophet Jeremiah, not Isaiah. Although both are major Old Testament prophets, their books are separate and they ministered during different periods.

beginner.question: In Jeremiah 29:11, God says his plans are 'to prosper you and not to harm you,' indicating that God's intentions for his people are good, not destructive.
beginner.explanation: This is correct. The verse explicitly contrasts prospering with harming, making it clear that God's plans are beneficial and protective, not harmful. This was a message of comfort to people in a difficult situation.

beginner.question: Jeremiah 29:11 is found in the New Testament, in one of Paul's letters to the early churches.
beginner.explanation: This is false. Jeremiah 29:11 is in the Old Testament, in the book of the prophet Jeremiah. Paul's letters are in the New Testament. Jeremiah prophesied hundreds of years before Paul was born.

beginner.question: Jeremiah 29:11 is part of a letter that the prophet Jeremiah sent to the Jewish exiles living in Babylon, encouraging them to settle in and trust God's plan.
beginner.explanation: This is correct. Jeremiah 29 is a letter sent to the exiles in Babylon. Jeremiah told them to build houses, plant gardens, and seek the welfare of the city, because God had plans to eventually restore them.

beginner.question: Philippians 4:13 says, 'I can do all things through Christ which strengtheneth me.' The apostle Paul wrote this verse while he was in prison.
beginner.explanation: This is correct. Paul wrote the letter to the Philippians while imprisoned, likely in Rome. Despite his difficult circumstances, he expressed confidence that Christ's strength enabled him to face any situation.

beginner.question: Philippians 4:13 says, 'I can do all things through Christ which strengtheneth me.' This verse was written by the apostle Peter during his missionary journeys.
