beginner.question: The New Testament ends with a book of prophecy about the end times. What is the last section or category of the New Testament called, represented by just one book — Revelation?
beginner.explanation: The book of Revelation is classified as prophecy or apocalyptic literature. It is the only primarily prophetic book in the New Testament and describes visions of the end of the age and God's final victory.

beginner.question: The Bible was written by approximately how many different authors over the course of roughly 1,500 years? These authors included kings, shepherds, fishermen, doctors, and prophets.
beginner.explanation: The Bible was written by approximately 40 different authors over about 1,500 years. Despite this diversity, Christians believe the Bible tells one unified story of God's plan for humanity.

beginner.question: The Old Testament was written over a period of roughly 1,000 years, and the New Testament was written in about 50 years. Approximately how long did it take for the entire Bible to be written from start to finish?
beginner.explanation: The Bible was written over approximately 1,500 years, from around 1400 BC (Moses writing the Torah) to about 95 AD (John writing Revelation). This makes it one of the most remarkable collections of literature in history.

beginner.question: The word 'Hosanna' is shouted by the crowds when Jesus rides into Jerusalem on a donkey in what is known as the Triumphal Entry. This Hebrew word is an exclamation of praise. What does 'Hosanna' literally mean?
beginner.explanation: Hosanna comes from the Hebrew 'hoshia na,' meaning 'save us, we pray' or 'save now.' Over time it became an exclamation of praise. The crowds shouted it as Jesus entered Jerusalem before his crucifixion.

beginner.question: The word 'Selah' appears 71 times in the book of Psalms. While scholars are not entirely certain of its meaning, it most likely served as a musical or liturgical instruction. What do most scholars believe 'Selah' indicates?
beginner.explanation: Most scholars believe 'Selah' indicates a pause for reflection or a musical interlude. Since the Psalms were originally sung in worship, Selah likely told the musicians or singers to pause briefly.

beginner.question: The first English translation of the entire Bible was produced in the 1380s, long before the printing press was invented. These hand-copied Bibles were associated with which English reformer and Oxford professor?
beginner.explanation: John Wycliffe supervised the first complete English translation of the Bible in the 1380s. Since the printing press hadn't been invented yet, every copy had to be written out by hand, making them rare and expensive.

beginner.question: In the original Hebrew Bible, the very first word of Genesis (and therefore the first word of the entire Bible) is 'Bereshit.' What does this Hebrew word mean?
beginner.explanation: Bereshit means 'In the beginning.' The very first verse of the Bible reads: 'In the beginning, God created the heavens and the earth' (Genesis 1:1). In Hebrew tradition, the book of Genesis is actually named 'Bereshit.'

beginner.question: The last word of the Bible, found at the very end of the book of Revelation, is a word commonly spoken in churches and at the end of prayers. What is the last word of the Bible?
beginner.explanation: The last word of the Bible is 'Amen.' Revelation 22:21 reads: 'The grace of the Lord Jesus be with God's people. Amen.' It is a fitting conclusion — the word 'Amen' means 'so be it' or 'truly.'

beginner.question: In the New Testament, the book that comes right after the four Gospels describes the coming of the Holy Spirit and the growth of the early church. What is this fifth book of the New Testament called?
beginner.explanation: The book of Acts (also called the Acts of the Apostles) is the fifth book of the New Testament. It describes how the early church was founded and spread throughout the Roman Empire after Jesus' ascension.

beginner.question: Paul wrote letters to churches in many cities across the Roman Empire. One of his most famous and theologically rich letters was written to believers in the capital city of the Empire. Which letter is this?
beginner.explanation: Romans is Paul's letter to the church in Rome, the capital of the Roman Empire. It is one of the longest and most theologically important of Paul's letters, laying out the foundations of Christian doctrine.

beginner.question: The Old Testament prophets are often divided into two groups based on the length of their books. The longer prophetic books (Isaiah, Jeremiah, Ezekiel, Daniel) are called what?
beginner.explanation: The longer prophetic books — Isaiah, Jeremiah, Lamentations, Ezekiel, and Daniel — are called the Major Prophets. 'Major' refers to the length of the books, not the importance of the prophets.

beginner.question: The last twelve books of the Old Testament — from Hosea to Malachi — are shorter prophetic books. Because of their shorter length, these prophets are collectively known as what?
beginner.explanation: The twelve shorter prophetic books from Hosea to Malachi are called the Minor Prophets. 'Minor' refers only to the shorter length of their books, not to the significance of their messages.

beginner.question: The book of Psalms is found roughly in the middle of the Bible. If you open most printed Bibles to the middle, you will likely land in or near which book?
beginner.explanation: Psalms sits roughly in the centre of the Bible. With 150 chapters, it is the longest book, and its central position makes it easy to find by simply opening a Bible to the middle.

beginner.question: The Bible contains many different genres or types of writing. Which of the following is NOT a genre found in the Bible?
beginner.explanation: Science fiction is not a genre found in the Bible. The Bible contains many genres including history, law, poetry, wisdom literature, prophecy, letters (epistles), gospels, and apocalyptic literature.

beginner.question: The Gutenberg Bible, the first major book printed with movable type in Europe, was printed in which language?
beginner.explanation: The Gutenberg Bible was printed in Latin, which was the language of scholarship and the Catholic Church in medieval Europe. It would be decades before the Bible was widely printed in everyday European languages.

beginner.question: The King James Version of the Bible, published in 1611, was commissioned by King James I. He was king of which country?
beginner.explanation: King James I was king of England and Scotland. He authorised a new English translation of the Bible in 1604 at the Hampton Court Conference, and it was completed in 1611 by a team of 47 scholars.

beginner.question: The New Testament letter to Philemon is one of the shortest books in the Bible. Paul wrote it to a man named Philemon about his runaway slave, Onesimus, who had become a Christian. How many chapters does Philemon have?
beginner.explanation: Philemon has only 1 chapter with 25 verses. It is one of the shortest books in the Bible. In this personal letter, Paul asks Philemon to welcome back his slave Onesimus as a brother in Christ.

beginner.question: The book of Psalms is actually divided into five smaller collections or 'books' within itself. How many of these internal divisions does the book of Psalms have?
beginner.explanation: The book of Psalms is divided into five books: Book I (Psalms 1-41), Book II (42-72), Book III (73-89), Book IV (90-106), and Book V (107-150). This fivefold division mirrors the five books of the Torah.

beginner.question: Which book of the Bible has the most chapters? This Old Testament book is a collection of songs and prayers, and its 150 chapters make it the longest book by chapter count.
beginner.explanation: Psalms has the most chapters in the Bible with 150. Each chapter is an individual psalm (song or prayer). The next longest by chapter count is Isaiah with 66 chapters.

beginner.question: Psalm 119 is the longest chapter in the entire Bible. It is an acrostic poem about the importance of God's word. Approximately how many verses does Psalm 119 have?
beginner.explanation: Psalm 119 has 176 verses, making it the longest chapter in the Bible. It is an acrostic poem with 22 sections, one for each letter of the Hebrew alphabet, with 8 verses per section (22 × 8 = 176).

beginner.question: The Septuagint is an ancient translation of the Old Testament that was created around the 3rd century BC. It translated the Hebrew Old Testament into which language?
beginner.explanation: The Septuagint is a Greek translation of the Hebrew Old Testament, created around 250-100 BC in Alexandria, Egypt. It was widely used by Greek-speaking Jews and early Christians.

beginner.question: The Latin Vulgate was a famous translation of the Bible completed by Saint Jerome around 400 AD. For over a thousand years, it was the standard Bible of the Western church. What language is the Vulgate written in?
beginner.explanation: The Vulgate is a Latin translation of the Bible. Jerome translated it from the original Hebrew and Greek texts. 'Vulgate' comes from 'vulgata,' meaning 'common,' as Latin was the common language of the Western Roman Empire.

beginner.question: Many of Paul's New Testament letters are named after the city or group they were sent to. His letter to the Corinthians was written to the church in which ancient Greek city?
beginner.explanation: Paul's letters to the Corinthians were written to the church in Corinth, a major city in ancient Greece. Paul founded this church during his second missionary journey (Acts 18) and later wrote to address various issues.

beginner.question: The last book of the Old Testament — and therefore the last book before the New Testament begins — is a short prophetic book. What is the name of this final Old Testament book?
beginner.explanation: Malachi is the last book of the Old Testament. After Malachi, there was a period of roughly 400 years (sometimes called the 'intertestamental period' or 'silent years') before the events of the New Testament.

beginner.question: The first book of the New Testament — and therefore the book that comes right after the Old Testament — shares its name with one of Jesus' twelve apostles who was a former tax collector. What is this book?
beginner.explanation: Matthew is the first book of the New Testament. The apostle Matthew, also called Levi, was a tax collector before Jesus called him to be a disciple. His Gospel was written primarily for a Jewish audience.

beginner.question: The Pentateuch is the term for the first five books of the Bible. The word 'Pentateuch' comes from Greek and literally refers to how many scrolls or volumes?
beginner.explanation: Pentateuch comes from the Greek 'pente' (five) and 'teuchos' (scroll/volume), meaning 'five scrolls.' It refers to Genesis, Exodus, Leviticus, Numbers, and Deuteronomy — the first five books of the Bible.

beginner.question: In Hebrew, the first five books of the Bible are called the 'Torah.' What does the Hebrew word 'Torah' mean?
beginner.explanation: Torah means 'law' or 'instruction' in Hebrew. The Torah contains God's laws and instructions for the Israelite people, including the Ten Commandments and detailed rules for worship and daily life.

beginner.question: When Jesus was asked which commandment is the greatest, he answered with a passage from the Old Testament book of Deuteronomy, known as the 'Shema.' The Shema begins with 'Hear, O Israel: The Lord our God, the Lord is ______.'
beginner.explanation: The Shema (Deuteronomy 6:4) declares 'Hear, O Israel: The Lord our God, the Lord is one.' This is the foundational statement of monotheism in Judaism. Jesus quoted it as the beginning of the greatest commandment.

beginner.question: The book of Genesis covers an enormous span of history. The first 11 chapters cover the period from creation to Abraham. Approximately what fraction of all biblical history do these opening chapters cover?
beginner.explanation: The first 11 chapters of Genesis cover over 2,000 years of history (from creation to Abraham), which represents more than half of the entire biblical timeline. The remaining 1,178 chapters of the Bible cover a shorter period.

beginner.question: How many books in the New Testament are attributed to the apostle John? He is credited with writing a Gospel, three short letters, and one prophetic book.
beginner.explanation: Five books are attributed to John: the Gospel of John, 1 John, 2 John, 3 John, and Revelation. This makes him the second most prolific New Testament author after Paul.

beginner.question: The apostle Peter, one of Jesus' closest disciples, wrote how many letters (epistles) that are included in the New Testament?
beginner.explanation: Peter wrote two letters in the New Testament: 1 Peter and 2 Peter. In his first letter, he encouraged persecuted Christians. In his second letter, he warned against false teachers.

beginner.question: The Bible is the best-selling and most widely distributed book in human history. Approximately how many languages has the full Bible been translated into as of the 21st century?
beginner.explanation: The full Bible has been translated into over 700 languages, and portions of the Bible exist in over 3,500 languages. It is the most translated book in history, reaching people in nearly every corner of the world.

beginner.question: The word 'Scripture' is often used as another name for the Bible. This word comes from the Latin 'scriptura.' What does 'scriptura' mean?
beginner.explanation: The word 'Scripture' comes from the Latin 'scriptura,' meaning 'writing' or 'a written document.' When Christians refer to 'the Scriptures,' they mean the sacred writings of the Bible.

beginner.question: The verse 2 Timothy 3:16 is often quoted about the nature of the Bible. It says, 'All Scripture is God-breathed and is useful for teaching, rebuking, correcting and training in righteousness.' What does 'God-breathed' mean in this context?
beginner.explanation: God-breathed (Greek: 'theopneustos') means that God inspired the human authors of the Bible. Christians believe God guided the writers through the Holy Spirit while still allowing their individual personalities and styles to come through.

beginner.question: The Old Testament book of Ruth tells the story of a woman from Moab who followed her Israelite mother-in-law Naomi back to Israel. Ruth eventually married Boaz and became the great-grandmother of which famous king?
beginner.explanation: Ruth became the great-grandmother of King David. The book of Ruth ends with a genealogy showing Ruth as the mother of Obed, who was the father of Jesse, who was the father of David.

beginner.question: In the New Testament, Paul wrote two letters to a young pastor he had mentored. These letters, along with the letter to Titus, are called the 'Pastoral Epistles' because they give advice on leading a church. Who was this young pastor?
beginner.explanation: Timothy was the young pastor Paul mentored. Paul wrote 1 Timothy and 2 Timothy to give him guidance on leading the church. Along with Titus, these three letters are called the Pastoral Epistles.

beginner.question: The Bible's longest book by word count is actually in the Old Testament and tells the story of the prophet who was called 'the weeping prophet' because he grieved over Israel's sins. Which book is the longest in the Bible by word count?
beginner.explanation: Jeremiah is the longest book of the Bible by word count. While Psalms has the most chapters (150), Jeremiah has more total words. Jeremiah is often called 'the weeping prophet' because of his deep sorrow over Israel's disobedience.

beginner.question: The very middle chapter of the Bible is Psalm 117. It is also notable for another reason — it holds the record for being the what?
beginner.explanation: Psalm 117 is the shortest chapter in the Bible, with only 2 verses. Despite its brevity, it contains a powerful message calling all nations to praise the Lord for his love and faithfulness.

beginner.question: The books of 1 and 2 Chronicles in the Old Testament largely retell the history found in the books of Samuel and Kings, but from a different perspective. Chronicles focuses especially on which kingdom?
beginner.explanation: The books of Chronicles focus primarily on the Southern Kingdom of Judah, particularly its kings and the temple worship in Jerusalem. They largely omit the Northern Kingdom of Israel.

beginner.question: Proverbs 1:7 states a foundational principle of the wisdom literature: 'The fear of the Lord is the beginning of ______.' What word completes this famous verse?
beginner.explanation: Proverbs 1:7 says 'The fear of the Lord is the beginning of knowledge.' A similar verse in Proverbs 9:10 says 'The fear of the Lord is the beginning of wisdom.' Both emphasise that reverence for God is the foundation of all true understanding.

beginner.question: The Hebrew word 'Elohim,' used over 2,500 times in the Old Testament, is one of the most common names for God. In Genesis 1:1, 'In the beginning, Elohim created the heavens and the earth,' what does 'Elohim' mean?
beginner.explanation: Elohim is a Hebrew word meaning 'God.' It is the very first name used for God in the Bible, appearing in Genesis 1:1. Interestingly, Elohim is grammatically plural, which some Christians see as a hint of the Trinity.

beginner.question: The book of Acts records that the early church began in which city, where the Holy Spirit descended on the disciples during the festival of Pentecost?
beginner.explanation: The church began in Jerusalem. On the day of Pentecost, the Holy Spirit came upon the disciples gathered in Jerusalem, and Peter preached a sermon that led about 3,000 people to believe in Jesus (Acts 2).

beginner.question: The Bible contains a famous verse that is sometimes called 'the Bible in miniature' because it summarises the entire Christian message in one sentence. This verse begins: 'For God so loved the world...' Where is this verse found?
beginner.explanation: John 3:16 is often called the most famous verse in the Bible: 'For God so loved the world that he gave his one and only Son, that whoever believes in him shall not perish but have eternal life.'

beginner.question: The Psalms were originally written to be sung during worship at the Temple in Jerusalem. What musical instruments are mentioned in Psalm 150 as being used to praise God?
options: A variety including trumpet, harp, lyre, tambourine, strings, pipe, and cymbals
beginner.explanation: Psalm 150 mentions an orchestra of instruments: trumpet, harp, lyre, tambourine, strings, pipe, and cymbals. It calls for praising God with every type of instrument available.

beginner.question: The New Testament book of James is a practical letter about living out one's faith through good deeds. According to tradition, which James wrote this letter?
beginner.explanation: The letter of James is traditionally attributed to James the brother of Jesus (also called James the Just), who became a leader of the church in Jerusalem. He emphasised that genuine faith produces good works.

beginner.question: The shortest of the four Gospels is known for its fast-paced style and frequently uses the word 'immediately' to move quickly from one event to the next. Which Gospel is this?
beginner.explanation: Mark is the shortest Gospel and is known for its energetic, action-oriented style. The Greek word 'euthys' (immediately/at once) appears about 40 times, giving the narrative a sense of urgency.

beginner.question: The longest of the four Gospels is also the Gospel that gives the most detailed account of Jesus' birth and contains many unique parables like the Good Samaritan and the Prodigal Son. Which Gospel is this?
beginner.explanation: Luke is the longest of the four Gospels. Written by Luke the physician, it includes the most detailed nativity narrative and many parables found nowhere else, such as the Good Samaritan and the Prodigal Son.

beginner.question: The Old Testament book of Esther is unique among biblical books for a very specific reason. What makes Esther unusual compared to every other book in the Bible?
options: It never mentions God by name
beginner.explanation: The book of Esther is the only book in the Bible that never directly mentions God by name. Despite this, the story clearly shows God's providential care as he works behind the scenes to save the Jewish people from destruction.

beginner.question: The Song of Solomon (also called Song of Songs) is a unique book in the Old Testament because it is primarily about what subject?
options: Romantic love between a man and a woman
beginner.explanation: The Song of Solomon is a love poem celebrating romantic love between a bride and groom. Jewish and Christian traditions have also interpreted it allegorically as describing God's love for his people.

beginner.question: The book of Ecclesiastes is a wisdom book in the Old Testament that reflects on the meaning of life. It begins with the famous phrase, 'Vanity of vanities, all is ______.'
beginner.explanation: Ecclesiastes opens with 'Vanity of vanities, all is vanity' (in many translations rendered as 'Meaningless! Meaningless! Everything is meaningless!'). The Hebrew word 'hevel' means 'breath' or 'vapour,' conveying life's fleeting nature.

beginner.question: The Bible contains a total of approximately how many chapters across all 66 books?
beginner.explanation: The Bible has 1,189 chapters in total — 929 in the Old Testament and 260 in the New Testament. Remember, these chapter divisions were not original but were added in the 13th century.

beginner.question: The book of Lamentations, found in the Old Testament, is a series of five poems mourning the destruction of Jerusalem. Who traditionally is believed to have written Lamentations?
beginner.explanation: Jeremiah is traditionally believed to have written Lamentations. He is known as 'the weeping prophet' because he wept over Jerusalem's sins and its destruction by the Babylonians in 586 BC.

beginner.question: What is the second book of the Bible? This book tells the dramatic story of how God delivered the Israelites from slavery in Egypt through Moses.
beginner.explanation: Exodus is the second book of the Bible. Its name means 'departure' or 'going out,' referring to the Israelites' departure from Egypt. It includes the ten plagues, the parting of the Red Sea, and the giving of the Ten Commandments.

beginner.question: Paul's letter to the Galatians is an important New Testament book about Christian freedom. Galatia was a region in what modern-day country?
beginner.explanation: Galatia was a region in central Asia Minor, which is modern-day Turkey. Paul visited Galatia during his missionary journeys and later wrote this letter to correct the churches there about the nature of salvation by faith.

beginner.question: The Hebrew Bible organises the Old Testament books differently than Christian Bibles. In the Hebrew Bible, the three main divisions are the Torah (Law), the Nevi'im (Prophets), and what third section?
beginner.explanation: The Hebrew Bible is divided into three sections: Torah (Law), Nevi'im (Prophets), and Ketuvim (Writings). Together they form the acronym 'TaNaKh,' which is the Jewish name for the Hebrew Bible.

beginner.question: The Hebrew name for the Jewish Bible is 'Tanakh.' This word is actually an acronym formed from the first letters of the three sections of the Hebrew Bible. What does the 'T' in Tanakh stand for?
beginner.explanation: The 'T' stands for Torah (Law), the 'N' for Nevi'im (Prophets), and the 'K' for Ketuvim (Writings). Together these three sections make up the complete Hebrew Bible, which Christians call the Old Testament.

beginner.question: The Psalms are filled with a Hebrew word, 'chesed,' that describes God's character. This word, often translated as 'steadfast love' or 'loving-kindness,' appears over 120 times in the Psalms alone. What does 'chesed' primarily mean?
beginner.explanation: Chesed is a rich Hebrew word meaning 'steadfast love,' 'loving-kindness,' or 'faithful love.' It describes God's loyal, covenant love for his people — love that never gives up even when they are unfaithful.

beginner.question: The Bible verse Jeremiah 29:11 is one of the most popular verses for encouragement. It says, 'For I know the plans I have for you, declares the Lord, plans to ______ you and not to harm you.' What word fills the blank?
beginner.explanation: Jeremiah 29:11 says 'plans to prosper you and not to harm you, plans to give you hope and a future.' God spoke these words through Jeremiah to the Israelites during their exile in Babylon, promising them a hopeful future.

beginner.question: In the early church, Christians initially used scrolls for their scriptures, but they eventually adopted a new format called a 'codex.' What is a codex?
beginner.explanation: A codex is an early form of a bound book with pages, as opposed to a rolled-up scroll. Early Christians were among the first to widely adopt the codex format, which made it easier to find specific passages.

beginner.question: The Bible was originally written on various materials before paper was widely available. What two materials were most commonly used for writing the biblical manuscripts?
beginner.explanation: Biblical manuscripts were written primarily on papyrus (made from the papyrus plant) and parchment (made from animal skins). Papyrus was common in Egypt, while parchment was more durable and widely used later.

beginner.question: The shortest of Paul's letters in the New Testament is addressed to a man named Philemon and is about a runaway slave. Approximately how many verses long is this letter?
beginner.explanation: Philemon has only 25 verses, making it the shortest of Paul's letters. Despite its brevity, it contains a powerful message about forgiveness, reconciliation, and treating others as equals in Christ.

beginner.question: Psalm 23, which begins 'The Lord is my shepherd,' uses the metaphor of God as a shepherd caring for his sheep. Which of the following is NOT mentioned in Psalm 23?
options: A valley of the shadow of death
beginner.explanation: A burning bush is not mentioned in Psalm 23 — that comes from the story of Moses in Exodus 3. Psalm 23 mentions green pastures, still waters, the valley of the shadow of death, a prepared table, and an overflowing cup.

beginner.question: The book of Job is part of the Wisdom Literature of the Old Testament. It addresses one of life's biggest questions. What is the main theme of the book of Job?
beginner.explanation: The book of Job wrestles with the question of why righteous people suffer. Job was a good man who lost everything, and the book explores his conversations with friends and ultimately with God about the meaning of his suffering.

beginner.question: Which of the following books is NOT part of the Old Testament? All the others were written before the time of Jesus and are included in the first section of the Bible.
beginner.explanation: Philippians is a New Testament letter written by the apostle Paul to the church in Philippi. Isaiah, Proverbs, and Deuteronomy are all Old Testament books.

beginner.question: Which of the following books IS part of the New Testament? Only one of these was written after the life of Jesus.
beginner.explanation: Hebrews is a New Testament book. Nehemiah, Ezekiel, and Habakkuk are all Old Testament books. Hebrews explains how Jesus fulfils and surpasses the Old Testament system of worship.

beginner.question: The book of Proverbs chapter 31 ends with a famous passage known as 'The Wife of Noble Character' (or 'The Virtuous Woman'). This passage is written in what poetic form, using the 22 letters of the Hebrew alphabet?
beginner.explanation: Proverbs 31:10-31 is an acrostic poem where each verse begins with a successive letter of the Hebrew alphabet (22 letters = 22 verses). This was a common poetic device in Hebrew literature.

beginner.question: The Hebrew alphabet, in which most of the Old Testament was written, has how many letters?
beginner.explanation: The Hebrew alphabet has 22 letters. This is why Psalm 119 has 22 sections and Lamentations has chapters based on the 22-letter acrostic pattern. Unlike English, Hebrew has no separate vowel letters.

beginner.question: The phrase 'Alpha and Omega' is used in the book of Revelation to describe God and Jesus. Alpha and Omega are the first and last letters of which alphabet?
beginner.explanation: Alpha and Omega are the first and last letters of the Greek alphabet. When God says 'I am the Alpha and the Omega,' it means 'I am the beginning and the end' — God encompasses all of existence.

beginner.question: The Old Testament book of Jonah tells the story of a prophet who tried to run away from God's command. God told Jonah to preach to which city?
beginner.explanation: God told Jonah to go to Nineveh, the capital of the Assyrian Empire and Israel's enemy. Jonah tried to flee in the opposite direction but was swallowed by a great fish. He eventually obeyed and preached to Nineveh.

beginner.question: The book of Hebrews in the New Testament contains a famous chapter often called 'The Hall of Faith' because it lists many Old Testament heroes who demonstrated great faith. What chapter of Hebrews is this?
beginner.explanation: Hebrews 11 is known as the 'Hall of Faith.' It lists heroes like Abel, Noah, Abraham, Sarah, Moses, Rahab, David, and many others who lived by faith in God's promises.

beginner.question: The final verse of the entire Bible is Revelation 22:21. Fittingly, it ends with a word of affirmation that has been used in worship for thousands of years. What is the very last word of the Bible?
beginner.explanation: The last word of the Bible is 'Amen.' Revelation 22:21 reads: 'The grace of the Lord Jesus be with God's people. Amen.' It is a perfect ending — 'Amen' means 'so be it' or 'truly,' affirming everything that has come before.

beginner.question: The Bible contains a total of 66 books, divided between the Old Testament and the New Testament.
beginner.explanation: This is correct. The Bible has 66 books in total — 39 in the Old Testament and 27 in the New Testament. This is the standard Protestant canon.

beginner.question: The Old Testament contains 39 books, spanning from the book of Genesis to the book of Malachi.
beginner.explanation: This is correct. The Old Testament has 39 books. Genesis is the first book and Malachi is the last, covering the period from creation to about 400 years before the birth of Jesus.

beginner.question: The New Testament contains 27 books, from the Gospel of Matthew to the book of Revelation.
beginner.explanation: This is correct. There are 27 books in the New Testament. Matthew is the first book and Revelation is the last, covering the life of Jesus and the early church.

beginner.question: The Bible contains a total of 72 books — 39 in the Old Testament and 33 in the New Testament.
beginner.explanation: This is false. The Bible has 66 books, not 72. The Old Testament has 39 books and the New Testament has 27 books (39 + 27 = 66).

beginner.question: The Old Testament contains 45 books, making it the larger of the Bible's two sections.
beginner.explanation: This is false. While the Old Testament is indeed the larger section, it contains 39 books, not 45. The New Testament has 27 books.

beginner.question: The New Testament has 32 books, including the four Gospels, Acts, many letters, and Revelation.
beginner.explanation: This is false. The New Testament has 27 books, not 32. It includes 4 Gospels, 1 book of history (Acts), 21 letters (epistles), and 1 book of prophecy (Revelation).

beginner.question: Genesis is the very first book of the Bible, and it begins with the story of God creating the heavens and the earth.
beginner.explanation: This is correct. Genesis is the first book of the Bible. Its opening verse, Genesis 1:1, reads: 'In the beginning God created the heavens and the earth.'

beginner.question: The first book of the Bible is Exodus, which tells the story of how God created the world.
beginner.explanation: This is false. The first book of the Bible is Genesis, not Exodus. Exodus is the second book and tells the story of the Israelites' deliverance from slavery in Egypt.

beginner.question: Revelation is the very last book of the Bible. It was written by the apostle John and describes visions of the end times.
beginner.explanation: This is correct. Revelation is the 66th and final book of the Bible. Written by the apostle John while exiled on the island of Patmos, it contains prophetic visions about the end of the age.

beginner.question: The last book of the Bible is the book of Jude, which was written by the brother of Jesus.
beginner.explanation: This is false. Jude is the second-to-last book of the Bible. The last book is Revelation. While Jude was indeed written by a brother of Jesus, it comes just before Revelation in the New Testament.

beginner.question: The four Gospels in the New Testament are Matthew, Mark, Luke, and John. They each tell the story of Jesus' life, death, and resurrection.
beginner.explanation: This is correct. Matthew, Mark, Luke, and John are the four Gospels. Each provides a unique perspective on Jesus' life and ministry, though they share many of the same events.

beginner.question: The four Gospels in the New Testament are Matthew, Mark, Luke, and Acts.
beginner.explanation: This is false. The four Gospels are Matthew, Mark, Luke, and John — not Acts. Acts is the fifth book of the New Testament and tells the story of the early church, not the life of Jesus.

beginner.question: There are four Gospels in the New Testament, and together they provide four different accounts of the life and ministry of Jesus Christ.
beginner.explanation: This is correct. The four Gospels — Matthew, Mark, Luke, and John — each tell the story of Jesus from a slightly different perspective. Matthew, Mark, and Luke are called the 'Synoptic Gospels' because they share much common material.

beginner.question: There are five Gospels in the New Testament: Matthew, Mark, Luke, John, and Acts.
beginner.explanation: This is false. There are only four Gospels: Matthew, Mark, Luke, and John. While Acts was written by the same author as Luke's Gospel, it is classified as a historical book about the early church, not a Gospel.

beginner.question: King David, the famous shepherd boy who defeated the giant Goliath, is traditionally credited as the primary author of the book of Psalms.
beginner.explanation: This is correct. David is credited with writing about 73 of the 150 psalms. He was known as a gifted musician and is called 'the sweet psalmist of Israel' in 2 Samuel 23:1.

beginner.question: The book of Psalms was mainly written by King Solomon, who was famous for his great wisdom.
beginner.explanation: This is false. The Psalms were mainly written by King David, not Solomon. Solomon did write a few psalms (such as Psalm 72 and 127), but he is primarily known as the author of Proverbs, Ecclesiastes, and Song of Solomon.

beginner.question: King Solomon, the son of King David, is traditionally credited as the primary author of the book of Proverbs.
beginner.explanation: This is correct. Solomon is the primary author of Proverbs. The very first verse of the book says: 'The proverbs of Solomon son of David, king of Israel.' God granted Solomon extraordinary wisdom (1 Kings 3:12).

beginner.question: The book of Proverbs was mainly written by the prophet Isaiah, one of the greatest Old Testament prophets.
beginner.explanation: This is false. Proverbs was mainly written by King Solomon, not Isaiah. Isaiah wrote the prophetic book that bears his name. Solomon was renowned for his wisdom and composed thousands of proverbs.

beginner.question: With 150 chapters, the book of Psalms is the longest book in the Bible by chapter count.
beginner.explanation: This is correct. Psalms has 150 chapters (or psalms), more than any other book in the Bible. The next closest are Isaiah with 66 chapters and Jeremiah with 52 chapters.

beginner.question: The book of Genesis is the longest book in the Bible because it covers the most history, from creation to Joseph's death in Egypt.
beginner.explanation: This is false. While Genesis covers an enormous span of history, it is not the longest book. Psalms is the longest by chapter count (150 chapters) and Jeremiah is the longest by word count. Genesis has 50 chapters.

beginner.question: Most of the Old Testament was originally written in Hebrew, the language of the ancient Israelite people.
beginner.explanation: This is correct. The vast majority of the Old Testament was written in Hebrew. Small portions (parts of Daniel and Ezra) were written in Aramaic, a closely related Semitic language.

beginner.question: The Old Testament was originally written in Greek, the same language as the New Testament.
beginner.explanation: This is false. The Old Testament was written primarily in Hebrew (with some Aramaic), not Greek. It is the New Testament that was written in Greek. The Old Testament was later translated into Greek (the Septuagint).

beginner.question: The New Testament was originally written in Koine Greek, the common everyday language of the eastern Roman Empire in the first century AD.
beginner.explanation: This is correct. Koine ('common') Greek was the everyday language of the eastern Mediterranean world. The New Testament authors wrote in this language so their message could reach the widest possible audience.

beginner.question: The New Testament was originally written in Latin because that was the official language of the Roman Empire.
beginner.explanation: This is false. While Latin was used in the western Roman Empire, the New Testament was written in Greek. Koine Greek was the common trade and communication language of the eastern Mediterranean, where Christianity first spread.

beginner.question: The word 'Gospel' comes from Old English and means 'good news,' which reflects the original Greek word 'euangelion.'
beginner.explanation: This is correct. 'Gospel' comes from the Old English 'godspel' meaning 'good story' or 'good news.' This translates the Greek 'euangelion,' which also means 'good news' — the message about Jesus Christ.

beginner.question: The word 'Gospel' means 'sacred law' and refers to the rules that Jesus established for his followers.
beginner.explanation: This is false. The word 'Gospel' means 'good news,' not 'sacred law.' It comes from the Greek word 'euangelion.' The Gospel message is about the good news of Jesus' life, death, and resurrection.

beginner.question: The title 'Christ' comes from the Greek word 'Christos,' which means 'Anointed One.' It is the Greek equivalent of the Hebrew word 'Messiah.'
beginner.explanation: This is correct. Both 'Christ' (Greek) and 'Messiah' (Hebrew) mean 'Anointed One.' In ancient Israel, kings and priests were anointed with oil to show they were specially chosen by God.

beginner.question: The word 'Christ' is Jesus' last name, just as 'Smith' or 'Jones' might be a family name today.
beginner.explanation: This is false. 'Christ' is not a surname — it is a title meaning 'Anointed One' (from the Greek 'Christos'). 'Jesus Christ' means 'Jesus the Anointed One' or 'Jesus the Messiah.'

beginner.question: The English word 'Bible' comes from the Greek word 'biblia,' which simply means 'books.'
beginner.explanation: This is correct. 'Bible' comes from the Greek 'biblia' meaning 'books.' This is appropriate because the Bible is not a single book but a collection of 66 individual books.

beginner.question: The word 'Bible' comes from a Hebrew word meaning 'the truth' or 'the word of God.'
beginner.explanation: This is false. The word 'Bible' comes from the Greek word 'biblia,' meaning 'books' — not from Hebrew. It reflects the fact that the Bible is a collection of many books.

beginner.question: The Bible is divided into two main sections: the Old Testament and the New Testament.
beginner.explanation: This is correct. The two major divisions of the Bible are the Old Testament (39 books, written before Jesus) and the New Testament (27 books, written after Jesus). Together they form the complete Christian Bible.

beginner.question: The first five books of the Old Testament — Genesis, Exodus, Leviticus, Numbers, and Deuteronomy — are known as the Torah or the Pentateuch.
beginner.explanation: This is correct. These five books are called the Torah (Hebrew for 'Law/Instruction') or the Pentateuch (Greek for 'five scrolls'). They are traditionally attributed to Moses.

beginner.question: The Old Testament contains sections categorised as Law, History, Poetry/Wisdom, and Prophecy.
beginner.explanation: This is correct. The Old Testament's 39 books can be grouped into four categories: Law (Genesis-Deuteronomy), History (Joshua-Esther), Poetry and Wisdom (Job-Song of Solomon), and Prophecy (Isaiah-Malachi).

beginner.question: The New Testament can be divided into four categories: Gospels, History (Acts), Letters (Epistles), and Prophecy (Revelation).
beginner.explanation: This is correct. The 27 New Testament books include 4 Gospels (Matthew-John), 1 historical book (Acts), 21 letters or epistles (Romans-Jude), and 1 prophetic book (Revelation).

beginner.question: The apostle Paul wrote more books of the New Testament than any other single author. He is credited with writing 13 letters.
beginner.explanation: This is correct. Paul wrote 13 of the 27 New Testament books — nearly half. His letters include Romans, 1 & 2 Corinthians, Galatians, Ephesians, Philippians, Colossians, 1 & 2 Thessalonians, 1 & 2 Timothy, Titus, and Philemon.

beginner.question: The apostle Peter wrote the most books in the New Testament, contributing 13 of the 27 New Testament books.
beginner.explanation: This is false. It was the apostle Paul, not Peter, who wrote the most New Testament books (13 letters). Peter wrote only 2 books: 1 Peter and 2 Peter.

beginner.question: The shortest verse in the Bible is John 11:35, which simply says 'Jesus wept.' It describes Jesus' reaction to the death of his friend Lazarus.
beginner.explanation: This is correct. John 11:35 — 'Jesus wept' — is the shortest verse in most English translations of the Bible. Jesus wept at the tomb of his friend Lazarus before miraculously raising him from the dead.

beginner.question: The shortest verse in the Bible is Genesis 1:1, which says 'In the beginning, God created the heavens and the earth.'
beginner.explanation: This is false. Genesis 1:1 is a famous verse but not the shortest. The shortest verse in the Bible is John 11:35: 'Jesus wept.' It has just two words in English.

beginner.question: Luke, the author of the third Gospel, also wrote the book of Acts. Both books are addressed to a man named Theophilus.
beginner.explanation: This is correct. Luke wrote both the Gospel of Luke and Acts. Both begin with a dedication to Theophilus, and Acts 1:1 explicitly references the Gospel as Luke's 'former book.'

beginner.question: The book of Acts was written by the apostle Paul, who described his own missionary journeys throughout the Roman Empire.
beginner.explanation: This is false. Acts was written by Luke, not Paul. While Paul is a central figure in Acts, Luke (a physician and Paul's travelling companion) is the author. Acts 1:1 references the 'former book' — the Gospel of Luke.

beginner.question: The word 'testament' in 'Old Testament' and 'New Testament' means 'covenant' — a binding agreement or promise between God and his people.
beginner.explanation: This is correct. 'Testament' comes from the Latin 'testamentum,' meaning 'covenant.' The Old Testament records God's covenant with Israel, and the New Testament records the new covenant through Jesus Christ.

beginner.question: The word 'testament' in 'Old Testament' and 'New Testament' means 'historical record' or 'chronicle.'
beginner.explanation: This is false. 'Testament' means 'covenant' or 'agreement,' not 'historical record.' The Old and New Testaments describe the old and new covenants between God and humanity.

beginner.question: The Hebrew word 'Amen,' used at the end of prayers, means 'so be it' or 'truly.' It expresses agreement and affirmation.
beginner.explanation: This is correct. 'Amen' is a Hebrew word meaning 'so be it,' 'truly,' or 'it is certain.' It has been used in Jewish and Christian worship for thousands of years to express agreement with a prayer or statement.

beginner.question: The word 'Amen' is a Greek word that means 'thank you' and was first used by the apostle Paul in his letters.
beginner.explanation: This is false. 'Amen' is a Hebrew word (not Greek) meaning 'so be it' or 'truly' (not 'thank you'). It was used in the Old Testament long before Paul — the word appears throughout the Psalms and other Old Testament books.

beginner.question: The Hebrew word 'Hallelujah' means 'Praise the Lord.' It combines 'hallelu' (praise) with 'Yah' (a short form of God's name).
beginner.explanation: This is correct. 'Hallelujah' is a compound Hebrew word: 'hallelu' means 'praise' and 'Yah' is a shortened form of 'Yahweh,' God's personal name. It appears frequently in the Psalms.

beginner.question: The word 'Hallelujah' is a Greek word meaning 'God is great' that was first used in the New Testament book of Revelation.
beginner.explanation: This is false. 'Hallelujah' is a Hebrew word (not Greek) meaning 'Praise the Lord' (not 'God is great'). While it does appear in Revelation 19, it originated in the Old Testament Psalms.

beginner.question: Johannes Gutenberg invented the printing press with movable type around 1440, and the first major book he printed was a Bible — known as the Gutenberg Bible.
beginner.explanation: This is correct. Gutenberg's printing press revolutionised book production. The Gutenberg Bible, printed around 1455 in Latin, was the first major book produced using movable metal type in Europe.

beginner.question: The Gutenberg Bible was printed in English so that ordinary people in England could read the Bible for themselves.
beginner.explanation: This is false. The Gutenberg Bible was printed in Latin, not English. Latin was the language of the Catholic Church and scholarship in medieval Europe. English Bibles came later, with translators like Tyndale and the KJV.

beginner.question: The Dead Sea Scrolls, discovered in caves near the Dead Sea beginning in 1947, include some of the oldest known copies of Old Testament books.
beginner.explanation: This is correct. The Dead Sea Scrolls, found near Qumran in Israel, contain manuscripts dating from the 3rd century BC to the 1st century AD. They include fragments from every Old Testament book except Esther.

beginner.question: The Dead Sea Scrolls were discovered in Egypt in the early 1900s and contain mostly New Testament manuscripts.
beginner.explanation: This is false on both counts. The Dead Sea Scrolls were discovered near the Dead Sea in Israel (not Egypt) starting in 1947 (not the early 1900s), and they contain primarily Old Testament texts (not New Testament).

beginner.question: The chapter and verse numbers found in modern Bibles (like 'John 3:16') were not part of the original manuscripts — they were added centuries later to help readers find specific passages.
beginner.explanation: This is correct. The original biblical authors did not use chapter or verse numbers. Chapter divisions were added around 1227 by Stephen Langton, and verse numbers were added in the 1500s by Robert Estienne.

beginner.question: The chapter and verse numbers in the Bible were written by the original authors to help readers navigate their texts.
beginner.explanation: This is false. The original biblical manuscripts had no chapter or verse numbers. Chapters were added in the 13th century by Stephen Langton, and verses were added in the 16th century by Robert Estienne.

beginner.question: The Bible was written by approximately 40 different authors over a period of roughly 1,500 years.
beginner.explanation: This is correct. The Bible was written by about 40 authors — including kings, shepherds, fishermen, a doctor, and a tax collector — over approximately 1,500 years, from around 1400 BC to 95 AD.

beginner.question: The Bible was written by a single author over the course of a few decades, which is why it has such a unified message.
beginner.explanation: This is false. The Bible was written by approximately 40 different authors over about 1,500 years. Despite this diversity of authors and timespan, Christians believe it has a unified message because it was inspired by God.

beginner.question: Luke, the author of the Gospel of Luke and the book of Acts, was a fisherman by profession before becoming a follower of Jesus.
beginner.explanation: This is false. Luke was a physician (doctor), not a fisherman. Colossians 4:14 identifies him as 'the beloved physician.' Peter and Andrew were the fishermen among Jesus' followers.

beginner.question: The apostle Paul, before his conversion to Christianity, was originally known by the name Saul and was from the city of Tarsus.
beginner.explanation: This is correct. Paul was originally called Saul and came from Tarsus in modern-day Turkey. He was a devout Pharisee who persecuted Christians before his dramatic conversion on the road to Damascus (Acts 9).

beginner.question: The King James Version of the Bible, one of the most influential English translations ever, was first published in 1611.
beginner.explanation: This is correct. The King James Version (also called the Authorised Version) was published in 1611, commissioned by King James I of England. It was produced by 47 scholars and has profoundly influenced English literature.

beginner.question: The King James Version was the first-ever English translation of the Bible, created in 1611.
beginner.explanation: This is false. While the KJV (1611) is one of the most famous English translations, it was not the first. Earlier English translations include John Wycliffe's Bible (1380s), William Tyndale's New Testament (1526), and the Geneva Bible (1560).

beginner.question: William Tyndale was the first person to translate the New Testament directly from Greek into English. He was executed for this work in 1536.
beginner.explanation: This is correct. William Tyndale translated the New Testament from Greek to English and had it printed in 1526. He was arrested, charged with heresy, and executed in 1536. Much of his translation later appeared in the King James Version.

beginner.question: The New Testament letters are also called 'epistles,' a word that comes from the Greek 'epistole,' meaning 'letter.'
beginner.explanation: This is correct. The 21 letters in the New Testament are formally called 'epistles.' The word comes from the Greek 'epistole' meaning 'letter' or 'message.' They include Paul's letters, the General Epistles, and more.

beginner.question: The Septuagint is an ancient Greek translation of the Old Testament that was created around the 3rd century BC.
beginner.explanation: This is correct. The Septuagint (often abbreviated LXX) was a Greek translation of the Hebrew Old Testament, created around 250-100 BC in Alexandria, Egypt, for Greek-speaking Jews.

beginner.question: The Latin Vulgate, translated by Saint Jerome around 400 AD, was the standard Bible of the Western church for over a thousand years.
beginner.explanation: This is correct. Jerome completed his Latin translation (the Vulgate) around 405 AD. It became the standard Bible of the Catholic Church and was used for over a millennium until the Reformation era.

beginner.question: The book of Psalms is divided into three smaller 'books' within itself, mirroring the three main sections of the Hebrew Bible.
beginner.explanation: This is false. Psalms is divided into five books, not three: Book I (1-41), Book II (42-72), Book III (73-89), Book IV (90-106), and Book V (107-150). This fivefold structure mirrors the five books of the Torah.

beginner.question: Psalm 119 is the longest chapter in the entire Bible, with 176 verses arranged as an acrostic poem based on the 22 letters of the Hebrew alphabet.
beginner.explanation: This is correct. Psalm 119 has 176 verses, making it the longest chapter in the Bible. It has 22 sections of 8 verses each, with each section corresponding to a letter of the Hebrew alphabet.

beginner.question: Psalm 117, with only 2 verses, is the shortest chapter in the entire Bible.
beginner.explanation: This is correct. Psalm 117 has just 2 verses, making it the shortest chapter in the Bible. Despite its brevity, it contains a universal call: 'Praise the Lord, all you nations; extol him, all you peoples.'

beginner.question: The book of Ruth is unique in the Bible because it is the only book that never mentions God by name.
beginner.explanation: This is false. Ruth mentions God many times. It is the book of Esther that never directly mentions God by name. Despite God's absence by name in Esther, the story clearly shows his providential care.

beginner.question: The word 'Torah' is Hebrew for 'law' or 'instruction' and refers to the first five books of the Bible.
beginner.explanation: This is correct. 'Torah' means 'law,' 'instruction,' or 'teaching' in Hebrew. It refers to the first five books (Genesis-Deuteronomy), also called the Pentateuch, which contain God's law given through Moses.

beginner.question: The word 'Pentateuch' comes from Greek and means 'five scrolls,' referring to the first five books of the Bible.
beginner.explanation: This is correct. 'Pentateuch' comes from the Greek 'pente' (five) and 'teuchos' (scroll/volume). It is another name for the Torah — the first five books of the Bible: Genesis, Exodus, Leviticus, Numbers, and Deuteronomy.

beginner.question: Moses is traditionally credited with writing the first five books of the Bible (the Pentateuch/Torah).
beginner.explanation: This is correct. Mosaic authorship of the Pentateuch is the traditional view. The Bible itself references Moses writing down God's law (Deuteronomy 31:9, 24), and Jesus referred to the law as being from Moses.

beginner.question: The Hebrew Bible is known as the 'Tanakh,' an acronym formed from the first letters of its three sections: Torah (Law), Nevi'im (Prophets), and Ketuvim (Writings).
beginner.explanation: This is correct. Tanakh is an acronym: T (Torah/Law), N (Nevi'im/Prophets), and K (Ketuvim/Writings). These three divisions organise the Hebrew Bible differently from the Christian Old Testament, though they contain the same books.

beginner.question: The Tanakh is the Jewish name for the New Testament.
beginner.explanation: This is false. The Tanakh is the Jewish name for the Hebrew Bible, which corresponds to the Christian Old Testament — not the New Testament. The acronym stands for Torah, Nevi'im, and Ketuvim.

beginner.question: Malachi is the last book of the Old Testament, and Matthew is the first book of the New Testament.
beginner.explanation: This is correct. Malachi is the final book of the Old Testament, and Matthew is the first book of the New Testament. Between them is a period of roughly 400 years often called the 'silent years.'

beginner.question: The last book of the Old Testament is Zechariah, which comes right before the New Testament begins with Matthew.
beginner.explanation: This is false. The last book of the Old Testament is Malachi, not Zechariah. Zechariah is the second-to-last book. Malachi is followed by Matthew, the first book of the New Testament.

beginner.question: The book of Acts records that the early church began in Jerusalem on the day of Pentecost, when the Holy Spirit came upon the disciples.
beginner.explanation: This is correct. Acts chapter 2 describes the day of Pentecost in Jerusalem, when the Holy Spirit descended on the disciples. Peter then preached and about 3,000 people believed, launching the Christian church.

beginner.question: John 3:16, which begins 'For God so loved the world...', is often called 'the Gospel in a nutshell' because it summarises the core Christian message in one verse.
beginner.explanation: This is correct. John 3:16 — 'For God so loved the world that he gave his one and only Son, that whoever believes in him shall not perish but have eternal life' — is considered by many to be the most famous verse in the Bible.

beginner.question: The Hebrew alphabet has 22 letters, and this number is reflected in several biblical poems and acrostic structures.
beginner.explanation: This is correct. The Hebrew alphabet has 22 letters. Psalm 119 has 22 sections (one per letter), each with 8 verses. Proverbs 31:10-31 and each chapter of Lamentations also follow the 22-letter acrostic pattern.

beginner.question: The Hebrew alphabet has 26 letters, the same number as the English alphabet.
beginner.explanation: This is false. The Hebrew alphabet has 22 letters, not 26. Unlike English, Hebrew originally had no written vowels — only consonants. Vowel marks (nikkud) were added much later to aid pronunciation.

beginner.question: In the book of Revelation, God describes himself as 'the Alpha and the Omega' — the first and last letters of the Greek alphabet — meaning 'the beginning and the end.'
beginner.explanation: This is correct. Alpha (Α) and Omega (Ω) are the first and last letters of the Greek alphabet. By using this phrase, God declares that he encompasses all of existence from beginning to end.

beginner.question: 'Alpha and Omega' are the first and last letters of the Hebrew alphabet, used in Revelation to describe God.
beginner.explanation: This is false. Alpha and Omega are the first and last letters of the Greek alphabet, not the Hebrew alphabet. The first and last letters of the Hebrew alphabet are Aleph and Tav.

beginner.question: The Bible has been translated into more languages than any other book in human history.
beginner.explanation: This is correct. The Bible has been translated into over 700 complete languages, with portions translated into over 3,500 languages. No other book in history has been translated into as many languages.

beginner.question: The Bible is the second best-selling book of all time, coming in just behind Shakespeare's collected works.
beginner.explanation: This is false. The Bible is the number one best-selling book of all time — not second. With an estimated 5 billion copies sold or distributed, no other book comes close. Shakespeare's works, while hugely popular, do not surpass the Bible.

beginner.question: The word 'Scripture' comes from the Latin 'scriptura,' meaning 'writing,' and is used as another name for the Bible.
beginner.explanation: This is correct. 'Scripture' comes from the Latin 'scriptura' meaning 'writing.' When Paul wrote 'All Scripture is God-breathed' (2 Timothy 3:16), he was referring to the sacred writings that form the Bible.

beginner.question: The apostle John wrote five books of the New Testament: the Gospel of John, 1 John, 2 John, 3 John, and Revelation.
beginner.explanation: This is correct. John is credited with five New Testament books, making him the second most prolific New Testament author after Paul (who wrote 13). John's writings emphasise love, truth, and the divinity of Jesus.

beginner.question: The apostle Peter wrote five books of the New Testament, including a Gospel and three letters.
beginner.explanation: This is false. Peter wrote only two New Testament books: 1 Peter and 2 Peter. He did not write any of the four Gospels. It was John who wrote five books (a Gospel, three letters, and Revelation).

beginner.question: The book of Isaiah is the longest book in the Bible by total word count, because it has 66 chapters — the same number as the total books in the Bible.
beginner.explanation: This is false. While Isaiah does have 66 chapters, the longest book by word count is actually Jeremiah. Psalms has the most chapters (150), but Jeremiah contains the most words overall.

beginner.question: The books of 1 and 2 Samuel, 1 and 2 Kings, and 1 and 2 Chronicles were always written as separate books from the very beginning — they were never combined.
beginner.explanation: This is false. In the original Hebrew Bible, Samuel, Kings, and Chronicles were each single books. They were divided into two parts when translated into Greek because the Greek text (with added vowels) was longer and required more scroll space.

beginner.question: The 'Major Prophets' of the Old Testament are called 'major' because their messages were more important than those of the 'Minor Prophets.'
beginner.explanation: This is false. The terms 'Major' and 'Minor' refer to the length of the books, not the importance of the messages. The Major Prophets (Isaiah, Jeremiah, Lamentations, Ezekiel, Daniel) simply have longer books.

beginner.question: There are twelve Minor Prophets in the Old Testament, from Hosea to Malachi, and they are called 'minor' because their books are shorter.
beginner.explanation: This is correct. The twelve Minor Prophets are: Hosea, Joel, Amos, Obadiah, Jonah, Micah, Nahum, Habakkuk, Zephaniah, Haggai, Zechariah, and Malachi. 'Minor' refers to their shorter length, not lesser importance.

beginner.question: The Bible contains approximately 1,189 chapters in total — 929 in the Old Testament and 260 in the New Testament.
beginner.explanation: This is correct. The Bible has about 1,189 chapters total. The Old Testament's 929 chapters make up roughly 78% of the Bible, while the New Testament's 260 chapters make up about 22%.

beginner.question: The Old Testament and New Testament have roughly the same number of chapters, with each containing about 600.
beginner.explanation: This is false. The Old Testament has 929 chapters and the New Testament has only 260 chapters. The Old Testament is significantly larger, comprising about 78% of the Bible's total chapters.

beginner.question: The last word of the entire Bible is 'Amen,' found at the end of Revelation 22:21.
beginner.explanation: This is correct. The final verse of the Bible is Revelation 22:21: 'The grace of the Lord Jesus be with God's people. Amen.' The word 'Amen' ('so be it') provides a fitting conclusion to the entire Bible.

beginner.question: The very first word of the Bible in the original Hebrew is 'Bereshit,' which means 'God spoke.'
beginner.explanation: This is false. 'Bereshit' means 'In the beginning,' not 'God spoke.' Genesis 1:1 opens with 'In the beginning God created the heavens and the earth.' The phrase 'God spoke' does not appear until later in the chapter.

beginner.question: Proverbs 1:7 says 'The fear of the Lord is the beginning of knowledge,' establishing reverence for God as the foundation of all wisdom.
beginner.explanation: This is correct. Proverbs 1:7 is a key verse that sets the tone for the entire book of Proverbs. It teaches that true knowledge and wisdom begin with a proper reverence and respect for God.

beginner.question: The book of Ruth in the Old Testament tells the story of a Moabite woman who became the great-grandmother of King Solomon.
beginner.explanation: This is false. Ruth became the great-grandmother of King David, not King Solomon. Ruth married Boaz, and their son was Obed, whose son was Jesse, whose son was David. Solomon was David's son — one generation later.

beginner.question: The Song of Solomon (Song of Songs) in the Old Testament is primarily a love poem celebrating romantic love between a bride and groom.
beginner.explanation: This is correct. The Song of Solomon is a love poem celebrating the beauty of romantic love. It has also been interpreted allegorically as representing God's love for Israel or Christ's love for the church.

beginner.question: The book of Ecclesiastes opens with the famous phrase 'The Lord is my shepherd, I shall not want,' which is also the beginning of Psalm 23.
beginner.explanation: This is false. 'The Lord is my shepherd, I shall not want' is the opening of Psalm 23, not Ecclesiastes. Ecclesiastes opens with 'Vanity of vanities, all is vanity' (or 'Meaningless! Meaningless! Everything is meaningless!').

beginner.question: The book of James in the New Testament was written by James the brother of Jesus, not James the apostle (son of Zebedee).
beginner.explanation: This is correct. The letter of James is traditionally attributed to James the brother of Jesus (also called James the Just), who became the leader of the Jerusalem church. James the son of Zebedee was martyred early (Acts 12:2).

beginner.question: Mark is the shortest of the four Gospels and is known for its fast-paced, action-oriented writing style.
beginner.explanation: This is correct. Mark is the shortest Gospel with only 16 chapters. It uses the word 'immediately' (Greek: euthys) about 40 times, giving the narrative an urgent, action-packed feeling.

beginner.question: John is the shortest of the four Gospels, with only 14 chapters.
beginner.explanation: This is false. Mark is the shortest Gospel, not John. The Gospel of John has 21 chapters, while Mark has only 16 chapters. John is actually one of the longer Gospels.

beginner.question: Luke is the longest of the four Gospels and contains many unique parables such as the Good Samaritan and the Prodigal Son.
beginner.explanation: This is correct. Luke's Gospel is the longest of the four Gospels. It contains at least 15 parables not found in the other Gospels, including the beloved stories of the Good Samaritan and the Prodigal Son.

beginner.question: Paul's letter to the Romans is considered one of the most theologically important books in the entire New Testament.
beginner.explanation: This is correct. Romans systematically presents the core doctrines of Christian faith, including sin, grace, justification by faith, and sanctification. Martin Luther's study of Romans helped spark the Protestant Reformation.

beginner.question: The Shema, one of the most important prayers in Judaism, comes from Deuteronomy 6:4 and begins with 'Hear, O Israel: The Lord our God, the Lord is one.'
beginner.explanation: This is correct. The Shema (from the Hebrew word for 'hear') is the foundational declaration of monotheism in Judaism. Jesus himself quoted it when asked about the greatest commandment (Mark 12:29).

beginner.question: 2 Timothy 3:16 says 'All Scripture is God-breathed,' meaning that Christians believe the Bible was inspired by God through human authors.
beginner.explanation: This is correct. 'God-breathed' (Greek: theopneustos) means that God inspired the human authors of the Bible. Christians believe God guided the writers through the Holy Spirit while allowing their individual styles to come through.

beginner.question: The letter to Philemon is the shortest of Paul's letters, with only one chapter and 25 verses.
beginner.explanation: This is correct. Philemon is Paul's shortest letter — just one chapter with 25 verses. Despite its brevity, it powerfully addresses themes of forgiveness and reconciliation, as Paul appeals on behalf of the slave Onesimus.

beginner.question: The book of Obadiah, one of the Minor Prophets, contains three short chapters and is one of the shorter books in the Old Testament.
beginner.explanation: This is false. Obadiah contains only one chapter (with 21 verses), not three. It is the shortest book in the Old Testament. It contains a prophecy against the nation of Edom.

beginner.question: The book of Lamentations is traditionally attributed to the prophet Jeremiah and mourns the destruction of Jerusalem by the Babylonians.
beginner.explanation: This is correct. Lamentations is a collection of five poems grieving the fall of Jerusalem in 586 BC. Jeremiah, known as 'the weeping prophet,' is the traditional author.

beginner.question: In the New Testament, Paul wrote two letters to a young pastor named Timothy, giving him advice on leading a church. These are known as part of the 'Pastoral Epistles.'
beginner.explanation: This is correct. 1 Timothy and 2 Timothy, along with Titus, are called the Pastoral Epistles because they contain pastoral advice for church leaders. Paul mentored Timothy and treated him like a son in the faith.

beginner.question: The Pastoral Epistles are 1 Corinthians, 2 Corinthians, and Galatians, because they were written to churches that needed pastoral care.
beginner.explanation: This is false. The Pastoral Epistles are 1 Timothy, 2 Timothy, and Titus — letters written to individual pastors about leading their churches. Corinthians and Galatians are general epistles written to entire congregations.

beginner.question: The Bible was originally written on papyrus (made from a plant) and parchment (made from animal skins) before paper was widely available.
beginner.explanation: This is correct. Papyrus, made from the papyrus plant, was common in Egypt and the ancient Near East. Parchment, made from treated animal skins, was more durable and gradually replaced papyrus for important documents.

beginner.question: Early Christians were among the first to widely adopt the 'codex' format (a bound book with pages) instead of scrolls for their scriptures.
beginner.explanation: This is correct. While Jews and Romans typically used scrolls, early Christians preferred the codex (bound pages) format. The codex was more portable, could hold more text, and made it easier to find specific passages.

beginner.question: Galatia, the region Paul wrote his letter to the Galatians about, was located in modern-day Egypt.
beginner.explanation: This is false. Galatia was located in modern-day Turkey (ancient Asia Minor), not Egypt. Paul founded churches there during his missionary journeys and later wrote his letter to correct their understanding of salvation by faith.

beginner.question: The Dead Sea Scrolls included fragments from every book of the Old Testament except the book of Esther.
beginner.explanation: This is correct. Among the approximately 900 manuscripts found in the Dead Sea Scrolls caves, fragments from every Old Testament book were identified except Esther. The reason for Esther's absence is debated by scholars.

beginner.question: The Dead Sea Scrolls proved that the Old Testament text had been remarkably well preserved over more than a thousand years of hand-copying.
beginner.explanation: This is correct. When the Dead Sea Scrolls (dating to about 200 BC-70 AD) were compared with later medieval manuscripts (about 1000 AD), they showed the text had been copied with remarkable accuracy over more than a millennium.

beginner.question: The first major book printed using Gutenberg's movable type printing press was a dictionary, not a Bible.
beginner.explanation: This is false. The first major book printed using Gutenberg's press was a Bible — the famous Gutenberg Bible, printed around 1455 in Latin. This invention revolutionised the spread of knowledge and literature.

beginner.question: Psalm 23, one of the most beloved passages in the Bible, was written by King Solomon and begins with 'The Lord is my shepherd; I shall not want.'
beginner.explanation: This is false. Psalm 23 was written by King David, not Solomon. David was a former shepherd himself, which makes the shepherd metaphor especially personal. Solomon wrote Proverbs, not this psalm.

beginner.question: The book of Job in the Old Testament addresses the question of why good people suffer, making it one of the Bible's wisdom books.
beginner.explanation: This is correct. Job is part of the Wisdom Literature. It tells the story of a righteous man who lost everything and explores the profound question of human suffering through his dialogues with friends and ultimately with God.

beginner.question: The word 'Hosanna,' shouted by crowds when Jesus entered Jerusalem, is a Hebrew word meaning 'save us, we pray.'
beginner.explanation: This is correct. 'Hosanna' comes from the Hebrew 'hoshia na' meaning 'save us, we pray.' Over time it evolved into an exclamation of praise. The crowds shouted it during Jesus' triumphal entry into Jerusalem.

beginner.question: The word 'Selah,' which appears 71 times in the book of Psalms, most likely indicates a musical pause or moment of reflection.
beginner.explanation: This is correct. While the exact meaning of 'Selah' is debated, most scholars believe it indicates a pause for reflection or a musical interlude. Since the Psalms were sung in worship, it was likely a performance instruction.

beginner.question: Hebrews chapter 11 is often called 'The Hall of Faith' because it lists many Old Testament heroes who demonstrated great faith in God.
beginner.explanation: This is correct. Hebrews 11 catalogues heroes of faith including Abel, Noah, Abraham, Sarah, Moses, Rahab, David, and many others. Each is commended for trusting God's promises.

beginner.question: Jeremiah 29:11, which says 'For I know the plans I have for you... plans to prosper you and not to harm you,' was originally spoken to the Israelites during their exile in Babylon.
beginner.explanation: This is correct. God spoke these words through Jeremiah to the Israelites exiled in Babylon, promising them hope and a future. While often applied broadly today, the original context was God's promise to restore Israel.

beginner.question: The books of the Bible are arranged in strict chronological order, with the earliest events first and the latest events last.
beginner.explanation: This is false. The books of the Bible are not in strict chronological order. They are generally arranged by genre (Law, History, Poetry, Prophecy in the OT; Gospels, History, Letters, Prophecy in the NT), not by when events occurred.

beginner.question: The Psalms were originally written to be sung during worship at the Temple in Jerusalem, not just read as poems.
beginner.explanation: This is correct. The Psalms were Israel's hymnbook. Many psalm headings include musical instructions (such as 'For the director of music' or 'With stringed instruments'), indicating they were composed for congregational worship.

beginner.question: The Matthew, Mark, and Luke Gospels are called the 'Synoptic Gospels' because they share many of the same stories and often tell them from similar perspectives.
beginner.explanation: This is correct. 'Synoptic' comes from the Greek 'synoptikos' meaning 'seeing together.' Matthew, Mark, and Luke share much common material and viewpoint, while John's Gospel is notably different in content and style.

beginner.question: All four Gospels — Matthew, Mark, Luke, and John — are called 'Synoptic Gospels' because they all tell the story of Jesus.
beginner.explanation: This is false. Only Matthew, Mark, and Luke are called the 'Synoptic Gospels.' John's Gospel is not considered synoptic because it contains significant unique material and a different theological emphasis from the other three.

beginner.question: The book of Jonah in the Old Testament tells the story of a prophet who was swallowed by a great fish after trying to run away from God's command to preach to Nineveh.
beginner.explanation: This is correct. God told Jonah to go to Nineveh, but Jonah fled by ship in the opposite direction. God sent a storm, and Jonah was swallowed by a great fish for three days before being vomited onto dry land. He then obeyed and went to Nineveh.

beginner.question: The Hebrew word 'Elohim,' used in the Old Testament, means 'Lord' and is the personal name of God revealed to Moses at the burning bush.
beginner.explanation: This is false. 'Elohim' means 'God,' not 'Lord.' The personal name revealed to Moses at the burning bush was 'YHWH' (Yahweh), often translated as 'LORD' in English Bibles. Elohim is a more general term for God.

beginner.question: Proverbs 31:10-31, the passage about 'The Wife of Noble Character,' is written as a Hebrew acrostic poem with each verse beginning with a successive letter of the Hebrew alphabet.
beginner.explanation: This is correct. The 22 verses of Proverbs 31:10-31 form an acrostic, with each verse beginning with the next letter of the 22-letter Hebrew alphabet. This literary device aided memorisation.

beginner.question: The book of Philippians is a letter Paul wrote while he was in prison, and it is often called the 'letter of sorrow' because of its heavy, mournful tone throughout.
beginner.explanation: This is false. Despite writing from prison, Philippians is actually called the 'letter of joy' — not sorrow. The words 'joy' and 'rejoice' appear throughout. Paul's famous exhortation 'Rejoice in the Lord always' (4:4) captures its upbeat spirit.

beginner.question: The Exodus, the second book of the Bible, tells the story of how God delivered the Israelites from slavery in Egypt through Moses.
beginner.explanation: This is correct. Exodus (meaning 'departure' or 'going out') is the second book of the Bible. It narrates the Israelites' liberation from Egyptian slavery through Moses, including the ten plagues and the parting of the Red Sea.

beginner.question: Genesis is the longest book in the Bible with over 200 chapters.
beginner.explanation: This is false. Genesis has 50 chapters, not over 200. The longest book by chapter count is Psalms with 150 chapters. No book in the Bible has 200 or more chapters.

beginner.question: The books of 1 Chronicles and 2 Chronicles in the Old Testament primarily focus on the history of the Southern Kingdom of Judah.
beginner.explanation: This is correct. While Samuel and Kings cover both the northern and southern kingdoms, Chronicles focuses primarily on the Southern Kingdom of Judah, particularly its kings, the Temple worship, and the Davidic lineage.

beginner.question: The Hebrew word 'chesed,' one of the most important words in the Old Testament, means 'justice' or 'righteous judgment' and describes God's role as a judge.
beginner.explanation: This is false. 'Chesed' means 'steadfast love,' 'loving-kindness,' or 'faithful love' — not 'justice.' It describes God's loyal, covenant love that never gives up, even when his people are unfaithful.

beginner.question: The Gutenberg Bible was the first book ever written in human history.
beginner.explanation: This is false. The Gutenberg Bible (c. 1455) was the first major book printed using movable type in Europe — not the first book ever written. Books and manuscripts had existed for thousands of years before Gutenberg.

beginner.question: John Wycliffe supervised the first complete translation of the Bible into English in the 1380s, before the printing press was invented.
beginner.explanation: This is correct. Wycliffe's Bible (1380s) was the first complete English translation. Since the printing press wasn't invented until the 1440s, every copy had to be hand-written, making them extremely rare and expensive.

beginner.question: The Great Isaiah Scroll, one of the most important Dead Sea Scrolls discoveries, contained a nearly complete copy of the Old Testament book of Isaiah.
beginner.explanation: This is correct. The Great Isaiah Scroll is one of the seven original Dead Sea Scrolls found in 1947. It contains all 66 chapters of Isaiah and is over 2,000 years old, making it the oldest complete copy of any biblical book.

beginner.question: The Old Testament was written over a period of roughly 200 years, and the New Testament was written in approximately 300 years.
beginner.explanation: This is false. The Old Testament was written over roughly 1,000 years (approximately 1400-400 BC), not 200. The New Testament was written in about 50 years (approximately 45-95 AD), not 300.

beginner.question: The New Testament was written over a period of about 500 years, from the time of Jesus until the fall of the Roman Empire.
beginner.explanation: This is false. The New Testament was written in roughly 50 years, from about 45 AD (James or Galatians) to about 95 AD (Revelation by John). The Roman Empire didn't fall until 476 AD.
