beginner.question: The Psalms were written by multiple authors, including David, Asaph, the sons of Korah, Moses, and Solomon, though David contributed the most.
beginner.explanation: This is correct. While David wrote about 73 psalms, other authors include Asaph (12 psalms), the sons of Korah (11 psalms), Solomon (2 psalms), Moses (Psalm 90), and Ethan. Some psalms are anonymous.

beginner.question: The book of Acts describes Paul's dramatic conversion on the road to Damascus, where he went from persecuting Christians to becoming one himself.
beginner.explanation: This is correct. Acts 9 describes how Saul (later Paul) was travelling to Damascus to arrest Christians when a blinding light from heaven and the voice of Jesus stopped him. This event transformed him into Christianity's greatest missionary.

beginner.question: The Bible was originally written in three languages: Hebrew (most of the Old Testament), Aramaic (small portions of the Old Testament), and Greek (the New Testament).
beginner.explanation: This is correct. Hebrew is the primary language of the Old Testament. Aramaic appears in portions of Daniel (2:4-7:28) and Ezra (4:8-6:18; 7:12-26). The entire New Testament was written in Koine Greek.

beginner.question: The Bible was originally written in only one language — Hebrew — and was later translated into Greek and other languages.
beginner.explanation: This is false. The Bible was originally written in three languages: Hebrew (most of the OT), Aramaic (parts of Daniel and Ezra), and Greek (the entire NT). It was not all written in Hebrew.

beginner.question: The 'Pastoral Epistles' — 1 Timothy, 2 Timothy, and Titus — are called 'pastoral' because they were written to individual pastors about how to lead their churches.
beginner.explanation: This is correct. Unlike Paul's other letters which were written to entire congregations, 1 & 2 Timothy and Titus were written to individual church leaders (pastors) with practical advice about church leadership and sound doctrine.

beginner.question: The book of Revelation was written by the apostle John while he was exiled on the island of Patmos in the Aegean Sea.
beginner.explanation: This is correct. Revelation 1:9 states that John was on the island of Patmos 'because of the word of God and the testimony of Jesus.' He was likely exiled there by the Roman authorities during a period of persecution.

beginner.question: The book of Revelation was written by the apostle Paul while he was imprisoned in Rome.
beginner.explanation: This is false. Revelation was written by the apostle John, not Paul, while John was exiled on the island of Patmos. Paul did write several letters from prison in Rome (Ephesians, Philippians, Colossians, Philemon), but Revelation was not one of them.

beginner.question: The Psalms contain more chapters than any other book in the Bible, and Psalms is located roughly in the middle of a printed Bible.
beginner.explanation: This is correct. With 150 chapters, Psalms has more chapters than any other book. Because of its central position and length, if you open most printed Bibles roughly to the middle, you will likely land in or near the Psalms.

beginner.question: The first five books of the Bible are sometimes called 'The Books of Moses' because Moses is traditionally believed to be their author.
beginner.explanation: This is correct. Genesis, Exodus, Leviticus, Numbers, and Deuteronomy are known as the Torah, the Pentateuch, or 'The Books of Moses.' The Bible itself credits Moses with writing them (Deuteronomy 31:9).

beginner.question: There are more letters (epistles) in the New Testament than any other type of book — 21 out of 27 books are letters.
beginner.explanation: This is correct. Of the 27 New Testament books, 21 are letters (epistles): 13 by Paul, plus Hebrews, James, 1 & 2 Peter, 1, 2 & 3 John, and Jude. The remaining 6 are 4 Gospels, Acts, and Revelation.

beginner.question: The four Gospels make up the majority of the New Testament, accounting for more than half of its 27 books.
beginner.explanation: This is false. The four Gospels are only 4 of the 27 New Testament books. The majority of New Testament books are letters (epistles) — 21 out of 27. The Gospels make up less than 15% by book count.

beginner.question: The phrase 'In the beginning God created the heavens and the earth' is the opening line of the entire Bible, found in Genesis 1:1.
beginner.explanation: This is correct. Genesis 1:1 — 'In the beginning God created the heavens and the earth' — is the very first verse of the Bible. It establishes God as the creator of everything that exists.

beginner.question: The word 'Amen,' which is the last word of the Bible in Revelation 22:21, is also used by Jesus throughout the Gospels, often translated as 'truly' or 'verily' when he says 'Truly, truly, I say to you.'
beginner.explanation: This is correct. Jesus frequently used 'Amen' at the beginning of his statements (translated as 'truly' or 'verily'). In John's Gospel, he often doubled it: 'Amen, amen' ('Truly, truly'). The word bookends the entire Bible, appearing from Genesis to Revelation.

beginner.question: According to the book of Genesis, God created the first man and woman and placed them in a beautiful garden as their home. What was the name of this garden where Adam and Eve first lived?
beginner.explanation: God planted the Garden of Eden in the east and placed Adam there to work it and take care of it. It was a perfect, beautiful place with every kind of tree that was pleasant to look at and good for food.

beginner.question: In the Garden of Eden, God told Adam and Eve they could eat from any tree except one special tree. What was the name of the forbidden tree that they were not allowed to eat from?
options: The Tree of the Knowledge of Good and Evil
beginner.explanation: God told Adam he was free to eat from any tree in the garden, but he must not eat from the Tree of the Knowledge of Good and Evil. God warned that eating from it would bring death.

beginner.question: In the Garden of Eden story, a cunning creature tempted Eve to eat the forbidden fruit by telling her she would become like God, knowing good and evil. What kind of animal spoke to Eve and tricked her?
beginner.explanation: The Bible says the serpent was more crafty than any other wild animal God had made. It deceived Eve by questioning God's command and telling her she would not die if she ate the fruit.

beginner.question: After Adam and Eve ate the forbidden fruit in the Garden of Eden, they realized they were naked and felt ashamed. According to Genesis, what did they use to make coverings for themselves?
beginner.explanation: After eating the forbidden fruit, Adam and Eve's eyes were opened and they realized they were naked. They sewed fig leaves together to make coverings for themselves before God found them hiding in the garden.

beginner.question: After Adam and Eve disobeyed God and were expelled from the Garden of Eden, God placed something at the entrance to prevent them from returning to the Tree of Life. What did God place there?
beginner.explanation: God drove Adam and Eve out of the garden and placed cherubim and a flaming sword that flashed back and forth to guard the way to the Tree of Life, ensuring they could not return.

beginner.question: In the book of Genesis, God planted the Garden of Eden and placed two special trees in the middle of it. One was the Tree of the Knowledge of Good and Evil. What was the other special tree called?
beginner.explanation: The two special trees in the middle of the Garden of Eden were the Tree of Life and the Tree of the Knowledge of Good and Evil. After Adam and Eve sinned, God blocked access to the Tree of Life.

beginner.question: The Old Testament prophet Micah predicted that a great ruler of Israel would be born in a small town near Jerusalem. According to this prophecy and the Gospel accounts, in which town was Jesus born?
beginner.explanation: The prophet Micah foretold that out of Bethlehem would come a ruler over Israel. Both Matthew and Luke confirm that Jesus was born in Bethlehem, fulfilling this ancient prophecy.

beginner.question: When Jesus was born in Bethlehem, his mother Mary had no proper place to give birth because there was no room at the inn. Where did Mary lay baby Jesus after he was born?
options: On the floor of the inn
beginner.explanation: Because there was no room for them at the inn, Mary wrapped baby Jesus in cloths and placed him in a manger — a feeding trough for animals. This humble beginning fulfilled the prophecy of a humble Messiah.

beginner.question: King David, the famous shepherd boy who defeated the giant Goliath, grew up in the same town where Jesus would later be born. What is the name of this town, also called 'the City of David'?
beginner.explanation: Bethlehem is called the City of David because King David grew up there as a shepherd boy before he became king of Israel. Centuries later, Jesus was born in the same town, continuing the royal line of David.

beginner.question: According to Luke's Gospel, on the night Jesus was born in Bethlehem, an angel appeared to a group of workers in the nearby fields and told them the good news. What was the occupation of these workers?
beginner.explanation: An angel of the Lord appeared to shepherds who were watching over their flocks at night near Bethlehem. The angel told them the Savior had been born and they would find the baby wrapped in cloths, lying in a manger.

beginner.question: According to Matthew's Gospel, wise men (Magi) from the East followed a star to Bethlehem to visit the newborn king, Jesus. What three gifts did they bring for the baby?
beginner.explanation: The wise men presented baby Jesus with three precious gifts: gold (a gift for a king), frankincense (used in worship, symbolising his divinity), and myrrh (a burial spice, foreshadowing his death).

beginner.question: According to Luke's Gospel, Joseph and Mary traveled to Bethlehem shortly before Jesus was born because the Roman emperor had ordered something that required everyone to return to their ancestral town. What did the emperor order?
beginner.explanation: Caesar Augustus ordered a census of the entire Roman world. Because Joseph was a descendant of King David, he had to travel from Nazareth to David's hometown of Bethlehem to register, bringing Mary with him.

beginner.question: After returning from Egypt, Joseph and Mary settled in a small town in the northern region of Galilee where Jesus would grow up. What was the name of this town where Jesus spent his childhood?
beginner.explanation: Joseph and Mary settled in Nazareth, a small town in Galilee, where Jesus grew up. This is why Jesus was often called 'Jesus of Nazareth' throughout his ministry.

beginner.question: In the Gospel of John, when Philip told Nathanael about Jesus of Nazareth, Nathanael responded with a famous skeptical question about the town where Jesus grew up. What did Nathanael say about Nazareth?
options: 'I have never heard of that place.'
beginner.explanation: Nathanael's sceptical question reveals that Nazareth was a small, insignificant village with no notable reputation. Despite his doubts, Nathanael went with Philip to meet Jesus and became a believer.

beginner.question: Jesus grew up in Nazareth working alongside his earthly father Joseph. According to the Gospels, the people of Nazareth knew Joseph by his trade. What was Joseph's profession?
beginner.explanation: Joseph was a carpenter (or craftsman), and Jesus would have learned this trade growing up. When Jesus later taught in the Nazareth synagogue, the people said, 'Isn't this the carpenter's son?'

beginner.question: The angel Gabriel was sent by God to visit a young woman in Nazareth to deliver an astonishing message — that she would give birth to the Son of God. What was the name of this young woman?
beginner.explanation: God sent the angel Gabriel to Nazareth to a virgin named Mary, who was pledged to be married to Joseph. Gabriel told her she would conceive and give birth to a son named Jesus, who would be called the Son of the Most High.

beginner.question: When Jesus returned to the town of Nazareth where he had grown up, he went to the synagogue on the Sabbath and read from the scroll of Isaiah. How did the people of his hometown respond to his teaching?
options: They crowned him as their king
They rejected him and tried to throw him off a cliff
They built a church in his honour
They asked him to stay and be their rabbi
beginner.explanation: The people of Nazareth were initially amazed, but then became furious when Jesus implied that God's blessings would extend to non-Jews. They drove him out of town and tried to throw him off a cliff, but he walked through the crowd and left.

beginner.question: Because Jesus grew up in the town of Nazareth in the region of Galilee, people often identified him by his hometown. What title was Jesus commonly called based on where he grew up?
beginner.explanation: Throughout the Gospels, Jesus is frequently called 'Jesus of Nazareth.' Even the sign placed on his cross read 'Jesus of Nazareth, King of the Jews,' and his followers were identified with this title.

beginner.question: King Solomon, the son of David, built the first great Temple where the Israelites could worship God. In which city did Solomon build this magnificent Temple?
beginner.explanation: Solomon built the Temple in Jerusalem on Mount Moriah. It took seven years to complete and was one of the most magnificent buildings in the ancient world, becoming the centre of Israelite worship.

beginner.question: According to the Gospels, Jesus was crucified just outside the walls of a major city during the Jewish festival of Passover. In which city was Jesus put on trial, sentenced, and crucified?
beginner.explanation: Jesus was arrested, tried before the Jewish council and the Roman governor Pontius Pilate, and crucified at Golgotha, just outside the walls of Jerusalem. This all happened during the Passover festival.

beginner.question: In the book of Acts, the Holy Spirit came upon the disciples with the sound of a rushing wind and tongues of fire on the day of Pentecost. In which city were the disciples gathered when this happened?
beginner.explanation: The disciples were gathered together in Jerusalem on the day of Pentecost when the Holy Spirit came upon them. This event is considered the birthday of the Christian church, and about 3,000 people believed that day.

beginner.question: When Jesus was twelve years old, his parents took him on a trip for the Passover festival. He stayed behind in the Temple, astonishing the teachers with his understanding. In which city's Temple did young Jesus amaze the teachers?
beginner.explanation: Joseph and Mary took the twelve-year-old Jesus to Jerusalem for the Passover feast. When they started home, they discovered Jesus was missing and found him three days later in the Jerusalem Temple, discussing Scripture with the teachers.

beginner.question: In the Old Testament, King David captured a fortress city from the Jebusites and made it his royal capital. This city later became the most important city in all of Israel's history. What city did David conquer and make his capital?
beginner.explanation: David captured the Jebusite fortress of Zion and renamed it the City of David — this is Jerusalem. It became the political and religious capital of Israel, and Solomon later built the Temple there.

beginner.question: According to the book of Revelation, at the end of time God will create a new heaven and a new earth. A holy city will come down out of heaven from God, beautifully prepared. What is the name of this heavenly city?
beginner.explanation: The book of Revelation describes a vision of the New Jerusalem coming down from heaven. It is described as a bride beautifully dressed for her husband, a place where God will dwell with his people and there will be no more death or sorrow.

beginner.question: In the book of Genesis, Jacob's son Joseph was sold into slavery by his jealous brothers and taken far away from his family. To which country was Joseph taken as a slave?
beginner.explanation: Joseph's brothers sold him to Ishmaelite traders who took him to Egypt. Despite this terrible start, God was with Joseph, and he eventually rose to become the second most powerful person in all of Egypt.

beginner.question: God sent ten terrible plagues upon a nation to convince its ruler, Pharaoh, to release the Israelites from slavery. The plagues included frogs, locusts, darkness, and the death of every firstborn son. Which country suffered these ten plagues?
beginner.explanation: God sent ten plagues upon Egypt because Pharaoh refused to let the Israelites go free. The final plague — the death of every firstborn son — finally convinced Pharaoh to release them, leading to the Exodus.

beginner.question: After the wise men visited baby Jesus in Bethlehem, an angel appeared to Joseph in a dream and warned him that King Herod wanted to kill the child. To which country did Joseph take Mary and baby Jesus to keep them safe?
beginner.explanation: Joseph obeyed the angel's warning and took Mary and Jesus to Egypt to escape King Herod's murderous plan. They stayed in Egypt until Herod died, fulfilling the prophecy: 'Out of Egypt I called my son.'

beginner.question: In the book of Exodus, a baby boy was hidden in a waterproof basket among the reeds of the Nile River to save him from Pharaoh's decree to kill all Hebrew baby boys. A princess found and adopted him. Who was this baby?
beginner.explanation: Baby Moses was placed in a basket on the Nile by his mother to save him from Pharaoh's order to kill all Hebrew baby boys. Pharaoh's own daughter found him, had compassion, and raised him as her own son in the Egyptian palace.

beginner.question: The Israelites lived as slaves in Egypt for many years, forced to make bricks and build cities for Pharaoh. God chose a man to confront Pharaoh and lead the Israelites out of slavery to freedom. Who did God choose?
beginner.explanation: God appeared to Moses in a burning bush and told him to go to Pharaoh and demand the release of the Israelites. Despite his reluctance, Moses obeyed and led the people out of Egypt in the great Exodus.

beginner.question: In the story of Joseph in Egypt, Pharaoh had a troubling dream about seven fat cows being swallowed up by seven skinny cows. Joseph was brought from prison to interpret this dream. What did the dream predict?
options: Seven wars followed by seven years of peace
Seven years of plenty followed by seven years of famine
Seven kings who would rule Egypt
Seven plagues that would strike the land
beginner.explanation: Joseph told Pharaoh that God was revealing the future: Egypt would enjoy seven years of abundant harvests, followed by seven years of severe famine. Joseph advised storing grain during the good years to survive the famine.

beginner.question: After the Israelites escaped from Egypt, God called Moses to the top of a mountain to receive the laws for his people, including the Ten Commandments. On which mountain did Moses receive God's law?
beginner.explanation: God called Moses to the top of Mount Sinai, which was covered in smoke and shook with thunder and lightning. There, God gave Moses the Ten Commandments and other laws for the people of Israel to follow.

beginner.question: When Moses went up Mount Sinai to receive God's law, the Israelites grew impatient waiting for him to come back down. They asked Aaron to make something for them to worship instead of God. What did Aaron make?
beginner.explanation: The Israelites collected their gold jewellery and Aaron melted it down to make a golden calf idol. When Moses came down from the mountain and saw them worshipping it, he was furious and smashed the stone tablets of the law.

beginner.question: The Ten Commandments that God gave to Moses on Mount Sinai were not written on paper or parchment, but on a special material that would endure. What were the Ten Commandments written on?
beginner.explanation: God wrote the Ten Commandments on two tablets of stone with his own finger. When Moses saw the people worshipping the golden calf, he threw down the tablets and broke them. God later gave Moses a second set.

beginner.question: Before Moses climbed Mount Sinai to meet God, the Bible describes dramatic signs that showed God's powerful presence on the mountain. Which of these dramatic events happened at Mount Sinai?
options: A rainbow appeared over the mountain
Thunder, lightning, smoke, and trumpet sounds
beginner.explanation: The Bible describes Mount Sinai covered in smoke because God descended on it in fire. There was thunder and lightning, a thick cloud, and a very loud trumpet blast. The whole mountain trembled violently, terrifying the people below.

beginner.question: Moses spent a long time on top of Mount Sinai receiving God's laws while the people waited below. According to the book of Exodus, how many days and nights did Moses spend on the mountain?
beginner.explanation: Moses stayed on Mount Sinai for forty days and forty nights, receiving God's law. During this extended absence, the impatient Israelites persuaded Aaron to make the golden calf.

beginner.question: Mount Sinai, where Moses received the Ten Commandments, is also known by another name in the Bible. The prophet Elijah also fled to this same mountain when Queen Jezebel threatened to kill him. What is Mount Sinai's other name?
beginner.explanation: Mount Sinai is also called Mount Horeb in the Bible. It was at Horeb that God first spoke to Moses from the burning bush, and later Elijah heard God's gentle whisper there after fleeing Jezebel.

beginner.question: When the Israelites fled Egypt, they found themselves trapped between the Egyptian army behind them and a large body of water ahead. God told Moses to stretch out his hand, and the waters parted. Which body of water did God miraculously divide?
beginner.explanation: God parted the Red Sea so the Israelites could cross on dry ground. This miraculous escape from the Egyptian army is one of the most famous events in the Bible and is celebrated in the Jewish festival of Passover.

beginner.question: After the Israelites safely crossed the Red Sea on dry ground, the Egyptian army chased after them into the parted waters with their chariots and horses. What happened to the Egyptian army?
options: They crossed safely and returned to Egypt
The waters closed over them and they drowned
They surrendered and let the Israelites go
beginner.explanation: After all the Israelites had safely crossed, God caused the walls of water to collapse back into place. The entire Egyptian army — chariots, horses, and soldiers — was swept away and drowned in the Red Sea.

beginner.question: After the Israelites crossed the Red Sea and saw the Egyptian army destroyed, Moses' sister led the women in a joyful celebration with singing and musical instruments. What was her name?
beginner.explanation: Miriam, the sister of Moses and Aaron, took a tambourine and led all the women in dancing and singing praises to God for delivering them from the Egyptians at the Red Sea.

beginner.question: According to the book of Exodus, God used a natural force all night long to push back the waters of the Red Sea and create a path of dry ground for the Israelites to cross. What did God use to part the waters?
beginner.explanation: God sent a strong east wind that blew all night long, driving back the waters of the Red Sea and turning the seabed into dry ground. The waters formed walls on both sides as the Israelites walked through.

beginner.question: Before the crossing of the Red Sea, God guided the Israelites through the wilderness using two miraculous signs — one for daytime and one for nighttime. What were these two guiding signs?
options: A rainbow by day and stars by night
A pillar of cloud by day and a pillar of fire by night
An angel by day and a voice by night
A bright light by day and thunder by night
beginner.explanation: God led the Israelites with a pillar of cloud during the day to guide their way, and a pillar of fire at night to give them light. These pillars never left their place in front of the people throughout their journey.

beginner.question: The dramatic crossing of the Red Sea, where God parted the waters for the Israelites to escape Pharaoh's army, is commemorated in a famous song of victory. Who led the singing of this celebration song?
beginner.explanation: Moses led the Israelites in singing a triumphant song praising God for his power and deliverance. This 'Song of Moses' celebrates God's victory over Pharaoh's army and is recorded in Exodus chapter 15.

beginner.question: In the New Testament, Jesus traveled to a river to be baptized by his cousin John the Baptist, marking the beginning of his public ministry. In which river was Jesus baptized?
beginner.explanation: Jesus was baptized in the Jordan River by John the Baptist. When Jesus came up out of the water, the heavens opened, the Holy Spirit descended on him like a dove, and God's voice said, 'This is my Son, whom I love.'

beginner.question: After Moses died, Joshua led the Israelites across a river to enter the Promised Land. God miraculously stopped the water from flowing so the people could cross on dry ground. Which river did Joshua lead them across?
beginner.explanation: God stopped the flow of the Jordan River so the Israelites could cross on dry ground into the Promised Land. This miracle echoed the earlier crossing of the Red Sea under Moses.

beginner.question: When Jesus was baptized in the Jordan River by John the Baptist, something remarkable descended upon Jesus as he came up out of the water. What came down from heaven and rested on Jesus?
options: The Holy Spirit in the form of a dove
beginner.explanation: As Jesus came up from the water of the Jordan River, the heavens opened and the Holy Spirit descended on him like a dove. A voice from heaven also spoke, saying, 'This is my Son, whom I love; with him I am well pleased.'

beginner.question: In the Old Testament, a Syrian military commander named Naaman suffered from a skin disease called leprosy. The prophet Elisha told him to wash in a specific river seven times to be healed. Which river was it?
beginner.explanation: Elisha told Naaman to wash seven times in the Jordan River. Naaman was initially angry, thinking the rivers of Damascus were better, but when he obeyed and dipped seven times, his skin was completely healed.

beginner.question: When the Israelites crossed the Jordan River under Joshua's leadership, God instructed them to take something from the middle of the dry riverbed as a permanent memorial of this miracle. What did they take?
beginner.explanation: Joshua instructed twelve men — one from each tribe — to take a stone from the middle of the Jordan. These twelve stones were set up as a memorial at their camp in Gilgal, so future generations would remember God's miracle.

beginner.question: The prophet Elijah parted the waters of the Jordan River just before he was taken up to heaven in a dramatic fashion. What did Elijah use to strike the water and part the river?
beginner.explanation: Elijah rolled up his cloak and struck the water of the Jordan, which divided to the right and left. He and Elisha crossed on dry ground. Shortly after, a chariot of fire appeared and Elijah was taken up to heaven in a whirlwind.

beginner.question: When the Israelites entered the Promised Land under Joshua's leadership, the first city they faced had massive walls and was tightly shut up. God told them to march around it for seven days. What city was this?
beginner.explanation: Jericho was the first city the Israelites conquered after crossing the Jordan River into the Promised Land. God gave them an unusual battle plan: march around the city walls for seven days, then shout, and the walls would fall.

beginner.question: Before the Israelites attacked the city of Jericho, Joshua sent two spies to scout the city. A woman who lived in the city wall hid the spies on her roof and helped them escape. What was her name?
beginner.explanation: Rahab hid the Israelite spies under stalks of flax on her roof and helped them escape through her window using a rope. Because of her faith and bravery, Rahab and her entire family were spared when Jericho's walls fell.

beginner.question: God gave the Israelites specific instructions about marching around the city of Jericho. On the seventh and final day, they had to march around the city a special number of times before shouting. How many times did they march around Jericho on the last day?
beginner.explanation: On the seventh day, the Israelites marched around Jericho seven times. On the final circuit, the priests blew their trumpets, the people gave a great shout, and the walls of Jericho collapsed, allowing them to take the city.

beginner.question: As the Israelites marched around the walls of Jericho following God's battle plan, priests carried the Ark of the Covenant and played instruments. What instruments did the priests blow as they marched?
beginner.explanation: Seven priests carried trumpets made from rams' horns and marched ahead of the Ark of the Covenant. They blew these trumpets continuously as the people marched silently around the city walls each day.

beginner.question: In the New Testament, Jesus healed a blind man who was sitting by the roadside begging near the city of Jericho. When the blind man heard that Jesus was passing by, he cried out loudly. What title did the blind man use to call Jesus?
beginner.explanation: The blind man called out 'Jesus, Son of David, have mercy on me!' The title 'Son of David' was a messianic title, showing the man believed Jesus was the promised Messiah descended from King David.

beginner.question: In one of Jesus' most famous parables, a man was attacked by robbers and left half dead on a dangerous road. A priest and a Levite both passed by without helping. This parable is set on the road between Jerusalem and which other city?
beginner.explanation: The Parable of the Good Samaritan takes place on the road from Jerusalem to Jericho, a steep, winding road through the wilderness that was known for being dangerous and plagued by bandits.

beginner.question: In the book of Genesis, people in the ancient world tried to build a tower that would reach the heavens so they could make a name for themselves. God stopped them by confusing their languages. What was this tower called?
beginner.explanation: The Tower of Babel was built in the land of Shinar. The people wanted to make a name for themselves and avoid being scattered. God confused their language so they couldn't understand each other, and they stopped building and scattered across the earth.

beginner.question: In 586 BC, a powerful empire conquered Jerusalem, destroyed Solomon's beautiful Temple, and forced the Jewish people into exile far from their homeland. Which empire carried the Jews into captivity?
beginner.explanation: King Nebuchadnezzar of Babylon besieged Jerusalem, broke through the walls, destroyed the Temple that Solomon had built, and deported most of the Jewish population to Babylon. This exile lasted about seventy years.

beginner.question: While living in exile in Babylon, a faithful Jewish man was thrown into a den of hungry lions as punishment for continuing to pray to God instead of to the king. God shut the lions' mouths and the man survived. Who was he?
beginner.explanation: Daniel was thrown into the lions' den by King Darius because Daniel refused to stop praying to God. An angel shut the mouths of the lions, and the next morning Daniel was found completely unharmed.

beginner.question: Three young Jewish men in Babylon refused to bow down and worship a golden statue that King Nebuchadnezzar had set up. They were thrown into a blazing furnace as punishment but survived. What were their names?
beginner.explanation: Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego were thrown into a fiery furnace heated seven times hotter than normal. The king was astonished to see four figures walking unharmed in the flames — the fourth looked like a divine being.

beginner.question: King Nebuchadnezzar of Babylon had a dream about a giant statue made of different materials — gold, silver, bronze, iron, and clay. He demanded his wise men interpret it. Who was the only person able to explain the dream?
beginner.explanation: Daniel was able to tell the king both the dream and its meaning, which none of the Babylonian wise men could do. God revealed the dream to Daniel in a vision, and Daniel credited God for the interpretation.

beginner.question: According to the book of Genesis, the Tower of Babel was built in the land of Shinar by people who wanted to reach the heavens. What was the main reason the people wanted to build such a tall tower?
options: To worship the sun and stars
To make a name for themselves and avoid being scattered
To build a temple for God
beginner.explanation: The people said, 'Come, let us build ourselves a city, with a tower that reaches to the heavens, so that we may make a name for ourselves; otherwise we will be scattered over the face of the whole earth.' God confused their language to stop them.

beginner.question: God told a prophet to travel to the great and wicked city of Nineveh to warn its people to turn from their evil ways. Instead of obeying, this prophet ran away and boarded a ship heading in the opposite direction. Who was this reluctant prophet?
beginner.explanation: God told Jonah to go to Nineveh, but Jonah didn't want to go because he didn't want God to show mercy to such wicked people. He fled to Joppa and boarded a ship to Tarshish, which was in the opposite direction.

beginner.question: When Jonah finally arrived in Nineveh and delivered God's warning that the city would be destroyed in forty days, the people's response was remarkable. How did the people of Nineveh respond to Jonah's message?
options: They attacked Jonah and threw him out
They repented and turned from their evil ways
They laughed at him and mocked him
beginner.explanation: The entire city of Nineveh repented — from the king down to the common people. They put on sackcloth, fasted, and turned from their evil ways. When God saw their genuine repentance, he had compassion and did not destroy the city.

beginner.question: After Jonah ran away from God's command to go to Nineveh, a terrible storm struck his ship. The sailors threw Jonah into the sea to stop the storm, and God sent a creature to rescue him. What swallowed Jonah?
beginner.explanation: The Bible says God provided a great fish to swallow Jonah. Inside the fish, Jonah prayed to God and repented. After three days and three nights, the fish vomited Jonah onto dry land.

beginner.question: Jonah spent time inside the belly of a great fish after being thrown from the ship. According to the book of Jonah, how long was Jonah inside the great fish before being spit out onto dry land?
beginner.explanation: Jonah was inside the great fish for three days and three nights. Jesus later referenced this event, comparing it to his own death and resurrection: 'As Jonah was three days and three nights in the belly of a huge fish, so the Son of Man will be three days and three nights in the heart of the earth.'

beginner.question: Nineveh, the city God sent Jonah to warn, was the capital of a powerful and feared ancient empire known for its military might and cruelty. Which empire was Nineveh the capital of?
beginner.explanation: Nineveh was the capital of the Assyrian Empire, which was one of the most powerful and feared empires in the ancient world. The Assyrians were known for their brutal military tactics and conquests of neighbouring nations.

beginner.question: After the people of Nineveh repented and God spared the city from destruction, the prophet Jonah had a strong emotional reaction. According to the book of Jonah, how did Jonah feel about God's decision to spare Nineveh?
options: He was overjoyed and praised God
He was angry and wanted the city destroyed
He was relieved and went home peacefully
He was confused and asked God to explain
beginner.explanation: Jonah was actually angry that God showed mercy to Nineveh. He said, 'I knew you are a gracious and compassionate God, slow to anger and abounding in love.' Jonah wanted the wicked city destroyed and was upset when God forgave them.

beginner.question: Jesus called his first disciples while they were working at their jobs near a large lake in northern Israel. Simon Peter and his brother Andrew were casting fishing nets into the water. By which body of water did Jesus call his first disciples?
beginner.explanation: Jesus was walking along the shore of the Sea of Galilee when he saw Simon Peter and Andrew casting their nets. He called out, 'Follow me, and I will make you fishers of men.' They immediately left their nets and followed him.

beginner.question: One night, the disciples were in a boat on the Sea of Galilee when a furious storm arose with waves crashing over the boat. The terrified disciples woke Jesus, who had been sleeping. What did Jesus do to stop the storm?
options: He prayed all night until it stopped
He spoke to the wind and waves, commanding them to be still
He asked the disciples to row to shore
He poured oil on the water to calm it
beginner.explanation: Jesus got up and rebuked the wind and said to the waves, 'Quiet! Be still!' The wind died down completely and the sea became calm. The amazed disciples said, 'Who is this? Even the wind and the waves obey him!'

beginner.question: According to the Gospels, Jesus walked on the water of the Sea of Galilee during a storm to reach his disciples' boat. One disciple asked to walk on water too but began to sink when he became afraid. Which disciple tried to walk on water?
beginner.explanation: Peter called out, 'Lord, if it's you, tell me to come to you on the water.' Jesus said, 'Come.' Peter walked on the water toward Jesus but became frightened by the wind and began to sink. Jesus caught him and said, 'You of little faith, why did you doubt?'

beginner.question: Jesus performed a famous miracle near the Sea of Galilee by feeding a massive crowd with a tiny amount of food. Thousands of people were fed and there were leftovers. How much food did Jesus start with to feed about 5,000 men?
beginner.explanation: A boy had five small barley loaves and two small fish. Jesus took this small lunch, gave thanks, and miraculously multiplied it to feed about 5,000 men plus women and children. Twelve baskets of leftovers were collected.

beginner.question: After his resurrection, Jesus appeared to his disciples on the shore of the Sea of Galilee. They had been fishing all night without catching anything. Jesus told them to cast their net on the other side of the boat. What happened?
options: The net broke from too many fish
They caught a huge number of fish
beginner.explanation: When the disciples obeyed Jesus and cast the net on the right side of the boat, they caught so many fish they couldn't haul the net in. John recognised Jesus and said, 'It is the Lord!' They counted 153 large fish.

beginner.question: The Sea of Galilee is known by several different names in the Bible. In the Gospel of John, it is referred to by a name connected to a major Roman city built on its western shore. What is this other name for the Sea of Galilee?
beginner.explanation: The Sea of Galilee is also called the Sea of Tiberias (after the Roman city of Tiberias on its shore) and the Lake of Gennesaret. It is actually a large freshwater lake, about 13 miles long and 8 miles wide.

beginner.question: In the Old Testament, the extremely salty body of water at the lowest point on Earth's surface is located near where God destroyed the wicked cities of Sodom and Gomorrah. What is this body of water called in the Bible?
beginner.explanation: The Dead Sea is called the 'Salt Sea' in the Old Testament because of its extremely high mineral and salt content. It sits in the Jordan Rift Valley and is the lowest point on the Earth's surface, about 1,400 feet below sea level.

beginner.question: The prophet Ezekiel had an amazing vision of water flowing from the Temple in Jerusalem toward the Dead Sea. According to this prophecy, what miraculous thing would happen when this water reached the Dead Sea?
options: The sea would dry up completely
The water would turn to wine
The salty water would become fresh and fill with fish
The sea would split in two
beginner.explanation: Ezekiel saw a river flowing from the Temple that grew deeper and deeper. When it reached the Dead Sea, the stagnant, salty water would become fresh, swarms of fish would fill it, and fruit trees would grow along its banks.

beginner.question: The Dead Sea is unique among all bodies of water on Earth because of an extreme physical characteristic. The Jordan River flows into it but no river flows out, causing minerals to accumulate. What makes the Dead Sea most famous?
options: It is the deepest lake in the world
It is the largest freshwater lake
It is the lowest point on the Earth's surface
It has the warmest water of any sea
beginner.explanation: The Dead Sea sits at approximately 1,400 feet (430 metres) below sea level, making it the lowest point on the Earth's surface. Its extreme saltiness — nearly ten times saltier than the ocean — means no fish or plants can survive in it.

beginner.question: In the Old Testament, a young shepherd who would later become king hid from the jealous King Saul in the wilderness near the Dead Sea. David found refuge in caves in this desolate region. Where specifically did David hide?
beginner.explanation: David hid in various wilderness areas near the Dead Sea, including the cave of Adullam and the wilderness of En Gedi. In one famous incident at En Gedi, David spared Saul's life when he found the king sleeping in a cave.

beginner.question: The Dead Sea region is famous not only for its biblical history but also for one of the greatest archaeological discoveries of the 20th century — ancient scrolls containing Old Testament texts. What are these scrolls commonly called?
beginner.explanation: The Dead Sea Scrolls were discovered between 1947 and 1956 in caves near Qumran on the northwestern shore of the Dead Sea. They include some of the oldest known copies of Old Testament books and are invaluable for biblical scholarship.

beginner.question: The Jordan River, which is one of the most important rivers in the Bible, flows south through the Jordan Rift Valley. Where does the Jordan River end its journey?
beginner.explanation: The Jordan River begins near Mount Hermon, flows through the Sea of Galilee, and ends at the Dead Sea. Because the Dead Sea has no outlet, water only leaves through evaporation, which has made it extremely salty over thousands of years.

beginner.question: In the Gospel of John, Jesus' close friends Mary and Martha sent word that their brother was very sick. By the time Jesus arrived at their village, the brother had been dead for four days, yet Jesus raised him. In which village did this amazing miracle happen?
beginner.explanation: Bethany was a small village about two miles east of Jerusalem. It was the home of Jesus' dear friends Lazarus, Mary, and Martha. Jesus raised Lazarus from the dead there, one of his greatest miracles.

beginner.question: When Jesus visited the home of Mary and Martha in Bethany, the two sisters reacted very differently. Martha was busy preparing food while Mary sat at Jesus' feet listening to his teaching. What did Jesus say about Mary's choice?
options: He told Mary to go help Martha
He said Mary had chosen the better part
He praised Martha for her hard work
He told both sisters to sit and listen
beginner.explanation: When Martha complained that Mary wasn't helping, Jesus gently told Martha that she was worried and upset about many things, but only one thing was needed. He said Mary had chosen what was better — listening to his teaching — and it would not be taken from her.

beginner.question: In one of Jesus' most powerful miracles, he called a dead man out of a tomb in the village of Bethany. The man had been dead and buried for four days. What was the name of this man whom Jesus raised from the dead?
beginner.explanation: Lazarus was the brother of Mary and Martha, and a close friend of Jesus. Jesus wept at his tomb before commanding, 'Lazarus, come out!' Lazarus walked out of the tomb still wrapped in burial cloths, alive and well.

beginner.question: In Bethany, a woman poured an extremely expensive jar of perfume on Jesus, and some people complained about the waste, saying the perfume could have been sold to help the poor. What did Jesus say about her action?
options: He said she did it to prepare him for his burial
He told her to sell the rest for charity
beginner.explanation: Jesus defended the woman, saying she had done a beautiful thing. He explained that she had anointed his body ahead of time for burial. He also said that wherever the gospel is preached, what she did would be told in memory of her.

beginner.question: The village of Bethany was located very close to Jerusalem, making it a convenient place for Jesus to stay during his visits to the holy city, especially during Passover week. Approximately how far was Bethany from Jerusalem?
beginner.explanation: John's Gospel states that Bethany was about fifteen stadia from Jerusalem, which is roughly two miles (three kilometres). Jesus often stayed in Bethany during his visits to Jerusalem, walking back and forth between the two.

beginner.question: In the village of Bethany, Jesus had dinner at the home of a man who was given a descriptive name in the Gospels, possibly because Jesus had healed him. According to Mark's Gospel, what was this man called?
beginner.explanation: Mark's Gospel records that Jesus was at the home of Simon the Leper in Bethany when a woman anointed him with expensive perfume. Simon may have been a man Jesus had previously healed of leprosy, which is why he retained the name.

beginner.question: On the night before his crucifixion, Jesus went to a garden near the Mount of Olives to pray. He was deeply troubled and asked his disciples to stay awake and pray with him. What was the name of this garden?
beginner.explanation: Gethsemane, meaning 'oil press,' was a garden at the foot of the Mount of Olives where Jesus often went to pray. On this night, Jesus prayed in great anguish, knowing he was about to be arrested, tortured, and crucified.

beginner.question: When Jesus prayed in the Garden of Gethsemane on the night before his crucifixion, he asked God if it was possible to avoid the suffering ahead. Despite his deep anguish, what did Jesus say to show his obedience to God's plan?
options: 'Not my will, but yours be done'
beginner.explanation: Despite his deep sorrow and dread, Jesus surrendered to God's plan by praying, 'Father, if you are willing, take this cup from me; yet not my will, but yours be done.' This prayer shows both his humanity and his perfect obedience.

beginner.question: Jesus took three of his closest disciples deeper into the Garden of Gethsemane to be near him while he prayed. He asked them to stay awake and keep watch, but they kept falling asleep. Which three disciples did Jesus take with him?
beginner.explanation: Peter, James, and John were Jesus' three closest disciples. They were also present at the Transfiguration and the raising of Jairus' daughter. In Gethsemane, despite Jesus' plea to stay awake, they fell asleep three times.

beginner.question: In the Garden of Gethsemane, Judas Iscariot arrived with a crowd of armed soldiers and Temple officials to arrest Jesus. Judas had arranged a signal to identify Jesus to the soldiers. How did Judas identify Jesus?
options: He greeted Jesus with a kiss
He shone a light on Jesus
beginner.explanation: Judas had told the soldiers, 'The one I kiss is the man; arrest him.' He went straight to Jesus and said, 'Rabbi!' and kissed him. This act of betrayal with a sign of friendship has made 'the kiss of Judas' a famous symbol of treachery.

beginner.question: When the soldiers came to arrest Jesus in the Garden of Gethsemane, one of the disciples drew a sword and struck the high priest's servant. Which body part did the disciple cut off?
beginner.explanation: Peter drew his sword and cut off the right ear of Malchus, the high priest's servant. Jesus immediately told Peter to put away his sword and then healed the servant's ear — his final miracle before his arrest.

beginner.question: According to Luke's Gospel, while Jesus was praying in extreme anguish in the Garden of Gethsemane, something unusual happened to his body that showed the intensity of his suffering. What does Luke describe?
options: His sweat became like drops of blood
He collapsed and could not stand
beginner.explanation: Luke, who was a physician, records that Jesus' anguish was so intense that his sweat became like drops of blood falling to the ground. An angel from heaven appeared to strengthen him during this overwhelming moment of suffering.

beginner.question: Jesus was crucified at a place outside the walls of Jerusalem. This place had a name meaning 'Place of the Skull' because of the shape of the rocky hill. What was this crucifixion site called?
beginner.explanation: Golgotha is an Aramaic word meaning 'Place of the Skull.' In Latin, it is translated as 'Calvary.' It was a well-known execution site just outside the city walls of Jerusalem where criminals were publicly crucified.

beginner.question: When Jesus was crucified at Golgotha, he was not executed alone. Two other people were crucified alongside him — one on his right and one on his left. Who were these two individuals?
beginner.explanation: Two criminals were crucified alongside Jesus, one on each side. One criminal mocked Jesus, but the other repented and asked Jesus to remember him. Jesus promised the repentant criminal, 'Today you will be with me in paradise.'

beginner.question: While hanging on the cross at Golgotha, Jesus spoke several memorable statements. In one of his first words from the cross, he asked God to forgive the people who were crucifying him. What did Jesus say?
options: 'Father, forgive them, for they do not know what they are doing'
'Into your hands I commit my spirit'
beginner.explanation: Even while suffering on the cross, Jesus showed extraordinary compassion by praying for his executioners. He asked God to forgive them because they didn't fully understand what they were doing — crucifying the Son of God.

beginner.question: At the moment Jesus died on the cross at Golgotha, an extraordinary event happened inside the Temple in Jerusalem. What dramatic thing occurred in the Temple at the exact moment of Jesus' death?
options: The curtain of the Temple was torn in two from top to bottom
beginner.explanation: The massive curtain that separated the Holy Place from the Most Holy Place — where only the high priest could enter once a year — was torn from top to bottom. This symbolised that through Jesus' death, all people now had direct access to God.

beginner.question: After Jesus died at Golgotha, a wealthy and respected man went to the Roman governor Pontius Pilate and bravely asked for permission to take Jesus' body down from the cross and bury it properly. Who was this man?
beginner.explanation: Joseph of Arimathea was a wealthy member of the Jewish council who had secretly believed in Jesus. He asked Pilate for Jesus' body, wrapped it in clean linen cloth, and placed it in his own new tomb carved out of rock.

beginner.question: At the crucifixion at Golgotha, one of the two criminals hanging beside Jesus repented and asked Jesus to remember him. Jesus responded with a powerful and comforting promise. What did Jesus promise the repentant criminal?
options: 'You will be forgiven after many years'
'Today you will be with me in paradise'
beginner.explanation: Jesus' promise to the dying criminal shows that salvation is available to anyone who turns to him in faith, even at the very last moment. The repentant criminal simply asked, 'Jesus, remember me when you come into your kingdom.'

beginner.question: Jesus performed his very first miracle at a wedding celebration in a small village in Galilee. The hosts had run out of wine, and Jesus miraculously turned water into wine. What was the name of this village?
beginner.explanation: The wedding at Cana in Galilee is where Jesus performed his first miraculous sign. By turning water into wine, he revealed his glory, and his disciples put their faith in him. It marked the beginning of his public ministry of miracles.

beginner.question: At the wedding in Cana where Jesus turned water into wine, someone noticed the wine had run out and brought this problem to Jesus' attention, expecting him to help. Who told Jesus about the wine shortage?
beginner.explanation: Jesus' mother Mary noticed the wine had run out and told Jesus, 'They have no more wine.' Even though Jesus said his time had not yet come, Mary told the servants, 'Do whatever he tells you,' showing her faith in him.

beginner.question: When Jesus turned water into wine at the wedding in Cana, the water was held in large stone jars used for Jewish ceremonial washing. According to John's Gospel, how many stone water jars were there?
beginner.explanation: There were six stone water jars, each holding twenty to thirty gallons. Jesus told the servants to fill them to the brim with water, and when they drew some out, it had turned into the finest wine the master of the banquet had ever tasted.

beginner.question: At the wedding in Cana, when the master of the banquet tasted the water that Jesus had turned into wine, he was surprised and said something to the bridegroom. Why was the master of the banquet surprised?
options: The best wine had been saved for last, which was unusual
There was far too much wine
The wine was a different colour than expected
beginner.explanation: Normally, the best wine was served first, and the cheaper wine was brought out later when guests couldn't tell the difference. The master of the banquet was amazed that the bridegroom had saved the best wine until last.

beginner.question: Jesus performed a second miracle connected to the village of Cana when a royal official from another city begged Jesus to come and heal his dying son. Jesus healed the boy from a distance. Where was the sick son located?
beginner.explanation: The royal official traveled from Capernaum to Cana to find Jesus and beg for his son's healing. Jesus told him, 'Go, your son will live.' The father believed Jesus' word, and when he returned home, he found his son had been healed at the exact moment Jesus spoke.

beginner.question: The miracle at the wedding in Cana, where Jesus turned water into wine, was significant because it was the first sign of Jesus' divine power. According to John's Gospel, what effect did this miracle have on Jesus' disciples?
options: They were confused and doubted him
They asked him to perform more miracles
They were afraid of his power
beginner.explanation: John's Gospel says that by this first miracle at Cana, Jesus 'revealed his glory, and his disciples believed in him.' This miracle was the beginning of Jesus demonstrating his divine power through miraculous signs.

beginner.question: In the Gospel of John, Jesus stopped at a well in the region of Samaria and asked a local woman for a drink of water. This was surprising because Jews and Samaritans normally refused to interact. Whose well was this?
beginner.explanation: Jacob's well was located near the town of Sychar in Samaria. It was the same well that the patriarch Jacob had dug centuries earlier. Jesus sat by this historic well, tired from his journey, when the Samaritan woman arrived.

beginner.question: When Jesus spoke to the Samaritan woman at the well, he told her he could give her something far better than ordinary water — something that would mean she would never be spiritually thirsty again. What did Jesus offer her?
beginner.explanation: Jesus told the woman, 'If you knew the gift of God and who it is that asks you for a drink, you would have asked him and he would have given you living water.' He explained that this living water would become a spring of water welling up to eternal life.

beginner.question: In one of Jesus' most famous parables, a man was robbed, beaten, and left half dead on the road from Jerusalem to Jericho. A priest and a Levite both walked past without helping. Who finally stopped to help the injured man?
beginner.explanation: A Samaritan — someone from a group that Jews looked down on — stopped, bandaged the man's wounds, took him to an inn, and paid for his care. Jesus used this parable to teach that true neighbours show mercy regardless of background.

beginner.question: During his conversation with the Samaritan woman at Jacob's well, Jesus demonstrated supernatural knowledge about her personal life that amazed her. What did Jesus reveal that he knew about her?
options: That she had had five husbands
That she was a Pharisee in disguise
That she had never been to the Temple
beginner.explanation: Jesus told the woman to go call her husband. When she said she had no husband, Jesus replied that she had had five husbands and the man she was currently with was not her husband. This supernatural knowledge convinced her he was a prophet.

beginner.question: In the book of Acts, after persecution scattered Christians from Jerusalem, one of the early church's seven deacons went to Samaria and preached the gospel with great success. Who was this evangelist who brought the good news to Samaria?
beginner.explanation: Philip went to a city in Samaria and proclaimed the Messiah. Crowds paid close attention to his message, and many were healed and delivered. There was great joy in that city as people believed and were baptized.

beginner.question: The region of Samaria was located between two other regions, and Jewish travellers would often take a longer detour just to avoid passing through it because of the hostility between Jews and Samaritans. Which two regions was Samaria situated between?
beginner.explanation: Samaria sat directly between Galilee in the north and Judea in the south. Jews travelling between the two regions would typically cross the Jordan and go around Samaria rather than pass through it, but Jesus deliberately went through Samaria.

beginner.question: In the book of Acts, a man named Saul was traveling to arrest followers of Jesus when a blinding light from heaven struck him down and he heard Jesus' voice. This dramatic conversion happened on the road to which city?
beginner.explanation: Saul was on his way to Damascus carrying letters authorising him to arrest Christians. A bright light from heaven flashed around him, he fell to the ground, and heard Jesus say, 'Saul, Saul, why do you persecute me?'

beginner.question: After Saul was struck blind on the road to Damascus, God sent a disciple living in Damascus to visit Saul, lay hands on him, and restore his sight. What was the name of this brave disciple whom God sent?
beginner.explanation: Ananias was initially afraid because he knew Saul's reputation as a persecutor of Christians. But God told him, 'Go! This man is my chosen instrument.' Ananias obeyed, laid hands on Saul, and Saul's sight was restored.

beginner.question: When Saul was struck down on the road to Damascus by a blinding light from heaven, he heard a voice speaking to him. What were the first words that the voice of Jesus said to Saul?
options: 'Follow me and I will save you'
'Saul, Saul, why do you persecute me?'
beginner.explanation: Jesus identified himself with his persecuted followers by asking Saul why he was persecuting him. When Saul asked, 'Who are you, Lord?', the voice replied, 'I am Jesus, whom you are persecuting.'

beginner.question: After his dramatic conversion on the road to Damascus, Saul became one of the greatest missionaries and writers in the New Testament. He later became known by another name. What was his new name?
beginner.explanation: Saul of Tarsus became known as the apostle Paul. He went on to establish churches throughout the Roman Empire and wrote thirteen letters (epistles) that became books of the New Testament, shaping Christian theology forever.

beginner.question: After his conversion in Damascus, Paul's bold preaching angered many people who plotted to kill him. His fellow believers had to help Paul escape from the city using a creative method. How did Paul escape Damascus?
options: He disguised himself as a Roman soldier
He hid in a merchant's caravan
He was lowered in a basket through an opening in the city wall
He escaped through an underground tunnel
beginner.explanation: Paul's followers lowered him in a large basket through an opening in the city wall under cover of darkness, allowing him to escape the Jews who were watching the city gates day and night to kill him.

beginner.question: Before his conversion on the road to Damascus, Saul had been given official authority to hunt down and arrest Christians. After his encounter with Jesus, Saul was left blind. How long was Saul blind before his sight was restored?
beginner.explanation: Saul was blind for three days after his encounter with Jesus on the Damascus road. During those three days he did not eat or drink anything. Then God sent Ananias to lay hands on him and restore his sight.

beginner.question: The apostle Paul wrote a famous letter to the Christians living in the capital city of the Roman Empire. This important letter, which explains the gospel of salvation by faith, became a book of the New Testament. What is it called?
beginner.explanation: Paul's letter to the Romans is one of the most theologically rich books in the Bible. Written before Paul had visited Rome, it systematically explains the gospel message — that all have sinned, but salvation comes through faith in Jesus Christ.

beginner.question: The apostle Paul traveled to Rome as a prisoner to stand trial before the Roman emperor, as was his right as a Roman citizen. According to the book of Acts, how did Paul travel to Rome for most of the journey?
beginner.explanation: Paul sailed by ship across the Mediterranean Sea to reach Rome. The voyage was dangerous — they encountered a terrible storm that lasted two weeks, and the ship eventually wrecked on the island of Malta before Paul finally reached Rome.

beginner.question: On Paul's dramatic sea voyage to Rome, a terrible storm battered the ship for many days. The crew gave up hope of being saved. Eventually, the ship ran aground and broke apart near the shores of an island. Which island was this?
beginner.explanation: After two terrifying weeks at sea, the ship ran aground and broke apart on the island of Malta. All 276 people on board made it safely to shore, just as Paul had promised based on an angel's message to him.

beginner.question: When Paul finally arrived in Rome as a prisoner, he was not locked in a typical dungeon. Instead, he was given a certain amount of freedom while awaiting his trial. How was Paul kept in Rome?
options: In a prison cell with other criminals
Under house arrest with a soldier guarding him
In a room beneath the Colosseum
Free to roam the city without restriction
beginner.explanation: Paul was allowed to live by himself in rented quarters with a Roman soldier guarding him. Despite being a prisoner, he was able to receive visitors and continued to preach about Jesus to everyone who came to see him for two full years.

beginner.question: While Paul was under house arrest in Rome, he did not waste his time. He wrote several important letters to churches that became books of the New Testament. These are sometimes called the 'Prison Epistles.' Which of these is one of Paul's letters written from Rome?
beginner.explanation: Ephesians is one of Paul's Prison Epistles, written during his house arrest in Rome. The others include Philippians, Colossians, and Philemon. These letters contain some of the most profound teachings about the Christian faith.

beginner.question: According to early Christian tradition, the apostle Paul was not the only prominent apostle who went to Rome. Which other leading apostle is traditionally believed to have traveled to Rome and been martyred there?
beginner.explanation: Early church tradition records that the apostle Peter also went to Rome. Peter's first letter hints at his presence there when he sends greetings from 'she who is in Babylon' — widely understood as a code name for Rome.

beginner.question: According to the book of Acts, after Jesus rose from the dead and spent forty days teaching his disciples, he ascended into heaven from a hill just east of Jerusalem. What is the name of this hill?
beginner.explanation: Jesus ascended into heaven from the Mount of Olives, which is located just east of Jerusalem across the Kidron Valley. After the ascension, the disciples returned to Jerusalem from the Mount of Olives, about a Sabbath day's walk away.

beginner.question: On Palm Sunday, Jesus rode into Jerusalem from the Mount of Olives while crowds waved palm branches and shouted praises. This triumphal entry fulfilled an Old Testament prophecy. What animal did Jesus ride into the city?
beginner.explanation: Jesus rode a donkey into Jerusalem, fulfilling the prophecy of Zechariah: 'See, your king comes to you, gentle and riding on a donkey.' The crowds spread their cloaks and palm branches on the road, shouting 'Hosanna!'

beginner.question: The prophet Zechariah wrote a prophecy about the Mount of Olives and the end times. According to Zechariah, what dramatic event will happen to the Mount of Olives when the Lord stands on it?
options: It will be covered in gold
It will split in two from east to west
It will become the tallest mountain
It will turn into a lake
beginner.explanation: Zechariah prophesied that on that day the Lord's feet will stand on the Mount of Olives, and the mountain will be split in two from east to west, forming a great valley. Half will move north and half will move south.

beginner.question: As Jesus ascended into heaven from the Mount of Olives, the disciples stood staring up at the sky in amazement. Two figures suddenly appeared beside them and spoke. Who were these two figures and what did they say?
options: Moses and Elijah, who blessed the disciples
Two angels, who said Jesus would return the same way he left
Gabriel and Michael, who gave them a new mission
Two prophets, who told them to go to the Temple
beginner.explanation: Two men in white (angels) appeared and told the amazed disciples, 'Why do you stand here looking into the sky? This same Jesus, who has been taken from you into heaven, will come back in the same way you have seen him go into heaven.'

beginner.question: The Garden of Gethsemane, where Jesus prayed in agony on the night before his crucifixion, is traditionally located on a specific part of the Mount of Olives. Where exactly on the Mount of Olives is Gethsemane?
options: In a cave near the summit
beginner.explanation: Gethsemane is located at the base of the Mount of Olives, in the Kidron Valley between the mount and the walls of Jerusalem. The name Gethsemane means 'oil press,' and it was an olive grove where Jesus often went to pray.

beginner.question: In the days before his crucifixion, Jesus sat on the Mount of Olives with his disciples looking across at the Temple in Jerusalem. He gave a long teaching about the future and the end times. What is this famous speech commonly called?
beginner.explanation: The Olivet Discourse is Jesus' extended teaching about the destruction of the Temple, the signs of the end times, and his second coming. It is called the Olivet Discourse because Jesus delivered it while sitting on the Mount of Olives.

beginner.question: The last book of the Bible, Revelation, was written by a follower of Jesus who had been exiled to a small, rocky island because of his faith. What was the name of this island where Revelation was written?
beginner.explanation: The apostle John was exiled to the small island of Patmos in the Aegean Sea because of his testimony about Jesus. While there, he received the dramatic visions of the end times that make up the book of Revelation.

beginner.question: The book of Revelation was written by a man who had been banished to the island of Patmos for preaching about Jesus. According to tradition and the text itself, who was the author of the book of Revelation?
beginner.explanation: The book identifies its author as John, who calls himself a servant of Jesus Christ. Church tradition holds that this was the apostle John, the beloved disciple, who received these visions while exiled on Patmos in his old age.

beginner.question: While exiled on the island of Patmos, the author of Revelation received his first vision on a particular day of the week. He heard a loud voice behind him like a trumpet telling him to write. On which day did this happen?
beginner.explanation: John says, 'On the Lord's Day I was in the Spirit, and I heard behind me a loud voice like a trumpet.' The Lord's Day refers to Sunday, the day Christians gathered to worship because it was the day Jesus rose from the dead.

beginner.question: In the book of Revelation, written from the island of Patmos, John was instructed to write messages to a specific number of churches in Asia Minor (modern-day Turkey). How many churches was he told to write to?
beginner.explanation: John was told to write to seven churches: Ephesus, Smyrna, Pergamum, Thyatira, Sardis, Philadelphia, and Laodicea. Each church received a unique message from Jesus — some with praise, some with warnings, and some with both.

beginner.question: On the island of Patmos, when John heard a loud voice like a trumpet and turned around, he saw an awe-inspiring vision of Jesus. Among the objects surrounding Jesus, what did John see him standing among?
beginner.explanation: John saw Jesus standing among seven golden lampstands, which represented the seven churches. Jesus held seven stars in his right hand (the angels of the churches) and had a sharp double-edged sword coming from his mouth.

beginner.question: The island of Patmos, where the book of Revelation was written, is a small, rocky island that the Romans used as a place of exile for prisoners. In which sea is the island of Patmos located?
beginner.explanation: Patmos is located in the Aegean Sea, off the coast of modern-day Turkey. It is a small, volcanic island about 10 miles long and 6 miles wide. The Romans used it as a penal colony, which is why John was sent there.

beginner.question: According to the book of Acts, the followers of Jesus were given a specific name for the very first time in a city that became a major centre for the early church. In which city were believers first called 'Christians'?
beginner.explanation: Acts 11:26 records that 'the disciples were called Christians first at Antioch.' This city became one of the most important centres of the early Christian church, second only to Jerusalem in significance.

beginner.question: The church in Antioch played a key role in spreading the gospel by sending out the first organised missionaries. Which two men did the Holy Spirit tell the church in Antioch to set apart for missionary work?
beginner.explanation: While the church leaders in Antioch were worshipping and fasting, the Holy Spirit said, 'Set apart for me Barnabas and Saul for the work to which I have called them.' This launched the first missionary journey that spread the gospel across the Roman Empire.

beginner.question: In the book of Acts, after Christians were scattered from Jerusalem by persecution, some believers traveled to Antioch and began preaching. The Jerusalem church sent a trusted man to check on these new believers. Who was sent?
beginner.explanation: The apostles in Jerusalem sent Barnabas to Antioch. When he arrived and saw the evidence of God's grace, he was glad and encouraged them all to remain true to the Lord. Barnabas then went to find Saul and brought him to Antioch.

beginner.question: The ancient city of Antioch, where believers were first called Christians, was one of the largest cities in the Roman Empire. In which modern-day country are the ruins of ancient Antioch located?
beginner.explanation: Ancient Antioch (also called Antioch on the Orontes) was located in what is now southeastern Turkey, near the modern city of Antakya. In its prime, it was the third-largest city in the Roman Empire after Rome and Alexandria.

beginner.question: A serious disagreement arose in the early church at Antioch about whether non-Jewish believers needed to follow all Jewish customs. This controversy led to an important meeting to settle the issue. What is this meeting commonly called?
beginner.explanation: The Council of Jerusalem (Acts 15) was convened to decide whether Gentile (non-Jewish) converts needed to be circumcised and follow the Law of Moses. The council decided that Gentile believers did not need to follow Jewish ceremonial laws.

beginner.question: In his letter to the Galatians, Paul describes a public confrontation he had with another apostle in Antioch. This apostle had been eating with non-Jewish Christians but stopped when certain Jewish Christians arrived. Which apostle did Paul confront?
beginner.explanation: Paul opposed Peter to his face in Antioch because Peter was acting hypocritically. Peter had been freely eating with Gentile believers but withdrew when Jewish Christians arrived, fearing their criticism. Paul publicly called out this inconsistency.

beginner.question: After the great flood that covered the entire earth in the book of Genesis, Noah's ark floated on the waters for many months before finally coming to rest. According to Genesis, where did the ark come to rest?
beginner.explanation: Genesis 8:4 says the ark came to rest on the mountains of Ararat. Mount Ararat is traditionally located in eastern Turkey. After the ark rested there, Noah sent out birds to check whether the floodwaters had receded.

beginner.question: Before the great flood, God told Noah to build an ark to save his family and the animals from the coming destruction. According to Genesis, how long did it rain during the great flood?
beginner.explanation: It rained for forty days and forty nights during the great flood. However, the waters continued to rise even after the rain stopped, and it was many more months before the earth was dry enough for Noah to leave the ark.

beginner.question: After the ark came to rest on the mountains of Ararat, Noah sent out birds to check if the floodwaters had receded. One bird returned with an olive branch in its beak, signalling that land was reappearing. What kind of bird was it?
beginner.explanation: Noah sent out a dove, and it returned with a freshly plucked olive leaf in its beak. This told Noah that the waters had receded enough for trees to be visible. The dove with an olive branch has become a universal symbol of peace.

beginner.question: After Noah and his family left the ark on the mountains of Ararat following the great flood, God made a covenant — a solemn promise — that he would never again destroy the earth with a flood. What sign did God place in the sky?
beginner.explanation: God placed a rainbow in the sky as a sign of his covenant promise to Noah and all living creatures that he would never again destroy the earth with a flood. Every rainbow is meant to be a reminder of this promise.

beginner.question: According to the book of Genesis, Noah brought his family onto the ark before the great flood to save them from the destruction. How many people in total were saved on the ark?
beginner.explanation: Eight people were saved on the ark: Noah, his wife, their three sons (Shem, Ham, and Japheth), and their three sons' wives. These eight people became the ancestors of all humanity after the flood.

beginner.question: God told Noah to bring animals onto the ark to preserve every species through the great flood. For the clean animals (those acceptable for sacrifice), God gave different instructions than for other animals. How many pairs of each clean animal was Noah told to bring?
beginner.explanation: God told Noah to bring seven pairs (male and female) of every clean animal, but only one pair of every unclean animal. The extra clean animals were needed because Noah would offer some as sacrifices to God after the flood.

beginner.question: In the book of Genesis, two neighbouring cities were so incredibly wicked that God decided to completely destroy them with fire and burning sulfur raining down from the sky. What were the names of these two cities?
beginner.explanation: Sodom and Gomorrah were destroyed by God because of the extreme wickedness of their inhabitants. God rained down burning sulfur from the sky, completely destroying both cities and everything growing in the surrounding plain.

beginner.question: Before God destroyed the wicked cities of Sodom and Gomorrah, he sent two angels to rescue a righteous man and his family from the city of Sodom. Who was this man whom the angels urgently told to flee before the destruction?
beginner.explanation: Lot was Abraham's nephew who had chosen to live in the prosperous city of Sodom. The two angels urged Lot to flee with his family before God destroyed the city. They even grabbed Lot by the hand to hurry him out.

beginner.question: As Lot and his family fled the burning destruction of Sodom, the angels warned them not to look back at the city. Lot's wife disobeyed this command and looked back. According to Genesis, what happened to her?
options: She turned into a pillar of salt
She was swept away by fire
beginner.explanation: When Lot's wife looked back at the burning city of Sodom, she was turned into a pillar of salt. Jesus later used her as a warning example, telling his followers, 'Remember Lot's wife!' — don't look back longingly at what God has judged.

beginner.question: Before destroying Sodom, God revealed his plan to Abraham. Abraham bargained with God, asking if he would spare the city if righteous people could be found there. What was the lowest number Abraham negotiated down to?
beginner.explanation: Abraham started by asking if God would spare Sodom for fifty righteous people, then gradually negotiated down to ten. God agreed he would not destroy the city if ten righteous people could be found — but sadly, not even ten were found.

beginner.question: Lot, the man who was rescued from Sodom before its destruction, was related to Abraham, the father of the Jewish nation. What was Lot's family relationship to Abraham?
beginner.explanation: Lot was the son of Abraham's brother Haran, making him Abraham's nephew. Lot had traveled with Abraham from Ur to Canaan. When their flocks grew too large to share the same land, Lot chose the well-watered plain near Sodom.

beginner.question: The destruction of Sodom and Gomorrah was one of the most dramatic displays of God's judgment in the Old Testament. According to the book of Genesis, what exactly did God rain down from heaven to destroy these two wicked cities?
beginner.explanation: God rained down fire and burning sulfur (brimstone) from heaven on Sodom and Gomorrah, completely annihilating both cities, all their inhabitants, and all the vegetation in the surrounding plain. Only Lot and his two daughters survived.

beginner.question: According to the book of Genesis, God planted a beautiful garden called the Garden of Eden 'in the east' as the first home for Adam and Eve.
