beginner.explanation: Genesis 2:8 says, 'Now the Lord God had planted a garden in the east, in Eden; and there he put the man he had formed.' God filled the garden with beautiful trees and rivers for Adam and Eve to enjoy.

beginner.question: In the Garden of Eden story, a sneaky serpent tempted Eve by telling her that eating the forbidden fruit would make her wise like God, knowing good and evil.
beginner.explanation: The serpent told Eve, 'God knows that when you eat from it your eyes will be opened, and you will be like God, knowing good and evil.' This was a deceptive half-truth that led Eve to disobey God's command.

beginner.question: According to Genesis, when Adam and Eve were expelled from the Garden of Eden for eating the forbidden fruit, God placed a mighty angel with a flaming spear at the entrance to guard the way back in.
beginner.explanation: God placed cherubim (a special type of angelic being) and a flaming sword — not a spear — that flashed back and forth to guard the way to the Tree of Life and prevent Adam and Eve from returning to the garden.

beginner.question: After Adam and Eve sinned by eating the forbidden fruit in the Garden of Eden, they felt ashamed of their nakedness and sewed fig leaves together to make coverings for themselves.
beginner.explanation: Genesis 3:7 says their eyes were opened and they realised they were naked. They sewed fig leaves together to make garments for themselves, then hid from God among the trees of the garden.

beginner.question: According to the book of Genesis, the Garden of Eden was watered by two rivers that flowed through it before splitting into smaller streams.
beginner.explanation: Genesis describes a single river flowing from Eden that divided into four rivers, not two. The four rivers were the Pishon, Gihon, Tigris, and Euphrates, watering the land in different directions.

beginner.question: In the Garden of Eden, there were three special trees in the middle of the garden: the Tree of Life, the Tree of the Knowledge of Good and Evil, and the Tree of Wisdom.
beginner.explanation: The Bible mentions only two special trees in the middle of the Garden of Eden: the Tree of Life and the Tree of the Knowledge of Good and Evil. There is no 'Tree of Wisdom' mentioned in the Genesis account.

beginner.question: According to the Gospels, Jesus was born in the town of Bethlehem, fulfilling the Old Testament prophecy from the prophet Micah that a ruler of Israel would come from this small town.
beginner.explanation: Micah 5:2 predicted that a ruler would come from Bethlehem, and both Matthew and Luke confirm Jesus was born there. Joseph and Mary traveled to Bethlehem because of a Roman census, placing them exactly where prophecy said the Messiah would be born.

beginner.question: According to Luke's Gospel, when Jesus was born in Bethlehem, Mary placed the baby in a comfortable cradle that had been prepared for him at the local inn.
beginner.explanation: There was no room for them at the inn, so Mary wrapped baby Jesus in swaddling cloths and laid him in a manger — a feeding trough for animals. Jesus' humble birth in such lowly conditions is a central part of the Christmas story.

beginner.question: In the Old Testament, King David grew up as a shepherd boy in Bethlehem, the same town where Jesus would be born centuries later. This is why Bethlehem is sometimes called 'the City of David.'
beginner.explanation: David was the youngest son of Jesse, a man from Bethlehem. David tended his father's sheep in the fields around Bethlehem before being anointed by the prophet Samuel as the future king of Israel.

beginner.question: According to Matthew's Gospel, the wise men who visited baby Jesus in Bethlehem brought gifts of gold, silver, and precious gems.
beginner.explanation: The wise men brought gold, frankincense, and myrrh — not silver and gems. Gold was a gift for a king, frankincense was used in worship (symbolising divinity), and myrrh was a burial spice (foreshadowing Jesus' sacrificial death).

beginner.question: According to Luke's Gospel, Joseph and Mary traveled from Nazareth to Bethlehem for a Roman census because Joseph was a descendant of King David and Bethlehem was David's ancestral town.
beginner.explanation: Caesar Augustus ordered a census of the entire Roman world, requiring everyone to register in their ancestral town. Because Joseph belonged to the house and line of David, he had to go from Nazareth to Bethlehem.

beginner.question: On the night Jesus was born in Bethlehem, an angel appeared to a group of wealthy merchants camping near the town and told them the good news about the Savior's birth.
beginner.explanation: The angel appeared to shepherds — not wealthy merchants — who were keeping watch over their flocks at night in the fields near Bethlehem. Shepherds were humble, common workers, which fits the theme of Jesus coming for ordinary people.

beginner.question: After returning from Egypt, Joseph and Mary settled in the town of Nazareth in the northern region of Galilee, where Jesus grew up. This is why he was commonly known as 'Jesus of Nazareth.'
beginner.explanation: Matthew 2:23 says Joseph settled in Nazareth so that what was spoken through the prophets would be fulfilled: 'He will be called a Nazarene.' Jesus spent most of his life in this small Galilean town before beginning his public ministry.

beginner.question: When Jesus returned to his hometown of Nazareth and taught in the local synagogue, the people there warmly welcomed him as a great prophet and immediately accepted his teaching.
beginner.explanation: The people of Nazareth actually rejected Jesus. They were initially amazed but then became furious at his teaching. They drove him out of town and tried to throw him off a cliff. Jesus said, 'A prophet is not without honour except in his own town.'

beginner.question: In the Gospel of John, Nathanael expressed doubt about Jesus' hometown by asking, 'Can anything good come from Nazareth?' This shows that Nazareth had a reputation as a small, insignificant village.
beginner.explanation: Nathanael's sceptical question reveals that Nazareth was not a respected or important town. It was a tiny village in Galilee with no mention in the Old Testament. Despite this, Nathanael went to meet Jesus and became a believer.

beginner.question: The town of Nazareth, where Jesus grew up, was located in the southern region of Judea, very close to Jerusalem and the Temple.
beginner.explanation: Nazareth was located in the northern region of Galilee, not in southern Judea. It was a considerable distance from Jerusalem — about 65 miles (100 km) to the north. Galilee was often looked down upon by people in Judea.

beginner.question: Jesus grew up in Nazareth where his earthly father Joseph worked as a carpenter or craftsman. The people of Nazareth knew Jesus as 'the carpenter's son.'
beginner.explanation: Matthew 13:55 records the people of Nazareth saying, 'Isn't this the carpenter's son?' Joseph was a craftsman who worked with wood, and Jesus would have learned this trade as a young man growing up in Nazareth.

beginner.question: The angel Gabriel appeared to a young woman named Elizabeth in the town of Nazareth to announce that she would give birth to the Son of God.
beginner.explanation: The angel Gabriel appeared to Mary, not Elizabeth, in Nazareth. Elizabeth was Mary's relative who was already pregnant with John the Baptist. Gabriel told Mary she would conceive by the Holy Spirit and give birth to Jesus.

beginner.question: King Solomon built the first Temple of God in the city of Jerusalem. The Temple was the centre of Israelite worship and took seven years to construct.
beginner.explanation: Solomon built the Temple on Mount Moriah in Jerusalem, completing it in about seven years. It was one of the most magnificent buildings in the ancient world, and the Ark of the Covenant was placed in its inner sanctum, the Most Holy Place.

beginner.question: According to the Bible, Jerusalem was founded and built from scratch by King Solomon, who created it as the capital of his kingdom.
beginner.explanation: Jerusalem existed long before Solomon. His father King David captured the city from the Jebusites and made it his capital, calling it the City of David. Solomon later enhanced it by building the Temple and palace there.

beginner.question: Jesus was crucified at a place called Golgotha, just outside the city walls of Jerusalem, during the Jewish festival of Passover.
beginner.explanation: Golgotha, meaning 'Place of the Skull,' was located outside the walls of Jerusalem. Jesus was arrested, tried, and crucified during Passover week. The timing was significant — Jesus is called the 'Lamb of God,' connecting his sacrifice to the Passover lamb.

beginner.question: On the day of Pentecost recorded in the book of Acts, the Holy Spirit came upon the disciples in the city of Antioch, and about three thousand people believed and were baptized.
beginner.explanation: The events of Pentecost took place in Jerusalem, not Antioch. The disciples were gathered in Jerusalem when the Holy Spirit came upon them with the sound of a rushing wind and tongues of fire. About 3,000 people believed that day in Jerusalem.

beginner.question: When Jesus was twelve years old, his parents took him to Jerusalem for the Passover festival. On the way home, they realised he was missing and found him in the Temple, discussing Scripture with the teachers.
beginner.explanation: Joseph and Mary traveled a whole day before realising Jesus wasn't with their group. They returned to Jerusalem and found him in the Temple, sitting among the teachers, listening and asking questions. Everyone was amazed at his understanding.

beginner.question: In the book of Revelation, the holy city called the New Jerusalem will be built by human hands on Earth before Jesus returns, as a place prepared for God's people.
beginner.explanation: The New Jerusalem is not built by humans — it comes down out of heaven from God, already prepared. Revelation 21:2 says John saw 'the Holy City, the new Jerusalem, coming down out of heaven from God, prepared as a bride beautifully dressed for her husband.'

beginner.question: In the book of Genesis, Jacob's son Joseph was sold into slavery by his jealous brothers and taken to Egypt, where he eventually rose to become the second most powerful person in the land.
beginner.explanation: Joseph's brothers sold him to traders heading to Egypt. Despite being a slave and then a prisoner, God was with Joseph. Pharaoh eventually appointed him as governor over all Egypt because of his God-given ability to interpret dreams.

beginner.question: According to the book of Exodus, God sent five plagues upon the land of Egypt to persuade Pharaoh to release the Israelites from slavery.
beginner.explanation: God sent ten plagues, not five. The plagues included water turning to blood, frogs, gnats, flies, livestock disease, boils, hail, locusts, darkness, and the final plague — the death of every firstborn son — which finally convinced Pharaoh to let the Israelites go.

beginner.question: After the wise men visited baby Jesus in Bethlehem, an angel warned Joseph in a dream to flee to Egypt because King Herod wanted to kill the child. Joseph obeyed and took Mary and Jesus there.
beginner.explanation: Joseph immediately got up in the middle of the night and took Mary and baby Jesus to Egypt to escape Herod's murderous plan. They stayed in Egypt until Herod died, fulfilling the prophecy from Hosea: 'Out of Egypt I called my son.'

beginner.question: In the book of Exodus, baby Moses was placed in a waterproof basket on the Nile River in Egypt to save him from Pharaoh's soldiers. He was found and adopted by Pharaoh's wife, the queen of Egypt.
beginner.explanation: Moses was found and adopted by Pharaoh's daughter, not Pharaoh's wife. Exodus 2:5-6 says, 'Pharaoh's daughter went down to the Nile to bathe... She saw the basket among the reeds and sent her female slave to get it. She opened it and saw the baby.'

beginner.question: According to the book of Exodus, the Israelites were slaves in Egypt for about fifty years before God sent Moses to lead them to freedom.
beginner.explanation: The Israelites were in Egypt for about 430 years, not fifty. During much of that time they were enslaved and oppressed. God heard their cries and sent Moses to demand their release from Pharaoh.

beginner.question: In the story of Joseph in Egypt, God gave Joseph the ability to interpret Pharaoh's dreams. Joseph correctly predicted that seven years of good harvests would be followed by seven years of terrible famine.
beginner.explanation: Pharaoh dreamed of seven fat cows eaten by seven thin cows, and seven healthy ears of grain swallowed by seven thin ears. Joseph explained that both dreams meant the same thing: seven years of abundance followed by seven years of famine.

beginner.question: According to the book of Exodus, God called Moses to the top of Mount Sinai, where he gave him the Ten Commandments — the foundational laws for the people of Israel.
beginner.explanation: God descended on Mount Sinai in fire, smoke, and thunder. He spoke the Ten Commandments to the people and then called Moses to the top of the mountain to receive the commandments written on two stone tablets.

beginner.question: When Moses went up Mount Sinai to receive God's law, the Israelites waited patiently and faithfully at the bottom of the mountain, praying and fasting until he returned.
beginner.explanation: The Israelites did not wait patiently. They grew impatient and asked Aaron to make them a god to worship. Aaron collected their gold jewellery and made a golden calf idol, which the people worshipped while Moses was on the mountain.

beginner.question: The Ten Commandments that God gave to Moses on Mount Sinai were written on two tablets of stone. When Moses came down and saw the people worshipping a golden calf, he threw the tablets and smashed them in anger.
beginner.explanation: Moses was so furious at the Israelites' idol worship that he threw down the stone tablets, breaking them at the foot of the mountain. Later, God told Moses to chisel out two new tablets, and God wrote the commandments again.

beginner.question: According to the book of Exodus, Moses spent ten days and ten nights on top of Mount Sinai receiving God's laws before returning to the Israelites.
beginner.explanation: Moses spent forty days and forty nights on Mount Sinai, not ten. This extended time on the mountain is what caused the Israelites to become impatient and build the golden calf while waiting for him to come back.

beginner.question: When God descended on Mount Sinai, the Bible describes dramatic signs of his presence, including thunder, lightning, a thick cloud of smoke, and the sound of a very loud trumpet.
beginner.explanation: Exodus 19:16-18 describes how Mount Sinai was covered in smoke because God descended on it in fire. Thunder, lightning, and a loud trumpet blast terrified the people, and the whole mountain trembled violently.

beginner.question: Mount Sinai, where Moses received the Ten Commandments, is also called Mount Horeb in the Bible. God first spoke to Moses at this mountain from within a burning bush.
beginner.explanation: Exodus 3:1 says Moses was tending sheep near 'Horeb, the mountain of God' when he saw the burning bush. Mount Sinai and Mount Horeb are the same location referred to by two different names throughout the Old Testament.

beginner.question: When the Israelites fled Egypt and were trapped between the Egyptian army and the Red Sea, God told Moses to raise his staff over the water. The sea parted, and the Israelites crossed on dry ground.
beginner.explanation: God told Moses to stretch out his hand over the sea. A strong east wind blew all night, dividing the waters and creating dry ground. The Israelites passed between walls of water on their right and left.

beginner.question: After the Israelites crossed the Red Sea, the Egyptian army also successfully crossed through the parted waters and continued to chase them into the wilderness.
beginner.explanation: The Egyptian army did chase the Israelites into the parted sea, but they did not make it across. God caused the waters to collapse back, and the entire Egyptian army — chariots, horses, and soldiers — was swept away and drowned.

beginner.question: According to the book of Exodus, God sent a strong east wind that blew all night long to push back the waters of the Red Sea, creating a path of dry ground for the Israelites.
beginner.explanation: Exodus 14:21 specifically says God drove the sea back with a strong east wind all that night and turned it into dry land. The waters were divided, forming walls on both sides of the path.

beginner.question: According to Exodus, God led the Israelites through the wilderness with a pillar of fire by day and a pillar of cloud by night to guide and protect them.
beginner.explanation: It was actually the other way around: God led them with a pillar of cloud by day to guide their way, and a pillar of fire by night to give them light. The pillar of cloud provided daytime guidance, and the fire provided nighttime illumination.

beginner.question: After safely crossing the Red Sea, Moses' sister Miriam led the women of Israel in singing and dancing with tambourines, praising God for his victory over Pharaoh's army.
beginner.explanation: Miriam, identified as a prophetess and the sister of Aaron, took a tambourine and led all the women in dancing and singing: 'Sing to the Lord, for he is highly exalted. Both horse and driver he has hurled into the sea.'

beginner.question: The dramatic parting and crossing of the Red Sea took place during the Israelites' escape from slavery in Babylon.
beginner.explanation: The Red Sea crossing happened during the Israelites' escape from Egypt, not Babylon. The Babylonian exile occurred centuries later when Nebuchadnezzar conquered Jerusalem. The Exodus from Egypt is the foundational story of Israel's deliverance.

beginner.question: According to the Gospels, Jesus was baptized in the Jordan River by John the Baptist. When Jesus came out of the water, the heavens opened and the Holy Spirit descended on him like a dove.
beginner.explanation: Jesus' baptism in the Jordan River marked the beginning of his public ministry. The Holy Spirit descended like a dove, and God's voice from heaven declared, 'This is my Son, whom I love; with him I am well pleased.'

beginner.question: When the Israelites crossed the Jordan River into the Promised Land under Joshua's leadership, they had to swim across because the river was too deep and wide to wade through.
beginner.explanation: God miraculously stopped the Jordan River from flowing. When the priests carrying the Ark of the Covenant stepped into the water, the river stopped in a heap, and the people crossed on completely dry ground — similar to the Red Sea crossing.

beginner.question: After the Israelites miraculously crossed the Jordan River on dry ground, Joshua instructed twelve men to take twelve stones from the middle of the riverbed as a memorial of what God had done.
beginner.explanation: One man from each of the twelve tribes of Israel carried a stone from the Jordan. These stones were set up at Gilgal as a permanent memorial so future generations would know that God dried up the Jordan for Israel to cross.

beginner.question: In the Old Testament, the Syrian commander Naaman suffered from leprosy and was told by the prophet Elisha to wash three times in the Jordan River to be healed. After obeying, Naaman was completely cured.
beginner.explanation: Elisha told Naaman to wash seven times in the Jordan River, not three. Naaman was initially reluctant, but when he obeyed and dipped in the Jordan seven times, his skin was restored and became clean like that of a young boy.

beginner.question: John the Baptist chose to baptise people in the Jordan River because the Old Testament said it was the holiest river in the world, more sacred than any other body of water.
beginner.explanation: The Bible does not describe the Jordan as the holiest river. John baptised there because of its practical accessibility and its deep connection to Israel's history — especially the crossing into the Promised Land — making it symbolically fitting for a new beginning.

beginner.question: The prophet Elijah parted the waters of the Jordan River by striking it with his cloak just before he was taken up to heaven in a chariot of fire and a whirlwind.
beginner.explanation: Elijah rolled up his cloak and struck the Jordan, and the water divided to the right and left. After crossing on dry ground with Elisha, a chariot of fire and horses appeared, and Elijah was taken up to heaven in a whirlwind.

beginner.question: According to the book of Joshua, the first city the Israelites conquered after crossing the Jordan River into the Promised Land was Jericho. God told them to march around its walls for seven days.
beginner.explanation: Jericho was tightly shut up because of the Israelites. God told Joshua the city was given to them — they had to march around it once a day for six days, then seven times on the seventh day. When they shouted, the walls collapsed.

beginner.question: When the Israelites attacked Jericho, they broke through the city walls using battering rams and siege ladders, just like other ancient armies of that time period.
beginner.explanation: The Israelites used no military equipment at all. God told them to simply march around the city, blow trumpets, and shout. On the seventh day, the walls of Jericho collapsed supernaturally — it was entirely God's power, not human weapons.

beginner.question: Before the Israelites attacked Jericho, Joshua sent two spies into the city. A woman named Rahab hid the spies on her roof and helped them escape because she believed in the God of Israel.
beginner.explanation: Rahab told the spies she had heard about God parting the Red Sea and knew the Lord had given the land to Israel. She hid the spies under flax on her roof and helped them escape through her window with a rope.

beginner.question: In the New Testament, Jesus passed through the city of Jericho but refused to enter it because of its sinful history from Old Testament times.
beginner.explanation: Jesus did not avoid Jericho — he entered it and ministered there. In Jericho, Jesus healed a blind beggar and visited the home of Zacchaeus, a tax collector who climbed a tree to see him. Jesus said, 'The Son of Man came to seek and save the lost.'

beginner.question: When the walls of Jericho fell, every single person in the city was destroyed with no exceptions — Rahab and her family were killed along with everyone else because they were not Israelites.
beginner.explanation: Rahab and her entire family were spared because she had hidden the Israelite spies and showed faith in God. The spies kept their promise to her, and Joshua ordered them to bring out Rahab and her relatives. She even appears in Jesus' genealogy in Matthew 1:5.

beginner.question: Jesus' famous Parable of the Good Samaritan is set on the road between Jerusalem and Jericho, a steep, winding road through the wilderness known for being dangerous and plagued by bandits.
beginner.explanation: The road from Jerusalem to Jericho descended about 3,000 feet over roughly 17 miles through rugged, desolate terrain. It was notorious for robbers hiding along the route, which made the parable's setting very realistic for Jesus' audience.

beginner.question: According to the book of Genesis, people in the land of Shinar tried to build the Tower of Babel to reach heaven. God confused their languages so they could no longer understand each other and they scattered across the earth.
beginner.explanation: The people said, 'Let us build a tower that reaches to the heavens, so we may make a name for ourselves.' God came down, confused their language, and scattered them. The place was called Babel because God confused (Hebrew: 'balal') the language there.

beginner.question: When the Babylonians conquered Jerusalem and destroyed Solomon's Temple in 586 BC, they kindly allowed all the Jewish people to stay in their homeland and continue worshipping God freely.
beginner.explanation: The Babylonians did not treat the Jews kindly. King Nebuchadnezzar's army destroyed Jerusalem and the Temple, killed many people, and forcibly deported most of the surviving Jewish population to Babylon, where they lived in exile for about seventy years.

beginner.question: While living in exile in Babylon, the prophet Daniel was thrown into a den of lions for refusing to stop praying to God. An angel shut the lions' mouths, and Daniel survived the entire night unharmed.
beginner.explanation: King Darius was tricked into signing a decree that anyone who prayed to anyone other than the king would be thrown to the lions. Daniel continued praying to God three times daily. God sent an angel to protect him, and the king was overjoyed to find Daniel alive.

beginner.question: The Tower of Babel described in Genesis was successfully completed, and it stood as the tallest structure in the ancient world for many centuries before eventually crumbling.
beginner.explanation: The Tower of Babel was never completed. God interrupted the construction by confusing the people's language so they couldn't communicate with each other. They stopped building and scattered across the earth, leaving the tower unfinished.

beginner.question: Three young Jewish men in Babylon — Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego — were thrown into a fiery furnace for refusing to worship King Nebuchadnezzar's golden statue, but they survived without any harm.
beginner.explanation: The furnace was heated seven times hotter than normal, yet the three men walked around unharmed inside it. The king saw a fourth figure in the flames who looked like a divine being. The men emerged without even the smell of smoke on their clothes.

beginner.question: King Nebuchadnezzar of Babylon was so humble and devout that he worshipped God faithfully throughout his entire reign, never straying from the true faith.
beginner.explanation: Nebuchadnezzar was proud and arrogant. He built a golden idol for people to worship and was struck by God with a period of madness where he lived like a wild animal, eating grass. Only after being humbled did he finally acknowledge God as the true ruler.

beginner.question: God commanded the prophet Jonah to go to the great city of Nineveh to warn its people to repent. Instead of obeying, Jonah ran away and boarded a ship sailing in the opposite direction.
beginner.explanation: God told Jonah to go east to Nineveh, but Jonah went west to Joppa and boarded a ship heading to Tarshish — as far from Nineveh as possible. Jonah didn't want the wicked people of Nineveh to receive God's mercy.

beginner.question: When Jonah was swallowed by a great fish after being thrown from the ship during a storm, he remained inside the fish for seven days and seven nights before being spit out onto dry land.
beginner.explanation: Jonah was inside the great fish for three days and three nights, not seven. Jesus later connected this to his own death and resurrection, saying, 'As Jonah was three days and three nights in the belly of a huge fish, so the Son of Man will be three days in the heart of the earth.'

beginner.question: After Jonah finally went to Nineveh and preached God's warning, the entire city — from the king to the common people — repented, put on sackcloth, and turned from their evil ways. God saw their repentance and spared the city.
beginner.explanation: Nineveh's repentance was remarkable. The king himself put on sackcloth and sat in ashes, and issued a decree for everyone — even the animals — to fast. When God saw that they turned from their evil ways, he relented from the disaster he had planned.

beginner.question: After the people of Nineveh repented and God spared the city from destruction, the prophet Jonah was overjoyed and praised God for his great mercy and compassion.
beginner.explanation: Jonah was actually furious that God showed mercy to Nineveh. He said he'd rather die than see the city spared. Jonah complained, 'I knew you are a gracious and compassionate God, slow to anger and abounding in love' — as if this was a bad thing.

beginner.question: Nineveh was the capital city of the ancient Assyrian Empire, which was known for its great military power and cruelty. The Bible says the city was so large that it took three days to walk across it.
beginner.explanation: Nineveh was one of the largest cities in the ancient world and served as the capital of Assyria. Jonah 3:3 describes it as 'a very large city; it took three days to go through it,' giving a sense of its enormous size.

beginner.question: While inside the belly of the great fish, Jonah refused to pray and remained stubbornly silent until the fish spit him out onto dry land.
beginner.explanation: Jonah actually prayed earnestly from inside the fish. He cried out to God from the depths of the sea, thanked God for rescuing him, and promised to fulfil his vows. The entire second chapter of Jonah is his prayer from inside the fish.

beginner.question: Jesus called his first disciples — Simon Peter and his brother Andrew — while they were fishing in the Sea of Galilee. He told them, 'Follow me, and I will make you fishers of men.'
beginner.explanation: Jesus was walking along the shore of the Sea of Galilee when he saw Simon and Andrew casting a net into the lake. He called them to follow him and promised to make them fishers of people. They immediately left their nets and followed him.

beginner.question: When a terrible storm struck the Sea of Galilee while Jesus and his disciples were in a boat, Jesus calmed the storm by praying quietly for several hours until the wind and waves gradually died down.
beginner.explanation: Jesus did not pray for hours. He simply stood up and spoke directly to the wind and waves, commanding, 'Quiet! Be still!' The storm stopped immediately and completely. The amazed disciples asked, 'Who is this? Even the wind and waves obey him!'

beginner.question: According to Matthew's Gospel, the disciple Peter briefly walked on the water of the Sea of Galilee toward Jesus during a storm, but he began to sink when he became afraid and took his eyes off Jesus.
beginner.explanation: Peter called out, 'Lord, if it's you, tell me to come to you on the water.' Jesus said, 'Come.' Peter walked on the water, but when he noticed the strong wind, he was afraid and began to sink. He cried out, 'Lord, save me!'

beginner.question: The Sea of Galilee is actually a large saltwater sea connected to the Mediterranean, similar to the Dead Sea in its extreme salt content.
beginner.explanation: The Sea of Galilee is actually a freshwater lake, not a saltwater sea. It is fed by the Jordan River and is full of fish. Its fresh water made it the centre of a thriving fishing industry in Jesus' time, unlike the lifeless Dead Sea.

beginner.question: Jesus performed the miracle of feeding about 5,000 men (plus women and children) with just five loaves of bread and two small fish near the Sea of Galilee. Twelve baskets of leftovers were collected.
beginner.explanation: A boy offered his small lunch of five barley loaves and two fish. Jesus gave thanks, broke the food, and the disciples distributed it to the huge crowd. Everyone ate until they were satisfied, and twelve baskets of leftover pieces were gathered.

beginner.question: After his resurrection, Jesus appeared to his disciples on the shore of the Sea of Galilee (also called Sea of Tiberias) and cooked them breakfast over a charcoal fire.
beginner.explanation: John 21 describes how Jesus appeared at the Sea of Tiberias after his resurrection. He had prepared a charcoal fire with fish and bread on it. He invited the disciples, 'Come and have breakfast.' During this meal, he restored Peter.

beginner.question: The Dead Sea, located in the Jordan Rift Valley, is the lowest point on Earth's surface. In the Bible, it is referred to as the 'Salt Sea' because of its extremely high salt content.
beginner.explanation: The Dead Sea sits about 1,400 feet below sea level and has a salt concentration nearly ten times that of the ocean. The Bible calls it the Salt Sea in several passages, including Genesis 14:3 and Numbers 34:12.

beginner.question: In the Bible, the Dead Sea is described as a thriving fishing area where several of Jesus' disciples worked as fishermen to earn their living.
beginner.explanation: The Dead Sea is far too salty for any fish to survive — it is essentially lifeless, which is why it's called the 'Dead' Sea. Jesus' fishermen disciples worked at the Sea of Galilee, which is a freshwater lake teeming with fish.

beginner.question: The Jordan River flows south through the Jordan Valley and empties into the Dead Sea. Because the Dead Sea has no outlet, water only leaves through evaporation, which has concentrated its salt over time.
beginner.explanation: The Jordan River is the Dead Sea's main water source, but no rivers flow out of it. Water can only escape through evaporation, leaving behind all the dissolved minerals and salts. This process over millennia has made it one of the saltiest bodies of water on Earth.

beginner.question: The prophet Ezekiel had a vision of water flowing from the Temple in Jerusalem to the Dead Sea, and in the vision the Dead Sea's salty water was healed and became fresh, filling with fish.
beginner.explanation: In Ezekiel's vision, a river flowed from the Temple that grew deeper as it traveled. When it reached the Dead Sea, the stagnant water became fresh, swarms of fish filled it, and fruit trees grew along its banks — a powerful image of God's restoration.

beginner.question: The ancient cities of Sodom and Gomorrah, which God destroyed with fire and sulfur, were located on the shores of the Sea of Galilee in northern Israel.
beginner.explanation: Sodom and Gomorrah were located near the Dead Sea region in the southern Jordan Valley, not the Sea of Galilee. Genesis describes Lot looking out over the well-watered plain of the Jordan near the Dead Sea when he chose to live in Sodom.

beginner.question: The famous Dead Sea Scrolls, discovered in caves near the Dead Sea, contain some of the oldest known copies of Old Testament books and are enormously important for biblical scholarship.
beginner.explanation: The Dead Sea Scrolls were found between 1947 and 1956 in caves near Qumran on the Dead Sea's northwestern shore. They include manuscripts of every Old Testament book except Esther, dating back to around 250-100 BC — over a thousand years older than previously known copies.

beginner.question: Bethany, where Jesus raised Lazarus from the dead, was a small village about two miles east of Jerusalem. Jesus often stayed there during his visits to the holy city.
beginner.explanation: John 11:18 says Bethany was about fifteen stadia (roughly two miles) from Jerusalem. Jesus had close friends there — Lazarus, Mary, and Martha — and frequently stayed at their home, especially during Passover week.

beginner.question: When Jesus arrived in Bethany after Lazarus had died, both sisters Martha and Mary calmly accepted the situation because they already knew Jesus would raise their brother from the dead.
beginner.explanation: Martha and Mary were grieving deeply. Martha went out to meet Jesus and said, 'Lord, if you had been here, my brother would not have died.' Mary fell at Jesus' feet weeping. Jesus himself was deeply moved and wept at the tomb.

beginner.question: In one of Jesus' greatest miracles, he raised his friend Lazarus from the dead in the village of Bethany. Lazarus had been buried in a tomb for four days before Jesus called him back to life.
beginner.explanation: Jesus went to the sealed tomb and commanded, 'Lazarus, come out!' The dead man walked out, still wrapped in burial cloths. This miracle was so powerful that many Jews who witnessed it believed in Jesus, but it also made the religious leaders want to kill him.

beginner.question: The village of Bethany was located far from Jerusalem, about a full day's journey away in the Galilean countryside to the north.
beginner.explanation: Bethany was not far from Jerusalem at all — it was only about two miles (three kilometres) away, on the eastern slope of the Mount of Olives. This short distance made it a very convenient place for Jesus to stay during visits to Jerusalem.

beginner.question: When Jesus visited the home of Mary and Martha in Bethany, Martha was busy with preparations while Mary sat at Jesus' feet listening to him teach. Jesus told Martha that Mary had chosen 'the better part.'
beginner.explanation: Martha was distracted by all the preparations and asked Jesus to tell Mary to help her. Jesus gently replied, 'Martha, Martha, you are worried and upset about many things, but few things are needed. Mary has chosen what is better, and it will not be taken away from her.'

beginner.question: In Bethany, Mary anointed Jesus with expensive perfume and wiped his feet with her hair. Jesus said she had done this to prepare his body for his coming burial.
beginner.explanation: Mary poured a pound of expensive nard perfume on Jesus' feet, filling the house with fragrance. When Judas objected to the 'waste,' Jesus defended her, saying, 'Leave her alone. She bought it so that she might keep it for the day of my burial.'

beginner.question: On the night before his crucifixion, Jesus went to a garden called Gethsemane on the Mount of Olives to pray. He was so deeply troubled that he told his disciples, 'My soul is overwhelmed with sorrow to the point of death.'
beginner.explanation: Jesus knew the cross was coming and was in intense anguish. He took Peter, James, and John deeper into the garden and told them his soul was overwhelmed with sorrow. He fell to the ground and prayed for God to take the cup of suffering away, if possible.

beginner.question: When Jesus asked his disciples Peter, James, and John to stay awake and pray with him in the Garden of Gethsemane, they faithfully stayed awake the entire time, supporting Jesus through his agony.
beginner.explanation: Despite Jesus' plea, the three disciples fell asleep not once but three times. Jesus found them sleeping each time and said to Peter, 'Couldn't you men keep watch with me for one hour?' Their sleepiness contrasts with Jesus' intense anguish.

beginner.question: In the Garden of Gethsemane, Judas Iscariot identified Jesus to the arresting soldiers by greeting him with a kiss — a pre-arranged signal that has become a famous symbol of betrayal.
beginner.explanation: Judas had told the soldiers, 'The one I kiss is the man; arrest him.' He went directly to Jesus, said 'Rabbi!' and kissed him. Jesus responded, 'Do what you came for, friend,' before the soldiers seized him.

beginner.question: The Garden of Gethsemane, where Jesus was arrested, was located inside the city walls of Jerusalem, right next to the Temple, making it a very public and well-guarded area.
beginner.explanation: Gethsemane was located outside the city walls, at the foot of the Mount of Olives, across the Kidron Valley from Jerusalem. It was an olive grove in a relatively secluded area, not a public place near the Temple.

beginner.question: When soldiers came to arrest Jesus in Gethsemane, Peter drew a sword and cut off the right ear of the high priest's servant named Malchus. Jesus immediately healed the servant's ear.
beginner.explanation: John identifies Peter as the one who drew the sword and Malchus as the servant. Jesus told Peter, 'Put your sword away!' and touched the servant's ear, healing it. This was Jesus' final miracle before his arrest — an act of compassion toward his enemy.

beginner.question: According to Luke's Gospel, while Jesus prayed in anguish in the Garden of Gethsemane, an angel appeared from heaven to strengthen him, and his sweat became like drops of blood falling to the ground.
beginner.explanation: Luke, who was a physician, records this unique detail. The intensity of Jesus' anguish was so extreme that his sweat fell to the ground like drops of blood. The medical condition of sweating blood (hematidrosis) can occur under extreme stress.

beginner.question: Jesus was crucified at a place called Golgotha, which means 'Place of the Skull.' The Latin translation of this name is 'Calvary.' The site was located just outside the walls of Jerusalem.
beginner.explanation: Golgotha comes from the Aramaic word for 'skull,' while Calvary comes from the Latin 'calvaria.' Executions were carried out outside city walls as a public deterrent. The letter to the Hebrews confirms Jesus suffered 'outside the city gate.'

beginner.question: Jesus was crucified all alone at Golgotha, with no other prisoners being executed that day, so that all attention would be on him.
beginner.explanation: Two criminals were crucified alongside Jesus — one on his right and one on his left. One criminal mocked Jesus, but the other repented and asked Jesus to remember him when he came into his kingdom. Jesus promised him paradise that very day.

beginner.question: While hanging on the cross at Golgotha, Jesus prayed for the people who crucified him, saying, 'Father, forgive them, for they do not know what they are doing.'
beginner.explanation: Even in extreme suffering, Jesus showed extraordinary compassion and forgiveness. Rather than cursing his executioners, he asked God to forgive them because they did not fully understand that they were crucifying the Son of God.

beginner.question: After Jesus died on the cross at Golgotha, his disciples quickly took his body and buried him in a tomb that belonged to the disciple Peter.
beginner.explanation: It was Joseph of Arimathea — a wealthy member of the Jewish council and a secret follower of Jesus — who asked Pilate for the body and placed it in his own new tomb cut out of rock. Peter did not provide the tomb.

beginner.question: At the exact moment Jesus died on the cross at Golgotha, the massive curtain inside the Temple in Jerusalem was miraculously torn in two from top to bottom, symbolising open access to God.
beginner.explanation: The Temple curtain separated the Holy Place from the Most Holy Place, where God's presence dwelt. It being torn from top to bottom — from God's direction downward — symbolised that Jesus' death removed the barrier between God and humanity.

beginner.question: A Roman centurion standing guard at the crucifixion at Golgotha was so moved by what he witnessed when Jesus died that he declared, 'Surely this man was the Son of God!'
beginner.explanation: This hardened Roman soldier had likely witnessed many crucifixions, but Jesus' death was different. The darkness, the earthquake, and the way Jesus died moved him to make this remarkable declaration of faith — a Gentile recognising Jesus' true identity.

beginner.question: According to the Gospel of John, Jesus performed his very first miracle at a wedding celebration in the village of Cana in Galilee, where he turned water into wine.
beginner.explanation: John 2:11 specifically calls this 'the first of the signs through which he revealed his glory.' The miracle at the wedding in Cana was the beginning of Jesus demonstrating his divine power, and his disciples believed in him because of it.

beginner.question: At the wedding in Cana, Jesus' disciples noticed the wine had run out and asked Jesus to do something about the problem.
beginner.explanation: It was Jesus' mother Mary, not his disciples, who brought the wine shortage to Jesus' attention. Mary told him, 'They have no more wine.' She then told the servants, 'Do whatever he tells you,' showing her faith that Jesus could handle the situation.

beginner.question: When Jesus turned water into wine at the wedding in Cana, he used six large stone water jars that were normally used for Jewish ceremonial washing. Each jar held twenty to thirty gallons.
beginner.explanation: The six stone jars were used for Jewish purification rites. Jesus told the servants to fill them to the brim with water. When the water was drawn out, it had become fine wine — perhaps 120 to 180 gallons in total.

beginner.question: The miracle of turning water into wine at the wedding in Cana was the last miracle Jesus performed before his crucifixion, serving as a final powerful sign of his divine authority.
beginner.explanation: The wedding at Cana was actually Jesus' first miracle, not his last. John 2:11 specifically says, 'What Jesus did here in Cana of Galilee was the first of the signs through which he revealed his glory.' He went on to perform many more miracles.

beginner.question: At the wedding in Cana, the master of the banquet was amazed because the best wine had been saved for last, which was the opposite of the usual custom of serving the best wine first.
beginner.explanation: The master of the banquet told the bridegroom, 'Everyone brings out the choice wine first and then the cheaper wine after the guests have had too much to drink; but you have saved the best till now.' He didn't know Jesus had made it from water.

beginner.question: Jesus' mother Mary instructed the servants at the wedding in Cana to 'Do whatever he tells you,' showing her faith that Jesus could solve the wine shortage.
beginner.explanation: Mary's instruction to the servants showed her complete trust in Jesus. Even though Jesus initially said, 'My hour has not yet come,' Mary was confident he would act. The servants obeyed and filled the jars with water as Jesus directed.

beginner.question: In the Gospel of John, Jesus broke social customs by speaking to a Samaritan woman at Jacob's well near the town of Sychar in Samaria. Jews and Samaritans normally refused to interact with each other.
beginner.explanation: The woman was surprised that Jesus, a Jewish man, would ask her for a drink. John explains, 'Jews do not associate with Samaritans.' Jesus broke through social, ethnic, and gender barriers to reach this woman with the message of eternal life.

beginner.question: In Jesus' famous Parable of the Good Samaritan, a Jewish priest was the first person to stop and help the beaten man lying on the road from Jerusalem to Jericho.
beginner.explanation: The priest did the opposite — he saw the injured man and passed by on the other side of the road without stopping. A Levite did the same. It was a Samaritan, someone from a group despised by Jews, who stopped and showed compassion.

beginner.question: During his conversation with the Samaritan woman at the well, Jesus offered her 'living water' that would become a spring welling up to eternal life, so she would never be spiritually thirsty again.
beginner.explanation: Jesus said, 'Whoever drinks the water I give them will never thirst. Indeed, the water I give them will become in them a spring of water welling up to eternal life.' The woman asked Jesus to give her this water.

beginner.question: Samaria was located in the far south of Israel, near the border with Egypt, which is why Jews rarely visited there during their travels between major cities.
beginner.explanation: Samaria was located between Galilee in the north and Judea in the south — right in the middle of Israel. Jews avoided it not because of distance but because of the deep ethnic and religious hostility between Jews and Samaritans.

beginner.question: The Samaritan woman at the well was so moved by her encounter with Jesus that she left her water jar and ran back to her town to tell everyone about him. Many Samaritans believed because of her testimony.
beginner.explanation: The woman was so excited about meeting Jesus that she forgot her water jar — the very reason she had come to the well. She told the townspeople, 'Come, see a man who told me everything I ever did. Could this be the Messiah?' Many came to believe.

beginner.question: In the book of Acts, after the deacon Philip went to Samaria and preached the gospel, many people believed and were baptised. The apostles in Jerusalem then sent Peter and John to pray for the new believers.
beginner.explanation: Philip's preaching in Samaria led to great joy in the city as many believed and were baptised. The apostles sent Peter and John, who prayed for the new Samaritan believers, and they received the Holy Spirit.

beginner.question: Saul of Tarsus was traveling to Damascus with letters authorising him to arrest followers of Jesus when a blinding light from heaven struck him down and he heard Jesus' voice speaking to him.
beginner.explanation: Saul was a zealous persecutor of Christians. On the road to Damascus, a light from heaven flashed around him, he fell to the ground, and heard, 'Saul, Saul, why do you persecute me?' This dramatic encounter transformed him into the apostle Paul.

beginner.question: After Saul was blinded on the road to Damascus, he immediately regained his sight on his own through prayer and fasting, without anyone else needing to help him.
beginner.explanation: Saul did not recover his sight on his own. God specifically sent a disciple named Ananias to lay hands on Saul to restore his sight. When Ananias placed his hands on him, something like scales fell from Saul's eyes and he could see again.

beginner.question: When Saul was struck down on the road to Damascus, he heard the voice of Jesus say, 'Saul, Saul, why do you persecute me?' The men traveling with Saul heard the sound but saw no one.
beginner.explanation: Jesus identified himself with his persecuted followers. When Saul asked, 'Who are you, Lord?' the voice replied, 'I am Jesus, whom you are persecuting.' The men with Saul stood speechless, hearing the sound but seeing nobody.

beginner.question: After his conversion in Damascus, Paul was able to leave the city freely and peacefully because the Jewish leaders there were supportive of his new Christian faith.
beginner.explanation: Paul's bold preaching in Damascus infuriated the Jews, who conspired to kill him. They watched the city gates day and night to catch him. Paul had to escape by being lowered in a basket through an opening in the city wall at night.

beginner.question: After his dramatic conversion on the road to Damascus, Saul (later called Paul) immediately began preaching in the Damascus synagogues that Jesus is the Son of God.
beginner.explanation: Saul's transformation was immediate and dramatic. After being baptised and spending time with the disciples in Damascus, he went straight to the synagogues and proclaimed that Jesus is the Son of God. Everyone was astonished at his complete turnaround.

beginner.question: Saul was blind for three days after his encounter with Jesus on the Damascus road. During those three days, he did not eat or drink anything until Ananias came and restored his sight.
beginner.explanation: Acts 9:9 says Saul was blind for three days and did not eat or drink anything. This period of blindness and fasting was a time of spiritual transformation. When Ananias came and laid hands on him, scales fell from his eyes.

beginner.question: The apostle Paul wrote a letter to the Christians in Rome that became one of the most important books of the New Testament. The book of Romans explains how people are saved by faith in Jesus Christ.
beginner.explanation: Paul's letter to the Romans is considered his theological masterpiece. It systematically explains the gospel: all have sinned, salvation comes through faith alone, and believers are justified by God's grace through the sacrifice of Jesus.

beginner.question: Paul voluntarily traveled to Rome as a free man on a missionary journey because he wanted to see the Colosseum and visit the famous Roman churches.
beginner.explanation: Paul traveled to Rome as a prisoner, not a free man. He had appealed to Caesar (as was his right as a Roman citizen) after being falsely accused. He was sent to Rome under guard to stand trial. The Colosseum was also not yet built during Paul's visit.

beginner.question: On Paul's sea voyage to Rome as a prisoner, a terrible storm battered the ship for two weeks. The ship eventually wrecked on the island of Malta, but all 276 people on board survived.
beginner.explanation: The storm was so severe that the crew gave up all hope. But Paul, after receiving an angel's message, assured everyone that no lives would be lost. The ship ran aground and broke apart, but all 276 people swam or floated safely to shore on Malta.

beginner.question: While Paul was imprisoned in Rome, he completely stopped all ministry work and did not write any letters to churches or receive any visitors.
beginner.explanation: Paul was incredibly productive during his Roman imprisonment. He lived under house arrest, welcomed all who came to visit, and wrote several important letters (Ephesians, Philippians, Colossians, Philemon) that became books of the New Testament.

beginner.question: When Paul arrived in Rome as a prisoner, he was placed under house arrest — allowed to live in his own rented quarters with a soldier guarding him, rather than being locked in a dungeon.
beginner.explanation: Acts 28:16 says Paul was allowed to live by himself with a soldier to guard him. He stayed in this arrangement for two full years, boldly preaching about Jesus and teaching about the Lord to everyone who visited him.

beginner.question: According to early Christian tradition, both the apostles Peter and Paul were martyred in Rome, making the city significant in early church history.
beginner.explanation: Church tradition records that both Peter and Paul were executed in Rome during Emperor Nero's persecution of Christians in the mid-60s AD. Peter is traditionally said to have been crucified upside down, while Paul, as a Roman citizen, was beheaded.

beginner.question: According to the book of Acts, Jesus ascended into heaven from the Mount of Olives, located just east of Jerusalem. The disciples then returned to Jerusalem to wait for the Holy Spirit.
beginner.explanation: After giving his final instructions, Jesus was taken up before their eyes and a cloud hid him from sight. Acts 1:12 says the disciples returned to Jerusalem from the Mount of Olives, which was a Sabbath day's walk from the city.

beginner.question: On Palm Sunday, Jesus walked into Jerusalem on foot from the Mount of Olives while the crowds stood silently and watched him pass without making any noise or celebration.
beginner.explanation: Jesus rode on a donkey (not on foot), and the crowds were anything but silent. They spread cloaks and palm branches on the road and shouted enthusiastically, 'Hosanna! Blessed is he who comes in the name of the Lord!' It was a loud, joyful celebration.

beginner.question: On Palm Sunday, Jesus rode into Jerusalem from the Mount of Olives on a donkey, fulfilling the prophecy of Zechariah that the king would come 'gentle and riding on a donkey.'
beginner.explanation: Zechariah 9:9 prophesied, 'See, your king comes to you, righteous and victorious, lowly and riding on a donkey.' By choosing a donkey instead of a war horse, Jesus showed he came as a peaceful king, not a military conqueror.

beginner.question: The Mount of Olives is located on the western side of Jerusalem, overlooking the Mediterranean Sea and the coastal plains of Israel.
beginner.explanation: The Mount of Olives is located east of Jerusalem, not west. It overlooks the Kidron Valley and the Temple Mount. The Mediterranean Sea is to the west of Jerusalem, not east. The Mount of Olives is named for the olive trees that once covered it.

beginner.question: As Jesus ascended into heaven from the Mount of Olives, two angels appeared and told the amazed disciples that Jesus would return in the same way they had seen him go into heaven.
beginner.explanation: Two men dressed in white (angels) asked, 'Men of Galilee, why do you stand here looking into the sky? This same Jesus, who has been taken from you into heaven, will come back in the same way you have seen him go into heaven.'

beginner.question: The Garden of Gethsemane, where Jesus prayed in agony before his arrest, is located at the base of the Mount of Olives, in the Kidron Valley between the mount and Jerusalem's city walls.
beginner.explanation: Luke 22:39 says Jesus went to the Mount of Olives as was his custom, and Gethsemane is traditionally placed at its western base. The name means 'oil press,' reflecting the olive groves that once grew there.

beginner.question: The apostle John was exiled to the small island of Patmos in the Aegean Sea because of his faith in Jesus. While there, he received the visions recorded in the book of Revelation.
beginner.explanation: Revelation 1:9 says John was 'on the island called Patmos because of the word of God and the testimony of Jesus.' The Romans used this small, rocky island as a place of exile for prisoners, and it was there that John received his extraordinary visions.

beginner.question: The book of Revelation was written from the island of Patmos, which was a large, wealthy trading port in the Mediterranean Sea known for its luxury and commerce.
beginner.explanation: Patmos was the opposite of wealthy and luxurious. It was a small, barren, rocky island in the Aegean Sea (not the Mediterranean proper) that the Romans used as a penal colony. John was banished there as punishment for his Christian faith.

beginner.question: While on the island of Patmos, John received his first vision on 'the Lord's Day.' He heard a loud voice behind him that sounded like a trumpet, telling him to write down what he saw and send it to seven churches.
beginner.explanation: Revelation 1:10-11 says, 'On the Lord's Day I was in the Spirit, and I heard behind me a loud voice like a trumpet, which said: Write on a scroll what you see and send it to the seven churches.' This began John's revelation.

beginner.question: While John was exiled on the island of Patmos, he wrote a letter to the Roman emperor requesting his release, and the emperor granted his freedom immediately.
beginner.explanation: The Bible records nothing about John petitioning the emperor. Instead of seeking his own freedom, John received divine visions about the future and faithfully wrote them down. The book of Revelation is what came from his time on Patmos.

beginner.question: In the book of Revelation, written from Patmos, John was instructed to write specific messages to seven churches in Asia Minor: Ephesus, Smyrna, Pergamum, Thyatira, Sardis, Philadelphia, and Laodicea.
beginner.explanation: Each of the seven churches received a unique message from the risen Jesus through John. Some churches were praised for their faithfulness, others were warned about compromising their faith, and some received both praise and correction.

beginner.question: On the island of Patmos, John saw a vision of the risen Jesus standing among seven golden lampstands. Jesus held seven stars in his right hand, which represented the angels (or leaders) of the seven churches.
beginner.explanation: Revelation 1:20 explains the symbolism: 'The seven stars are the angels of the seven churches, and the seven lampstands are the seven churches.' Jesus' presence among the lampstands showed he was actively involved with and caring for his churches.

beginner.question: According to the book of Acts, the followers of Jesus were first called 'Christians' in the city of Antioch. This became the name by which believers in Jesus would be known throughout history.
beginner.explanation: Acts 11:26 records this milestone: 'The disciples were called Christians first at Antioch.' The name likely meant 'Christ's people' or 'followers of Christ.' Antioch had become a major centre of the faith as the gospel spread beyond Jerusalem.

beginner.question: The church in Antioch was the very first Christian church ever established, founded directly by Jesus himself during his earthly ministry before his crucifixion.
beginner.explanation: The first church was in Jerusalem, not Antioch. The Antioch church was founded later by believers who were scattered from Jerusalem during persecution. These scattered Christians brought the gospel to Antioch, where the church grew rapidly.

beginner.question: The church in Antioch sent out Barnabas and Saul (Paul) on the first organised missionary journey after the Holy Spirit specifically directed the church to set them apart for this work.
beginner.explanation: While the church leaders were worshipping and fasting, the Holy Spirit said, 'Set apart for me Barnabas and Saul for the work to which I have called them.' After fasting and praying, the church laid hands on them and sent them off.

beginner.question: Antioch, where believers were first called Christians, was a small and insignificant village with only a few hundred residents in the ancient world.
beginner.explanation: Antioch was far from small — it was the third-largest city in the entire Roman Empire after Rome and Alexandria, with a population of perhaps 500,000 people. Its size and importance made it a strategic base for spreading Christianity throughout the Roman world.

beginner.question: In his letter to the Galatians, Paul describes how he publicly confronted the apostle Peter in Antioch because Peter had been hypocritical about eating with non-Jewish believers.
beginner.explanation: Peter had been freely eating with Gentile Christians in Antioch, but when certain Jews from Jerusalem arrived, Peter withdrew and separated himself, fearing their criticism. Paul opposed him to his face because his behaviour was leading others astray.

beginner.question: After persecution scattered Christians from Jerusalem, some believers traveled to Antioch and preached the gospel to Greeks (non-Jews) as well as Jews. Many people believed and turned to the Lord.
beginner.explanation: This was a pivotal moment — the gospel was deliberately preached to non-Jews for the first time in a major way. 'The Lord's hand was with them, and a great number of people believed and turned to the Lord,' establishing Antioch as a diverse Christian community.

beginner.question: After the great flood in the book of Genesis, Noah's ark came to rest on the mountains of Ararat. Noah then sent out birds to check whether the floodwaters had receded.
beginner.explanation: Genesis 8:4 says the ark rested on the mountains of Ararat in the seventh month. Noah first sent out a raven, then sent a dove multiple times. The dove eventually returned with an olive leaf, showing the waters were going down.

beginner.question: According to the book of Genesis, it rained for forty days and forty nights during the great flood that covered the entire earth in Noah's time.
beginner.explanation: Genesis 7:12 says, 'Rain fell on the earth forty days and forty nights.' However, the floodwaters continued to rise and remained on the earth for much longer — about 150 days — before they began to recede.

beginner.question: After the ark landed on Mount Ararat, Noah sent out a raven that returned with an olive branch in its beak, showing that the floodwaters had receded enough for trees to be visible.
beginner.explanation: It was a dove, not a raven, that returned with the olive leaf. Noah did send out a raven first, but the raven kept flying back and forth without returning with anything. It was the dove on its second trip that brought back a freshly plucked olive leaf.

beginner.question: After the great flood, God promised Noah that he would never again destroy the earth with a flood. God placed a rainbow in the sky as a sign of this everlasting covenant.
beginner.explanation: God said, 'I have set my rainbow in the clouds, and it will be the sign of the covenant between me and the earth.' Whenever the rainbow appears in the clouds, God said he would remember his promise never to flood the entire earth again.

beginner.question: According to the book of Genesis, twelve people in total were saved on Noah's ark during the great flood — Noah, his wife, and their five sons with their five wives.
beginner.explanation: Eight people were saved on the ark, not twelve. They were Noah, his wife, their three sons (Shem, Ham, and Japheth), and the three sons' wives. First Peter 3:20 confirms that 'eight in all, were saved through water.'

beginner.question: God instructed Noah to bring seven pairs of every clean animal onto the ark, but only one pair of every unclean animal. The extra clean animals were needed for sacrifices after the flood.
beginner.explanation: Genesis 7:2 says, 'Take with you seven pairs of every kind of clean animal, a male and its mate, and one pair of every kind of unclean animal.' After the flood, Noah built an altar and sacrificed some of the clean animals to God.

beginner.question: In the book of Genesis, God destroyed the wicked cities of Sodom and Gomorrah by raining down fire and burning sulfur (brimstone) from the sky, completely annihilating both cities and everything in the surrounding plain.
beginner.explanation: Genesis 19:24-25 says, 'The Lord rained down burning sulfur on Sodom and Gomorrah — from the Lord out of the heavens. Thus he overthrew those cities and the entire plain, destroying all those living in the cities — and also the vegetation in the land.'

beginner.question: Before God destroyed Sodom, he sent two angels to rescue Abraham and his wife Sarah from the city because they were the only righteous people living there.
beginner.explanation: The angels were sent to rescue Lot (Abraham's nephew), not Abraham. Abraham did not live in Sodom — he lived elsewhere in Canaan. Lot was the one who had chosen to settle in Sodom. The angels took Lot, his wife, and two daughters out of the city.

beginner.question: As Lot and his family fled the destruction of Sodom, they were warned not to look back. Lot's wife disobeyed and looked back at the burning city, and she was turned into a pillar of salt.
beginner.explanation: The angels specifically commanded, 'Don't look back.' But Lot's wife looked back toward Sodom, and she became a pillar of salt. Jesus later used her as a cautionary example, telling his followers to 'Remember Lot's wife' — do not cling to what God has judged.

beginner.question: Before destroying Sodom, God revealed his plan to Abraham, who bargained with God. Abraham asked if God would spare the city if righteous people could be found there, negotiating the number down from fifty to ten.
beginner.explanation: Abraham appealed to God's justice, asking, 'Will you sweep away the righteous with the wicked?' He negotiated from fifty down to forty-five, forty, thirty, twenty, and finally ten. God agreed to spare Sodom for ten righteous people — but even ten could not be found.

beginner.question: Lot, who was rescued from Sodom before its destruction, was Abraham's younger brother who had traveled with him from Ur to the land of Canaan.
beginner.explanation: Lot was Abraham's nephew, not his brother. Lot was the son of Haran, who was Abraham's brother. When their flocks grew too large to share the same land, Abraham let Lot choose where to go, and Lot chose the well-watered plain near Sodom.

beginner.question: Jesus referenced the destruction of Sodom and Gomorrah in his teachings, comparing it to the sudden judgment that would come at the end of the age, warning people to be prepared.
beginner.explanation: In Luke 17:28-30, Jesus said, 'It was the same in the days of Lot. People were eating and drinking, buying and selling, planting and building. But the day Lot left Sodom, fire and sulfur rained down from heaven. It will be just like this on the day the Son of Man is revealed.'

beginner.question: God called a man named Abram to leave his homeland in Ur of the Chaldeans and go to a new land that God would show him. This man became known as the father of many nations. What was his name changed to by God?
beginner.explanation: God changed Abram's name to Abraham, meaning 'father of many nations,' as a sign of the covenant He made with him. This name change reflected God's promise that Abraham would be the ancestor of many peoples and kings.

beginner.question: God promised Abraham and his elderly wife Sarah that they would have a son, even though they were both very old. Sarah even laughed when she heard the promise. What was the name of this long-awaited son?
beginner.explanation: The promised son was named Isaac, which means 'he laughs,' because Sarah laughed when she heard she would have a baby at such an old age. Isaac was born when Abraham was 100 and Sarah was 90.

beginner.question: God tested Abraham's faith by asking him to sacrifice his only son Isaac on a mountain. Abraham obeyed, but at the last moment God stopped him and provided a substitute sacrifice. What animal did God provide instead?
beginner.explanation: God provided a ram caught in a thicket by its horns as a substitute sacrifice for Isaac. This showed that God would provide for His people and was a foreshadowing of God providing His own Son as a sacrifice for humanity.

beginner.question: Before God changed his name, the man who would become the father of many nations was called by a different name. He lived in Ur of the Chaldeans with his family before God called him. What was Abraham's original name?
beginner.explanation: Abraham was originally called Abram, which means 'exalted father.' God changed his name to Abraham, meaning 'father of many nations,' when He established His covenant with him at the age of 99.

beginner.question: Abraham's nephew traveled with him when he left Ur, but eventually they separated because the land could not support both their large flocks. This nephew later settled near the wicked city of Sodom. What was his name?
beginner.explanation: Lot was Abraham's nephew who traveled with him from Ur. When their herds grew too large, they parted ways. Lot chose the lush Jordan Valley near Sodom, while Abraham stayed in the hill country of Canaan.

beginner.question: When God made a covenant with Abraham, He took Abraham outside and pointed to the night sky to illustrate how many descendants Abraham would have. God said his descendants would be as numerous as what?
options: The grains of wheat in a field
beginner.explanation: God told Abraham to look up at the night sky and try to count the stars, saying his descendants would be just as countless. This was God's way of promising that Abraham would become the father of a great multitude of people.

beginner.question: Abraham was living in a land called Ur of the Chaldeans, in Mesopotamia, when God called him to leave everything behind. What did God tell Abraham to go to?
options: A land God would show him
beginner.explanation: God told Abraham to leave his country, his people, and his father's household and go to a land that God would show him. Abraham obeyed in faith without knowing exactly where he was going, trusting God completely.

beginner.question: Abraham's wife Sarah was very old when God promised she would bear a son. She laughed at the idea because she thought she was too old to have children. Approximately how old was Abraham when his son Isaac was finally born?
beginner.explanation: Abraham was 100 years old when Isaac was born, and Sarah was about 90. Their son's birth was a miracle that demonstrated God's power to fulfil His promises regardless of human limitations.

beginner.question: Abraham sent his most trusted servant on a long journey to find a wife for his son Isaac. The servant prayed for guidance and found a young woman at a well who generously offered water to him and his camels. What was her name?
beginner.explanation: Rebekah was found at a well and showed great kindness by drawing water for the servant and all his camels. The servant recognised this as God's answer to his prayer, and Rebekah agreed to return with him to marry Isaac.

beginner.question: A baby boy was hidden in a waterproof basket among the reeds of the Nile River because Pharaoh had ordered all Hebrew baby boys to be killed. A royal woman found the baby and raised him as her own. Who found baby Moses?
beginner.explanation: Pharaoh's daughter discovered baby Moses while bathing in the Nile. She felt sorry for the crying baby and decided to adopt him, unknowingly fulfilling God's plan to raise Israel's future deliverer in the Egyptian palace.

beginner.question: While tending sheep in the desert near Mount Horeb, Moses saw something extraordinary — a bush that was on fire but was not burning up. When he went to investigate, someone spoke to him from the bush. Who spoke to Moses?
beginner.explanation: God Himself spoke to Moses from the burning bush, calling him by name and instructing him to remove his sandals because he was standing on holy ground. God then commissioned Moses to go to Egypt and lead His people to freedom.

beginner.question: God sent ten terrible plagues upon Egypt to convince Pharaoh to release the Israelite slaves. The plagues included frogs, locusts, and darkness. What was the final and most devastating plague that finally broke Pharaoh's stubbornness?
beginner.explanation: The tenth and final plague was the death of every firstborn son in Egypt, from Pharaoh's own son to the firstborn of prisoners. This devastating plague finally convinced Pharaoh to let the Israelites leave Egypt.

beginner.question: After escaping Egypt, the Israelites were trapped between Pharaoh's approaching army and a large body of water with no way across. God performed a mighty miracle through Moses to save them. What did God do?
options: Sent a strong wind to blow them across
Parted the Red Sea so they could walk through
Caused the water to freeze solid
beginner.explanation: God parted the Red Sea by having Moses stretch out his hand, creating a path of dry ground with walls of water on both sides. The Israelites crossed safely, and when the Egyptian army pursued, the waters crashed back and drowned them.

beginner.question: Moses climbed a mountain to meet with God and receive instructions for the people of Israel. After spending 40 days on the mountain, Moses came down carrying stone tablets. What was written on these tablets?
options: A map to the Promised Land
The names of the twelve tribes
beginner.explanation: God wrote the Ten Commandments on two stone tablets and gave them to Moses on Mount Sinai. These commandments became the foundation of God's law for Israel, covering their relationship with God and with one another.

beginner.question: Moses received the Ten Commandments from God on a famous mountain in the wilderness. This mountain is one of the most important locations in the Old Testament. What was the name of this mountain?
beginner.explanation: Mount Sinai, also called Mount Horeb, is where God gave Moses the Ten Commandments. The mountain was covered in smoke and fire, and the people trembled at God's awesome presence there.

beginner.question: Moses told God that he was not a good speaker and could not go to Pharaoh alone. God appointed someone to go with Moses and speak on his behalf. Who was this person who helped Moses communicate with Pharaoh?
beginner.explanation: Aaron, Moses' older brother, was appointed by God to be Moses' spokesman. Aaron would speak to Pharaoh on Moses' behalf and also helped perform some of the signs and wonders before the Egyptian court.

beginner.question: After leaving Egypt, the Israelites wandered in the wilderness for many years because they disobeyed God and were afraid to enter the Promised Land. How many years did the Israelites spend wandering in the desert?
beginner.explanation: The Israelites wandered for 40 years in the wilderness as a consequence of their lack of faith when the spies returned from Canaan. God decreed that the entire generation who refused to trust Him would die before the next generation could enter the Promised Land.

beginner.question: When Moses came down from Mount Sinai after receiving the Ten Commandments, he found the people of Israel worshipping a golden idol they had made. What shape was this golden idol?
beginner.explanation: The Israelites had melted their gold jewellery and Aaron shaped it into a golden calf for them to worship. Moses was so angry at their disobedience that he smashed the stone tablets and destroyed the golden idol.

beginner.question: A young shepherd boy from Bethlehem volunteered to fight a giant Philistine warrior that the entire Israelite army was terrified of. This giant had been taunting the Israelites for 40 days. What was the giant's name?
beginner.explanation: Goliath was a Philistine giant from Gath who stood over nine feet tall and wore heavy bronze armour. He challenged the Israelites to send a champion to fight him, but no soldier dared until the young shepherd David stepped forward.

beginner.question: The shepherd boy David bravely faced the giant Goliath while the entire Israelite army watched in fear. David did not use a sword or armour. What simple weapon did David use to defeat Goliath?
beginner.explanation: David used a sling and a smooth stone from a nearby stream to strike Goliath in the forehead. The giant fell face down, and David then used Goliath's own sword to finish the battle, winning a great victory for Israel.

beginner.question: Before David became king, he served in the court of Israel's first king, who was tall and handsome but eventually disobeyed God. This king grew jealous of David and tried to kill him. Who was Israel's first king?
beginner.explanation: Saul was Israel's first king, chosen by God and anointed by the prophet Samuel. Although he started well, Saul repeatedly disobeyed God and became jealous of David's popularity, spending years trying to hunt David down and kill him.

beginner.question: David was known as a talented musician who was called to the royal court to play soothing music for King Saul whenever the king was tormented by an evil spirit. What instrument did David play?
beginner.explanation: David played the harp, also called a lyre, with great skill. The soothing music would calm King Saul and bring him relief from the tormenting spirit. David's musical talent was one of the reasons he was first brought into the royal court.

beginner.question: David composed many beautiful songs and poems that became part of the Bible. These writings express praise, prayer, sorrow, and trust in God. What book of the Bible contains most of David's compositions?
beginner.explanation: The book of Psalms contains most of David's songs and poems. Roughly half of the 150 Psalms are attributed to David, including famous ones like Psalm 23 ('The Lord is my shepherd') and Psalm 51, his prayer of repentance.

beginner.question: David was the youngest of several brothers and worked as a humble shepherd in Bethlehem before being chosen by God to be king. The prophet Samuel came to his family's home to anoint the next king. Who was David's father?
beginner.explanation: Jesse of Bethlehem was David's father. When the prophet Samuel came looking for the next king, Jesse presented seven of his sons, but God rejected them all. Only when Jesse sent for his youngest, David, did God say, 'This is the one.'

beginner.question: God made a special promise to King David that his royal line would last forever. Centuries later, a very famous descendant was born in David's hometown of Bethlehem, fulfilling this promise. Who was this descendant?
beginner.explanation: Jesus Christ was the ultimate fulfilment of God's covenant with David. Born in Bethlehem, Jesus is called the 'Son of David' in the New Testament. His eternal kingdom is the everlasting throne God promised to David's line.

beginner.question: David had a very close friendship with the son of King Saul. These two men loved each other like brothers and made a covenant of loyalty, even though Saul wanted David dead. Who was David's closest friend?
