beginner.explanation: Jonathan, King Saul's son, became David's dearest friend. Despite knowing that David would become king instead of himself, Jonathan remained loyal to David and even risked his father's anger to protect him.

beginner.question: When the prophet Samuel visited Jesse's house to anoint the next king of Israel, God rejected all the older sons. Samuel asked Jesse if he had any other sons, and Jesse mentioned the youngest was out tending sheep. How many sons did Jesse have in total?
beginner.explanation: Jesse had eight sons, and David was the youngest. Samuel examined seven of Jesse's sons before asking if there were any more. David was then brought in from the fields, and God told Samuel to anoint him as the future king.

beginner.question: A young Jewish orphan girl became queen of Persia and used her royal position to save her entire people from a plot to destroy them. This brave woman risked her own life by approaching the king uninvited. What was her name?
beginner.explanation: Esther became queen of Persia after being selected in a kingdom-wide search for a new queen. When she learned of a plot to kill all the Jews, she bravely approached King Ahasuerus to plead for her people, even though uninvited visits to the king could result in death.

beginner.question: Queen Esther risked her life to stop a wicked official who had plotted to destroy all the Jewish people throughout the Persian Empire. This man was furious that Esther's cousin would not bow to him. Who was this villain?
beginner.explanation: Haman was a powerful official in the Persian court who hated the Jews, especially Mordecai, who refused to bow before him. Haman devised a plan to kill every Jewish person in the empire, but Esther's bravery exposed his plot and saved her people.

beginner.question: Esther was an orphan raised by her older cousin, who adopted her as his own daughter after her parents died. This devoted cousin later uncovered a plot against the king and helped save the Jewish people. What was his name?
beginner.explanation: Mordecai raised Esther as his own child and advised her even after she became queen. He discovered a plot to assassinate the king, and his refusal to bow to Haman set in motion the events that led to the Jewish people's deliverance.

beginner.question: Esther was chosen as the new queen of Persia after the previous queen was removed. The Persian king held a great search to find the most beautiful woman in the kingdom. What was this king's name?
beginner.explanation: King Ahasuerus, also known as Xerxes, ruled the vast Persian Empire. After Queen Vashti was removed for disobeying him, the king held a search for a new queen and chose Esther for her beauty and grace.

beginner.question: Before going to speak to the king about the plot to destroy her people, Esther asked all the Jewish people in the city to support her through a spiritual practice for three days. What did she ask them to do?
beginner.explanation: Esther asked all the Jews in Susa to fast for three days and nights before she approached the king. She and her maids also fasted. This showed her dependence on God's help in this dangerous and courageous act.

beginner.question: The wicked Haman was so enraged that Mordecai would not bow down to him that he built a tall structure on which to execute Mordecai. In a dramatic twist, Haman himself ended up being killed on it. What did Haman build?
beginner.explanation: Haman built a gallows 75 feet high, intending to hang Mordecai on it. But when Queen Esther exposed Haman's evil plot, the king ordered Haman to be hanged on the very gallows he had prepared for Mordecai — a powerful example of justice.

beginner.question: The Jewish people celebrate their deliverance through Queen Esther's bravery with a special annual holiday that involves feasting, gift-giving, and reading the book of Esther. What is this Jewish holiday called?
beginner.explanation: Purim celebrates the deliverance of the Jewish people from Haman's plot to destroy them. The name comes from the word 'pur' meaning 'lot,' because Haman cast lots to choose the date of the planned massacre.

beginner.question: When Mordecai urged Esther to speak up and save her people from destruction, he reminded her that perhaps God had placed her in the palace for this very purpose. In what ancient empire did the story of Esther take place?
beginner.explanation: The story of Esther took place in the Persian Empire, with the main events occurring in the capital city of Susa. The Persian Empire at this time stretched from India to Ethiopia, and the Jews were living there after the Babylonian exile.

beginner.question: A woman from the land of Moab showed incredible loyalty to her Israelite mother-in-law after both their husbands died, choosing to leave her homeland and follow her. Who was Ruth's famous mother-in-law?
beginner.explanation: Naomi was Ruth's mother-in-law. After both women lost their husbands, Naomi decided to return to Bethlehem. Ruth refused to leave Naomi's side, choosing to travel with her to a foreign land rather than stay in familiar Moab.

beginner.question: When Naomi decided to return to her homeland of Israel, her daughter-in-law Ruth made one of the most famous declarations of loyalty in all of Scripture: 'Where you go, I will go.' What country did Ruth leave behind to stay with Naomi?
beginner.explanation: Ruth left her homeland of Moab, located east of the Dead Sea, to accompany Naomi back to Bethlehem. This was a tremendous act of devotion, as Ruth was leaving behind her family, friends, and everything she knew.

beginner.question: After arriving in Bethlehem, Ruth needed to find food for herself and Naomi. She went to the fields to collect leftover grain that harvesters had dropped, which was a practice allowed by God's law for the poor. What is this practice called?
beginner.explanation: Gleaning was the practice of allowing the poor, widows, and foreigners to pick up grain that was left behind after the harvest. God had commanded farmers not to harvest the edges of their fields so the needy could gather food.

beginner.question: Ruth happened to gather grain in the fields of a wealthy and kind landowner in Bethlehem. This man noticed Ruth's hard work and devotion to Naomi, treated her generously, and eventually married her. What was his name?
beginner.explanation: Boaz was a wealthy and honourable landowner in Bethlehem who showed great kindness to Ruth. He admired her loyalty to Naomi and her courage in leaving Moab. As a close relative of Naomi's late husband, Boaz fulfilled the role of kinsman-redeemer by marrying Ruth.

beginner.question: Ruth and Boaz had a son together in Bethlehem. This child became very special in Israel's history because he was the grandfather of one of Israel's greatest kings. What was Ruth and Boaz's son called?
beginner.explanation: Ruth and Boaz named their son Obed. Obed became the father of Jesse, who became the father of King David. This placed Ruth, a foreign woman from Moab, in the direct family line of both King David and Jesus Christ.

beginner.question: Ruth was not originally an Israelite, but through her marriage to Boaz in Bethlehem, she became an ancestor of Israel's most famous king. Her great-grandson ruled Israel during its golden age. Who was Ruth's great-grandson?
beginner.explanation: King David was Ruth's great-grandson. The family line goes Ruth and Boaz, then Obed, then Jesse, then David. This shows how God can use anyone, even a foreign widow, in His great plans.

beginner.question: When Naomi returned to Bethlehem after years away, she was so filled with grief over losing her husband and sons that she told the townspeople to call her by a new name meaning 'bitter.' What name did Naomi want to be called?
beginner.explanation: Naomi asked to be called Mara, meaning 'bitter,' because she felt God had dealt bitterly with her after the deaths of her husband and two sons. However, God later restored her joy through Ruth's marriage to Boaz and the birth of her grandson Obed.

beginner.question: Ruth is honoured as one of the few women specifically mentioned in the genealogy of Jesus in the New Testament. She settled down and raised her family with Boaz in a town that would later become famous as the birthplace of Jesus. What town was this?
beginner.explanation: Ruth and Boaz settled in Bethlehem, which centuries later would become the birthplace of Jesus. Ruth's inclusion in Jesus' family line shows God's love for all people, regardless of nationality or background.

beginner.question: A young Israelite man was taken captive to Babylon but rose to a position of great power because of his wisdom and faithfulness to God. When the king made a law against praying, this man kept praying and was thrown to wild animals. Who was he?
beginner.explanation: Daniel was taken to Babylon as a young man but remained faithful to God his entire life. Even when King Darius signed a law forbidding prayer to anyone but the king, Daniel continued praying openly to God, resulting in him being thrown into a den of lions.

beginner.question: King Darius was tricked by jealous officials into signing a law that anyone who prayed to anyone other than the king for 30 days would be thrown into a den of lions. Daniel ignored this law and kept praying. How many times a day did Daniel pray?
beginner.explanation: Daniel prayed three times a day, facing Jerusalem, just as he had always done. He refused to hide his prayers or change his habits, even though the punishment was being thrown into a den of hungry lions.

beginner.question: Daniel was thrown into a den of hungry lions for disobeying the king's law against praying. But God miraculously protected Daniel through the entire night. How did God keep Daniel safe from the lions?
options: He put the lions to sleep
He sent an angel to shut the lions' mouths
He created a wall of fire around Daniel
beginner.explanation: God sent an angel who shut the lions' mouths so they could not harm Daniel. When King Darius rushed to the den the next morning, he found Daniel completely unharmed, which proved Daniel's innocence and God's power.

beginner.question: When Daniel and his friends first arrived as young captives in Babylon, the king offered them rich food and wine from the royal table. Daniel politely refused this food and asked for something simpler instead. What did Daniel ask to eat?
beginner.explanation: Daniel asked for vegetables and water instead of the king's rich food, because the royal food may have been offered to idols or violated Jewish dietary laws. After ten days, Daniel and his friends looked healthier than those who ate the king's food.

beginner.question: The king of Babylon had a troubling dream about a giant statue made of different materials that none of his wise men could interpret. God revealed the meaning to Daniel, who explained it to the impressed king. Who was this king?
beginner.explanation: King Nebuchadnezzar had a dream of a great statue with a head of gold, chest of silver, belly of bronze, legs of iron, and feet of clay. Daniel explained that each part represented a future kingdom. The king was so impressed he promoted Daniel to a high position.

beginner.question: King Belshazzar held a great feast and arrogantly used sacred cups stolen from the Jewish temple in Jerusalem to drink wine. Suddenly, a mysterious hand appeared and wrote a message on the wall. Who was called to interpret this terrifying writing?
beginner.explanation: Daniel was summoned to interpret the writing on the wall after all the king's wise men failed. Daniel read the words 'Mene, Mene, Tekel, Parsin' and told Belshazzar that God had judged his kingdom and it would be given to the Medes and Persians.

beginner.question: Because of his exceptional wisdom and faithfulness, Daniel was promoted to one of the highest positions in the kingdom. Jealous officials plotted against him but could find no fault except in his devotion to God. What was Daniel's high rank?
beginner.explanation: Daniel was one of three administrators set over the entire kingdom, and the king planned to elevate him even further. Daniel's integrity was so remarkable that his enemies admitted the only way to trap him was through his faithfulness to God.

beginner.question: When Daniel was thrown into the lions' den, King Darius was deeply troubled and could not sleep all night. Early the next morning, the king rushed to the den and called out anxiously. What did Darius discover?
options: Daniel had escaped through a tunnel
Daniel was alive and completely unharmed
The lions had been removed by soldiers
Daniel was badly injured but alive
beginner.explanation: King Darius found Daniel alive and completely unharmed. Daniel told the king that God had sent an angel to shut the lions' mouths because he was innocent. The overjoyed king then issued a decree honouring Daniel's God.

beginner.question: Daniel served faithfully under multiple kings during his long life in Babylon and Persia. Even as a captive in a foreign land, he never compromised his faith. Under which empire was Daniel first taken captive?
beginner.explanation: Daniel was taken captive as a young man when the Babylonian Empire conquered Jerusalem around 605 BC. He served under Babylonian kings like Nebuchadnezzar and Belshazzar, and then under Persian kings like Darius and Cyrus after Persia conquered Babylon.

beginner.question: Jacob had twelve sons, but he clearly loved one son more than the others and gave him a beautiful, distinctive gift that made his brothers extremely jealous. What was this special gift that caused so much envy?
beginner.explanation: Jacob gave Joseph a richly ornamented coat of many colours, showing him special favour among his twelve sons. This gift, along with Joseph's dreams of future greatness, fuelled his brothers' intense jealousy and resentment.

beginner.question: Joseph had dreams that suggested his family would one day bow down to him. In one of these dreams, Joseph saw objects in a field that bowed before his object. What were these bowing objects in his dream?
beginner.explanation: Joseph dreamed that he and his brothers were binding sheaves of grain in the field, and his brothers' sheaves gathered around and bowed down to his sheaf. This dream infuriated his brothers and deepened their jealousy.

beginner.question: Joseph's jealous brothers plotted against him and sold him to travelling merchants for pieces of silver. The merchants took the young man far from his family to a land where he became a slave. Where did Joseph end up?
beginner.explanation: Joseph was taken to Egypt by the Ishmaelite merchants and sold as a slave to Potiphar, an officer of Pharaoh. Although this was a terrible ordeal, God used it to place Joseph exactly where He needed him to save many lives during a future famine.

beginner.question: After arriving in Egypt, Joseph worked as a servant in the household of a wealthy Egyptian officer. Joseph was falsely accused by this man's wife and thrown into prison even though he was innocent. Who was Joseph's Egyptian master?
beginner.explanation: Potiphar was the captain of Pharaoh's guard who purchased Joseph as a slave. God blessed everything Joseph did, and Potiphar put him in charge of his entire household. But Potiphar's wife falsely accused Joseph, and he was unjustly sent to prison.

beginner.question: While Joseph was in prison in Egypt, he correctly interpreted the dreams of two fellow prisoners who had worked in Pharaoh's palace. What had been these two men's jobs before they were imprisoned?
options: A chariot driver and a musician
beginner.explanation: Pharaoh's cupbearer and baker were imprisoned and both had troubling dreams on the same night. Joseph interpreted the cupbearer's dream as a sign he would be restored to his position, and the baker's dream as a sign of his execution. Both came true.

beginner.question: Pharaoh had a mysterious dream about seven fat cows grazing by the Nile being swallowed up by seven thin, sickly cows. None of his wise men could explain it. Joseph told Pharaoh the dream was a warning from God about what?
options: A war with a neighbouring country
Seven years of plenty followed by seven years of famine
beginner.explanation: Joseph explained that the seven fat cows represented seven years of abundant harvests, and the seven thin cows represented seven years of severe famine. Joseph advised Pharaoh to store grain during the good years to survive the famine.

beginner.question: Because Joseph correctly interpreted Pharaoh's dream and proposed a wise plan to save Egypt from the coming famine, Pharaoh promoted Joseph to an incredibly powerful position. What role did Pharaoh give Joseph?
beginner.explanation: Pharaoh made Joseph the second most powerful man in all of Egypt, with authority over the entire land. Joseph went from being a slave and prisoner to wearing Pharaoh's signet ring and riding in the royal chariot — an astonishing rise to power through God's plan.

beginner.question: During the great famine, Joseph's brothers traveled from Canaan to Egypt to buy grain, not realising that the powerful Egyptian official before whom they bowed was their own brother they had sold into slavery years ago. How many brothers did Joseph have?
beginner.explanation: Joseph had eleven brothers, as Jacob had twelve sons in total. Ten of the older brothers had sold Joseph into slavery, while the youngest, Benjamin, had not been involved. When the brothers came to buy grain, Joseph's childhood dreams of them bowing to him came true.

beginner.question: When Joseph finally revealed his true identity to his shocked brothers in Egypt, he forgave them and told them not to be upset for selling him into slavery. He said God had used their actions for a greater purpose. What was Joseph's father's name?
beginner.explanation: Jacob, also called Israel, was Joseph's father. Joseph invited his entire family to come live in Egypt during the famine. When Jacob learned that Joseph was alive and ruling Egypt, he was overwhelmed with joy and journeyed to Egypt to be reunited with his beloved son.

beginner.question: After Moses died, God appointed a new leader to take the Israelites across the Jordan River and into the Promised Land. This man had been Moses' loyal assistant for many years. What was his name?
beginner.explanation: Joshua son of Nun had served as Moses' faithful assistant and was one of only two spies who trusted God's promise to give them the land. God commissioned Joshua to lead Israel, promising to be with him just as He had been with Moses.

beginner.question: When the Israelites arrived at the fortified city of Jericho, God gave Joshua very unusual battle instructions. Instead of attacking with weapons, the people were to march around the city walls in silence. For how many days did they march?
beginner.explanation: God told Joshua to have the people march around the city of Jericho once a day for six days, and seven times on the seventh day. On the seventh day, after the final lap, the priests blew trumpets and the people shouted, and the walls miraculously collapsed.

beginner.question: On the seventh day of marching around Jericho, the Israelites completed their final lap, the priests blew their trumpets, and all the people let out a great shout. What happened to the mighty walls of Jericho?
beginner.explanation: The massive walls of Jericho collapsed completely, falling down flat so the Israelites could charge straight into the city. This was a miraculous victory that showed God's power and proved that He was fighting for His people.

beginner.question: Before the Israelites attacked Jericho, Joshua sent two spies to scout the city. A woman who lived in the city walls hid the spies from the king's soldiers and helped them escape. In which city did this woman named Rahab live?
beginner.explanation: Rahab lived in the city of Jericho and her house was built into the city wall. She hid the two Israelite spies on her rooftop and helped them escape through her window. In return, the spies promised to spare Rahab and her family when Jericho fell.

beginner.question: When the Israelites needed to cross the Jordan River to enter the Promised Land, God performed a miracle similar to the parting of the Red Sea. The priests carried the Ark of the Covenant into the river. What happened to the water?
options: It stopped flowing and piled up
It became shallow enough to wade
beginner.explanation: As soon as the priests' feet touched the Jordan's edge, the water upstream stopped flowing and piled up in a heap. The entire nation of Israel crossed on dry ground, just as their parents' generation had crossed the Red Sea with Moses.

beginner.question: Joshua had been one of twelve spies Moses sent to explore the Promised Land years earlier. Ten spies gave a fearful report, but only two trusted God's promise to give them the land. Who was the other faithful spy besides Joshua?
beginner.explanation: Caleb and Joshua were the only two spies out of twelve who encouraged the people to trust God and enter the Promised Land. Because of their faith, they were the only adults from that generation who eventually entered the land.

beginner.question: Near the end of his life, Joshua gathered all the people of Israel together and challenged them to make a choice about their faith. He made a famous declaration about his own family's commitment. What did Joshua say?
options: 'As for me and my household, we will serve the Lord'
'Trust in the Lord with all your heart'
beginner.explanation: Joshua told the people they must choose whom they would serve — the false gods or the true God. He then boldly declared his own family's decision. This challenge is one of the most quoted statements of faith commitment in the Bible.

beginner.question: During a battle against five Amorite kings, Joshua needed more daylight to finish the fight. He prayed an extraordinary prayer, and God performed an amazing miracle. What did God make the sun do?
beginner.explanation: At Joshua's prayer, God made the sun stand still in the middle of the sky for about a full day, giving the Israelites enough time to defeat their enemies completely. The Bible says there has never been a day like it before or since.

beginner.question: God called a man to save Israel from the Midianites, but this man felt he was the most unlikely hero possible — the least important person in the weakest clan in all Israel. What was his name?
beginner.explanation: Gideon protested that his clan was the weakest in the tribe of Manasseh and that he was the least in his family. Despite Gideon's self-doubt, God chose him specifically to show that victory comes through God's power, not human strength.

beginner.question: When God first called Gideon, an angel appeared to him while he was secretly threshing wheat in a winepress to hide it from the raiding Midianites. Despite Gideon's fear and insecurity, the angel greeted him with a surprising title. What did the angel call Gideon?
beginner.explanation: The angel called Gideon a 'mighty warrior,' which must have seemed ironic since Gideon was hiding from the enemy at the time. This greeting revealed how God saw Gideon — not by his current fear, but by what he would become through God's strength.

beginner.question: Gideon wanted to be absolutely sure God was really calling him, so he asked God for a miraculous sign using a piece of wool laid on the ground overnight. Gideon asked God to make the fleece wet while the ground stayed dry, and then the reverse. How many times did Gideon test God with the fleece?
beginner.explanation: Gideon tested God twice with the fleece. The first night he asked for the fleece to be wet and the ground dry, and the second night he asked for the opposite. God graciously performed both signs to reassure the doubtful Gideon.

beginner.question: God told Gideon that his army of 32,000 soldiers was too large to fight the Midianites, because Israel might boast that they won by their own strength. God reduced the army dramatically. How many soldiers did God leave Gideon with?
beginner.explanation: God reduced Gideon's army from 32,000 all the way down to just 300 men. This was so Israel could never claim the victory was by their own power. With such a tiny force against a massive enemy, it was clear that God won the battle.

beginner.question: Gideon's 300 soldiers defeated the massive Midianite army using very unusual weapons — not swords or spears. Each soldier carried three surprising items into battle. What were these three items?
beginner.explanation: Each of Gideon's 300 men carried a trumpet, an empty clay jar, and a burning torch hidden inside the jar. At Gideon's signal, they blew their trumpets, smashed the jars, and held up the torches, causing panic and confusion in the Midianite camp.

beginner.question: God chose which of Gideon's soldiers would stay by testing them at a stream. The men drank water, and God selected those who drank in a specific way. How did the 300 chosen soldiers drink the water?
options: They lapped from their hands like a dog
They drank while lying face down
beginner.explanation: God told Gideon to watch how the men drank water from the stream. Those who cupped the water in their hands and lapped it up were chosen, while those who knelt down to drink were sent home. Only 300 men lapped the water.

beginner.question: Before attacking the Midianites, God told Gideon to sneak into the enemy camp at night. There, Gideon overheard a Midianite soldier telling another about a dream of something rolling into the camp and flattening a tent. What was it in the dream?
beginner.explanation: The Midianite soldier dreamed of a round loaf of barley bread tumbling into the camp and striking a tent so hard that it collapsed. His companion interpreted the dream as a sign that God had given the Midianites into Gideon's hands.

beginner.question: After Gideon's stunning victory over the Midianites, the grateful Israelites wanted to make him their king and start a royal dynasty. But Gideon refused the crown. What reason did Gideon give for turning down the offer?
options: He was too old to be king
The Lord would rule over them
He wanted to return to farming
beginner.explanation: Gideon told the people, 'I will not rule over you, nor will my son rule over you. The Lord will rule over you.' He recognised that Israel's true king was God Himself, and he did not want to take that place.

beginner.question: An angel appeared to a childless couple from the tribe of Dan and announced that they would have a son with supernatural strength. This child was to be set apart for God from birth as a Nazirite and would deliver Israel from the Philistines. What was his name?
beginner.explanation: Samson was born to Manoah and his wife after an angel announced his birth. He was set apart as a Nazirite from birth, meaning he could never cut his hair, drink wine, or touch anything dead. God gave him supernatural strength to fight the Philistines.

beginner.question: Samson had been set apart for God from birth as a Nazirite, and the source of his incredible strength was connected to something he was never supposed to cut. What was the source of Samson's supernatural strength?
beginner.explanation: Samson's uncut hair was the outward sign of his Nazirite vow and his special relationship with God. As long as his hair remained uncut, God's Spirit gave him supernatural strength. When Delilah had his hair cut, the Lord's strength departed from him.

beginner.question: In one of his famous feats of strength, young Samson once tore apart a ferocious animal with his bare hands while travelling with his parents. Later he found something sweet inside its carcass. What animal did Samson kill?
beginner.explanation: Samson killed a young lion with his bare hands when the Spirit of the Lord came powerfully upon him. Later, when he passed by the lion's carcass, he found that a swarm of bees had made honey inside it, which he scooped out and ate.

beginner.question: A Philistine woman named Delilah was paid a large sum of money by the Philistine rulers to discover the secret of Samson's incredible strength. Samson lied to her several times before finally telling the truth. How many times did Samson deceive Delilah?
beginner.explanation: Samson lied to Delilah three times about the source of his strength — telling her he could be bound with fresh bowstrings, new ropes, and woven hair. Each time the Philistines tried and failed. Only on the fourth attempt did he reveal the truth about his hair.

beginner.question: After Delilah discovered Samson's secret and had his hair cut while he slept, the Philistines captured the weakened Samson. What cruel thing did the Philistines do to Samson after capturing him?
beginner.explanation: The Philistines gouged out Samson's eyes, bound him with bronze shackles, and forced him to grind grain in a prison like an animal. It was a humiliating end for Israel's strongest man, caused by his disobedience to God's commands.

beginner.question: The Philistines brought the captured, blinded Samson to their temple to mock him during a great festival honouring their god. Thousands of Philistines gathered on the roof to watch and celebrate. What was the name of this Philistine god?
beginner.explanation: Dagon was the chief god of the Philistines. They believed Dagon had delivered Samson into their hands, and they held a great celebration in his temple. But God had one final act of strength in store for Samson.

beginner.question: In his final act, the blinded Samson prayed to God one last time for strength and pushed apart the two main pillars holding up the Philistine temple of Dagon, with thousands of people on the roof. What happened?
options: The temple collapsed, killing Samson and many Philistines
beginner.explanation: The temple collapsed and killed Samson along with about 3,000 Philistines. The Bible says Samson killed more Philistines in his death than in his entire life. His final prayer showed his return to trusting God in his last moments.

beginner.question: Before Samson was born, the angel told his mother that certain things were forbidden as part of the Nazirite vow her son would keep from birth. Besides never cutting his hair, what was Samson forbidden to drink?
beginner.explanation: As a Nazirite, Samson was forbidden from drinking wine or any other fermented drink, cutting his hair, or touching dead bodies. These restrictions symbolised his special dedication to God's service from before his birth.

beginner.question: God saw that the entire world had become extremely wicked, but one man stood out because he was righteous and walked faithfully with God. God told this man to build a massive boat to survive a coming flood. Who was this righteous man?
beginner.explanation: Noah was the only righteous man in a world filled with violence and wickedness. God chose to save Noah and his family because of his faithfulness. Noah obeyed God's unusual command to build an enormous boat, even though it had never rained before.

beginner.question: God gave Noah detailed instructions to build a massive boat to save his family and representatives of every animal species from a worldwide flood. This famous boat is one of the most well-known objects in the Bible. What is it called?
beginner.explanation: Noah's ark was an enormous wooden vessel, roughly 450 feet long, 75 feet wide, and 45 feet high. It had three decks and was designed by God to house Noah's family and pairs of every animal kind to preserve life during the great flood.

beginner.question: God instructed Noah to bring animals onto the ark so they would survive the great flood and repopulate the earth afterward. For most animal species, how many of each kind did God tell Noah to bring?
beginner.explanation: God told Noah to bring two of every kind of animal — one male and one female — to keep them alive during the flood. For clean animals used in sacrifice, Noah was to bring seven pairs, but for most species it was one pair.

beginner.question: After the floodwaters had covered the earth, Noah waited patiently and eventually sent out birds to test whether the water had receded. One bird came back carrying an olive branch in its beak, a sign of hope. What kind of bird was it?
beginner.explanation: Noah sent out a dove, and when it returned with a freshly plucked olive leaf, Noah knew the waters were receding and dry land was appearing. The dove with an olive branch has since become a universal symbol of peace and hope.

beginner.question: During the great flood in Noah's time, rain poured from the sky and water burst from the ground, covering the entire earth. For how many days and nights did it rain continuously during this catastrophic flood?
beginner.explanation: It rained for 40 days and 40 nights, and the floodwaters continued to rise as springs of the deep also burst open. The waters covered even the highest mountains, and it took many months for the earth to dry out completely.

beginner.question: After the flood ended and Noah's family safely left the ark, God placed a beautiful sign in the sky as a promise that He would never again destroy the entire earth with a flood. What was this sign of God's covenant?
beginner.explanation: God set a rainbow in the clouds as a visible reminder of His covenant promise to Noah and all living creatures. Every time a rainbow appears after rain, it serves as a sign that God remembers His promise never to flood the earth again.

beginner.question: Noah's three sons went into the ark with him and their wives, and after the flood they helped repopulate the earth. These three men are considered ancestors of all the nations of the world. What were their names?
beginner.explanation: Shem, Ham, and Japheth were Noah's three sons. After the flood, their descendants spread across the earth and became the ancestors of all nations. Shem's line eventually led to Abraham and the nation of Israel.

beginner.question: After Noah and his family finally left the ark and stepped onto dry land, Noah wanted to thank God for saving them from the flood. What was the first thing Noah built after leaving the ark?
beginner.explanation: Noah built an altar to the Lord and offered burnt sacrifices of clean animals and birds as an expression of gratitude. God was pleased with Noah's offering and made His covenant promise never to curse the ground or destroy all life with a flood again.

beginner.question: God gave Noah very specific building instructions for the ark, including the type of wood to use and that it should be coated with pitch to make it waterproof. What kind of wood was the ark constructed from?
beginner.explanation: God told Noah to build the ark from gopher wood, which many scholars believe was a type of cypress. The wood was to be coated inside and out with pitch (a tar-like substance) to make the massive vessel waterproof.

beginner.question: A powerful prophet of God challenged 450 prophets of a false god named Baal to a contest on Mount Carmel. He dared them to call down fire from heaven to prove whose god was real. What was this bold prophet's name?
beginner.explanation: Elijah stood alone against 450 prophets of Baal on Mount Carmel in one of the most dramatic events in the Old Testament. He challenged them to prove Baal's power by calling fire from heaven, and when they failed, Elijah called on God, who answered with fire.

beginner.question: On Mount Carmel, the prophets of Baal spent all day shouting and dancing, trying to get their god to send fire down on their sacrifice. They failed completely. Then Elijah prayed a simple prayer to the true God. What happened to Elijah's sacrifice?
options: A strong wind blew it away
Rain put out the altar fire
beginner.explanation: Fire fell from heaven and consumed Elijah's sacrifice, the wood, the stones, the soil, and even the water Elijah had poured over everything. The watching crowd fell to the ground and cried, 'The Lord — He is God!'

beginner.question: Before the famous contest on Mount Carmel, Elijah had boldly appeared before the wicked king of Israel and declared that there would be no rain in the land until Elijah said so, causing a severe three-year drought. Who was this wicked king?
beginner.explanation: King Ahab was one of the most wicked kings in Israel's history. He married the foreign princess Jezebel and allowed her to promote Baal worship throughout Israel. Elijah's declaration of drought was God's judgement on Ahab and the nation's idolatry.

beginner.question: After his triumphant victory on Mount Carmel, Elijah suddenly became afraid and ran for his life when a powerful and wicked queen sent a death threat against him. This queen was married to King Ahab. What was her name?
beginner.explanation: Queen Jezebel was a Phoenician princess who brought Baal worship into Israel and had many of God's prophets killed. When she heard that Elijah had defeated and killed her prophets of Baal, she sent a messenger threatening to kill him within 24 hours.

beginner.question: When Elijah fled in fear from Queen Jezebel, he collapsed under a tree in the wilderness, exhausted and wanting to die. God sent angels to care for him with food and water, then told him to journey to a specific mountain. What mountain was it?
beginner.explanation: God directed the exhausted Elijah to travel to Mount Horeb, also known as Mount Sinai — the same mountain where God had given Moses the Ten Commandments. There, God spoke to Elijah in a gentle whisper and restored his courage and mission.

beginner.question: At the end of Elijah's earthly life, God took him to heaven in a dramatic and unique way, rather than letting him die a natural death. Elijah is one of only two people in the Bible who never experienced death. How was Elijah taken to heaven?
options: He walked through a shining door
In a chariot of fire and a whirlwind
He disappeared in a flash of light
beginner.explanation: A chariot of fire and horses of fire appeared, and Elijah was taken up to heaven in a whirlwind while his servant Elisha watched. This dramatic departure confirmed Elijah's special status as one of God's greatest prophets.

beginner.question: Before Elijah was taken to heaven in the chariot of fire, his faithful servant and successor asked to receive a double portion of Elijah's spirit so he could continue the prophetic ministry. What was this servant's name?
beginner.explanation: Elisha was Elijah's faithful companion and successor. He asked for a double portion of Elijah's spirit — not twice the power, but the inheritance of the firstborn son, signifying that he was Elijah's spiritual heir. God granted this request.

beginner.question: During the great drought Elijah had declared, God provided for the prophet in a miraculous way by sending birds to bring him food while he camped beside a brook. What did the birds bring Elijah to eat each morning and evening?
beginner.explanation: God sent ravens to bring Elijah bread and meat both in the morning and evening while he lived beside the Brook Cherith. He drank from the brook until it dried up due to the drought, after which God sent him to a widow in Zarephath.

beginner.question: Jesus chose a simple fisherman from the Sea of Galilee to become one of His closest disciples and eventually the leader of the early church. Before Jesus gave him a new name, this disciple was known as Simon. What new name did Jesus give him?
beginner.explanation: Jesus gave Simon the name Peter, which means 'rock,' saying, 'On this rock I will build my church.' Peter became the leader of the twelve apostles and played a central role in spreading the gospel after Jesus' resurrection and ascension.

beginner.question: On the night Jesus was arrested in the Garden of Gethsemane, Peter denied knowing Jesus three times, exactly as Jesus had predicted. Immediately after Peter's third denial, something happened that made Peter weep bitterly. What was it?
beginner.explanation: A rooster crowed immediately after Peter's third denial, just as Jesus had predicted at the Last Supper. The sound reminded Peter of Jesus' words, and he went outside and wept bitterly, devastated by his failure to stand by his Lord.

beginner.question: Peter and his brother were both fishermen casting nets into the Sea of Galilee when Jesus walked by and called them to follow Him, saying He would make them 'fishers of men.' Who was Peter's brother?
beginner.explanation: Andrew was Peter's brother. Andrew had actually been a disciple of John the Baptist first and was one of the very first to follow Jesus. He then brought his brother Simon Peter to meet Jesus, leading to one of the most important partnerships in early Christianity.

beginner.question: After Jesus ascended to heaven, Peter preached a powerful sermon on the day of Pentecost in Jerusalem when the Holy Spirit came upon the believers. His sermon was so effective that a large number of people believed in Jesus that day. About how many?
beginner.explanation: About 3,000 people believed in Jesus and were baptised on the day of Pentecost after Peter's sermon. This explosive growth marked the dramatic beginning of the Christian church, powered by the Holy Spirit.

beginner.question: King Herod had Peter arrested and imprisoned, planning to put him on trial the next day. That night, while Peter slept between two soldiers chained to both of them, God sent someone to rescue him. Who freed Peter from prison?
beginner.explanation: An angel appeared in Peter's prison cell, causing his chains to fall off. The angel led Peter past the guards and through the iron gate of the prison, which opened by itself. Peter then went to a house where believers were praying for him.

beginner.question: After His resurrection, Jesus appeared to Peter and the disciples by the Sea of Galilee and asked Peter the same question three times, giving Peter a chance to affirm his love and reverse his three denials. What question did Jesus ask Peter?
beginner.explanation: Jesus asked Peter 'Do you love me?' three times, once for each of Peter's three denials. Each time Peter affirmed his love, Jesus responded by commissioning him to care for His followers, saying 'Feed my lambs' and 'Feed my sheep.'

beginner.question: In one of the most memorable miracles involving the disciples, Peter stepped out of a boat and actually walked on the surface of the water toward Jesus during a storm. But then something went wrong. What happened when Peter lost focus?
options: He was swept away by a wave
He fell back into the boat
beginner.explanation: Peter began to sink when he took his eyes off Jesus and focused on the fierce wind and waves around him. He cried out, 'Lord, save me!' and Jesus immediately reached out His hand and caught him, saying, 'You of little faith, why did you doubt?'

beginner.question: At a place called Caesarea Philippi, Jesus asked His disciples the most important question: 'Who do you say I am?' Peter gave a bold and famous answer that delighted Jesus. What did Peter declare?
options: 'You are the Christ, the Son of the living God'
beginner.explanation: Peter declared, 'You are the Christ, the Son of the living God.' Jesus responded that this truth had been revealed to Peter by God the Father Himself, and that upon this confession of faith, Jesus would build His church.

beginner.question: Before becoming one of the greatest missionaries and authors in the New Testament, this man actively persecuted the early Christians. He even stood by approvingly while Stephen, the first Christian martyr, was stoned to death. What was his original name?
beginner.explanation: Saul of Tarsus was a devout Pharisee who believed Christians were dangerous heretics. He obtained official letters to arrest Christians and was present at Stephen's death. His dramatic conversion on the road to Damascus transformed him into the apostle Paul.

beginner.question: On the road to Damascus, where Saul was heading to arrest more Christians, a blinding light from heaven suddenly knocked him to the ground. He heard the voice of Jesus asking, 'Why are you persecuting me?' What happened to Saul after this encounter?
options: He was blind for three days
beginner.explanation: Saul was struck blind for three days after his encounter with Jesus on the road to Damascus. His companions led him into the city, where he neither ate nor drank for three days until God sent Ananias to restore his sight.

beginner.question: After Saul was blinded on the road to Damascus, God sent a believer in Damascus to lay hands on Saul so he could receive his sight back and be filled with the Holy Spirit. This man was initially afraid because he knew of Saul's reputation. What was his name?
beginner.explanation: Ananias was a faithful disciple in Damascus whom God spoke to in a vision, telling him to go find Saul. Despite his fear of Saul's reputation as a persecutor, Ananias obeyed God, laid hands on Saul, and said, 'Brother Saul, receive your sight.'

beginner.question: After his conversion, Paul (formerly Saul) dedicated his life to spreading the gospel across the Roman Empire, travelling thousands of miles by land and sea to establish churches. How many major missionary journeys did Paul undertake?
beginner.explanation: Paul went on three major missionary journeys, during which he travelled across modern-day Turkey, Greece, and other parts of the Roman Empire. He preached in synagogues, marketplaces, and homes, establishing churches in cities like Corinth, Ephesus, and Philippi.

beginner.question: Paul wrote many letters to churches and individuals that became books of the New Testament. These letters, called epistles, contain essential Christian teaching on faith, love, and living for God. Approximately how many New Testament books did Paul write?
beginner.explanation: Paul is credited with writing 13 books of the New Testament, from Romans to Philemon. These letters were addressed to churches he had founded or visited, and to individuals like Timothy and Titus. They form a large part of Christian theology.

beginner.question: During a missionary journey, Paul and his companion Silas were beaten and thrown into prison in the city of Philippi for casting out a spirit from a slave girl. While they sang hymns at midnight, something dramatic happened. What was it?
options: An angel appeared and opened the doors
A violent earthquake shook the prison open
The guards fell asleep and they escaped
A fire burned through the chains
beginner.explanation: A violent earthquake shook the foundations of the prison, causing all the doors to fly open and everyone's chains to come loose. The jailer, seeing the open doors, was about to kill himself but Paul stopped him. The jailer and his family became believers that night.

beginner.question: Paul had a faithful young companion whom he mentored as a spiritual father. Paul wrote two letters to this young man, encouraging him to be strong in faith and to lead the church well. Who was this young protégé?
beginner.explanation: Timothy was Paul's beloved spiritual son and ministry partner. Paul wrote him two personal letters (1 and 2 Timothy), urging him not to let anyone look down on his youth and to guard the gospel message entrusted to him.

beginner.question: Paul was a Roman citizen by birth, which gave him special legal rights that most people in the empire did not have. He used this citizenship to appeal his legal case to the highest authority in Rome. To whom did Paul appeal?
beginner.explanation: As a Roman citizen, Paul had the legal right to appeal his case to the emperor himself. When he was on trial before the governor Festus, Paul invoked this right by declaring, 'I appeal to Caesar!' This resulted in Paul being sent to Rome.

beginner.question: When Joshua sent two spies to scout the city of Jericho before the Israelite invasion, a woman who lived in the city walls risked her life to hide them from the king's soldiers. What was this brave woman's name?
beginner.explanation: Rahab was a woman living in Jericho whose house was built right into the city wall. Despite the danger, she hid the Israelite spies and helped them escape, because she believed that God had given the land to Israel.

beginner.question: Rahab hid the two Israelite spies from the king of Jericho's soldiers by taking them up to her rooftop and concealing them under something she had laid out to dry. What did Rahab hide the spies under?
beginner.explanation: Rahab hid the spies under stalks of flax that she had laid out on her flat rooftop to dry. Flax was used to make linen, and it was common to dry it on rooftops. The soldiers searched but did not think to look under the flax.

beginner.question: After hiding the spies, Rahab helped them escape from Jericho because her house was built into the city wall. She used a long item to lower them down from her window so they could flee to the hills. What did she use?
beginner.explanation: Rahab lowered the spies through her window using a scarlet (red) rope, since her house was built into the outer city wall. This same rope became part of the sign that would later save Rahab and her family when Jericho was destroyed.

beginner.question: Before leaving, the Israelite spies told Rahab to mark her house with a specific item hanging from her window so that the Israelite army would know to spare everyone inside. What was Rahab supposed to display?
beginner.explanation: The spies told Rahab to tie a scarlet cord in her window so the Israelites would recognise her house and spare everyone inside during the attack on Jericho. Rahab obeyed, and when the walls fell, her section remained standing and her family was saved.

beginner.question: Rahab told the Israelite spies that the people of Jericho were absolutely terrified because they had heard about God's mighty acts on behalf of Israel. What specific miraculous event had the people of Jericho heard about?
options: The parting of the Red Sea
beginner.explanation: Rahab told the spies that everyone in Jericho had heard how God parted the Red Sea for the Israelites when they came out of Egypt. This news had spread throughout the region and filled the people with dread, because they knew Israel's God was powerful.

beginner.question: Despite her sinful past, Rahab was honoured by God and included in one of the most important lists in the New Testament. Her faith and bravery earned her a place alongside Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob. What list was Rahab included in?
beginner.explanation: Rahab is listed in Matthew's genealogy of Jesus Christ. She married an Israelite named Salmon and became the mother of Boaz, who married Ruth. Through this line came King David and ultimately Jesus, showing God's grace in including Rahab in the Messiah's ancestry.

beginner.question: After Jericho fell, Rahab was welcomed into the nation of Israel and married an Israelite man. She became the mother of Boaz, who later married Ruth the Moabitess. What was Rahab's husband's name?
beginner.explanation: Rahab married Salmon, an Israelite from the tribe of Judah. Their son Boaz inherited his mother's kindness and showed great compassion to Ruth, a foreign widow. This family line led directly to King David and then to Jesus Christ.

beginner.question: Rahab's home was uniquely positioned in Jericho, which was how she was able to lower the spies down to escape outside the city. Where exactly was Rahab's house built?
options: On a hill outside the city
beginner.explanation: Rahab's house was built directly into the thick city wall of Jericho, a common practice for lower-class residents in ancient cities. This location allowed her to lower the spies from her window directly to the ground outside the city.

beginner.question: Three young Jewish men in Babylon refused to bow down and worship a giant golden statue that the king had erected, even though everyone else in the kingdom obeyed. What were the Babylonian names of these three brave men?
beginner.explanation: Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego (their Babylonian names) refused to worship the golden image, declaring that their God could save them from the furnace, and even if He did not, they would still never worship a false god.

beginner.question: King Nebuchadnezzar built a golden statue 90 feet tall and commanded everyone to bow down when they heard music playing. When Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego refused, the furious king threatened them. What did he threaten to do?
options: Throw them in prison for life
Throw them into a blazing furnace
beginner.explanation: Nebuchadnezzar threatened to throw the three men into a blazing furnace if they refused to worship his golden statue. He arrogantly asked, 'What god will be able to rescue you from my hand?' — not realising he was about to find out.

beginner.question: When Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego were thrown into the blazing furnace, King Nebuchadnezzar jumped up in amazement because he saw something incredible inside the fire. Instead of three men, he saw four figures walking around unharmed. How did the king describe the fourth figure?
options: Like a son of the gods
beginner.explanation: Nebuchadnezzar said the fourth figure looked 'like a son of the gods.' Many believe this was a pre-incarnate appearance of Christ or an angel sent by God. The presence of this fourth figure protected the three men from any harm in the fire.

beginner.question: King Nebuchadnezzar was so furious at the three men's refusal to bow to his golden statue that he ordered the furnace heated far beyond its normal temperature. How much hotter did the king order the furnace heated?
beginner.explanation: Nebuchadnezzar ordered the furnace heated seven times hotter than usual. It was so intensely hot that the soldiers who threw the three men into the furnace were themselves killed by the extreme heat.

beginner.question: When Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego walked out of the blazing furnace completely unharmed, the astonished officials gathered around to examine them. What remarkable thing did they notice about the three men's condition?
options: Their clothes were white as snow
Not a hair was singed and there was no smell of fire
Their skin had turned to gold
beginner.explanation: The officials examined the three men and found that the fire had done absolutely no damage — not a hair on their heads was singed, their robes were not scorched, and there was not even a smell of smoke on them. God's protection was total and complete.

beginner.question: Before they were given Babylonian names, the three friends who survived the furnace had Hebrew names. The one renamed Shadrach had a Hebrew name meaning 'God is gracious.' What was Shadrach's original Hebrew name?
beginner.explanation: Hananiah was Shadrach's Hebrew name. When the young Jewish captives arrived in Babylon, the chief official gave them Babylonian names as part of an effort to assimilate them into Babylonian culture. Hananiah became Shadrach, Mishael became Meshach, and Azariah became Abednego.

beginner.question: When Nebuchadnezzar gave Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego one last chance to bow before the golden statue or face the furnace, the three men gave a courageous reply. What bold thing did they tell the king?
options: They would bow only if the king bowed first
Even if God did not save them, they would not worship the statue
They would pray and God would destroy the statue
They challenged the king to enter the furnace with them
beginner.explanation: The three men told the king that their God was able to deliver them from the furnace, but even if He chose not to, they still would never serve the king's gods or worship his golden image. This is one of the greatest statements of faith in the entire Bible.

beginner.question: After witnessing the miracle of the three men surviving the blazing furnace completely unharmed, the astonished King Nebuchadnezzar responded by making an official decree. What did the king decree?
options: All people must worship the God of Israel
No one may speak against the God of Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego
The golden statue must be torn down
The three men must become priests
beginner.explanation: Nebuchadnezzar decreed that anyone who spoke against the God of Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego would be cut to pieces and their houses destroyed, because no other god could save like this. He also promoted the three men to even higher positions in Babylon.

beginner.question: In the early church, seven men were chosen to help distribute food to widows. One of these men, full of God's grace and power, performed great wonders among the people and became the first person to die for the Christian faith. Who was he?
beginner.explanation: Stephen was the first Christian martyr. He was a man of outstanding faith who performed signs and wonders. His bold preaching angered the religious leaders, who had him dragged before the Sanhedrin and ultimately stoned to death.

beginner.question: The religious leaders in Jerusalem could not defeat Stephen's wisdom when he debated with them, so they brought false witnesses to accuse him before the Jewish ruling council. What did these false witnesses accuse Stephen of?
options: Speaking blasphemy against Moses and God
beginner.explanation: The false witnesses claimed Stephen spoke blasphemous words against Moses and God, and against the temple and the law. In reality, Stephen was proclaiming Jesus as the fulfilment of Moses' teachings and the temple's purpose.

beginner.question: Before his death, Stephen gave a long and powerful speech to the Jewish ruling council, recounting the history of God's faithfulness to Israel from the very beginning. What was this ruling council called?
beginner.explanation: The Sanhedrin was the supreme Jewish court and ruling council, consisting of 70 elders plus the high priest. Stephen stood before this powerful body and fearlessly delivered a sweeping history of Israel, showing how the people had repeatedly rejected God's messengers.

beginner.question: As Stephen was being stoned to death by the angry crowd, he looked up to heaven and saw an incredible vision that he described aloud. What did Stephen see in his final moments?
options: Jesus standing at the right hand of God
beginner.explanation: Stephen, filled with the Holy Spirit, gazed into heaven and saw the glory of God with Jesus standing at His right hand. He cried out, 'Look! I see heaven open and the Son of Man standing at the right hand of God!' This vision comforted him as he died.

beginner.question: Stephen's very last words before he died echoed something Jesus had said while He was dying on the cross. As the stones struck him, Stephen knelt down and cried out a prayer. What did Stephen ask God to do?
options: 'Do not hold this sin against them'
beginner.explanation: Stephen's final prayer was, 'Lord, do not hold this sin against them.' This echoed Jesus' prayer on the cross, 'Father, forgive them.' Stephen showed Christlike forgiveness even as he was being killed, demonstrating remarkable faith and love.

beginner.question: A young man who would later become the great apostle Paul was present at Stephen's stoning and actually approved of it. He guarded the coats of those throwing the stones. What was this young man's name at the time?
beginner.explanation: Saul of Tarsus (later known as Paul) watched over the cloaks of those who stoned Stephen and gave his full approval. This event was significant because Saul went on to become one of Christianity's greatest advocates — a transformation Stephen's martyrdom may have helped spark.

beginner.question: Stephen was one of seven men the apostles appointed to serve the early church. These seven were chosen to resolve a specific complaint about unfair food distribution. Which group had complained about being overlooked?
beginner.explanation: The Greek-speaking (Hellenistic) Jewish widows complained that they were being overlooked in the daily food distribution, while the Hebrew-speaking widows were being cared for. The apostles appointed seven men, including Stephen, to ensure fair and loving distribution.

beginner.question: A Jewish man serving as cupbearer to the Persian king received devastating news that the walls of Jerusalem were broken down and its gates had been burned. He wept, prayed, and asked the king for permission to go and rebuild them. Who was this man?
beginner.explanation: Nehemiah was deeply moved when he heard about Jerusalem's ruined state. Despite having a comfortable position in the Persian court, his love for his homeland drove him to fast, pray, and courageously ask King Artaxerxes for permission and resources to rebuild the city walls.

beginner.question: Before returning to Jerusalem, Nehemiah held an important and trusted position in the Persian king's court. His role involved tasting the king's drinks to ensure they were not poisoned. What was Nehemiah's job title?
beginner.explanation: Nehemiah was the cupbearer to King Artaxerxes, a position of great trust and access to the king. The cupbearer tasted the king's wine to verify it wasn't poisoned, which meant the king trusted Nehemiah with his life.

beginner.question: Nehemiah asked the Persian king for permission and resources to travel to Jerusalem and rebuild its broken walls. The king granted his request and even provided letters of safe passage and timber. What was this Persian king's name?
beginner.explanation: King Artaxerxes of Persia granted Nehemiah's request to go to Jerusalem. He provided official letters for safe passage through the territories and an order for timber from the king's forest. God had moved the king's heart to support Nehemiah's mission.

beginner.question: Despite constant opposition, threats, and attempts at sabotage from enemies, Nehemiah and the people of Jerusalem worked tirelessly to rebuild the city walls. Remarkably, how long did it take to complete the entire wall?
beginner.explanation: The walls of Jerusalem were rebuilt in an astonishing 52 days. When the surrounding nations heard this, they were afraid because they recognised that this work had been accomplished with the help of God. It was a miraculous achievement.

beginner.question: Nehemiah faced persistent opposition from enemies who tried to stop the rebuilding through mockery, threats, and conspiracies. Two of the main opponents were Sanballat the Horonite and another man. Who was this second main opponent?
beginner.explanation: Tobiah the Ammonite was one of Nehemiah's main opponents, along with Sanballat. They mocked the rebuilding effort, saying even a fox could knock down the wall, and later plotted violence and schemes to stop the work. But Nehemiah persevered.

beginner.question: Because of constant threats from enemies during the rebuilding of Jerusalem's walls, Nehemiah organised the workers in an unusual way. Half the workers built while the other half did something else. What did the other half do?
beginner.explanation: Nehemiah armed half the workers with spears, shields, bows, and armour to stand guard while the other half built. Even the builders worked with one hand while holding a weapon in the other. They were ready for an attack at any moment.

beginner.question: After the walls were completed, the people gathered together and a priest read aloud from God's Law for hours while the people stood and listened. Many wept as they heard the words. Who read the Book of the Law to the people?
beginner.explanation: Ezra the priest and scribe read the Book of the Law (Torah) to the assembled people from early morning until midday. The people stood and listened attentively, and when they understood the words, they wept because they realised how far they had strayed from God's commands.

beginner.question: Nehemiah discovered that wealthy Jewish nobles were exploiting their own people by charging heavy interest on loans and taking their lands and children as collateral during hard times. What did Nehemiah demand the wealthy do?
options: Pay a tax to the temple
Return the lands and stop charging interest
Give half their wealth to the poor
beginner.explanation: Nehemiah confronted the nobles and officials, demanding they return the fields, vineyards, olive groves, and houses they had taken, and stop charging excessive interest. The wealthy agreed, and Nehemiah made them take an oath before the priests to keep their promise.

beginner.question: Nehemiah's enemies, Sanballat and Geshem, sent messages inviting Nehemiah to meet them on the plain of Ono, likely planning to ambush and harm him. Nehemiah saw through their scheme and gave a famous reply. What did he say?
options: 'I am doing a great work and cannot come down'
beginner.explanation: Nehemiah replied, 'I am doing a great work and cannot come down. Why should the work stop while I leave it and come down to you?' He sent this same reply four times when they kept trying. His focus and determination kept the project on track.

beginner.question: God told a man named Abraham to leave his homeland in Ur of the Chaldeans and go to a new land that God would show him. Abraham obeyed God's call and left his home, even though he did not know where he was going.
beginner.explanation: Abraham demonstrated extraordinary faith by obeying God's call to leave everything familiar behind. He departed from Ur with his wife Sarah and nephew Lot, trusting God completely even without knowing the destination.

beginner.question: Abraham's wife Sarah was very old when God promised them a son. She was about 90 years old when their promised son Isaac was finally born, making his birth a miraculous fulfilment of God's promise.
beginner.explanation: Sarah was approximately 90 years old and Abraham was 100 when Isaac was born. His name means 'he laughs' because Sarah had laughed when she first heard the seemingly impossible promise.

beginner.question: God tested Abraham's faith by asking him to sacrifice his son Isaac on a mountain. Abraham refused to obey because he could not bear to lose his beloved son.
beginner.explanation: Abraham did not refuse — he obeyed God's difficult command, trusting that God had a plan. He took Isaac to Mount Moriah and was about to carry out the sacrifice when an angel stopped him at the last moment, and God provided a ram as a substitute.

beginner.question: Before God changed his name to Abraham, this father of many nations was originally called Abram, which means 'exalted father.' God changed his name as part of the covenant promise.
beginner.explanation: God changed Abram's name to Abraham, meaning 'father of many nations,' when He established His covenant with him at the age of 99. The name change signified the expanded scope of God's promise.

beginner.question: God promised Abraham that his descendants would be as numerous as the fish in the sea, filling every ocean on the earth.
beginner.explanation: God actually promised Abraham that his descendants would be as numerous as the stars in the sky and the sand on the seashore, not the fish in the sea. God took Abraham outside and pointed to the night sky to illustrate this promise.

beginner.question: Abraham was from the land of Egypt before God called him to journey to the land of Canaan, the Promised Land.
beginner.explanation: Abraham was not from Egypt. He was from Ur of the Chaldeans, in Mesopotamia (modern-day Iraq). God called him to leave Ur and travel to Canaan. Abraham did visit Egypt briefly during a famine, but it was not his homeland.

beginner.question: When God tested Abraham by asking him to sacrifice Isaac, an angel stopped Abraham at the last moment. God then provided a ram caught in a thicket as a substitute sacrifice.
beginner.explanation: Just as Abraham raised the knife, the angel of the Lord called from heaven and stopped him. Abraham then saw a ram caught by its horns in a nearby thicket, and he sacrificed the ram instead of his son.

beginner.question: Abraham's servant found a wife for Isaac at a well in a distant land. The young woman named Rebekah generously offered to draw water for the servant and all his camels.
beginner.explanation: Abraham sent his servant to find a wife for Isaac among his relatives. The servant prayed for guidance, and Rebekah showed remarkable kindness by offering water to the servant and his ten camels — a significant amount of work that demonstrated her character.

beginner.question: Abraham's nephew Lot settled in the prosperous city of Jerusalem near Abraham after they decided to part ways because the land could not support both their large flocks.
beginner.explanation: Lot settled near Sodom in the lush Jordan Valley, not Jerusalem. He chose what looked like the best land, but Sodom turned out to be a wicked city. Abraham stayed in the hill country of Canaan.

beginner.question: Baby Moses was placed in a waterproof basket and set among the reeds of the Nile River to save him from Pharaoh's order to kill all Hebrew baby boys. Pharaoh's daughter found him and adopted him.
beginner.explanation: Moses' mother hid him for three months, then placed him in a papyrus basket coated with tar and pitch among the reeds. Pharaoh's daughter found the crying baby, felt compassion, and raised him in the royal palace.

beginner.question: When Moses saw the burning bush in the desert, God told him to go to Babylon and free the Israelites from slavery there.
beginner.explanation: God sent Moses to Egypt, not Babylon, to free the Israelites. The Israelites were enslaved in Egypt under Pharaoh. Babylon is a completely different ancient empire that would come into the story centuries later during Daniel's time.

beginner.question: God sent ten plagues upon Egypt to convince Pharaoh to let the Israelites go free. The final and most devastating plague was the death of every firstborn son in Egypt.
beginner.explanation: The tenth plague was the death of the firstborn, striking every Egyptian household from Pharaoh's palace to the prisoner's dungeon. The Israelites were protected by putting lamb's blood on their doorposts — the origin of the Passover celebration.

beginner.question: Moses parted the Red Sea by stretching out his hand as God commanded, allowing the Israelites to walk through on dry ground with walls of water on both sides.
beginner.explanation: God told Moses to stretch his hand over the sea, and a strong east wind drove the water back all night, creating a path of dry ground. The Israelites crossed safely, but when the Egyptian army followed, the waters returned and destroyed them.

beginner.question: Moses received the Ten Commandments from God on Mount Carmel in northern Israel, near the Mediterranean Sea.
beginner.explanation: Moses received the Ten Commandments on Mount Sinai (also called Mount Horeb) in the wilderness, not Mount Carmel. Mount Carmel is where Elijah later had his famous contest with the prophets of Baal — a completely different event.

beginner.question: Moses' older brother Aaron served as his spokesman when they went before Pharaoh to demand the release of the Israelites, because Moses felt he was not a good speaker.
beginner.explanation: Moses told God he was slow of speech, so God appointed Aaron to speak on his behalf. Aaron accompanied Moses to Egypt and spoke to Pharaoh, relaying God's message to let His people go.

beginner.question: Moses was allowed to enter the Promised Land briefly as a reward for his many years of faithful leadership, before dying peacefully in his own home.
beginner.explanation: Moses was not allowed to enter the Promised Land because he disobeyed God at Meribah by striking a rock instead of speaking to it. God allowed him only to see the land from the top of Mount Nebo before he died there.

beginner.question: While Moses was on Mount Sinai receiving the Ten Commandments for 40 days, the impatient Israelites made a golden calf from their jewellery and began worshipping it as an idol.
beginner.explanation: The people grew anxious when Moses was on the mountain for so long. They pressured Aaron to make gods for them, so Aaron collected their gold earrings and formed a golden calf. Moses was furious when he came down and saw the idolatry.

beginner.question: The Israelites wandered in the wilderness for 20 years before finally entering the Promised Land under Joshua's leadership.
beginner.explanation: The Israelites wandered for 40 years, not 20. God declared that the entire generation who refused to trust Him after the spies' report would die in the wilderness. Only after 40 years did the next generation, led by Joshua, enter the Promised Land.

beginner.question: David was a young shepherd boy from Bethlehem when he volunteered to fight the giant Philistine warrior named Goliath, whom the entire Israelite army was too afraid to face.
beginner.explanation: David was the youngest son of Jesse, tending his father's sheep in Bethlehem. He came to the battlefield to bring food to his older brothers and was outraged that Goliath was taunting God's army. He bravely volunteered when no soldier would.

beginner.question: David defeated the mighty giant Goliath by using a sword and shield in fierce hand-to-hand combat, overpowering the Philistine with his superior fighting skills.
beginner.explanation: David did not use a sword and shield. He refused King Saul's armour and instead used his shepherd's sling and a smooth stone from a nearby stream. He struck Goliath in the forehead with the stone, and the giant fell face down on the ground.

beginner.question: David was a talented musician who played the harp so beautifully that King Saul would call for David whenever Saul was tormented by an evil spirit, because the music brought him relief.
beginner.explanation: David was known for his skill with the lyre (harp). When an evil spirit troubled King Saul, David's playing would bring peace and relief. This musical ability was one of the first reasons David was brought into the royal court.

beginner.question: David was the oldest of Jesse's sons and was already serving in Saul's army at the battle when Goliath first challenged the Israelites to send out a champion.
beginner.explanation: David was actually the youngest of Jesse's eight sons, not the oldest. His three oldest brothers were the ones serving in Saul's army. David was still at home tending his father's sheep and only came to the battlefield to deliver food.

beginner.question: David wrote many of the Psalms in the Bible, which are songs and poems expressing praise, prayer, sorrow, repentance, and deep trust in God.
beginner.explanation: David is credited with writing roughly half of the 150 Psalms, including famous ones like Psalm 23 ('The Lord is my shepherd') and Psalm 51 (his prayer of repentance). His musical and poetic gifts made him known as the 'sweet psalmist of Israel.'

beginner.question: David became king of Israel immediately after killing Goliath, and King Saul willingly handed over the throne to him.
beginner.explanation: David did not become king immediately. After defeating Goliath, Saul grew jealous of David's popularity and spent years trying to hunt him down and kill him. David had to flee and hide for a long time before finally becoming king after Saul's death.

beginner.question: David had a close and loyal friendship with Jonathan, the son of King Saul. Jonathan remained devoted to David even though he knew David would become king instead of himself.
beginner.explanation: Jonathan and David's friendship is one of the most celebrated in Scripture. Jonathan unselfishly supported David's future kingship, warned David when Saul tried to kill him, and even risked his own father's anger to protect his friend.

beginner.question: God promised King David that his royal line would last forever, a promise that was ultimately fulfilled through Jesus Christ, who is called the 'Son of David' in the New Testament.
beginner.explanation: God's covenant with David promised that his throne would be established forever. This promise found its ultimate fulfilment in Jesus, who was born in David's line and whose kingdom has no end.

beginner.question: David was from the town of Nazareth, which was a small village in the northern part of Israel, in the region of Galilee.
beginner.explanation: David was from Bethlehem, not Nazareth. Bethlehem is located in the southern region of Judah. Nazareth is a town in Galilee associated with Jesus' upbringing, centuries after David. David's hometown of Bethlehem later became the birthplace of Jesus.

beginner.question: Queen Esther bravely risked her life by approaching King Ahasuerus without being summoned, because anyone who came uninvited to the king could be put to death unless the king extended his golden sceptre.
beginner.explanation: Persian law was strict — approaching the king uninvited was punishable by death. Esther fasted and prayed for three days before taking this enormous risk. When the king saw her, he was pleased and extended his golden sceptre, sparing her life.

beginner.question: Esther was raised by her wealthy father, a successful merchant who lived in the city of Susa and arranged for her to enter the king's palace.
beginner.explanation: Esther was an orphan whose parents had died. She was raised by her older cousin Mordecai, who adopted her as his own daughter. It was not a wealthy father but a devoted cousin who cared for her.

beginner.question: The villain Haman plotted to destroy all the Jewish people in the Persian Empire because Esther's cousin Mordecai refused to bow down to him, which filled Haman with furious rage.
beginner.explanation: Haman was so enraged by Mordecai's refusal to bow that he decided to destroy not just Mordecai, but all the Jewish people throughout the Persian Empire. He obtained a royal decree for their annihilation on a date chosen by casting lots.

beginner.question: Before approaching the king to plead for her people's lives, Esther asked all the Jews in the city of Susa to fast for three days and nights on her behalf.
beginner.explanation: Esther recognised she needed spiritual support before this dangerous mission. She told Mordecai to gather all the Jews in Susa to fast for three days. She and her maids also fasted, showing her dependence on God's help.

beginner.question: Haman was honoured and promoted by King Ahasuerus after his plot against the Jewish people was discovered, living out his days in great prosperity.
beginner.explanation: Haman was not honoured — he was executed. When Queen Esther exposed Haman's plot to destroy her people, the enraged king ordered Haman to be hanged on the very gallows Haman had built for Mordecai. Justice was served dramatically.

beginner.question: The Jewish holiday of Purim celebrates the deliverance of the Jewish people from Haman's evil plot, thanks to Queen Esther's courageous intervention before King Ahasuerus.
beginner.explanation: Purim is celebrated annually by Jewish communities worldwide. The name comes from 'pur,' meaning 'lot,' because Haman had cast lots to choose the date for the planned massacre. The festival includes reading the book of Esther, feasting, and giving gifts.

beginner.question: Queen Esther openly told everyone in the palace that she was Jewish from the very moment she became queen, proudly displaying her heritage.
beginner.explanation: Esther kept her Jewish identity a secret on Mordecai's instruction. She did not reveal her nationality or family background to anyone in the palace until the critical moment when she needed to plead with the king to save her people from Haman's plot.

beginner.question: Mordecai told Esther that perhaps God had placed her in the royal palace as queen 'for such a time as this' — meaning her position was part of God's plan to save her people.
