beginner.explanation: Mordecai's famous words reminded Esther that her rise to queen was not mere coincidence but divine providence. He told her that deliverance for the Jews would come from somewhere even if she remained silent, but that she had been positioned by God for this very moment.

beginner.question: Ruth was a woman from the land of Moab who demonstrated remarkable loyalty to her Israelite mother-in-law Naomi, refusing to leave her side after both their husbands died.
beginner.explanation: Ruth's loyalty to Naomi is one of the most beautiful examples of devotion in the Bible. Despite having every reason to stay in her homeland, Ruth chose to accompany Naomi to Bethlehem, leaving behind her family, friends, and culture.

beginner.question: Ruth famously told Naomi, 'Where you go, I will go; where you stay, I will stay. Your people will be my people, and your God my God.' These words have become one of the most beloved declarations of commitment in the Bible.
beginner.explanation: Ruth's declaration to Naomi is among the most quoted passages in Scripture. It demonstrates Ruth's total commitment — not just to Naomi personally, but also to Naomi's people and to Naomi's God. It was a pledge of lifelong loyalty.

beginner.question: When Ruth arrived in Bethlehem with Naomi, she found work as a servant in the king's palace, where she impressed the royal household with her hard work.
beginner.explanation: Ruth did not work in a palace. She went to the fields to collect leftover grain, a practice called gleaning, which was a provision in God's law for the poor and foreigners. She happened to work in the field of Boaz, a wealthy landowner.

beginner.question: Boaz was the kind and wealthy landowner in Bethlehem who noticed Ruth's hard work in his fields, showed her great generosity, and eventually married her.
beginner.explanation: Boaz was impressed by Ruth's devotion to Naomi and her willingness to work hard in a foreign land. He instructed his workers to leave extra grain for her and ensured her safety. As a close relative of Naomi's late husband, he had the right to marry Ruth as kinsman-redeemer.

beginner.question: Ruth and Boaz had a daughter named Miriam who grew up to become a famous prophetess in the land of Israel.
beginner.explanation: Ruth and Boaz had a son, not a daughter, and his name was Obed, not Miriam. Obed became the father of Jesse, who was the father of King David. Miriam was Moses' sister, who lived centuries before Ruth.

beginner.question: Ruth was the great-grandmother of King David, which placed her — a foreign woman from Moab — directly in the ancestral line that would ultimately lead to Jesus Christ.
beginner.explanation: Ruth married Boaz, and their son was Obed. Obed's son was Jesse, and Jesse's son was David. This means Ruth, a Moabite woman, was David's great-grandmother and is listed in the genealogy of Jesus in the Gospel of Matthew.

beginner.question: Naomi was so heartbroken when she returned to Bethlehem after losing her husband and sons that she told the townspeople to call her 'Mara,' a name meaning 'bitter.'
beginner.explanation: Naomi felt that God had dealt bitterly with her, taking away her husband and both sons in Moab. She told the women of Bethlehem not to call her Naomi ('pleasant') but Mara ('bitter'). God later restored her joy through Ruth and the birth of Obed.

beginner.question: Ruth was originally from the land of Egypt and moved to Bethlehem in search of better work opportunities after her husband died.
beginner.explanation: Ruth was from Moab, not Egypt. Moab was a nation located east of the Dead Sea. Ruth moved to Bethlehem not for work but out of loyalty to her mother-in-law Naomi, who was returning to her Israelite homeland.

beginner.question: Daniel continued to pray to God three times a day with his windows open toward Jerusalem, even after King Darius signed a law making it illegal to pray to anyone but the king for 30 days.
beginner.explanation: Daniel's faithfulness to God was unwavering. Despite knowing the law meant being thrown to the lions, he went home and prayed just as he always had — three times daily, kneeling before open windows facing Jerusalem.

beginner.question: When Daniel was thrown into the lions' den for disobeying the king's prayer law, the lions attacked and severely injured him, but Daniel survived because of his strong willpower.
beginner.explanation: The lions did not touch Daniel at all. God sent an angel who shut the mouths of the lions completely. When King Darius came the next morning, Daniel was entirely unharmed — not a single scratch was found on him.

beginner.question: Daniel and his friends refused to eat the rich food from the Babylonian king's table when they first arrived as captives. Instead, Daniel asked to eat only vegetables and drink water.
beginner.explanation: Daniel resolved not to defile himself with the royal food and wine, likely because it had been offered to idols or violated Jewish dietary laws. After a ten-day trial of vegetables and water, Daniel and his friends looked healthier than those who ate the king's food.

beginner.question: Daniel was able to interpret King Nebuchadnezzar's troubling dream about a giant statue when none of the king's own wise men, magicians, or astrologers could, because God revealed the meaning to Daniel.
beginner.explanation: Nebuchadnezzar demanded that his wise men not only interpret the dream but tell him what it was, since he had forgotten it. When all failed, Daniel prayed, and God revealed both the dream and its meaning. Daniel credited God, not himself, for the interpretation.

beginner.question: The mysterious writing on the wall at King Belshazzar's feast was interpreted by the king's chief magician, who was well-trained in reading ancient scripts.
beginner.explanation: None of the king's wise men or magicians could interpret the writing. It was Daniel who was summoned and interpreted the message: 'Mene, Mene, Tekel, Parsin' — meaning God had numbered Belshazzar's kingdom, found him wanting, and would divide it.

beginner.question: Daniel was an old man when he was first taken as a captive from Jerusalem to Babylon by King Nebuchadnezzar's army.
beginner.explanation: Daniel was a young man, likely a teenager, when he was taken captive to Babylon around 605 BC. He was selected along with other young men of noble birth who were intelligent and good-looking, to be trained for service in the Babylonian court.

beginner.question: Daniel served only under one king during his entire time in Babylon, remaining loyal to King Nebuchadnezzar throughout his life.
beginner.explanation: Daniel served under multiple kings across both the Babylonian and Persian empires. He served Nebuchadnezzar, Belshazzar, and then Darius and Cyrus of Persia. His faithful service spanned decades and multiple changes of power.

beginner.question: When King Darius found Daniel alive and unharmed in the lions' den the next morning, the king praised Daniel's God and issued a decree honouring Him throughout the kingdom.
beginner.explanation: King Darius was overjoyed to find Daniel alive. He ordered Daniel lifted out, then issued a decree that in every part of his kingdom, people must fear and reverence the God of Daniel, declaring Him the living God whose kingdom will never be destroyed.

beginner.question: Daniel's faithfulness to God in prayer, despite the threat of the lions' den, ultimately resulted in his enemies being thrown into the very same den of lions they had devised for him.
beginner.explanation: After Daniel's miraculous deliverance, King Darius ordered that the officials who had falsely accused Daniel be thrown into the lions' den. This time, the lions overpowered them immediately, demonstrating that Daniel's survival was truly a divine miracle.

beginner.question: Joseph's father Jacob gave him a beautiful coat of many colours, which made his eleven brothers extremely jealous because it showed Joseph was their father's favourite son.
beginner.explanation: Jacob loved Joseph more than his other sons because Joseph was born to him in his old age. The special coat was a visible sign of favouritism that deepened the brothers' existing jealousy, which Joseph's prophetic dreams only made worse.

beginner.question: Joseph's brothers sold him to Egyptian soldiers who took him directly to work as a servant in Pharaoh's palace from the very start.
beginner.explanation: Joseph was sold to Ishmaelite (or Midianite) travelling merchants, not Egyptian soldiers. These merchants took him to Egypt, where he was sold to Potiphar, an officer of Pharaoh and captain of the guard — not to Pharaoh's palace directly.

beginner.question: Joseph correctly interpreted Pharaoh's dream about seven fat cows being swallowed by seven thin cows as a prophecy about seven years of plenty followed by seven years of severe famine throughout the land.
beginner.explanation: Joseph explained that both of Pharaoh's dreams (the cows and the grain) had the same meaning: Egypt would enjoy seven years of abundant harvests, followed by seven years of devastating famine. Joseph's wise plan to store grain during the good years saved millions of lives.

beginner.question: Joseph rose from being a prisoner in an Egyptian jail to becoming the second most powerful man in all of Egypt, because of his God-given ability to interpret dreams and his wise leadership.
beginner.explanation: Pharaoh was so impressed by Joseph's interpretation and his plan to save Egypt from famine that he appointed Joseph as governor over all Egypt. Joseph went from wearing prison clothes to wearing fine linen and Pharaoh's signet ring in a single day.

beginner.question: When Joseph's brothers came to Egypt to buy grain during the famine, Joseph immediately revealed his identity and forgave them on the spot.
beginner.explanation: Joseph did not reveal himself right away. He first tested his brothers over multiple visits to see if they had changed. He spoke harshly, accused them of being spies, demanded they bring Benjamin, and planted a silver cup in Benjamin's sack before finally revealing his true identity.

beginner.question: Joseph told his brothers not to be angry with themselves for selling him into slavery, because God had used their actions to save many lives during the great famine.
beginner.explanation: When Joseph revealed himself, his brothers were terrified. But Joseph comforted them, saying, 'It was not you who sent me here, but God.' He saw the bigger picture — God had used even their evil act to position Joseph to save Egypt, Israel, and many nations from starvation.

beginner.question: Joseph had only two brothers who were jealous of him and conspired to sell him into slavery, while the rest of his brothers tried to protect him.
beginner.explanation: Joseph had eleven brothers in total. While Reuben tried to save Joseph and Benjamin was too young to be involved, the majority of the brothers conspired against Joseph out of jealousy over his coat and dreams, eventually selling him to the merchants.

beginner.question: While in prison in Egypt, Joseph correctly interpreted the dreams of Pharaoh's cupbearer and baker. The cupbearer was restored to his position, but the baker was executed, exactly as Joseph predicted.
beginner.explanation: Joseph told the cupbearer his dream meant he would be restored to serving Pharaoh in three days, and told the baker his dream meant he would be executed in three days. Both interpretations came true precisely as Joseph had said.

beginner.question: Joseph was falsely accused by Potiphar's wife of wrongdoing and thrown into prison, even though he had done nothing wrong and had actually tried to flee from her advances.
beginner.explanation: Potiphar's wife repeatedly tried to seduce Joseph, but he refused, saying he could not sin against God. One day she grabbed his garment and he fled, leaving it in her hand. She used the garment as false evidence, and Joseph was unjustly imprisoned.

beginner.question: Joshua was Moses' faithful assistant who was chosen by God to lead the Israelites across the Jordan River and into the Promised Land after Moses died.
beginner.explanation: Joshua had served Moses faithfully for decades, including as one of the twelve spies. God told Joshua to be strong and courageous, promising to be with him just as He had been with Moses, as he led the people into their inheritance.

beginner.question: God told Joshua to attack the walls of Jericho using battering rams and siege ladders, which was the standard military strategy of the time for conquering fortified cities.
beginner.explanation: God's instructions were entirely unconventional. Instead of military force, He told Joshua to have the people march silently around the city once daily for six days, and seven times on the seventh day, then shout. The walls fell by God's miraculous power, not human weapons.

beginner.question: On the final day of marching around Jericho, the Israelites gave a great shout and blew their trumpets, and the massive walls of the city fell down flat, allowing the Israelites to charge straight in.
beginner.explanation: After marching around Jericho seven times on the seventh day, the priests blew their trumpets and the people let out a mighty shout. The walls collapsed completely, and the Israelites charged into the city from every direction.

beginner.question: Joshua and Caleb were the only two of the original twelve spies sent to explore Canaan who gave a positive report, trusting that God would help them conquer the land despite its strong inhabitants.
beginner.explanation: While ten spies spread fear about the land's giant inhabitants, Joshua and Caleb urged the people to trust God. Because of their faith, they were the only two adults from that generation who survived the 40 years of wandering and entered the Promised Land.

beginner.question: When the Israelites needed to cross the Jordan River, they built a series of bridges from logs and stones to allow the entire nation to walk across safely.
beginner.explanation: The Israelites did not build bridges. God miraculously stopped the Jordan River from flowing — the water piled up upstream while the riverbed became dry ground. The priests carrying the Ark stood in the middle until everyone crossed, just as at the Red Sea.

beginner.question: Joshua challenged the Israelites with the famous words: 'Choose for yourselves this day whom you will serve... but as for me and my household, we will serve the Lord.'
beginner.explanation: Near the end of his life, Joshua gathered Israel at Shechem and challenged them to decide whom they would follow. His bold personal declaration of commitment has become one of the most quoted and celebrated statements of faith in all of Scripture.

beginner.question: During a battle, God answered Joshua's extraordinary prayer by making the sun stand still in the sky, giving the Israelites enough daylight to completely defeat their enemies.
beginner.explanation: Joshua prayed for the sun to stand still over Gibeon and the moon over the Valley of Aijalon. God answered, and the sun did not go down for about a full day. The Bible says there has never been a day like it, before or since.

beginner.question: Joshua was from the tribe of Judah, the same tribe as King David and Jesus Christ, which is why he was chosen to lead Israel.
beginner.explanation: Joshua was from the tribe of Ephraim, not Judah. He was the son of Nun, of the tribe of Ephraim. Being chosen to lead had nothing to do with his tribe — it was because of his faithful service to Moses and his trust in God.

beginner.question: When God first called Gideon to save Israel from the Midianites, Gideon was secretly threshing wheat inside a winepress because he was hiding the grain from the raiding Midianite armies.
beginner.explanation: The Midianites had been oppressing Israel for seven years, destroying their crops and livestock. Gideon was so afraid of the Midianites that he was threshing wheat in a winepress — a confined space meant for pressing grapes, not threshing grain — to hide his food.

beginner.question: God told Gideon that his army of 32,000 men was too small and needed to recruit more soldiers before they could fight the massive Midianite army.
beginner.explanation: God said the opposite — the army was too large, not too small. God reduced it from 32,000 to just 300 men so that Israel could not boast that they won by their own strength. The tiny force made it undeniable that the victory belonged to God alone.

beginner.question: Gideon tested God twice using a wool fleece, first asking for it to be wet while the ground stayed dry, and then asking for the reverse. God graciously performed both signs to reassure him.
beginner.explanation: Gideon was uncertain about his calling and asked God for two signs with a wool fleece. On the first night, the fleece was soaked but the ground dry. On the second night, the fleece was dry but the ground was wet with dew. Both times, God patiently confirmed His call.

beginner.question: Gideon's small army of 300 soldiers defeated the massive Midianite army using trumpets, empty clay jars, and torches hidden inside the jars as their weapons of battle.
beginner.explanation: In the middle of the night, Gideon's 300 men surrounded the Midianite camp. At his signal, they blew their trumpets, smashed the clay jars to reveal the blazing torches, and shouted, 'A sword for the Lord and for Gideon!' The Midianites panicked and fled.

beginner.question: After his stunning victory over the Midianites, Gideon gladly accepted the people's offer to make him king of Israel and established a royal dynasty.
beginner.explanation: Gideon refused the offer to be king. He told the Israelites, 'I will not rule over you, nor will my son rule over you. The Lord will rule over you.' He recognised that God alone was Israel's true king.

beginner.question: The angel of the Lord called Gideon a 'mighty warrior' when he first appeared to him, even though Gideon was hiding from the Midianites at the time and felt he was the weakest man in his family.
beginner.explanation: God saw Gideon's potential, not his present fear. The greeting 'mighty warrior' reflected what God knew Gideon would become through divine empowerment. God often calls people according to His purpose, not their current circumstances.

beginner.question: God reduced Gideon's army from 32,000 to 300 by testing how the soldiers drank water from a stream. Those who lapped water from their hands were the ones chosen to fight.
beginner.explanation: God first sent home the 22,000 who were afraid, leaving 10,000. Then He had the remaining men drink water from a stream. The 300 who cupped the water in their hands and lapped it up were chosen, while the rest who knelt down were sent home.

beginner.question: Gideon defeated the Midianites by using conventional military weapons like swords, spears, and shields in a traditional daytime battle formation.
beginner.explanation: Gideon used no conventional weapons at all. His 300 men carried only trumpets, empty clay jars, and torches. They attacked at night in a surprise raid, creating confusion with noise and sudden light, causing the Midianites to turn on each other and flee in panic.

beginner.question: Samson was set apart for God from birth as a Nazirite, which meant he could never cut his hair, drink wine or fermented drinks, or touch anything dead.
beginner.explanation: The angel who announced Samson's birth outlined the Nazirite vow his mother must follow during pregnancy and that Samson must keep for life. These restrictions symbolised total dedication to God. Samson's uncut hair was the visible sign of this covenant.

beginner.question: Samson's incredible strength came from a special diet of fruits and vegetables that his mother fed him from childhood, giving him supernatural physical power.
beginner.explanation: Samson's strength did not come from any diet. It came from the Spirit of the Lord and was connected to his Nazirite vow, symbolised by his uncut hair. When his hair was cut and the vow was broken, the Lord's strength departed from him.

beginner.question: Delilah was a woman paid by the Philistine rulers to discover the secret of Samson's great strength so they could capture and subdue him.
beginner.explanation: Each of the five Philistine rulers offered Delilah 1,100 pieces of silver — a fortune — to find out the source of Samson's strength. She persistently nagged and pressured Samson until he finally gave in and told her about his hair.

beginner.question: After Delilah betrayed him, Samson's hair was cut while he slept, and he lost his supernatural strength because the Lord's presence had departed from him.
beginner.explanation: Delilah had Samson's hair shaved off by a man while he slept on her lap. When Samson woke, he did not realise the Lord had left him. The Philistines easily overpowered him for the first time, showing his strength had always come from God, not the hair itself.

beginner.question: When the Philistines captured Samson after his hair was cut, they put him in a comfortable prison cell and treated him humanely as a respected enemy warrior.
beginner.explanation: The Philistines treated Samson cruelly. They gouged out his eyes, bound him with bronze shackles, and forced him to grind grain in prison — work typically done by animals or the lowest slaves. It was a brutal and humiliating captivity.

beginner.question: In his final act, the blinded Samson prayed to God for strength one last time, pushed apart the pillars of the Philistine temple of Dagon, and brought the entire building crashing down on himself and about 3,000 Philistines.
beginner.explanation: Samson's final prayer, 'O Lord God, remember me and strengthen me just this once,' was answered. He pushed the two central pillars apart, collapsing the temple and killing more Philistines in his death than in his entire life. It was a tragic yet redemptive end.

beginner.question: Samson once killed a lion with his bare hands when the Spirit of the Lord came powerfully upon him. He later found a swarm of bees and honey inside the lion's carcass.
beginner.explanation: Young Samson was on his way to Timnah when a lion attacked him. The Spirit of the Lord rushed upon him, and he tore the lion apart with his bare hands. When he passed by later, bees had made a honeycomb inside the carcass.

beginner.question: Samson immediately told Delilah the true secret of his strength the very first time she asked him, because he trusted and loved her completely.
beginner.explanation: Samson did not tell the truth right away. He lied to Delilah three times — first saying he could be bound with fresh bowstrings, then new ropes, then by weaving his hair into a loom. Only after she nagged him relentlessly did he reveal the truth about his hair.

beginner.question: God chose Noah to build the ark because Noah was a righteous man who walked faithfully with God in a time when the rest of the entire world had become extremely wicked and violent.
beginner.explanation: The Bible says Noah was a righteous man, blameless among the people of his time, and he walked faithfully with God. While the rest of the world was filled with corruption and violence, Noah stood out as the one man God found worthy of saving.

beginner.question: God told Noah to bring five of every kind of animal onto the ark to ensure there would be enough genetic diversity for the species to survive after the flood.
beginner.explanation: God told Noah to bring two of every kind of animal — one male and one female — not five. For clean animals (those suitable for sacrifice), Noah brought seven pairs, but for most species the number was two.

beginner.question: It rained for 40 days and 40 nights during the great flood in Noah's time, and the waters continued to rise as springs from the deep also burst open, eventually covering the entire earth.
beginner.explanation: The rain fell for 40 days and nights, and the springs of the great deep also burst open, flooding the earth from both above and below. The waters rose until even the highest mountains were covered by more than 20 feet.

beginner.question: Noah sent out a dove from the ark to check if the floodwaters had receded, and the dove returned carrying an olive branch in its beak — a sign that vegetation was growing again.
beginner.explanation: Noah first sent out a raven, which flew back and forth. He then sent a dove, which returned with nothing the first time. The second time, the dove returned with a freshly plucked olive leaf, showing dry land and growing trees. The third time, it did not return at all.

beginner.question: After the great flood, God placed a bright new star in the sky as a sign that He would never again destroy the entire earth with water.
beginner.explanation: God placed a rainbow in the sky, not a star. The rainbow was the sign of God's covenant with Noah and all living creatures — a visible promise that He would never again destroy the earth with a flood. It appears in clouds after rain as a reminder.

beginner.question: Noah had three sons named Shem, Ham, and Japheth, who together with their wives were saved on the ark and later became the ancestors of all the nations on earth.
beginner.explanation: Shem, Ham, and Japheth and their wives — eight people in total — survived the flood on the ark. After the flood, their descendants spread across the earth. The Bible traces the origins of various nations back to these three sons of Noah.

beginner.question: After leaving the ark, Noah built an altar and offered sacrifices to God as an expression of deep thankfulness for saving his family from the flood.
beginner.explanation: The very first thing Noah did after leaving the ark was build an altar and offer burnt offerings to God. This act of worship showed Noah's grateful heart. God was pleased with the offering and responded by establishing His covenant and the sign of the rainbow.

beginner.question: Noah's ark was constructed from metal and stone to withstand the immense force of the floodwaters during the worldwide deluge.
beginner.explanation: The ark was made of wood, not metal and stone. God specifically instructed Noah to build it from gopher wood (likely a type of cypress) and to coat it inside and out with pitch to make it waterproof. It was an enormous wooden vessel.

beginner.question: After the flood, God promised Noah that He would never again send any kind of natural disaster to the earth, ensuring permanent safety for all future generations.
beginner.explanation: God specifically promised never to flood the entire earth again — He did not promise to end all natural disasters. The rainbow covenant was specifically about floodwaters, not earthquakes, storms, or other events. God's promise was precise and specific.

beginner.question: The prophet Elijah boldly challenged 450 prophets of the false god Baal to a contest on Mount Carmel to prove who the true God of Israel was.
beginner.explanation: Elijah stood alone against 450 prophets of Baal (and 400 prophets of Asherah) in one of the most dramatic confrontations in the Bible. He challenged them to set up sacrifices and call on their god to send fire, while he would call on the Lord.

beginner.question: During the contest on Mount Carmel, the prophets of Baal successfully called down fire from heaven onto their sacrifice before Elijah had his turn.
beginner.explanation: The prophets of Baal failed completely. They called on Baal from morning until evening — shouting, dancing, and even cutting themselves — but nothing happened. Elijah even mocked them, suggesting Baal might be sleeping. Only when Elijah prayed did God send fire.

beginner.question: Elijah told King Ahab that there would be no rain in Israel until Elijah gave the word, and this declaration resulted in a severe drought lasting about three years.
beginner.explanation: Elijah's bold declaration was an act of divine judgement against the idolatry that King Ahab and Queen Jezebel had promoted. The devastating three-year drought brought the nation to its knees and set the stage for the dramatic showdown on Mount Carmel.

beginner.question: At the end of his earthly life, Elijah was taken up to heaven in a chariot of fire and a whirlwind, rather than dying a natural death, while his servant Elisha watched.
beginner.explanation: As Elijah and Elisha walked together, a chariot of fire and horses of fire suddenly appeared and separated them. Then Elijah went up to heaven in a whirlwind. Elisha cried out and picked up Elijah's fallen mantle, inheriting his prophetic ministry.

beginner.question: After his great victory on Mount Carmel, Elijah was so confident and empowered that he immediately marched to Queen Jezebel's palace and confronted her face to face.
beginner.explanation: Despite his triumphant victory, Elijah actually fled in fear when Jezebel sent a death threat. He ran into the wilderness, collapsed under a tree, and prayed to die. Even the bravest prophet experienced moments of despair and exhaustion.

beginner.question: During the drought he had declared, God sent ravens to bring bread and meat to Elijah morning and evening while the prophet camped beside the Brook Cherith.
beginner.explanation: God miraculously provided for Elijah through ravens — birds that would normally eat the food themselves. They brought bread and meat each morning and evening, while Elijah drank from the brook until it eventually dried up due to the prolonged drought.

beginner.question: Elisha was Elijah's faithful servant and successor who asked for a double portion of Elijah's spirit and went on to continue the prophetic ministry in Israel.
beginner.explanation: Elisha faithfully served Elijah and refused to leave his side even on Elijah's final day. He asked for a double portion of Elijah's spirit — the spiritual inheritance of the firstborn — and God granted this request. Elisha performed many powerful miracles.

beginner.question: Elijah lived in a grand palace provided by King Ahab while serving as the royal court prophet in Israel.
beginner.explanation: Elijah did not live in any palace. He was a wilderness prophet who lived a humble, nomadic life. He camped by brooks, stayed with a poor widow, hid in caves, and was constantly on the run from King Ahab and Queen Jezebel, who wanted him dead.

beginner.question: Before Jesus gave him the name Peter (meaning 'rock'), this apostle was a fisherman named Simon who made his living on the Sea of Galilee with his brother Andrew.
beginner.explanation: Simon Peter was a working fisherman when Jesus called him. He and his brother Andrew were casting nets into the Sea of Galilee when Jesus invited them to follow Him and become 'fishers of men.' Peter left his nets and followed immediately.

beginner.question: On the night Jesus was arrested, Peter bravely defended Jesus before the authorities and never denied knowing Him, standing firm even under threat of death.
beginner.explanation: Peter actually denied knowing Jesus three times that night, exactly as Jesus had predicted. When questioned by servants and bystanders, Peter swore he had never met Jesus. After the third denial, a rooster crowed and Peter wept bitterly in shame and sorrow.

beginner.question: Peter's brother Andrew was also a disciple of Jesus. Andrew had previously been a follower of John the Baptist before meeting Jesus.
beginner.explanation: Andrew was one of John the Baptist's disciples who heard John declare Jesus as the 'Lamb of God.' Andrew immediately followed Jesus and then brought his brother Simon Peter to meet Him, leading to one of the most significant introductions in the New Testament.

beginner.question: On the day of Pentecost, Peter preached a powerful sermon in Jerusalem that resulted in about 3,000 people believing in Jesus and being baptised — the explosive beginning of the early church.
beginner.explanation: When the Holy Spirit came upon the believers at Pentecost, Peter stood up and boldly preached to the crowd, explaining the death and resurrection of Jesus. About 3,000 people responded in faith and were baptised that day, launching the church's rapid growth.

beginner.question: Peter walked on water toward Jesus on the Sea of Galilee and never once sank, because his faith was perfectly strong throughout the entire experience.
beginner.explanation: Peter did initially walk on water toward Jesus, but when he noticed the strong wind and waves, he became afraid and began to sink. He cried out, 'Lord, save me!' and Jesus immediately caught him, saying, 'You of little faith, why did you doubt?'

beginner.question: After His resurrection, Jesus asked Peter 'Do you love me?' three times by the Sea of Galilee, giving Peter a chance to affirm his love once for each of his three denials.
beginner.explanation: This tender scene by the charcoal fire paralleled Peter's three denials. Each time Peter affirmed his love, Jesus commissioned him to care for His followers. This was Jesus' way of restoring Peter and confirming his role as a leader of the church.

beginner.question: Peter freed himself from prison by picking the locks on his chains while the guards were sleeping, then quietly slipped out through a back door.
beginner.explanation: Peter did not free himself. An angel appeared in his cell, causing a light to shine and his chains to fall off. The angel led him past the guards (who did not notice) and through the iron gate, which opened by itself. Peter thought he was dreaming.

beginner.question: Peter declared that Jesus is 'the Christ, the Son of the living God' when Jesus asked His disciples at Caesarea Philippi who they believed He was.
beginner.explanation: This confession is considered a pivotal moment in the Gospels. Jesus said Peter's declaration was divinely revealed by the Father in heaven, not discovered through human reasoning. Jesus then called Peter 'blessed' and spoke of building His church on this confession of faith.

beginner.question: Before his dramatic conversion, Paul (then called Saul) actively persecuted Christians, approving of Stephen's death and obtaining letters to arrest believers in Damascus.
beginner.explanation: Saul was a zealous Pharisee who considered the early Christians to be dangerous heretics. He was present at Stephen's stoning, then went from house to house in Jerusalem arresting believers, and was travelling to Damascus to arrest more when Jesus appeared to him.

beginner.question: Paul was converted to Christianity after spending years studying a scroll about Jesus that he found in the Jerusalem temple library.
beginner.explanation: Paul's conversion was sudden and dramatic, not gradual through study. On the road to Damascus, a blinding light from heaven struck him down and he heard Jesus' voice asking, 'Saul, Saul, why do you persecute me?' He was blind for three days before his sight was restored.

beginner.question: After Paul was blinded on the road to Damascus, God sent a believer named Ananias to lay hands on him so he could receive his sight back and be filled with the Holy Spirit.
beginner.explanation: God spoke to Ananias in a vision, telling him to go to the house where Saul was staying. Despite being afraid of Saul's reputation, Ananias obeyed, laid his hands on Saul, and said, 'Brother Saul, the Lord Jesus has sent me so that you may see again.'

beginner.question: While Paul and his companion Silas were imprisoned in Philippi and singing hymns to God at midnight, a powerful earthquake shook the prison, opening all the doors and loosening every prisoner's chains.
beginner.explanation: Paul and Silas had been beaten and thrown into the inner cell with their feet in stocks. At midnight, as they prayed and sang hymns, a violent earthquake shook the prison. All doors flew open and chains fell off, but no prisoners escaped. The stunned jailer became a believer.

beginner.question: Paul only travelled on one missionary journey before settling down permanently as a pastor in the city of Rome.
beginner.explanation: Paul went on three major missionary journeys, covering thousands of miles across the Roman Empire. He visited dozens of cities in modern-day Turkey, Greece, and beyond, establishing churches in each place. He did eventually go to Rome, but as a prisoner, not as a settled pastor.

beginner.question: Paul wrote approximately 13 books of the New Testament, including letters to churches in cities like Rome, Corinth, Galatia, Ephesus, Philippi, Colossae, and Thessalonica.
beginner.explanation: Paul's letters (epistles) form a significant portion of the New Testament. He wrote to entire church communities and to individuals like Timothy, Titus, and Philemon. These letters address theology, practical Christian living, church leadership, and encouragement during persecution.

beginner.question: Paul mentored a young man named Timothy and wrote him two personal letters, encouraging him to be strong in his faith and bold in his role as a church leader despite his youth.
beginner.explanation: Timothy was like a son to Paul. In his letters, Paul encouraged Timothy to 'fan into flame the gift of God' and not to let anyone look down on him for being young. These deeply personal letters show the importance of mentoring the next generation of believers.

beginner.question: Paul was not a Roman citizen and had no special legal rights during his various trials before governors and courts in the Roman Empire.
beginner.explanation: Paul was indeed a Roman citizen by birth, which was a rare and valuable status. This citizenship protected him from being flogged without trial, gave him the right to a fair hearing, and ultimately allowed him to appeal his case directly to Caesar in Rome.

beginner.question: Rahab hid two Israelite spies on her rooftop under stalks of flax that she had laid out to dry, when the king of Jericho sent soldiers to find and capture them.
beginner.explanation: Rahab quickly took the spies to her roof and concealed them under stalks of flax she was drying. When the king's men came searching, she told them the spies had already left the city, sending the soldiers on a wild chase toward the Jordan River.

beginner.question: Rahab helped the Israelite spies escape from Jericho by disguising them as local merchants and smuggling them out through the main city gate at night.
beginner.explanation: Rahab did not use disguises or the gate. She lowered the spies down from her window using a rope, because her house was built directly into the city wall. This gave her a window facing outside the city, allowing the spies to descend and escape to the hills.

beginner.question: The Israelite spies told Rahab to hang a scarlet cord from her window so the attacking army would know to spare her house and everyone inside it when Jericho fell.
beginner.explanation: The scarlet cord was the agreed-upon sign between Rahab and the spies. As long as the red cord was visible in her window and her family stayed inside the house, they would be saved when the Israelites conquered Jericho. Rahab obeyed and was spared.

beginner.question: Rahab told the Israelite spies that the people of Jericho were absolutely terrified because they had heard how God parted the Red Sea for the Israelites when they escaped from Egypt.
beginner.explanation: Rahab confessed that the entire city was melting with fear because of the Israelites' God. Specifically, she mentioned hearing about the parting of the Red Sea and the victories over the Amorite kings. She declared, 'The Lord your God is God in heaven above and on the earth below.'

beginner.question: After the destruction of Jericho, Rahab was sent away from the Israelites because she was a Canaanite foreigner who did not belong among God's people.
beginner.explanation: Rahab was not sent away. She and her entire family were rescued from Jericho and welcomed into the nation of Israel. She married an Israelite named Salmon, had a son named Boaz, and became an ancestor of both King David and Jesus Christ.

beginner.question: Rahab is honoured in the New Testament as an example of faith. She is included in the genealogy of Jesus in Matthew and praised in both the book of Hebrews and the book of James.
beginner.explanation: Despite her past, Rahab is celebrated as a hero of faith. Hebrews 11:31 praises her for welcoming the spies in peace. James 2:25 uses her as an example of faith demonstrated through action. And Matthew 1:5 places her in Jesus' own family tree.

beginner.question: Despite having a sinful past, Rahab married an Israelite man named Salmon and became the mother of Boaz, who later showed the same kindness to the foreigner Ruth.
beginner.explanation: Rahab's story demonstrates God's grace — her past did not define her future. She married Salmon and had Boaz, who inherited his mother's compassion for outsiders. Boaz showed remarkable kindness to Ruth, another foreigner who came to trust in Israel's God.

beginner.question: Rahab lived in a grand house in the centre of Jericho, which is why the spies chose to hide there — because it was the last place the king's soldiers would search.
beginner.explanation: Rahab did not live in the city centre. Her house was built directly into the outer city wall, which was why she could lower the spies from her window to the ground outside the city. This was a modest location, not a grand central residence.

beginner.question: Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego were three young Jewish men in Babylon who refused to bow down and worship King Nebuchadnezzar's golden statue, even when threatened with being thrown into a blazing furnace.
beginner.explanation: When the music played and everyone else in the kingdom bowed before the 90-foot golden statue, these three men stood firm. They told the king they would not worship his image, declaring that their God could save them — but even if He did not, they still would not bow.

beginner.question: When Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego were thrown into the blazing furnace, they were badly burned and scarred but miraculously survived the ordeal.
beginner.explanation: They were not burned at all. When they emerged from the furnace, not a hair on their heads was singed, their robes were not scorched, and there was not even the smell of fire on them. God's protection was absolute and complete.

beginner.question: King Nebuchadnezzar was astonished to see four figures walking freely inside the blazing furnace, even though only three men had been thrown in. The king said the fourth figure looked 'like a son of the gods.'
beginner.explanation: The king peered into the furnace and was amazed to see four men walking around unbound and unharmed instead of three. The mysterious fourth figure is believed by many to have been an angel or a pre-incarnate appearance of Christ, sent by God to protect the faithful three.

beginner.question: The furnace that Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego were thrown into was heated seven times hotter than usual on King Nebuchadnezzar's furious orders, making it so hot that the soldiers who threw them in were killed by the heat.
beginner.explanation: Nebuchadnezzar was so enraged by the three men's defiance that he ordered the furnace superheated to seven times its normal temperature. The extreme heat killed the mighty soldiers who carried the bound men to the furnace, yet the three Jews inside were completely unharmed.

beginner.question: Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego told King Nebuchadnezzar that they would agree to bow to the golden statue if God did not save them from the fiery furnace.
beginner.explanation: They said the exact opposite. Their courageous declaration was: 'Our God is able to deliver us, but even if He does not, we want you to know, O king, that we will not serve your gods or worship the image of gold.' Their obedience was unconditional.

beginner.question: After witnessing the miracle of the fiery furnace, King Nebuchadnezzar praised the God of Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego and issued a decree protecting the right to worship their God.
beginner.explanation: The astonished king proclaimed that no other god could save like this. He decreed that anyone who spoke against the God of Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego would be severely punished. He also promoted the three men to even higher positions in Babylon.

beginner.question: Before they were given Babylonian names, Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego were originally named Hananiah, Mishael, and Azariah — Hebrew names that honoured the God of Israel.
beginner.explanation: When the Jewish captives arrived in Babylon, the chief official renamed them as part of the Babylonian assimilation process. Hananiah ('God is gracious') became Shadrach, Mishael ('Who is what God is?') became Meshach, and Azariah ('The Lord helps') became Abednego.

beginner.question: Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego were wealthy Egyptian merchants who came to Babylon to trade goods and were caught up in Nebuchadnezzar's golden statue ceremony by accident.
beginner.explanation: They were not Egyptian merchants. They were young Jewish men from the kingdom of Judah who were taken to Babylon as captives when Nebuchadnezzar conquered Jerusalem. They were trained in Babylonian language and literature and held official positions in the government.

beginner.question: Stephen was one of seven men chosen to serve the early church, and he was described as a man full of God's grace and power who performed great wonders and signs among the people.
beginner.explanation: Stephen stood out among the seven deacons for his exceptional faith and the miraculous signs he performed. He debated with powerful opponents in the synagogues, and no one could stand against the wisdom and the Spirit with which he spoke.

beginner.question: Stephen was the last of the apostles to be killed for his faith in Jesus, dying peacefully of old age in a Roman prison many years after the other apostles.
beginner.explanation: Stephen was the first Christian martyr, not the last. He was stoned to death by an angry mob relatively early in the church's history, shortly after Pentecost. He did not die of old age but was violently killed for his bold testimony about Jesus.

beginner.question: As Stephen was being stoned to death, he looked up to heaven and saw a vision of Jesus standing at the right hand of God, and he described this vision aloud to those around him.
beginner.explanation: Filled with the Holy Spirit, Stephen gazed into heaven and saw the glory of God with Jesus standing at His right hand. He cried out, 'Look! I see heaven open and the Son of Man standing at the right hand of God!' This vision gave him comfort in his final moments.

beginner.question: Stephen's final words before he died echoed Jesus' words on the cross — he prayed aloud asking God not to hold the sin of his killers against them.
beginner.explanation: Just as Jesus prayed 'Father, forgive them, for they do not know what they are doing,' Stephen knelt down and cried out, 'Lord, do not hold this sin against them.' This remarkable act of forgiveness in the face of death mirrored his Saviour's own example.

beginner.question: The young man Saul, who later became the apostle Paul, tried desperately to save Stephen from being stoned by the angry mob but was overpowered by the crowd.
beginner.explanation: Saul did not try to save Stephen. On the contrary, he approved of Stephen's execution and even guarded the coats of those who stoned him. Saul was an active persecutor of the church at this time. His conversion came later, on the road to Damascus.

beginner.question: Before his death, Stephen gave a lengthy and powerful speech to the Jewish ruling council (the Sanhedrin), recounting the entire history of Israel from Abraham all the way through to Jesus.
beginner.explanation: Stephen's speech in Acts 7 is the longest recorded speech in the book of Acts. He traced God's faithfulness through Abraham, Joseph, Moses, David, and Solomon, showing that Israel had repeatedly rejected God's messengers — just as they rejected Jesus.

beginner.question: The stoning of Stephen triggered a widespread persecution against the early church in Jerusalem, which actually helped spread Christianity as believers scattered to new regions and shared the gospel wherever they went.
beginner.explanation: After Stephen's death, a great persecution broke out and believers fled Jerusalem, scattering throughout Judea, Samaria, and beyond. Wherever they went, they preached the gospel. What the persecutors intended as destruction, God used to spread Christianity across the Roman world.

beginner.question: Nehemiah was serving as cupbearer to King Artaxerxes of Persia when he received the devastating news that the walls of Jerusalem were broken down and its gates had been burned with fire.
beginner.explanation: Nehemiah's brother Hanani brought the heartbreaking news from Jerusalem. Despite having a comfortable and prestigious position in the Persian court, Nehemiah was deeply moved and spent days weeping, fasting, and praying about the condition of his beloved homeland.

beginner.question: Nehemiah rebuilt the walls of Jerusalem in three full years with the help of a large professional construction army sent by the Persian king.
beginner.explanation: The walls were rebuilt in just 52 days, not three years, and not by a professional army. The work was done by ordinary citizens of Jerusalem — priests, merchants, goldsmiths, perfume-makers, and others — all working together under Nehemiah's inspiring leadership.

beginner.question: Nehemiah faced determined opposition from Sanballat and Tobiah, who tried to stop the rebuilding of Jerusalem's walls through mockery, threats, conspiracies, and deception.
beginner.explanation: Sanballat and Tobiah mocked the Jews, saying a fox could knock down their wall. They plotted armed attacks, tried to lure Nehemiah into traps, and hired false prophets to frighten him. Despite all this, Nehemiah persevered and the wall was completed.

beginner.question: Because of constant threats from enemies, Nehemiah had his workers carry weapons while they rebuilt the walls, with half the men standing guard while the other half constructed.
beginner.explanation: Nehemiah armed the people with swords, spears, and bows. From that day on, half worked on construction while the other half stood guard. Even the builders worked with one hand while holding a weapon in the other, staying ready to fight at a moment's notice.

beginner.question: When Nehemiah first arrived in Jerusalem, he announced his plans to rebuild the walls immediately upon arrival, holding a public meeting to rally the people before even inspecting the damage.
beginner.explanation: Nehemiah did not announce his plans immediately. He secretly went out at night with a few men to inspect the broken walls and burned gates, carefully surveying the damage before revealing his intentions to anyone. Only after his quiet inspection did he present his plan.

beginner.question: After the walls were rebuilt, Nehemiah himself read the Book of the Law aloud to all the people of Jerusalem, and they wept as they listened to God's words.
beginner.explanation: It was the priest Ezra, not Nehemiah, who read the Book of the Law to the assembled people. Ezra stood on a high wooden platform and read from early morning until midday. Nehemiah was present as governor, but Ezra was the one who read the Scriptures.

beginner.question: Nehemiah was a priest in the Jerusalem temple before the walls were destroyed, which is why he felt such a strong connection to the city and its people.
beginner.explanation: Nehemiah was not a priest. He was a cupbearer to the Persian king — a high-ranking court official, not a religious leader. His connection to Jerusalem came from his Jewish heritage and deep love for his people, not from serving in the temple.

beginner.question: When his enemies repeatedly tried to lure him away from the construction work, Nehemiah gave the famous reply: 'I am doing a great work and cannot come down. Why should the work stop while I leave it?'
beginner.explanation: Sanballat and Geshem sent messages four times inviting Nehemiah to meet on the plain of Ono, likely planning to harm him. Each time, Nehemiah sent the same resolute reply, refusing to be distracted from the critical work God had called him to do.

beginner.question: When Nehemiah first heard the news about Jerusalem's broken walls and burned gates, he immediately wept, mourned, fasted, and prayed for days — showing his deep love for his people and his city.
beginner.explanation: Nehemiah's response reveals his tender heart. He sat down and wept, mourned for days, fasted, and prayed before God. His prayer acknowledged the nation's sins and appealed to God's promises. Only after this time of grief and prayer did he take practical action.

beginner.question: Before Jesus called him to be a disciple, Simon Peter worked as a fisherman on the Sea of Galilee with his brother Andrew. What was Peter's occupation before following Jesus?
beginner.explanation: Simon Peter was a fisherman by trade. He and his brother Andrew were casting their nets into the Sea of Galilee when Jesus called them to follow Him and become 'fishers of men.'

beginner.question: When Jesus first met Simon, He gave him a new name that means 'rock' in Greek. This name reflected the role Simon would play in the early church. What name did Jesus give to Simon?
beginner.explanation: Jesus gave Simon the name Peter, which comes from the Greek word 'Petros' meaning 'rock.' Jesus said, 'You are Peter, and on this rock I will build my church.' In Aramaic, the name is 'Cephas.'

beginner.question: On the night Jesus was arrested, Peter was warming himself by a fire in the courtyard of the high priest. Servants and bystanders asked if he knew Jesus. How many times did Peter deny knowing Jesus that night?
beginner.explanation: Peter denied knowing Jesus three times, just as Jesus had predicted at the Last Supper. After the third denial, a rooster crowed and Peter remembered Jesus' words. He went outside and wept bitterly.

beginner.question: During a storm on the Sea of Galilee, the disciples saw Jesus walking on the water toward their boat. One disciple asked to walk on the water too and stepped out of the boat. Who walked on the water toward Jesus?
beginner.explanation: Peter asked Jesus to call him out onto the water. When Jesus said 'Come,' Peter stepped out and actually walked on the water. But when he looked at the wind and waves, he became afraid and began to sink, and Jesus reached out to save him.

beginner.question: After Jesus rose from the dead and ascended to heaven, the Holy Spirit came upon the disciples at Pentecost. Who stood up and preached a powerful sermon that day, leading about 3,000 people to believe?
beginner.explanation: Peter boldly preached the first sermon of the early church on the day of Pentecost. He explained that Jesus, who had been crucified, was raised from the dead by God. About 3,000 people were baptised and added to the church that day.

beginner.question: When Jesus asked His disciples, 'Who do you say I am?', one disciple spoke up immediately and gave a bold answer. Who said, 'You are the Christ, the Son of the living God'?
beginner.explanation: Peter declared that Jesus was the Christ (the Messiah) and the Son of the living God. Jesus responded by saying Peter was blessed because this truth had been revealed to him by God the Father, not by human reasoning.

beginner.question: When soldiers came to arrest Jesus in the Garden of Gethsemane, one disciple drew a sword and cut off the ear of the high priest's servant named Malchus. Who struck the servant with a sword?
beginner.explanation: Peter drew his sword and cut off the right ear of Malchus, the high priest's servant. Jesus told Peter to put his sword away and then healed the servant's ear, showing mercy even as He was being arrested.

beginner.question: After Jesus' resurrection, Peter and John were going to the temple when they met a man who had been unable to walk since birth. He was begging at the gate called Beautiful. What did Peter do for this man?
beginner.explanation: Peter said, 'Silver or gold I do not have, but what I do have I give you. In the name of Jesus Christ of Nazareth, walk.' The man was instantly healed, jumped to his feet, and began walking and praising God.

beginner.question: Jesus told Peter that before a certain animal made a sound, Peter would deny knowing Him three times. What animal did Jesus say would signal Peter's denials?
beginner.explanation: At the Last Supper, Jesus predicted that before the rooster crowed, Peter would deny knowing Him three times. Peter insisted he never would, but later that night, it happened exactly as Jesus said.

beginner.question: After His resurrection, Jesus appeared to the disciples by the Sea of Galilee and had breakfast with them. He asked Peter the same question three times — matching the three denials. What question did Jesus ask Peter?
beginner.explanation: Jesus asked Peter 'Do you love me?' three times, matching the three times Peter had denied Him. Each time Peter said yes, Jesus told him to 'Feed my lambs' and 'Take care of my sheep,' restoring Peter and giving him the mission to lead the early church.

beginner.question: Peter was part of Jesus' closest inner circle of three disciples, who were present at special events like the Transfiguration and the raising of Jairus' daughter. Besides Peter, which two disciples made up this inner circle?
beginner.explanation: Peter, James, and John formed Jesus' inner circle. They were the only three disciples present when Jesus was transfigured on the mountain, when He raised Jairus' daughter from the dead, and when He prayed in Gethsemane before His arrest.

beginner.question: While Peter was imprisoned by King Herod, the early church gathered to pray for his release. That night, something miraculous happened in the prison. What happened to Peter?
beginner.explanation: An angel appeared in Peter's cell, his chains fell off, and the angel led him past the guards and through the iron gate, which opened by itself. Peter went to the house where believers were praying and knocked on the door, astonishing everyone.

beginner.question: Peter received a vision from God in which a large sheet was lowered from heaven containing all kinds of animals. A voice told Peter to eat, but Peter refused because some were considered unclean. What was God teaching Peter through this vision?
options: That all people are accepted by God
To follow Jewish food laws more strictly
beginner.explanation: God was teaching Peter that the gospel was for everyone, not just Jewish people. This vision prepared Peter to visit Cornelius, a Roman centurion, and share the good news with Gentiles (non-Jewish people) for the first time.

beginner.question: At the Last Supper, Jesus began washing His disciples' feet. When He came to Peter, Peter initially refused. What did Jesus say would happen if He didn't wash Peter's feet?
options: Peter would have no part with Him
Peter would have to leave the group
beginner.explanation: When Peter refused, Jesus said, 'Unless I wash you, you have no part with me.' Peter then changed his mind completely and asked Jesus to wash not just his feet but his hands and head too! Jesus' foot-washing demonstrated servant leadership.

beginner.question: Peter is mentioned as having a wife in the New Testament. We know this because Jesus healed a member of Peter's family who was sick with a fever. Who did Jesus heal at Peter's home?
beginner.explanation: When Jesus went to Peter's house, He found Peter's mother-in-law lying in bed with a fever. Jesus touched her hand and the fever left her. She got up and began serving them, showing that the healing was immediate and complete.

beginner.question: On the Mount of Transfiguration, Jesus' appearance changed and became radiant. Two figures from the Old Testament appeared alongside Him, witnessed by Peter, James, and John. Who appeared with Jesus at the Transfiguration?
beginner.explanation: Moses and Elijah appeared talking with Jesus during the Transfiguration. Peter was so amazed that he offered to build three shelters — one for Jesus, one for Moses, and one for Elijah. Then a voice from a cloud said, 'This is my Son, whom I love. Listen to him!'

beginner.question: Peter's original name was Simon. Jesus gave him the name Peter in Greek. In the Aramaic language (which Jesus spoke daily), what was the Aramaic version of this new name?
beginner.explanation: In Aramaic, the name Jesus gave Simon was 'Cephas,' which means 'rock.' The Greek translation is 'Petros' (Peter). Both names carry the same meaning — Jesus was declaring that Peter would be like a rock, a firm foundation for the early church.

beginner.question: Peter and Andrew were originally from a fishing village on the shore of the Sea of Galilee. This same town was also the hometown of the disciple Philip. What town were they from?
beginner.explanation: Peter, Andrew, and Philip were all from the town of Bethsaida, located on the northern shore of the Sea of Galilee. Bethsaida was a fishing village, which explains why Peter and Andrew worked as fishermen before Jesus called them.

beginner.question: After denying Jesus three times and hearing the rooster crow, Peter was overcome with emotion. What did Peter do after remembering Jesus' prediction about his denials?
options: He went outside and wept bitterly
beginner.explanation: After the rooster crowed, Peter remembered that Jesus had warned him about his three denials. Overcome with guilt and sorrow, Peter went outside and wept bitterly. This shows how deeply he regretted denying his Lord.

beginner.question: In the book of Acts, Peter raised a woman named Tabitha (also called Dorcas) back to life in the city of Joppa. Tabitha was beloved in her community for a particular kind of charitable work. What was she known for?
beginner.explanation: Tabitha (Dorcas) was beloved in Joppa for always doing good and helping the poor, especially by making robes and clothing for widows. When she died, Peter came, prayed, and told her to get up — and she opened her eyes and sat up alive.

beginner.question: When Peter was walking on water toward Jesus and began to sink because he was afraid, he cried out for help. What did Jesus do immediately?
options: Reached out His hand and caught him
Sent a wave to push him back
Let him sink to teach a lesson
beginner.explanation: When Peter began to sink, he cried out, 'Lord, save me!' Jesus immediately reached out His hand and caught him, then said, 'You of little faith, why did you doubt?' They climbed into the boat and the wind died down.

beginner.question: After the resurrection, Peter and some disciples went fishing on the Sea of Galilee but caught nothing all night. A man on the shore told them to cast their net on the other side. What happened when they obeyed?
options: They caught so many fish they couldn't haul the net in
beginner.explanation: When the disciples cast their net on the right side as instructed, they caught 153 large fish — so many they couldn't pull the net into the boat. John recognised the man on shore was Jesus, and Peter jumped into the water and swam to Him.

beginner.question: Peter wrote two letters (epistles) that are included in the New Testament, encouraging believers who were suffering for their faith. What are these letters called?
beginner.explanation: The apostle Peter wrote two letters to early Christians, known as 1 Peter and 2 Peter. In these letters, he encouraged believers who were suffering for their faith and warned against false teachers.

beginner.question: At Pentecost, Peter explained to the crowd that the disciples were not drunk but were filled with the Holy Spirit, fulfilling a prophecy. Which Old Testament prophet did Peter quote?
beginner.explanation: Peter quoted the prophet Joel, saying, 'In the last days, God says, I will pour out my Spirit on all people.' This helped the crowd understand that the miraculous events of Pentecost were God's plan foretold long ago.

beginner.question: When Peter visited the Roman centurion Cornelius and shared the gospel with his household, something surprising happened while Peter was still speaking. What happened to Cornelius and his family?
options: The Holy Spirit came upon them
beginner.explanation: While Peter was still speaking, the Holy Spirit fell on Cornelius and all who heard the message. They began speaking in tongues and praising God. This amazed the Jewish believers with Peter, because God had given the same gift to Gentiles.

beginner.question: Andrew, one of Jesus' twelve disciples, was the brother of another very well-known apostle who became a leader of the early church. Who was Andrew's brother?
beginner.explanation: Andrew and Peter (Simon Peter) were brothers. They both grew up as fishermen on the Sea of Galilee. Andrew was actually the one who first met Jesus and then brought his brother Peter to meet Him.

beginner.question: Before Andrew became a disciple of Jesus, he was already following another important figure who preached repentance and baptised people in the Jordan River. Who was Andrew following before he met Jesus?
beginner.explanation: Andrew was originally a disciple of John the Baptist. When John the Baptist saw Jesus and said, 'Look, the Lamb of God,' Andrew followed Jesus. He then went and told his brother Peter that they had found the Messiah.

beginner.question: According to the Gospel of John, after hearing John the Baptist point out Jesus as 'the Lamb of God,' Andrew was one of the very first to follow Jesus. What makes Andrew notable among the disciples?
options: He was among the first called
beginner.explanation: Andrew was one of the first two disciples to follow Jesus, making him one of the very first people Jesus called. After spending time with Jesus, Andrew immediately went to find his brother Peter and brought him to Jesus too.

beginner.question: When Jesus needed to feed over 5,000 people with very little food, one disciple brought a young boy to Jesus who had a small lunch of five loaves and two fish. Who brought the boy to Jesus?
beginner.explanation: Andrew found a boy in the crowd who had five small barley loaves and two small fish. Even though he wondered 'how far will they go among so many?', Andrew brought the boy to Jesus, and Jesus miraculously fed the entire crowd.

beginner.question: Andrew and Peter were both fishermen before becoming disciples. They worked on a large freshwater lake in northern Israel that features prominently in Jesus' ministry. What body of water did they fish on?
beginner.explanation: Andrew and Peter were fishermen on the Sea of Galilee, also known as the Lake of Gennesaret. This large freshwater lake in northern Israel was a major centre of the fishing industry. Jesus called them while they were casting their nets there.

beginner.question: When some Greek visitors wanted to meet Jesus during the Passover festival, they first approached Philip, who then told Andrew. What did Andrew and Philip do together with the Greeks' request?
options: Told the Greeks to go away
Took the Greeks to the temple
beginner.explanation: Philip went to Andrew with the Greeks' request, and together they went to tell Jesus. This shows Andrew and Philip often worked as a pair. Andrew is frequently seen in the Gospels as someone who brought others to Jesus.

beginner.question: Andrew is often remembered for his role as someone who brought others to Jesus. When Andrew first met Jesus and became convinced He was the Messiah, what was the very first thing Andrew did?
options: He went to find his brother Simon Peter
beginner.explanation: After spending time with Jesus and becoming convinced He was the Messiah, the very first thing Andrew did was find his brother Simon Peter. He told him, 'We have found the Messiah,' and brought Peter to Jesus.

beginner.question: Andrew, along with Peter, James, and John, privately asked Jesus a question while sitting on the Mount of Olives overlooking the temple. What did they ask Jesus about?
options: When the temple would be destroyed and the signs of the end times
Who would be the greatest in heaven
beginner.explanation: After Jesus predicted the temple's destruction, these four disciples privately asked Him when it would happen and what signs would signal the end of the age. Jesus responded with what is known as the Olivet Discourse, describing future events.

beginner.question: Although Peter and Andrew were originally from Bethsaida, they lived in another town during Jesus' ministry that became like Jesus' home base in Galilee. Which town did Peter and Andrew call home?
beginner.explanation: While originally from Bethsaida, Peter and Andrew lived in Capernaum during Jesus' ministry. Capernaum became Jesus' home base in Galilee, and it was at Peter and Andrew's house there that Jesus healed Peter's mother-in-law.

beginner.question: When Jesus was calling His first disciples by the Sea of Galilee, He saw Andrew and Peter casting a net into the water. He invited them to follow Him using a memorable phrase. What did Jesus say?
options: Follow me, and I will make you fishers of men
beginner.explanation: Jesus used language the fishermen would understand, saying 'Follow me, and I will make you fishers of men.' This meant instead of catching fish, they would now reach people with God's message. Andrew and Peter immediately left their nets and followed.

beginner.question: James, one of Jesus' twelve disciples, was the son of a fisherman named Zebedee and the brother of another apostle. Who was James' brother?
beginner.explanation: James and John were brothers, both sons of a fisherman named Zebedee. Jesus called them while they were in their father's boat mending fishing nets on the Sea of Galilee. Together with Peter, they formed Jesus' closest inner circle.

beginner.question: Jesus gave the brothers James and John a special nickname because of their bold and fiery personalities. What did Jesus call James and John?
beginner.explanation: Jesus nicknamed James and John 'Boanerges,' which means 'Sons of Thunder.' This likely referred to their passionate and sometimes impulsive nature. They once asked Jesus if they should call down fire from heaven on a Samaritan village that rejected Him.

beginner.question: James, the son of Zebedee, holds a significant distinction among the twelve apostles. According to the book of Acts, King Herod had James killed. What makes James notable in this regard?
options: He was the first apostle to be martyred
beginner.explanation: King Herod Agrippa I had James killed with the sword, making him the first of the twelve apostles to be martyred for his faith. This is recorded in Acts chapter 12, shortly before Herod also imprisoned Peter.

beginner.question: James and his brother John once made a bold request of Jesus, and their mother even came to ask on their behalf. What did James and John ask for?
options: To sit at Jesus' right and left hand in His kingdom
To be the first to preach
beginner.explanation: James and John asked to sit in the positions of highest honour — at Jesus' right and left hand — in His kingdom. Jesus told them they didn't understand what they were asking and asked if they could drink the cup of suffering He would drink.

beginner.question: Jesus took only three of His twelve disciples up a high mountain where His appearance was transformed — His face shone like the sun and His clothes became brilliant white. What is this event called?
beginner.explanation: The Transfiguration was when Jesus took Peter, James, and John up a high mountain and was transformed before them. His face shone like the sun, His clothes became brilliant white, Moses and Elijah appeared, and God's voice spoke from a cloud.

beginner.question: When a Samaritan village refused to welcome Jesus and His disciples, James and John had a dramatic reaction that showed their fiery 'Sons of Thunder' personalities. What did the brothers suggest doing?
options: Building a wall around the village
beginner.explanation: James and John were so offended by the village's rejection that they asked Jesus, 'Lord, do you want us to call fire down from heaven to destroy them?' Jesus rebuked them for this reaction, showing that His mission was to save people, not destroy them.

beginner.question: James and John were working with their father Zebedee when Jesus called them to follow Him. What were they doing at that exact moment?
options: Mending their fishing nets in a boat
beginner.explanation: James and John were in a boat with their father Zebedee, mending their fishing nets, when Jesus called them. They immediately left the boat and their father and followed Jesus, showing their wholehearted commitment.

beginner.question: The night before Jesus was arrested, He took Peter, James, and John deeper into the Garden of Gethsemane to be near Him while He prayed in great anguish. What did Jesus ask these three disciples to do?
beginner.explanation: Jesus was deeply sorrowful and asked Peter, James, and John to stay and keep watch while He prayed. However, when He returned, He found them sleeping. This happened three times, and Jesus said, 'The spirit is willing, but the flesh is weak.'

beginner.question: James the son of Zebedee was executed by a ruler who was persecuting the early church. Which king ordered James to be put to death by the sword?
beginner.explanation: King Herod Agrippa I had James put to death with the sword to persecute the early church. When Herod saw that this pleased the Jewish leaders, he also arrested Peter. James' martyrdom shows the serious dangers the early apostles faced.

beginner.question: Jesus chose only three of His twelve disciples to witness Him raising a 12-year-old girl from the dead. The girl was the daughter of a synagogue leader named Jairus. Which three disciples were present?
beginner.explanation: Jesus allowed only Peter, James (son of Zebedee), and John to enter the room with Him when He took the girl's hand and said 'Talitha koum!' ('Little girl, I say to you, get up!'). She immediately stood up and walked around.

beginner.question: The apostle John is often described in the Gospels with a special title that shows how close he was to Jesus. What is John often called?
beginner.explanation: John is referred to as 'the disciple whom Jesus loved' or 'the beloved disciple' in the Gospel of John. This doesn't mean Jesus didn't love the others, but it highlights the especially close friendship between Jesus and John.

beginner.question: At the Last Supper, one disciple was reclining next to Jesus and even leaned on His chest. Peter motioned to this disciple to ask Jesus who the betrayer would be. Who was leaning on Jesus?
beginner.explanation: John, the beloved disciple, was reclining next to Jesus at the Last Supper. When Jesus said one of them would betray Him, Peter signalled to John to ask who it was. John leaned back against Jesus and asked, 'Lord, who is it?'

beginner.question: As Jesus was dying on the cross, He looked down and saw His mother Mary standing nearby with one of His disciples. Jesus entrusted His mother's care to this disciple. Who did Jesus ask to take care of Mary?
beginner.explanation: From the cross, Jesus saw His mother and John standing nearby. He said to Mary, 'Woman, here is your son,' and to John, 'Here is your mother.' From that time on, John took Mary into his home and cared for her.

beginner.question: The apostle John wrote one of the four Gospels — an account of Jesus' life, death, and resurrection that focuses heavily on Jesus' identity as the Son of God. Which Gospel did John write?
beginner.explanation: John wrote the Gospel of John, the fourth Gospel. His account is unique because it focuses on Jesus' identity as the Son of God and includes events not found in the other three Gospels, such as Jesus turning water into wine and raising Lazarus.

beginner.question: Besides his Gospel, John also wrote the last book of the Bible, which describes visions of the end times, the defeat of evil, and the coming of a new heaven and new earth. What is this book called?
beginner.explanation: John wrote the book of Revelation (also called the Apocalypse) while exiled on the island of Patmos. In it, he recorded visions from God about the end of the world, the defeat of evil, and the coming of a new heaven and new earth.

beginner.question: In addition to his Gospel and Revelation, John also wrote three short letters in the New Testament that emphasise love, truth, and warnings against false teachers. What are these letters called?
beginner.explanation: John wrote three epistles: 1 John, 2 John, and 3 John. These letters emphasise God's love, the importance of walking in truth, and warnings against false teachers. The phrase 'God is love' comes from 1 John.

beginner.question: John recorded one of the most famous verses in the entire Bible, which begins, 'For God so loved the world that he gave his one and only Son...' In which chapter and verse of John's Gospel is this found?
beginner.explanation: John 3:16 is perhaps the most well-known verse in the Bible. It summarises the entire gospel message: God loved the world so much that He sent Jesus so that everyone who believes in Him will not perish but have eternal life.

beginner.question: John was exiled to a small island in the Aegean Sea where he received the dramatic visions recorded in the book of Revelation. What island was John exiled to?
beginner.explanation: John was banished to the island of Patmos, a small rocky island in the Aegean Sea, likely by the Roman emperor Domitian for preaching about Jesus. It was there that he received the visions that make up the book of Revelation.

beginner.question: On Easter morning, when Mary Magdalene reported the empty tomb, two disciples ran to see for themselves. John arrived first. What did John do when he reached the empty tomb?
options: He looked in but didn't enter at first
He called for the other disciples
beginner.explanation: John outran Peter to the tomb but only bent down and looked in at the linen wrappings without entering. When Peter arrived, he went straight inside. Then John also entered, and the Gospel says he 'saw and believed' that Jesus had risen.

beginner.question: John's father was a fisherman on the Sea of Galilee who had hired servants, suggesting the family was relatively well-off. What was the name of John's father?
beginner.explanation: John and his brother James were the sons of Zebedee, a fisherman on the Sea of Galilee. The family apparently had a successful fishing business, as they had hired servants working for them. John left this comfortable livelihood to follow Jesus.

beginner.question: John opens his Gospel with a famous passage: 'In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God.' Who is 'the Word' referring to?
beginner.explanation: In the opening of his Gospel, John calls Jesus 'the Word' (Greek: Logos). He explains that Jesus existed from the very beginning with God, that Jesus is God, and that through Him all things were made. This is one of the clearest statements of Jesus' divinity.

beginner.question: After Peter and John healed the lame man at the temple gate, they were arrested by the Jewish leaders and told to stop preaching about Jesus. What did Peter and John boldly say in response?
options: They said they must obey God rather than men
