beginner.explanation: When ordered to stop speaking about Jesus, Peter and John boldly replied, 'Which is right in God's eyes: to listen to you, or to him? We cannot help speaking about what we have seen and heard.' They refused to be silenced.

beginner.question: John is believed to be the only one of the twelve original apostles who was present at the crucifixion. Where was John standing as Jesus died on the cross?
options: At the foot of the cross near Jesus' mother
beginner.explanation: While most disciples fled when Jesus was arrested, John stayed and was present at the crucifixion, standing near Jesus' mother Mary. This is where Jesus entrusted Mary to John's care, showing the deep bond between them.

beginner.question: Among the twelve apostles, John is traditionally believed to have been the youngest. Church tradition also holds he is unique among the twelve in how he died. How is John believed to have died?
options: He died of natural causes in old age
beginner.explanation: Church tradition holds that John lived to a very old age, possibly into his 90s, and died of natural causes in Ephesus. He is believed to be the only one of the twelve original apostles who was not killed for his faith, though he was exiled to Patmos.

beginner.question: John once tried to stop a man who was driving out demons in Jesus' name because the man was not part of their group of disciples. What did Jesus tell John about this?
options: He was right to stop him
Do not stop him — whoever is not against you is for you
Report the man to the temple
beginner.explanation: Jesus told John, 'Do not stop him, for whoever is not against you is for you.' This taught the disciples that God's work is not limited to their exclusive group, and they should not be possessive about who can serve in Jesus' name.

beginner.question: Before becoming one of Jesus' twelve disciples, Matthew had a very unpopular job that was despised by most Jewish people because it involved working for the occupying Roman Empire. What was Matthew's occupation?
beginner.explanation: Matthew was a tax collector (also called a publican). Tax collectors were hated by their fellow Jews because they collected money on behalf of the occupying Roman government and were often known for overcharging to enrich themselves.

beginner.question: Matthew was known by two names in the Gospels. The Gospels of Mark and Luke use a different name when describing his calling. Besides Matthew, what other name was he called?
beginner.explanation: Matthew was also known as Levi. Mark and Luke call him 'Levi' when describing how Jesus called him, while the Gospel of Matthew uses 'Matthew.' It was common in that culture for people to have two names.

beginner.question: When Jesus saw Matthew sitting at his tax collector's booth, He spoke just two words to call him. Matthew immediately got up, left everything, and followed. What did Jesus say to Matthew?
beginner.explanation: Jesus simply said 'Follow me' to Matthew as he sat at his tax booth. Matthew immediately got up, left everything behind, and followed Jesus — showing that Jesus' call was powerful enough to make someone walk away from a profitable career instantly.

beginner.question: After Matthew decided to follow Jesus, he threw a big celebration at his house to introduce Jesus to his friends and colleagues. What kind of people came to this party?
beginner.explanation: Matthew invited his friends — other tax collectors and people considered 'sinners' — to a great banquet with Jesus. The Pharisees criticised Jesus for eating with such people, but Jesus replied, 'I have not come to call the righteous, but sinners.'

beginner.question: Matthew is traditionally credited with writing one of the four Gospels. His Gospel was written primarily for a Jewish audience and contains many references to Old Testament prophecies. Which Gospel did Matthew write?
beginner.explanation: Matthew wrote the Gospel of Matthew, the first book of the New Testament. His Gospel frequently quotes Old Testament prophecies to show that Jesus was the promised Messiah. It begins with a genealogy tracing Jesus' lineage to Abraham and David.

beginner.question: Tax collectors in Jesus' time were considered traitors by fellow Jews because they worked for a foreign power that occupied and ruled over Israel. Which empire did tax collectors like Matthew work for?
beginner.explanation: Tax collectors like Matthew worked for the Roman Empire, which occupied and ruled over Israel at that time. They collected taxes from their own people on behalf of Rome, and many added extra charges to enrich themselves, making them deeply despised.

beginner.question: When the Pharisees complained that Jesus was eating dinner with tax collectors and sinners at Matthew's house, Jesus responded with a well-known saying about who He came to help. What did Jesus say?
options: Blessed are the poor in spirit
It is not the healthy who need a doctor, but the sick
beginner.explanation: Jesus told the Pharisees, 'It is not the healthy who need a doctor, but the sick. I have not come to call the righteous, but sinners.' He was explaining that His mission was to reach people who knew they needed God's grace.

beginner.question: Matthew's tax booth was located in a major hub of commerce on the trade route near the Sea of Galilee, where Jesus based much of His ministry. In which town was Matthew's tax booth?
beginner.explanation: Matthew's tax booth was in Capernaum, an important commercial centre on the northern shore of the Sea of Galilee. It sat on a major trade route, making it a key location for collecting customs taxes on goods passing through the area.

beginner.question: Matthew's Gospel begins with a genealogy — a family tree — of Jesus, tracing His lineage back to two very important figures in Jewish history. Who are these two key ancestors Matthew highlights?
beginner.explanation: Matthew opens his Gospel by tracing Jesus' family line from Abraham (the father of the Jewish nation) through King David (Israel's greatest king) to Jesus. This showed his Jewish readers that Jesus was the promised Messiah from David's royal line.

beginner.question: The Gospel of Matthew records one of Jesus' most famous teachings, preached on a hillside, that includes the Beatitudes and the Lord's Prayer. What is this famous sermon called?
beginner.explanation: The Sermon on the Mount, found in Matthew chapters 5-7, is Jesus' most famous teaching. It includes the Beatitudes ('Blessed are the poor in spirit...'), the Lord's Prayer, and key teachings like 'love your enemies' and 'do not worry about tomorrow.'

beginner.question: After Jesus rose from the dead, He appeared to the disciples, but one apostle was not present and refused to believe unless he could see and touch Jesus' wounds. Which disciple is known for this moment of doubt?
beginner.explanation: Thomas was not with the other disciples when Jesus first appeared after His resurrection. He declared he would not believe unless he could see the nail marks in Jesus' hands and put his finger in them. This is why he is often called 'Doubting Thomas.'

beginner.question: When Jesus appeared to Thomas eight days later and invited him to touch His wounds, Thomas was overwhelmed and made a powerful declaration of faith. What did Thomas say?
beginner.explanation: When Thomas saw the risen Jesus and was invited to touch His wounds, he exclaimed, 'My Lord and my God!' This is one of the strongest declarations of Jesus' divinity in the entire New Testament. Jesus then said, 'Blessed are those who have not seen and yet have believed.'

beginner.question: Thomas had another name that appears in the Gospel of John. The name 'Thomas' means 'twin' in Aramaic, and his other name means the same thing in Greek. What was Thomas' Greek name?
beginner.explanation: Thomas was also known as 'Didymus,' which means 'twin' in Greek, just as 'Thomas' means 'twin' in Aramaic. The Bible does not tell us who his twin was, but this double name confirms he was known by both his Aramaic and Greek names.

beginner.question: When Jesus decided to go back to Judea to visit His friend Lazarus who had died — even though it was dangerous because Jewish leaders wanted to kill Him — Thomas showed surprising courage. What did Thomas say to the other disciples?
options: Let us also go, that we may die with him
beginner.explanation: Despite his later reputation for doubt, Thomas showed great bravery here. He rallied the other disciples, saying they should go with Jesus even if it meant death. This shows Thomas was deeply loyal and brave, not just the 'doubter' he is often remembered as.

beginner.question: During the Last Supper, Jesus told His disciples He was going to prepare a place for them. Thomas asked a sincere question that prompted one of the most famous statements Jesus ever made. What did Thomas ask?
options: Lord, we don't know where you are going, so how can we know the way?
beginner.explanation: Thomas honestly admitted he didn't understand, asking how they could know the way if they didn't know the destination. Jesus answered with one of the most famous statements in the Bible: 'I am the way, the truth, and the life. No one comes to the Father except through me.'

beginner.question: When Jesus appeared to Thomas and showed him His hands and side, He gently encouraged Thomas with specific words about doubt and faith. What did Jesus tell Thomas?
options: Stop being afraid and start being brave
beginner.explanation: Jesus gently encouraged Thomas by saying, 'Stop doubting and believe.' This was not a harsh rebuke but a loving invitation to faith. Thomas responded with the powerful confession 'My Lord and my God!'

beginner.question: After Thomas declared 'My Lord and my God!', Jesus made a statement about a group of people who would be especially blessed in the future. Who did Jesus say would be blessed?
options: Those who have not seen and yet have believed
Those who give money to the poor
beginner.explanation: Jesus said, 'Because you have seen me, you have believed; blessed are those who have not seen and yet have believed.' This refers to all future believers — including Christians today — who believe in Jesus without having physically seen Him.

beginner.question: Thomas was absent when Jesus first appeared to the disciples after the resurrection in a locked room. How many days later did Jesus appear again, this time with Thomas present?
beginner.explanation: Eight days after Jesus' first appearance, He came again while the disciples were in the house — this time with Thomas present. Jesus specifically addressed Thomas and invited him to touch His wounds, knowing about Thomas' earlier declaration of doubt.

beginner.question: According to ancient church tradition, Thomas travelled far from Israel after Jesus' ascension to spread the gospel. Which country is Thomas traditionally believed to have brought Christianity to?
beginner.explanation: Church tradition holds that Thomas travelled all the way to India to preach the gospel. The 'Saint Thomas Christians' of southern India trace their faith back to the apostle Thomas. This tradition is ancient and deeply held.

beginner.question: When Jesus appeared to the disciples in the locked room after His resurrection, He showed them something as proof of His identity — that He was the same Jesus who had been crucified. What did He show them?
options: His hands and side with the wounds
beginner.explanation: Jesus showed His disciples the nail marks in His hands and the wound in His side from the spear that had pierced Him during the crucifixion. These wounds proved He was the same Jesus who had died on the cross, now alive again.

beginner.question: Philip was one of the first disciples Jesus called. According to the Gospel of John, what was the first thing Philip did after deciding to follow Jesus?
options: He found Nathanael and told him about Jesus
He started preaching in the streets
beginner.explanation: After Jesus called Philip, Philip immediately went to find his friend Nathanael (Bartholomew) and told him, 'We have found the one Moses wrote about — Jesus of Nazareth.' When Nathanael was sceptical, Philip simply said, 'Come and see.'

beginner.question: Before the miracle of feeding the 5,000, Jesus tested one of His disciples by asking where they could buy enough bread for the huge crowd. Which disciple did Jesus direct this question to?
beginner.explanation: Jesus asked Philip, 'Where shall we buy bread for these people to eat?' The Gospel notes that Jesus asked this to test Philip, because He already knew what He was going to do. Philip responded that even eight months' wages wouldn't buy enough.

beginner.question: Philip was from the same hometown as two other well-known disciples, Andrew and Peter. This was a fishing village on the shore of the Sea of Galilee. What town was Philip from?
beginner.explanation: Philip was from Bethsaida, a fishing village on the shore of the Sea of Galilee. He shared this hometown with Andrew and Peter. Bethsaida would have been a close-knit community, which may explain why several disciples came from there.

beginner.question: During the Last Supper, Philip made a request of Jesus that prompted one of Jesus' most important teachings about His relationship with God the Father. What did Philip ask?
options: Lord, show us the Father and that will be enough for us
beginner.explanation: Philip asked Jesus to show them the Father. Jesus responded, 'Anyone who has seen me has seen the Father,' teaching that He and the Father are one. This is one of the clearest statements about Jesus being God.

beginner.question: When Jesus called Philip to follow Him, Philip's calling is unique because Jesus specifically sought him out and found him. Where did Jesus find and call Philip?
options: On a boat in the sea
beginner.explanation: Jesus found Philip in Galilee and said to him, 'Follow me.' Unlike some other disciples who were introduced by friends or family, Jesus directly and personally called Philip. Philip then went and brought Nathanael to Jesus.

beginner.question: When some Greek visitors wanted to see Jesus during the Passover festival, they went to Philip first. Why might the Greeks have felt comfortable approaching Philip specifically?
options: He had a Greek name and was from a region near Gentile territory
He was standing at the gate
beginner.explanation: Philip had a Greek name (Philippos, meaning 'lover of horses') and was from Bethsaida, near the predominantly Gentile region of the Decapolis. The Greek visitors may have felt more comfortable approaching someone with Greek cultural connections.

beginner.question: When Philip calculated the cost of feeding the crowd of 5,000, he mentioned a specific amount of money that still wouldn't be enough. How much did Philip say wouldn't suffice?
beginner.explanation: Philip calculated that even 200 denarii — roughly eight months' wages for a labourer — would not buy enough bread for each person to have even a small bite. This highlighted the impossible scale of the situation, making Jesus' miracle even more amazing.

beginner.question: When Philip told Nathanael about Jesus, saying He was from Nazareth, Nathanael was sceptical and made a dismissive comment about the town. What did Nathanael say about Nazareth?
options: Nazareth! Can anything good come from there?
beginner.explanation: Nathanael's response showed that Nazareth was considered an insignificant village. He doubted anything noteworthy could come from such a small, unremarkable place. But Philip wisely didn't argue — he simply replied, 'Come and see.'

beginner.question: When Philip brought Nathanael (also called Bartholomew) to meet Jesus, Jesus said something remarkable about Nathanael's character before they had even spoken. What did Jesus say?
options: You will be a great leader
Here is a true Israelite, in whom there is no deceit
You are a son of thunder
beginner.explanation: Jesus praised Nathanael's honest and sincere character, calling him 'a true Israelite in whom there is no deceit.' Nathanael was amazed that Jesus knew his character without having met him and asked how Jesus knew him.

beginner.question: When Nathanael asked Jesus how He knew him, Jesus revealed He had supernaturally seen Nathanael somewhere specific before Philip had even called him. Where had Jesus seen Nathanael?
beginner.explanation: Jesus told Nathanael, 'I saw you while you were still under the fig tree before Philip called you.' Whatever Nathanael had been doing under that fig tree — perhaps praying or studying Scripture — Jesus had supernaturally seen him, which convinced Nathanael.

beginner.question: After Jesus told Nathanael He had seen him under the fig tree, Nathanael was so amazed that he made a bold declaration about who Jesus was. What did Nathanael say?
options: Rabbi, you are the Son of God! You are the King of Israel!
You are John the Baptist returned!
beginner.explanation: Nathanael's declaration was one of the earliest and most powerful confessions of Jesus' true identity. From a single encounter, he recognised Jesus as both the Son of God and the King of Israel, showing remarkable spiritual perception.

beginner.question: Nathanael is generally believed to be the same person as Bartholomew, who appears in the lists of the twelve apostles. Why do scholars believe Nathanael and Bartholomew are the same person?
options: Bartholomew means 'son of Tolmai' (a surname) and he's always listed next to Philip
beginner.explanation: 'Bartholomew' is not a first name but a surname meaning 'son of Tolmai.' In the Synoptic Gospels, Bartholomew is always listed alongside Philip. In John's Gospel, Philip's close friend is called Nathanael. So scholars conclude Bartholomew's first name was Nathanael.

beginner.question: Nathanael was from a town in Galilee that is also famous for being the location of Jesus' first miracle — turning water into wine at a wedding. Where was Nathanael from?
beginner.explanation: Nathanael was from Cana in Galilee, as mentioned in John 21:2. This is the same town where Jesus attended a wedding and turned water into wine, His first recorded miracle.

beginner.question: The twelve apostles included two men named James. One was the son of Zebedee and John's brother. The other, sometimes called 'James the Less,' was the son of which man?
beginner.explanation: James the son of Alphaeus was one of the twelve apostles. He is called 'James the Less' or 'James the Younger' to distinguish him from the more prominent James, son of Zebedee. He faithfully served as one of Jesus' chosen twelve.

beginner.question: James the son of Alphaeus is sometimes referred to by a nickname that distinguishes him from James the son of Zebedee. What nickname is this James given?
beginner.explanation: James the son of Alphaeus is called 'James the Less' or 'James the Younger' (Greek: 'mikros,' meaning small or younger) to distinguish him from the other apostle named James. This may refer to his younger age, shorter stature, or lesser prominence.

beginner.question: The twelve apostles included several sets of duplicate first names, which is why many disciples had surnames or nicknames. How many sets of duplicate first names were among the twelve?
beginner.explanation: Among the twelve, there were two men named James (son of Zebedee and son of Alphaeus), two named Simon (Peter and Simon the Zealot), and two named Judas (Judas Iscariot and Judas son of James/Thaddaeus) — three sets of duplicates.

beginner.question: One of the twelve apostles was named Judas, but he was NOT Judas Iscariot who betrayed Jesus. This faithful Judas asked Jesus a question at the Last Supper. By what other name is this good Judas known?
beginner.explanation: Judas the son of James, also known as Thaddaeus, was one of the twelve faithful apostles. He is carefully distinguished in the Gospels from Judas Iscariot, the betrayer. He asked Jesus why He would reveal Himself to the disciples but not to the world.

beginner.question: At the Last Supper, the faithful Judas (not Iscariot) asked Jesus why He planned to reveal Himself to His disciples but not to the whole world. How did Jesus answer?
options: Anyone who loves me will obey my teaching, and my Father will love them
The world does not deserve it
beginner.explanation: Jesus answered that He reveals Himself to those who love Him and keep His word. The Father and Son make their home with those who love and obey. Knowing God is about a personal relationship of love and obedience, not grand public displays.

beginner.question: Thaddaeus is identified differently in different Gospel lists. In Matthew and Mark he is called Thaddaeus, but Luke uses a different name. What does Luke call this apostle?
beginner.explanation: Luke identifies this apostle as 'Judas son of James' while Matthew and Mark call him 'Thaddaeus.' Scholars believe these are the same person known by different names. Luke carefully adds 'not Iscariot' to avoid confusion with the betrayer.

beginner.question: One of Jesus' twelve apostles was called 'Simon the Zealot.' His nickname likely referred to something about his background before becoming a disciple. What does 'the Zealot' suggest about Simon?
options: He was passionate about Jewish independence from Rome
He was from a wealthy family
beginner.explanation: 'Zealot' likely indicates that Simon was associated with the Zealots, a Jewish political movement fiercely opposed to Roman rule. Some Zealots used violence to fight for independence. Jesus chose Simon despite this radical background.

beginner.question: The twelve apostles included two men named Simon. One was Simon Peter, who became the leader of the early church. Who was the other Simon among the twelve?
beginner.explanation: Simon the Zealot was the second Simon among the twelve apostles. While Simon Peter became the most prominent leader, Simon the Zealot faithfully served as one of the twelve. His political background as a Zealot made him very different from Matthew the tax collector.

beginner.question: It is remarkable that Jesus chose both Simon the Zealot and Matthew the tax collector, because their backgrounds would normally make them bitter enemies. Why would a Zealot and a tax collector be enemies?
options: Zealots opposed Rome while tax collectors worked for Rome
beginner.explanation: Zealots were passionate Jewish nationalists who hated Roman rule and saw tax collectors as traitors for collaborating with Rome. That Jesus brought them together as brothers shows the transformative power of following Christ.

beginner.question: One of Jesus' twelve chosen disciples ultimately betrayed Him to the religious authorities, leading to His arrest and crucifixion. Who betrayed Jesus?
beginner.explanation: Judas Iscariot, one of Jesus' own twelve apostles, betrayed Him to the chief priests and elders. Despite travelling with Jesus, hearing His teachings, and witnessing His miracles for three years, Judas chose to hand Jesus over.

beginner.question: Judas Iscariot agreed to betray Jesus to the chief priests in exchange for money. The amount was shamefully small — the same as the Old Testament price of a slave. How much was Judas paid?
beginner.explanation: Judas received 30 pieces of silver — the price of a slave in Old Testament law — to betray Jesus. This fulfilled a prophecy from the book of Zechariah. The amount was shamefully small for such a terrible act of betrayal.

beginner.question: When Judas came to the Garden of Gethsemane with soldiers to arrest Jesus, he used a pre-arranged signal to identify Jesus to the armed crowd. What did Judas do to point out Jesus?
options: He put a hand on His shoulder
beginner.explanation: Judas had told the soldiers, 'The one I kiss is the man; arrest him.' He went up to Jesus, said 'Greetings, Rabbi!' and kissed Him. Using a gesture of friendship and respect as a signal for arrest made the betrayal especially shocking.

beginner.question: Among the twelve apostles, Judas Iscariot was given a specific role that involved managing the group's shared resources. What was Judas' role among the twelve?
options: He was the keeper of the money bag
beginner.explanation: Judas was the treasurer of the group, carrying the money bag that held the funds the disciples shared. The Gospel of John notes that Judas used to steal from the money bag, showing his love of money was a weakness even before the betrayal.

beginner.question: When a woman poured expensive perfume on Jesus at a dinner in Bethany, Judas objected and said the perfume should have been sold and the money given to the poor. According to John's Gospel, why did Judas really object?
options: He genuinely cared about the poor
He wanted to steal from the money bag
beginner.explanation: John's Gospel reveals that Judas didn't actually care about the poor. He objected because, as keeper of the money bag, he used to help himself to what was in it. He wanted the perfume sold so the money would pass through his hands.

beginner.question: At the Last Supper, Jesus told His disciples that one of them would betray Him. He identified the betrayer by dipping a piece of bread and giving it to someone. Who did Jesus hand the bread to?
beginner.explanation: Jesus dipped a piece of bread and gave it to Judas Iscariot, identifying him as the betrayer. Jesus then said, 'What you are about to do, do quickly.' Judas took the bread and went out into the night.

beginner.question: After Judas betrayed Jesus, he was overcome with guilt and tried to return the 30 pieces of silver. When the chief priests refused to take the money back, what did Judas do with the coins?
options: He gave them to the poor
He threw them into the temple
beginner.explanation: Overcome with remorse, Judas threw the thirty silver coins into the temple and left. He declared he had sinned by betraying innocent blood, but the chief priests dismissed him, saying it was his problem, not theirs.

beginner.question: After Judas returned the 30 pieces of silver, the chief priests couldn't put the 'blood money' back in the temple treasury. So they used it to buy a field. What was this field used for?
beginner.explanation: The chief priests bought a potter's field to serve as a burial place for foreigners. This field became known as the 'Field of Blood' — Akeldama in Aramaic — because it was purchased with the blood money from Jesus' betrayal.

beginner.question: The name 'Iscariot' likely tells us something about where Judas was from. What is the most common interpretation of the name 'Iscariot'?
options: It means 'man from Kerioth' — a town in Judea
beginner.explanation: 'Iscariot' is believed to come from 'Ish Kerioth,' meaning 'man of Kerioth,' a town in southern Judea. If correct, this would make Judas the only one of the twelve apostles who was not from Galilee, potentially making him an outsider.

beginner.question: When Jesus was arrested in the Garden of Gethsemane after Judas' kiss of betrayal, how did Jesus address Judas?
options: Friend, do what you came for
beginner.explanation: Even in this moment of ultimate betrayal, Jesus addressed Judas as 'Friend.' He also asked, 'Judas, are you betraying the Son of Man with a kiss?' Jesus' calm and compassionate response, even to His betrayer, shows His extraordinary character.

beginner.question: At the Last Supper, when Jesus announced one of the disciples would betray Him, the disciples were shocked. What did each disciple ask Him, one by one?
options: Surely you don't mean me, Lord?
beginner.explanation: Each disciple asked 'Surely you don't mean me, Lord?' Even Judas asked the same question, and Jesus answered him, 'You have said so,' confirming Judas was the betrayer.

beginner.question: The prophet Zechariah, writing hundreds of years before Jesus, made a prophecy about thirty pieces of silver that was fulfilled when Judas betrayed Jesus. What did Zechariah prophesy about this amount?
options: That it would be given to a king
That it would be thrown to the potter in the house of the Lord
That it would be donated to the poor
That it would be melted down
beginner.explanation: Zechariah 11:12-13 prophesied that thirty pieces of silver would be thrown to the potter in the house of the Lord. This was fulfilled when Judas threw the silver into the temple and the priests used it to buy a potter's field.

beginner.question: After Jesus' betrayal and death, Judas Iscariot could not live with what he had done. According to the Gospel of Matthew, how did Judas die?
beginner.explanation: Matthew 27:5 records that after returning the silver and being rejected by the chief priests, Judas went away and hanged himself. His story is a tragic example of remorse without repentance — he turned to despair rather than seeking God's forgiveness.

beginner.question: Judas Iscariot travelled with Jesus for the entire duration of His public ministry, hearing His teachings and witnessing His miracles. Approximately how long was Judas a disciple before he betrayed Jesus?
beginner.explanation: Judas was with Jesus for approximately three years — the full duration of His public ministry. During that time, Judas heard Jesus' teachings, witnessed miracles, and was even sent out with the other eleven to preach and heal.

beginner.question: In the Garden of Gethsemane, Judas arrived with a large armed crowd carrying swords and clubs to arrest Jesus. Who had sent this crowd?
beginner.explanation: The chief priests and elders of the Jewish religious establishment sent the armed crowd. They had been plotting to kill Jesus because they saw Him as a threat to their authority. Judas led them to Jesus at night, away from the crowds.

beginner.question: After Judas Iscariot's death, the remaining eleven apostles felt it was important to restore the number to twelve. Who was chosen to replace Judas as the twelfth apostle?
beginner.explanation: Matthias was chosen to take Judas Iscariot's place among the twelve apostles. This happened between Jesus' ascension and the day of Pentecost. Matthias had been a follower of Jesus from the beginning and had witnessed His resurrection.

beginner.question: When the apostles selected a replacement for Judas, they proposed two candidates. One was Matthias, who was ultimately chosen. Who was the other candidate?
options: Joseph called Barsabbas (also known as Justus)
beginner.explanation: The two candidates were Matthias and Joseph called Barsabbas (also known as Justus). Both had been with Jesus from John the Baptist's ministry through the resurrection. The apostles prayed and cast lots, and Matthias was chosen.

beginner.question: To be considered as a replacement for Judas Iscariot, a candidate had to meet a specific requirement about their time with Jesus. What was this requirement?
options: They had to have been with Jesus from His baptism through His ascension
They had to be from Galilee
They had to be a relative of Jesus
beginner.explanation: Peter stated that the replacement had to have accompanied Jesus from the baptism by John the Baptist through His ascension — and most importantly, be a witness of the resurrection. This was crucial for the apostle's role as an eyewitness.

beginner.question: The method used to choose between Matthias and Joseph Barsabbas was a common practice in Old Testament times for determining God's will. What method did the apostles use?
options: A vote by show of hands
beginner.explanation: After praying, the apostles cast lots and Matthias was chosen. Casting lots was an ancient practice for discerning God's will. This is the last time casting lots is mentioned in the Bible — after Pentecost, the Holy Spirit guided decisions directly.

beginner.question: Peter quoted a psalm of David when explaining why Judas needed to be replaced. The psalm says, 'May another take his place of leadership.' Which book of the Bible contains this prophecy?
beginner.explanation: Peter quoted Psalm 109:8, 'May another take his place of leadership,' to explain that replacing Judas was fulfilling Scripture. He also quoted Psalm 69:25. The early church saw these psalms as prophetically pointing to Judas' fate.

beginner.question: Before becoming one of the most important apostles in the New Testament, Paul was actually an enemy of the early Christians who persecuted and arrested believers. What was Paul's name before his conversion?
beginner.explanation: Paul was originally known as Saul of Tarsus. As Saul, he was a devout Pharisee who believed early Christians were heretics and actively persecuted them. He was present at the stoning of Stephen, the first Christian martyr, and approved of his death.

beginner.question: Saul (Paul) was travelling to a specific city with letters authorising him to arrest Christians when he had a dramatic encounter with the risen Jesus that changed his life forever. Which city was Saul heading to?
beginner.explanation: Saul was on the road to Damascus, Syria, carrying letters from the high priest authorising him to arrest any Christians he found there. As he neared the city, a bright light from heaven suddenly flashed around him and he heard Jesus' voice.

beginner.question: When Jesus appeared to Saul on the road to Damascus in a blinding light, He spoke to Saul directly. What did the voice of Jesus say to Saul?
options: Saul, Saul, why do you persecute me?
beginner.explanation: Jesus said, 'Saul, Saul, why do you persecute me?' Saul asked, 'Who are you, Lord?' and Jesus replied, 'I am Jesus, whom you are persecuting.' This made Saul realise that by persecuting Christians, he had been persecuting Jesus Himself.

beginner.question: After his encounter with Jesus on the road to Damascus, Saul was left unable to see. He didn't eat or drink anything until a disciple came and healed him. How long was Saul blind?
beginner.explanation: Saul was blind for three days. During this time, he fasted completely. God then sent a disciple named Ananias to lay hands on Saul. Something like scales fell from his eyes, he regained his sight, was baptised, and began his new life following Jesus.

beginner.question: Paul was born and grew up in a city outside of Israel, which gave him Roman citizenship — a status that protected him several times during his ministry. Where was Paul from?
beginner.explanation: Paul was from Tarsus, a major city in the Roman province of Cilicia (modern-day Turkey). As a native of Tarsus, he was a Roman citizen by birth. This citizenship gave him legal rights, including the right to appeal to Caesar.

beginner.question: Paul was not one of the original twelve apostles, but he became the most prolific writer in the New Testament. Approximately how many of the 27 New Testament books are letters written by Paul?
beginner.explanation: Paul wrote 13 of the 27 New Testament books — nearly half! These include Romans, 1 and 2 Corinthians, Galatians, Ephesians, Philippians, Colossians, 1 and 2 Thessalonians, 1 and 2 Timothy, Titus, and Philemon.

beginner.question: Before becoming an apostle, Paul studied under one of the most respected Jewish teachers of his day in Jerusalem. Paul belonged to the strictest Jewish religious group. What group did Paul belong to?
beginner.explanation: Paul was a Pharisee, the strictest sect of Judaism, trained under the renowned teacher Gamaliel. He described himself as 'a Pharisee, descended from Pharisees' and 'a Hebrew of Hebrews.' His knowledge later helped him explain how Jesus fulfilled prophecy.

beginner.question: Besides being an apostle, Paul also worked a trade to support himself during his missionary journeys so he wouldn't be a financial burden to the churches he served. What was Paul's trade?
beginner.explanation: Paul was a tentmaker by trade. While travelling and preaching, he often worked making tents to support himself. In Corinth, he worked alongside Aquila and Priscilla, who were also tentmakers.

beginner.question: During one of Paul's journeys, he was shipwrecked on an island. While gathering firewood, a venomous snake bit him, but he was completely unharmed. What island was this?
beginner.explanation: Paul was shipwrecked on Malta while being transported to Rome as a prisoner. A venomous snake fastened itself to his hand. The islanders expected him to die, but he was completely unharmed, which amazed everyone.

beginner.question: Paul was present at the death of Stephen, the first Christian martyr, who was killed by stoning for preaching about Jesus. What role did Saul (Paul) play at Stephen's stoning?
options: He guarded the cloaks of those stoning Stephen and approved of his death
beginner.explanation: The men who stoned Stephen laid their cloaks at the feet of young Saul. Acts notes that Saul approved of the execution. This was before his conversion. Stephen's death may have planted seeds that later contributed to Saul's transformation.

beginner.question: Paul wrote a famous passage about love that is often read at weddings, beginning, 'Love is patient, love is kind...' In which of Paul's letters is this passage found?
beginner.explanation: The famous 'love chapter' is found in 1 Corinthians chapter 13. Paul wrote it to the church in Corinth to explain that love is the greatest of all spiritual gifts. The passage describes love as patient, kind, not envious, and never failing.

beginner.question: Paul was eventually arrested and sent to Rome as a prisoner because he exercised a legal right as a Roman citizen. What did Paul do that led to his transfer to Rome?
options: He asked to preach in Rome
beginner.explanation: When Paul felt he wasn't getting justice in Caesarea, he exercised his right as a Roman citizen to have his case heard by the Emperor (Caesar) in Rome. He was then sent as a prisoner to Rome, where he continued to preach under house arrest.

beginner.question: God told the disciple Ananias in Damascus to go and heal Saul's blindness. Ananias was initially afraid because he knew Saul persecuted Christians. What did God tell Ananias about Saul's future?
options: He is my chosen instrument to carry my name to the Gentiles
beginner.explanation: God told Ananias, 'Go! This man is my chosen instrument to proclaim my name to the Gentiles and their kings and to the people of Israel.' Despite his fears, Ananias obeyed and called Saul 'Brother Saul' — a remarkable act of faith.

beginner.question: During his second missionary journey, Paul had a vision at night of a man pleading for help. This vision led Paul to bring the gospel to a new continent for the first time. Where was the man from?
beginner.explanation: Paul saw a man from Macedonia saying, 'Come over to Macedonia and help us.' Paul interpreted this as God directing him to bring the gospel to Europe for the first time. He sailed to Macedonia and preached in Philippi and Thessalonica.

beginner.question: While imprisoned in Philippi after being beaten, Paul and his companion Silas did something remarkable at midnight that led to a miraculous event. What did Paul and Silas do in prison?
beginner.explanation: Despite being beaten and chained, Paul and Silas sang hymns and prayed at midnight. A violent earthquake shook the prison, all doors flew open, and everyone's chains came loose. The jailer and his family became believers that night.

beginner.question: Barnabas was an early Christian from the island of Cyprus who became a close companion of Paul on missionary journeys. The apostles gave him the nickname 'Barnabas.' What does this name mean?
beginner.explanation: The apostles gave Joseph the nickname 'Barnabas,' which means 'Son of Encouragement' (or 'Son of Consolation'). He earned this name because he was known for encouraging and supporting others, including being the first to welcome the converted Paul.

beginner.question: When the early Christians in Jerusalem were selling possessions to share with those in need, Barnabas made a notable act of generosity that is specifically highlighted in the book of Acts. What did Barnabas sell?
beginner.explanation: Barnabas sold a field he owned and brought all the money to the apostles to distribute to those in need. This act of generosity is specifically highlighted in Acts, contrasting sharply with Ananias and Sapphira, who lied about their offering.

beginner.question: After Saul (Paul) converted to Christianity, the believers in Jerusalem were afraid of him because he had been their persecutor. Who vouched for Paul and introduced him to the apostles?
beginner.explanation: When Paul tried to join the disciples in Jerusalem, they were all afraid of him. Barnabas took Paul, brought him to the apostles, and told them about Paul's conversion and bold preaching. Without Barnabas, Paul might have been rejected by the church.

beginner.question: Barnabas and Paul had a sharp disagreement before their second missionary journey that led them to part ways and travel separately. What was the disagreement about?
options: Whether to take John Mark with them
beginner.explanation: Barnabas wanted to take his cousin John Mark, but Paul refused because Mark had deserted them during the first journey. The disagreement was so sharp they split up — Barnabas took Mark to Cyprus, while Paul chose Silas and went through Syria.

beginner.question: Barnabas was originally from which island in the Mediterranean Sea? He later returned to this island on a missionary journey with John Mark.
beginner.explanation: Barnabas was a Levite from Cyprus, a large island in the eastern Mediterranean. He and Paul visited Cyprus on their first missionary journey. After they parted ways, Barnabas returned to Cyprus with John Mark to continue spreading the faith.

beginner.question: When Jesus first called His disciples by the Sea of Galilee, He used a memorable phrase that related to their fishing profession and described their new mission. What did Jesus promise to make them?
beginner.explanation: Jesus told Simon Peter and Andrew, 'Follow me, and I will make you fishers of men.' This powerful metaphor meant they would now 'catch' people for God's kingdom instead of fish. It connected their familiar livelihood to their new spiritual mission.

beginner.question: On the night before His crucifixion, Jesus shared a final meal with His twelve disciples. During this meal, He broke bread and shared wine, telling them to remember Him. What is this meal called?
beginner.explanation: The Last Supper was the final meal Jesus shared with His twelve disciples before His arrest and crucifixion. He broke bread and shared wine, telling them to do this in remembrance of Him. Christians continue this through Communion or the Eucharist.

beginner.question: At the Last Supper, Jesus did something unexpected that shocked His disciples. He wrapped a towel around His waist and performed a task normally done by the lowest servant in a household. What did Jesus do?
beginner.explanation: Jesus washed His disciples' feet, a task so lowly that even Jewish slaves were not required to do it. By washing their feet, Jesus taught that true leadership means serving others humbly. He told them to follow His example.

beginner.question: After His resurrection, Jesus gave His disciples a final instruction before ascending to heaven — to go into all the world and make disciples of all nations. What is this command known as?
beginner.explanation: The Great Commission is Jesus' command: 'Go and make disciples of all nations, baptising them in the name of the Father and of the Son and of the Holy Spirit, and teaching them to obey everything I have commanded you.' This became the church's mission.

beginner.question: Jesus once sent His twelve disciples out in pairs to preach, heal the sick, and cast out demons. He gave them specific instructions about what NOT to bring. What did Jesus tell them not to take?
beginner.explanation: Jesus told the twelve to travel light — no extra money, bag, bread, or second tunic. They were to depend entirely on God's provision and the hospitality of those who received them, learning to trust God completely.

beginner.question: On the day of Pentecost — 50 days after Jesus' resurrection — the disciples were gathered together when something miraculous appeared over each of them. What appeared?
beginner.explanation: On Pentecost, the Holy Spirit came upon the disciples and what looked like tongues of fire appeared and rested on each of them. They were filled with the Holy Spirit and began speaking in other languages, sharing the gospel with people from many nations.

beginner.question: Before Jesus chose His twelve disciples, He spent an entire night doing something important to prepare for this significant decision. What did Jesus do all night long?
beginner.explanation: Jesus went up on a mountainside and spent the entire night praying to God the Father. When morning came, He called His followers and chose twelve to be His apostles. This shows how seriously Jesus took this decision and sought God's guidance.

beginner.question: When Jesus ascended to heaven, He promised the disciples He would send them a helper or advocate who would be with them forever, teach them, and give them power. What did Jesus call this helper?
beginner.explanation: Jesus promised to send the Holy Spirit, also called the Advocate or Comforter, to be with His disciples forever. The Holy Spirit would teach them, remind them of Jesus' words, and give them power to be His witnesses. This was fulfilled at Pentecost.

beginner.question: Simon Peter was a fisherman by trade before Jesus called him to be a disciple. He worked on the Sea of Galilee with his brother Andrew.
beginner.explanation: Simon Peter and his brother Andrew were both fishermen on the Sea of Galilee. Jesus found them casting their nets and called them to follow Him, promising to make them 'fishers of men.'

beginner.question: Jesus gave Simon the name 'Peter,' which means 'rock' in Greek. In Aramaic, the equivalent name is 'Cephas.'
beginner.explanation: Both 'Peter' (Greek: Petros) and 'Cephas' (Aramaic) mean 'rock.' Jesus gave Simon this name to signify the foundational role Peter would play in building the early church.

beginner.question: On the night Jesus was arrested, Peter denied knowing Jesus two times before a rooster crowed, just as Jesus had predicted.
beginner.explanation: Peter denied knowing Jesus three times, not two. Jesus had predicted at the Last Supper that Peter would deny Him three times before the rooster crowed, and it happened exactly as foretold.

beginner.question: During a storm on the Sea of Galilee, Peter stepped out of the boat and walked on the water toward Jesus, but began to sink when he became afraid.
beginner.explanation: Peter asked Jesus to call him out onto the water, and he actually walked on the surface toward Jesus. But when he noticed the strong wind and waves, he became afraid, lost faith, and began to sink. Jesus immediately caught him.

beginner.question: Peter preached the first sermon of the early church on the day of Pentecost, and about 3,000 people were baptised and added to the church that day.
beginner.explanation: When the Holy Spirit came at Pentecost, Peter stood up and boldly preached to the crowd in Jerusalem. He explained that Jesus, who had been crucified, was raised from the dead by God. About 3,000 people responded by being baptised.

beginner.question: Peter was one of the twelve disciples, but before following Jesus, he worked as a carpenter in the town of Nazareth.
beginner.explanation: Peter was not a carpenter — he was a fisherman on the Sea of Galilee. Jesus' earthly father Joseph was the carpenter. Peter worked with fishing nets and boats before Jesus called him.

beginner.question: When Jesus asked His disciples, 'Who do you say I am?', Peter boldly answered, 'You are the Christ, the Son of the living God.'
beginner.explanation: Peter's declaration was a pivotal moment in the Gospels. Jesus responded by saying Peter was blessed because this truth had been revealed to him by God the Father, not by human reasoning.

beginner.question: When soldiers came to arrest Jesus in the Garden of Gethsemane, Peter drew a sword and cut off the ear of a Roman soldier standing guard.
beginner.explanation: Peter cut off the ear of the high priest's servant, named Malchus — not a Roman soldier. Jesus told Peter to put away his sword and then miraculously healed the servant's ear.

beginner.question: Peter and John healed a man who had been unable to walk since birth at the temple gate called Beautiful, saying, 'In the name of Jesus Christ of Nazareth, walk.'
beginner.explanation: Peter told the lame beggar, 'Silver or gold I do not have, but what I do have I give you. In the name of Jesus Christ of Nazareth, walk.' The man was instantly healed and began walking and leaping and praising God.

beginner.question: After Peter denied Jesus three times, Jesus later appeared to him after the resurrection and asked Peter 'Do you love me?' three times, restoring him to ministry.
beginner.explanation: By the Sea of Galilee after the resurrection, Jesus asked Peter 'Do you love me?' three times — once for each denial. Each time Peter affirmed his love, Jesus gave him the commission to 'Feed my lambs' and 'Take care of my sheep.'

beginner.question: Peter, James, and Andrew made up Jesus' closest inner circle of three disciples who were present at special events like the Transfiguration.
beginner.explanation: The inner circle was Peter, James, and John — not Andrew. These three were the only disciples present at the Transfiguration, the raising of Jairus' daughter, and Jesus' prayer in Gethsemane.

beginner.question: While Peter was imprisoned by King Herod, an angel appeared in his cell, his chains fell off, and the angel led him past the guards and through the iron gate to freedom.
beginner.explanation: The early church was gathered praying for Peter's release. That night, an angel came, Peter's chains fell off, and the angel led him past sleeping guards through a gate that opened by itself. Peter went to the prayer meeting, astonishing everyone.

beginner.question: In Peter's vision from God, a large sheet descended from heaven containing only clean animals that were acceptable to eat under Jewish law.
beginner.explanation: The sheet contained all kinds of animals, including those considered unclean under Jewish dietary laws. God was teaching Peter that the gospel was for all people — Jews and Gentiles alike — not just those who followed Jewish customs.

beginner.question: When Jesus visited Peter's home, He healed Peter's mother-in-law who was sick in bed with a fever. She got up immediately and began serving them.
beginner.explanation: Jesus touched Peter's mother-in-law's hand and the fever instantly left her. The fact that she immediately got up and served them shows the healing was complete and instantaneous. This also confirms that Peter was married.

beginner.question: Peter wrote two letters in the New Testament, known as 1 Peter and 2 Peter, encouraging believers who were suffering for their faith.
beginner.explanation: The apostle Peter authored two epistles. In 1 Peter, he encouraged Christians enduring persecution. In 2 Peter, he warned against false teachers and urged believers to grow in their knowledge of God.

beginner.question: After denying Jesus three times and hearing the rooster crow, Peter felt no remorse and simply carried on with his night as usual.
beginner.explanation: Peter was devastated. The Bible says he went outside and 'wept bitterly' after remembering Jesus' prediction. Peter's deep remorse shows how much he loved Jesus and how painful the denial was for him.

beginner.question: Peter's original hometown of Bethsaida, on the shore of the Sea of Galilee, was also the hometown of the disciples Andrew and Philip.
beginner.explanation: Peter, Andrew, and Philip all came from Bethsaida, a fishing village on the northern shore of the Sea of Galilee. During Jesus' ministry, Peter and Andrew moved to Capernaum, which became Jesus' base of operations.

beginner.question: At the Last Supper, when Jesus came to wash Peter's feet, Peter initially refused, saying that Jesus should never wash his feet.
beginner.explanation: Peter was shocked that his Lord and Teacher would perform such a lowly task. He protested, 'You shall never wash my feet!' But when Jesus said Peter would have no part with Him otherwise, Peter eagerly asked to have his hands and head washed too.

beginner.question: On the day of Pentecost, Peter quoted the prophet Isaiah to explain to the crowd what was happening with the Holy Spirit.
beginner.explanation: Peter quoted the prophet Joel, not Isaiah. He cited Joel 2:28-32, saying, 'In the last days, God says, I will pour out my Spirit on all people.' This prophecy explained the miraculous events the crowd was witnessing.

beginner.question: In the city of Joppa, Peter raised a woman named Tabitha (also known as Dorcas) from the dead. She was known for making clothing for the poor.
beginner.explanation: Tabitha was beloved for her charitable works, especially making clothes for widows. When she died, believers sent for Peter. He prayed, then said 'Tabitha, get up!' She opened her eyes and sat up, and many people believed because of this miracle.

beginner.question: After Jesus rose from the dead, Peter and some disciples went fishing on the Sea of Galilee but didn't catch any fish at all, even after following a stranger's instructions from the shore.
beginner.explanation: When the stranger (Jesus) told them to cast their net on the right side of the boat, they caught an enormous haul of 153 large fish — so many they could barely haul the net in. John then recognised the man on shore was Jesus.

beginner.question: Jesus told Peter, 'On this rock I will build my church, and the gates of Hades will not overcome it,' after Peter declared Jesus was the Christ.
beginner.explanation: After Peter confessed that Jesus was 'the Christ, the Son of the living God,' Jesus responded with this powerful declaration. He gave Peter the 'keys of the kingdom of heaven,' signifying Peter's foundational role in the early church.

beginner.question: When Peter walked on water and began to sink, Jesus waited to see if Peter could save himself before finally reaching out to help him.
beginner.explanation: Jesus did not wait at all. When Peter cried out 'Lord, save me!', Jesus immediately reached out His hand and caught him. Jesus then gently rebuked Peter's doubt, saying, 'You of little faith, why did you doubt?'

beginner.question: Peter drew a sword and cut off the right ear of Malchus, the high priest's servant, when soldiers came to arrest Jesus in the Garden of Gethsemane.
beginner.explanation: In a bold but misguided attempt to defend Jesus, Peter struck Malchus with his sword. Jesus immediately told Peter to put his sword away and then healed the servant's ear — showing mercy even at His own arrest.

beginner.question: Peter drew his sword and cut off the ear of Pontius Pilate's personal servant when Jesus was being arrested in the Garden of Gethsemane.
beginner.explanation: Peter cut off the ear of the high priest's servant, whose name was Malchus — not a servant of Pontius Pilate. Pilate was the Roman governor; the arrest was organised by the chief priests and Jewish elders.

beginner.question: Andrew was the brother of Simon Peter, and both brothers were fishermen on the Sea of Galilee before following Jesus.
beginner.explanation: Andrew and Peter were brothers who worked together as fishermen. Andrew was actually the first of the two to meet Jesus — he had been a follower of John the Baptist and followed Jesus after John pointed Him out as 'the Lamb of God.'

beginner.question: Before following Jesus, Andrew was already a disciple of John the Baptist, who baptised people in the Jordan River and preached repentance.
beginner.explanation: Andrew was one of two disciples of John the Baptist who followed Jesus after the Baptist pointed Him out saying, 'Look, the Lamb of God!' Andrew then went and found his brother Peter to bring him to Jesus.

beginner.question: After Andrew first met Jesus, he waited several days before telling his brother Simon Peter about it, wanting to be sure first.
beginner.explanation: Andrew went to find his brother Simon Peter immediately — it was the very first thing he did. He told Peter, 'We have found the Messiah,' and brought him straight to Jesus. Andrew's eagerness to share the good news is one of his defining traits.

beginner.question: When Jesus needed to feed over 5,000 people, it was Andrew who brought a boy with five barley loaves and two small fish to Jesus.
beginner.explanation: Andrew found the boy in the crowd with the small lunch and brought him to Jesus. Although Andrew wondered how such a small amount could help, Jesus used it to miraculously feed the entire multitude with food to spare.

beginner.question: Andrew was originally from the city of Jerusalem and moved to the Sea of Galilee region to take up fishing as a trade.
beginner.explanation: Andrew was from Bethsaida, a fishing village on the shore of the Sea of Galilee. He did not move there from Jerusalem. Bethsaida was his hometown, where he and his brother Peter grew up and worked as fishermen.

beginner.question: Andrew was one of the very first disciples to follow Jesus, making him among the earliest of all Jesus' followers.
beginner.explanation: According to John's Gospel, Andrew was one of the first two people to follow Jesus after hearing John the Baptist identify Him as the Lamb of God. This makes Andrew one of the earliest of all Jesus' disciples.

beginner.question: When some Greek visitors wanted to meet Jesus during the Passover festival, Philip told Andrew, and together they brought the request to Jesus.
beginner.explanation: The Greeks approached Philip first, likely because of his Greek name and connections. Philip then told Andrew, and the two of them together went to Jesus with the request. This partnership between Philip and Andrew appears more than once in the Gospels.

beginner.question: Andrew was part of Jesus' inner circle of three disciples, along with his brother Peter and James the son of Zebedee.
beginner.explanation: The inner circle was Peter, James, and John — not Andrew. Despite being Peter's brother, Andrew was not among the three disciples Jesus took to the Transfiguration, Jairus' house, or Gethsemane.

beginner.question: Andrew, along with Peter, James, and John, privately asked Jesus about the signs of the end times while sitting on the Mount of Olives.
beginner.explanation: After Jesus predicted the temple's destruction, these four disciples privately asked Him when it would happen and what signs to expect. Jesus responded with the Olivet Discourse, describing future events and the end of the age.

beginner.question: Jesus called Andrew and Peter while they were mending their nets at home, saying, 'Follow me, and I will make you shepherds of men.'
beginner.explanation: Jesus called them while they were casting their nets into the Sea of Galilee (not mending nets at home), and He said 'fishers of men,' not 'shepherds of men.' The phrase 'fishers of men' connected their fishing trade to their new mission.

beginner.question: James and his brother John were the sons of a fisherman named Zebedee. They were working with their father when Jesus called them to follow Him.
beginner.explanation: James and John were in their father Zebedee's boat mending fishing nets when Jesus called them. They immediately left the boat and their father to follow Jesus, along with the hired servants who also worked for Zebedee.

beginner.question: Jesus nicknamed James and John 'Boanerges,' which means 'Sons of Thunder,' likely because of their bold and fiery personalities.
beginner.explanation: The nickname 'Boanerges' or 'Sons of Thunder' probably reflected their passionate, sometimes impulsive temperaments. They once asked Jesus if they should call down fire from heaven on a Samaritan village that rejected them.

beginner.question: James, the son of Zebedee, was the last of the twelve apostles to die, living to a very old age like his brother John.
beginner.explanation: James was actually the first of the twelve apostles to be martyred, not the last. King Herod Agrippa I had him killed with the sword, as recorded in Acts 12:1-2. His brother John, by contrast, is believed to have been the last apostle to die.

beginner.question: James was one of the three disciples in Jesus' inner circle, along with Peter and John. They witnessed special events like the Transfiguration and Jesus' prayer in Gethsemane.
beginner.explanation: Peter, James, and John formed the inner circle. They alone witnessed the Transfiguration on the mountain, were present when Jesus raised Jairus' daughter, and were taken deeper into Gethsemane to pray with Jesus the night before His crucifixion.

beginner.question: When a Samaritan village refused to welcome Jesus, James and John asked if they should call down a flood from heaven to destroy the village.
beginner.explanation: James and John asked about calling down fire from heaven, not a flood. Their fiery suggestion earned them their 'Sons of Thunder' reputation. Jesus rebuked them, teaching that His mission was to save people, not destroy them.

beginner.question: King Herod Agrippa I had James, the son of Zebedee, killed with the sword, making James the first of the twelve apostles to be martyred for his faith.
beginner.explanation: Acts 12:1-2 records that Herod Agrippa I had James put to death by the sword as part of a persecution of the early church. When Herod saw this pleased the Jewish leaders, he also arrested Peter.

beginner.question: James and John asked Jesus for permission to build a special temple in His honour, a request that upset the other ten disciples.
beginner.explanation: James and John asked to sit at Jesus' right and left hand in His kingdom — positions of highest honour and power. They didn't ask to build a temple. The other ten disciples were indignant when they heard about this ambitious request.

beginner.question: James and John left their father Zebedee's carpentry workshop to follow Jesus when He called them.
beginner.explanation: James and John left their father Zebedee's fishing boat, not a carpentry workshop. They were fishermen, not carpenters. They were mending nets with their father when Jesus called them to become 'fishers of men.'

beginner.question: James, along with Peter and John, was with Jesus in the Garden of Gethsemane the night before His crucifixion and was asked to stay awake and keep watch while Jesus prayed.
beginner.explanation: Jesus took Peter, James, and John deeper into Gethsemane and asked them to keep watch while He prayed in great anguish. Sadly, they kept falling asleep. Jesus found them sleeping three times and said, 'The spirit is willing, but the flesh is weak.'

beginner.question: Peter, James, and John were the only three disciples present when Jesus raised the daughter of Jairus, a synagogue leader, from the dead.
beginner.explanation: Jesus only allowed Peter, James, and John — His inner circle — to enter the room when He raised the 12-year-old girl. He took her hand and said 'Talitha koum!' ('Little girl, get up!'), and she immediately stood up alive.

beginner.question: The apostle John is often referred to as 'the beloved disciple' or 'the disciple whom Jesus loved' in the Gospel of John.
beginner.explanation: The phrase 'the disciple whom Jesus loved' appears several times in John's Gospel. While Jesus loved all His disciples, this title highlights the especially close bond between Jesus and John, who was part of the inner circle.

beginner.question: At the Last Supper, it was Peter who was reclining next to Jesus and leaned on His chest to ask who the betrayer would be.
beginner.explanation: It was John, not Peter, who was reclining next to Jesus and leaned on His chest. Peter actually motioned to John (the beloved disciple) to ask Jesus who the betrayer was, since John was sitting closest to Jesus.

beginner.question: As Jesus was dying on the cross, He looked down and entrusted the care of His mother Mary to the apostle John, saying, 'Here is your mother.'
beginner.explanation: Jesus saw His mother and John standing at the foot of the cross. He said to Mary, 'Woman, here is your son,' and to John, 'Here is your mother.' From that time on, John took Mary into his own home and cared for her.

beginner.question: The apostle John wrote the Gospel of John, the fourth and final Gospel in the New Testament, which focuses on Jesus' identity as the Son of God.
beginner.explanation: John's Gospel is unique among the four Gospels. It includes events and teachings not found in Matthew, Mark, or Luke, such as Jesus turning water into wine, raising Lazarus, and extensive teachings about His divine nature.

beginner.question: John wrote the book of Revelation while he was exiled on the island of Patmos, a small rocky island in the Aegean Sea.
beginner.explanation: John was banished to Patmos, likely by the Roman emperor Domitian, for his testimony about Jesus. While there, he received the dramatic visions of the end times and heaven that make up the book of Revelation.

beginner.question: John was exiled to the island of Cyprus, where he wrote the book of Revelation about his visions of the end times.
beginner.explanation: John was exiled to the island of Patmos, not Cyprus. Patmos is a small rocky island in the Aegean Sea. Cyprus is a much larger island associated with Barnabas, who was from there.

beginner.question: In addition to his Gospel and Revelation, John also wrote three short letters in the New Testament known as 1 John, 2 John, and 3 John.
beginner.explanation: John authored five New Testament books in total: the Gospel of John, 1 John, 2 John, 3 John, and Revelation. His letters emphasise love, truth, and the importance of walking in the light.

beginner.question: When Peter and John ran to Jesus' empty tomb on Easter morning, Peter arrived first but waited outside for John before entering.
beginner.explanation: It was the other way around. John outran Peter and arrived first, but he only looked in without entering. When Peter arrived, he went straight inside the tomb. Then John followed him in and 'saw and believed.'

beginner.question: Church tradition holds that John was the only one of the twelve original apostles who died of natural causes in old age, rather than being martyred.
beginner.explanation: While all the other apostles are traditionally believed to have been martyred for their faith, John is believed to have lived into his 90s and died of natural causes in Ephesus. He was, however, persecuted and exiled to Patmos.

beginner.question: John 3:16, one of the most famous verses in the Bible, was written by the apostle Peter in one of his letters to the early church.
beginner.explanation: John 3:16 — 'For God so loved the world that he gave his one and only Son...' — was written by the apostle John in his Gospel, not by Peter. It records words Jesus spoke to Nicodemus during a nighttime conversation.

beginner.question: John opens his Gospel with the words, 'In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God,' referring to Jesus.
beginner.explanation: The opening of John's Gospel is one of the most profound passages in the Bible. By calling Jesus 'the Word' (Logos), John declares that Jesus existed from the very beginning, was with God, and was God — a clear statement of Christ's divinity.

beginner.question: John and his brother James were nicknamed 'Sons of Thunder' by Jesus because of their bold, passionate personalities.
beginner.explanation: Jesus gave James and John the Aramaic nickname 'Boanerges,' meaning 'Sons of Thunder.' Their fiery temperament was demonstrated when they wanted to call fire from heaven on a Samaritan village that rejected Jesus.

beginner.question: When the Jewish leaders ordered Peter and John to stop preaching about Jesus, they replied that they must obey God rather than men.
beginner.explanation: After healing the lame man and being arrested, Peter and John were commanded to stop speaking about Jesus. They boldly replied, 'Which is right in God's eyes: to listen to you, or to him? We cannot help speaking about what we have seen and heard.'

beginner.question: John was the oldest of the twelve apostles and the first to die, according to church tradition.
beginner.explanation: Church tradition holds the opposite: John was the youngest of the twelve apostles and the last to die. He is believed to have lived into his 90s and died of natural causes in Ephesus, while James his brother was the first apostle martyred.

beginner.question: John was one of the three disciples in Jesus' inner circle, along with Peter and James. They were present at the Transfiguration and other key events.
beginner.explanation: Peter, James, and John formed the inner three. They alone witnessed Jesus' transfiguration on the mountain, were present at the raising of Jairus' daughter, and accompanied Jesus deepest into the Garden of Gethsemane.

beginner.question: Before becoming a disciple of Jesus, Matthew (also known as Levi) was a tax collector — a profession that was despised by most Jewish people.
beginner.explanation: Matthew worked as a tax collector in Capernaum, collecting taxes on behalf of the Roman Empire. This job was deeply hated because tax collectors were seen as traitors who collaborated with Rome and often cheated their own people.

beginner.question: Matthew was also known by the name Barnabas in the Gospels of Mark and Luke.
beginner.explanation: Matthew was also called Levi, not Barnabas. The Gospels of Mark and Luke refer to him as 'Levi' when describing his calling by Jesus. Barnabas was a completely different person — a companion of Paul from Cyprus.

beginner.question: When Jesus called Matthew, He simply said 'Follow me,' and Matthew immediately got up from his tax booth and followed, leaving everything behind.
beginner.explanation: Jesus' call was simple yet powerful: just two words — 'Follow me.' Matthew's response was immediate and total. He got up, left his lucrative tax booth behind, and followed Jesus without hesitation.

beginner.question: After deciding to follow Jesus, Matthew held a great banquet at his house and invited many tax collectors and sinners to meet Jesus.
beginner.explanation: Matthew threw a large dinner party at his house to celebrate his new life and introduce his friends to Jesus. The guests were fellow tax collectors and people labelled 'sinners' by religious society — the very people Jesus came to reach.

beginner.question: The Pharisees praised Jesus for going to Matthew's house and eating dinner with tax collectors and sinners, recognising His compassion.
beginner.explanation: The Pharisees criticised Jesus harshly for eating with tax collectors and sinners. They asked His disciples, 'Why does your teacher eat with such people?' Jesus responded, 'It is not the healthy who need a doctor, but the sick.'

beginner.question: Tax collectors like Matthew were hated by their fellow Jews because they worked for the occupying Roman Empire and often overcharged people to enrich themselves.
beginner.explanation: Tax collectors served Rome's interests and were seen as traitors to their own people. Many charged more than required and pocketed the difference. This made them among the most despised members of Jewish society.

beginner.question: Matthew is traditionally credited with writing the Gospel of Mark, the second book of the New Testament.
beginner.explanation: Matthew wrote the Gospel of Matthew, the first book of the New Testament — not the Gospel of Mark. Mark's Gospel is traditionally attributed to John Mark, a companion of Peter and Paul.

beginner.question: Matthew's Gospel begins with a genealogy that traces Jesus' family line from Abraham through King David to Jesus, showing He was the promised Messiah.
beginner.explanation: Matthew opens his Gospel with a detailed genealogy. By tracing Jesus' lineage from Abraham (father of the Jewish nation) through David (Israel's greatest king), Matthew demonstrated to his primarily Jewish audience that Jesus was the long-awaited Messiah.

beginner.question: When the Pharisees criticised Jesus for eating with sinners at Matthew's house, Jesus responded by saying, 'It is not the healthy who need a doctor, but the sick.'
beginner.explanation: This was Jesus' way of explaining that His mission was specifically to reach those who recognised their need for God's help. He came not for those who considered themselves righteous, but for those who knew they were sinners.

beginner.question: Matthew's tax booth was located in Capernaum, a town on the northern shore of the Sea of Galilee that served as a major hub for Jesus' ministry.
beginner.explanation: Capernaum sat on a major trade route, making it an ideal location for collecting customs taxes. This town also served as Jesus' home base during His Galilean ministry, where He performed many miracles and taught in the synagogue.

beginner.question: Thomas, one of Jesus' twelve disciples, refused to believe Jesus had risen from the dead until he could see and touch the nail marks in Jesus' hands and the wound in His side.
beginner.explanation: Thomas was absent when Jesus first appeared to the disciples. He declared, 'Unless I see the nail marks in his hands and put my finger where the nails were, and put my hand into his side, I will not believe.' This is why he is called 'Doubting Thomas.'

beginner.question: When Thomas finally saw the risen Jesus, he said, 'I still need more proof before I can believe you have risen from the dead.'
beginner.explanation: Thomas did not ask for more proof. When he saw Jesus, all his doubt vanished instantly. He made one of the most powerful declarations of faith in the Bible: 'My Lord and my God!' — recognising Jesus as both Lord and God.

beginner.question: Thomas was also known by the Greek name 'Didymus,' which, like the name Thomas in Aramaic, means 'twin.'
beginner.explanation: Both 'Thomas' (Aramaic) and 'Didymus' (Greek) mean 'twin.' The Bible never tells us who Thomas' twin was, but this double naming in two languages shows he was known in both Aramaic-speaking and Greek-speaking communities.

beginner.question: When Jesus decided to go to Lazarus despite the danger, Thomas bravely told the other disciples, 'Let us also go, that we may die with him.'
beginner.explanation: This often-overlooked moment shows Thomas' courage and loyalty. Knowing that the Jewish leaders in Judea wanted to kill Jesus, Thomas rallied the others to go anyway, even if it meant death. He was far braver than his 'Doubting' nickname suggests.

beginner.question: Thomas was present in the room when Jesus first appeared to the disciples after the resurrection and showed them His hands and side.
beginner.explanation: Thomas was absent when Jesus first appeared. The other disciples told Thomas they had seen the Lord, but he refused to believe without seeing for himself. Jesus returned eight days later specifically to address Thomas' doubt.

beginner.question: Thomas' honest question at the Last Supper — 'Lord, we don't know where you are going, so how can we know the way?' — prompted Jesus to say, 'I am the way, the truth, and the life.'
beginner.explanation: Thomas' straightforward admission of confusion led to one of Jesus' most famous and important statements. Without Thomas' honest question, we might not have this powerful declaration that Jesus Himself is the way to God.

beginner.question: After seeing the risen Jesus, Thomas declared, 'My Lord and my God!' — one of the strongest affirmations of Jesus' divinity in the entire New Testament.
beginner.explanation: Thomas' exclamation goes beyond calling Jesus a teacher or prophet — he called Jesus both 'Lord' and 'God.' Jesus accepted this declaration without correction, confirming its truth. This is one of the clearest statements that Jesus is God.

beginner.question: Jesus reappeared to Thomas the very next day after His first appearance to the other disciples, to address Thomas' doubt right away.
beginner.explanation: Jesus did not return the next day. He waited eight days before appearing again — this time with Thomas present. The waiting period may have been intentional, giving Thomas time to hear the testimony of the others before seeing Jesus himself.

beginner.question: After seeing the risen Jesus, Thomas' doubt was completely overcome, and he made one of the strongest declarations of Jesus' divinity found anywhere in the New Testament.
beginner.explanation: Thomas went from refusing to believe to making the profound confession 'My Lord and my God!' His story shows that honest doubt, when brought to Jesus, can lead to the deepest faith. Thomas became a powerful witness of the resurrection.

beginner.question: Jesus said, 'Blessed are those who see and believe,' praising Thomas specifically for needing to see the evidence before accepting the resurrection.
beginner.explanation: Jesus said the opposite: 'Blessed are those who have NOT seen and yet have believed.' Rather than praising Thomas for needing evidence, Jesus highlighted that future believers who accept by faith — without physically seeing — are especially blessed.

beginner.question: After Jesus called Philip, the first thing Philip did was find his friend Nathanael and tell him about Jesus of Nazareth.
beginner.explanation: Like Andrew who immediately told his brother Peter, Philip immediately shared the news with his friend Nathanael. He said, 'We have found the one Moses wrote about in the Law.' When Nathanael was sceptical, Philip simply said, 'Come and see.'

beginner.question: Before the miracle of feeding the 5,000, Jesus asked Peter where they could buy enough bread for the crowd. This was a test of Peter's faith.
beginner.explanation: Jesus asked Philip, not Peter, where to buy bread for the crowd. The Gospel of John specifically notes that Jesus asked Philip to test him, since Jesus already knew what He was going to do. Philip responded that 200 denarii wouldn't be enough.

beginner.question: Philip, Andrew, and Peter were all from the same town of Bethsaida, a fishing village on the shore of the Sea of Galilee.
beginner.explanation: John 1:44 specifically notes that Philip was from Bethsaida, 'the town of Andrew and Peter.' This shared hometown may explain why several of the earliest disciples knew each other and were called in quick succession.

beginner.question: At the Last Supper, Philip asked Jesus, 'Lord, show us the Father and that will be enough for us,' prompting Jesus to teach about His unity with the Father.
beginner.explanation: Philip's request led to one of Jesus' most important theological statements: 'Anyone who has seen me has seen the Father.' Jesus taught that He and the Father are so united that to know Jesus is to know God the Father.

beginner.question: Philip calculated that 50 denarii — about two months' wages — would be enough to buy bread for the crowd of 5,000 before Jesus performed the miracle.
beginner.explanation: Philip said that even 200 denarii (about eight months' wages) would NOT be enough to buy bread for everyone. He wasn't saying they could afford it — he was emphasising the impossibility of feeding such a huge crowd.

beginner.question: When Nathanael doubted that anything good could come from the small town of Nazareth, Philip wisely responded by simply inviting him to 'Come and see' for himself.
